'oppression, distress', apparently from \*aga-laikja-, could theoretic.ally be a folk-etymological distortion of \*aglaitja-, but is more likely to have been derived from the verb \*agan- 'to fear' (q.v.) with the suffix \*-laiklj)a-, cf. ON -leikr.

\*agan- pret-pres. 'to fear' - Go. ogan, 3sg. og pret.-pres. 'id.' (IE) - Gr. li)coμm 'to grieve, lament' < \*h₂egh-e-; Olr. -agadar 'to fear' < \*h₂e-h₂09h·e-. The Gothic verb is conjugated as a preterite-present of the reduplicating class, PGm. 3sg. \*oge, 3pl. \*iigun < \*h₂e-h₂ogh-e, \*h₂e-h₂ogh-nt, which is formally close to Olr. -agadar < \*h₂e-h₂ogh- (Matasovic 2009: 26). The full-grade root \*iig- served as the basis for the Gothic infinitive form ogan sis, while the original zero grade survived in the petrified ptc. un-agands adj. 'fearless'. Also cf. \*agiz-, Go. agei, OHG agf, egf f. 'fear' < \*agfn-, \*ogjan- 'to scare' and OE oga m. 'terror' < \*iigan-.

\*agana- n. 'bait' - ON agn n. 'id.', Far. agn n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂el{H-ono- (IE) - Identical to Skt. afona- n. 'food'. A NGm. formation closely related to Skt. <iSana-, a derivation from Skt. asncfo 'to eat'. See also \*aiwjan-.

<!-- pdf-page: 042; section: dictionary -->

\*agipo- f. 'harrow' - OE egede f. 'id.', OFri. eide f. 'id.', OS egitha f. 'id.', MDu. egede, eegde, eg f. 'id.', OHG egida f. 'id.', MHG egede, egde f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ok-et(EUR) - OPru. aketes, Lith. akJCios f.pl. 'harrow' (with -J- after akJti 'to harrow'); Lat occa f. 'harrow' ( < \*otek-eh₂-), W aged 'id.'. A European word. The form \*agiflo- may be a remodeling of \*agefli-, the equivalent of \*oteki- as found in the other languages; synchronically it looks like a derivation from \*agjan-, cf. OS gi-eggian, OHG ecken 'to harrow', with the same suffix as in OHG erida 'plough' < \*ariflo- and ON sigo 'scythe' < \*segiflo- (EWAhd: II, 958). However, the verb is attested later than the noun and could be a back-formation. No further etymology. Oss. adreg 'harrow', apparently metathesized from \*agred, clearly belongs here too, but may have been borrowed from a European language. Gr. 64tva f. 'harrow(?)' is unrelated, as it seems to be derived from 64uc; adj. 'sharp, pointed'.

\*agiz- n. 'fear' - Go. agis n. 'id.', OE ege m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂egh-es- (IE) - Identical to Gr. axoc; n. 'sadness, pain'. An s-stem nom. \*agaz, pl. \*agizo < \*h₂egh-os, \*h₂egh-es-eh₂. The stem variant \*agiz- was generalized at an early stage (cf. Go. agis), and gave rise to a secondary i-stem \*agi- in West Germanic following the regular loss of final \*-z (cf. \*luniz- 'linchpin'). The original s-stem served as the basis for OHG egison, MHG, MDu. eisen w.v. 'to fear' < \*agisojan-, which in turn gave OHG agiso, egiso, OS egiso, OE egesa m. 'fear' < \*agisan-. Related to \*agan- and \*ogjan-.

\*agjo- f. 'edge, blade' - ON egg f. 'edge', OFri. edze m. 'edge, blade', OS eggia f. 'edge, sword', OHG ecka, egga f. 'edge, point, corner', G Ecke f. 'corner, edge' ⇒ \*h₂el<-ieh₂- (IE). A ieh₂-stem to the PIE root \*h₂el<- 'sharp', for which see e.g. \*ahiz-.

\*ag/kkon- f. 'magpie' - OE age, agu f. 'id.', WFri. akke c. 'id.', OHG aga(?) f. 'id.', MHG age f. 'id.' (GM). The co-occurrence of OE age < \*agon- and WFri. akke < \*akkon- could point to an old n-stem paradigm nom. \*ago, gen. \*akkaz < \*H6gh-on, \*Hogh-n-6s. The word further occurs in a compound with \*star(r)a(n)- in the other WGm. languages, cf. OS agastria, MLG he(g)ister, MDu. exter, aecster, Du. ekster 'id.' < \*aga-str-jon-. OHG agalstra, egilistra, G Elster, on the other hand, was created to a diminutive \*agila-. Further note OHG agaza < \*agatiin-.

\*agla- adj. 'painful' - Go. agls\* adj. 'disgraceful' ⇒ \*h₂egh-/o- (IE) - Skt. aghci- adj. 'evil, bad, dangerous', YAv. aya- adj. 'bad, evil' < \*h₂ogh-o-; Gr. &xvuμm 'to grieve, lament for' < \*h₂egh-neu-.

<!-- pdf-page: 043; section: dictionary -->

An adjective possibly related to \*agan- 'to fear' and cognates. Also cf. Go. agljan\* 'to harm', Nw. egle 'to provoke, incite', OE eglan 'to trouble', ME eilen 'to trouble, afflict, harm', E ail 'to trouble' < \*agljan- and Go. aglo f. 'affliction'. See also \*agalaitja- and \*aglu-.

\*aglu- adj. 'difficult' - Go. aglus\* adj. 'difficult, toilsome', OE egle adj. 'difficult', E obs. ail adj. 'loathsome' (IE). The original u-stem that is attested as Go. aglus\* resurfaces as the ja-stem eg(e)le in OE. No clear etymology, but probably related to \*ag/a-.

\*agra- m. 'flood' - OE eagor 'sea, flood', E eagre 'tidal bore', Du. agger c. 'sea swell during low tide' ⇒ \*h₂ekW-r6- (EUR). A ro-stem created to the same root as found in \*ahwo- 'water'. E acker 'turbulent current in the sea; ripple furrow in the water' probably does not belong here, but may be identical to \*akra- 1 'field', though with a more primary meaning 'furrow'.

\*ahano- - \*agano- f. 'chaff, awns' - Go. ahana f. 'chaff, ON pgn f. 'id.', Far. egn f. 'awn, beard of barley', Elfd. aungen f. 'id.', OE reg nan f.pl. 'awns, chaff, refuse', E awns, OHG agana f. 'chaff, awn, straw', G Ahne f. 'fiber from hemp or flax' ⇒ \*h₂er1f.l-on-eh₂- (IE) - Close to Lat agna f. 'ear of grain' < \* h₂eClf.l-e/on-eh₂- (the absence of Thurneysen's law points to syncope, Jürgen van den Heuvel, p.c.); also cf. Gr. iiKwv, -ovTo<; m. 'javelin, dart', OPru. ackons 'awn' < \*h₂ek-on-. An o-stem alternating between \*ahanoand \*agano- < quasi-PIE

\*h₂erlf.J-on-eh₂- - \*h₂eCk'l-6n-eh₂-. The origin of the Verner alternation is unclear, as accentual mobility is unexpected in secondary eh₂-stems. It is possible, however, that the feminine developed from a neuter collective form \*h₂eCk'l-on-eh₂- (cf. Lat. agna) created to the n-stem \*h₂efk'l-on- as supported by Gr. CiKwv. It must then be assumed that the Verner alternation was adopted from this n-stem, which no doubt did have an accentually mobile paradigm. See also \*ahila- - \*agi/a- and \*ahiz-.

\*ahila- - \*agila- m. 'awn' - OE egl f. 'mote', ME ei/e 'awn, prickle', OHG ahi/ m. 'awn', G dial. Ache/ f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂elf.-i/-eh₂- (IE) - Close to Gr. aJ01 f. 'point' < \*h₂elf.-eh₂-. Derived from the root \*h₂elf.- 'sharp' (cf. \*ahiz- 'ear'). With the same semantics (but different suffixations), cf. Cz. osina f. 'awn' and possibly Arm. hask 'ear' < \*h₂ell- plus a velar suffix.

\*ahiz- n. 'ear' - Go. ahs n. 'id.', ON ax n. 'id.', Far. aks n. 'id.', OE ear, rehher, eher n. 'id.', E ear, OFri. iir pl. 'harvest', Du. aar c. 'ear', OHG ahar, ehir n. 'id.',

<!-- pdf-page: 044; section: dictionary -->

G Ahre f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂eK-es- (IE) - ToA cik, ToB cike 'end', Lat. acus, gen. aceris n. 'husk, chaff. An s-stem to the root \*h₂eK- 'sharp'. The original paradigm \*ahaz, pl. \*ahizo < \*h₂eK-os, \*h₂K-es-eh₂ gave rise to a number of secondary stem formations, viz. Go. ahs, ON ax < \*ahsa-, OHG ehir < \*ahiza- and Du. aar <

\*ahaza-. See also \*ahano- - \*agano-.

\*ahjan- w.v. 'to think' - Go. ahjan w.v. 'to assume, think' (GM). No clear etymology. The verb is obviously related to Go. aha m. 'mind, understanding' < \*ahan-, Go. ahma m. 'spirit' < \*ahman- and PGm. \*ahto'attention' ( q.v.), but it lacks clear extra-Gm. cognates. The connections with \*augon- 'eye' < \*h₃e'J<w- (Osthoff 1910: 175) and Gr. oKVo<; m. 'hesitation, shrinking' (Pokorny IEW: 774) do not convince, at any rate. Theoretically, the verb could be identical to ON reja w.v. 'to graze' < \*ahjan- (through a meaning 'to ruminate'), but it is preferable, in my view, to reconstruct this verb as \*aiwjan- (q.v.).

\*ahslo- f. 'shoulder, armpit' - ON pxl f. 'shoulder', Far. 11ksl f. 'id.', OE eaxl f. 'armpit', OS ahs/a f. 'shoulder', OHG ahsa/a, ahs/a f. 'id.' (IE) - Lat ala f. 'armpit, wing', W echel f. 'axis' < \*h₂eks-(i)/-eh₂-. Derived from PIE \*h₂eKs-i- with an /-suffix, cf. \*nab/an- 'navel'.

\*ahso- f. 'axle' - Nw. dial. oks f. 'tip of the hub', OE eax m. 'axle', MDu. asse f. 'id.', Du. as c. 'id.', OHG ahsa f. 'id.', G Achse f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂eKs-eh₂- (IE) - Skt ak$a- 'axle', YAv. afo- 'armpit' < \*h₂e/{-so-; OPru. assis, Lith. asis m. 'axle, axis', CS osb, Ru. os' f. 'id.', Lat. axis m. 'id.', Olr. ais f. 'back' ( < "spine"?) < \*h₂eKs-i-; Gr. &Ɍwv m. 'axle' < \*h₂eKs-on-. Cf. \*ahs/6- and the diminutive ON pxull, Far. aksil, Elfd. aksel m. 'axle' < \*ahsula-.

\*ahtau num. 'eight' - Go. ahtau num. 'id.', ON atta num. 'id.', Far. atta num. 'id.', Elfd. atta num. 'id.', OE eahta num. 'id.', E eight, OFri. achta num. 'id.', OS ahto num. 'id.', Du. acht num. 'id.', OHG ahto num. 'id.', G acht num. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₃eKt-eh₃u (IE) - ToA okiit, ToB okt, Skt. a$td, a$tc1u, YAv. asta, Lith. astuoni, Arm. ut', Alb. tete, Gr. OKTW, Lat. octo, Olr. ocht, W wyth, Bret. eiz num. 'eight'. The Indo-European word for 'eight'. Originally, the numeral may have come about as a dual formation to Av. asti- 'width of four fingers, palm' < \*h₃eK-ti- (Henning 1948: 69). The meaning of this Avestan word is not entirely certain, however. See also \*ahtudan-.

<!-- pdf-page: 045; section: dictionary -->

\*ahto- f. 'consideration, attention' - OE eaht, reht, eht f. 'deliberation', MDu. acht f. 'attention', OHG ahta f. 'esteem, reflection, consideration, thought', MHG ahte f. 'evaluation; attention' (DRV). Derived from \*ahjan- 'to think'. Also cf. OE eahtian, OS ahton, Du. achten, OHG ahton, G achten w.v. 'to mind, deem' < \*ahtojan- and ON, Far. ret/a w.v. 'to intend' < \*ahtilon-.

\*ahtudan- num. 'eighth' - Go. ahtuda num. 'id.', ON atti, attandi, attundi num. 'id.', Far. attandi num. 'id.', Elfd. dttund num. 'id.', OE eahtooa num. 'id.', E eighth, OFri. achtenda, achtunda, achtanda num. 'id.', OS ahtodo num. 'id.', Du. achtste num. 'id.', OHG ahtodo num. 'id.', G achte num. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂eKt-h₃u-tHo- (IE) - ToA oktiint, ToB oktante num. 'eighth' < \*h₃eKt-h₃-ntHo-; Skt. a$tama-, YAv. astama-, Oss. restrem, Lith. iismas, OCS osm'b, Ru. vos'm6j, SCr. osmi num. 'id.' < \*h₃e/(t-mHo-; Gr. oycSoo<;, Lat octiivus num. 'id.' < \*h₃e/(t-h₃u-o-. · The ordinal to \*ahtau 'eight' ( q.v.).

\*ahurna- m. 'maple' - OHG ahorn, ahurn, acharn m. 'id.', G Ahorn m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ak[-no- (NIE) - Lat. acer, -eris n. 'maple' < \*ak-r- (cf. acernus adj. 'made of maple'); hardly to Gr. Hsch. aKacro<; 'maple' and aKapva 'laurel-tree'. A European word, possibly of non-IE origin. The main form, PGm. \*ahurna-, was adopted by Slavic, cf. OCS avor-ov'b 'made of maple'. The Upper German form Acher may continue \*ahra- without a nasal, to which Ahre and A.hre are secondary feminine derivations in \*-OJo- (cf. OHG voraha, MoG FOhre 'pine' < \*furhwjo(n)- vs. ON Jura < \*furhwon-). The latter variant probably served as the source for Sw. iira and Da. rere!.trre. It seems unlikely, at any rate, that these forms continue an ablauting form \*ehiron- through ON \*rera (thus Torp 1909: 23).

\*ahwo- f. 'river' - Go. afva f. 'body of water, river', ON a f. 'river', Far. a f. 'river; stream, brook', Elfd. d f. 'id.', OE ea f. 'stream', OS aha f. 'water, river', OHG aha f. 'river', MHG ahe f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂ekw-eh₂- (EUR) - Identical to Lat. aqua f. 'water'. A Germanic-Italic isogloss. The word has been claimed to stem from a non-IE language (Beekes 1998), but it is more likely to be a formal variant of Hitt bapa- c. 'river', Skt ap-, OAv. ap- f. 'water' < \*h₂ep-, Lat. amnis f. 'stream, river' < \*h₂ep-ni- and Olr. ab, aub f., W afon f. 'river' < \*h₂epHon(with \*-pH- > \*b as in Skt. pfbati 'to drink' < PIE \*pi-ph₃-e-). Perhaps the cluster \*-pH- developed into \*-kw- in Germanic and Latin. See further \*agra-, \*aujo- and \*egja-.

\*aibra- adj. 'harsh' - OE iifor adj. 'vehement, dire, hateful', OHG eipar, eiber, eiver adj. 'harsh, rough' (GM).

<!-- pdf-page: 046; section: dictionary -->

A ra-stem adjective, possibly related to MHG ifer, G Eifer m. 'zeal, eagerness' < \*ifra-.

\*aida- m. 'pyre' - OE ad m. 'pyre, fire', OFri. a.de OHG eit m. 'fireplace, pyre', MHG eit m. 'fire, oven' ⇒ \*h₂eidh-o- (IE) - Identical to Skt. edha- m. 'firewood', Gr. al-Boe; m. 'firebrand', Olr. aed 'fire'; also cf. Gr. aWoc; adj. 'burnt'. The word appears to continue an extension \*h₂ei-dhhi-o- "what is put on the fire" to the root \*h₂ei- 'to burn', cf. \*aiza- 'oar'. Also cf. OHG, MHG eiten w.v. 'to burn, harden with fire' < \*aidjan-.

\*aidja- n. 'isthmus' - ON eio, eioi n. 'id.', Far. eioi n. 'id.', Nw. eid(e) n. 'isthmus; passable valley' ⇒ \*h₁oi-tio- (ORV). A NGm. word derived from the PIE root \*h₁ei- 'to go', for which see \*idiijan-. It is perhaps possible to start from a nominalization of a gerundive in \*-tio- meaning 'passable'.

\*aigan- pret.-pres. 'to own, possess' - Go. aigan, 3sg. aih, aig, 3pl. aigun, aihun pret.-pres. 'id.', ON eiga, 3sg. a, 3pl. eigu pret.-pres. 'to own, possess; to have', Far. eiga, 3sg. eigur, 3pl. eiga pret.-pres. 'id.', Elfd. iega w.v. 'to possess', OE iigan, 3sg. ah, 3pl. agon pret.-pres. 'id.', E to owe, OFri. aga, 3sg. ag, eg, 3pl. aga, ega pret.-pres. 'to be obliged, owe, possess', OS egan, 3pl. egun pret.-pres. 'to own, possess; to have', OHG eigan, heigan, 3pl. eigun pret.-pres. 'to have, own, keep, receive', MHG eigen, heigen pret.-pres. 'id.' ⇒ \*(h₂e-)h₂6ik-(?) (IE) - ToB aik- 'to known' (< "to have in mind"), Skt. fse, f$te 'to control, possess', OAv. ise, YAv. iste 3sg.med. 'to have' < \*h₂e-h₂ili-. A preterite-present 3sg. \*aihe, 3pl. \*aigunp. The lack of ablaut in this verb is unique, the expected paradigm being \*aihe, \*igunp < \*h₂6ili-e, \*h₂il<-6t. It is possible that the ablaut was removed at an early stage, but if so, the question still remains why the paradigm of only this preterite-present was leveled, while all others kept their ablaut intact right through to the modern Germanic languages. It therefore seems likely that the verb belonged to the reduplicating class with o-grade throughout the preterite (cf. Seebold 1970: 70). I thus reconstruct the Pre-Gm. paradigm as \*h₂e-h₂6ili-e, \*h₂e-h₂i-J<-nt, which regularly should have produced PGm. \*aihe ( < \*aike by Osthoff's law), \*aigunp. See also \*aigena- and \*aihti-.

\*aigena- adj. 'own' - ON eiginn adj. 'id.', Far. egin adj. 'id.', Elfd. iegen adj. 'id.', OE agen adj. 'id.', E own, OFri. ain, Jin adj. 'id.', OS egan adj. 'id.', Du. eigen adj. 'id.', OHG eigan adj. 'id.', G eigen adj. 'id.' (DRV). An adjective derived from \*aigan- (q.v.), whence Go. ga-aiginon, eigenen w.v. 'to make one's own, to appropriate' < \*aigeniijan-. Also cf. Go. aigin, ON eigin, OE iigen, OFri. ain, ein, OS egan, OHG eigan n. 'property'.

<!-- pdf-page: 047; section: dictionary -->

\*aiginp- n. 'shoot, barb' - ON ei9in n. 'new sprout of corn', Far. e9i n. 'corn, straw; sprout, shoot; barleyfield', Nw. eiend(e) f./n. 'grain germ, barb', Sw. dial. ii.jen(ne) n. 'grain, insignificant thing' ⇒ \*h₂eik-ent-(?) (IE). Related to \*ai9/a- 'barb' (q.v.). The exact derivation of the word is unclear. Nw. eiend(e) and Sw. ii.jenne probably point to ON \*ei9endi < \*ai9indja- < \*h₂ei/(-ent-io-(?), but ON ei9in lacks the dental and Far. e9i does not even show a trace of the final n. All these formal difficulties make the reconstruction of the original form rather problematic. A neuter participle \*ai9inp- < \*h₂eiK-ent? No clear extra-Germanic parallels. MW e9in pl. 'shoots, sprouts, blades' looks like a Norse loan, but borrowing in the opposite direction cannot be excluded.

\*aigla- m. 'shoot' - Sw. dial. e9e/, ii.jel m. 'seed sprout' ⇒ \*h₂eiK-(t)lo- (EUR) - Close to Gr. aTJ<AoL m.pl. 'corners of an arrow' < \*h₂ei/(-(t)lo-; OPru. aycu/o f. 'needle', CS i9/a, Ru. i9lci, SCr. i9/a f. 'id.' < \*h₂il<-tle-h₂-; also cf. Gr. aixμd f. 'point of a spear, spear', Lith. iesmas, jiesmas m. 'spit, broach', OPru. aysmis 'id.' < \*h₂eil<-smo/eh₂-. A Germanic-Greek isogloss derived from a root \*h₂eiK- 'barb' (cf. Pokorny IEW: 15). For the semantics, cf. Far. ncil f. 'needle; sprout, shoot, germinating seed corn' ( < \*neplo-). Related to \*ai9inp- 'sprout'.

\*aihti- f. 'property, belongings; family' - Go. aihts f. 'property', ON rett f. 'family', Far. rett f. 'lineage, descent, family', OE reht f. 'possessions, property, lands', OHG eht f. 'possession, goods, wealth' (DRY). A ti-stem created to the root of \*ai9an- (q.v.). Note that the sometimes connected Ir. icht m. 'progeny, tribe' (quasi < \*h₂il<-ti-) is more likely to have been derived from root \*iek- 'to speak' (see \*jehan-) in view of the related MW ieith f. 'language, nation, race' < \*iek-ti-.

\*alhtrojan- w.v. 'to beg' - Go. aihtron wv. 'id.' (GM). Etymology uncertain: perhaps somehow derived from \*aikan- 'to appropriate' (q.v.). Unrelated to Gr. oiJCTp6׵ adj. 'miserable'.

\*aik- f. 'oak' - ON eik f. 'id.', Far. eik f. 'id.', Elfd. iek f. 'id.', OE iic, pl. rec f. 'id.', E oak, OS ek f. 'id.', Du. eik c. 'id.', OHG eih f. 'id.', G Eiche f. 'id.' ⇒ \*aig- (EUR) - Gr. aiyl.Awljl m. 'kind of oak'; ?Lat. aesculus f. 'winter oak'; ?Lith. qzuolas, diiuols, ciuiuolas, Latv. uozuols, OPru. ansonis 'oak'. Also cf. OHG eihhi/a, G Eichel f., Du. eikel c. 'acorn' < \*aikilon-. A European word with no certain IE etymology. The generally compared Gr. aiyiAwljl 'kind of oak' could be a compound of \*ai'.4 and AW'ltTJ 'cork', which opens the possibility of unifying the Greek and Germanic words into a PIE root noun \*ai!}-s. However, this root noun inflection may also be an indication that we are dealing with a non-IE item (cf. \*arwit-). This is supported by the fact

<!-- pdf-page: 048; section: dictionary -->

that the derivation of the word is problematic in the other languages. The derivation of Lat. aesculus 'kind of oak-tree' ( < \*aig-sculus?) is obscure, and of the Baltic forms only the variant Lith. aiiuols < \*aig-61 can regularly be related. Further cf. Basq. ezkur 'acorn' (cf. Genaust 1996: 42; differently Trask 2008: 188).

\*aikan- s.v. 'to make one's own(?)' - Go. afaikan s.v. 'to deny, abjure', OHG in-eihhan s.v. 'to sacrifice; dedicate' (DRV). The fact that Go. aikan and OHG eihhan (cf. ChWdW9: 245) are reduplicated verbs, which typically have an o-grade of the root, could imply a pre-Gm. formation \*HoiCB1-e-. One could then compare Latv. afcinfit 'to call, invite' < \*Hoig-n(e)h₂- and Olr. eigid 'to cry' < \*h₁dg1-e-. From the Germanic perspective, however, it is more likely that the root \*aik- continues \*aikk- ( < \*Hoik-n-1, and developed from the preterite-present \*aigan- 'to own' (q.v.) with a semantic shift 'to make one's own' > 'to accept' (cf. EWAhd: II, 972). This is supported by OHG ur-eiche adj. 'own' < \*uz-aikja- and OHG (gi-)eihhon w.v. 'to claim', MHG eichen w.v. 'to award, to dedicate' < \*aikolja)n- as well as MHG eichenen w.v. 'to claim' < \*aiknjan- (a media-passive factitive "to make oneself own"). Note that the comparison of OHG eihhinon with the aforementioned Latv. afcinfit is deceptive anyway, because the Latvian ending -inti.- is a conflation of PBalt. \*-nii- and \*-in- from PIE \*-neh₂- - \*-ph₂-, and cannot be equated with the element -en- of MHG eichenen.

\*aikjan- w.v. 'to irritate' - Nw. eikja w.v. 'to annoy, pester' ⇒ \*h₂oi9-eie(IE) - Identical to Skt. ejayati 'to shake'; also cf. Skt. ejati 'to move, stir, flutter' < \*h₂ei9-e-; Gr. aiyi<; f. 'rushing storm, hurricane' < \*h₂ei9-i-; OCS igra f. 'entertainment, dance, play' < PSI. \*jbgra < \*h₂i9-reh₂-. A causative preserved in both Sanskrit and Germanic. Also cf. OE iico/, iicul adj. 'excited (by fear)' < \*aikala- and ON eikinn adj. 'wild, furious', Ice!. eikinn adj. 'restive (of bulls)', Nw. eikjen adj. 'restive, stubborn', Elfd. ietjin adj. 'harsh, strong, sharp' < \*aikina-.

\*aikwernan- - \*ikurnan- m. 'squirrel' - ON fkorni m. 'id.', Far. fkorni m. 'id.', Elfd. aikuonn m. 'id.', OE iicweorn(a) m. 'id.', MDu. eecoren m. 'id.', Du. eekhoren c. 'id.', OHG eihhurno, eihhorno m. 'id.', G Eichhornchen n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂ei-h₂u-r (IE) - NP varvarah 'squirrel' < \*h₂uer-h₂uer-o-; Lat. vfverra f. 'ferret' < \*h₂ui-h₂uer-neh₂-; Olr. faru f. 'squirrel', Ir. iora 'id.' < \*h₂i-h₂uer-(?); Gael. feorag, W gwywer 'id.' < \*h₂ui-h₂uer-; Lith. vovere, v6vere, Latv. vavere 'id.' < \*h₂ue-h₂uer-ieh₂-; Lith. vaiveris, Ru. veverica 'id.' < \*h₂uoi-h₂uer-io- etc. A reduplicated formation comparable to \*bebura- 1 'beaver' (q.v.) < \*bhe-bhr, gen. \*bhi-bhr-6s (Beekes 1995: 190). The Germanic word (together with Ir. iora?) preserved the simplest type of reduplication, \*h₂ei-h₂ur, gen.

<!-- pdf-page: 049; section: dictionary -->

\*h₂i-h₂ur-6s, which resulted in a paradigm \*aikwur, \*lkuraz (for the change \*-aiHu- > PGm. \*-aikw-, cf. \*taikwer-). After the loss of initial laryngeals, the daughter languages restored the reduplication in several different ways, giving rise to a plethora of stem variants.

\*ailida- m. 'fire' - ON e/dr m. 'id.', Far. e/dur m. 'id.', Elfd. jiild m. 'id.', OE ii!led m. 'fire; fire blight' (DRY). A formation derived from \*ailjan- (OE re/an w.v. 'to burn') which in turn is a factitive to the noun \*aila- as implied by OE al n. 'fire' < \*ai/la- < \*aid/a- < \*h₂eidh-/o-, for which cf. \*aida-. W aelwyd f. 'fireplace' and Bret. oelad 'id.' were borrowed from early Anglo-Saxon.

\*aima- m. 'steam, smoke' - ON eimr m. 'fire, smoke, steam', Far. eimur m. 'embers', Nw. eim m. 'vapor, smoke, trace, semblance, inkling' ⇒ \*h₁oi-mo- (1E). A mo-stem whose meaning makes it tempting to reconcile it with the root \*h₂ei- 'to burn' (see \*aida-, \*aiza-), which would imply a proto-form \*h₂oi-mo-. However, ON, Icel. fm n. 'dust, dirt', Far. fm n. 'soot', Nw. im n. 'thin layer (e.g. of dust, flower or soot)' < \*ima- rather implies a root \*h₁eiwith \*h₁. See also \*aima-uzjon-.

\*aima-uzjon- f. 'embers' - ON eimyrja f. 'id.', OE remyrie f. 'id.', E embers, MDu. amere f. 'id.', OHG eimuria f. 'id.', MHG eimer(e) f. 'id.' (DRY). A compound of \*aima- 'steam, smoke' and \*usjon-, cf. ON ysja f. 'fire' < \*Hus-ieh₂-, See also \*uznan-.

\*aina- num. 'one' - Go. ains num. 'id.', ON einn num. 'id.', Far. ein num. 'id.', Elfd. ien num. 'id.', OE iin num. 'id.', E one, OFri. an, en num. 'id.', OS en num. 'id.', Du. een num. 'id.', OHG ein num. 'id.', G ein num. 'id.' ⇒ \*Hoi-Hn-o- (IE) - Identical to Lat. unus, Olr. 6en, W un; OPru. ainan acc., Lith. vfenas, Latv. viens num. 'id.'; also cf. Gr. oivi] f. 'one (on a dice)' < \*Hoi-Hn-eh₂-; OCS in'b 'some(one), other', Ru. in6j 'different, other' < \*Hi-Hn-o-; Skt. eka- 'one' < \*Hoi-ko-; Av. aeuua- 'id.', OP aiva- 'id.' < \*Hoi-uo-. A West Indo-European numeral derived from an IE root \*Hoi-, for which cf. \*aiwa/o- 1.

\*ainahan- adj. 'single' - Go. ainaha adj. 'id.', ON einga indecl. 'id.', OE anga, renga, enga adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*Hoi-Hno-Ko- (DRY) - Formally close to Lat. unicus adj. 'single'. Derived from \*aina- (q.v.).

\*ainalifa- num. 'eleven' - Go. ainlif num. 'id.', ON ellifu num. 'id.', Far. ellivu num. 'id.', Elfd. el/av num. 'id.', OE endlufon num. 'id.', E eleven, OFri. and/ova,

<!-- pdf-page: 050; section: dictionary -->

al/ewa, a Iva, el/eva, el/eve num. 'id.', OS el/eban num. 'id.', Du. elf num. 'id.', OHG einlef num. 'id.', G elfnum. 'id.' ::} \*Hoi-Hno-lipo- (NEUR) - Close to Lith. vienui5-lika num. 'eleven'. In both Germanic and Baltic, the words for 'eleven' and 'twelve' were compounds meaning "one past ten" and "two past ten". This way of counting seems to pre-date the arrival of the Indo-Europeans in Northern Europe, and may be a relic from a lost language that was spoken in this area. Note that the two branches use different roots for the element "left", which proves that the two formations were created independently. See also \*twalifa- and \*llban-.

\*ainja- m. 'juniper' - ON einir m. 'id.', Far. eini n. 'id.' (also cf. eini-ber 'juniper berry'), Nw. eine m. 'id.', Sw. en(e) c. 'id.', Elfd. ien m., iene n. 'id.', Da. ene-brer 'id.', LG en(e)ke 'id.', G dial. Einbeerbaum 'id.' ::} \*h₁oi-n-io- (IE) - Hitt. ejan- n. 'a certain evergreen tree' < \*h₁ei-n-; Lat. iilni-pe/irus m. 'juniper' < \*h₁oi-no- (with unexplained j- and etymologically obscure second member). A word that is likely to have been a collective formation, especially in view of the neuters Far. eini and Elfd. iene. The word is usually reconstructed as PGm. \*jainja- on the basis of the comparison with Lat. iiini-perus, but the West Germanic forms exclude initial \*j. If Hitt. ejan- is related (Kroonen 2011a: 32), we may reconstruct a static n-stem \*h₁6i-n, gen. \*h₁ei-n-s.

\*airi adv. 'early' - ON cir adv. 'id.', Far. (margin ciri 'early in the morning', OE <ir adv. 'before', OFri. er prep., conj. 'id.', OS er adv. 'id.', Du. eer conj. 'id.', OHG e(r) adv., conj. 'before, earlier', MHG e(r) adv. 'earlier, sooner' ::}

\*h₂ei-er-i (IE) - Av. aiiara, gen. aiiqn n. 'day' < \*h₂ei-r/n-; Gr. ƀpl 'in the morning' < \*h₂ei-er-i; Gr. Tjtplo<; adj. 'matutinal' < \*h₂eier-io-. PGm. \*airi is identical to Gr. ƀpl, continuing the locative of an old r/n-stem meaning 'day' (or 'daybreak'?), cf. Av. aiiara. The e of \*ajeri was regularly raised to \*i after \*j, and triggered the merger of \*aji- with \*ai- (cf. \*aiza- 'oar' < PIE \*h₂ei-es-). Note that ON, Icel. aria, Sw. aria, Da. cirle adv. 'early' developed from cirliga by some sort of syncope, cf.fastl(ig}a 'firmly', framarl(ig)a 'in a forward position', ill(ilig)a 'badly, hideously' and gerla, gprla 'fully', harola 'very', var/a 'hardly'.

\*airinon- w.v. 'to run an errand' - Go. airinon w.v. 'to be an emissary, to be a messenger', ON cirna w.v. 'to intercede, to pray'. (DRV). Derived from \*airu- 'messenger' (q.v.).

\*airiz adv. 'earlier' - Go. airis adv. 'id.', ON cior adv. 'before, earlier' ::} \*h₂ei-er-is (IE).

<!-- pdf-page: 051; section: dictionary -->

An adverbial comparative often derived from \*ārōr < \*air-þera- (see \*airi) with dissimilation of the first r after the monophthongization of \*ai to ā (thus Torp 1909: 3). Or should we rather assume dissimilation of \*āriR to \*āðiR (before the syncope of i, but after the monophthongization of \*ai to \*ā)? If so, the Old Norse form must originally have been identical to Go. airis < \*air-iz. For similar dissimilations, cf. Go. izwis obl.du. 'you' vs. ON yðr < PN \*iōðiR < \*iRwiR and Icel. hleið < \*hlōði < \*hluzan-, for which see \*hluza-. The traditional connection with \*edra- 'quick' seems less attractive.

\*airō- f. 'oar' - ON ár f. 'id.', Far. ár f. 'id.', Elfd. åra f. 'id.', OE ār f. 'id.', E oar ⇒ \*h₃eiH-r-eh₂- (IE) - Hitt. hišša- c. 'carriage pole' < \*h₃iH-so-; Gr. οἴᾱξ, -ᾱκος m. 'handle of the rudder, rudder' < \*h₃(e)iH-s-; Skt. īṣā- f. 'drawbar' < \*h₃iH-s-eh₂-; Sln. ojé n. 'thill' < \*h₃iH-es-; Lith. íena f. 'rod' < \*h₃eiH-n-eh₂-. No certain etymology. The meaning 'oar' makes connection with the verb \*rōan- 'to row' < \*h₁roh₁-e- seem attractive, but this is formally impossible. The word has more convincingly been compared to the PIE s-stem \*h₃eiH-s, \*h₃iH-es- as continued by the aforementioned forms. The Germanic word cannot directly be derived from this s-stem, however, as \*aizō- would give ON \*\*eir. It must therefore be reconstructed as \*HeiH-r-eh₂-. Could it together with Lith. íena have split off from an old heteroclitic noun \*h₃óiH-r, gen. \*h₃eiH-n-s?

\*airu- m. 'messenger' - Go. airus m. 'id.', ON árr m. 'messenger, servant', Icel. árr m. 'id.', OE ār m. 'id.', OS ēru m. 'id.' (GM). Also cf. Go. airinōn w.v. 'to be an emissary, to be a messenger', ON árna w.v. 'to intercede, to pray' < \*airinōn-. No further etymology.

\*aiskō- f. 'demand' - OFri. āske f. 'claim', OHG eisca f. 'question' ⇒ \*h₂ois-sk-eh₂- (IE/NEUR) - Latv. ieska f. 'desire' < \*h₂ois-sk-eh₂- (whence Lith. ieškóti 'to look for', Latv. ieskāt 'to look for lice'); Skt. iccháti 'to long for', Av. isaiti 'id.' < \*h₂is-sk-e-; Skt. icchā- f. 'wish, demand', Arm. aycʿ 'visit, inspection', OCS iska f. 'demand' < \*h₂is-sk-eh₂- (whence OCS iskati 'to search, seek', Ru. iskátʹ 'id.', SCr. iskati 'to wish' < \*h₂is-sk-eh₂-). An eh₂-stem derived from a PIE sk-present \*h₂is-sk-e-, cf. Skt. iccháti 'to long for', Av. isaiti 'id.'. In Germanic, it gave rise to OE āscian w.v. 'to question, interrogate', E to ask, OFri. āskia w.v. 'to investigate', MDu. eiscen, escen w.v. 'to demand', Du. eisen w.v. 'id.', OHG eiscōn w.v. 'to claim, demand' < \*aiskōjan-, which is derivationally parallel to Lith. ieškóti and Latv. ieskāt. Also cf. OE ǣsce f. 'question, search' < \*aiskjōn-.

\*aisō(ja)n- w.v. 'to rush' - ON eisa w.v. 'to rush, dash' ⇒ \*h₁ois-eh₂(-ie)- (IE) - Skt. iṣyáti 'to set in motion, send' < \*h₁is-ie-; Av. fraēšiiā 1sg.pres.act. 'to

<!-- pdf-page: 052; section: dictionary -->

urge' < \*h₁eis-ie-; Skt. eṣá- adj. 'quick' < \*h₁ois-o-; Lat. īra f. 'anger, rage' < \*h₁eis-eh₂-. An iterative or denominative verb without cognates in the WGm. languages.

\*aison- f. 'fireplace' - ON eisa f. 'embers', Nw. eise f. 'fire, smith's fire', MLG ese f. 'forge, fireplace' ⇒ \*h₂ois-eh₂- (IE?). Usually derived from \*h₂eidh-s- (cf. \*aida- 'pyre'), but \*h₂ei-s- would be more straightforward. Possibly related to \*aiza- 'ore' < \*h₂ei-es- ( q.v).

\*aista/6- m./f. 'kiln' - OE ast m. 'oven', E oast, MLG eiste f. 'oast house', MDu. eest, est m./f. 'drying kiln', Du. eest c. 'oast; kiln' ⇒ \*h₂ei-sth₂-o- (DRY). Possibly a compound of the root of \*aida- 'fire' with the locational suffix \*-sth₂-o- (for which cf. \*sten- 'to stand'). See also \*aison-.

\*aisten- w.v. 'to respect' - Go. aistan w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂eisd-eh₁-ie- (IE) - Skt. ftte 'to invoke, worship'; OAv. ifasoit 3sg.pres.opt. 'to implore' < \*Hisd-s-. A stative formation containing the root \*Heisdၹ. which is probably an extension of \*h₂eis- (see \*aizo-), perhaps with the suffixed root \*dh₃- ("to give respect"?). PIE \*h₂eisd- is well-attested in Indo-Iranian, but the appurtenance of Gr. aUioμm 'to respect' is uncertain given the fact that the expected outcome of PIE \*h₂eisd-e- would be \*\*a'(ͽoμm.

\*aistiin- n. 'testicle' - ON eista n. 'id.', Far. eista n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂oid-sth₂-o- (EUR) - OCS isto n. 'testicle', pl. 'kidneys' < \*h₂id-sth₂-os-; Lith. inkstas m. 'testicle, kidney' < \*h₂id-sth₂-o- (with nasal from [sCios 'womb, entrails, interior' = \*instra-); Alb. eshke 'kidney' < \*h₂oid-sk-eh₂-(?). A North Germanic word with some attractive *comparanda* in Balto-Slavic. The neuter n-stems were productive in Old Norse in words for body parts, which leaves the derivational base \*aist(a)-. This element probably consists of the root \*h₂eid- 'to swell' (cf. \*aita-) and a suffix \*-sth₂-o-.

\*aita- m. 'ulcer' - Du. coll. eit 'ear wax', OHG eiz m. 'abcess, boil', MHG eiz m. 'ulcer, boil' ⇒ \*h₂oid-o- (EUR) - Identical to OCS jado, Ru. jad 'poison', SCr. 'ijed m. 'gall, poison, anger' < \*h₂oid-o- (or \*h₁ed-o-?); further cf. Gr. olotw 'to swell' < \*h₂oid-eie- and Arm. aytnum, aor. ayteay 'to swell' < \*h₂eid-. Cf. the diminutive ON eitill, Far. eitil m. 'lymph node', OHG eiz(z}ala f. 'gallnut', MHG eizel n. 'boil' < \*aitila/6-. See also \*aitra- and \*aiston-.

\*aitra- n. 'poison; pus' - ON eitr n. 'venom, poison', Far. eitur n. 'id.', Elfd. ietter n. 'venom; pus', OE attor n. 'poison', WFri. atter, etter c. 'pus', OS ættar

<!-- pdf-page: 053; section: dictionary -->

n. 'poison', Du. etter n. 'pus', OHG eitar, eittar n. 'poison, pus', MHG eiter n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂oid-ro- (EUR) - Formally close to OCS jadra n.pl. 'embrace, bosom'. A formation derived from the PIE root \*h₂eid- 'to swell', cf. \*aita-. Also cf. Sw. obs., dial. etter adj. 'poisonous; purulent' < \*aitra-.

\*aiþa- m. 'oath' - Go. aiþs m. 'id.', ON eiðr m. 'id.', Far. eiður m. 'id.', OE āþ m. 'id.', E oath, OFri. āth, ēth m. 'id.', OS ēth m. 'id.', Du. eed c. 'id.', OHG eid m. 'id.', G Eid m. 'id.' ⇒ \*Hóito- (EUR) - Identical to OIr. oeth m. 'oath'. A Germanic-Celtic isogloss. Given the close formal and semantic agreement between PGm. \*aiþa- and PCelt. \*oito-, it is unlikely that the formation goes back to Proto-Indo-European, only to surface in two neighboring branches at the far end of the IE-speaking area. It is more probable that the word somehow arose in a shared cultural zone with similar legal traditions. Etymologically, the word is often derived from the root \*h₁ei- 'to go', cf. Sw. ed-gang "oath-walk". Unrelated to Hitt. hāi- 'to believe, to trust' (pace Puhvel 1984-: III 9-10), which is more commonly written hā- < \*h₂/₃eh₂/₃- (Kloekhorst 2008: 267). See also \*aiþja-.

\*aiþin- - \*aiþōn- f. 'mother' - Go. aiþei f. 'id.', ON eiða f. 'id.', OHG fuotar-eidi f. 'nanny', MHG eide f. 'mother' ⇒ \*ait-ih₂-(?) (GM). A nursery word, cf. Basq. aita 'father'. Hardly from \*aiþa- 'oath' (q.v.) in the sense of "legally acknowledged mother" (pace Mezger 1960: 68). The somewhat enigmatic form ON edda f. 'great-grandmother' could be a hypocoristic formation to eiða.

\*aiþja- adj. 'capable of taking an oath' - Go. uf-aiþeis\* adj. 'bound by an oath', OE āðe adj. 'qualified to swear an oath' (DRV). The meaning of the Old English form is clearly gerundival. Usually, gerundives are created to verbs, but in this particular case, the derivational base appears to have been \*aiþa- 'oath'.

\*aiþma- m. 'son-in-law' - OE āðum m. 'son-in-law; brother-in-law', OFri. āthum, āthom, āthem m. 'son-in-law, father-in-law', OHG eidum, eidam m. 'son-in-law', MHG eidem m. 'son-in-law, father-in-law' (GM). No certain etymology. The word is often linked to \*aiþa- 'oath', implying that the original meaning of the word was "son/father-in-oath". I personally prefer to derive the word from the same root as found in \*aiþin- - \*aiþōn-, i.e. with an original meaning "relative on the mother's side". Note that the word is usually reconstructed as \*aiþuman-, even though there is no compelling evidence for the u-vowel; Old High German, for instance, has both eidum and eidam, which means that the second vowel could be anaptyctic. The reconstruction of a suffix \*-uman- would furthermore make little sense,

<!-- pdf-page: 054; section: dictionary -->

because this is the comparative suffix \*-uman- < \*-T[lh₂-on-, cf. \*fruman-, \*ufuman- etc.

\*aiwa/o- 1 m./f. 'eternity, age' - Go. aiws m. 'id.', OFri. ewe f. 'id.', OS ewa f. 'id.', Du. eeuw c. 'century', OHG ewa f. 'eternity' ⇒ \*h₂ei-u- (IE) - Skt. &yu- n. 'life, lifetime', OAv. iiiiii, gen.yaos n. 'life, lifetime, time', Lat. aevus m., aevum n. 'period of time' < \*h₂ei-u-; Gr. alwv m./f. '(life)time, long time, eternity' < \*h₂ei-u-on- (cf. orig. Joe. aitv 'always'). The Latin and Germanic forms both go back to thematic \*h₂ei-uo-, but the word was originally inflected as an athematic u-stem, nom. \*h₂6i-u, gen. \*h₂i-eu-s (cf. the Avestan paradigm). I assume that this u-stem served as the basis for the verbal root \*Hieu-, as evinced by ToB yaw- 'to ripen, mature'. See also \*ajuki- 'eternal' and \*junga- 'young'.

\*aiwa/o- 2 m./f. 'law' - OE re, rew f. 'law, religion, marriage', OFri. ewe, e f. 'law', OS eo, eu m. 'law', OHG ewa, ea, eo f. 'law, right, will, contract', MHG ewe, e f. 'marriage, alliance' (IE). Probably related to Lat. jiis, jiiris n. 'law' < \* Hieu-es- and etymologically identical to \*aiwa/ii- 1 (q.v.). Hardly to Lat. aequus 'level, equal' < \*h₂eikwoor to Skt. eviis m.pl. 'conduct, habit, usage, custom' < \*h₁oi-uo-, which is derived from the root \*h₁ei- 'to go' (see \*aidja- and \*idiijan-).

\*aiwiska- adj. 'shameful' - MHG eisch adj. 'horrible' ⇒ \*h₂eigwh-isk-o- (IE) - To Skt. an-ehc1s- adj. 'flawless' < \*v-h₂eigwh-os-; unrelated to Gr. afoxoc; n. 'shame', which could be reconstructed as \*h₂eigwh-sk-es-, but was actually derived from a'(.Soμm 'to be ashamed' < \*h₂eid-e- (Lamberterie 1990: 83Sff.). Also cf. the derived Go. aiwiski n. 'shame, disgrace' < \*aiwiskja-, OE rewisc f. 'dishonour, disgrace, offence' < \*aiwiskii- and Go. aiwiskon, ga-aiwiskon w.v. 'to ill-treat, make ashamed' < \*aiwiskiijan-.

\*aiwjan- w.v. 'to graze' - ON æja w.v. (pret. aoa) 'to rest, eat', Du. eeuwen w.v. 'to feed, graze' (GM). Usually interpreted as a causative formation to the root \*h₂eKH- 'to eat', cf. Skt. iifayati 'to make eat' (for which cf. \*agana-), starting from a proto-form \*ahjan-. Still, the implied PIE causative \*h₂o/{H-eie- should have given PGm. \*agjan-, ON \*egja, which is not the case. The connection with Du. eeuwen (also cf. MDu. eeusel n. 'pasture') < \*aiwjan- (cf. De Vries 1962: 681) is therefore preferable.

\*aiza- n. 'ore, brass' - Go. ais, gen. aizis n. 'ore', ON eir n. 'brass, copper', Ice!. eir n. 'brass, copper', OE iir, rer n. 'ore, brass, copper', E ore, OS er n. 'ore', MDu. eer n. 'copper, metal', OHG er n. 'ore', MHG er n. 'ore, iron' ⇒

<!-- pdf-page: 055; section: dictionary -->

\*h₂ei-es- (IE) - Skt. ayas-, Av. aiiah- n. 'metal, copper', Lat. aes, aeris n. 'ore' < \*h₂ei-es-. A thematicized s-stem. The Germanic form developed from \*aizathrough \*ajez- < \*h₂ei-es- with regular loss of the medial e after j (cf. \*airi 'early' and \*laiza- 'clay'). The original PIE s-stem \*h₂ei-os, \*h₂(e)i-es-os may have been derived from the root \*h₂ei- 'to burn', cf. \*aida- and \*aison-. Also cf. OE reren, OHG erfn, G ehern adj. 'iron, brazen' < \*aizfna-.

\*aizo- f. 'respect, benevolence' - ON eir f. 'mercy', OE ii.r f. 'honor, dignity; kindness, mercy', OFri. ere f. 'honor; tribute', Du. eer c. 'honor', OHG era f. 'id.', G Ehre f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂ois-eh₂- (IE). Also cf. ON eira w.v. 'to spare, forgive', Far. eira w.v. 'to save, spare; to husband', OE ii.rian w.v. 'to respect', OFri. aria w.v. 'id.', OHG eren, eron w.v. 'to honor, adore', G ehren w.v. 'to honor' < \*aizen-. Derived from the PIE root \*h₂eis-, which in extended form also occurs in \*aisten-.

\*ajja- n. 'egg' - Go. Crim. ada n.pl. 'id.', ON egg n. 'id.', Far. egg n. 'id.', Elfd. egg n. 'id.', OE ǣg n. 'id.', WFri. aai n. 'id.', OS ei n. 'id.', Du. ei n. 'id.', OHG ei n. 'egg', G Ei n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂6u-i6- (IE) - Identical to YAv. aem acc. 'id.', MP xii.y-ag 'id.', Arm. ju 'id.' (with unclear j-). Gr. ״6v n. 'id.', Lat. ovum n. 'id.', OW ui, MW wy M. 'id.', OCS aice, Ru.jajc6, SCr.jajce n. 'id.' ( < PSlav. \*aj-bce). A neuter (iJo-stem directly continuing the PIE word for 'egg'. The word is generally taken to have come about as a vṛddhi-formation \*h₂6u-i6- to PIE \*h₂eu-is, gen. \*h₂u-ei-s 'bird', cf. Skt. vis, gen. ves, Lat. avis. Less attractive is the reconstruction as \*6-h₂u-io- with the marginally attested preposition \*o 'toward' (Schindler 1969). I consider the word to be crucial to establishing the relative chronology as well as the phonetic conditioning of several important Germanic sound laws. PGm. \*ajja- has been derived from the Siever's variant \*oijo- by Jasanoff 1978b: 85 under the assumption that Osthoff's law gave rise to an intermediate form \*aija- (Zair 2011: 292). In my view, PGm. \*ajja- rather developed from \*oui6- by 1) regular loss of the labial glide after \*o; 2) pretonic shortening of \*o to \*o (Dybo's law); and 3) gemination of \*-j- to \*-li- (Holtzmann's law). It follows from this chronology that Holtzmann's law was triggered by the following accent (thus already Kluge 1879: 128), and not by the assimilation of an adjacent laryngeal (Smith 1941). See also \*wfa- '(fly) egg'. •ajuki- adj. 'eternal' - Go. ajuk-dups f. 'eternity', OE rece adj. 'eternal' ⇒ \*h₂oi-u-gwih₃- (IE). An old derivation from \*aiwa/o- 1 'age' (q.v.) with a different realization of the PIE \*u. The origin of the PGm. k-suffix is uncertain, but with Lat. iugis adj. 'constant' and Av. yauuae-jf- 'living forever' < PIE \*h₂ieu-gwih₃-, it is at-

<!-- pdf-page: 056; section: dictionary -->

tractive to assume that PGm. \*ajuki- continues \*h₂oiu-gwih₃- ( < \*-gwh₃-i-) "everliving" (Weiss 1995).

\*akan- 1 s.v. 'to drive' - ON aka s.v. 'id.', Far. aka s.v. 'to drive; to push, shove', Elfd. aka s.v. 'to drive' ⇒ \*h₂eg-e- (IE) - ToAB cik- 'to lead', Skt. ajati 'to drive', YAv. azaiti 'id.', Arm. acem 'id.', Gr. ayw 'id.', Lat. ago 'id.', Olr. aigid 'id.'. Also cf. \*akra- 1 'field'.

\*akan- 2 s.v. 'to ache' - OE acan s.v. 'to ache', E to ache ⇒ \*h₂13eg-e- (IE) - Skt ciji- m./f. 'race, competition, fight', YAv. cizi- m. 'greed, desire', NP ciz 'greed' < \*h₂;3og-i-. A strong verb, possibly related to YAv. cizi- 'greed' < \*h₂13eg-i-, which can be formally identical with OE ece m. 'pain' < \*aki-. No further cognates. Is the verb originally identical to \*akan- 1, cf. Nw. dial. aka med 'to irritate'?

\*akra- 1 m. 'field' - Go. akrs m. 'id.', ON akr m. 'id.', Far. akur m. 'id.', Elfd. aker m. 'uncultivated field', OE cecer m. 'field', OFri. ekker, eker m. 'id.', OS akkar m. 'id.', Du. akker c. 'id.', OHG ackar m. 'id.', G Acker m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂eg-ro(IE) - Skt ajra- m. 'field', Arm. art m. 'id.', Gr. aypoc; m. 'id.', Lat. ager m. 'id.'. A PIE ro-stem to the root of \*akan- 'to drive'.

\*akra- 2 n.(?) 'some kind of metal' - EDu. aecker 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂erg-ro-(?) (IE?) - Skt [jrci- adj. 'shining reddishly, bright-colored', Gr. apy6c; adj. 'shining white' < \*h₂rg-ro-; Skt. rajata- n. 'silver' < \*h₂reg-nt-o-; YAv. arazata- n. 'id.', OP ardt- n. 'id.', Oss. cerzcet 'ore', Arm. arcat' 'silver', Lat. argentum n. 'id.', Olr. argat n. 'id.', OW argant, MW ariant 'id.' < \*h₂rg-nt-o-. The word aecker is attested only in Early Modern Dutch in a list of metal names (Doorman 1940: 106): Alderhande Cooper, Aecker, Iser, Stael, Blick, Messinck, ende diergelycke harde ende weecke meta/en 'All sorts of copper, aecker, iron, steel, sheet metal, brass, and similar hard and soft metals'. Could the word, similar to Gr. apy6c;, have been dissimilated from \*arkra- < \*h₂erg-ro- 'shining', an adjective related to the word for the PIE word for 'silver'? Or is the word rather identical to MDu. aker, eker '(metal) bucket', an old loan from Lat. aquarium?

\*akrana- n. 'tree fruit, acorn' - Go. akran n. 'fruit', ON akarn n. 'acorn', OSw. akarn n. 'id.', OE cecern n. 'id.', E acorn, MDu. aker m. 'id.', MHG ackeran m./n. 'acorn, beechnut', G Eckern n. 'acorn' (with intrusive umlaut from the plural) ⇒ \*ag-r (EUR/NIE?) - Olr. ciirne m. 'sloe, fruit of the blackthorn, kernel', MW eirin pl. 'sloes, berries, plums' < PCelt. \*agrn-io-; W aeron coll. 'fruits, berries' < PC!. \*agr-on-ci; Lith. uoga 'berry' < \*h₂og-eh₂-; OCS agoda

<!-- pdf-page: 057; section: dictionary -->

'fruit', Ru. j<igoda 'berry', SCr. jilgoda 'wild strawberry, berry'; ToAB oko n. 'fruit, effect'. A clear Europeanism. The Germanic word appears to contain the element •-ana- that can be retrieved from the semantically comparable ON a/din, Far. poet. aldan, OSw. a/don n. 'tree-fruit, mast' < \*aldana-, and more generally from the Swedish berry-suffix -on, cf. OSw. hiupon 'rose hip' (see \*heupa/on-) and smultron n. 'wild strawberry' (with -on- < \*-anu < \*-on-eh₂as in W aeron 'fruits, berries'?). This leaves a more primary base \*agr-, which happens to be especially close to the Celtic forms, but which nevertheless remains derivationally obscure. Note that the appurtenance of ToB oko (see Adams 1999: 109-110) is uncertain, as it may have been derived from the PIE root \*h₂eug- 'to grow'.

\*alcwesi- f. 'axe' - Go. aqizi f. 'id.', ON !i1X f. 'id.', Far. fiJks f. 'id.', Elfd. okse f. 'id.', OE rex f. 'id.', E axe, OFri. axe f. 'id.', OS akus f. 'id.', Du. aks c. 'id.', OHG ackus f. 'id.', G Axt f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂egwis-ih₂-(?) (NIE?) - Gr. aɌ[VT] f. 'axe' < \*h₂egws-i-; Lat. ascia f. 'axe, trowel' < \*h₂esk-ieh₂-(?). The formal incongruences between Germanic, Latin and Greek are suggestive of a non-Indo-European origin (cf. Bjorvand/Lindeman 2000: 1085). If this is correct, there is room for the comparison with Akkadian /)a$$inu 'axe' and Aram. /)a$$inci 'id.'. Note that the meaning of the occasionally adduced Myc. a-qi-ja is uncertain.

\*alan- s.v. 'to grow up; to rear' - Go. a/an s.v. 'to grow on, feed on', ON ala s.v. 'to give birth to, nourish, support' ⇒ \*h₂el-e- (EUR) - Identical to Lat. a/ere 'to nourish', Olr. alid 'to feed', W alu 'to bear young', Bret halaff 'to bear young, calve'. A strong verb to the PIE root \*h₂el-. See also \*alda-, \*aldi-, \*aljan- and \*aldra-.

\*ala(n)- m. 'awl' - ON air m. 'id.' ( < \*ala-), Nw. ale m. 'thin stick to dry meat on' ( < \*alan-) ⇒ \*h₁ohrl6-(?) (IE). Generally assumed to be related to \*elo- with the same meaning, but the ablaut is problematic. Vocalic differences between two semantically similar words usually point to an ablauting paradigm in the parent language, but it is unclear how this could explain the alternation \*a - \*e in this particular case. One possibility would be to reconstruct PIE \*h₁eh₁/-eh₂, gen. \*h₁f;i1l-h₂-6s > PGm. \*elo, \*alaz with regular(?) vocalization of the laryngeal between two stops, cf. \*ebanfl- - \*ebund- 'evening'. Alternatively, one could reconstruct \*ala- as an independent formation \*h₁oh₁/-6-, assuming that the resulting form \*o/6- developed into \*ala- by pretonic shortening. See also \*alasno-.

<!-- pdf-page: 058; section: dictionary -->

\*alasno- f. 'awl' - MLG alse, e/se(ne) 'id.', MDu. alsene, e/sen(e}, e/s{e) 'id.', Du. els c. 'id.', OHG a/ansa, alunsa f. 'id.' (DRV). A formation apparently created to \*ala- with the suffix \*-asno-, cf. Go. arfuazna 'arrow' and h/aiwazna 'grave'. The Middle Germanic forms with e-vocalism are sometimes assumed to point to a variant \*a/isno-, but the derivationally less problematic \*a/asnjo- would work, too.

\*albi- f. 'river' - ON e/fr f. 'id.', Elfd. ove f. 'id.', MLG elve 'riverbed' (GM). Likely to be related to the name of the river Elbe.

\*a/but- f. 'swan' - ON plpt, gen. alptar, elptr f. 'id.', Ice!. a/ft f. 'id.', OE ielfetu f. 'id.', E obs. elk(?) 'swan, goose', MHG a/biz, e/biz f. 'swan' ⇒ \*a/bhVd- (NIE) - Close to Ru. /ebed' (< PSlav. \*elbedb-) < \*h₁e/bhedh-i-, SCr. lilbrid (< \*olbpdb) < \*h₁0/bhond-i-, arch. lebrit ( < \*elbptb) m. 'swan'. The Germanic form can be reconstructed as \*a/but- with the suffix for animals that is also found in \*heruta- 'deer' and \*hurnuta-/o- 'hornet', and is often connected to the PIE adjective \*h₂e/bh-o- 'white', cf. Lat a/bus. The Slavic forms, on the other hand, seem to preclude that the word is an intra-Germanic coinage. They point to a set of conspicuously close, but phonetically irregular proto-forms, e.g. \*h₁e/bhedh-, \*h₁0/bhond/t-. It has been argued, on the basis of these formal difficulties, that the word is of non-IE origin (Derksen 1999). This is corroborated by the possibility that the Slavic forms contain the suffix \*-VdCh₃_ that is also found in other substrate words (cf. \*arwit- 'pea') and by the fact that the Germanic word is inflected as a root noun. Also note the utterly irregular relation with SCr. kilp m. 'swan' < \*kip- and Lith. gulbis f. 'id.', SCr. dial. gflb m. 'id.' < \*gChl/bh-. There seems to be no connection, on the other hand, with Lat. olor m. 'swan' < \*h₁el-or; Olr. e/u f. 'id.' < \*h₁el-ieh₂- and W a/arch, pl. e/eirch 'swan' < \*h₁el-r-sko-.

\*alda- adj. '(grown) old' - Go. alþeis adj. 'old' ( < \*alþja-), ON ellri comp. 'id.', OE eald adj. 'id.', E old, OFri. aid, auld adj. 'id.', OS aid adj. 'id.', Du. oud adj. 'id.', OHG alt adj. 'id.', G alt adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂el-tó- (DRV) - Lat. altus adj. 'high', Olr. alt m. 'height, cliff < \*h₂el-to-; W allt f. 'hill' < \*h₂e/-teh₂-. An adjective continuing the original participle of the verb \*a/an- 'to grow; to bring up' (q.v.). It gave rise to ON elli, OE ild(e), ildu, OS eldi, OHG alti, elti f. '(old) age' < \*alþīn-. See also \*a/di- and \*aldra-.

\*aldi- f. 'age' - Go. alps f. 'generation, age', ON pld f. 'age', pl. 'mankind' ⇒ \*h₂e/-tf- (DRV). A ti-stem derived from the strong verb \*a/an- ( q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 059; section: dictionary -->

\*aldo(n)- f. 'hollow (of a tree)' - ON alda f. 'wave, roller', Icel. alda f. 'wave, small land ridge', Far. alda f. 'wave', Nw. aide f. 'wave', dial. aide, olde 'billow, rivulet, well, (wooden) trough', Sw. dial. alla 'furrow, vessel', OE aldaht, a/dot 'trough(?), channel(?)', G Bav. a/den 'furrow' ⇒ \*Ho/dh-eh₂- (NEUR) - Lith. eldija, aldija f. 'hollowed out tree trunk, canoe', OCS /adii, al[o)dii, al[b)dija, Ru. ladja 'boat', dial. 16dj<i 'boat, trough', SCr. dial. liidja f. '(river) ship' < \*Ho/dh-ih₂-, gen. \*-ieh₂-s. An Indo-European word for 'boat'.

\*aldra- n. 'age, life( span)' - Go. fram-aldrs\* adj. 'aged', ON aldr m. 'id.', OE aldor n. 'life; age', OS aldar n. 'id.', MDu. ouder n. 'id.', OHG altar, aldar n. 'id.', G Alter n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂el-tro- (DRV) - Olr. com-altar m. 'joint fosterage', ml-altar m. 'bad fosterage'. An instrumental formation to the verb \*a/an- (q.v.).

\*algi- 1 m. 'elk' - ON elgr m. 'id.', Far. elgur m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁ol-lt-i- (IE) - Identical to ORu. losb, Ru. los', Pol. lo§ m. 'elk' < \*h₁ol-l<-i[o)-; further cf. Skt. fsyam. 'male antelope' < \*h₁l-l<-io-. Together with Sw. dial. elgja, ilgja f. 'elk cow' < \*algji5n- the word is derived from the IE word for 'deer', cf. Gr. £A.mpo<; 'deer' < \*h₁el-o-bho-. The suffix \*-I<- is reminiscent of the \*-/{- of \*h₂rt-1<0- 'bear', cf. Hitt. bartakka-, Skt. fk$a-, Gr. apKTO<;, Lat. ursus, Olr. art, W arth 'bear, warrior'. Cf. \*elha[n)-.

\*algi· 2 m. 'slush' - Icel. elgur m. 'slush, melting snow' (GM?). The word has been connected to Lat. a Igor m. 'cold', which may reflect an s-stem \*h₂elg<hl-os- (Liden 1897: 66), but in spite of the fact that the PGm. form \*algi-, too, can theoretically have evolved from an s-stem \*algiz- (cf. \*hwali- 'whale'), it is more likely to be related to Far. elgja w.v. 'to pour (down the throat)' < \*algjan-. Also note that the identification of Ice!. elgur with ON elgr\*, as found in the kennings alpj6iJ elgjar ga/ga and ceoiveors elgjar, is uncertain, as it probably means 'elk' (see \*algi- 2) in these contexts (cf. De Vries 1962: 100; Heide 2001). Related to ODu. alk- top. 'mud'?

\*algjan- w.v. 'to be nauseated' - Icel. elgja w.v. 'id.', Nw. elgja w.v. 'id.' (GM). A verb reminiscent of \*walk/gon- (q.v.). Also cf. WFri. algje w.v. 'to be disgusted' < \*algi5jan-.

\*algojan- w.v. 'to protect' - OE ealgian, algian w.v. 'to defend' ⇒ \*h₂elk-eh₂-ie-? (IE) - Skt. r6.k$ati 'to protect, guard, deal'; Gr. aA.E:Ɍw 'to ward off, defend' < \*h₂lek-se-. The Germanic verb displays Schwebeablaut, or, more properly, the enlarged stem \*h₂lek-se- shifted the ablaut slot one place to the right after the addition of the suffix (cf. \*aukan- vs. \*wahsan-).

<!-- pdf-page: 060; section: dictionary -->

\*alh- m. 'temple' - Go. a/hs, dat. a/h m. 'id.', OE ealh m. 'id.' ⇒ \*a/k- (NIE) - Lith. aTkas m. '(holy) grove on a hill', Latv. ၻ/ks m. 'idol' (with a > e by Rozwadowski's change). A Baltic-Germanic word with no clear IE cognates. The link with \*h₂/ek'to ward off, defend' (cf. \*algojan-) mentioned by Pokorny IEW: 32 is arbitrary. Given the root noun inflection, it is conceivable that the word was adopted from a local non-IE language by Germanic and Baltic after their arrival in Europe.

\*alino- f. 'forearm; cubit' - Go. aleina f. 'cubit, ell', ON pin f. 'forearm; cubit', Far. a/in f. 'ell', Elfd. aln f. 'id.', OE eln f. 'id.', E ell, MDu. elne, el/en, elle f. 'forearm; cubit', Du. el c. 'cubit', OHG elena, elna f. 'id.', G Elle f. 'id.' ⇒ \*Heh₃/-en-eh₂- (IE) - Gr. WAEVT\ f. 'elbow, underarm', Lat. ulna f. 'forearm' < \*Heh₃/-en-eh₂-; Gr. Hsch. wAA.6v 'elbow' < \*Heh₃/-n-o-?; Gr. wA.fiv f. 'elbow', Olr. uilen 'angle', W elin, Bret. ilin 'id.' < \*Heh₃/-ࢯn-. The Germanic word together with Lat. ulna goes back to PIE \*Heh₃/-en-eh₂- through an intermediate form \*olena- with pretonic shortening. The suffix was later substituted by \*-fno-. In PIE, the word was probably inflected as an amphidynamic n-stem, nom. \*Heh₃/-on, gen. \*Hh₃/-n-6s, acc. \*Hh₃/-en-m. Note that the latter case also may have given PGm. \*a/en- by vocalization of the second laryngeal.

\*aliskon- w.v. 'to tend to(?)' - ON elska w.v. 'to love', Far. a/ska, elska w.v. 'id.' (GM). A derivative of \*a/an- 'to grow; to rear', cf. Da. op-elske 'to grow (e.g. plants), to raise (cattle)' (Magnusson 1989: 152). Definitely unrelated to ON elja f. 'concubine' < \*aljon- and \*lustu- 'desire' (q.v.).

\*alis/z- m. 'alder' - ON p/r, air m. 'id.', Nw. or, older m. 'id.', OE a/or, a/er m. 'id.', E alder, MDu. else m. 'id.', Du. els c. 'id.', OHG erila, elira f. 'id.', G Erle f. 'id.' ⇒ \*a/is- (EUR) - Lat a/nus f. 'alder' < \*h₂els-no-; Lith. alksna, Latv. alksna f. 'alder thicket; marsh' < \*h₂els-n-eh₂-; Ru. ol'xa, SCr. dial. je/ha f. 'alder' < \*h₂elis-eh₂-; Cz. o/Se, dial. je/5e, SCr. jelfo f. 'id.' < \*h₂elis-ieh₂- (both formations with unclear variation of PSI. a- and e-). The Slavic forms all have a suffix \*-is-, which is parallel to MDu. else < \*alisan- and OHG elira < \*a/izo(n)-. This probably means that the suffix of ON plr and OE a/or < \*a/uz- has a secondary vowel, perhaps following an early remodeling of the word into an s-stem. The s-stem inflection is potentially also supported by the Verner alternation of Du. els and G Erle, but it is not impossible either that the word was originally inflected as a root noun. No certain IE etymology: perhaps a European substrate word (cf. Schrijver 1991: 40; De Vaan 2008: 34-5). Also cf. Ice!. elri n. 'id.' < \*a/uzja-.

<!-- pdf-page: 061; section: dictionary -->

\*alja- pron. 'someone else' - Go. aljis\* pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂el-io- (IE) - Identical to Arm. ayl adj. 'other', Gr. ἄλλος adj. 'id.', Lat. alius 'id.', OIr. aile, W eil pron. 'id.'. 
\*aljan- w.v. 'to make grow, rear' - Go. aljan\* w.v. 'to make grow, fatten', Nw. dial. elja s./w.v. 'to breed, rear' ⇒ \*h₂o/-eie- (ORV). The causative to \*a/an- (q.v.). The strong forms occurring in Nw. elja, pret. ol/alde seem to be secondary, though a strong present \*aljan- beside \*a/an- would be reminiscent of e.g. veksa 'to grow' < \*wahsjan- vs. vaksa 'id.' < •wahsan-. It remains too small a basis for the reconstruction of a byform \*h₂el-ie-, however.

\*aljana- n. 'zeal' - ON elj'an n./f. 'endurance, energy', OE el/en n. 'courage', OS el/ean n. 'courage, power', OHG ellian, el/an n. 'courage', MHG el/en n. 'zeal, courage' (IE) - Hitt. balai - balijanzi 'to set in motion' < \*h₂l-6i-ei, \*h₂/-i-enti; Gr. laMw 'to send off, to stretch forth' < \*h₂i-h₂l-ie-. A formation created with the neuter •-ana-suffix (cf. \*akrana-), perhaps to an unattested verb \*aljan-. In view of the original meaning 'zeal', I assume a connection with the aforementioned Hittite and Greek forms, but others have compared e.g. Lat. a/acer adj. 'lively'. Also cf. ON e/ta w.v. 'to pursue' < \*a/-atjan-.

\*alkon- f. 'auk' - ON alka f. 'razor-bill', Far. ci/ka f. 'id.', OE ea/ce f. 'auk', E auk (EUR?) - To Lat. olor m. 'swan' < \*h₁el-or-; Olr. elu 'id.' < \*h₁el-on-; W a/arch 'id.' < \*h₁el-r-sk-o-? A formation apparently derived from the root \*al{/)- as continued by Sw. al-/6.gel 'long-tailed duck', dial. al m. 'id.' < \*ala- and Ice!. haf-el/a, hciv-ella, Far. 6gv-e/la f. 'id.' < \*a/ljon- (Torp 1909: 22; Magnusson 1989: 11 ). For the k-suffix, see \*balika/on- 'coot' and \*habuka- 'hawk'. If E obs. elk 'swan' belongs here, too, the connection with Lat o/or 'swan' gains some credibility. Modern E auk can be analyzed as a Norse loan.

\*alla- adj. 'all' - Go. alls adj. 'all, every', ON al/r adj. 'all, entire, whole', OE ea/I adj. 'all', OFri. al, of adj. 'all', OHG al{/) adj. 'all, every, complete'

\*h₂el-n6- (EUR) - Osc. allo adj.f. 'whole' < \*h₂el-neh₂-; Olr. ui/e adj. 'all', Lith. aliaiadv. 'completely' (with the adv. suffix -ai] < \*h₂0/-io-. The double I in Gm. developed out of the cluster -In-, cf. \*Julia- 'full'. The originally suffixless form still occurs as \*ala-, cf. Go. ala-, ON al-, OE ;;el-, OS, OHG ala- in compounds, cf. Go. ala-brunst'OA.6KaucrTov'. •a/up- n. 'ale, beer' - ON þI n. 'beer', OE ea/op n. 'ale', OS alo-fat n. 'ale-vat', MDu. ale, ael n. 'id.', MHG al-schaf n. 'ale vessel' ⇒ \*h₂el-u-(?) (GM).

<!-- pdf-page: 062; section: dictionary -->

No generally accepted etymology for this word, which was borrowed from Germanic as e.g. Lith. a/us, Fi. olut and Oss. reluton / ilreton. Several suggestions: 1) to Lat. aliimen 'alum' starting from an original meaning "bitter drink" (cf. Pokorny IEW: 33-4); 2) to Hitt. alwanza- 'magic' (Polome 1996); 3) to Skt. aruШa- 'reddish', Av. auru5a- 'bright, white' (Bjorvand 2007).

\*amaitjo· f. 'ant' - OE kmette f. 'id.', E ant, MDu. amete, emete, eemt f. 'id.', OHG ameiza f. 'id.', G Ameise f. 'id.' (DRY?). Origin unclear. The word is usually assumed to be a compound with the root of \*maitan- 'to cut', but the form and origin of the first element is difficult to establish. One could reconstruct \*uz-maitjo- "out-cutter" in which unstressed \*uz would give PWGm. \*a. I personally find it more attractive to connect \*emon- 2 'larva'. The word may then be a haplologized compound \*emo-maitjo- "larva cutter". A third option is to assume a substrate word connected with Gr. μtoa<; 'destructive insect in beans'. The Germanic word must then be analyzed as having an a-prefix (cf. \*arut- 'ore') combined with a variant of the it-suffix that in Greek appears as -LO-, -Lfl-, -Lvfl- (cf. \*arwit'pea' ' Gr. £pi:,Lvflo<;), ie. \*a-m-id-. Uncertain.

\*amazan· m. 'bunting' - OE omer, amore 'scorellus (unidentified bird)', E yellow-hammer, OHG amoro m. 'bunting', MHG amer, amoro m. 'bunting' (GM). No extra-Gm. cognates. Formally compatible with \*ams/On- 'blackbird' (q.v.).

\*ambahta- m. 'servant' - Go. andbahts m. 'servant, minister', MDu. ambacht m. 'servant', OHG ambaht m. 'servant, employee, official', MHG ambet, amt m. 'servant, caretaker' (Lw). A loanword from Celtic, cf. Gaul. ambactus 'vassal', W amaeth m. 'servant' < \*h₂mbhi- 'around' (cf. \*umbl) + \*h₂eg-to- 'goer' (see \*akan- 1). Unlike PCelt. \*rig- 'king' (see \*rik-), the word entered Germanic after the great sound shifts. Also cf. ON amb<itt f. 'bondwoman; concubine' < \*ambahto-.

\*amban- m. 'belly' - OS ambon pl. 'id.', OHG amban 'id.' ⇒ \*h₃embh-on- (IE). An n-stem in Schwebeablaut relation to PIE \*h₃n6bh-s, gen. \*h₁mbh-es (for which see \*nabo- and \*nab/an-). The OHG na-stem results from secondary thematization, cf. Lat. umbo 'boss (of a shield); protuberance' < \*h₁embh-on-.

\*am(m)on- w.v. 'to irritate' - ON ama w.v. 'to vex, molest', Ice!. ama w.v. 'to trouble, vex', Far. ama w.v. 'to spend', Nw. ama, amma w.v. 'to chafe, grate; incite' ⇒ \*h₃emh₃-neh₂- (IE) - Skt. amiti 'to take hold of, enforce' < \*hЩmh₃-e-; Skt amayati 'to hurt' < \*h₃omh₁-eie-; Skt. amiva- f. 'suffering,

<!-- pdf-page: 063; section: dictionary -->

disease' < \*h₃emh₃-ueh₂-; Lith. iimyti 'to push' < \*h₃mh₃-ie-(?); Gr. oμvuμL, -uμm 'to swear' < \*h₃m-nehr (with \*-neu- replacing \*-neh₃-). The alternation of \*amon- and \*ammon- (Nw. amma) could point to an iterative 3sg. \*amopi, 3pl. \*ammumanpi < \*h₃emh₃-neh₂-ti, \*h₃emh₃-nh₂-enti.

\*ampra- adj. 'sharp, sour' - ON apr adj. 'hard, sharp; sad', Nw. amper adj. 'bitter' ( « LG), MDu. amper adj. 'sour, bitter, harsh', Du. amper adv. 'barely' ⇒ \*Hom-ro- (IE) - Skt. am/a-, ambla- adj. 'bitter' < \*Hom-ro-. A Germanic-Indic isogloss. The further connection with Lat. amarus 'bitter' is uncertain, because its long a cannot be explained from the proto-form underlying PGm. \*ampra- < \*Hom-ro- (cf. De Vaan 2008: 37). In view of Hehr. mar 'bitter' (cf. Lewis-Short), the word may be of Semitic origin. The connection with Skt. ama-, Gr. wμ6c; adj. 'raw' < \*h₁oh₁m-o- (for which see \*emon 1) as given by Pokorny JEW: 777-778 is uncertain. See also \*ampra/on-.

\*ampra/on- m./f. 'sorrel, dock' - OE ampre, ompre f. 'sorrel; varicose vein', OHG ampfaro m. 'sorrel', G Ampfer m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂;3em-ro- (DRV) - Skt. am/a-, ambla- m. 'sorrel', Latv. amuo/s 'yellow-sorrel'. The Germanic word seems to have been derived from \*ampra- (q.v.). In Baltic, we find a parallel formation derived from the same root, perhaps an ablauting /-stem nom. \*h₂em-61, gen. \*h₂m-l-6s.

\*amsa- m. 'shoulder' - Go. ams\* m. 'id.', Nw. cis m. 'hill, ridge', Elfd. qs m. 'ridge' ⇒ \*h₃em-so- (IE) - Identical to Skt. arrsa- m. 'shoulder', Oss. wam, won, on / iwona? 'shoulder blade', ToA es, ToB antse m. 'shoulder' (with unexpected \*a), Arm. us 'id.', Gr. iliμoc; m. 'shoulder, shoulder with the upper arm' < \*h₃em-so-; Lat umerus 'id.' m. < \*h₃em-es-o-. PGm. \*amsa- can together with e.g. Skt arrsa- be reconstructed as \*hЪm-so-. In view of the ablaut with Lat. umerus and Arm. us, this formation must be a thematization of a more primary s-stem \*h₃em-os, gen. \*h₃m-s-6s (cf. h₂eus-os, \*h₂us-s-6s 'daybreak' under \*austron-). Note that the m of \*amsa- is based on the Gothic form only: ON ass '1) hill; 2) beam' is a merger of \*amsa- and \*ansa- (for the original nasality of the ON vowel, cf. Elfd. qs 'ridge', Nystrom 1995).

\*amslon- f. 'blackbird' - OE osle f. 'id.', E ousle, OHG amsala f. 'id.', G Amsel f. 'id.' ⇒ \*a-ms/- (NIE) - Lat merula f. 'blackbird' < \*mesal-eh₂-; W mwyalch f. 'id.', Bret. moua/c'h 'id.', OCo. moelh 'id.' < \*mesa/-k-eh₂-. The alternation of Gm. \*ams/- vs. Italo-Celtic \*mesa/- has been interpreted as pointing to a non-IE origin (Schrijver 1997). If correct, the root \*mesa/was reduced to \*ms/- in Germanic by the addition of the notoriously non-IE a-prefix (for another word with this prefix, see \*a rut- 'ore'). Alternatively, it

<!-- pdf-page: 064; section: dictionary -->

is possible to project the word back into Proto-Indo-European by reconstructing an amphikinetic s-stem \*h₂em-os, \*h₂ms-6s, \*h₂m-es- (cf. \*amazan'bunting') plus a diminutive /-suffix. The limitation of the word to the European languages makes this possibility less attractive, however.

\*ana prep., adv. 'on(to), to, by' - Go. ana prep. 'on, at, during, in', ON a prep./adv. 'on(to)', Far. a prep./adv. 'on, at', Elfd. q prep./adv. 'on', OE on prep./ adv. 'id.', E on, OFri. on, an, en, in prep./adv. 'id.', OS an prep./ adv. 'id.', Du. aan prep./ adv. 'id.', OHG ana, an prep./adv. 'id.', G an prep./ adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂enh₂ (IE) - Gr. ava adv./prep. 'up along', Umb. an-, am- pref., Olr. anpref. 'from'. An old IE adverb and preposition. Note that the laryngeal appears to have been vocalized in word-final position. The resulting a-vowel was lost in the individual daughter languages, but remained in compounds, whence it was restored. For a similar restoration, see \*aba.

\*anad- f. 'duck' - ON pnd, pl. endr f. 'id.', Far. ont, pl. entur f. 'id.', Elfd. and f. 'id.', OE amid, cened, ened f. 'id.', WFri. ein c. 'id.', MDu. aent, eent m./f. 'duck', OHG anut, enit f. 'id.', G Ente f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂enh₂-et- (IE) - Skt iitf-, atf- f. 'duck', Oss. ace 'wild duck' ( < Plr. \*ii ti-cf-), Gr. vfjcrcra, Att. vfjna f. 'id.' < \*h₂nh₂-t-ih₂-(?); Lat. anus, gen.sg. -atis f. 'duck', < \*h₂enh₂-et-; OPru. antis 'id.', Lith. antis f. 'id.' < \*h₂enh₂-t-; CS pty, ORu. uty, SCr. iitva f. 'id.' < \*h₂enh₂-t-u(e)h₂-. A root noun nom. \*anap, gen. \*anadiz continuing a PIE t-stem nom. \*h₂enh₂-t, gen. \*h₂nh₂-t-es. The daughter languages generalized different stem variants, but Latin anat- and Gm. \*anad- both go back to the same stem variant \*h₂enh₂-et- with two generalized full grades (on which see Beekes 1985: 63-4). Note that ON pnd in the nominative shows the effects of u-umlaut, which was introduced analogically in all feminine root nouns; there is no evidence for a proto-form \*anud-, as cited by e.g. Pokorny IEW: 41-2. Further note that Far. ont with its word-final t appears to be built on the nominative form \*anap, for which see \*smeuho- 'smew'. The change of word-final \*p to t is corroborated by Far. vart (beside varO), Nw. vart, Elfd. wart, the preterite to ON veroa 'to become' < \*werpan-.

\*anadan- m. 'breath; spirit' - ON andi m. 'breath; mind', Far. andi m. 'id.', Elfd. ande m. 'spirit', OE anda, onda m. 'envy, anger, zeal', ME onde 'breath, emotion, envy', OS ando m. 'offense', OHG anado m. 'emulation, envy', MHG ande m. 'hurt' ⇒ \*h₂enhreto- (DRY). Derived from \*anan- 'to breathe' (q.v). Also cf. ON pnd f. 'breath; mind, soul', Far. ond f. 'breath; breeze' < \*anado-, the denominal verb \*anadojanas continued by ON anda w.v. 'to breathe', OHG anadon, anton w.v. 'to strive, rail against', MHG anden w.v. 'to avenge', refl. 'to hurt', G ahnden w.v. 'to

<!-- pdf-page: 065; section: dictionary -->

punish, avenge', and the tertiary OFri. ondema, omma m. 'breath' < \*an(a)dman- (for the loss of internasal -d-, cf. OFri. mundman, momman m. 'guardian'). With a prefix, cf. ON erendi, erendi n. 'breathing pause' < \*uz-andja- and OE oroo n. 'breath' < \*uz-anpa-. •anan- s.v. 'to breathe' - Go. us-anan s.v. 'to breathe out' ⇒ \*h₂enhre- (IE) - Skt. ciniti 'to breathe', Olr. anaid 'to wait' < \*h₂enh₂-; further cf. ToB anask- 'to inhale' < \*h₂nhrske-; Ru. von' f. 'stench' < \*h₂enhri- (with unexpected v); Olr anal f. 'breath', w anadyl f. 'id.' < \*h₂enhrtl-eh₂-, An originally athematic verb preserved only by Gothic, Old Irish and Sanskrit. Also cf. ON erendi, erendi n. 'breathing pause' < \*uz-andja- and OE oroo n. 'breath' < \*uz-anpa-, whence OE eoian w.v. 'to breathe' < \*anpjan-. See further \*anadan- and \*unsti-.

\*andiz adv. 'earlier, rather' - ON enn adv. 'rather, than; again', Far. enn adv. 'id.', OE end adj. 'formerly, of old', OHG enti adv. 'earlier' ⇒ \*h₂ent-is (IE). An adverbial comparative to the root \*h₂ent- 'front', for which cf. \*andja'end' and \*anpja- 'forehead'.

\*andja- m. 'end, extreme' - Go. andeis m. 'end, extremity', ON endir m. 'end', OE ende m. 'end, back', E end, OFri. e(i)nda m. 'end, verdict', OS endi m. 'beginning, end, aim', Du. eind n. 'end', OHG anti, enti m./n. 'end, edge, front', MHG ente, ende m./n. 'end, direction', G Ende n. 'end' ⇒ \*h₂ent-i6- (IE) - Close to Skt. cintya- adj. 'located at the end', Gr. avTto<; adj. 'set against, opposed to' < \*h₂ent-io- and Skt. cinta- m. 'end, border, edge' < \*h₂ent-o-. A pan-Germanic ja-stem. The oldest languages point to a masculine formation; in the West Germanic dialects, the word became neuter at a late stage.

\*and/a- m.(?) 'saltmarsh grass' - OFri. ondul, andel 'id.', LG andel 'id.' (GM). An originally Frisian word without a certain etymology. The comparison with Skt. and has- n. 'sprout of the Soma-plant' ( = PFU \*ant3, cf. Komi od 'spring greens, germ, sprout'), Gr. av-Oo<; n. 'flower' < \*h₂endh-os- (cf. Schwenter 1951: 244) is semantically weak.

\*andura- m. 'snow-shoe, skid(?)' - ON pndurr m. 'id.', Nw. onder f. 'the shorter ski of two', Elfd. andur m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁ondh-ur- (IE) - Gr. E:v-OETv 'to come, go' < \*h₁{e)ndh-; Skt adhvan-, OAv. aduuan-, YAv. aof3an- m. 'road' < \*h₁ndh-uon-. A Nordic word probably derived from the PIE root \*h₁endh- 'to go' with the heteroclitic ur/n-suffix. Also cf. ON andri and Ice!. andra w.v. 'to wander, go fast, ski', both with syncopated \*u, cf. Fi. antura 'sole'.

<!-- pdf-page: 066; section: dictionary -->

\*angan- m. 'curve; neck' - Go. \*hals-agga m. 'neck', ON angi m. 'tip, jag', Far. angi m. 'vertebral process; cog, tooth, prong', Nw. dial. ang-boge m. 'id.', OHG ango m. 'hinge, cardinal point; prickle', G Swab. hals-anke f. 'neck' â

\*h₂enk-6n- (IE) - Gr. ayKwv m. 'curve, elbow' < \*h₂enk-on-; Skt. arika- m. 'hook, clamp', Gr. oyKo<; n. 'hook', Gr. oyKO<; adj. 'curved' < \*h₂onk-o-. An n-stem of considerable antiquity, probably formed to an adjective \*h₂onk-6-. See also \*angra- 1.

\*angaza- m. 'worry' - ON angr m. 'grief, Far. angur m. 'repentance, remorse', poet. 'anxiety, worry' ⇒ \*h₂emgh-os-o- (IE) - Skt. aT}1has- n. 'distress, trouble', YAv. qzah- n. 'anxiety, narrowness', Lat. angor m. 'suffocation, anxiety' < \*h₂emgh-es-. The Germanic form seems to be a thematization of an older s-stem \*h₂emgh-os, gen. \*h₂emgh-es-os related to \*angwu-. Also cf. Elfd. aungger adj. 'sorry' < \*angaza-.

\*angra- 1 m. 'lowland, meadow' - ON angr m. 'bay', MDu. anger m. 'meadow, enclosed piece of land', OHG angar m. 'uncultivated meadow', MHG anger m. 'grassland' ⇒ \*h₂enk-r6- (EUR) - Lat. ancrae f.pl. 'valley, gorge' < \*h₂enk-reh₂-. With Lat. ancrae, the Germanic form can be reconstructed as \*angra-. In view of Gr. CiyKo<; n. 'valley', it is alternatively possible to assume a proto-form \*angaza- < \*h₂enk-os-o-, i.e. a thematicized s-stem. See also \*angan-.

\*angra- 2 m. 'weevil' - OHG angar m. 'corn weevil', G Engerling 'cockchafer grub' ⇒ \*h₂engwh-ro- (NEUR) - Ru. ugor', SCr. ugor m. 'eel', OPru. angurgis m. 'id.', Lith. ungurys m. 'id.' < \*h₂engwh-r-io-. A North-European word probably related to \*unkan- 'snake' (q.v.).

\*ang_ula- m. 'hook, tip' - ON r;mgull m. 'angle, hook', OE ongel m. 'fishing-rod', E angle, MLG angel m. 'prickle, sting; hinge', OHG angul m. 'fishing-rod, prick, hook' ⇒ \*h₂enk-ul-6- (IE). See \*angan-, \*anhula- and \*anhulO-.

\*angwu- adj. 'narrow' - Go. aggwus adj. 'id.', ON engr adj. 'id.', OE enge adj. 'id.', OFri. enge adj. 'id.', WFri. eang adj. 'afraid', EFri. ong adj. 'id.', MDu. enghe adj. 'narrow', Du. eng adj. 'scary; narrow', OHG angi, engi adj. 'narrow', G eng adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂emgh-u- (IE) - Skt a1]1hu- adj. 'narrow', MP hnzwgadj. 'id.', Arm. anj-uk adj. 'narrow; difficult', OCS pz'bkb, Ru. uzkij, SCr. uzak adj. 'narrow', Olr. cumung, W cyfyng adj. 'id.' < \*(kom-)h₂emgh-u-. An old u-stem to the root \*h₂emgh-, cf. Hitt. bamanki - baminkanzi 'to tie, betroth' < \*h₂m-6n-gh-ei, \*h₂m-n-gh-enti; Gr. avxw 'to tie up, to strangle', Lat ango 'to throttle, to choke, to strangle' < \*h₂engh-e-. Also cf. OHG angust, G

<!-- pdf-page: 067; section: dictionary -->

Angst f. 'fear' < \*angusti- and the factitive Go. ga-aggwjan w.v. 'to oppress, constrain, cramp', ON engja, engva w.v. 'to make narrow', MDu. engen w.b. 'to make narrow' < \*angwjan-.

\*anhula- m. 'shoot, tip' - ON (Jll, all m. 'shoot' ⇒ \*h₂enk-ul-o- (IE) - Gr. tiyKUAoc; adj. 'curved, bent'. See \*angan-, \*angula- and \*anhulO-.

\*anhulo- f. 'strap' - ON (JI, al f. 'belt, strip', Far. al f. 'strap, belt; stretch', OE ol-pwang 'halter, snare, strap' ⇒ \*h₂enk-ul-eh₂- (IE) - Gr. ayKUATJ f. 'belt'. See \*angan-, \*anhula- and \*angula-.

\*ankjon- f. 'ankle' - ON ekkja f. 'heel', OHG ancha, encha f. 'ankle' ⇒ \*h₂eng-ieh₂- (DRV). See \*ankula-.

\*ankon- 'w.v. 'to complain' - MLG anken w.v. 'to moan, grunt' ⇒ \*h₃enh₃-(?) (IE?) - Hitt. bannai - bannanzi 'to sue, judge' < \*h₃e-h₃n6h₃-ei, \*h₃e-h₃nh₃-enti (Kloekhorst 2008: 282/5); Gr. ovoμm 'to blame, to treat scornfully' < \*h₃enh₃-e-. If MLG anken has a secondary k-suffix (cf. OE bi-stealcian 'to stalk' < \*stalkon- vs. \*stelan- 'to stalk; steal'), it may contain the PIE root \*h₃enh₃- 'to blame'. Ice!. anka 'to irritate, bother', Far. anka 'to groan, moan', Nw., Da. anke 'to complain; appeal (to a higher court)' were borrowed from Low German (cf. Magnusson 1989: 20-1).

\*ankula- m. 'ankle' - ON pk/a, pkkla n., pkli m. 'id.', Far. ekil m. 'id.', Elfd. okkel m. 'id.', OFri. ankel m./n. 'id.', MDu. ankel m. 'id.', Du. enkel c. 'id.', OHG enchil m. 'id.', G Enke/ m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂eng-ul- (IE) - Skt. anguri- f. 'finger, toe', Oss. cengwy/3 f cengul3ce m. 'finger' < \*h₂eng-ul-i-. The Germanic forms in \*-ula- are particularly close to Skt. anguri-, which contains a similar suffix. In German and Dutch, the suffix was apparently replaced by \*-ila-. It seems less likely, at any rate, that OHG enchil directly corresponds to Skt. anguri- through \*iinkiili- (with chain umlaut). See also \*ankjon-.

\*ankwan- m. 'butter' - OHG ancho m. 'id.', MHG anke m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₃ngw-n(IE) - Lat. unguen n. 'fat, grease', Dir. imb n. 'butter', MW ymenyn m. 'id.' (withyn-suffix) < \*h₃ngw-n-. A proterodynamic n-stem \*h₃engw-n, gen. \*h₃ngw-en-s created to a verbal root, cf. the Skt. n-present an<ikti, 3pl. anj<inti 'to anoint, smear' < \*h₃f)-ne-gw-ti, \*h₃f)·n-gw-enti.

<!-- pdf-page: 068; section: dictionary -->

\*ansa- m. 'beam' - Go. ans m. 'wooden beam', ON ass m. 'thick pole, beam, yard of a sail', Far. asur m. 'lath; axle, shaft; post', Nw. as m. 'axle, beam, hill, ridge (of a hill)', Elfd.guov-qs m. 'joist', MHG ans-boum m. 'beam' (GM). Of unclear origin. The alleged link with Skt. anas-, Lat onus n. 'load' < \*h₃en-os- is doubtful because the required intermediate meaning "carrier beam" is unattested. It has been suggested that ON ass is identical to \*amsa'shoulder' (q.v.) < \*h₃em-os-, but the two words are kept distinct in Gothic. The connection with Lat. asser m. 'wooden beam' (Petersson 1921: 19) is phonologically impossible. Also cf. Fi. ansas 'beam'.

\*ansjo- f. 'handle, ring, eyelet' - ON ;es f. 'thread-hole in a shoe', Far. ;es f. 'id.', WFri. oes c. 'eyelet', MLG ose f. 'ring-shaped handle' ⇒ \*h₂ens-ieh₂ (IE) - Gr. Ηvia n.pl. 'reins, bridles' < \*h₂ens-ih₂-; Lat. ansa f. 'handle, loop' < \*h₂ens-h₂-; Oir. eis(s)i m.pl. 'reins' < \*h₂ens-io-; OPru. ansis 'hook', Latv. uoss, uoss f. 'ear (of a jug)' < \*h₂ens-i-; Lith. qsa f. 'ear (of a jug), button-hole', Latv. uosa f. 'handle, ear, eyelet' < \*h₂ens-eh₂-. An old ieh₂-stem.

\*ansti- f. 'love, favor' - Go. ansts f. 'beneficence, grace', ON ast f. 'love, affection', Far. ast f. 'id.', OE est m./f. 'favor, consent, grace', OFri. evest 'envy', OS anst f. 'grace', OHG anst f. 'thanks, favour, grace' ⇒ \*h₃enh₂-sti- (DRV). A sti-formation to the preterite-present \*unnan- ( q.v.).

\*ansu- m. 'god' - ON ass, 6ss, pl. ;esir m. 'id.', OE OS m. 'id.', OS as, OS m. 'id.', OHG ans(i)- prop. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂ems-u- (IE) - Close to Hitt. bassu- c. 'king', OAv. ahu-, YAv. aT)hu- m. 'lord' < \*h₂ms-u-; also cf. Skt. asura-, Av. ahura- m. 'god, lord' < \*h₂msu-ro-. Also cf. Jordanes anses (pl.) 'demigods'. An ancient Indo-European word ultimately related to Hitt basi - bassanzi 'to give birth, to beget', which in view of CLuv. bamsa/i- c. 'grandchild' must be reconstructed as \*h₂ems(Kloekhorst 2008: 293).

\*anpara- adj./num. 'the other (of two), the second' - Go. anpar num. 'id.', ON annarr pron./num. 'id.', Far. annar pron./num. 'id.', OE ooer adj./num. 'id.', E other, OFri. other adj./num. 'id.', OS othar, athar, andar adj. 'other', Du. ander adj. 'id.', OHG andar adj. 'id.', G andere adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂en-tero- (IE) - Skt. antara- adj. 'distant, different, other', YAv. ai:itara- adj. 'other, second', Oss. ;end;er adj. 'other' < \*h₂en-tero-; OPru. antars, anters, Lith. afl.tras, dial. afl.taras, Latv. uotrs num. 'second'. A pronoun and adjective as well as the ordinal pertaining to \*twa- 'two' (q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 069; section: dictionary -->

•anpja- n. 'forehead' - ON enni n. 'forehead; steep crag', Far. enni n. 'forehead, brow; sharp projection on a steep mountain side', Nw. dial. enne, enna n. 'id.', OSw. cenne n. 'id.', Elfd. enne n. 'id.', OHG andi, endi n. 'id.', MHG e{i)nde n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂enti-o- (IE) - Lith. antis m. 'bosom' < \*h₂ent-io-; Lat antiae f.pl. 'forelocks' < \*h₂ent-iehr. Created to the original root noun \*h₂ent-s, Joe. \*h₂ent-i 'forehead, front', cf. Hitt banza, loc. bante 'id.' (with restoration of \*banze to bante). Note that Nw. dial. enna was incorporated into the class of neuter n-stems denoting body parts. See further \*andja- and \*umbi. •anpo- - \*ando- f. 'antechamber' - ON pnn, pnd f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂enHt-h₂- (IE) - Skt &ta- f.pl. 'door-frame, door-posts', YAv. aaa-huua Joe.pl. 'house' ( < •aaa-), Arm. dr-and (\*dhur-) 'porch, threshold', Lat. antae f.pl. 'square pilasӁers' < \*H(e)nHt-(e)h₂-; YAv. qitJiiii- f.pl. 'door-post' < \*h₂enHt-ieh₂-. An ablauting hi-stem, nom. \*h₂enHt-h₂, gen. \*h₂nHt-h₂-6s (Beekes apud De Vaan 2003: 136). The Verner alternation proves that the accentual mobility, which was originally coupled with the IE ablaut alternation, was still intact in PGm. Since the root \*h₂enHt- is too long to be primary, it is possible to think that the word arose as a collective form of a neuter t-stem created to the preposition \*h₂enh₂ 'on', viz. \*h₂enh₂-t-h₂. Another possibility is to reconstruct a compound of the same preposition with the root \*h₂et(H}- (LIV2: 273), cf. Skt. atati 'to go'. •apaldra/o- m./f. 'apple-tree' - ON apaldr m. 'id.', Far. apaldur m. 'id.', OE apuldre f. 'id.', OHG apfoltra f. 'id.', G apfalter, ajfa/ter f. 'id.' (DRV). A dendronym created to \*apla- with the tree-suffix \*-dra-. •apan- m. 'monkey, ape' - ON api m. 'id.', Far. apa f. 'id.', Sw. apa c. 'id.', OE apa m. 'id.', E ape, OS apo m. 'id.', Du. aap c. 'id.', OHG ajfo m. 'id.', G Ajfe m. 'id.' ⇒ \*Hab-on- (NIE). The word has irregular *comparanda* in related and unrelated languages, which suggest a non-Indo-European origin, cf. Skt. kapf- 'monkey', Gr. Kijnoi; 'long-tailed monkey', Hsch. aJpavac; ( = aJJavac;?) 'KEhol TOU<; 1æp1<omflTj1<ouc;', Hehr. qof, Akk. uqupu, iqupu, aqupu, Copt. sapi, OEgypt. gfj 'monkey, vel sim.' (cf. Witzel 2001: 44). The lack of an initial velar in Germanic suggests that the word was adopted as \*h₂eb- or \*h₃eb- at a relatively early stage. •apla- m. 'apple' - Go. Crim. ape/ 'id.', OE cep/, appel m. 'id.', E apple, OS appul m. 'id.', Du. appel c. 'id.', OHG apful, apfel m. 'id.', G Apfel m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂eb-ol- (EUR) - Olr. ubull n. 'id.', W afal m. 'id.' < \*h₂eb-1-; Lith. obuo!ys, (dial.) 6buo/as m. 'id.', Latv. abuols, dial. abuls m. 'apple, clover

<!-- pdf-page: 070; section: dictionary -->

(wood-sorrel?)', OPru. woble, wabelcke 'apple' < \*h₂eb-(o)l-; OCS ab/'bko n., Ru.jabloko n., SCr.jabuka f. 'id.' < \*h₂eb-l-k-. The West Germanic forms point to a form \*apla- (or \*apli- in view of the plural OHG epfili, G Apfel). Nordic has a secondary neuter formation epli, continuing \*aplja-, but it also offers proof for an ablauting form \*apal- as supported by \*apaldra/6- 'apple-tree' and \*apala-grewa- 'dapple-gray', cf. ON apal-grar, OS apul-gre adj. 'id.', OHG apfel-grii., G apfel-grau. Together, the ablauting variants \*apla- and \*apal- appear to support the reconstruction of a Proto-Indo-European /-stem nom. \*h₂eb-6l, gen. \*h₂eb-l-os, acc. \*h₂eb-ol-m, which looks rather archaic. There are some important linguistic indications, however, that the word for 'apple' did not belong to the oldest layer of the Indo-European proto-language (Markey 1988). The word is 1) limited to the West Indo-European languages; 2) it contains the phoneme \*b, which was marginal in PIE; and 3) it bears some resemblance to the South European word for 'apple', viz. Gr. μfjt.ov, Lat. mii.lum < \*meh₂l-om. This may be an indication that the word entered the Indo-European continuum some time after its disintegration. Within Germanic, also compare Ice!. epill m. 'pancreas, lymph gland' < \*apila-.

\*aran- 1 m. 'eagle' - Go. ara m. 'id.', ON ari, prn m. 'id.', Da. fdrn c. 'id.' (>> Far. tJrn f.), OE earn m. 'id.', Du. arend c. 'id.', OHG aro, arn m. 'id.', MHG are, ar m. 'id.', G Aar m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₃er-on- (IE) - Identical to Hitt. bii.ran- c. 'eagle'; further cf. Gr. opvu; m./f. 'bird' < \*h₃(e)r-ni-; Lith. ere/is, Latv. ၷrglis m. 'eagle' (with a > e by Rozwadowsky's change), OPru. arelie 'id.' < \*h₃er-el-io-; OCS orol'b, Ru. ore/, SCr. orao m. 'id.' < \*h₃er-l-i-; formally problematic: Olr. irar, ilar, W eryr 'id.' < \*eriro-. An old n-stem \*h₃er-on, \*h₃(e)r-n-6s that is preserved as such only in Hittite and Germanic. In Nordic, the ace.pl. \*arnuns < \*h₃er-n-1J.S gave rise to a split-off stem \*arnu-, which is fully parallel to the u-stem PN \*kattu- vs. PGm. \*katton- 'cat' (q.v.). OE earn, OHG arn and Du. arend continue thematicized \*arna-.

\*aran- 2 m. 'phalanx bone(?)' - OSw. are m. 'part of the thumb, phalanx(?)' ⇒ \*h₂erh₂-on-(?) (IE?). The word is a hapax in Old Swedish, which renders its status uncertain. Formally and semantically, however, it is compatible with the root of \*arma- 1 'arm' (q.v.).

\*arba- m./n. 'working animal, worker(?)' - ON arfr m. 'ox' ⇒ \*h₃orbh-o- (IE) - Arm. orb 'orphan', OCS rab'b, ORu. rob'b, SCr. rob m. 'servant, slave'; Lat orbus adj. 'deprived of, childless, orphaned'; Gr. 6p<pav6c; m. 'orphan', adj. 'orphaned'.

<!-- pdf-page: 071; section: dictionary -->
