'sinew', pl. 'bowels' ⇒ \*h₁eh₁t-r- (IE) - Gr. KTOP n. 'heart' < \*h₁eh₁t-r-; unrelated is Olr. inathar m. 'entrails, bowels', MCorn. inederen 'extum' < \*h₁en-utro- (Anders J0rgensen, p.c.). The word, which may contain the same root as \*epman- - \*edman- (q.v.), alternates between \*epro- and \*edro- in Germanic, and this may point to an originally mobile paradigm, e.g. sg. \*h₁eh₁t-r, pl. \*h₁h₁t-6r. Also cf. the collective OHG inn-ii.d(i)ri, MHG in-ceder(e) n. 'entrails, veins', OLFra. inn-ethron dat.pl. 'visceribus' < \*eprja-.

\*eudra- - \*iidra- n. 'udder' - ON jugr n. 'id.', Far. jugur n. 'id.', Elfd. juor n. 'id.', OE uder n. 'id.', E udder, OFri. uder n. 'id.', WFri. jaer n. 'id.', NFri. Hall. jaodar n. 'id.', MLG jeder, jiidder n. 'id.', Du. uier c. 'id.' (dial. jadder 'teat' « OFri. \*jadder), OHG utar(o) m. 'id.', G Euter n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁(e)uHdh-r/n- (IE) - Skt. ddhar - ildhan- n. 'udder'< \*h₁uHdh-r/n-; Gr. o(;-8ap, -aTO<; n. 'id.' < \*h₁ouHdh-r/nt-; Lat. uber n. 'breast; udder' < \*h₁e/ouHdh-r-. While the PGm. form \*udra- is backed up by Skt. udhar, the e-grade \*eudra- (or perhaps rather \*eudur in view of the dissimilation of \*-IJ- to \*-gin ON jugr, cf. fj9gur '4' < \*fe91Juru < \*fedur-6) < \*h₁euHdh-r- is uniquely Germanic. Together with Gr. o(;-8ap < \*h₁ouHdh-r-, the material provides evidence for a static paradigm \*h₁6uHdh-r, gen. \*h₁euHdh-n-s, but the origin of the zero-grade root \*h₁uHdh- remains uncertain. The ultimate derivation of the word is not entirely clear, but it is possible that it was created to a root \*h₁euHdh- as evinced by Ru. dial. udit' 'to ripen (of grain); to swell up with liquid' < \*h₁ouHdh-eie- (Vine/Yokoyama 2010). This may be a dhhi-extension to a more primary base \*h₁eu(H)- possibly preserved in Hitt. uya- 'nurse(?)' (Melchert 1986: 111, 114). The heteroclitic r/n-suffix was strongly associated with body parts in Indo-European.

\*eup - \*upp adv. 'up, upwards' - Go. iup adv. 'id', ON upp, Far. upp, Elfd. upp adv. 'id.', OE up adv. 'id.', E up, OFri. up, op adv. 'id.', OS up adv. 'id.', Du. op adv., prep. 'up; on', OHG uf, G auf adv., prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*(h₁e)up-n- (DRV?). An ablauting directional adverb, related to \*uba 'under; above' (q.v.), with a geminate that was shortened in the full-grade form (cf. Luhr 2000: 190). The geminate arose regularly in the locatival form \*uppai < \*up-noi, whose suffix can be compared to the one of e.g. OCS V'bne 'outside' < \*ud-noi (cf. \*ut). It is difficult to determine whether \*eup and \*upp had a suffix \*-e or \*-a

<!-- pdf-page: 161; section: dictionary -->

\*ewwadjon- 121 corresponding to the vowels of e.g. Lat. pone 'from behind' < \*pos(t)-ne or OCS von'b 'out' < \*ud-nom. This is possible, but it cannot be excluded that both \*eup and \*upp were simply back-formed to the locative \*uppai by the removal of the ending (Kroonen 2010c: 374). Note that the long vowel of OHG uf, G aufis due to monosyllabic lengthening (cf. Luhr l.c.).

\*ewwadjon- f. 'tit' - ON igoa f. 'small bird', Far. poet igoa f. 'tit(?)', Nw. igde f. 'id.' (NIE). An exclusively Nordic word derived from a more primitive masculine form Nw. egde < \*ewwadan-. Somehow connected to lmandra Saa. avig3ine 'tit(?)' as cited by Qvigstad 1902: 270? Either way, the word could be a loanword from an extinct Pre-Nordic language, for which cf. \*awadf' eiderduck'.

### F

\*fader- m. 'father' - Go. fadar m. 'id.', ON faoir m. 'id.', Far. faoir m. 'id.', Elfd. fader m. 'id.', OE feeder m. 'id.', E father, OFri. feder m. 'id.', OS fadar m. 'id.', Du. vader c. 'id.', OHG fatar m. 'id.', G Vater m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ph₂-ter- (IE) - ToA pacar, ToB pacer, Skt pitcir-, OAv. pta, acc. pataram, YAv. pita, acc. pitaram, OP pita, NP pidar, Oss. fyd / ftdce, Gr. na-oip, gen. na-rp6c;, Lat. pater, -tris, Olr. athir, athair m. 'father'. The ludo-European word for 'father', a hysterokinetic ter-stem, nom. \*ph₂-t҉r, gen. \*ph₂-tr-6s, acc. \*ph₂-ter-m. See also \*fadurwjan-.

\*fadi- m. 'lord' - Go. brup-faps m. 'bridegroom', hunda-faps, pusundi-faps m. 'leader of hundred/thousand' ⇒ \*pot-i- (IE) - ToA pats 'husband', Skt. pati-, Av. paiti- m. 'lord, ruler, husband', Gr. n6mc; m. 'husband', Lith. pats m. 'id.' < \*pot-i-; Lat. potis adj. 'capable, powerful' < \*pot-i-. An old ludo-European word often used as a second member of compounds, e.g. Skt. vis-pa ti- 'lord of the house', Li th. vies-pats 'lord', Lat. hos-pes 'guest, host', Ru. gos-pod' 'Lord, god'. Also cf. Hitt. -pat adv. 'the same, also, even', Av. xvae-paiti- 'himself, Lith. pats 'self, adv. pat 'even'. An unattested PGm. form \*swabadi- ( < \*suo-poti-) has been reconstructed on the basis of PSlav. \*svo-bodb 'free' (Vaillant 1958: §277), but this word is actually derived from an adjective \*suobho-, cf. Latv. at-svabinat 'to set free' < \*suobh-neh₂-, with the Slavic suffix \*-od-.

\*fadojan- w.v. 'to feed' - OHGfaton w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ph₂t-eh₂-ie- (EUR).

<!-- pdf-page: 162; section: dictionary -->

122 \*fadurwjan- Related to \*fodjan- (q.v.). Also cf. OHG fatunga f. 'feeding, fattening' and MLG vedeme, veme, G Fehme f. 'mast/feeding on acorns' < \*fadmjo-.

\*fadurwjan- m. 'paternal uncle, father's brother' - OEfeedera m. 'id.', OFri. federia, fedria, feria, fadria m. 'id.', MDu. vedder m. 'father's brother; uncle; brother's son', OHG fatureo,fetiro m. 'father's brother', MHG vetere, veter m. 'father's brother, brother's son, male cousin', G Vetter m. 'male cousin' ⇒ \*ph₂-tr-u-ion- (IE) - Skt. pitrvya-, YAv. trliriia- m. 'father's brother' < \*ph₂tr-u-io-; Lat. patruus m. 'id.' < \*ph₂tr-uo-; Gr. mhpwc; m. 'male relative, father's brother' < \*ph₂tr-ou-. A PIE derivation from \*fader- (q.v.). The Germanic word is identical to the lndo-Iranian formation, but Greek appears to attest to an amphikinetic u-stem \*ph₂-tr-ou, gen. \*ph₂-tr-u-os. For the suffix \*-wjan-, cf. \*suhturwjan-.

\*fagjan- w.v. 'to please' - Go.fulla-fahjan w.v. 'to satisfy' ⇒ \*pok-eie- (IE). The causative to \*fehan-. The same form of the root is found in Go. faheps f. 'joy, gladness', which was derived from an unattested Go. stative \*fahan continuing \*fagen-. Note that the effects of Vemer's law were undone in Gothic.

\*fagra- adj. 'fit (good, beautiful)' - Go. fagrs adj. 'fit, good', ON fagr adj. 'beautiful, handsome', Far. fagur adj. 'id.', OE feeger adj. 'id.', E fair, OS fagar adj. 'id.', OHG /agar adj. 'id.', MHG vager adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ph₂l{-r6- (IE) - Lat. pax f. 'peace' ( < "settlement") < \*peh₂K- and pactus 'agreed' < \*ph₂K-to-. An adjective created to the root of \*fogjan- 'to fit together' (q.v.). The comparison with \*fehan- 'to rejoice' is probably erroneous, as ro-adjectives usually take the zero-grade of the root. Also cf. the factitive Go. ga-fahrjan w.v. 'to prepare' < \*fagrjan-.

\*fahiz- n. 'sheep' - ON Jeer n. 'id.', OSw. far n. 'id.', Sw. far n. 'id.' ⇒ \*p6K-es(IE) - Lat. pecus, -oris n. 'sheep; cattle'. An old s-stem \*fahaz, gen. \*fahizaz. The Nordic forms can be unified into a proto-form \*fahaz-, assuming that ON Jeer was affected by R-mutation. Related to the root of\*fahsa- and \*fahti- (q.v.).

\*fahsa- n. 'hair of the head' - ON fax n. 'mane', OE feax n. 'hair of the head', OFri. fax n. 'id.', OS fahs n. 'id.', OHG fahs n. 'id.', MHG vahs m.f n. 'id.' ⇒ \*pol<-so- (IE) - Gr. nEKO<; n. 'fleece' < \*pel<-es-; Gr. n6Koc; m. 'wool, fleece' < \*pol<-o-; Arm. asr 'sheepwool, fleece' < \*poK-u-. A thematicized s-stem closely related to \*fahiz- and \*fahti- (q.v.). See also \*Jehu-.

<!-- pdf-page: 163; section: dictionary -->

\*fahti- 123

\*fahti- m. 'fleece' - OSw. fret m. 'id.', OE feht 'vellus', OFri. fecht m. 'wool, fleece', MDu. vacht m./f. 'fleece', Du. vacht c. 'fleece, fur' ⇒ \*poJ<-ti- (IE) - Gr. n£Kw 'to comb (oneself), to card, to shear', Lith. pesti (pesu) 'to pluck' < \*peJ<-e-. A ti-stem to an unattested o-grade present \*fahan- < \*p6J<-e-. Related to the PIE verb \*peJ<-e- 'to pull out hair' as preserved by Lithuanian. See also \*Jehu-.

\*faigi- adj. 'bound to die' - ON feigr adj. 'id.', OE f:i!ge adj. 'id.', E fey, OFri. Jach, fai adj. 'id.', OS feg, fegi adj. 'id.', Du. veeg adj. 'id.', OHG feigi adj. 'id.', G feige adj. 'coward', Swi.feig adj. 'slow, lazy' (IE). The form and meaning of the word point to a gerundive "who is to die". Within Germanic, the adjective can be linked to \*faiha- 1 'hostile' (Heidermanns 1993: 184-85), but it is also possible to derive it directly from the PIE root \*peiJ<- 'to carve, paint', for which see \*faiha- 2. The original meaning must then have been 'marked for death'.

\*faiha- 1 adj. 'hostile' - OE fiih, fiig adj. 'hostile, criminal', E foe, OFri. /ii.ch adj. 'guilty, outlawed', MDu. vee, ge-vee adj. 'hostile', OHG gi-feh adj. 'id.', MHG ge-vech adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*p6i<k'lo- (IE) - Skt. pifona- adj. 'evil, treacherous' < \*piJ<-; Olr. oech m. 'enemy' < \*poirJ<l-o-; Lith. pefkti (peikiu) 'to blame' < \*peik-ie- (also cf. Lith. piktas adj. 'angry' < \*pik-to-). An adjective derived from a root \*peiJ<- or \*peik-. Also cf. Go. ga-faihon w.v. 'to defraud' and OS ii-fehian, OHG fehen 'to treat in a hostile way', whence OE f:i!hO, OFri. faithe, feithe, (M)Du. vete, OHG fehida, G Fehde f. 'feud' < \*faihipo-. See also \*faigi-. \*faiha- 2 adj. OE fiih adj. 'colored', ME Jou adj. 'particolored, variegated, spotted, streaked', OS feh adj. 'colorful', OHG feh adj. 'id.', MHG vech adj. 'variegated' ⇒ \*p6iJ<o- (IE) - ToAB pik- 'to write', Skt. pirrisati 'to carve, cut, adorn', YAv. fra-pixsta- ptc. 'adorned' < \*pi-n-J<-; Lat. pingere 'to paint' < \*pil<-ne-; Gr. noLKiAos adj. 'varicolored' < \*poiJ<-ilo-; Lith. piesti 'to paint, write', OPru. peisiii 'they write' < \*peiJ<-; OCS pbsati (pisp) 'to write' < \*p(e)iJ<-ie-; OCS pbstn adj. 'varicolored' < \*piJ<-ro-. An adjective to the root \*peiJ<- 'to paint'. Also cf. the factitive OHG fehen, MHG vehen w.v. 'to colorize, stain' < \*faihjan- and ON fa w.v. 'to draw, paint' < \*faihojan-.

\*faima(n)- m. 'foam' - Nw. feim(e) m./n. 'thin layer of dew, dust, floating fat', MHG veim m. 'foam, dross' (IE) - Skt. phena- m. 'foam' < \*speh₃i-no-; Lat. spuma f. '(sea) foam' < \*speh₃i-m-eh₂-; OCS pena, Ru. pena, SCr. pjena f. 'foam' < \*peh₃i-no-; OPru. spoayno 'foam' < \*peh₃i-neh₂-; Lith. spaine f. 'foam

<!-- pdf-page: 164; section: dictionary -->

124 \*faitastreaks' < \*speh₃i-n-ieh₂-; Mir. uan, MW ewyn m. 'froth' < \*peh₃iu-ino- (with dissimilation of the first !). The different stems \*(s)peh₃i-m- and \*(s)peh₃i-n- can be reconciled into a single mn-stem paradigm nom. \*(s)peh₃i-m(o}n, gen. \*(s)ph₃i-mn-6s (Matasovic 2004: 126). The no-stem arose by dissimilation of the m in the genitival cluster \*-mn- against the preceding labial element (cf. \*budman- -

\*buttaz 'bottom' and \*hr/man- - \*hr/pan- 'hoarfrost'). De Vaan 2008: 583 points to the problem that no actual mn-stem is attested, but Nw.feime m./f. < \*peh₃i-mon- can be adduced as such. Also note Nw. Jim 'layer of dust or dew' < \*fima-, which may continue the original zero-grade \*ph₃i-mon- (with laryngeal metathesis).

\*faita- adj. 'fat' - ON feitr adj. 'id.', Far. feitur adj. 'id.', Elfd. fiet adj. 'id.', MLG vet adj. 'id.', MHG veiz adj. 'id.', G Cimb. voaz adj. 'id.' (IE?). An adjective probably related to \*fajOJa- (q.v.) < \*poih₃-o-. The Germanic root-final t has no extra-Germanic parallel (Heidermanns 1993: 187), but can tentatively be identified as the zero grade of the root \*deh₃- 'to give', thus starting from a meaning 'to fatten up' ( < "to give to eat''). PGm. \*faitagave rise to a factitive \*faitjan- (ON feita, OHG feizzen 'to make fat'), whose ptc. \*faitida- was again lexicalized, cf. OE [ci!tt, E fat, (M)Du. vet, OHG[eizzit, G feist adj. 'fat'. See also \*fitnan-.

\*fajOJa- adj. 'fat(?)' - Du. dial. vei adj. 'fat, lush, fertile' ::) \*poih₂-o- (IE) - Skt payate 'to swell' < \*peiH-e-; Av. poWpa- 'fat' < \*peiH-tuo-. An adjective derived from the PIE root \*peiH- (cf. LIV2 s.v. \*peiH-). Probably related to \*faita- 'fat' (q.v.).

\*/aka- n. 'enclosure, section' - OE /rec n. 'space, interval, distance, portion of time', OFri. fek n. 'part of the house, niche', MDu. vac n. 'compartment, section', Du. vak n. 'space; profession', OHG [ah n. 'wall, compartment', G Fach n. 'compartment, panel; discipline' ::) \*phi9-o- (IE) - Skt pdjas- n. 'side, surface(?)' < \*pehiB-os-; Gr. m']yvuμL 'to attach, join' < \*pehiB-neu-; Lat pango 'to insert firmly, fix' < \*phi9-neh₂- (with n-infixation through Thurneysen's law); Lat pti.gus m. 'district' < \*pehiB-o-. Also cf. MLG vake, MDu. vake, vaec, Du. vaak adv. 'often'.

\*fakk/gon- w.v. 'to become sleepy' - E to fag w.v. 'to tire, weary', obs. 'to hang loose, flap', Scot to faik w.v. 'to fail from weariness; to cease moving' (IE). A West Germanic iterative: the consonant variation of E to fag < \*faggonand Scot. to faik < \*fakon- is suggestive of a paradigm 3sg. \*fakkopi, 3pl. \*fagunanpi < \*po(f<l-neh₂-. In view of E fag 'fag-end, hanging end of a piece of cloth, untwisted end of a rope', it seems likely that the meaning 'to become

<!-- pdf-page: 165; section: dictionary -->

\*falan- 125 tired' developed from older 'to fray, ravel out', cf. Brit E frayed 'ragged, worn out'. This semantic field is compatible with Lith. pesti (pesu) 'to tousle, ruffle, tear off hair' < PIE \*peK-e-, which is related to e.g. \*fehu- 'cattle' and \*fahti- 'fleece'. Further note the oblique subject verbs OLFra. [aeon and MDu. vaken 'to sleep', which appear to be denominal formations to MLG viik, MDu. vake, vaec m. 'sleepiness' (cf. Du. Klaas Vaak 'the Sandman') < \*fakanor to an adj. \*fak(k)a- as perhaps implied by LG fakk 'tired, weak'.

\*falan- m. 'board' - OE fa/a m. 'id.' (NEUR) - ORu. pol'b m. 'foundation', Ru. pol m. 'floor'; Ukr. pol m. 'sleeping bench' < \*pol-o- (also cf. Ru. polfca, SCr. polica f. 'shelf and with an ablauting root OCS palica f. 'staff, Ru. palica f. 'club, cudgel', SCr. piilica f. 'stick, staff; Ru. palka f. 'stick, staff < \*pol-). The connection with the Slavic forms is beyond doubt, but the relation between the roots \*pol- and \*pol-(?) is unclear (Derksen 2008: 390, 410). See also \*faluda- and \*felo-.

\*falgo- f. 'fallow land' - ME falge, fa/we 'arable land lying idle; plowing as part of the fallowing process', Efallow, OFri.fallach 'arable land', MDu. valge f. 'id.' ⇒ \*polh₁tk'l-eh₂- (EUR) - Gaul. olca 'arable land' ( = Fr. ouche 'id.'); perhaps also Lith. plė̃sti (-siu, -siau), Latv. plēst 'to tear, pluck; to plow land for the first time' < \*pleh₁ḱ-ie-. Also cf. MHG valgen, Du. dial. valgen, WFri.felgje 'to plow' < \*falgojan- and OEfealh f. 'harrow' < \*fa/go-.

\*falkan- m. 'falcon' - ON falki m. 'id.', Far. falkur m. 'id.', OSw. falke,falker m. 'id.', MDu. valce m. 'id.', Du. valk c. 'id.', OHG fa/co m. 'id.', G Falke m. 'id.' (GM?). A West Germanic word: the Norpic forms are irregular, and thus point to borrowing from West Germanic. No certain etymology. The derivation from \*falwa- 'pale, fallow' is possible, but uncertain in spite of the potential parallel G dial. Falke m. 'fallow horse'. The latter word has -lk- from -lch-, and belongs to G Pal.fa/ch adj. 'pale' < \*fa/ha- < \*p61K-O-, cf. Lith. pal5as adj. 'pale (of cows)'. Slightly more attractive is the link with OPru. pele 'harrier'. The Germanic word must then have a k-suffix like e.g. \*alkon-, \*balika/on- and \*habuka-. Latfalco was adopted from Germanic.

\*fallan- s.v. 'to fall' - ON falla s.v. 'id.', Far. fa Ila s.v. 'id.', Elfd. folla s.v. 'id.', OE[allan s.v. 'id.', E to fall, OFri.falla s.v. 'id.', OS[allan s.v. 'id.', Du. vallen s.v. 'id.', OHG[allan s.v. 'id.', Gfallen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂p6-h₃/h₁- (IE) - Identical to Gr. arr6Uuμt, -μat 'to wreck, destroy, lose', mid. 'to go to waste, be ruined, be lost', Lat aboleo, -ere 'to destroy; banish; abolish', Arm. p'lanim 'to fall', Lith. pulti (puolu), Latv. pult 'to fall', OPru. ail-pallai 'finds'.

<!-- pdf-page: 166; section: dictionary -->

126 \*falljan- The strong verb synchronically functions as an o-grade intensive verb, but arose as a univerbation of the adverb \*h₂p6 'off (for which see \*fan€) and the PIE root \*h₃e/h₁- 'to fall' (Praust 2005; Neri 2007), cf. Hitt. yallannai - yallannijanzi 'to trample down, flatten' and Gr. o.UuμL The Germanic form requires the reconstruction of a nasal suffix in view of the root-final double I. In order to arrive at PGm. \*fallan- from Pre-Gm. \*peh₃/-n-e-, we must therefore assume that a form \*peh₃/-ne- gave \*palne- under Osthoff's law and subsequently \*falle- under Kluge's law. See also \*falljan-.

\*falljan- w.v. 'to fell' - ON fella w.v. 'id.', Far. fella w.v. 'id.', Elfd. fella s.v. 'id.', OE fiellan w.v. 'id.', E to fell, OFri. fella w.v. 'id.', OS fellian w.v. 'id.', Du. vellen w.v. 'id.', OHGfellan w.v. 'id.', Gfiillen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The causative to \*fallan- (q.v.).

\*falon- f. 'plain' - OSw. fa la f. 'id.', Sw. fa la 'id.' ⇒ \*po/h₂-eh₂- (IE) - OCS polje, Ru. pole, SCr. polje n. 'field' < \*polh₂-io-. Derived from the PIE root \*pelh₂- 'flat', for which see \*fulmo-.

\*falþan- s.v. 'to fold, ply' - Go. falþan s.v. 'id.', ON falda s.v. 'to hood oneself, Far. falda w.v. 'to put on clothes', Elfd. folda w.v. 'to hem, seam', OE fealdan s.v. 'to fold', E to fold, WFri. faldzje w.v. 'id.', MDu. volden w.v. 'id.', Du. vouwen s./w.v. 'id.', OHG faldan, faltan s.v. 'id.', G fa/ten w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*p61-t-e- (IE) - Cf. Gr. c5mA6oi;, c5mA.oui; adj. 'twofold', Lat. duplus adj. 'double, twofold' < \*dui-plo-; Arm. haf 'time' < \*pol-o-(?). · An o-grade intensive verb. The Pre-Gm. root-final \*t appears to be suffixal. Also cf. MDu. volde, Du. vouw, MHG valte, G Falte f. 'fold' < \*fa/do- and Go. ain-falþs, ON einjaldr, OE ii.n-feald, OFri., OS en-fald, OHG einjald adj. 'simple' < \*-falda-.

\*faluda- m. 'pen(?)' - Da. fold m. 'enclosure, pasture', OE fald, falod, falud m. 'fold, stable', E fold, OS faled m. 'id.', MDu. vaelde, vaelt m. 'enclosure, yard; dunghill', Du. mest-vaalt c. 'dunghill' (GM). A word with an original meaning 'enclosure', possibly related to 'board' and esp. OE/a/a 'tabula' < \*fa/an- (q.v.). The word contains the \*-uda- suffix that also occurs in e.g. OS rakud m. 'house', OE rceced n. 'house, hall' < \*rakuda-. Theoretically, it could be the zero grade of the PIE root \*uedh- 'to bind, tie' (for which see \*wedan-), but this is uncertain.

\*falwa- adj. 'pale' - ON fplr adj. 'id.', OE fealo adj. 'id.', E fallow, OS falu adj. 'fallow, yellow', Du. vaal adj. 'faded, drab', OHG falo adj. 'fallow, yellow', G falb adj. 'fallow, dun' ⇒ \*pol-uo- (IE) - Lat. pullus adj. 'drab-colored, sombre', Lith. palvas adj. 'light yellow, straw-colored', OCS plaVb adj. 'white', Ru. pol6vyj, polov6j adj. 'pale yellow, sandy', SCr. plav adj. 'blond, blue' <

<!-- pdf-page: 167; section: dictionary -->

\*fanan- 127 \*pol-uo-; Skt paru$a- adj. 'grey, grey-brown, dirty', YAv. pourufo- adj. 'grey (of hair)' < \*pelu-so-. A European adjective with the uo-suffix for color names.

\*fanan- m. 'cloth' - Go. Jana m. 'id.', OE Jana m. 'flag, banner', E vane, OFri. Jona m. 'id.', OS fa no m. 'id.', Du. vaan c. 'id.', OHG fa no m. 'id.', G Fahne m. 'id.' ⇒ \*peh₂-on- (EUR) - Gr. mlVTI f. 'the thread of the woof; woof, Lat. pannus m. 'piece of cloth, rag' < \*pehi-no/eh₂-. A European word. If \*fanan- is related to Gr. mlVT) and Lat. pannus ( < \*panus by the littera-rule), as is assumed by Pokorny IEW: 788, it seems logical to derive the Germanic form from \*ph₂-no- or \*peh₂-n6- (with Dybo's law). From this perspective, it is tempting to assume that the Gm. n-stem (like the Latin and Greek forms?) was remodeled from \*p(e)h₂-on, gen. \*ph₂-n-6s. Less likely is the connection with OCS o-pona f. 'curtain' < \*ponhi-eh₂-, which was derived from the root \*penhi- 'to spin'.

\*fandon- w.v. 'to investigate' - OE fandian w.v. 'to test, investigate, visit', OFri.fand(el)ia w.v. 'to visit a sick person', WFri. (early) /dn/e w.v. 'to collect fish traps', fandelje w.v. 'to collect, fetch', MDu. vanden w.v. 'to test, investigate, visit', OHG fandon w.v. 'to investigate', MHG vanden w.v. 'to visit', G fahnden w.v. 'to search, hunt for' ⇒ \*pont-neh₂- (DRV?). An o-grade iterative to \*finþan-. See also \*fundon-.

\*fane prep. 'from' - OFri. fan prep. 'id.', OS fan, Jana prep. 'id.', Du. van prep. 'id.', OHG Jana, Jona prep. 'id.', G van prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂po-neh₁ (DRV) - Lith. pa- pref., OCS po, Ru. po, SCr. po prep. 'after, on, by, at', Alb. pa prep. 'without'. A directional derived from the element \*fa, continuing the original allative case \*h₂p-6 of a PIE root noun \*h₂ep-, with the ablative suffix \*-ne < \*-neh₁. See further under the closely related \*aba.

\*fangi- m. 'catch' - Go. ga-fahs m. 'id.', ON fengr m. 'id.', OE Jeng m. 'grasp, span, hug', OFri.feng,fang,fong m. 'catch, capture', OHGfang m. 'id.', G Fang m. 'id.' (DRV). An i-stem derived from \*fanhan- (q.v.).

\*fangjan- - \*fankjan- w.v. 'to ignite' - MDu. ont-fengen, -fenken w.v. 'id.', MHG vengen, enpfengen, venken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ponk-eie- (GM). A causative to the strong verb \*finkan-, which replaces expected \*finhan< \*penk-e-: the alternation of \*fangjan- MDu. ont-fengen, MHG vengen) and \*fankjan- (MDu. ont-fenken, MHG venken) indicates that both \*finkan- and \*fankjan- adopted their root-final consonant from the iterative \*funkon- < \*pnk-neh₂- (q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 168; section: dictionary -->

128 \*fanhan-

\*fanhan- s.v. 'to catch' - Go. fahan s.v. 'id.', ON fa s.v. 'id.', Far. faa s.v. 'id.', Elfd . .fi1 s.v. 'id.', OE/on s.v. 'id.', OFri.fa s.v. 'id.', OSfahan s.v. 'id.', Du. vangen s.v. 'id.', OHGfahan s.v. 'id.', Gfangen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*p6nl<-e- (NEUR). A univerbation of the adverb \*h₂po and the root \*h₁enl<- 'to obtain' (or to PIE \*h₂enll- 'to reach', Scheungraber 2012). A similar univerbation is \*bringan- 'to bring' (q.v.) < \*pro-h₁enll-e-. Also cf. \*fangi-.

\*fanja- n. 'fen, swamp' - Go. Jani n. 'mud', ON fen n. 'fen, bog', OE fen m./n. 'marsh; mud', E fen, OFri. Jenne m. 'pasture', OS Jeni n. 'fen', MDu. veen, vene· n. 'bog', Du. veen n. 'id.', ven n. 'small lake', OHGfenni n. 'swamp', MHG venne n. 'id.', G Fenn n. 'id.' ⇒ \*pon-io- (EUR) - OPru. pannean acc. 'ditch' < \*pan-io-; Gaul. anam acc.f. 'paludem' < \*pen-eh₂- (with Joseph's rule -eRa- > -aRa-?), Mir. an, en 'water' < \*pen- (cf. enach n. 'swamp' < \*pen-akos-?). A collective noun potentially identical to OPru. pannean. The derivation of the Irish forms, esp. the variation of Mir. en and an, is problematic, but it is possible that radical e was mutated into a by Joseph's rule. Related to OE fyne 'mold'?

\*fanþjan- m. 'foot soldier(?)' - OE feoa m. 'foot soldier; troop', OHG fendo m. 'foot soldier, pedestrian', MHG vende m. 'pedestrian, boy' (DRV). Derived from \*finþan- with the agentive jan-suffix.

\*faran- s.v. 'to go' - Go.faran s.v. 'id.', ONfara s.v. 'id.', Elfd.fara s.v. 'id.', OE faran s.v. 'id.', E to fare, OFri. Jara s.v. 'id.', Du. varen w.v. 'to sail', OHG faran s.v. 'to go, travel, pass', G fahren s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*p6r-e- (IE) - Skt. pf-parti 'to bring across' < \*pi-par-; OAv. (fra-)fra lsg.subj.aor. 'to cross' < \*pre-pr-e-; Skt. parayati 'to make cross', YAv. ni-paraiieiTJti 3pl.pres. 'to lead to', OCS pariti 'to fly' < \*por-eie-; Gr. m:tpw 'to pierce, to penetrate' < \*per-ie-. An a-grade "intensive-iterative" verb. The ablaut of the preterite, i.e. \*for-, arose secondarily on the basis of verbs with roots of the structure CHC. See also \*fardi-, \*farjon-, \*farma-, \*Jori·, \*forjan- and \*furdu-.

\*fardi- f. 'journey' - ONferlJ f. 'id.', Far.ferlJ f. 'id.', Elfd.ferd f. 'id.', OEfierd f. 'expedition', OFri. ferd f. 'journey, voyage', OS Jard f. 'id.', Du. vaart f. 'canal', OHG fart f. 'trip, voyage', G Fart f. 'id.' ⇒ \*por-t[- (DRV). A ti-stem to the strong verb \*faran- (q.v.).

\*fargjan- w.v. 'to push (down)' - Icel. fergja w.v. 'to press, put pressure on', Far.fergja w.v. 'to press, squeeze, push', Nw.fergja w.v. 'to press/weigh down' ⇒ \*h₂po-h₂ork-eie- (IE) - Close to Lat. porcere 'to prevent' <

\*h₂po·h₂ork-eie-; also cf. Hitt. barzi - barkanzi 'to hold, have' < \*h₂erk-ti, \*h₂rk-enti; Gr. ap1c£w 'to avert, defend; to suffice', Lat. arceo 'to contain; to

<!-- pdf-page: 169; section: dictionary -->

\*farha- 129 keep out' < \*h₂erk-eie-; Ann. arge/ 'hindrance' (on the /-suffix, see Martirosyan 2009: 133). The connection with Skt. pp:uikti - prncati 'to fill, give abundantly' < 3sg. \*pr-n-ek-ti, 3pl. \*pr-n-k-enti and Skt. piparkti 'id.' < \*pi-pe/ork- and Mir. ercaid 'to fill' < \*perk-eh₂-ie- is not impossible, as the original PIE meaning may have been 'to stuff, whence 1) 'to fill' and 2) 'to weigh down'. However, it seems preferable to assume a direct link with Lat porcere 'to prevent', which continues the root \*h₂e/ork- prefixed with the adverb \*h₂p-6. The fact that this univerbation is found in both Latin and Germanic probably means that it continues an old PIE syntagm. Also cf. ON, Far., Nw. farga w.v. 'to press', which in combination with Nw. dial. farka w.v. 'to press someone into something' points to an iterative 3sg. \*fark6/Ji, 3pl. \*fargunanpi < Pre-Gm. \*pork-neh₂-ti, \*pork-nh₂-enti.

\*farha- m. 'farrow, pig' - OEfearh m. 'id.', Efarrow, OHGfarah,farh n. 'id.', MHG varch m. 'id.' ⇒ \*porl<-o- (IE) - Identical to Khot. pa'sa, Kurd. purs 'pig', Lat porcus m. 'id.', Olr. poet. ore m. 'id.', Lith. paf5as m. 'castrated boar' < \*porl<-o-. One of the Indo-European words for 'pig(let)'. In view of the possible connection with Gr. rr6pKo<; m. 'fishing-net' and Arm. ors 'hunt; catch; game' < \*porl<-o-, the word may have originally referred to the wild boar rather than the domesticated animal. Also cf. the diminutives MDu. verkijn, varkijn, Du. varken n. 'pig' < \*farha-kina- and OHGfarhili(n), MHG varchelfn, G Ferkel n. 'id.' < \*farha-lfna-.

\*farjon- f. 'ferry' - ON ferja f. 'id.', MLG vere f./n. 'id.', MDu. vere f./n. 'id.', Du. veer n. 'id.', MHG vere f. 'id.', G Fiihre f. 'id.' (ORV). Derived from Go. farjan, OS ferian, MHG vern 'to travel by boat', whence also OHG ferjo, MHG, MDu. vere m. 'ferryman'. The jan-verb is again based on ON far n. '(means of) passage, ship', OE Jeer n. 'journey; ship', OHG var n. 'place to cross a river' < \*Jara- (cf. Gr. rr6poi; m. 'passage (over a body of water), ford', MP, NP btir 'shore, bank'). Also cf. ON ferja 'to ferry', OE ferian 'to convey' < \*farjojan-.

\*farma- m. 'moving(?)' - ON farmr m. 'freight, cargo', Far. farmur m. 'id.', Nw. farm m. 'cargo; flock (of cattle)', OE fearm m. 'id.', OS farm m. 'onslaught', OHG farm m. 'fast ship', MHG varm m. 'id.' ⇒ \*por-mo- (ORV). A mo-stem based on the strong verb \*fa ran- ( q.v.).

\*farna- m. 'fern' - OE /earn, fern m. 'id.', E fern, OS farn m./n. 'id.', MDu. varen m. 'id.', Du. varen c. 'id.', OHG farn, farm m./n. 'id.', G Fam m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ptorH-no- (IE) - Skt paf\la- n. 'feather, leaf, Av. parana- n. 'feather, wing' <

<!-- pdf-page: 170; section: dictionary -->

130 \*farwa- \*ptorH-no-; Gr. rrn:pi.c; 'fern' < \*pterH-i-; Lith. papartis 'id.', Ru. paporot' 'id.' < \*po-ptorH-ti-; Gaul. ratis, Mir. raith f., W rhedyn pl. 'id.' < \*ptrH-ti-. A no-stem derived from an IE element \*pterH- that could be related to \*fefm5- 'feather' (q.v.). The initial cluster \*pt- was simplified to \*p- in all languages except Greek.

\*farwa- adj. 'colorful' - OHG faro adj. 'id.', MHG vare adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*porli-u6- (IE) - Skt. pffoi- adj. 'spotted, varicolor' < \*prli-ni-; Gr. nEpKVoc; adj. 'dark-spotted' < \*perli-no-; Mir. ere adj. 'spotted, red', MW erch adj. 'spotted" < \*perli-o-. An adjective derived from the PIE root \*perli- 'spotted' with the suffix -uo-, which is common in chromonyms. Also cf. OHG farawa, G Farbe, MLG varwe, verwe f. 'color', MDu. varuwe, verwe f. 'color, paint', Du. verf c. 'paint' < \*farw6- and OFri.ferwen adj. 'colorful' < \*farwina-.

\*farza(n)- m. 'young bull' - ON farri m. 'bull', OE fearr m. 'bull, ox', MDu. var(r)e m. 'young bull', Du. var c. 'id.', OHG farro m. 'bull, ox', G Farre m. 'young bull' ⇒ \*pors-6(n)- (EUR). An n-stem possibly created to the adj. E farrow, WFri. fear, Du. vaar adj. 'without calf < \*far(z)wa-. The underlying root \*pors- can be an extension of \*por- 'to be with young', cf. Lat. pari6 'to give birth to, bear' < \*pr-ie-, Lith. perhi 'to brood, sit on eggs' < \*per-eh₁- and - within Germanic - \*fora- 'piglet'. Also cf. the feminine MHG verse, G Fiirse, MLG verse, MDu. vaerse, ve(e)rse f., Du. vaars c. 'heifer' < \*farsj6- < \*p6rs-ieh₂-.

\*fasa/iin- m./f. 'fringe; fiber' - OE /res n. 'fringe' ( < \*fasa-), MDu. vase f. 'fiber; fringe', OHG Jaso m., fasa f. 'fiber, fringe, thread', MHG vase m./f. 'id.', G Faser m. 'fiber' (DRV?). An etymologically difficult word. It has been compared to Ru. pcismo n. 'string' < \*poHs-mo-(?), thus arriving at a Pre-Gm. formation \*p!Js-on-, but within Germanic, \*fasa/6n- can hardly be separated from \*fes6n- ( < \*fis6n-), which developed from the PIE root \*tpis-, cf. Gr. nT[cmw 'to pound or grind corn in a mortar'. I therefore assume that \*fasa/6n- was created on the basis of an unattested tudciti-verb \*fisan- < \*tpis-e- that was transferred to the class 4/5 strong verbs after a-mutation had taken place. For a similar derivational history, cf. \*hrepan-, \*stekan- and \*swepan- which, too, seem to be originally zero-grade presents. In view of the secondary iteratives \*hrutt6n(see \*hrudjan-) and \*stuk(k}6n- belonging to these verbs, it is furthermore likely that the postulated \*fesan- gave rise to a formation \*fus(s}6n-, as implied by G fus(s )en 'to fray' (cf. G Rhnl. fuse/ 'fuzz, frills; drizzle'), E to fuzz 'to ravel out; to cover with particles; to blur', to which also MLG vossen 'with loose fur' is related. Also cf. ON f9sull m. 'thread' < \*fasula-.

<!-- pdf-page: 171; section: dictionary -->

\*fastu- 131

\*fastu- adj. 'firm, solid' - ON fastr adj. 'id.', Far.fastur adj. 'id.', Elfd. fast adj. 'id.', OE fæst adj. 'id.', E fast, OFri.fest adj. 'id.', OS fast,Jesti adj. 'id.', Du. vast adj. 'id.', OHG festi adj. 'id.', G fest adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ph₂sth₂-o- (IE) - Arm. hast adj. 'firm, steady, standing still, tough' < \*pHst-i-. A u-stem (remodeled into a ja-stem in WGm.) directly related to Arm. hast. The combination of these forms straightforwardly points to a PIE root \*pl,;lst-; the reconstruction \*post- is impossible, because this would have given Arm. \*\*host (note that the connection with Skt. pastya- 'homestead' < \*h₂po-sth₂-io-(?) therefore must be abandoned). I derive this root from \*phiO-sth₂-, i.e. a compound of \*pehiB- 'to become firm' (cf. Gr. rn'JyvuμL 'to fasten, fix, make solid') and \*steh₂- 'to stand'. Apparently, the g was lost in the heavy cluster \*ph₂gsth₂-. Within Germanic, also cf. Go. fastan, ON fasta, OE fastian, OFri. festia, OHG fasten w.v. 'to fast' < \*fasten- and the derived Go.fastubni, OS fastunnia f. 'fasting' < \*fast-umnjo-.

\*fata- n. 'vat, vessel' - ON fat n. 'id.', OE fæt n. 'id.', E vat, OS fat n. 'id.', Du. vat n. 'id.', OHG faz n. 'id.', MHG vaz n. 'id.', G Fafi n. 'id.' ⇒ \*podo- (NIE) - Lith. puodas 'pot'. The Lithuanian word is identical to PGm. \*fata-. These forms are reminiscent of ON pottr, OE, OFri. pot 'pot', which, however, have been explained as loanwords from Fr. pot ( < VLat. pottus < Lat. potus 'drinking cup'). Fi. pa ta, Hung. fazek 'pot' < PFU \*pata has been analyzed as a loanword from PIE (Parpola/Carpelan 2007: 122), but it is highly unlikely, in view of the limitation to Baltic and Germanic, that the word was inherited from the parent language.

\*fatta- adj. 'bent' - ON fattr adj. 'bowed backward' ⇒ \*pt-no-(?) (IE?) - To Lat. pandus 'bent' < \*pt-n6-? The Germanic word has been compared to Lat. pandus, which developed from \*pt-n6- through a form \*pat-no- with schwa secundum that underwent Thurneysen's law (\*-tn- > \*-nd-). However, the comparison can only be maintained by assuming a similar schwa secundum in Germanic, for which the evidence is extremely limited (see \*hesan- - \*hazan- 'hare').

\*fat(t)on- w.v. 'to fetch' - ON fa ta w.v. 'to step', Ice!. fatta w.v. 'to catch on, take in', Far. fatta (in: lukki fatti teg! 'may you prosper') w.v. 'to catch; grasp', OE fatian w.v. 'to fetch', OFri. fatia w.v. 'to load', MLG vaten w.v. 'to grasp, pack, load, prepare, dress', MDu. vaten, vatten w.v. 'to seize, assume, understand', Du. vatten, dial. vaten w.v. 'to seize, understand', OHG fazzon w.v. 'to load, pack, prepare', G /assen w.v. 'to catch, grasp' ⇒ \*pod-neh₂(NEUR) - OCS po-pasti 'to fetch' < \*p6d-e-. An iterative created to a lost o-grade iterative-intensive verb \*fatan- < \*p6d-e-, as retrieved from OCS po-pasti.

<!-- pdf-page: 172; section: dictionary -->

132 \*fapma-

\*faþma- m. 'fathom' - ON faðmr m. 'id.', Far. favnur m. 'id.', Elfd. farm m. 'id.', OE fæþm m. 'fathom; bosom', E fathom, OFri. fethm m. 'id.', OS fathmos m.pl. 'two stretched arms', Du. vadem c. 'fathom', OHGfadam,fadum m. 'id.', G Faden m. 'thread' ⇒ \*pot(h₂)-mo- (WEUR) - Gael. aitheamh f. 'thread, fathom (of rope)', OW etem, W edau 'thread' < \*pet(h₂)-i-m-eh₂-. A Germanic-Celtic formation (cf. Hamp 2007) usually derived from the PIE root \*peth₂- 'to spread', cf. Gr. πετάννυμι. Further cf. MDu. vedemen w.v. 'to measure with outspread arms, fathom' < \*faþmjan- and OE f«!amian w.v. 'to fathom, to embrace', OHGfadamon w.v. 'to spin, to sew' < \*faþmōjan-.

\*faþō- f. 'fence' - Go. faþa f. 'hedge, fence, wall', MHG vade f. 'fence' ⇒ \*ph₂-teh₂-(?) (IE?). No accepted etymology. Perhaps from \*p!J.2-t-eh₂- to PIE \*peh₂-t- 'to protect, herd, graze' (see \*fOdjan-), or to Hitt pattar - pattan- n. 'basket' if the underlying root \*potH- originally designated 'wickerwork'. The variant MHG vatte seems to point to a geminated proto-form \*faþþōn-.

\*faujan- 1 w.v. 'to let rot' - ON feyja w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pouH-eie- (IE) - Skt. pilyati 'to become foul/putrid; to stink' < \*puH-ie-; Lith. pilti (puvu) 'to rot' < \*puH-. The causative to a PIE present \*puH-ie- 'to rot'. See also \*fUan- and \*fula-.

\*faujan- 2 w.v. 'to sift' - OHG fowen,fewen w.v. 'id.', MHG vewen w.v. 'id.' ⇒

\*pouh₂-eie- (IE) - Skt. puncfo 'to clean' < \*pu-n-eh₂-; Lat. purus 'clean', Olr. ur, w ir 'green, fresh' < \*puh₂-r-o- etc. An old causative to the PIE root \*peuh₂-.

\*fawa- adj. 'few' - Go. Jaus\* adj. 'id.', ON far adj. 'id.', Far. faur adj. 'id.', Elfd. /tier adj. 'id.', OE/ea adj. 'id.', E/ew, OS fa adj. 'id.', OHGfo adj. 'id.' ɢ \*ph₂u-o(EUR) - Gr. naupoc; adj. 'small, little, few', Lat. parvus adj. 'small' (with metathesis) < \*peh₂u-ro-; Lat. paucus adj. 'few, small in number' < \*peh₂u-llo-. The OHG formfoh has been adduced to reconstruct the Germanic form as \*fauha-, so as to equate it with Lat. paucus, but the h can be epenthetic in this form. This leaves \*fawa- as the only possible proto-form. It must be derived from a PIE zero-grade form \*p!J.2u-o-, with (regular) vocalization of the laryngeal, as the full-grade \*peh₂u-o- would have given \*\*Joa-.

\*fean- s.v. 'to blame' - Go. faian\* s.v. 'id.' ɢ \*pehre- (IE) - Gr. πῆμα n. 'harm' < \*peh₂-men- (also cf. am'Jμwv adj. 'unharmed' < \*n-peh₂-mon-). A strong verb related to \*fi(j)en- (q.v.). The Gothic form (attested only once as 3pl.pres.mid. faianda) is to be interpreted as fean, with t lowered from e in hiatus position; the digraph ai does not allow us to assume a j-suffix (cf. e.g. Seebold 1970: 188).

<!-- pdf-page: 173; section: dictionary -->

\*fedwar- 133

\*fedwar- num. 'four' - Go. fidwor num. 'id.', ON fj6rir, acc. fj6ra, f. fj6rar, n. fjpgur, gen. fjpgurra, dat. jj6rum num. 'id.', Far. JYra num. 'id.', Elfd. fiuorer, acc. fiuora, f.fiuorer, n.fiuorq, dat.fiuorum num. 'id.', OEfeower num. 'id.', E four, OFri. fiuwer, flower, fiawer num. 'id.', OS fiuwar, fior, n. fieri, dat. fiwarun num. 'id.', Du. vier num. 'id.', OHG fior num. 'id.', G vier num. 'id.' ⇒ \*kwet-uor- (IE) - ToA stwar, ToB stwer, Skt. catvaras, acc. caturas, n. catvari, YAv. ca8þaro, acc. caturq, Arm. c'or-k' < \*kwet-u(o)r-; Gr. -rfoaap£s, Dor. -rE-cop£s, Olr. ceth(a)ir, OW petguar, MW pedwar, Lith. keturi, acc. keturis, OCS cetyre, f./n. cetyri, Ru. cetjre, SCr. cetiri num. 'id.' < \*kwet-ur-; Lat. quattuor num. 'id.' < \*kwt-u6r(-h₂). The IE word for 'four' (with \*/- instead of \*hw- due to influence from \*ftmfe 'five'). The reconstruction of the Germanic paradigm is not entirely straightforward 1) because of the isolated position of Go. fidwor < \*fedwor, appa,rently continuing an old collective formation \*kwet-u6r(-h₂), and 2) because of the difficulties posed by the NWGm. forms. ON fj6rir, -ar, fjpgur continue \*fedur-aiz, \*-oz, \*-o through syncopated \*fjpareR, \*fjparaR, \*fjpauru: \*a was lost before r (causing lengthening of the preceding diphthong \*jp) in the masculine and feminine forms, while in the neuter, it changed into \*g due to the labial environment (as in ON jugr 'udder' < \*eudur-). In WGm., the material points to \*fewar-, but it is unclear how this form could have developed from \*fedwar- (PIE < \*kwet-uor-) in a regular way (for an attempt, cf. Stiles 1984). The first dental may have been dissimilated in the ordinal \*fewurp/dan- < quasi-PIE \*kwet-ur-tHo-, just as \*sebunp/dan- 'seventh' developed from \*septm-tHo-. Given the exclusively WGm. distribution of \*fewar-, it seems pointless, at any rate, to reconstruct a Pre-Gm. stem \*pekw6r- (cf. Ross/Berns 1992: 583). For the retention of the dental, cf. Go. fidur-falps adj. 'four-fold', OSw. ficeper-tiugher 'liable to forty (marks)', OE fyaer-fete 'with four feet' and probably also OLFra. (Sal.) fitter-thuschunde 'four thousand'. Despite all these formal problems, the Germanic evidence roughly appears to be in agreement with the other IE languages, which point to an inflected paradigm m. \*kwet-uor-es, acc. \*kwet-ur-ns (Skt. catvcfras, acc. catUras), n. \*kw(e)t-u6r (cf. Lat. quattuor).

\*fehan· s.v. 'to rejoice' - OE gefeon, -feohan s.v. 'to be glad, rejoice, exult', OHG gi-fehan s.v. 'to rejoice' ⇒ \*pel<-e- (IE). A not too well attested strong verb. Its past participle \*fagena- became lexicalized as ON feginn, Elfd. faingen, OE fcegen adj. 'happy', and served as the basis for Go. faginon, ON fagna, Elfd. faingen, OHG faginon, OE fcegenian 'to gladden' < \*fagenojan-. See also \*fehi- and \*fagjan-.

\*fehi· adj. 'satisfactory(?)' - Go. ga-fehaba adv. 'properly' (DRV). A V[ddhi-gerundive to the strong verb \*fehan-.

<!-- pdf-page: 174; section: dictionary -->

134 \*fehtan-

\*fehtan- s.v. 'to fight' - OE feohtan s.v. 'id.', E to fight, OFri. fiuchta s.v. 'id.', Du. vechten s.v. 'id.', OHGfehtan s.v. 'id.', Gfechten s.v. 'id.' (GM). A verb assumed by many to be akin to Gr. rrEKTW 'to comb, card, shear', Lat pecto 'to pluck' < \*pe/{-t-e- (for which see \*fahti-), but the connection depends on the conjectural semantic shift from 'to pluck, pull hair' > 'to fight' (cf. LIV2: 467).

\*fehu- n. 'cattle' - Go. faihu n. 'movable goods, possessions, cattle', ON fe, gen. fjár n. 'cattle, property, money', Far. fre, gen. flggjar n. 'cattle', OE feoh, fioh n. 'cattle, property, money', Efee, OSfehu n. 'cattle; property', Du. vee n. 'cattle', OHG fihu n. 'cattle, animal, money', G Vieh n. 'cattle' ⇒ \*peK-u- (IE) - Skt. p<ifo-, pasri- m./n. 'cattle', Av. pasu- m. 'id.', Lat. pecu n. 'flock, herd' (-u < obi. \*-ou-, cf. genu 'knee' < \*gen-ou-), OPru. pecku, Lith. pekus m. 'cattle' < \*pel<-u-. An ancient PIE u-stem derived from the root \*pe/{- 'to pluck wool', for which see \*fahti- and \*fakk/gon-. The derivation of \*pel<-u- from this root is a clear semantic indication that the first livestock to be kept by the Indo-Europeans consisted of sheep, not cows.

\*fel(e)sa- m. 'mountain' - ON fell, fjall n. 'id.', Far. fjall n. 'id.', OS felis,filis, ft/is m. 'rock, stone', OHGfelis m. 'rock, cliff, MHG ve/s(e) m. 'id.', G Fe/sen m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pe/o/-es-o- (EUR) - Olr. ail f. 'boulder, rock grave-stone' < \*pl-s-i-; Gr. Hsch. rrE>J .. cx 'stone' < \*pe/-s-eh₂-. ON fell, fjall po ins to \*fel(V)sa- (with assimilation of the s and breaking of -e- to -ja-), but the WGm. attestations can go back to both \*felesa- or \*Jalisa-. The latter form is supported by OFr. falise, Fr. fa/aise, fa/oise, a Franconian loanword. The Swiss dialects that differentiate between high e from OHG \*iiz and low e from OHG e, cf. Jaun and Appenzellfe/sa, are ambiguous in this particular case, however, because the vowel may have arisen due to secondary raising of low to high e before \*i in the next syllable (Karsten 1903: 259). On the morphological level, the word looks like a thematicization of an old s-stem. The expected paradigm \*pe/-os, gen. \*pl-es-(o]s fails to account for the o-grade, however. Perhaps, we are dealing with an originally static s-stem, viz. nom. \*p6/-s, gen. \*pel-s-s.

\*felgo- f. 'rim ofa wheel' - OEfea/g f. 'id.', Efelly, MDu. velge, vellige f. 'felly; patella', Du. velg c. 'felly', OHG felga f. 'felly, hoop', G Felge f. 'felly' ⇒ \*pe/gh-eh₂- (NEUR) - Ru. p6loz m. 'runner, skid', Slov. pliiz m. 'plow sole; strip' < \*po/gh·o-. An etymologically ambiguous word possibly related to the aforementioned Slavic forms. Another possibility is to connect \*felhan- in the sense of 'to go'. The link with e.g. Skt. parfr<i- n. 'flank, side', NP pah/U 'side, rib', Oss.

<!-- pdf-page: 175; section: dictionary -->

\*felhan- 135 Jars 'side, flank' < Ilr. \*piircua- cannot be maintained in view of its evident relationship to OCS pn,si f.pl. 'breast' < \*prl{- (cf. Pokorny IEW: 820).

\*felhan- s.v. 'to hide' - Go. fi/han s.v. 'id.', ON feta s.v. 'to hide, conceal; to give in trust', OE felan, feolan, felgan s.v. 'to stick, adhere; to proceed, go, reach; to undergo', OS bi-felhan s.v. 'to entrust, bestow, command; to bury', Du. velen w.v. 'to suffer', OHG felhan, fel(a)han s.v. 'to trust, hand over' ⇒ \*pelk-e- (GM). Given the disparate semantics of the attestations it is difficult to get to an extra-Germanic etymology. The connection with OPru. pelkis 'cloak' is uncertain, at any rate, as the meaning 'to conceal' as well as 'to entrust, bestow, command' may have developed from 'to stow'. The meanings 'to go' and 'to suffer' as found in Old English and Dutch still remain problematic, and may point to an etymologically dissimilar strong verb related to \*fulgen- 'to follow' (q.v.).

\*fella- n. 'membrane, skin, hide' - Go. pruts-fill n. 'leprosy', ON fell, /jail n. 'skin', OSw. fire/ n. 'id.', Sw. fjiill n. 'id.', OE fell n. 'id.', E fell, OS fell n. 'skin, membrane', Du. vet n. 'id.', OHG fel n. 'skin, pelt', G Fell n. 'id.' ⇒ \*pe/-n6- (EUR) - Gr. m::Uopaq>o<; adj. 'sewing skins together' < \*pet-no-; Lat. pel/is f. 'skin' < \*pel-ni-; OCS pe/ena f. 'band for swathing children', Ru. pelend f. 'shroud, nappy', SCr. pe/ena f. 'bandage, shroud' < \*pel-en-eh₂-. All the other IE languages point to a suffix with a nasal, and it is therefore legitimate to similarly derive the Gm. form from \*pel-n6-. It is possible that this stem developed from the genitive of the mn-stem \*felman- 'membrane' in which the m was dissimilated, i.e. \*pe/-mn-6s, but not necessarily so.

\*felma(n)- m. 'membrane' - OE reger-felma m. 'skin of an egg', E film, MDu. velm, vilm m. 'film' ⇒ \*pel-mon- (EUR) - Gr. nO .. μa n. 'sole of the foot' < \*pet-men-. A considerably old mn-stem to the root of \*fella- 'skin' (q.v.). In Anglo-Frisian, the word seems to have been supplanted by the diminutive: OE ft/men n. 'skin, membrane, husk', OFri. fi/mene f. 'skin (on the knee?)' < \*felm-fna/6-.

\*felo- f. 'board' - ON fjp/ f. 'id.', Far. fjRJ/ f. 'id.', Elfd. fyol f. 'id.' ⇒ \*pe/-eh₂- (NEUR). An e-grade formation closely related to \*fa/an- (q.v.).

\*fetpa- m. 'field' - OSw. fiiilder m. 'isolated field', OE fe/d, feald m. 'field', E field, OFri.feld n. 'id.', OSfeld m. 'id.', Du. veld n. 'id.', OHGfeld n. 'id.', G Feld n. 'id.' ⇒ \*pe/th₂-0· (IE). Derived from the PIE root \*pelth₂· 'flat', for which see \*fuldo-.

<!-- pdf-page: 176; section: dictionary -->

136

\*felu- - \*fulu-

\*felu- - \*fulu- adj. 'much, many' - Go. filu adj. 'id.', ON fjpl- pref. 'id.', Far. fjflJI- pref. 'id.', OE Jela, Jeala, Jeola adj. 'id.', OFri. Jelo, Jele, Jule, Joie adj. 'id.', OS filu,filo adj. 'id.', MDu. veel adj. 'id.', OHG filu,filo adj. 'id.', G vie/ adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*pelh₁-u- (IE) - Skt. puni-, OAv. pouru-, YAv. pouru- adj. 'much, abundant', OP paruv adj. 'much, too much, very', NP firih comp. 'more', Oss. fyr /fur adj. 'much' < \*plh₁-u-; Gr. noA.uc; adj. 'much, many, often' < \*polh₁-u-; Olr. ii adj. 'many' < \*pelh₁-u-. A u-stem to the root \*p/ehi- 'to fill', for which cf. \*Julia- 1 'full' < \*plhi-n6-. In Proto-Germanic the adjective may originally only have occurred in the neuter form \*Jelu, taking the following noun in the genitive. In PIE, it had an ablauting paradigm, e.g. common gender \*pelh₁-us, gen. \*plh₁-u-6s vs. neuter \*p6lhi-u, gen. \*plh₁-eu-s. This paradigm explains the ablaut difference between e.g. Gr. noft.U and Go. filu. Notably, Old Frisian Jule, fole and Du. dial. veul together with Ilr. \*prHu- appear to preserve the zero-grade root (Bremmer 2005). See also \*jlaizan-.

\*felwo- f. 'willow' - OHG Jelwa,felawa f. 'id.', G Fe/be f. 'id.' ⇒ \*tpel-u-eh₂-(?) (EUR) - Gr. 1tTEAfo f. 'elm tree' ( = Lat. tilia f. 'lime tree'?) < \*tpel-eu-eh₂-, Arm. t'eli 'id.' < \*tpel-ieh₂-(?). A European word with a remarkable reminiscence in Oss. Jcerv / Jarwe 'alder' (Hiibschmann 1887: 65). The different formations may all go back to an /-stem, e.g. \*tp-el, gen. \*tp-l-6s, acc. \*tp-el-m. The appurtenance of Ru. topol' m. 'poplar', which theoretically can be adduced to reconstruct an ablauting proto-form < \*top-I-, remains uncertain, as this word is likely to have been derived from Ru. top' 'swamp' (Falileyev /Owen 1999).

\*fergunja- n. 'mountain' - Go. Jairguni n. 'mountain range', ON fjprgyn f. 'Mother Earth', OEfirgen n. 'mountain', OHG Firgunnea f. 'Ore Mountains' ⇒ \*perkw-n-ieh₂- (IE). An etymologically debated word. The original meaning may have been 'mountainous forest' (cf. Celt. Lat. Hercynia Silva), and in that case, the word can be compared to \*Jerhwa- 1 'oak' and \*furhwiin- 'fir'. Within Germanic, however, the word can also be analyzed as the feminine equivalent of \*ferga- 'god', cf. ON fjprg n.pl. 'gods' with the feminine unjo-suffix. If so, the proto-form is to be reconstructed as \*perJc<wl-n-ieh₂-, which precludes the direct linkage with Lith. perkunas, Latv. pҊrkuons m. 'thunder' < \*perkw-u-Hn-o- (also cf. Ru. perun 'thunderbolt'). For the latter, cf. Ice!. fjarg-veour n. 'storm'.

\*ferhwa- 1 m./n. 'oak(?)' - ON poet. fjprr m. 'tree', OHG fereh-eih f. 'id.', G Ferch n. 'id.' ⇒ \*perJ<w-o- (WEUR) - Lat. quercus f. 'oak-tree' < \*perJ<w-u-; Olr. ceirt 'apple-tree', W perth 'bush' < \*perkʷ-t-?

<!-- pdf-page: 177; section: dictionary -->

\*ferhwa- 2 137 Also cf. Lomb.fereha 'oak' < \*ferh{w)o-. Some further connect \*fergunja'mountain' (q.v.).

\*ferhwa- 2 n. 'life, essence' - Go. fairluus m. 'world', ON f]pr n. 'life, pith', OE feorh m./n. 'life; man', OFri. ferech n.(?) 'life', OS ferah, ferh n. 'life, spirit', OHG ferah,ferh n. 'id.', MHG verch n. 'id.' ⇒ \*perkw-o- (EUR). Possibly related to \*ferhwa- 1 'oak', as the oak-tree symbolized vitality in Germanic mythology.

\*ferrai adv. 'far' - Go.fairra adv. 'id.', ONj]arri adv. 'id.', OE/eorr adv. 'id.', Efar, OS ferro adv. 'id.', Du. ver adv. 'id.', OHG/erro adv. 'id.', MHG verre adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*per-n-oi- (IE). A locative adverb originally ending in \*-noi. Cf. \*ferrjan-.

\*ferrjan- w.v. 'to be far; to remove' - ON firra w.v. 'to lose sight of; to deprive of, OFri.firia w.v. 'to be far away', OHGfirren w.v. 'to remove; to be far away', MHG virren w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The related OE firsian w.v. 'to go beyond, remove' derives from \*ferrisojan- with the same suffix as in e.g. irsian w.v. 'to be angry' (see \*erzja-).

\*fersno- f. 'heel' - Go. fairzna f. 'id.', OE ftersn f. 'id.' ( < \*fersni-), MDu. versene, verse f. 'id.', OHG fersana f. 'id.', G Fersne f. 'id.' ⇒ \*tpers-neh₂- (IE) - Hitt. par5na- c. 'heel(?)' (cf. par5nae-zi 'to squat down, crouch') < \*tper-sno-; YAv. pii5nii- f. 'heel', Gr. rri'.:pvri f. 'id.', Lat. perna f. '(upper) leg, thigh' < \*tper-sneh₂-; Skt pārṣṇi- f. 'id.' < \*tper-sni-. The IE word for 'heel'. The intra-Germanic difference between Go. fairzna, OHG fersana < \*tper-sneh₂- and OE fiersn < \*tper-sni- is neatly mirrored by Gr. rrtpvf], Lat. perna and Skt. pdr$ɣi-. The root \*tper- may have developed from \*pd-sperH- "to kick with the foot" (Lubotsky 2006), for which see \*Jot- and \*spurnan-.

\*fertan- - \*fretan- s.v. 'to fart' - ON freta s.v. 'id.', Far.freta w.v. 'id.', OSw. ficerta w.v. 'id.', Sw. j]iirta w.v. 'id.', Da.fjcerte w.v. 'id.', OE feortan s.v. 'id.', E to fart, MDu. verten, vorten s.v. 'id.', OHG ferzan s.v. 'id.', MHG verzen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pred-e- (IE) - Skt. párdate 'to fart', YAv. paraoan 3pl.pres. 'id.', Gr. ni'.:pow, ntpooμm 'id.', Alb. pjerdh 'id.' < \*perd-e-; Ru. perdet', SCr. pfdjeti 'id.' < \*prd-eh₁-; Lith. persti (perdiiu, perdiiau) 'id.' < \*perd-ie-. An onomatopoetic formation from PIE times.

\*ferudi adv. 'last year' - ON f fjpro 'id.', Far. f fjrJr adj. 'id.', Nw. i fjor adj. 'id.', Elfd. ifiuord adj. 'id.', MHG vert adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*per-ut-i (IE) - Identical to Skt. p<irut, Gr. ntpuu1, Olr. inn-uraid, Arm. heru adv. 'id.' < \*per-ut(-i): a petrified

<!-- pdf-page: 178; section: dictionary -->

138 \*fesonlocative of PIE \*uet- 'year', cf. Hitt. !!-itt- c. 'year' < \*uet-; Gr. fro<; n. 'id.', Alb. vjet, vit m. 'id.' < \*uet-es-. A fossilized PIE adverbial phrase meaning 'last year'. It contains the original locative of a PIE root noun \*uet- 'year', for which cf. \*wej:>ru-.

\*feson- f. 'chaff, fiber' - OS fesa f. 'pod'. MDu. vese f. 'fiber, husk; fringe', Du. vezel c. 'fiber', OHG fesa f. 'id.', MHG vese f. 'id.', G Swi. Rhntl. feasa f. 'id.' (IE) - Skt. pináṣṭi, piṃṣánti 'to crush', Lat pinsere 'id.' < \*tpi-n-(e]s-; Gr. πτίσσω 'to pound or grind corn in a mortar' < \*tpis-ie-; Gr. πτισάνη 'hulled barley' < \*tpis-n[e]h₂-; Lith. piestas, Ru. pest m. 'pestle' < \*tpois-to-; Lith. paisjti 'to peel barley' < \*tpois-eie-; Ru. psen6 n. 'millet', SCr. psena f. 'clover' < \*tpis-en-o/eh₂-. OHG fesa and MHG vese must have had real e rather than umlauted a in view of Swi. Rhntl. feasa (with ea < OHG e). This means that, if the connection with PIE \*tpis- is correct (esp. note Ice!. fis n. 'fluff, chaff), a-mutation must have lowered \*i to \*e. The collective OHG ga-vissa - ga-bissa f. 'chaff < quasi-PIE \*kom-tpis-io- shows a remarkable Verner alternation similar to the doublet OHG mezzisahs - mezzirahs 'knife' (for which see \*sahsa-). See also \*fasa/on- 'fringe, fiber'.

\*fetan- s.v. 'to go; to fall' - ON feta s.v. 'to step', Far. feta w.v. 'to stride, pace, step out', OE fetan s.v. 'to fall'. OHG fezzan, gijezzan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pM-e(IE) - Skt. padyati 'to move, to go; to fall' < \*ped-ie-; OCS padati, Ru. padat' (padaju), SCr. padati 'to fall' < \*pod-eh₂-ie-. An IE verbal formation related to \*Jot- 'foot'. See also the pertaining iterative \*futon-.

\*fetura/o- m./f. 'fetter' - ON fjpturr m. 'id.', Far. /jldtur n. 'id.', OSw. ji<l!tur m. 'id.', OE feter. fetor f. 'id.', E fetter, OS feteros m.pl. 'id.', Du. veter c. 'shoelace', OHG vezzera f. 'fetter', MHG vezzer f. 'fetter; manacle' ⇒ \*ped-ur-o/eh₂- (IE). Derived from the PIE word for 'foot' (see \*Jot-), cf. Gr. n:E:Sri f. 'fetter', Lat pedica f. 'id.'.

\*fepan- m. 'penis(?)' - MDu. vede m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pet-on- (WEUR?) - Lat penis m. 'tail, penis' < \*pet-sn-i-. The Latin word is usually derived from \*pes-ni- in view of Skt. pasas-, Gr. n:fo<; n. 'penis' < \*pes-os-, but \*pet-sni- is possible, too (cf. De Vaan 2008: 458). As a result, MDu. vede (for which cf. Gysseling 1987: 53) receives a possible etymology. See also \*fu/:>i- 'vagina'.

\*fepro- f. 'feather' - ON fjpar f. 'id.', Far. fjeaur f. 'feather; fin', Elfd. fjaaer f. 'feather', OE feoer f. 'id.', E feather, OS fethera f. 'id.', Du. veer c. 'id.', OHG

<!-- pdf-page: 179; section: dictionary -->

\*feuhtjon- 139 fedara, federa f. 'id.', G Feder f. 'id.' ⇒ \*peth₂-r/n- (IE) - Hitt. pattar pattan-, pettar - pettan- n. 'feather, wing' < \*peth₂-r, \*pth₂-en-s; Skt patra- n. 'wing, feather' < \*peth₂-ro-; Gr. m£pov n. 'wing' < \*pt(h₂)-er-o-; Lat penna f. 'feather' < \*peth₂-n-ehr; OW atar pl. 'bird', MW adar 'id.' < \*pthrr-, Olr. en 'bird', W edn 'id.' < \*pethrno-. A heteroclitic derived from the PIE root \*peth₂- 'to fly', cf. Skt patati 'to fly; hit', Gr. nfroμm 'to fly', Lat peto, -ere 'to reach; to rush', MW eh-ed- 'to fly' and the intensive Gr. no-raoμm 'to fly around' < \*poth₂-eie-. Also cf. the collective ja-stem ON fiori, OE fioere, Du. ge-vederte, MHG ge-videre, G Ge-fteder n. 'plumage' < \*gajeprja-.

\*feuhtjon- f. 'spruce' - OHG ftuhta f. 'id.', MHG viehte f. 'id.', G Fichte f. 'id.' ¸

\*peul<-t-ih₂- (IE) - Waigali puc, piic 'pine' < \*peul<-i-; Gr. nEuKTJ f. 'spruce' < \*peul<-eh₂-; OPru. peuse 'id.' < \*peul<-ieh₂-; Lith. pusis f. 'id.' < \*pul<-i-. A word with a largely European distribution. In view of Waigali put, piic, however, the word must also have existed in Iranian (Morgenstierne 1945: 229). From there, it probably spread into Armenian, where it surfaces as the second member of the compound t'ef-aws 'holm oak, evergreen oak; pine' (Olsen 2012). For the first element, see Arm. t'efi 'elm' under \*felwo-.

\*feukan- - \*fikan- s.v. 'to blow' - ON Jjuka s.v. 'to be blown away; to fly off, Far. fika s.v. 'to drift; to dash, dart; to be blown away; to be rumored', EDu. ftjcken.fuycken 'to thrust', G fauchen w.v. 'to hiss, snarl' (ORV). In view of the root-final k and the alternation between \*eu and \*ii, it seems likely that the strong verb was back-formed to the iterative \*fuk(k)on- (q.v.). Also cf. MDu.fuke f. 'fish trap', Du.fuik c. 'id.' < \*fikon-.

\*fewurþ/dan- num. 'fourth' - ON fj6roi num. 'id.', Far. fj6roi num. 'id.', Elfd. ftuord num. 'id.', OE feoroa, feoweroa num. 'id.', E fourth, OFri. fiord a, ftiirda, fterda num. 'id.', OS fiortho num. 'id.', Du. vierde num. 'id.', OHG ftordo num. 'id.', G vierte num. 'id.' ⇒ \*kwet-ur-to- (IE) - Skt tur{ya-, turya-, Av. tiiiriianum. 'fourth' < \*kwt-ur-iHo-; Lat quiirtus < \*kwtur-tHo- (for expected \*quortus); OW petuerid num. 'id.' < \*kwet-ur-iHo-; ToA start, ToB starte, Gr. -rfrapToc;, Hom. also -rfrparnc;, Skt. caturthci-, Lith. ketviftas, Latv. cetuf'tais, OCS cetvrbt'b, Ru. cetvertyj, SCr. cetvfti num. 'id.' < \*kwet-ur-tHo-; Arm. c'or-ir num. 'id.' < \*kwet-or-. The ordinal to \*fedwar- 'four' (q.v.). In PIE, the root had the zero grade, but since \*kwtur-t6- in PGm. would have given \*\*hurda-, it was bound to become replaced by an analogical full-grade form. This \*kweturtHo- lost its first t due to dissimilation, and thus gave PGm. \*fewurp/dan- (with initial / after \*fimftan- 'fifth' (q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 180; section: dictionary -->

140 \*fifaldra/o-

\*fifaldra/o- m./f. 'butterfly' - Far. jirvaldur m. 'id.', OSw. jicedhal m. 'id.', Sw. fjaril, dial. fjaralder c. 'id.', Elfd. fyorolder m. 'id.', OE fifea/de f. 'id.', OS fifoldara f. 'id.', MDu. vivalter, viveltere m./f. 'id.', OHG fivaltra f. 'id.', G Feifalter m./f. 'id.' ⇒ \*p(e)i-pol- (EUR) - Lat. papili6, -6nis m. 'butterfly'; OPru. pepelis 'bird'; OPru. penpalo, Lith. pfepala, Latv. pafpala, Ru. perepel 'quail'. A reduplicated formation, probably to be reconstructed as \*/f-faldra/6- or \*fe{aldra/6- (cf. OSw. jicedhal m. 'id.' < ON \*fjaoraldr < \*fjafraldr < \*fefaldra-). Also cf. the neuter ON ftfrildi, lcel. jiorildi, Nw. dial. jivrelde n. 'butterfly' < \*jifraldjan- ( < \*fifaldrjan-). The formation has some parallels in Latin and Balto-Slavic, but the material is severely distorted and cannot be reconciled into one single proto-form.

\*filj)and- m. 'enemy' - Go. fijands m. 'id.', ON Jjandi m. 'id.', Far. ftggindi m. 'id.', OE feond m. 'id.', E fiend, OFri. jiand,fil nd, fin(d) m. 'id.', ODu. fiunt m. 'id.', Du. vijand c. 'id.', OHG fiant m. 'id.', G Feind m. 'id.' ⇒ \*phri-ont- (DRV). A lexicalized participle derived from \*jilj)en- (q.v.).

\*fllj)en- w.v. 'to hate' - Go. fijan, jian w.v. 'id.', ON Jja w.v. 'id.', Far. ftggja w.v. 'id.', OE feon w.v. 'id.', OHG fien w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*phri-ehr (IE) - Skt. pfyati 'to blame'; Skt. pfyu- 'reviler' < \*ph₁-i-u-. A stative verb of clear Indo-European origin. The root \*phrei- arose as an i-present to \*pehr, which in Germanic can be retrieved from \*fean- (q.v.). See also \*jilj)and-.

\*fimfe num. 'five' - Go.fimfnum. 'id.', ON fimm num. 'id.', OEfifnum. 'id.', E five, OFri.fifnum. 'id.', OSfifnum. 'id.', Du. vijfnum. 'id.', OHGjim/num. 'id.', G finf num. 'id.' ⇒ \*penkʷe (IE) - Hitt. panza-, ToA pan, ToB pis, Skt. pafica, YAv. pal)ca, MP pan], Oss. fondz, Arm. hing, Alb. pese / pese, Gr. n£vrE, Lat. qufnque, Olr. c6ic, MW pymp, W pimp, Bret. pemp num. 'five'. Germanic has \*fimfe for expected \*finhwe. This is due to a sporadic change of PIE \*kw to Pre-Gm. \*p after resonants in words starting with a labial, cf. \*wulfa- 'wolf < \*ufkw-o-.

\*fimftan- num. 'fifth' - Go. fimfta num. 'id.', ON jimti, femti num. 'id.', Far. fimti num. 'id.', Elfd. femt num. 'id.', OE fifta num. 'id.', E fifth, OFri. fifta num. 'id.', OS fifto num. 'id.', Du. vijfde num. 'id.', OHG jimto, finfto num. 'Id.', G fiinfte num. 'id.' ⇒ \*penkʷ-t6- (IE) - Skt. pakthci-, YAv. puxoa (with -u- from Plr. \*5ustHa- 'sixth') num. 'fifth' < \*pnkw-tHo-; ToA pant, ToB pirikte, Lat. qufntus, Gr. n£μmoc;, Lith. pefiktas, Latv. piekts, OPru. piencts, OCS pF;tb, Ru. pjatyj, SCr. petf, Alb. peste num. 'id.' < \*penJ<w-tHo-; Gaul. ponpetos, Olr. c6iced, OW pimphet num. 'id.' < \*penkwe-tHo-.

<!-- pdf-page: 181; section: dictionary -->

\*fingra- 141 The ordinal number pertaining to \*fimJe. In PIE, the formation originally had a zero grade, viz. \*pnJ<w-tH6-, as can still be observed in Indo-Iranian. In Germanic, it was replaced by the full-grade root of the cardinal.

\*fingra· m. 'finger' - Go. figgrs m. 'id.', ON fingr m. 'id.', OE finger m. 'id.', E finger, OFri. finger m. 'id.', OS fingar m. 'id.', Du. vinger c. 'id.', OHG fingar m. 'id.', G Finger m. 'id.' ⇒ \*penk.W-r6- (IE). A ro-stem usually assumed to be derived from the numeral \*fimJe 'five' < PIE \*penk.W-e. The labialization of the \*kw was lost before the operation of Verner's law.

\*finkan· s.v. 'to sparkle, burn' - MDu. vinken s./w.v. 'to sparkle; to smol· der', G obs.finkeln w.v. 'to burn, hurt' (DRY). As the alternation of the pertaining causative \*Jangjan- - \*Jankjanshows, the root-final \*k of this strong verb must be secondary, continuing a (shortened) geminate that spread from the iterative \*funkon- (q.v.). Also cf. the deverbal nouns MHG vine m. 'spark' < \*finka- and MHG vanke m. 'id.' < \*Jankan-.

\*finkja(n)· m. 'finch' - OE fine m. 'id.', E finch, MDu. vink(e) m./f. 'id.', Du. vink c. 'id.', OHG finko m. 'id.', G Fink m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ping-o- (EUR?) - Gr. Hsch. rr(yya 'young bird' < \*ping-eh₂-(?). An exclusively Germanic word. Formally close to \*spinka(n)- ( q.v.). \*fin(n)on- f. OSw. fina f. 'fin', Sw. Jena c. 'fin; chaff, husk', MDu. vinne f. 'fin; wing; prickle; awn' ⇒ \*pin-(m)on- (WEUR?) - Olr. ind, inn n. 'end, extremity, tip, point, edge, surface' < \*pinno-(?). The alternation between ON fina < \*finon- and WGm. \*finno points to an n-stem \*Jina, gen. \*finnaz < \*pin-on, \*pin-n-6s. The variant MDu., EDu. vimme 'fin; prickle, awn' can furthermore be adduced to reconstruct a more primary mn-stem \*fimmo, \*finnaz < \*pfn-mon, \*pin-mn-6s (with loss of m in the cases with zero grade of the suffix), but this vimme may also have arisen secondarily by assimilation of the root-final nasal to the initial J (cf. Du. priem < \*preuna-). No generally accepted etymology. The link with Olr. ind 'extremity' is attractive, not least in view of the semantics of Sw. Jena 'chaff, hus\c' and MDu. vine 'bran(?)', which could have developed from 'awn'. Problematic, on the other hand, is the connection with Lat pinna 'feather, wing, fin, parapet', which does not continue alleged \*pinn-ehr, but represents a dialect form of penna 'pen' < \*pet-neh₂- (De Vaan 2008: 458). Unlikely, too, are the connections with e.g. Skt. sphya- m. 'stick' and Lat. spfna 'spine, thorn', which unlike the Germanic forms have an initial s.

<!-- pdf-page: 182; section: dictionary -->

142 \*finj:>an-

\*finþan- s.v. 'to find; to feel' - Go. finþan s.v. 'id.', ON finna s.v. 'id.', OE findan s.v. 'id.', E to find, OFri. finda s.v. 'id.', OS fidan, findan s.v. 'id.', ODu. findan s.v. 'id.', Du. vinden s.v. 'id.', OHG findan s.v. 'id.', G finden s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pent-e- (IE) - Skt. pánthās, gen. path6s m. 'road, path, course'. YAv. pantā, OAv. abl. paθō m. 'road, path'. MP pand 'path', Oss. frendreg 'id.', Gr. rra-roc:; m. 'road'. rr6v-roc:; m. 'sea', Arm. hun, gen. hni 'ford', Lat pons m. 'bridge, path'. OCS pptb m. 'road', OPru. pintis 'id.' < PIE \*p6nt-eh₁-s, gen. \*pnt-h₁-6s. See also \*fanþjan-, \*fandon-, \*fundon- and \*paþa-.

\*fisan- s.v. 'to blow' - ON ffsa s.v. 'id.', Icel. ffsa w.v. 'to blow (on a fire); to fart'. Far.jfsa w.v. 'to blow, draw; to get to fly, stir up; to hiss; to snort'. Elfd. faisa s.v. 'to fart', MHG vfsen s.v. 'to fart' ⇒ \*peis-e-(?) (WEUR) - Lat. splro, -are 'to breathe' < \*speis-eh₂-ie-; W fftln 'breath' < \*spoi-n-eh₂-. An originally onomatopoetic verb related to \*fison- (q.v.). The semantic spectrum ranges between 'to blow', 'to hiss'. 'to whisper' and 'to fart'. If Lat. spfrare is related, the PIE form of the verb may have been \*psei-s-e-; normally, the cluster \*ps- would develop into Gm. \*sp- (cf. \*stelan- 'to sneak; to steal' < \*tsel-e-), but in this case, the s may have been lost due to dissimilation.

\*fiska- m. 'fish' - Go. fisks m. 'id.', ON fiskr m. 'id.', Far. fiskur m. 'id.', Elfd. fisk m. 'id.', OE fisc m. 'id.', E fish, OFri. fisk m. 'id.', OS fisk m. 'id.', Du. vis c. 'id.', OHG fisc m. 'id.', G Fisch m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pisk-o- (WEUR) - Lat. piscis m. 'fish' < \*pisk-i-; Olr. [asc m. 'id.' < \*peisk-o-. A West Indo-European word. The connection with Skt. piccha- 'calf of the leg' < \*pili-sk-eh₂- (Sadovsky 1973: 85-6), perhaps justified by the widespread folk association of the calf of the leg with the belly of a fish (Mallory/ Adams 2006: 146) is highly uncertain. Slightly more attractive, then, is the comparison with Skt. piccha- n. 'feather'. Cz. pisk 'young feather'. The alleged cognate Ru. piskar' 'gudgeon', on the other hand, was derived from a verbal root, cf. OCS pi ska ti 'to pipe', Ru. piscat' 'to squeak' (cf. Hamp 1973b ), and therefore cannot be related. Also cf. Go. fiskon, ON fiska, OE fiscian, fixian, OFri. fiskia, Du. vissen, OHG fiskon, G fischen w.v. 'to fish' < \*fiskojan-, which is formally close to Lat. piscor, -ari < \*pisk-eh₂-ie-.

\*fison- w.v. 'to blow' - Nw. fisa w.v. 'to blow; to fart; to whisper', MDu. vesen w.v. 'to whisper' (ORV). The iterative pertaining to the strong verb \*jisan- ( q.v.).

\*fitnan- w.v. 'to become fat' - ON fitna w.v. 'id.', Far.fitna w.v. 'id.' (ORV). An inchoative verb derived from \*faita- (q.v.). With the same ablaut, cf. ON fita f., Far. fiti f. 'fat' < \*fiton-, \*-fn-, whence ON, Far. fita w.v. 'to make fat' < \*fitojan-.

<!-- pdf-page: 183; section: dictionary -->

\*flahan- 143

\*Jlahan· s.v. 'to flay, strip' - ON fla s.v. 'to flay; to strip (of clothes)', Elfd. fla w.v. 'id.', OE f/ean s.v. 'to flay', MDu. vlaen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pl6k-e- (EUR) - Gr. n>.aſ, -aK6<; f. 'plane, plain; flat stone, board, table' < \*pJk-. An a-grade intensive verb related to \*flaho- and \*f/oho- ( q.v.).

\*jlahO· f. 'strip' - ON f/a f. 'strip of land, meadow' ⇒ \*pl6k-eh₂- (DRV) - Latv. plaka f. 'low lying place, plain' < \*plok-eh₂-. Together with Gr. nA.aø -aK6<; f. 'flat surface, board, slab', ON fla has been derived from a PIE root noun \*plak- (cf. Orel 2003: 104). However, the Greek alpha and the Gm. \*a have two entirely different origins, i.e. a vocalized \*/ and an \*o respectively. Also note that, in Old Norse, many monosyllabic o-stems acquired an analogical root-noun inflection, cf. t<i, pl. ter f. 'toe' < \*taihwo-. This taken into consideration, I derive the word from \*pl6k-eh₂-, which happens to be close to Latv. plaka. See also \*f/ahan-.

\*Jlahsa· n. 'flax' - OE f/eax n. 'id.', Ef/ax, OFri.f/ax n. 'id.', MLG vlas m. 'id.', Du. vlas n. 'id.', OHG f/ahs m. 'id.', G Flachs m. 'id.' (DRY). A thematicized s-stem usually derived from the PIE root \*plel<- 'to braid' (see \*flehtan-). It is equally attractive to connect the word to \*flakk/gon- 1 'to beat, thresh' (q.v.).

\*Jlaina· adj. 'flat(?)' - Nw. flein adj. 'bald, naked; flat' ⇒ \*ploi-no- (NEUR?) - Lith. plikas adj. 'bald', Latv. pliks adj. 'bare, bald' < \*pli-ko-; CS plesb adj. 'bald patch' < \*ploi-s-io-. A marginally attested adjective that is often connected to the aforementioned Balta-Slavic forms. The meaning 'bald' is probably secondary, however, as Nw. f/ein also means 'flat'. The straightforward connection with Elfd. f/ina w.v. 'to smile' < \*f/inon-, on the other hand, does seem to point to a primary meaning 'bare' (whence 'to bare the teeth'). Related to OSw.jlen n. 'tumor (of the skin)' and Nw.flein m. 'growth, wound'?

\*Jlaizan- comp. 'more' - Go.flaiza comp. 'id.', ONf/eiri comp. 'id.', Far.fleiri comp. 'id.', Elfd. f/ierer comp. 'id.' ⇒ \*pl6h₁-is-on- (IE) - Gr. nA.dwv comp. 'more' < \*pleh₂-is-on-; Lat plii.s, -ris comp. 'id.' < \*ploh₁-is-. The comparative of \*felu- \*fulu- (q.v.). The development of \*f/aizanfrom \*pl6h₁-is-on- proves that Verner's law preceded the shortening of the long diphthongs (Osthoff's law), as \*pl6ison- would have given \*\*flaisan-. For a similar scenario, cf. \*maizan- 'more' < \*meh₂-is-on- and \*winda- 'wind' < \*h₂uehi-ent-o-.

\*Jlaka- adj. 'flat' - MDu. vlac adj. 'id.', Du. vlak adj. 'id.', OHG flah adj. 'id.', G flach adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*pl6k-o- (EUR) - Gr. nA.aſ, -aK6<; f. 'plane, plain; flat stone, board, table' < \*pJk-; Lith. pl<ikanas, Latv. plakans adj. 'flat' < \*plok-ono-.

<!-- pdf-page: 184; section: dictionary -->

144 \*flakk/g6n- 1 A West Germanic adjective, the derivational base of ablauting ON fl6ki, OE floe m. 'flounder' < \*floka[n)-. The cluster is usually compared to Lat. plaga f. 'net; expanse, territory' and Gr. rrA.ayLa n.pl. 'sides, flanks', but the Latin word cannot be derived from \*plog-eh₂- (nor from \*plg-eh₂- or \*plh₂-g-eh₂-), and the original meaning of the Greek root was 'oblique, bent', not 'flat', cf. Gr. rrA.ayw<; adj. 'oblique, sloping; crooked' < \*plg-io-. In addition, the Germanic material indicates that the original root was \*plok- rather than \*plog-, cf. ON flagna beside ON, Far. flakna w.v. 'to flake (off), split', lcel., Nw. flaga w.v. 'id.' and esp. lcel.flar, Nw.fld adj. 'flat, wide', Elfd.fld adj. 'flat; slanted' < \*flaha- < \*pl6k-o-. It follows that the root-final \*k of \*flaka- is a reduction of an original geminate, cf. MDu. vlacken w.v. 'to flatten' < \*flakkon-. See also \*flahan- 'to flay'.

\*flakk/gon- 1 w.v. 'to beat' - E to flack 'to beat with a flail', G flacken w.v. 'to beat wool' ⇒ \*plh₂k-neh₂- (EUR). An o-grade iterative, variant of the zero-grade E to flog ( < OE \*floggian) < \*fluggon-. Both formations appear to be (secondary) iteratives to \*flokan(q.v.), cf. OCS plakati 'to beat one's breast; to lament' < \*pleh₂k-eh₂-.

\*flakk/gon- 2 w.v. 'to flutter' - Nw.flaga w.v. 'to fluctuate', MEflakken w.v. 'to beat feebly, flutter', MDu. vlacken, vlaken w.v. 'to flame', Du. flakkeren w.v. 'to flutter', OHG flagaron w.v. 'to flutter', G Swi. flacken w.v. 'to flare' ⇒ \*plok-neh₂- (GM). An iterative that in spite of the dictionaries (Franck 1949: 166; Pokorny IEW: 832-3) cannot be equated with \*flakkon- 'to swerve'. It is more likely that the verb represents a (secondary?) a-grade variant of \*flukk/gi5n- 'to fly' (q.v.). Also cf. ON flaga f. 'quick raid', Far. flaga f. 'gust of wind', MHG vlage f. 'id.', Du. vlaag c. 'id.' < \*flagon- and OE flacor adj. 'flickering' < \*flakra-.

\*flakkon- w.v. 'to swerve' - ON flakka w.v. 'to rove about', Far. flakka w.v. 'to wander about, roam' ⇒ \*plofgLneh₂- (EUR) - Gr. rrA.a0w 'to make devious, repel, dissuade from the right path, bewilder', rrA.a0oμm 'to go astray, wander about' < \*p/fgl-ie-. An o-grade iterative. The Greek word is assumed to be without a good etymology by Beekes 2010: 1201, but the connection with PGm. \*flakkon- < \*p/ofgLneh₂- (cf. Torp 1909: 249) is formally and semantically unproblematic.

\*flata- adj. 'flat' - ON flatr adj. 'flat, level', OS flat adj. 'flat, shallow', MDu. vlat (infl. v/adde) adj. 'id.', OHG flaz adj. 'flat' (IE) - Skt. p[thu- adj. 'broad', Gr. nA.aru<; adj. 'flat' < \*plth₂-u-; Lith. platus adj. 'broad, wide' < \*ploth₂-u- (cf. Lith. plasti (p/antu) 'to broaden').

<!-- pdf-page: 185; section: dictionary -->

\*flatja- 145 Related to MLG vlot adj. 'flat' < \*flota-. The root،final t must be secondary from \*tt, as the word is likely to be related to Lith. platits. The original geminate is preserved by e.g. G Rhnl. Flatzen m. 'slab' < \*flattan- vs. OHG jlado, G Fladen, MDu. vlade, vlaey, Du. vlaai, ME flathe 'flat cake' < \*flapan-. See also \*flatja-.

\*Jlatja- n. 'floor' - ON jlet n. 'raised flooring', OE flett n. 'floor of a house, dwelling', Ejlat, OFri.jlet n.? 'house; home', OSflet n. 'hall, house', OHGjlezzi n. 'floor; flat area', MHG vletz(e) n. 'id.' (DRY). Aja-stem derived from \*flata- 'flat' (q.v.).

\*Jlaugjan- w.v. 'to let fly' - Go. us-jlaugjan\* w.v. 'id.', ON jleygja w.v. 'id.', MHG er-vlougen w.v. 'id.' (NEUR). The causative to \*fleugan- (q.v.).

\*Jlauho- f. 'flea' - ON jl6, pl.firer f. 'id.', OE jleah,flea f. 'id.', E flea, WFri. flie c. 'id.', MDu. vloo, vlo(o)y f. 'id.', Du. vlo c. 'id.', OHG jlo{h) m. 'id.', G Floh m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pl6uk-eh₂- (IE) - Skt. plu$i- m. 'flea', Sogd. 'f3S'h 'id.', Arm. lu 'id.' < \*plus-; Alb. plesht m. 'id.' < \*pious-; Gr. t!JUAA.a f. 'id.' < \*psul-; Lat. pulex 'id.' < \*pus/-; Lith. blusa f. 'id.', Rues bl'bxa, Ru. bloxci, Ser. buha f. 'id.' < \*blus-eh₂-. An inherited Indo-European word. Due to the many tabooistic distortions of the word in the different IE languages, it is difficult to reconstruct its original form.

\*Jlauja- n. 'ship' - ON jley n. 'id.', Far. poet. flay 'vessel, craft' ⇒ \*plou-io(IE) - Identical to Gr. nA.oTov n. 'ship'; further cf. ToB plewe m. 'id.', Gr. nA.60<;, nA.ou<; m. 'id.', Ru. plov m. 'ship, barge' < \*plouo-. Derived from the PIE verb \*pleu-e- 'to sail', for which see \*flauma- and \*floan-. The OE gloss floege 'navicula' appears to be a Norse loanword (Pons-Sanz 2000: 94-5).

\*Jlauma- m. 'stream' - ON jlaumr m. 'eddy' ⇒ \*plou-mo- (IE) - Skt. piavate 'to swim, flow', Gr. nA.Ew 'to travel by sea, sail, navigate', Lat. pluit, pluere 'to rain', oes pluti 'to flow, sail' < \*p/eu-e-; oes plaviti 'to let swim' < \*plou-eie-; Olr. 16 'water' < \*plou-o-(?). See also \*flauja- and \*floan-.

\*Jlauti- f. 'cream' - ON flautir f.pl. 'whipped milk', OE jlf{e)te f. 'cream' (DRY). See \*jleutan-.

\*Jlaupi- f. 'cross-beam' - ON jleyor f. 'id.' ⇒ \*plout-ih₂- (EUR) - Lith. p/aiitas, Latv. plduts m. 'shelf, sideboard', Ser. pluto n. 'flotsam' < \*plou-t6-;

<!-- pdf-page: 186; section: dictionary -->

146 \*flehtan- Lat pluteus m. 'movable screen of wood or wickerwork, parapet' < \*pl(o)utplus the usually adjectival suffix -eus ( < \*-eio-).

\*jlehtan- s.v. 'to braid, plait' - ON fletta w.v. 'id.', OE fleohtan s.v. 'id.', MLG vlechten s.v. 'id.', MDu. v/echten s.v. 'id.', Du. vlechten s.v. 'id.', OHGflehtan s.v. 'id.', G flechten s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*p/eli-t-e- (EUR) - Identical to Lat plecto, -ere 'to plait, twine', OCS plesti, Ru. plestf (pletu), SCr. plesti 'to plait' < \*p/eli-t-e-, which is an extension of the root \*pleli-, cf. Gr. rrAiKw 'to braid, to knit, to wind, to twine' < \*p/eli-e- and Skt prafoa- m. 'plaited basket, turban', YAv. arazato.frafoa- adj. 'having a silver helmet' < \*ploli-no-.

\*fleugan- s.v. 'to fly' - ON fljrlga s.v. 'id.', Far. flugva s.v. 'id.', Elfd. fliuoga s.v. 'id.', OE fleogan s.v. 'id.', E to fly, OFri. fliaga s.v. 'id.', MDu. vliegen s.v. 'id.', Du. vliegen s.v. 'id.', OHG fliugan, fliogan s.v. 'id.', G fliegen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pMuk-e(NEUR) - Lith. plailkti (plaukiil) 'to swim, float' < \*p/ouk-ie- and plunksna f. 'feather' < \*plu-n-k-sneh₂-. In Germanic, the form \*fleugan- emerges for expected \*fleuhan- < \*p/euk-e-. Pokorny explains this as the result of deliberate disambiguation with \*pleuhan-, but it is more attractive to assume interference with the iterative \*flukk/gon- < \*pluk-neh₂-. Another such interference is evinced by MDu. vlieken 'to fly' (cf. also MDu. vlieke, vleke f. 'feather; arrow' < \*fleukjon-, whence OFr.fleche, Fr.fleche 'arrow'), which in spite of its weak conjugation must continue strong \*fleukan- with a secondary \*k from the iterative. Similarly, MDu. vluycken 'to transport over water', which is close to the meaning of Lith. plailkti, is likely to continue Pre-Gm. \*fliikan- with both the \*ii and the \*k pointing to influence from \*flukk/g6n-. See also \*flaugjan-, \*flugi- and \*Jug/a-.

\*fleugon- f. 'fly' - Nw. fljuge f. 'id.', OE fleoge f. 'id.', E fly, MLG vlege f. 'id.', MDu. vliege f. 'id.', Du. vlieg c. 'id.', MHG fliuga, flioga f. 'id.', G Fliege f. 'id.' (DRV). Derived from \*fleugan-. Also cf. ON fluga f. 'id.' with a zero grade.

\*fleusiz- n. 'fleece' - OE fleas, flies n. 'id.', E fleece, MDu. vlies, vluus n. 'fleece; tuft', Du. vlies n. 'id.', MHG vlies, vlius n. 'id.' ⇒ \*pleus-o- (NEUR) - Lith. plaufaf m.pl. 'bast' < \*pious-a-; Lith. pluskos f.pl., Latv. p/uska f. 'tuft of hair' < \*plus-k-eh₂-. The co-existence of OE fleas, (M)Du., MHG vlies < \*fleusa- and OE flies, MDu. vluus, MHG v/ius n. 'id.' < \*fleusi- points to an old neuter s-stem \*fleusaz, gen. \*fleusizaz < \*p/eus-os, \*p/eus-es-os.

\*jleuta- n. 'stream' - ON flj6t n. 'stream, river', OE fleot n. 'estuary, sea; fleet, ship', E fleet, MLG flet m./n. 'river', MDu. vii et m. 'body of water' (DRV).

<!-- pdf-page: 187; section: dictionary -->

\*fleutan- 147 An a-stem to the strong verb \*fleutan- (q.v.). Also cf. ON flj6tr, MDu. vliet adj. 'fast' < \*fleuta-.

\*fleutan- s.v. 'to float, stream' - ON flj6ta s.v. 'id.', OE fleotan s.v. 'to float', OFri. fliata s.v. 'id.', OS fliotan s.v. 'id.', MDu. vlieten s.v. 'id.', Du. vlieten s.v. 'id.', OHG fliozan s.v. 'id.', G flieflen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*p/eud-e- (IE) - Olr. luaidid 'to move' < \*pJeud-e-; Lith. plilsti (p/astu) 'to float, stream, run' < \*plud-ske-. A strong verb to the PIE root \*pleud-. See also \*fleuta-, \*flauti-, \*jluta- and \*flut(t)on-.

\*fleupra- m. 'elder' - WFri. flear 'elder; soggy area', MLG vleder m. 'elder', ODu. fliethar 'id.', MDu. vlieder, vlier m. 'id.', Du. vlier c. 'id.' (GM). A North-Sea Germanic word. In Frisian, it is only attested in the modern dialects, but Du. dial. v/aar, attested in the originally Frisian province of North-Holland, implies an OFri. form \*fliar (cf. Franck 1949: suppl., 183). No certain etymology. The word seems to contain the \*pra-/dra-suffix common in tree-names (cf. \*apaldra-). In view of the WFri. meaning 'soggy place', it is perhaps possible to link the word to the PIE root \*p/eu- 'to float, swim', for which see \*flauma-. Since the elder has a preference for moist soil, this meaning can also be secondary, however.

\*flitan- s.v. 'to strive, fight' - OE flitan s.v. 'to strive, contend, dispute', MDu. vliten s./w.v. 'to be enthusiastic; to strive for', OHG jlfzan s.v. 'to strive, try, fight' ⇒ \*pJeid-e- (WEUR) - MW llwydaw 'to succeed' < \*pleid-. A Germanic-Celtic isogloss (Schumacher 2004: 521-2).

\*floan- s.v. 'to flow' - ON fl6a w.v. 'to flood; be flooded', OE jlowan s.v. 'to flow', E to flow, MLG vloien w.v. 'to flow; flood', MDu. vloeyen w.v. 'to flow; swim; flood', Du. vloeien w.v. 'to flow' ⇒ \*plou-e- (EUR) - Gr. rrA.ww 'to swim' < \*pl6u-e-; OCS p/aviti 'to let swim' < \*plou-eie-. An old strong verb. The alternation of -w- in Anglo-Saxon and -j- in Low German points to a hiatus, viz. \*floan-. This hiatus arose after the regular loss of \*u after \*6 in a formation \*pl6u-e- (cf. Go. sauil 'sun' < \*soel < \*seh₂u-e/). Gr. rrA.ww has been adduced to alternatively derive \*floan- from a root variant \*pleh₃- (cf. rrA.w•oc; adj. 'floating' < \*p/h₃-to-?), but the Germanic evidence clearly points to \*pl6u-e-, cf. \*flauja- 'ship' (= Gr. rrA.oTov) < \*p/ou-io- and \*flauma- < \*p/ou-mo-. See also \*flodu-.

\*flodu- m./f. 'flood' - Go.flodus m. 'id.', 0Njl6o n. 'id.', Far.fl6o f. 'high tide; heavy rain', OE flod m./n. 'id.', E flood, OS flod m./f. 'flood, river', Du. vloed c. 'flood; high tide', OHG fluot f. 'flood', G Flut f. 'river; tide' ⇒ \*ploh₃-tU- (ORV). A tu-stem created to the root of \*floan-. The u-suffix has been replaced by a- and i-stems in many languages, but must nevertheless be reconstructed

<!-- pdf-page: 188; section: dictionary -->

148 \*flohOon the basis of the Gothic form. In ON, the original u-stem genitive fl6oar also seems to have been preserved in compounds, e.g. icel.fl6oar-mal (also flreoar-mal) n., Far.fl6oar-mali m. 'high water mark'.

\*flOhO- f. 'layer' - ON fl6 f. 'layer', OE floh f. 'chip', OHG fluoh f. 'rock, cliff, MHG vluo f. 'id.', G dial. Fluh f. 'id.' (DRY). Derived from the adjective \*flaha-, for which see \*flaka-.

\*flokan- s.v. 'to slap, mourn, lament' - Go.flokan\* s.v. 'to lament', OEflocan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pl6h₂k-e- (EUR) - Gr. rrl.$aaw 'to strike, slap, thrust, hit' < \*plh₂k-ie-; Lat. plango, -ere 'to slap' < \*plh₂k-neh₂-; OCS plakati st: (placp) 'to beat one's breast; lament' < \*pleh₂k-ie-; Lith. pl6kis, p/Okis m. 'flogging, blow with a stick' < \*pleh₂k-i- (and with secondary ablaut: Lith. plakti 'to beat, chastise'?). An o-grade reduplicating verb, formally and semantically close to Gr. rrl.$aaw and OCS plakati. The apparent incongruity of Gm. \*flak- with PIE \*pleh₂k- can be resolved by assuming that the strong verb was influenced by the corresponding iterative \*flakk/gon- (q.v.). It is not necessary to postulate a PIE root variant \*pleh₂g- with root-final \*g. Lat plango may have developed from \*plh₂k-neh₂- with -ng- < \*-ngn- < \*-kn- according to Thurneysen's law, and also Gr. TIATJYli f. 'hit, wound' can have come about by analogy within Greek itself.

\*flora- m. 'floor' - ON fl6rr m. 'floor of a cowshed', OE flor m./f. 'floor; attic', E floor, Du. vloer c. 'id.', OHG fluor m. 'field', G Flur m. 'id.' ⇒ \*p!e/6h₂-ro(EUR) - Identical to Olr. lar m. 'floor'; also cf. OPru. plonis 'threshing floor' < \*p/eh₂-no-; Lith. pl6nas adj. 'thin', Latv. plans adj. 'even, flat, thin' < \*pleh₂-no-; Lat. pliinus adj. 'flat' < \*plh₂-n6-. Within Germanic also cf. MHG flarre f. 'slab' < \*flarron- (EWAhd: III, 422) with \*flarr- < Pre-Gm. \*plorn6-, the genitive stem from an original paradigm \*p/Or-on, gen. \*p/Or-n-6s? This may in turn be a distortion of an old heteroclitic, e.g. nom. \*pl6h₂-r, gen. \*p/eh₂-n-s.

\*flugi- adj. 'able to fly' - E fledge, MDu. vlugghe ad.j. 'being able to fly; fast, quick', OHG flucki adj. 'able to fly', MHG vliicke adj. 'id.' (DRY). A gerundive to \*fleugan- 'to fly' (q.v.). The o-grade is found in ON fleygr adj. 'id.' < \*flaugja-.

\*flukka(n)- m. 'group' - ON flokkr m. 'troop, host; flock', Far. flokkur m. 'multitude, crowd, party; flock (of birds)', OEflocc n. 'flock, band, company', Eflock, MLG vlocke 'group' (DRY). The original meaning of the word was 'group of moving people or animals', cf. Far. flokkur 'flock of birds', E flock (of birds or sheep), and this al-
