<!-- pdf-page: 189; section: dictionary -->

\*flukkan- 149 lows for the connection with \*fleugan- 'to fly'. It was derived from the appurtenant iterative \*flukk/gon-. Also cf. \*flukka(n)-.

\*jlukkan- m. 'flake' - MDu. vlocke f. 'flake, tuft, fluff, Du. vlok c. 'id.', OHG flocko m. 'id.', G Flocke m. 'id.' (DRV). The word has been analyzed as a loanword from Lat. floccus m. 'tuft of wool', but there are other options. It may have been derived from an iterative \*flukkon-, cf. Nw.flokka w.v. 'to pull out (e.g. hay)', G Rhnl.flocken w.v. 'to loosen hay', which in turn can be related to Lith. pl<iukas m. '(single) hair, color of an animal, (pl.) hair, fur'. The iterative that is most likely to be related, however, is \*flukk/gon- 'to fly, flutter' (q.v.). Note that its o-grade variant \*flakk/gon- seems to have been the source of the no doubt related E flake.

\*jlukk/gon- w.v. 'to fly, flutter' - MDu. vlogen w.v. 'to fly', G flocken w.v. 'to fly around, flutter' (NEUR). The iterative to \*fleugan- (q.v.). See also \*flakk/gon-.

\*jluta- n. 'floating' - ON flot n. 'floating; grease', OE flot n. 'sailable body of water, sea', Du. vlot n. 'raft' (DRV). A regular zero-grade neuter formation to the strong verb \*fleutan-.

\*flut(t)on- w.v. 'to float, stream' - ON flota w.v. 'id.', OE flotian w.v. 'id.', MDu. vlot(t)en w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*plud-neh₂·(?) (DRv?). The iterative to \*fleutan-. Also cf. the frequentative OEfloterian w.v. 'to be tossed on the waves'.

\*fneusan- - \*fnii.san· s.v. 'to sneeze' - ON hnj6sa s.v. 'id.', Far. nj6sa s.v. 'id.', Nw. njosa, nysa s.v. 'id.', OEfneosan s.v. 'id.', MEfnesen, snesen s.v. 'id.', E to sneeze, MDu. niesen, fniesen s.v. 'id.', Du. niezen, obs. fniezen w.v., MHG pfnii.sen s.v. 'to snort, sneeze' ⇒ \*pneus-e- (IE) - Gr. rrvtw 'to blow, cough, breathe' < \*pneu-e-. Seebold 1970: 269 reconstructs PGm. \*hneusan- on the basis of the Nordic forms, thus isolating them from WGm. \*fneusan-, but it seems more likely that the sequence \*Jn- merged with \*hn- (also cf. MDu. niesen). This is confirmed by Far. fros n. 'sneezing', a (dissimilated) variant of nos n. 'id.' < ON \*hnos < \*hnusa-. As a result, there seem to be no objections against reconstructing the root as \*fneus-, which appears to be related to PIE \*pneu'to breathe' (with the s-extension pointing to an aoristic root, cf. Gr. EITVEUC1E 'breathed, blew'). Ultimately, the root is likely to be onomatopoetic, cf. OHG fnehan s.v. 'to snort, breathe heavily' < \*fnehan-. See also \*fnuz(z)on-.

<!-- pdf-page: 190; section: dictionary -->

150 \*fnuz(z)on-

\*fnuz(z)fm- w.v. 'to sneeze' - Ice!. hnerra w.v. 'id.', MHG pfnurren w.v. 'to swell; to snort; to grumble' ⇒ \*pnus-neh₂- (IE). Possibly the iterative to \*fneusan- - \*fnusan- (q.v.). Also cf. ON hn!<1ri, hneri, Ice!. hnerri, OE fnora m. 'sneezing' < \*fnuz(z)an-. Some derive Ice!. hnerra, obs. hnor(r)a from the noun (Magnusson 1989: 347), however.

\*fodjan- w.v. 'to feed; to rear' - Go. fodjan w.v. 'id.', ON freoa w.v. 'id.', OE Jedan w.v. 'id.', E to feed, OFri. feda w.v. 'id.', OS fOdian w.v. 'id.', Du. voeden w.v. 'id.', OHG fuottan w.v. 'id.', MHG viieten w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*poh₂t-eie- (EUR) - Gr. mnfoμcn 'to eat' < \*ph₂t-eie-; Gr. &:nacrTO<; 'without food' < \*n-ph₂t-to-. A causative probably created to the marginally attested root \*peh₂t-, possibly an extension of \*peh₂- 'to protect; to graze', cf. Lat. piisco, -ere 'to graze' < \*peh₂-ske-. It is less attractive to explain the dental of the Germanic verb as resulting from back-formation from \*fodra- 'fodder', not least in view of \*fadolja)n-.

\*fodra- n. 'fodder' - Go. fodr n. 'scabbard, sheath, casing', ON Joor n. 'fodder, foddering', OE /Odor n. 'f؍dder; sheath', E fodder, MLG vOder n. 'fodder; casing', MDu. voeder n. 'lining, cover; fodder', Du. voer, voeder n. 'fodder', OHG fuotar n. 'id.', MHG vuoter n. 'id.', G Futter n. 'id.' ⇒ \*peh₂-dhro- (IE) - Lat. pabulum n. 'fodder' < \*peh₂-dh/o-. A merger of two instrumental nouns. In the meaning 'fodder', it is related to \*fodjan- 'to feed'. The attestations meaning 'sheath' or 'casing', on the other hand, were derived from the PIE root \*peh₂-, i.e. 'to protect', cf. ToA piis-, ToB piisk- 'to guard'.

\*fogjan- w.v. 'to join together' - OE fegan w.v. 'id.', E to fay, OS fogian w.v. 'id.', Du. voegen w.v. 'id.', OHG fuogan w.v. 'id.', G fiigen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*poh₂li-eie(IE) - Hitt. piiski - piiskanzi 'to stick in, to fasten, to plant; to set up; to impale' < \*peh₂li-ske-; YAv. paiti.auua.pasii! 3sg.subj. 'to chain together' < \*peh₂l<- (cf. Skt. p&5a- m. 'snare, bond, chain, noose', Av. fiabis <lat.pl. 'with fetters' < \*p(e)h₂l<-; Gr. m'Jcrcrw, Att. m'Jnw 'to fix' < \*peh₂k-ie- (with secondary g: m'JyvuμL 'to fix; to congeal'); Lat. pango, -ere 'to attach, fasten' < \*ph₂k-neh₂- (with \*-kn- > \*-ng- according to Thurneysen's law). A causative verb to a further unattested strong verb \*fahan-, whence MHG vuoge f. 'joint', G Fuge, MLG voge, MDu. voege f., Du. voeg c. 'joint' < \*Jago-. See also \*fagra-.

\*foljan- w.v. 'to feel' - OE felan w.v. 'id.', E to feel, OFri. fela w.v. 'id.', OS gi-folian w.v. 'id.', Du. voe/en w.v. 'id.', Gfihlen w.v. 'id.' (GM). An etymologically obscure word. Within Germanic, the appurtenance of ON fa/ma 'to grope' and \*fulmo- 'palm of the hand' (q.v.) is uncertain. Not

<!-- pdf-page: 191; section: dictionary -->

\*fOra- 151 convincing either are the connections with OCS palbcb, Ru. palec 'finger' and Lat pa/po, -are 'to stroke, soothe'.

\*fora- m. 'piglet' - OE for m. 'pig, hog', OS for n. 'immature pig', MLG vor 'skinny piglet' ⇒ \*por-o- (DRV?) - Gr. rr6pu;, -we; f. 'calf, heifer' < \*por-i-. A word apparently continuing Pre-Gm. \*por-o-, an o-stem derived from the root \*per- 'to give birth', for which see \*farza(n)-.

\*for - \*fun- n. 'fire' - Go.for, gen.funins n. 'id.', ON fYrr,filrr m. 'fire'.funi m. 'flame', Far. fYrur m. 'fire; lighthouse', funi m. 'dense smoke', OE fYr n. 'id.', E fire, OFri. ftur, fior n. 'id.', WFri. fjoer n. 'id.', OS fiur n. 'id.', ODu. fuir n. 'id.', Du. vuur n. 'id.', OHG fiur, fuir n. 'id.', G Feuer n. 'id.' ⇒ \*peh₂-ur, \*ph₂-uen-s (IE) - Hitt. paḫḫur, gen. paḫḫuenas n. 'fire', Gr. 1tUp n. 'id.', Arm. hur 'id.', OPru. panno 'id.'. An old heteroclitic formation, probably to be reconstructed as Pre-Germanic \*peh₂-ur (hardly \*ph₂-uor as per Simms 2009, as this would become \*fawor), gen. \*ph₂-un-6s, loc. \*ph₂-uen-i. The heteroclisy was preserved by Proto-Germanic, which appears to have had a paradigm \*for (with \*-ou- > \*-o-), gen. \*funins (for older \*funaz < \*pun6s with Dybo's law), dat. \*fu(w)eni. The different languages leveled out the r/n-alternations. Gothic, for instance, incorporated the word into the neuter n-stems, while West Germanic generalized the forms with r. The attestations with front mutation, e.g. ON fYrr, OE JYr, OHG fuir, fiur, are based on a dative form \*fu(w)eni. For similar cases of umlaut in PIE heteroclitic nouns, see \*eudra- \*udra- 'udder', \*libro- 'liver' and \*neura/on- 'kidney'. OEfior and OFri. ftur, WFri. fjoer, apparently from \*feuri, continue the same case form, though with metathesis.

\*fori- adj. 'passable; able to go' - ON frerr adj. 'able to go; capable of being passed, safe', OE /ere adj. 'passable; able to go', OHG gi-fuori adj. 'fit, convenient' (DRY). A vṛddhi-gerundive to \*faran- (q.v.). The difference in meaning between 'able to go' and 'passable' depends on the diathetic role, i.e. passive or active.

\*forjan- w.v. 'to lead' - ON frera w.v. 'id.', OE feran w.v. 'to go', OFri. fera w.v. 'id.', OS forian w.v. 'id.', Du. voeren w.v. 'id.', OHG fuorran, fuoren w.v. 'id.', Gfiihren w.v. 'id.' (DRY). The causative to \*faran- (q.v.). The vowel \*o is secondary, and in synchronic Germanic functions as the o-grade of \*a. It is not to be equated with the a of Skt. parayati 'to take across', which continues \*por-eie- by Brugmann's law.

<!-- pdf-page: 192; section: dictionary -->

152 \*fOt-

\*fat- m. 'foot' - Go. fotus m. 'id.', ON f6tr, pl. fretr m. 'foot; leg', Far. f6tur, pl. f£1tur m. 'foot', Elfd.feot m. 'foot; leg', OE/ot, pl. fat m. 'foot', E/oot, OFri.fot m. 'id.', OS Jot m. 'id.', Du. voet c. 'id.', OHG feoz m. 'id.', G Fufl m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pod(IE) - Hitt piit- - pat- c. 'foot', Skt. p&t, pad- m. 'id.', YAv. pad- m. 'id.', OP piida- 'id.', Arm. otn 'id.', Gr. Dor. rrwc;, lon.-Att rrouc;, rro66c; m. 'id.' < \*pod-; Lat. pes, pedis m. 'id.', Olr. ed 'space, interval' < \*ped-. The IE word for 'foot', a static root noun nom. \*p6d-s, gen. \*ped-s, \*p6d-m. Germanic generalized the lengthened grade of the root. See also \*fetan- and \*futon-.

\*fōþra- n. 'load; vessel; fodder' - OE fōþor n. 'cartload; fodder', MLG voder n. 'cartload', MDu. voeder n. 'wine vat; lead weight', OHG feodar n. 'fodder; cartload', MHG vuoder n. 'id.', G Fuder m. 'cartload' ⇒ \*peh₂;3-tro- (GM/IE) - Skt. patra- n. 'vessel. pot'. Given the pan-WGm. meaning 'load' or 'weight', it seems likely that the word continues \*forpra-, i.e. an instrumental noun to \*forjan-, by dissimilation of the first r. The outcome may have merged with two other instrumental nouns, viz. 1) \*fopra- from PIE \*peh₃-tro- 'drinking cup, vessel', and 2) \*fOdra- 'fodder' (q.v.) < quasi-PIE \*peh₂(t)-tro-.

\*fraisan- s.v. 'to tempt' - Go. fraisan s.v. 'id.', OE friisian w.v. 'to ask, inquire, tempt' ⇒ \*pr6is-e- (IE?). A strong verb attested only in Gothic. It gave rise to OFri. /res, OS fresa, OHG freisa f. 'danger; scare' < \*fraiso-, whence OE friisian 'to try', OS freson 'to try, risk', Du. vrezen 'to fear', MHG vreisen 'to endanger' < \*fraisojan- and ON freista w.v. 'to tempt' < \*fraistojan-, which implies a noun \*fraisto- < \*prois-teh₂-. An archaic formation is Go. fraistubni f. 'temptation' < \*proist-mn-ieh₂-. Etymology unclear. From \*pro-h₂is- "to ask forth" (Hoffmann 1903: 38)? Cf. \*aisko-.

\*fraiwa- n. 'seed' - Go.fraiw n. 'id.', ON fr«!,frj6 n. 'id.', Elfd.frie n. 'id.' (IE?). A word with a debated etymology. The connection with Lat. prae9nans (implying PGm. \*frai9wa-) is implausible on many different levels (pace Wiedemann 1904: 43-44), and the same is true for the alleged links with PIE \*sper- 'to sow' and Lat. parere 'to bring forth' (Bugge 1899: 457) < \*pr(H)-ie-. Within Germanic, the attestation of ON frjar, frj6r, fr«!r, lee!. frj6r adj. 'fertile; prolific' < \*fraiwa- clearly seems to point to a stem \*/rai(wJmeaning 'fecund'. Both in form and meaning, this element is reminiscent of Freyr 'fertility deity' < \*frauja-. We may therefore consider the possibility that \*fraiwa- was metathesized from \*frawja-, a collective of some kind. The word is then likely to be related to ON fry90 f. 'blossoming, excellence' < \*fruwwipo-. For a similar metathesis, cf. \*hraiwa- 'flesh' < \*krou-io-.

<!-- pdf-page: 193; section: dictionary -->

\*fraton- 153

\*fraton- w.v. 'to fart' - ON frata w.v. 'id.', Far. frata w.v. 'id.', Elfd. frata w.v. 'id.', MHG varzen w.v. 'id.', Gfarzen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pord-neh₂- (IE). An o-grade iterative to the strong verb \*fertan- - \*fretan- ( q.v.).

\*frapjan- s.v. 'to understand' - Go. frapjan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*pr6t-ie- (NEUR) - Lith. prasti (prantil) 'to become accustomed to, dial. to apprehend', su-prasti 'to understand' < \*pro-n-t-. A strongjan-verb with close cognates in Baltic. Also cf. Go.frapi n. 'mind' < \*frapja-, OHG frad adj. 'nimble, quick; vigorous, strenuous' < \*frapa- and \*frOda- 'wise' (q.v.). The appurtenance of OHG ant-frist 'translation' < \*-fresti-(?) is uncertain (see Liberman 2008: 211).

\*frauja(n)- m. 'lord' - Go.frauja m. 'id.', OEfrea m. 'id.', 0Sfr6,fr6io m. 'id.', MDu. vroon, vrone m. 'lord' (with n generalized from the oblique cases), OHGfr6 m. 'id.', MHG vr6 m. 'id.' (GM). Unclear etymology. The assumed etymological link with Skt pilrva- adj. 'front, first', YAv. pauruua-, paouruua-, pouruua- adj. 'being in front, first, former', OCS. pnv'b adj. 'first' < \*prH-uo- is problematic, since the full-grade form \*proHu-io- would yield PGm. \*\*fr6jan- (with \*-6u- giving \*-6-) rather than \*fraujan-.

\*frawa- adj. 'quick, light' - ON frar, fr6r adj. 'swift, lightfooted', Icel. frar adj. 'id.', Far. fr6ur adj. 'cheerful, gay, happy', OS frii., fro adj. 'happy', OHG frao, fr6 adj. 'id.', G froh adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*prou-o- (IE) - Skt. pravati 'to jump' < \*preu-e-. Also cf. OHG frawida, frewida, G Freude f. 'joy' < \*frawip6- and probably also the ablauting Du. vreugd(e) c. 'id.' < \*fruwip6- (with \*-uwi- > \*-ugi-).

\*fregi- adj. 'renowned' - ON frcegr adj. 'famous', OE ge-frci!ge adj. 'renowned, celebrated', OS gi-friigi adj. 'known, famous' (DRY). A vṛddhi-gerundive to \*frehnan- (q.v.). The original meaning was "being rumored".

\*frego- f. 'question' - Du. vraag c. 'id.', OHGfrii.ga f. 'question, investigation, doctrine', G Frage f. 'question' (IE) - OAv.f(a)rasii- f. 'question', Lat prex f. 'prayer, supplication', W rheg 'gift; curse' < \*preK{-eh₂)-. An 6-stem derived from the preterite root of the strong verb \*frehnan-: there is no direct link between the lengthened grade of \*freg6- and the one found in the root noun Skt. prds- f. 'dispute, questioning, statement or assertion in a debate or lawsuit'. The 6-stem served as the basis for OHG frii.gen - frii.g6n, G fragen, OS friigon, OFri. fregia 'to ask' < \*freg6jan-. Related to \*fursk6- (q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 194; section: dictionary -->

154 \*frehnan-

\*frehnan- s.v. 'to announce, rumor' - Go. fraihnan s.v. 'to ask a question', ON fregna s.v. 'to hear of, be informed of; to ask', OE frignan s.v. 'to ask, inquire', gi-frignan s.v. 'to find out, hear about, learn something through asking', OS fregnan s.v. 'to ask', gijregnan s.v. 'to hear tell of, learn about' ⇒ \*preJ<-ne- (IE) - Skt. prafoa- m. 'question' < \*pre/oJ<-no-; Lith. piFsti (persu) 'to propose (a marriage); to offer'; Lith. prasyti 'to ask, demand', OCS prositi, Ru. prosft' (prosu) 'to ask', SCr. prositi 'to beg, ask for a girl's hand' < \*proJ<-eie-. A strong verb with a nasal suffix that originally only occurred in the present, cf. Go. fraihnan, pret. frah -frehum and ON fregna, pret fra -fragum. In Old Saxon and Old English, the verb generalized the nasal, and switched to the third class: OS (gi-)fregna, pret. fragn, frugnun and OE (ge-)frignan, (ge-)fringan, pret. frregn, frregn - frugnon, frungon. Originally, \*frehnansimply meant 'to ask', while the meaning 'to learn' of the perfective variant \*ga-frehnan- seems to have developed out of "to have inquired". Related to \*frego- and \*fursko-.

\*freka- adj. 'avaricious' - Go. faihu-friks adj. 'id.', ON frekr adj. 'greedy; harsh; rigorous', Far. frekur adj. 'avid, covetous; severe; vigorous; audacious', Elfd. frek adj. 'friendly, nice', OE free adj. 'eager, greedy, bold', MDu. vrec adj. 'greedy', Du. vrek c. 'avaricious person', OHG freh adj. 'eager, avid', MHG vrech adj. 'brave' ⇒ \*preg-o- (EUR) - Pol. pragnqc 'to yearn for', Cz. prahnouti 'to covet' < \*prog-ne-; Slov. praziti 'to stew' < \*prog-eie-; W rhewydd adj. 'lascivious' < \*preg-io-; OBret. rogedou pl. 'orgies' (Lane 1933: 258). An adjective to a European root \*preg- 'to desire'. Ablauting with OEfrrec, MDu. vrac adj. 'avaricious' < \*fraka- < \*prog-o-.

\*freknon- (. 'freckle' - lcel. frekna f. 'id.', Far. fr¢kna f. 'id.', Nw. frekne, frokne f. 'id.', Sw. friikne 'id.', Da. fregne 'id.', E freckle (NEUR) - Close to Pol. prftgaty adj. 'striped' < \*prng-eh₂-. Most of the Nordic forms can be derived from ON \*frekna (whence ME frekne, E obs.frecken as well as freckle), but Far.frf:fkna and Nw.frokne seem to continue a paradigm \*frakna, obl. \*frpknu. It seems that the original plural \*freknur was reanalyzed as a root noun form continuuing ON \*freknr < \*frfJknr after the vocalization of -r to -ur. This then triggered the back-formation of a singular \*frakna and an oblique \*frpknu in Faroese and Norwegian.

\*freusan- s.v. 'to freeze' - ON frj6sa s.v. 'id.', Elfd. friuosa s.v. 'id.', OE freosan s.v. 'id.', E to freeze, MLG vresen s.v. 'id.', MDu. vriesen s.v. 'id.', Du. vriezen s.v. 'id.', OHG friosan s.v. 'id.', G frieren s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*preus-e- (WEUR) -

<!-- pdf-page: 195; section: dictionary -->

\*freupan- 155 W rhew m. 'frost, rime' < \*preuso-; ?Lat pruina f. 'frost' ( < \*pruryina < \*prus-u-iH-neh₂, Hamp 1973a?). An old strong verb. The Verner alternation \*freusan-, ptc. \*fruzana- was resolved in most modern Germanic languages, cf. E to freeze - frozen vs. G frieren - ge-froren, but retained in Du. vriezen - ge-vroren and Ice!. frj6sa freoinn ( < ON fr'1rinn.frerinn with dissimilation of the second r, cf. ON fr'1r, frer, icel.freo- n. 'frost'). The original past participle \*prus-to- was preserved as \*frusta- (q.v.). Further cf. Go.frius\* n.(?) 'frost' < \*freusa-.

\*freupan- s.v. 'to froth' - OE ri-freooan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*preutH-e- (IE) - Skt pr6thati 'to snort (of horses)' < \*preutH-e-; Av. fra68ataspa- 'with snorting horses'. A marginally attested strong verb, the source of some nominal formations in Nordic, i.e. ON, Ice!. frauo n. 'froth', Far. froyour m. 'id.' < \*fraudija- < \*proutH- and ON, Far.frooa, Nw.frode f., Da.frdde c. 'id.' < \*frup6n-. The root \*preutH- may be an extension of PIE \*pru-, cf. Skt. vi-pru- 'to spray in all directions' < \*pru-. Another extension to the same root may be \*prus-: Skt pru$r:i6ti, OCS prysnpti 'to sprinkle' (with secondary \*y) < \*prus-ne- and Ice!. freyr m. 'froth' < \*frauza-. A primitive meaning 'to blow' is implied by Nw. dial.froe f. (=/rode) 'gust of wind'.

\*fri(j)a- adj. 'free' - Go. freis adj. 'id.', OE freo, fri adj. 'id.', E free, OFri. fri adj. 'id.', OS fri adj. 'id.', Du. vrij adj. 'id.', OHG fri adj. 'id.', G frei adj. 'id.' ¸

\*priH-o- (IE) - Skt. priya- adj. 'dear' < \*priH-o-. Also cf. Go. frei-hals, ON frjals, OE freols adj. 'free' < \*friOJa-halsa- and the nominalized OHG, OFri.fri-hals m. 'freedom'. The meaning 'free' arose from 'related', a shift that points to a clan-based societal system. Cognate with \*fri0}6n- (q.v.).

\*fri(j)on- w.v. 'to love' - Go. frijon w.v. 'to love, like', ON frja w.v. 'to love', Far. frfggja w.v. 'to propose, court', OE freogan, frigan w.v. 'to love, marry', OS frion w.v. 'id.', Du. vrijen s.v. 'to make love' ⇒ \*priH-eh₂- (IE) - Close to OCS prijati 'to take care of, SCr. prijati 'to please, agree with' < \*priH-eh₂-; further cf. Skt priyate 'to be pleased' < \*priH-ie-. An old PIE verbal formation. See also \*friOJa- and \*friOJ6nd-.

\*frilj)ond- m. 'friend' - Go. frijonds m. 'id.', ON frrendi m. 'friend; relative', Far. frrendi m. 'relative', OE freond m. 'friend, loved one, relative', E friend, 0Fri.friii.nd,fri6nd m. 'id.', ODu.friuntm. 'id.', Du. vriend c. 'id.', OHGfriunt m. 'friend, loved one', G Freund m. 'friend' (DRV). The lexicalized participle of the verb \*fri0}6n- (q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 196; section: dictionary -->

156 \*fripu-

\*fripu- m. 'friendship, peace' - ON friOr m. 'peace; security; friendship', Far. friaur m. 'peace; tranquility', Elfd. fria m. 'id.', OE freoao m. 'id.', OFri. fretho m. 'id.', OS frithu,frethu m. 'peace; security', Du. vrede c. 'peace', OHG fridu m. 'peace; protection, security', G Friede m. 'peace' (ORV). A tu-stem derived from \*fri(j)a- < \*priH-o-: the short Gm. \*i cannot have regularly developed from the form \*priH-tu-, so it must be assumed that the tu-stem was derived at a relatively late stage, i.e. after the merger of \*-faand \*-ija-, or - if Dybo's law applied, at a stage when the pretonic shortening of\*prfW6- to \*priWo- had already taken place.

\*froda- adj. 'wise, experienced' - Go. froþs adj. 'id.', ON fr6or adj. 'id.', OE frod adj. 'id.', OFri. frod adj. 'id.', OS frOd adj. 'id.', OHG fruot adj. 'id.', MHG vruot adj. 'id.' (NEUR) - Close to Lith. protas m. 'sense', Latv. prats m. 'mind; will' (with secondary PBalt. \*a). An o-grade adjective related to \*fraþjan- (q.v.). Also cf. Go. frodei, ON frreoi, OHGfruoti, MHG vruote f. 'wisdom, sense'.

\*froi adv. 'early' - MLG vro adv. 'early', MDu. vroech adj. 'id.', Du. vroeg adv., OHGfruo adv. 'id.', MHG vruo adv. 'id.', Gfruh adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*pr6H-i (IE). A petrified locative identical to Gr. rrpwi, rrp£i> adv. 'early in the morning' (for which cf. Skt. priitar adv. 'early in the morning' < \*proH-ter). The adverb gave rise to the adj. \*froja-, cf. OHG fruoji, G fruhe, which is formally identical to Gr. rrpwwc;.

\*frug/kkan- m. 'frog' - OEfrogga m. 'id.', MEfrogge,frock,froke 'id.', Efrog ⇒ \*pruk-en- (GM). The formal variation within ME, i.e. frogge < \*fruggan-, frock < \*frukkaand froke < \*frukan- points to an old n-stem \*frugo, gen. \*frukkaz < \*pr(tgh-on, \*prugh-n-6s or perhaps rather \*pruk-բn. \*pruk-n-6s. The original paradigm was split up into 1) \*frugo, \*fruggaz on the one hand, and 2) \*fruko, \*frukkaz on the other. ON frauki m. 'frog' may be related, too, if it continues an ablauting form \*fraukan- from Pre-Gm. \*fraukkan-. The word has alternatively been derived from \*frauokan- (\*-kan- is a diminutive suffix), but the alleged derivational base, i.e. ON frauar, Ice!. frauour, Nw. frau(d), OSw. fredher, Da. fre 'frog' probably developed from \*fraugr (with -g- > \*-a- in labial environment as in fjpgur n. '4' < \*fjpdur < \*feduro). ME frrld, E obs. fraud 'id.' is therefore likely to have been adopted from Old Norse. No certain etymology: the usual comparison with Ru. prygat', prjgnut' 'to leap' is highly doubtful, as the accent implies a root \*pruHgh- or \*prug- rather than \*prugh-. This leaves the intra-Germanic connection with \*fruska( q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 197; section: dictionary -->

\*fruman- 157

\*fruman- num. 'former, first' - Go. fruma num. 'id.', ON frum- pref. 'first, oldest', OE forma num. 'id.', E former, OFri. forma num. 'id.', OS formo num. 'id.' ⇒ \*prH-mH-on- (IE) - OPru. pirmas, Lith. pirmas num. 'first' < \*prH-mHo-; Gr. rrpaμoc; m. 'foremost' < \*pr-mH-o-. It is likely that the numeral contains the comparative suffix PGm. \*-uman< \*-mH-on-, but the expected outcome of a proto-form \*pr-mH-on- would be \*\*furman-. Possibly, this form was remodeled into \*fruman- on the basis of other ordinals ending in \*-uman-. A similar remodeling took place in Latin, where primus replaced expected \*purmus < \*pr-mH-o- or \*pramus < \*prh₂-mH-o- due to the productivity of the suffix \*-imus (cf. optimus).

\*fruska- m. 'frog' - ON froskr m. 'id.', OE forsc, frox m. 'id.', OHG /rose m. 'id.', G Frosch m. 'id.' (GM). A formation that in all probability continues older \*fru9-ska- with the same root element as in \*fru9/kkan- 'frog'. The derivation from \*frauda'froth' (see \*freupan-) was inspired by ON frauor 'frog', but this form is likely to have developed from \*frau9r in view of ON frauki 'id.' < \*frauk(k)an-.

\*frusta- m./n. 'frost' - ONfrost n. 'id.', OEforst m. 'id.', Efrost, OFri.forst m. 'id.', OS frost m. 'id.', Du. vorst c. 'id.', OHG frost m. 'id.', G Frost m. 'id.' ⇒ \*prus-to- (DRV). Derived from \*freusan- (q.v.).

\*fian- m. 'mold' - ONfai m. 'rottenness', Far.fa9vi m. 'id.' ⇒ \*puH-on- (IE). An n-stem to the PIE root \*puH-, for which see \*faujan- 1.

\*fugla- m. 'bird' - Go. fu9/s m. 'id.', ON /09/, fu9/ m. 'id.', Far. fu9/ur m. 'id.', Elfd. fu9el m. 'id.', OE fu90/ m. 'id.', E fowl, OFri. fu9el m. 'id.', OS fu9al m. 'id.', Du. vo9el c. 'id.', OHGfo9al,fu9al m. 'id.', G Vogel m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pluk-16- (DRV). Some connect Li th. pailkStis 'bird' < \*pouk-sti- (e.g. Magnusson 1989: 214), but the form \*fu9la- is more likely to have developed from \*flu9-la(by dissimilation), and may thus have been derived from \*fleu9an- 'to fly' (q.v.).

\*fuhon- f. 'she-fox' - Go.fauho f. 'id.', ON f6a,faa f. 'id.', MLG vo f. 'id.', OHG voha f. 'id.', MHG vohe f. 'id.' (IE). The feminine to \*fuhsa- 'fox' (q.v.). Another formation with the same meaning is represented by ME Jixen(e}, vixen, E vixen (with southern v- for f), MHG viihsinne, G Fiichsin, but unlike \*fuhon-, this is a secondary derivation from \*fuhsa-.

\*fuhsa- m. 'fox' - ON fox n. 'deceit', Ice!. fox n. 'fox', OE fox m. 'id.', E fox, WFri.foks m. 'id.', EFri.foaks m. 'id.', MLG vos(se) m. 'id.', MDu. vos m., vosse f.

<!-- pdf-page: 198; section: dictionary -->

158 \*fuk(k)on- 'id.', Du. vos c. 'id.', OHG fuhs m. 'id.', G Fuchs m. 'id.' ⇒ \*puk-so- (IE) - Skt pucha- m. 'tail, rod' < \*pul<-sk-o-. Since the semantic link between 'tail' and 'fox' is paralleled by Lith. uodega 'tail' > uodegis 'fox', it is reasonable to compare Germanic \*fuhsa- to Skt. pucha-. This provides the word with an Indo-European background. Note that the Nordic word for 'fox', ON refr, Icel. refur, Far. revur, Nw. rev, Sw. riiv, Da. r<l!v (also cf. Fi. repo), was borrowed from Iranian, cf. Oss. ruvas / robas (Skt. lopiisa-, Av. raopi-, Sogd. ropas). This is supported by Spa. raposo, raposa, apparently a loanword from the same Sarmatian or Alanic source (Bnmdal 1928: 10). See also \*fuh6n- 'she-fox'.

\*fuk(k)on- w.v. 'to blow' - Icel. fokka w.v. 'to mess around, rush', MLG vucken w.v. 'to rush, scud', MDu. vocken w.v. 'to blow', MHG fochen w.v. 'to blow' ⇒ \*pug-neh₂- (EUR?) - Either to Latv. pilga f. 'gust of wind' < \*pug-eh₂- or to Arm. p'ukf'J 'breath, wind, fart' < \*pug-o- (with sound symbolic aspiration?). The different dialects with their vacillation between \*fukk6n- and \*fuk6npoint to a formation \*pug-neh₂-, which could be related to Latv. pilga and Arm. p'ulef'J. The sometimes compared Lith. pilksti (pilksCiu) 'to cough, snarl', on the other hand, is a loanword from LG pusten 'to pant, snarl' (with klusilspring in the sequence -us-). See also \*feukan- - \*ftikan-.

\*ftila- adj. 'filthy, foul' - Go. fuls adj. 'foul, putrid', ON fell adj. 'foul, stinking; mean', OE fill adj. 'foul, nasty', E foul, OFri. fill adj. 'foul; vile', OS fulitha f. 'filth', MLG ftil adj. 'foul, bad', Du. vuil adj. 'dirty', OHG ftil adj. 'rotten, decayed', G faul adj. 'putrid; lazy' ⇒ \*puH-lo- (IE) - Lith. pziliai m.pl. 'pus' < \*puH-1-io- and piaulaim.pl. 'mold' < \*peuH-1-io-. A lo-stem derived from a PIE root \*puH-. See also \*faujan- 1.

\*fulan- m. 'foal' - Go. fula m. 'id.', ON Joli m. 'id.', Elfd. fuoli m. 'id.', OE fola m. 'id.', E Joie, MLG vole m. 'id.', OHG folo m. 'id.', G Fohlen m. 'id.' ⇒ \*p/H-on(IE) - Gr. nwl.o<; m./f. 'foal' < \*p6/H-o-, W ebawl 'id.' < \*h₁ekwo-p6/H-o-; Alb. pe/e 'mare' < \*p6/H-neh₂-. Also cf. ON, Far., OSw. fyl, Sw. fol, Nw. fel(l}, Da. ffi1/ n. 'id.' < \*fulja- < \*p/H-io-, the WGm. diminutive MDu. volen, vu/en, Du. veulen, OHG vuli, vulfn, MHG viile, viilfn, G Fullen n. 'foal' < \*ful-lfn(a)- and the Franconian feminine MHG viilche, MDu. vilke, Du. dial. vulke, vilke f. '(young) mare' < \*fulik6(n)-. In IE, the word is sometimes reconstructed with a \*u in the root, viz. \*p(o)ul- (cf. Orel 2000: 14), but this seems to be precluded by W ebawl. I therefore reconstruct a root noun \*polH-s, gen. \*p/H-6s (Lubotsky 1988: 132). This formation may ultimately have been derived from a verbal root \*pelH- as in Alb. pjell 'to give birth' (cf. Huld 1984: 102).

<!-- pdf-page: 199; section: dictionary -->

\*fuldo- 159

\*fuldo- f. 'earth, ground, field, the world' - ON fold f. 'id.', Far. poet.fold f. 'id.', OE folde f. 'id.', OS folda f. 'id.' ::) \*plth₂-eh₂- (IE) - Cf. Skt. p[thvf-, p[thivf- f. 'earth, Mother Earth', YAv. zqm parai9Plm, paraape ... zamo 'broad earth' < \*plth₂-u-ih₂-. The Germanic and Indo-lranian forms continue a feminine form of the adj. 'flat'. In Indo-European, it presumably occurred with the word for 'land' in a sacral context. See also \*fetpa-.

\*fulgen· w.v. 'to follow, accompany' - ON fylgja w.v. 'id.', Far. fy/gja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. fya w.v. 'id.', OE fylgan,folgian w.v. 'id.', E to follow, OSfolgon w.v. 'id.', Du. vo/gen w.v. 'id.', OHG fa/gen w.v. 'id.', G folgen w.v. 'id.' ::) \*plk-[eh₁)-ie- (GM). ON fylgja and OE fylg{e)an continue a formation \*fulgjan- that can be reconciled with OE fe[o)lan s.v. 'to proceed, go, reach' < \*felhan- < \*pelk-e-. Extra-Gm. *comparanda* are unreliable: Bret. heul 'to follow' probably continues \*se/gh- rather than \*pe/gh- and the connection with CS p!'bzati, Ru. polzat', SCr. piizati 'to crawl' must be abandoned in view of the accent pointing to \*plg-eh₂- rather than \*plgh-ehi-.

\*fulla- 1 adj. 'full' - Go. fulls adj. 'id.', ON fullr adj. 'id.', Far. fullr adj. 'id.', Elfd. full adj. 'id.', OE full adj. 'id.', E full, OS full adj. 'id.', Du. vol adj. 'id.', OHG fol adj. 'id.', G vol/ adj. 'id.' ::) \*plhi-no- (IE) - Skt. purria-, Av. parana-, Olr. lan 'id.', Lith. pilnas, OCS p!'bn'b adj. 'filled, full' < \*plhi-no-. An adjective continuing the original past participle of the verbal root

\*pelhi- 'to fill'. See also \*felu- - \*fulu-.

\*fulla- 2 n. 'cup' - ON full n. 'goblet', OE full n. 'cup' ::) \*pl-n6- (IE) - Gr. ntla f. 'milk pail; drinking bowl, goblet' < \*pel-ih₂- and Gr. nelll<;, neA.t<; 'basin' < \*pel-i-s, gen. \*pe/-i-6s; OCS po/'b 'scoop' < \*pol-a-. An Indo-European word for a certain vessel. Unrelated to \*fulla- 1.

\*fulmo- f. 'palm of the hand' - ME folm f. 'id.', OS folmos f.pl. 'both hands', OHG fol ma f. 'palm' ::) \*plh₂-m-eh₂- (IE) - Gr. mv.aμT) f. 'palm of the hand' ( = Lat. pa/ma) < \*plh₂-em-eh₂-; Olr. ltim f. 'hand' < \*plh₂-m-ehi-. An old m-stem. Within Germanic, it is possible to connect ON fa/ma w.v. 'to grope' < \*falmojan-, a verb possibly derived from a lost mo-stem \*falmaz < \*polh₂-mo-.

\*fundon- w.v. 'to try to find(?)' - OE fundian w.v. 'to try to find, to strive, intend', OS fundon w.v. 'to strive, proceed' ::) \*pnt-neh₂-(?) (DRV?). The iterative to \*ftnpan- (q.v.). PGm. \*fandon- can be an o-grade variant of the same iterative.

<!-- pdf-page: 200; section: dictionary -->

160 \*funhsti-

\*funhsti- f. 'fist' - OE JYst f. 'id.', E fist, MDu. vuust f. 'id.', Du. vuist c. 'id.', OHG ftlst f. 'id.', G Faust f. 'id.' ⇒ \*pn/(N-ti- (IE) - Identical to OCS pˣstb f. 'fist'. Probably a derivation from PIE \*penkwe 'five', for which see \*fimfe.

\*funhtu- adj. 'humid, moist' - OE ftlht adj. 'id.', OS ftlht adj. 'id.', MDu. vocht(e), vucht(e) adj. 'id.', Du. vochtig adj. 'id.', OHG ftlht, ftlhti adj. 'id.', G feucht adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*pnk-tu- (IE?) - Skt parika- m. 'mud' < \*pe/onk-o-(?). A u-stem adjective that became remodeled into a ja -stem in the continental WGm. languages. Possibly related to Skt parika- (if this word is of IE origin). The traditional comparison with \*fanja- 'bog' is doubtful: it would imply that both Skt. and Gm. added a k-suffix, which is rather uncertain.

\*funkon- w.v. 'to sparkle' - MDu. vonken w.v. 'to smolder; to sparkle', MHG vunken w.v. 'to shimmer, shine, sparkle', ent-vunken 'to ignite' ⇒ \*pnk-neh₂- (GM). The iterative to the strong verb \*finkan- as well as the derivational base for OHG Junko, G Funke(n), MLG vunke, MDu. vonke, Du. vonk, ME Junke 'spark' < \*funkan-. The etymon has no extra-Gm. cognates, but it is possible that the meaning 'to spark' arose in elliptic use of the verb \*fanhan- 'to catch (fire)'. The received derivation of the n-stem \*funkan- from PIE \*ph₂un- 'fire' (for which see \*for - \*fun-) is erroneous (pace Pokorny JEW: 828).

\*furdu- m. 'ford' - OE ford m. 'id.', OS ford m. 'id.', MDu. vort, voorde m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pr-tu- (IE) - Av. paratu- m. 'crossing', MP puh/, NP pul 'bridge', Lat portus m. 'entrance, port, refuge', OW rit, W rhyd m. 'ford' < \*pr-tu-. Also cf. the full grade ON fjpror m. 'fjord' < \*ferpu- < \*per-tu-.

\*furh· f. 'furrow' - ON for f. 'id.', OE furh f. 'id.', E furrow, OFri. furch f. 'id.', MDu. vore f. 'id.', Du. voor c. 'id.', OHG furh, furuh d. 'id.', G Furche f. 'id.' ⇒ \*pfk- (IE) - Skt. pcirsiina- m. 'rift' < \*pe/or/(-ono-; Lat. porca f. 'ridge between two furrows' < \*p(o)rl<-eh₂-; Mir. etarche, etrige, eitre f. 'id.' < \*h₁enter-prl<-ieh₂-; MW rhych m./ f. 'furrow'? An archaic root noun \*furh-z, dat. \*furh-i (cf. OE ffrh). WFri.furg and Du. dial.furg (in the originally Frisian territories) point to a Verner variant OFri. \*furg (cf. Weijnen 1996: 228), which is in agreement with the reconstruction of a root noun \*p6rk-s, gen. \*prk-es, dat. \*prk-f. Also cf. the ablauting Nw. dial.Jere m. 'ridge between two furrows' < \*ferhan-.

\*furhno· f. 'trout' - OE forn(e) f. 'id.', OS forhna.furhnia f. 'id.', MDu. voorne f. '?', Du. voorn c. 'roach', OHG forhana f. 'trout', MHG forhen, forhel f. 'id.' ⇒ \*pfl<-neh₂- (IE) - Olr. ere m. 'perch, salmon' < \*perl<-o-.

<!-- pdf-page: 201; section: dictionary -->

\*furhta- 161 An old derivation from the root \*perl<- 'spotted', for which see \*farwa-. Note that the root stress prevented Kluge's law from operating: the outcome of\*prl<-neh₂- would have been \*furko-.

\*furhta- adj. 'fearful' - Go. faurhts adj. 'id.', OS for[a)ht adj. 'id.', OHG fora ht adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*prk-to- (IE) - ToAB priiskii- 'to fear' < \*prk-sk-. A Tocharian-Germanic isogloss; the proposed connection with Arm. erkn, pl. erkun-k' 'labor pains, pang; fear' is formally problematic because the form \*pergw- as reconstructed by Pedersen 1906: 398-9 would give Arm. \*\*herk-. See further \*furhtjan-.

\*furhtjan- w.v. 'to fear' - Go.faurhtjan w.v. 'id.', OEfyrhtan.fryhtan w.v. 'to frighten; tremble with fear', ME frighten w.v. 'to frighten, become frightened', OFri. fruchta w.v. 'id.', OHG furihtan w.v. 'to fear', G ftirchten w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The formation \*furhtjan- unites two different formations: 1) a simple denominative verb 'to fear' created to \*furhto-, and 2) a factitive 'to frighten' derived from \*furhta- (q.v.).

\*furht6· f. 'fright' - OFri. fruchta f. 'id.', OS forhta f. 'id.', OHG forhta f. 'id.', MHG vorhte, vorht f. 'id.' ⇒ \*prk-t-eh₂· (DRV). See \*furhta- and \*furhtjan-.

\*furhwon- f. 'fir' - ON Jura f. 'id.', OE furh f. 'id.', OHG voraha, vorha f. 'id.', MHG vorhe f. 'id.', G Forhe f. 'id.' ⇒ \*pfkw-eh₂- (IE). A zero-grade formation related to \*ferhwa- 1 'oak' (q.v.).

\*furi adv.jprep. 'in front of, for' - Go. faur adv.jprep. 'id.', ON fyr prep. 'id.', OE for, fore prep. 'id.', E for, OS for, fora prep. 'id.', Du. voor prep./adv. 'for; before', OHG furi prep./adv. 'id.', G ftir prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*prh₂·i (IE) - Gr. nap, napu, napa( adv./prep. 'besides, by, from, next to, alongside', Lat. prae adv./prep. 'in front of, before' < \*prh₂(-ei).

\*firjan- w.v. 'to castrate' - OE [a-)fYran w.v. 'id.', Du. vuren, vieren w.v. 'id.', OHG ar-firian w.v. 'id.' (NEUR) - Lith. pjauti (pjauju) 'to cut, slaughter', Latv. p]ailt 'to mow, harvest' < \*peuH-e- (cf. OPru. piuclan 'scythe' < \*peuH-tlo-). A factitive to an unattested adj. \*fira-, cf. OHG ur-fir m. 'eunuch' < \*JUra< \*puH-ro-.

\*fursa- m. 'gush(?)' - ON fors,foss n. 'torrent; waterfall', Far. fossur m. 'id.', OSw. fors m. 'id.', Elfd. fuoss m. 'id.' ⇒ \*pfs-o- (IE) - Hitt. papparszi papparfonzi 'to sprinkle' < \*po-pars-ti, \*-enti; ToA priisii·, ToB piirsii- 'id.' < \*pers-; Skt. pf$ant- adj. 'speckled, spotted'; Lith. puFslas m. 'spatter, spray' <

<!-- pdf-page: 202; section: dictionary -->

162 \*fursko- \*prs-/o-; OCS prax'b m. 'dust', Ru. p6rox m. '(gun-)powder', SCr. priih m. 'dust, powder' < \*pors-o-; Ru. dial. perx 'dandruff, SCr. dial. pfh 'coal dust', Slov. pfh 'dust, mold' < \*prs-o-; OCS prbstb, Ru. arch. perst' f. 'dust, earth', Latv. pirkstis m. 'spark in ashes, heat' < \*prs-ti( o )-. Also cf. Icel., Far.fossa, Elfd.fuossa w.v. 'to gush, cascade' < \*fursojan-.

\*fursko- f. 'inquiry' - OHG forsca f. 'id.', MHG vorsche f. 'id.' ⇒ \*prK-sk-eh₂- (IE/DRV) - Skt prchOti 'to ask', Av. parasaiti 'to ask, want', Lat posco 'to ask, require', Olr. arcaid 'to ask', W archaf'id.' < \*prK-ske-. A feminine \*prl{-sk-eh₂- derived from the well-attested IE sk-present \*prK-ske-. It again served as the basis for the verb \*furskojan-, cf. OHG forscon, G forschen, Du. vorsen 'to inquire, investigate'. See also \*frehnanand \*frego-.

\*furtu- m. 'fart' - MDu. vort m. 'id.', OHG furz m. 'id.', G Furz m. 'id.' ⇒ \*prd-u- (IE). A u-stem to the strong verb \*fertan- (q.v.).

\*futon- w.v. 'to go' - Nw.fota w.v. 'to step; to go' (DRV). The iterative to \*fetan- (q.v.).

\*fuPi- f. 'vagina' - 0 N fuo f. 'id.', Icel. fuo f. 'vagina; holes in a block of wood (for fishing rods)', Nw. fud f. 'vagina; anus, backside', MHG vut f. 'vagina' ⇒ \*put-i- (GM). Usually connected with the root of \*[Ula- 'putrid' (cf. Pokorny IEW: 848-9), but in view of the Ice!. verb fuoa 'to drill a hole in a tree' and feroa-fuoa 'buckle; travel bag', this seems impausible. MHG votze (cf. LG hunds-fott, Du. honds-vod 'worthless guy', lit 'dog vagina') < \*futton- and ON fytta < \*futtjon- point to a geminated root \*futt-, which may have arisen in an n-stem \*fu/Jo, gen. \*futtaz < \*put-on, \*put-n-6s. No further etymology, though close to Lat. pubes f.pl. 'private parts' < \*peudh-i-.

### G

\*gadojan- w.v. 'to fit (together)' - OFri. gadia w.v. 'to engage in', MDu. gaden w.v. 'to fit, please, mate', OHG be-gaton w.v. 'to gather', MHG (ge-)gaten w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*9hodh-eh₂-ie- (DRV) - Ru. godft' (goiu) 'to wait, loiter', SCr. goditi 'to please' < \*ghodh-eie-; OCS god'b m. '(suitable) time, year', Ru. god m. 'year', SCr. god m. 'important holiday, year, right time' < \*ghodh-o-.

<!-- pdf-page: 203; section: dictionary -->

\*gadurojan- 163 Probably a denominal verb derived from an adjective \*gada- < \*ghodh-o-, cf. OHG gi-gat adj. 'suitable'. Als cf. \*gadurojan- and \*gOda-.

\*gadurojan- w.v. 'to gather' - OE gredrian w.v. 'id.', OFri. gaduria w.v. 'id.', MDu. gaderen w.v. 'id.', Du. ver-gaderen 'id.' (ORV). A verb derived from an adverb \*gadur, cf. OE to gredere, OFri. gader, gadur adv. 'together'. It is possible that the derivational base consisted of the root \*gad- (see \*gadojan-) and the locative suffix \*-r as seen in \*hwar 'where' < \*kwo-r and \*par 'there' < \*to-r. For a typologically similar formation, cf. Lith. dabai' 'now' from the root \*dhobh- 'to suit' (see \*daban-) and the same locative suffix.

\*gaidwa- n. 'lack' - Go. gaidw n. 'id.', OE giid n. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghoidh-uo- (NEUR). Although Go. gaidw can be interpreted as a \*-pwa- derivation of a PGm. root \*gai- < PIE \*ghh₂-ei- 'to be open', the link with \*gfda- 'desire' (q.v.) and the related Lith. gefsti and OCS zbdati seems more economical (Torp 1909: 133; Derksen 2008: 565).

\*gaisjan- w.v. 'to terrify' - Go. us-gaisjan\* w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghois-eie- (IE) - YAv. zae5a- adj. 'terrible' < \*ghois-o-. A causative to the root \*gfs-, cf. Go. us-geisnan w.v. 'to be aghast, terrified'. Also cf. ON geiski m. 'panic, fear' < \*gaiskan- as well as \*gaista- 'spirit'.

\*gaista- m. 'spirit, ghost' - OE giist m. 'breath, spirit, soul, ghost', E ghost, OFri. giist, gest m. 'spirit, ghost, demon', OS yest m. 'soul, vitality, spirit, demon', Du. geest m. 'spirit', OHG geist m. 'id.', G Geist m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghois-d-o- (IE). The word can be derived from \*gaisjan- 'to terrify' (q.v.) < \*ghois-eie- with a to-suffix: \*ghois-to- (cf. EWAhd: IV, 130-1). In view of the clearly denominative OE gci!stan 'id.' < \*gaistjan-, however, it is more probable that Germanic had an adjective identical to Av. zoitda- adj. 'terrible' < \*ghois-do-. The noun \*gaista- would then be parallel to Skt. Mr;Ja- m. 'anger'. Further cf. Lith. teisti (teidtiu) 'to wound' < \*\*gheis-d-ie-.

\*gait- f. 'goat' - Go. gaits f. 'id.', ON geit f. 'id.', Far. geit f. 'id.', Elfd. djiet f. 'id.', OE giit f. 'id.', E goat, OS get f. 'id.', Du. geit c. 'id.', OHG geiz f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghaid- (WEUR) - Lat haedus m. 'young goat-buck, kid' < \*ghaid-o-. Also cf. the diminutive Go. gaitein n. 'little kid' < \*gaitfna- and OE greten, OHG geizfn adj. 'of goat' < \*gaitina-, which is formally comparable to Lat. haedfnus adj. 'of a kid' < \*ghaid-ein-o-. As to the etymology, the a-vocalism in Latin as well as the fact that the Germanic word is inflected as a root noun which is common in non-IE elements - indicate that the word may have been borrowed from an indigenous European language. It has earlier been suggested that the word was adopted from Semitic \*gadj- 'goat', cf. Arab.

<!-- pdf-page: 204; section: dictionary -->

164 \*gaizajadyun, Hehr. geoi, Berb. agiiyd, at the PIE level. It is more conceivable, however, that both ltalo-Germanic \*ghaid- and PSem. \*gadi- both were borrowed from a third source (D'iakonov 1985: 132). Archaeological and genetic evidence suggests that the goat was first domesticated in the Zagros Mountains as early as ten thousand years ago (cf. Naderi et al. 2008). In all likelihood, the 'goat' word petered through an old continuum of agricultural and cattle breeding cultures that had expanded into Europe from the East in the millennia preceding the arrival of the Indo-Europeans.

\*gaiza- m. 'spear, tip' - ON geirr m. 'id.', Nw. geir m. 'leister, eel spear; small tip; ness, headland; small mackerel', OE gii.r m. 'id.', OFri. ger-, OS ger m. 'id.', MDu. geer m. 'id.', OHG ger m. 'id.', MHG ger m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghois-6- (WEUR) - Olr. gae m., MW gwaew m./f. 'spear' < \*ghaiso-. A Germanic-Celtic isogloss. In view of the a-vocalism, it has been claimed that the Celtic word was borrowed from Germanic (Matasovic 2009: 155). This is supported by the retention of an apparently more primitive meaning in the Norwegian dialects, viz. 'small tip' and esp. the presence of ablauting cognates such as ON gfsli m. 'staff, ski stick' < \*gfsla- 1 (q.v.) < \*gheis-, which is semantically close to ON geisl m. 'ski stick', geisli m. 'stick; beam, ray' < \*gaisla(n)-. Note that the appurtenance of Gr. xa1o<; m., xa1ov n. 'shepherd's staff and Skt he:;as- n. 'quickness, vigour, fire, missile' is semantically difficult.

\*galan- s.v. 'to shout, sing, chant' - ON gala s.v. 'to crow; to sing, chant', Far. gala s.v. 'to crow; to call out', Elfd. glilci s.v. 'to shout, call', OE galan s.v. 'to sing, enchant, call', MDu. galen s./w.v. 'to be noisy', OHG galan s.v. 'to sing, enchant' ⇒ \*gh6/-e- (NEUR) - Arm. gefgefem 'to sing beautifully, quiver, vibrate' < \*ghe/-ghe/-; Ru. na-galit' 'to shout, sing', galit'sja 'to mock' < \*gho/-eie-; Ru. dial. gal' f. 'fun, laughter' < \*ghof-i-. An o-grade intensive verb with a meaning 'to call' and - more secondarily - 'to chant'. It served as the basis for e.g. ON galdr m., OE gealdor n. 'fairy song' < \*gal-dra-, OHG galstar, MHG galster n. ''id.' < \*gal-stra- and the secondary causative \*goljan-. Note that the corresponding iterative \*gullon( q.v.) gave rise to the secondary strong verb \*gellan- ( q.v.).

\*gald(j)a- adj. 'barren, not (yet) pregnant' - ON geldr adj. 'id.' (also 'yielding no milk'), OSw. galder adj. 'id.', OE gielde adj. 'id.', MDu. gelde, gelt adj. 'id.', OHG gait adj. 'id.', MHG gait adj. 'id.', G gelt adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*gho/-t6- (WEUR?) - To OW gylym, MW geleu, gelyf'knife' < \*ghe/-mo-? Also cf. the factitive ON gelda w.v. 'to castrate' < \*galdjan- and the derived \*galtan- 'boar' (q.v.). The adjective can be derived from both \*gho/dh·o- and \*gho/-t6- and has been compared to Go. gilpa m. 'knife' < \*ghef-ton- (cf. OW

<!-- pdf-page: 205; section: dictionary -->

\*galgan- 165 gy{ym). Or can there be a link with the verb \*galan- in the sense of 'to bewitch' (> 'to make infertile')?

\*galgan- m. 'stake?' - Go. ga/ga m. 'stake, pole for crucifixion, gallows', ON galgi m. 'gallows', OE gealga m. 'id.', E gallows, OFri. galga m. 'id.', Du. galg c. 'id.', OHG galgo m. 'gallows, cross', G Ga/gen m. 'gallows; boom' ⇒ \*gho/gh-eh₂- (NEUR) - Identical to Lith. za/ga f. 'long, thin stake'. A word originally meaning 'stick', cf. gelgja f. 'stick, pole' < \*ga/gjon-. Fi. sa/ko, SaaN cuolggu, Mordv. salgo 'stick, pole' < PFU \*§a/ka have been claimed to be borrowed from PIE (cf. Koivulehto 2007: 240), but it is problematic, from this perspective, that the word is only attested in Baltic and Germanic. Note that the sometimes quoted link with Arm. jafk 'branch, rod, stick, staff, whip' < \*gh/g- is uncertain because of formal reasons (cf. Martirosyan 2008: 409).

\*galla/on- m./f. 'bile' - ON gall n. 'id.', Far. gall n. 'id.' ( < \*gal/a-), Elfd. go/le m. 'id.', OE geal/a m. 'id.', E gall, OS gal/a f. 'id.', Du. gal c. 'id.', OHG gal/a f. 'id.', MHG gal/e f. 'id.', G Galle f. 'id.' ⇒ \*gho/(h₃)-on- (IE) - Av. ziira- 'bile', Gr. x6A.o<; m. 'id.' < \*gho/h₃-o-; Lat. fel, Jellis n. 'id.' < \*ghe/h₃-n-(?); Lith. tu/Zis ( < \*iultis), Latv. zuf(k)ts f. 'id.' < \*gh/h₃-ti-; OCS zlbcb, Ru. zelc', SCr. ziic f. 'bile' < \*gh/h₃-ki-. An n-stem generally derived from the PIE root \*gh/h₃- 'yellow' (cf. PGm.

\*gelwa- - \*gulu-. It is possible, however, that the original word was merely influenced by this root. Problematic is the appurtenance of Lat. fe/, which points to initial \*gwh (or \*bh): the assumption of an ad hoc change \*gh > fin dialectal Latin (cf. Mallory/Adams 1997: 217; De Vaan 2008: 209) remains problematic.

\*galra- m. 'swelling(?)' - Nw. ga/der m./n. 'swelling in the foot of horses' ⇒ \*gho/H-r- (Driessen 2003) (IE) - Close to Olr. galar n. 'sickness, disease, physical pain', MW galar m. 'grief, lament' < \*gho/H-r-; also cf. Lith. iala f. 'damage' < \*ghofH-eh₂-; Hitt kallar- adj. 'inauspicious, unpropitious, baleful' < \*gho/H-ro-; ocs Z'b/'b, Ru. z/oj, SCr. zao adj. 'bad' < \*gh/H-o-. Also cf. ON galli m. 'flaw', OE geal/a m. 'skin lesion', MLG galle m. 'damage' < \*gal/an- < nom. \*gh6/H-on, gen. \*gho/H-n-6s. Nw. galder can be identical to Olr. galar if it continues \*gho/H-r- rather than \*ghe/H-r- (the latter with Joseph's rule). The appurtenance of Gr. xoMpa 'bile' (< \*gho/h₁-er-h₂-?) is more uncertain.

\*galtan- m. '(castrated) boar' - ON galti, gþltr m. 'id.' (DRV). An n-stem created to \*galdOJa- 'infertile'. The formation must in view of the (shortened) geminate have arisen prior to Kluge's law. The original Pre-Gm. paradigm probably was \*ga/do, gen. \*galttaz < \*gho/dh-on,

<!-- pdf-page: 206; section: dictionary -->

166 \*gamala- \*gho/dh-n-6s or perhaps rather with a zero-grade genitive \*ghjdh-n-6s in view of the derived feminine ON gyltr, gylta f. 'young (infertile?) sow' < Pre-Gm. \*gultt-fz, gen. \*gultt-joz < \*gh/dh-n-ih₂-s, \*gh/dh-n-ieh₂-s (OHG galza, gelza, MHG galze, gelze, MDu.gelte f. 'castrated sow' < \*gelt(t)jon- is a more recent derivation). The u-stem ON g9ltr split off from the original ace.pl. \*galtuns < \*gho/dh-n-ns (cf. ON 9rn m. 'eagle' < \*arnu- vs. < \*aran- 1).

\*gamala- adj. 'old' - ON gamall adj. 'id.', OE gamol, gomel adj. 'id.', MDu. game/ adj. 'id.', Du. gammel adj. 'rickety' ⇒ \*lfom-h₂el-o-? (DRV?). Uncertain etymology. The word has been derived from \*ghi-om-, an ablaut variant of \*ghei-m 'winter' (cf. Torp 1909: 127) on the assumption that age was measured by counting winters, but this seems too far-fetched. Perhaps from \*ga-mela- (prefix \*ga- + \*me/a- 'time') with the stress on the first syllable (Kluge 1883: 70). Or, by the same principle, from \*gam-ala- < \*lfom-h₂el-o- to \*alda- 'old' < \*h₂el-tó-. Also cf. OE gamelian, MLG game/en w.v. 'to age' < \*gamalojan- and the past participles ON gamlaor, OS gi-gamalod 'aged'.

\*gamman- m. 'stall(?); hut(?)' - ON gammi m. 'Sarni hut; earthen hut', Elfd. gamme m. 'porch', Da. gamme c. 'animal stall; earthen hut' ⇒ \*ghos-m6- (EUR) - Arm. gom 'fold (for cattle)' < \*ghos-mo-. The comparison of the Germanic and Armenian words implies a proto-form \*ghos-m6- (Olsen 1999: 198), as \*-zm- regularly gives PGm. \*-mm-. The root of this formation can perhaps be identified with \*ghes- 'to eat', cf. YAv. gaT)har.iti 3pl.pres. 'id.' < \*ghes-e-.

\*ganazan- m. 'gander' - OE gandra, E gander, MLG ganre m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghh₂en-os-on- (IE). A formation derived from the root of \*gans- 'goose' (q.v.) < PIE ghh₂en-sby adding the masculine suffix \*-azan-, for which cf. \*katazan- 'tomcat'.

\*ganen- w.v. 'to open' - ON gana w.v. 'to gape; gaze, stare' ⇒ \*ghh₂-neh₂- (IE) - Gr. XUCJKW 'to yawn' < \*ghh₂-ske-; Gr. xaivw 'to yawn' < \*ghh₂-n-ie- and xavo<; n. 'throat, mouth' < \*ghh₂-n-os-. A stative verb, in all likelihood a remodeling of an original nasal present \*ghh₂-neh₂·, cf. Gr. xatvw and xaoKW (the \*n is unlikely to be radical in view of e.g. Gr. xao<;). Also cf. ON gan n. 'yawning' and the secondary Far. gana w.v. 'to crane one's neck; to gape, stare' < \*genen- as well as Far. g6na w.v. 'to stare, gape' < \*gonen-.

\*gangan- - \*gungan- s.v. 'to go' - Go. gaggan s.v. 'id.', ON ganga s.v. 'id.', OE gangan, gongan s.v. 'id.', OFri. gunga s.v. 'id.', OS gangan s.v. 'id.', OHG

<!-- pdf-page: 207; section: dictionary -->

\*gangi- 167 gangan s.v. 'id.', MHG gangen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*gh6ngh-e- (IE) - Lith. ieflgti (iengiu) 'to stride' < \*ghengh-ie-. The verb was originally suppletive in Germanic, cf. the Gothic preterite iddja < \*ejjo- (also OE eode < \*ejj-Od- with secondary weak ending), but in NWGm. we find a reduplicated preterite \*geanga - \*geangume, cf. ON gekk - gengum, OHG gieng, giengum. OFri. gungan is probably to be analyzed as a zero-grade form \*gungan- (cf. recently Mottausch 1996), although the u-vocalism has also been explained by internal Frisian developments (cf. Seebold 1970: 213). From the PIE perspective, however, it is likely that the Gm. strong verbs with a-vocalism evolved from the class of athematic verbs corresponding to the Hittite bi-verbs with similar o/£'-ablaut The Frisian zero grade would then be a vestige of the original paradigm 3sg. \*gh6ngh-ti, 3pl. \*ghngh-enti. See also \*gangi- and \*ganhti-.

\*gangi· adj. 'passable' - ON gengr adj. 'able to walk', OE genge adj. 'effective', OFri. genze adj. 'current, valid', MDu. genge adj. 'current, valid', OHG gengi adj. 'current', MHG genge adj. 'id.', G giingig adj. 'current, usual' (ORV). The gerundive to \*gangan- - \*gungan- (q.v.).

\*ganhen- w.v. 'to inspect; to take into account(?)' - ON ga w.v. 'to heed', Ice!. ga w.v. 'to inspect', Far. g<ia w.v. 'to notice, become aware of, Elfd. gq w.v. 'to notice; to observe' (GM). The widely accepted etymological connection with OCS goveti 'to revere' (cf. Torp 1909: 121) and Lat faveo 'to be favorable' < \*gwhou-eh₁- (cf. Schrijver 1991: 441-2), implying a PGm. form \*gawen-, is contradicted by the nasal vowel of Elfd. gq (also cf. Elfd. gq-los 'indifferent' = Far. g<i(a)-leysur < \*ganha-lausa-) as noticed by Noreen 1886: 16-17. Similarly, the derived ON gceta w.v. 'to watch, tend, take care of must be reconstructed as \*ganhatjan- rather than \*gawatjan- in view of the nasal vowel of Elfd. gf;ta. It follows that the apparently obvious link with \*gauma- 'attention' must be abandoned.

\*ganhti· f. 'doorway' - Go. fram-gahts f. 'progress', ON gátt f. 'rabbet of a doorpost; doorway', Far. gatt f. 'threshold; doorway', Elfd. gqtt f. 'doorpost' (ORV). A ti-stem to \*gangan- - \*gungan-. The nasality of the Proto-Germanic vowel is still attested in Elfd. gqtt. Also cf. OSw. dura-gcete n. 'doorframe' <

\*ganhtja- and - with an e-grade - MHG giht f. 'course, journey' may continue \*ginhti- < \*ghengh-ti-.

\*ganhu· adj. 'fast' - MDu. gii., gauw adj. 'fast. quick, rash', Du. gauw adv. 'soon', OHG gii.hi adj. 'fast, sudden', G jiih adj. 'sudden, precipitous' (GM).

<!-- pdf-page: 208; section: dictionary -->

168 \*gans- Also cf. OHG gii.hida f. 'immediacy', OE gehou f. 'stir, trouble' < \*ganhifloand OHG gii.hen, gii.hOn w.v. 'to hurry'. The Dutch forms point to a labial element that cannot be retrieved from OHG gii.hi. It has been claimed that it was introduced analogically after e.g. MDu. blii., gen. blii.wes 'blue' (cf. Franck 1949: 176), but it seems more likely that the labial is original. I therefore reconstruct a u-stem \*ganhu-, assuming that it was remodeled into a ja-stem in Old High German. Heidermanns 1993: 231 reconstructs \*ganha-, on the other hand. No further etymology. Unrelated to \*gangan- - \*gungan'to go' (q.v.) and Lith.guvus adj. 'quick, agile'.

\*gans- f. 'goose' - ON gas, pl. gress f. 'id.', Far. gas, pl. gees f. 'id.', Elfd. gqs, pl. gˣser f. 'id.', OE gos f. 'id.', E goose, WFri. goes c. 'id.', EFri. Gous f. 'id.', Du. gans c. 'id.', OHG gans f. 'id.', G Gans f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghh₂en-s- (IE) - Skt. hal'!lsa- m. 'goose', Gr. xilv, xr1v6<; m./f. 'id.', Olr. geis f. 'swan', OPru. sansy 'goose', Lith. iqsis f. 'id.', Ru. gus' 'id.' < \*ghh₂en-s-; Lat. ii.nser m.(f.) 'id.' ( < \*ghh₂ens-ro-) < \*ghh₂en-s-. Most of the forms in the European languages can be derived from \*gheh₂n-s- (with Osthoff's law), but the short vowel of Skt. hal'!ls<i- proves that the actual proto-form was \*ghh₂en-s-. Also cf. \*ganazanand \*ganuta(n)- 'gander' (q.v.). The latter formation has a variant \*gunuta- in G dial. Gunz, which points to a zero-grade root \*ghh₂p- that split off from the original genitive \*ghh₂n-s-6s. The same zero-grade genitive has been postulated to account for the depalatalization in PSlav. \*gpsb (Derksen 2008: 184).

\*ganta- adj. 'whole, entire' - OHG ganz adj. 'id.', G ganz adj. 'id.' (GM/DRV?). A High German word that is assumed to have spread to Frisian, Dutch and Low German, cf. OFri. gans, MDu. gansch, Du. gans, MLG gans, ganz adj. 'entire' (hence Da., Nw. ganske, Sw. ganska adv. 'quite'). Etymology unclear. Not to OLith. gandiiaus comp. 'rather, better', Lith. ne-gandas adj. 'dissatisfied' as proposed by Heidermanns 1993: 232: these nominal formations were coined to gand&i 'to be satisifed', a late derivation from gana 'enough'. Not related either to Gr. xavl>ov 'with opened mouth' (pace Schmidt 1913/14). There is a factitive OHG genzen w.v. 'to safeguard', G er-gii.nzen 'to add, complement' < \*gantjan-, and in view of the meaning of the pertaining Swi. an-gii.nzen w.v. 'to tap; to cut (off); to touch', it is possible to assume a link with G Rhnl. gunzen w.v. 'to cut off (vine shoots)' < \*gunton-. The semantic link remains unclear, however, and it is not unlikely that two different verbs merged here. I therefore tentatively propose a link with Swi. er-ginzen w.v. 'to cringe, be startled': if this verb continues strong \*gintan(q.v.), the meaning of the adjective \*ganta- may have developed from 'terribly' to 'very much' in adverbial use.

<!-- pdf-page: 209; section: dictionary -->

\*ganuta(n)- 169

\*ganuta(n)- m. 'gander' - OE ganot m. 'id.', MDu. gent m. 'id.', Du. jan-van-gent 'gannet', OHG ganzo, ganazzo m. 'gander', MHG ganze m. 'id.' (IE). A formation derived from the root of \*gans- 'goose' (q.v.) < PIE ghh₂en-sby adding the animal suffix \*-uta- (for which cf. \*heruta- 'deer') or the variant \*-ita- as in MDu. gent. In addition, G Rhnl., Lorr. Gunz m. 'gander' points to a variant \*gunuta- with zero grade of the root It may preserve the oblique stem of an originally ablauting paradigm \*gans, gen. \*gunzaz < \*ghh₂en-s, \*ghh₂n-s-6s.

\*gapp/bon- w.v. 'to joke' - ON gabba w.v. 'to mock, make game of one', OE gabbian w.v. 'to mock; to chat', OFri. gabbia w.v. 'to denounce, accuse, prosecute', MLG gabben, gabberen w.v. 'to joke, monkey about', MDu. gabben w.v. 'to joke', Du.ginne-gappen 'id.' (GM). An iterative formation 3sg. \*gappopi-, 3pl. \*gabunanpi < \*ghHbh-neh₂-, no doubt related to \*gapon- 'to gape', cf. ON gapa, OE gapian, Du. gapen, (M)HG gaff en, cf. for the shift of meaning: Nw. dial. gapa w.v. 'to gape; gaze; insult'. Lith. gabli6ti and Pol. gabac 'to vex' were borrowed from Low German.

\*garda- m. 'courtyard' - Go. gards m. 'house, household enclosure, courtyard', ON garor m. 'fence, wall; yard; courtyard, court', Far. garour m. 'fence; enclosure; farm; ring; gust of wind creating ripples in the water', OE geard m. 'id.', OS gard m. 'field, ground, house', OHG gart m. 'enclosure' ⇒ \*ghordh-o- (IE) - Lith. gaFdas m. 'fold, pen', OCS grad'b m. 'city, town', Alb. gardh m. 'hedge, fence' < \*ghordh-o-; also cf. Skt.g[hti- m. 'house' < \*gh[dh-6-. An o-stem derived from the root \*gherdh-, for which see \*gerdan-. The . original Proto-Indo-European meaning 'circle' has been preserved in Faroese, cf. s/Qin garo um ein 'to form a (defensive) circle around someone'. Also cf. OFri. garda m. 'family property, court', OS gardo m. 'garden', OHG garto, G Garten m. 'id.' < \*gardan-.

\*garno- f. 'intestines' - ON g9rn f. 'id.' ⇒ \*gh6r-n-eh₂- (EUR) - Lith. iarna f. 'intestine'; Lat. hernia f. 'hernia' < \*gher-n-ieh₂-. Also cf. the collective OE micgern, OS mid-garni, OHG mitti-garni n. 'paunch-fat' < \*midja-garnja- and the semantically more secondary ON garn, OE gearn, OHG garn, G Garn 'yarn' < \*garna-. Apparently, intestines were used for making strings.

\*garp/bOn- / \*gurp/bOn- w.v. 'to gobble; to belch' - Far. gorpa w.v. 'to cough violently; to vomit', Nw. garpa, garva, gurpa w.v. 'to talk loudly; belch; gormandize' ⇒ \*ghorbh-neh₂- (IE) - Hitt. karti.pi - karipanzi 'to devour, to consume' < \*gh6rbh-ei, \*ghrbh-enti.

<!-- pdf-page: 210; section: dictionary -->

170 \*garwa- An iterative 3sg. \*garpopi, 3pl. \*garbunanpi beside 3sg. \*gurpopi, 3pl. \*gurbunanpi < \*gh(o]rbh-neh₂-. The ablaut doublet is a direct reflection of the o/¢ ablaut of the corresponding bi-verb in Hittite (Kroonen 2012). In Indo-European, the root may have come about as an onomatopoeia.

\*garwa- adj. 'ready, finished' - ON gprr adj. 'id.', OE gearo adj. 'id.', OS garu adj. 'id.', Du. gaar adj. 'id.', OHG garo adj. 'id.', G gar adj. 'id.' (IE). Probably a prefixed variant of \*arwa-, i.e. \*ga-arwa-, for which see \*garwjan-.

\*garwjan- w.v. 'to prepare, make ready' - ON g¢r(v)a, gera w.v. 'to prepare; do', Far. gera w.v. 'to do', Elfd. djiira w.v. 'id.', OE gierwan w.v. 'to prepare, put on, clothe, adorn', E to gear, OS garwian, gerwian w.v. 'to provide, prepare, dress', OHG garawen w.v. 'to prepare, equip, decorate', G gerben w.v. 'to tan' (DRV). A factitive verb derived from \*garwa- 'ready, quick' (q.v.). For a variant without the prefix \*ga-, cf. G Wais. arwen 'to toil' (Schott 1842: 266) < \*arwojan-? Also cf. OS gi-garwi, gi-gerwi n. 'clothing, official dress' < \*garwja-.

\*gasti- m. 'guest' - Go. gasts m. 'id.', ON gestr m. 'id.', Far. gestur m. 'id.', Elfd. djiist m. 'id.', OE giest m. 'id.', OFri. jest m. 'id.', OS gast m. 'id.', Du. gast c. 'id.', OHG gast m. 'id.', G Gast m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghost-i- (EUR/WEUR?) - OCS gostb, Ru.gost', SCr.gost m . 'guest', Lat. hostis m. 'stranger, enemy' < \*ghost+. The word denoted the opposite of IE \*sokH-i- 'associate' (see \*sagja- 1) in the West Indo-European area, and it is therefore likely that the i-suffixes are parallel. ON gista w.v. 'to stay the night' < \*gestjan- apparently implies an e-grade of the root, but it has also been claimed that it represents a Nordic "forth-formation" to gestr (De Vries 1962: 168).

\*gata- n. 'hole' - ON gat n. 'id.', OE geat n. 'gate', E gate, OFri. jet n. 'hole', OS gat n. 'id.', Du. gat n. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghod-o- (IE). The word has been compared to Skt. htidati 'to shit' < \*ghed-e- and Gr. xt0w 'id.' < \*ghed-ie- (cf. Lehmann 1986: 151), but the meaning 'anus', which occurs in several Germanic languages, is secondary. Instead, the word is to be compared to \*getan- 'to find (a way)' and the closely related \*gatwon'road, way' ( q.v.). The meaning 'hole' can easily have developed from 'passage', cf. OE geat 'gate' (notwithstanding the possibility that this word was borrowed from Old Norse or Middle Low German).

\*gatwon- f. 'road, alley' - Go. gatwo f. 'street, main road', ON gata f. 'path, way, road', MLG gate f. 'alley, street', OHG gazza f. 'id.', G Gasse f. 'id.' ⇒ \*9hod-ueh₂- (IE).

<!-- pdf-page: 211; section: dictionary -->

\*gap ma- 171 Related to \*getan- 'to find (a way)' (q.v.). Also cf. \*gata- 'hole'. The difference between EGm. \*gatwon- and NGm. \*gaton- can be resolved by assuming that ON gata is a back-formation from the oblique case of a paradigm \*gptva, \*gptu (Noreen 1904: §84), an analogy that is also evident in e.g. ON tjara f. 'tar' < \*terwa/on- (q.v.).

\*gapma- m. 'cabin, room' - MDu. gadem m./n. 'id.', OHG gadum, gadem n. 'id.', MHG9adem, 9aden n. 'cabin, barn' (ORV?). Of unknown origin. It is possible that the formation must be analyzed as \*ga-pmo- < \*ghH-tmo- (EWAhd: IV, 6-7), but the proposed link with \*ghehr 'to go' (see \*gen-) is tenuous. It seems more attractive to assume that the WGm. form \*gapma- developed from \*gadma- by Bahder's law, i.e. the devoicing of voiced fricatives before resonants. If that is the case, the link with \*gadojan- 'to suit' (Torp 1909: 123) can theoretically be maintained in view of the parallel derivation of G Ge-mach, Du. ge-mak n. 'bower, chamber' from \*makojan- 'to match' (see \*maka-).

\*gauja- adj. 'district, region' - Go. gawi n. 'id.', OE -ge n. 'id.', OFri. gii., ge n. 'village, parish', MLG go n. 'county', MDu. gou(w), gooy n. 'id.', MHG gou, gen. gouwes n. 'id.', G Gau n. 'id.' (EUR?). Etymology uncertain. The early 19th century comparison with Arm. gawat 'district' (cf. Seebold 196 7: 128ff.) is doubtful in view of Oss. qa?w / ga?w 'village'. The link with Gr. o'(TJ f. 'village' < \*Hou-ieh₂- can theoretically be maintained by postulating a prefixed formation \*ga-auja- < \*Hou-io-, but nevertheless remains uncertain (Beekes 2010: 1055). Lehmann 1986: 153 assumes a link with \*aujo- 'wetland' in view of the frequent combination of the word with river names in toponyms.

\*gaujan- w.v. 'to throw; to spawn' - MDu. goyen w.v. 'id.', Du. gooien w.v. 'to throw' ⇒ \*gh6u-eie- (IE?). Perhaps related to the PIE root \*gheu- 'to pour', for which see \*geutan-.

\*gauka- m. 'cuckoo' - ON gaukr m. 'id.', OE geac m. 'id.', MDu. gooc m. 'cuckoo; fool', OHG gouh m. 'id.', MHG gouch m. 'id.' (GM) - Close to Lith. geguze f. 'cuckoo' < \*9he-9hugh-ieh₂-. An old onomatopoetic word. Also cf. Cimb. kucko m. 'id.'.

\*gauma- m. 'heed, attention' - ON gaumr m. 'id.', Far. geymur, geymi m. 'id.', MDu. goom m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghough-mo- (IE) - Skt. g6.hati 'to hide' (with analogical ii, Goto 1987: 296-7), YAv.guzaeta 3sg.opt. 'id.' < \*ghugh-e-. In spite of the apparent similarities, the word cannot be related to ON ga w.v. 'to heed', as this verb continues \*ganhen-, not \*gawen-. I therefore propose a relationship with the lndo-lranian root \*gfwlheugh- 'to hide', as-

<!-- pdf-page: 212; section: dictionary -->

172 \*gaupnosuming that this meaning developed from a more primary 'to heed, guard'. PGm. \*gauma- can then be reconstructed as PIE \*ghough-mo- (for the loss of the root-final velar, cf. \*drauma-). Note that the semantic development from 'to heed' to 'to hide' is also found in the derived \*gaumjan-, cf. Go. gaumjan 'to notice', ON geyma 'to watch, observe, stow, hide', OE gieman 'id.', OS g6mian 'id.', OHG goumen 'id.'. See also \*gumen-.

\*gaupn6- f. 'hollow of the hand' - ON gaupn f. 'both hands held together', OHG gaufana f. 'hollow of the hand', MHG goufe f. 'id.' (NEUR?). In view of e.g. OE geap adj. 'crooked, bent, curved' < \*gaupa-, the word must have been derived from the strong verb underlying OE geopan 'to take up, swallow'. It follows that any direct comparison with Lith. ziupsnis 'hollow of the hand' (thus Pokorny IEW: 449) is erroneous.

\*gaupon- f. 'lynx' - ON gaupa f. 'id.', Far. geypa f. 'id.', Nw. gaupe f. 'id.' (GM). An exclusively Nordic word with no certain etymology. It seems attractive to me, however, to connect the word to Swi. Rhntl. g6ppa 'to play (of cats)' < \*gaubb-. Unrelated to \*geupan- 'to be hollow' (q.v.).

\*gaura- adj. 'sad' - Go. gaurs adj. 'grieved, pained, sad', OHG g6rag adj. 'poor, little, meager' ( < \*gauraga-) ⇒ \*ghour-o- (IE) - Skt. ghora- 'terrible' < \*ghou-ro-; Lith. ziaurus adj. 'cruel, savage' < \*gheu-ru- (whence Ru. forit' (forju) 'to reprove, scold', SCr. zuriti se 'to hurry, complain'); Olr. gure f. 'pain; pangs' < \*ghou-r-ieh₂-. Also cf. Go. gauripa f. 'sadness' < \*gaurip6-. The appurtenance of the ablauting inchoative OS gornon w.v. 'to lament, be worried' < \*gurn6n- is uncertain in view of the variants grornon 'to be worried' and gnornon 'to lament'.

\*gazda- m. 'prickle' - Go. gazds m. 'sting, goad', ON gaddr m. 'goad, spike'. Ice!. gaddr m. 'goad, spike', OS gard m. 'rod', OHG gart m. 'prickle', MHG gart m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghazdh·o- (WEUR) - Olr. gat m. 'withe, osier' < \*ghazdh·o-; Lat. hasta f. 'spear-shaft, lance', Olr. gas m./f. 'sprig, shoot, twig' < \*ghast-o/eh₂-. A Germanic-Celtic isogloss; Olr. gat and PGm. gazda- are formally identical, but Lat. hasta and Olr. gas point to a variant with a voiceless dental. Since this variation cannot possibly be of IE origin, it is likely that the word originates from a local European language. For a similar alternation of -zdhand \*-st-, see \*Japan- - \*luppan- 'lath'. Also cf. OS gerda, OFri. jerde, OE gierd, Eyard, OHG garta, gerta, G Gerte f. 'rod, stick' < \*gazdjon-.

\*geban- s.v. 'to give' - Go. giban s.v. 'id.', ON gefa s.v. 'id.', Far. geva s.v. 'id.', Elfd. djiiva s.v. 'id.', OE giefan s.v. 'id.', E to give, OFri. jeva,jewa, jowa, jouwa s.v. 'id.', OS geban s.v. 'id.', Du. geven s.v. 'id.', OHG geban s.v. 'id.', G geben s.v.

<!-- pdf-page: 213; section: dictionary -->

\*gebi- 173 'id.' ⇒ \*lfo(m)-h₁ep- (IE) - Hitt. epzi, appanzi 'to take, seize' < \*h₁ep-ti, \*h₁p-enti; Skt. iipn6ti 'to reach, gain, take possession of < \*h₁e-h₁p-neu-; Lat. aplscor 'to seize, obtain' < \*h₁p-i-ske-; Alb. jap,jep 'to give' < \*h₁op-eie-. The verb is usually compared to Olr. gaibid 'to take, hold' < \*ghHbh-ie- (cf. Pokorny JEW: 407-9), but this is formally impossible because of the laryngeal. LIV2: 193 connects Lith. gebJti (gebU) 'to be able; to be used to' < \*ghebh-eh₁-ie-, but the semantics are problematic. An alternative etymology was offered by Kortlandt 1992: 104-5, who assumed a remodeling of \*ga-ef < \*lfo(m)-h₁ep-, thus establishing a direct connection with Lat. coepl 'seized' < \*lfo(m)-h₁eh₁p-. See also \*gebi-, \*geb6- and \*geb6n-.

\*gebi· adj. 'having been given' - ON grefr adj. 'quiet, meek', Far. grevur adj. 'doughty, excellent, honest', OFri. jeve, geve adj. 'usual(?)', MLG geve adj. 'pleasant', MHG grebe adj. 'acceptable, enjoyable, convenient' (ORV). The V[ddhi-gerundive to \*geban- (q.v.). The original meaning 'to be given, having been given' developed into a whole range of derived meanings in the daughter languages.

\*gebla(n)· - \*gabla- m. 'top' - Go. gibla m. 'pinnacle', OHG gebal m. 'id.', MHG gebel m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghebhh₂-1-o- (rn) - ToA spiil 'head', Gr. K£q>exA.i) f. 'head, the uppermost or top part' < \*ghebhh₂-l-. Also cf. OHG gibil, G Giebel, MDu. gevel m., Du. gevel c. 'gable' < \*gebilaand - with another root vowel - ON gafl m. 'gableside, point of an island' < \*gab/a- < \*ghobh(h₂)-lo-. The ablaut in the root and the suffix could point to an originally ablauting /-stem, e.g. \*ghebh-(o)l, gen. \*ghbh-/-6s, acc.

\*ghobh-e/-m. For another o-grade accusative, cf. \*belkan- - \*balkan- - \*bulkan- 'beam'.

\*geb6- f. 'gift, present' - Go. giba f. 'id.', ON gjpf f. 'id.', OE giefu f. 'id.', OFri. jeve,jowe,jouwe f. 'id.', OS geba, giba f. 'id.', OHG geba f. 'id.', MHG gebe f. 'id.' (ORV). Derived from \*geban- (q.v.).

\*geb6n- f. 'gift; giving' - ON gafa f. 'id.', Far. gava f. 'id.', Elfd. gava f. 'id.', MDu. gave f. 'id.', Du. gave c. 'id.', MHG giibe f. 'id.' (ORV). Derived from \*geban- (q.v.).

\*gela· m. 'lust' - OSw. girel-skaper m. 'unchastity' ⇒ \*ghe/-o- (NEUR) - OCS ze/eti 'to covet' < \*ghe/-ehr. Also cf. MDu. galen w.v. 'to long for, covet'.

\*geldan- s.v. 'to pay, be worth something' - Go. fra-gildan s.v. 'to repay, compensate', ON gjalda s.v. 'to repay, pay for', Far. gjalda s.v. 'to pay, suffer',

<!-- pdf-page: 214; section: dictionary -->

174 \*gellan- OE gieldan s.v. 'to yield, pay, repay', E to yield, OS geldan s.v. 'to pay, reward', Du. gelden s.v. 'to be true, valid', OHG geltan s.v. 'to compensate, pay, repent', G gelten s.v. 'to apply, count' ⇒ \*gheldh-e- (EUR) - OCS zlesti (i/edp) 'to pay, compensate' < \*gheldh-e-; Olr. gell n.(m.) 'pledge surety, promise' < \*gheldh-o-. A European word with potential cognates only in Celtic and Slavic (the sometimes compared Gr. TEA:Boc; n. 'debt, payment due' cannot be related, as it points to \*gwheldh-os- rather than \*gheldh-os-). Also cf. Go. gild 'tax', ON gjald, OE gield, OFri. jeld, OS geld, OHG gelt n. 'payment, compensation' < \*gelda-.

\*gellan- s.v. 'to sound, yell' - ON gjalla s.v. 'to yell, scream', Far. gjalla s.v. 'to sound', OE giellan 'to yell', E to yell, MDu. gellen, gillen s./w.v. 'to yell', Du. gillen w.v. 'id.', OHG gellan s.v. 'to sound, yell', MHG gellen s.v. 'to sound, cry', G gellen w.v. 'to yell' (DRY). The verb is sometimes derived from a Pre-Gm. n-present \*ghel-n-, but it is more likely that it arose as a back-formation to \*gullon-, the iterative to \*galan- (q.v.).

\*gelwa- - \*gulu- adj. 'yellow' - ON gulr adj. 'id.', Far. gulur adj. 'id.', Elfd. guol adj. 'id.', OE geolo adj. 'id.', Eyellow, OS gelo, gelu adj. 'id.', OHG gelo adj. 'id.', MHG gel adj. 'id.', G gelb adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*gh(e)lh₃-u(o)- (IE) - Lat helvus adj. 'yellow, dun', Lith. zeivas 'greenish' < \*ghelh₃-UO-; Skt. hcfri- (hiri- in compounds), Av. zairi- 'yellow, greenish' < PIE \*ghelh₃-i-; Gr. xA.wp6c; 'pale green, greenish yellow' < \*ghlh₃-r6-; Lith. zelti (zeliu) 'to grow, flourish', Latv. zeft < \*ghelh₃-ie-; OCS zelen'b 'green', Ru. zelenyj < \*ghelh₃-en-o-. Since ON gulr does not show any traces of a-mutation, like e.g. holr 'hollow' < \*hula-, it is likely to coninue an old u-stem \*gulu-. It is further theoretically possible that both the full grade \*gelwa- and the zero grade \*gulucontinue an ablauting u-stem, viz. \*geluz, gen. \*gulwaz < \*ghelh₃-u-s, \*ghlh₃-u-6s (for which cf. \*blewa- 'blue').

\*gen- suppl.v. 'to go' - Far. poet. gci suppl.v.(?) 'id.', Nw. ga suppl.v. 'id.', OSw. ga suppl.v. 'id.', Elfd. go suppl.v. 'id.', EDa. ga suppl.v. 'id.', Da. gti suppl.v. 'id.', OE grin suppl.v. 'id.', E to go, OFri. giin, gen suppl.v. 'id.', OS giin suppl.v. 'id.', MDu. gaen suppl.v. 'id.', Du. gaan suppl.v. 'id.', OHG giin, gen suppl.v. 'id.', G gehen suppl.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghehi- (IE) - Skt. jahiiti 'to leave behind', OAv. auua-zaza! 3pl.pres.inj. 'id.'; Gr. KL)(T]μL 'to overtake, reach, arrive, meet' < \*ghe/i-gheh₁-. The derivation of this Germanic verb, which existed alongside \*gangan( q.v.), is not straightforward. Most NWGm. forms point to \*gen-, including Scand. ga, which in view of the nasalization of Selbumalet ga cannot be explained as a Low German loan. OHG giin, gen, for instance, can be derived

<!-- pdf-page: 215; section: dictionary -->

\*gerdan- 175 from an athematic present \*gemi, \*gesi, \*gepi < \*gheh₁-mi, -si, ti (pl. \*ghh₁-me, -th₁e, -enti > \*game, \*gape, \*ginpi?). OE gii.n, on the other hand, can only have developed from \*gai-, which seems to imply an i-present 3sg. \*ghhi-oi-ti, 3pl. \*ghh₁-i-enti. We may also consider the possibility that the stem \*gai- arose from \*ga-1-, i.e. the perfective prefix \*ga- plus \*-f-, the continuant of PIE root \*h₁ei- 'to go' (Kluge 1894, s.v. gehn; Kortlandt 1990). This explanation is probably supported by the co-occurrence of Go. ga-iddja and iddja 'went' < \*(ga-)ejjo.

\*gerdan- s.v. 'to gird' - Go. -gairdan\* s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*gherdh-e- (IE). The verb is reconstructed on the basis of the Gothic past participle bi-gaurdans. This form has been taken as an independent "Partizipialbildung" to \*gurdjan- ( q.v.), but Far. gjaroa 'to form circles in the water' in spite of its weak conjugation can be adduced for the reconstruction of a strong verb \*gerdan- 'to surround, encircle' (note OHG gart m. 'circular dance, round dance' < \*garda-). Within Germanic, also cf. Go. gairda, ON gjpro f. 'girdle' < \*gerdo- and the zero-grade instrumental noun ON gyro ill, OE gyrdel, OFri. gerdel, OHG gurtil, G Giirtel 'girdle' < \*gurdila-.

\*gerna- adj. 'desirous' - Go. -gairns adj. 'id.', ON gjarn adj. 'id.', Far. -gjarnur adj. 'id.', Elfd. djienna adv. 'id.', OE georn adj. 'id.', OFri. gerne, jerne adv. 'id.', OS gern adj. 'id.', Du. gaarne adv. 'id.', OHG gern adj. 'id.', G gern[e) adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*gherh₁-no- (IE) - Hitt karija(tt)a[ri) 'to be gracious', Skt htiryati 'to enjoy, like', Gr. xaipw 'to rejoice', Lat. horior 'to encourage, urge' < \*gh(e)rh₁-ie-. A na-stem adjective apparently derived from OFri. jeria, OS geron, OHG geren, -on w.v. 'to desire' < \*geren-. Also cf. Go. gairnjan, ON girna, OE giernian, OS girnean w.v. 'to desire' < \*gernjan-. In Schwebeablaut relationship to \*gredu- (q.v.).

\*gersto- f. 'barley' - OS gersta f. 'id.', Du. gerst c. 'id.', OHG gersta f. 'id.', G Gerste f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghersd-eh₂- (EUR/NIE?) - Lat. hordeum 'barley' < \*ghr(s)d-io-; Arm. gari, gen. garwoy 'wheat' < \*ghr-io-; Gr. KpLa{j, epic KpT f. 'barley', Alb. drithe 'cereal, grain' < \*ghrisdh, pl. \*ghrisdh-eh₂• PGm. \*gersto- and Lat. hordeum go back to a root \*ghersd-, but the sound correspondences with the other forms are irregular. This may point to a non-IE origin, perhaps borrowing from a European agricultural substrate. The alleged link to Hitt. karas- n. 'wheat, emmer-wheat' (cf. Kloekhorst 2008: 444) is erroneous, this word being related to \*hersja[n)- 'millet' (q.v.).

\*gestra- adv. 'the previous or next day' - Go. gistra-dagis adv. 'tomorrow', ON f grer, f gjar adv. 'yesterday; tomorrow', Icel. ( grer, gjar adv. 'yesterday', Far. f gjar adv. 'id.', OSw. f gii.r adv. 'id.', OE geostran-dreg, giestran-dreg adv.

<!-- pdf-page: 216; section: dictionary -->

176 \*getan- 'id.', E yesterday, MDu. gist(e)ren adv. 'id.', Du. gisteren adv. 'id.', OHG gestaron adv. 'id.', G gestern adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhgh-es-ro- (IE) - Gr. χθές, Alb. dje < \*dhgh-es, Lat heri < \*dhgh-es-i (with loc. ending), Skr. hytis < \*dhgh-ies (with -y- analogically after sadytis 'today' < \*srp-di-es). Go. gistra- as well as the WGm. forms unambiguously point to PGm. \*gestra- < \*dhghes-ro- with t-epenthesis between \*s and \*r (cf. \*swester- 'sister'), and directly correspond to Gr. χθές; the r-suffix is probably locatival, as in \*hwar 'where', \*par 'there' and \*gadur 'together' (see \*gadurojan-). The reconstruction of the form underlying ON gjtir and grer, on the other . hand, is problematic. Most dictionaries reconstruct an ablauting form \*dhgh-es or \*dhgh-i-es without an r-suffix. Since initial \*kj-, \*gj- and \*ghj- regularly lose their glide (cf. \*gain- and \*kewwan-), the form gjtir is usually explained from gtir (cf. OSw. i giir, Sw, Da., Nw. i gar) with analogical palatal g after grer < PN \*giiR with R-fronting (e.g Hellquist 1921: 231). As this fronting is strongest in West Norse, but surprisingly weak in Faroese (cf. Far. dj6r - Icel. dyr 'animal' < \*deuza- and Far. kar - Icel. ker 'vessel' < \*kaza-), this indeed neatly explains the opposition of Far. gjtir vs. Icel. grer. However, if the variation between the two variants is dialectally distributed, it seems unlikely that the palatal g could cross over from grer to gjtir. I would therefore like to explain both OEN giir and OWN grer as the regular outcomes of PN \*giiR, and assume that the initial palatal of gjcfr is due to the preceding preposition f. This \*gaR < PGm. \*gez presumably got its lengthened vowel from being a monosyllable (cf. \*iit < \*ud), but was explained by Gqsiorowski 2012 as resulting from z-loss before r. On the PIE level, \*dhgh-es is likely to represent the fossilized genitive of an old root noun \*dhegh-s, gen. \*dhgh-es (see \*daga-) or of the s-stem perhaps implied by ON dregr < \*dogiz- (Puhvel 1987).

\*getan- s.v. 'to find (a way), to be able' - Go. bi-gitan s.v. 'to find, discover', ON geta s.v. 'to get; to be able to', Elfd. djieta w.v. 'to try, guess', OE bi-gietan s.v. 'to beget', E to get, beget.forget, OFri. ur-ieta w.v. 'to forget', OS bi-getan s.v. 'to find', Du. ver-geten s.v. 'to forget', OHG bi-gezzan s.v. 'to obtain', fir-gezzan 'to forget', G vergessen s.v. 'to forget' ⇒ \*ghed-e- (IE) - Gr. χανδάνω 'to hold, include, contain' < \*gh-n-d-nh₂-e-(?); Lat prae-hendo, -ere 'to grasp, seize' < \*gh-(e)n-d-; Olr. ro-geinn 'to take place', MW genni fJannaf) 'to contain, find room in' < \*gh-n-d-, Alb. gjendem 'l am found' < \*gh-en-d-. In most languages, the root \*ghed- forms nasal presents, but Germanic has a thematic verb. Its original meaning seems to have been 'to seize an opportunity' or 'to find a way'. This semantic background allows for a connection with \*gatwon- 'path, way' and \*gata- 'hole' (q.v.), which are usually considered to be etymologically obscure.

<!-- pdf-page: 217; section: dictionary -->

\*geuban- - \*giiban- 177

\*geuban- - \*guban- s.v. 'to blow, rush' - Nw. gjuva, guva s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*gheubh-e- (GM). A strong verb with many different derivations in the Nordic dialects, e.g. Ice!. gjufur m. 'drizzle', Nw. gjuv n. 'haze' < \*geuba-; Nw. gauv m. 'gust, smell' < \*gauba- (whence gauva w.v. 'to smoke, foam; rush; teem with'); Nw. geyve w.v. 'to smoke, steam, seethe' < \*gaubjan-; Ice!. gufa, Far. guva f. 'steam, vapor', Nw. gove f. 'smoke, haze' < \*gubon- etc. Also cf. the iterative \*gub[b}On-.

\*geupan- s.v. 'to be hollow' - OE geopan s.v. 'to take up, swallow' (NEUR?) - Lith .gailbti 'to cover; vault' < \*ghoubh-. No clear etymology. If the frequentative E to gobble is related, it is likely that the strong verb continues Pre-Gm. \*geuppan- with a root-final geminate. The corresponding iterative 3sg. \*guppo/Ji, 3pl. \*gubunanþi would at any rate point to a formation \*ghubh-neh₂- with root-final \*bh. For the original meaning 'to vault, be hollow', cf. \*gaupno- 'hollow of the hand' and Nw. gop n. 'ravine' < \*gupa-.

\*geutan- s.v. 'to pour' - Go. giutan s.v. 'id.', ON gj6ta s.v. 'to drop, throw, cast young', Nw. gyte, gjote s.v. 'to pour, run, spawn', OE geotan s.v. 'to pout (out)', OS giotan s.v. 'id.', Du. gieten s.v. 'id.', OHG giozan s.v. 'to pour, mix, melt', G gieften s.v. 'to pour' (DRV) - Skt. juh6ti 'to pour; to sacrifice' < \*yhu-gheu-; ToAB ku- 'to pour, offer a libation', Gr. xf.w 'to pour, shed; to smelt' < \*gheu-e-. The verb is clearly derived from the PIE root \*gheu- 'to pour' (for which see \*gaujan-), but the root-final t is without counterpart in the other IE languages. Lat [undo, -ere 'to pour' is often adduced to reconstruct a root extension \*gheud- (with nasal infix in Latin), but it is not entirely certain that the roof-final PGm. -t and Lat. -d are homologous. If PGm. \*geutan- continues Pre-Gm. \*geuttan-, it must be a back-formation to the iterative \*gut[t)on- (q.v.).

\*gida- m. 'desire, greed' - MHG git m. 'greed, stinginess' ⇒ \*gheidh-o(NEUR) - Lith. geisti fgeidtia) 'to long for, desire' < \*gheidh-ie-; OCS tbdati 'to wait, await' < \*ghidh-eh₂-ie-. Also cf. OE gftsian 'to desire', MHG gftsen, G geizen 'to be stingy' < \*gfd-is-ojan- and \*gaidwa- 'lack' (q.v.).

\*gigen- w.v. 'to desire' - Go. ga-geigan w.v. 'id.', Far. giggja w.v. 'to praise; to envy' ⇒ \*ghei-ghh₂-oi- (IE) - Skt. jehamii.na- ptc. 'to open the mouth, breathe heavily' < \*yhei-ghh₂-. A stative verb continuing a reduplicated i-perfect \*ghei-ghh₂-oi- to the root \*yheh₂- as found in \*ganen- (q.v.). It is derivationally parallel to \*biben- and

<!-- pdf-page: 218; section: dictionary -->

178 \*gimban- \*riren- (Uhlenbeck 1905: 283). OHG gingen w.v. 'to desire' < \*gingen- has a nasal infix.

\*gimban- s.v. 'to hop' - MHG gimpen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghembh-e- (GM). No clear etymology. Nw. gimpa w.v. 'to hop' < \*gimpan- is the same verb, but adopted its final -p- (< \*-pp-) from the iterative \*gump/bon- (q.v.).

\*ginan- s.v. 'to gape, yawn' - ON gfna s.v. 'id.', OE to-ginan s.v. 'id.' (DRV) - Hitt. kfnuzi - kinuyanzi 'to open (up), break open' < \*ghhri-nu-; Lat. hio, -are . 'to be wide open, gape', Lith. ii6ti (ii6ju) 'to open (one's mouth)', OCS zijati 'to open (one's mouth)', Ru. zijat' (zijaju) 'yawn, gape', SCr. zljati 'yawn, shout' < \*ghh₂-i-eh₂-ie-.
