A strong verb based on \*ginen- (q.v.), a nasal present created to the i-present \*ghh₂-ei- (Lubotsky 2011: 107-9). It in turn gave rise to the iterative OE gcinian, OHG geinon 'to yawn' < \*gainon- and possibly also to OHG in-ginnan 'to open up, split' < \*ginnon- continuing quasi-PIE \*ghin-neh₂- or

\*ghh₂-i-nu-e-, cf. Hitt. kinuzi - kinyanzi. Also cf. \*giwen- and \*giwojan-.

\*ginen- s.v. 'to stand open(?)' - Far. gina w.v. 'to straddle, walk with long strides', OHG ginen w.v. 'to yawn', MHG ginen, genen w.v. 'id.', G giihnen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghh₂-i-n-eh₁- (DRV?) - OCS zinpti, Ru. razfnut', SCr. z)'nuti 'to open (one's mouth) < \*ghh₂-i-ne-. A stative verb related to \*ginan- (q.v). It seems to have been derived from an old nasal present \*ghh₂-i-neh₂- (with pretonic shortening).

\*ginnan- s.v. 'to begin' - Go. du-ginnan s.v. 'id.', OE be-ginnan s.v. 'id.', E to begin, OS be-ginnan s.v. 'id.', Du. be-ginnen s.v. 'id.', OHG bi-ginnan s.v. 'id.', G be-ginnen s.v. 'id.' (EUR?). A strong verb with no certain etymology. Possibly related to OCS po-ct;ti (po-Cbnp) 'to begin' < \*ken- and Oir. cet 'begin' < \*kn-to-: the initial \*g must then have developed from PIE \*k in a prefixed form. Others have compared Alb. ze 'to touch, catch, begin' < \*ghen-.

\*gintan- s.v.(?) 'to be startled(?)' - G Swi. er-ginzen w.v. 'to cringe, shiver' (NEUR) - Lith. gijstas m. 'scare' < \*ghond-to-; Latv. gafidincit 'to scare' < \*ghond-nh₂-. The Swiss and Baltic forms have not yet been compared, but their form and meaning point to a shared root \*ghend- 'to startle'. See also \*ganta-.

\*gisla- 1 m. 'shaft(?)' - ON gfsl m. 'staff, ski stick', Icel. gfsli m. 'beam, ray', OHG Lomb. gisil 'arrow' (WEUR). Also cf. the originally oxytone Ice!. gill, Nw. dial. gil m. 'bright spot in clouds, halo around the sun' < \*gizla- < \*gheis-16- and - with ablaut - Nw.

<!-- pdf-page: 219; section: dictionary -->

\*gisla- 2 179 dial. gjell m. 'interrupted rainbow' < \*gizla- < \*ghis-16-. Cognate with \*gaiza'tip, spear'.

\*gisla- 2 m. 'hostage' - ON gfsl m. 'id.', OE gisel m. 'id.', OFri. jesel- 'id.', OS gisal m. 'id.', Du. gijzelaar m. 'id.', OHG gisal m. 'id.', G Geisel m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghei-slo- (WEUR?) - Olr. gfall m. 'hostage', MW gwystyl, gwystl 'pledge, surety, hostage' < \*gheislo-. A Germanic-Celtic isogloss of ambiguous origin. Given the potential ablaut correlation of Olr. gfall with gel/ n. 'pledge, surety' within Celtic, it seems logical to assume that the Germanic word was borrowed from Celtic (cf. Matasovic 2008: 159). MLG gise, on the other hand, would then have to be a back-formation from the forms with -/-, which is rather uncomfortable. Moreover, Olr. gel/ can alternatively be reconstructed as \*ghe/dh·o-, thus becoming comparable to PGm. \*geldan- (q.v.).

\*giwen- w.v. 'to desire, long for' - OE giwian, giowian w.v. 'id.', OHG ana-giwen w.v. 'id.' (IE). A stative verb with the same root as \*ginan- 'to gape, yawn' (q.v.). In view of the secondary meaning 'to desire', it must be an older formation than the related stative \*ginen- (q.v.). The intervocalic \*w has a parallel in \*giwojan-. Also cf. ON gja f. 'sensuality' < \*giwo-.

\*giwojan- w.v. 'to yawn' - MDu. gewen w.v. 'id.', Du. geeuwen w.v. 'id.', OHG gewon w.v. 'id.', MHG giwen, gewen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghh₂-ei-u-eh₂-ie- (IE) - ToA sewififi· 'to yawn(?)' < \*ghih₂-u-, ToB kiiyii- 'to open' < \*ghh₂-i-u-e-(?); RuCS zevati, Ru. zevat' (zevaju) 'to yaw', dial. 'to shout, cry', SCr. zijevati 'to yawn, cry' < \*ghh₂-ei-u-eh₂-ie-. The verb, which is related to \*ginan-, was probably derived from a noun \*giwo- < \*ghh₂-i-u-eh₂· in view of the u-suffix, cf. e.g. ON gja f. 'chasm, rift, crack' < \*giwo- and OE giw, geow m. 'vulture' < \*giwa-. The labial has a parallel in Slavic and Tocharian, however, and can therefore be projected back into the IE proto-language (Lubotsky 2011: 107-9).

\*glada- adj. 'bright, happy' - ON glaor adj. 'glad; bright', Far. glaour adj. 'happy, glad', Elfd. glao adj. 'id.', OE gleed adj. 'id.', E glad, OFri. gled adj. 'smooth, slippery', Du. glad adj. 'id.', OHG glat adj. 'bright, light; smooth', G glatt adj. 'smooth, slippery' (DRY) - ?Lith. zleja f. 'dusk' < \*gh/ehi-i-eh₂-. This adjective superficially appears to continue \*ghlodh-6- or \*ghladh·o( cf. Lat. glaber adj. 'bald'), but actually represents an intra-Germanic, quasi-PIE zero-grade derivation \*gh/P1-t6- to \*gloan- < \*ghl6h₁-e-, cf. the derivation of\*blada- 'leaf from \*blOan- 'to bloom' (q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 220; section: dictionary -->

180 \*glantjan-

\*glantjan- w.v. 'to shine, be bright' - Ice!. gletta-st w.v. 'to make fun (of)', Far. gletta w.v. 'to grin, roar with laughter', Nw. gletta w.v. 'to peep', Sw. dial. gliinta w.v. 'to clear up, shine through; to be ajar' ⇒ \*ghlond-eie- (EUR). The intransitive meaning of the verb may indicate that this formal causative continues an originally intensive formation. See further \*glintan-.

\*glapp/bOn- w.v. 'to slip off - Ice!. afglapa w.v. 'to disturb, confuse', glappa-st w.v.refl. 'to fail', Sw. dial. glapa w.v. 'to be open' ⇒ \*ghlobh-neh₂- (NEUR). The o-grade iterative to \*glupp/bon- (q.v.). Also cf. Ice!. g/Opp n.pl. 'accident' < \*glappa-; ON glepja w.v. 'to confuse; to confound; to beguile' < \*glapjan- and the ablauting ON glcepr m. 'crime' < \*glopi-.

\*glasa- - \*glaza- n. 'glass' - Ice!. gler n. 'glass', Far. glas n. 'glass', gler n. '(glassy) ice', OSw. glar n. 'glass', Elfd. glas n. 'id.', OE glces n. 'id.', E glass, OHG glas n. 'id.', G Glas n. 'id.' (DRV). An old s-stem derived from \*gl6an- (q.v.). Also cf. OE glաr m. 'amber', MLG gliir 'resin' < \*gleza- ( = Lat. glesum 'amber').

\*glauma- m. 'jubilation' - ON glaumr m. 'id.', OE gleam m. 'joyous noise, jubilation' ⇒ \*ghlou-mo- (EUR) - Close to OCS glum'b m. 'joke, game'; further cf. Gr. XAEUTj 'id.' < \*ghleu-s-eh₂-. A mo-stem to a root \*ghleu-.

\*glawwa- adj. 'sharp-sighted' - Go. glaggws\* adj. 'cautious', ON gl/i1ggr adj. 'clear-sighted; clear; stingy', OE gleaw adj. 'clear-sighted, wise', OS glau adj. 'wise', OHG glau adj. 'cautious, wise' ⇒ \*ghlouH-6- (EUR) - Olr. gluair adj. 'clear, clean' < \*ghlouH-ri-. Related to \*gliinen-. Also cf. ON gluggr, gluggi m. 'window', Far. gluggi m. 'peephole' < \*gluwwa(n)-.

\*gleppan- s.v. 'to slip off - Far. gleppa s.v. 'to slip out of position; to fail, let down', Nw. gleppa s.v. 'to slip; to turn out differently' (NEUR) - Lith. g/ebti (.glembil) 'to become smooth' < \*gh/e-m-bh-e-. The West Norse forms can be derived from either \*glempan- or \*gleppan-, but in either case, the root final consonant represents a (shortened) geminate that was adopted from the iteratives \*glupp/bon- and \*glapp/bon(q.v.). For the original consonantism, cf. OE glofn. 'cliff < \*gluba-; Nw. glove f. 'rift, ravine' < \*glub6n- and Du. gleuf c. 'groove, slot' < \*glubi-.

\*gleupan- - \*gliipan- s.v. 'to gape' - Nw. gljupa, glupa s.v. 'to gape; to snatch with the mouth; to devour', Sw. dial. gliopa, glupa s.v. 'to devour',

<!-- pdf-page: 221; section: dictionary -->

\*glidan- 181 Elfd. glaupa s.v. 'id.', E dial. gloup 'to swallow', OFri. g/Upa s.v. 'to sneak'(?), MDu. glupen w.v. 'to squint. lurk'. Du. gluipert c. 'creep' (DRV). A back-formation from the iterative \*glupp/bon-. For the meaning of Sw. dial. gljopa 'to swallow', cf. Da. dial. glubbe i sig 'to devour؋. E obs. globbe, glop 'id.' (to gobble may have been dissimilated from the frequentative \*globble). Also cf. Ice!. gl[j)upr adj. 'soft, porous', Nw. dial. gl[j)up, glyp adj. 'eager' ( < 'open'?) < \*gleupa- - \*glilpa- and the causative ON gleypa, Nw. gl!dypa w.v. 'to devour' < \*glaupjan-.

\*glidan- s.v. 'to glide' - OE glidan s.v. 'id.', E to glide, OFri. glida s.v. 'id.', OS glidan s.v. 'id.', Du. glijden s.v. 'id.', MHG gliten s.v. 'id.', G gleiten s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghfeidh-e- (NEUR?) - Perhaps to Lith. glitUs adj. 'slippery' < \*gh/i-tu-. The link with Lith. glitus is semantically attractive, but implies that Pre-Gm. \*ghleidh- is a dh-extension of a root \*ghlei-. Also cf. ON gleior adj. 'standing astride' < \*glaida- and the iterative \*glitton-.

\*gliman- s.v. 'to shine' - Nw. glim a s.v. 'id.', MHG glimen s.v. 'id.' (DRV?). A strong verb related to e.g. OHG glimo, gleimo m. 'firefly' < \*gliman-, \*glaiman- and OE glrem m. 'gleam' < \*glaimi-; possibly, the strong verb was back-formed to these stems in m. See also \*gliwa-.

\*glintan· s.v. 'to shine, look' - MHG glinzen s.v. 'to shine, gleam' ⇒ \*ghfe-n-d-e (EUR) - OCS glf;dati, Ru. dial. gljadat', SCr. gli!dati 'to look at, see' < \*ghl(e)nd-eh₂-ie-; Ru. gljadet"to look at' < \*ghl(e)nd-ehrie-; Latv. glefist2 'to (scarcely) perceive', Olr. as-gleinn 'to examine' < \*ghlend-. Also cf. OHG, MHG glanz adj. 'bright', MHG glanz, G Glanz m. 'shine' < \*ghlond-o-. See also \*glantjan-, \*glunten- and \*glut(t)on-. Icel. glitta 'to glint' may continue \*glintjan-.

\*glitan· s.v. 'to shine, sparkle' - OS glitan s.v. 'id.', OHG glizan s.v. 'id.', G gleiflen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*gh/eid-e- (GM). No doubt a back-formation to the iterative \*glit(t)on-. Also cf. OE glitenian, OHG glizinon, MHG glitzenen w.v. 'to shine' < \*glitnon-; Ice!. glitra, OE glitorian, (MH)G glitzern 'to glitter' < \*glitrojan-. OHG glizemo m. 'shine' < \*glit-man- gave rise to the verb underlying Go. glitmunjan- < \*glid-mn-ie-. The root \*ghleid- seems to be an extension of\*ghlei-, see \*gliwa-.

\*glit(t)on- w.v. 'to shine, sparkle' - Ice!. glita w.v. 'to gleam, flash', MDu. glitten w.v. 'to shine, sparkle', G glitzen w.v. 'to shine' ⇒ \*gh/id-neh₂- (GM). The iterative to \*glitan- (q.v.). Note that Ice!. glitta w.v. 'to glint' < \*glittjan-(?) may belong here, too.

\*glitton- w.v. 'to glide' - G glitzen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghfidh-neh₂- (NEUR?).

<!-- pdf-page: 222; section: dictionary -->

182 \*gliwa- The iterative to the strong verb \*glidan- ( q.v.).

\*gliwa- n. 'joy' - ON glj n. 'id.', OE gliw, gleow n. 'jesting; fun; game', E glee ⇒ \*ghlei-uo- (WEUR) - Olr. gle adj. 'bright' < \*ghlei-uo-. Also cf. ON glja, Far. gliggja, Nw. glOJa w.v. 'to shine' < \*gliwen- <

\*ghfi-u-ehi- and - with a different suffix - \*gliman-.

\*gloan- s.v. 'to glow' - OE glowan s.v. 'id.', E to glow, OS gloian w.v. 'id.', Du. gloeien w.v. 'id.', OHG gluoan w.v. 'id.', G glilhen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghl6h₁-e- (EUR) . - MW glo m. 'charcoal' < \*ghloh₁-uo-. An o-grade intensive verb to a root \*ghleh₁- (for the color of the laryngeal, cf. Icel. glrer adj. 'transparent', Nw. dial. glee adj. 'pale yellow' < \*g/eja-). The verb could alternatively be linked with \*glawwa- 'sharp-sighted' and \*g/U.nen- 'to peer', but only if one accepts the Proto-Germanic development \*-ou- > \*-6-. Also OE glom 'twilight, gloom', MDu. gloem n. 'dregs' < \*gloma- < \*ghloh₁-mo- and the closely related adj. MDu. gloem adj. 'gloomy; turbid'. See further \*glodi-.

\*glodi- f. 'glow' - ON g/60 f. 'red-hot embers', Far. gl6o f. 'glow; ember', Elfd. glyoo f. 'glow', OE gled f. 'burning coal, embers', E gleed, OFri. glad, gled f. 'glow', MDu. gloet m./f. 'id.', Du. gloed c. 'id.', OHG gluot f. 'red-heat', G Glut f. 'glow' ⇒ \*ghloh₁-ti- (DRV). A ti-stem derivation to the root of\*gloan- (q.v.).

\*gliinen- w.v. 'to peer' - Sw. dial. gluna w.v. 'to stare, peer' ⇒ \*gh/uH-n-ehi(EUR) - Latv. glinet 'to lour'. A Baltic-Germanic isogloss, possibly related to \*gloan- 'to glow' (cf. Sw. glo w.v. 'to glow; to stare') and \*glawwa- (q.v.). Also cf. Nw. glyma w.v. 'to lour' < \*gliimjan- and MLG gliimen 'id.', MDu. glumende ptc. 'vicious (of dogs)' < \*gliimen-.

\*glunten- w.v. 'to peer' - ON glotta w.v. 'to sneer, grin, smirk', Far. glotta w.v. 'to clear up, (of the sun) to peep through the clouds', Nw. glutta w.v. 'to peer, peep' ⇒ \*gh/nd-eh₁- (EUR). The stative belonging to \*g/intan- ( q.v.).

\*glupp/bon- w.v. 'to gape' - Icel. glopa w.v. 'to drop', Far. gloppa w.v. 'to stand a little open; put ajar', Nw. g/opa w.v. 'to be open; to gulp', Elfd. glupa w.v. 'to gobble, devour' ⇒ \*ghl(m)bh-neh₂- (NEUR) - Lith. glebti (glembu) 'to become smooth' < \*ghle-m-bh-e-. An old iterative formation 3sg. \*glu(m)popi, 3pl. \*glu(m)bunanpi < \*gh/(m)bh-neh₂-ti, \*gh/(m)bh-nh₂-enti. It is difficult to determine whether or not the Nordic verbs originally contained a nasal in the root because of the

<!-- pdf-page: 223; section: dictionary -->

\*glut(t)on- 183 change -mp- > -pp- in West Nordic. The situation is further complicated by the fact that we seem to be dealing with an infix in view of the Lithuanian verb, for which cf. the a-grade iterative represented by \*glapp/bon- 'to slip off and the strong verb \*gleppan- 'to slip' ( < 'to slip through a hole'?). The alternation of Far. gloppa and Nw. glopa 'to be open', however, does seem to presuppose a non-nasal formation \*glup(p)on-. Perhaps both nasalized and non-nasalized roots occurred side by side in Germanic.

\*glut(t)on- w.v. 'to peer' - E to gloat, MHG glotzen, glutzen w.v. 'to watch with widespread eyes, stare' ⇒ \*gh/nd-neh₂- (EUR). An iterative formation to the strong verb \*glintan- ( q.v.). The lack of the nasal in WGm. is remarkable, and points to \*ghjd-neh₂- (with remodeled zero grade) rather than \*gh/r:id-neh₂-, as one would expect on the basis of e.g. the stative \*glunten- (q.v.). Possibly, the radical n was dissimilated in the Pre-Gm. iterative 3sg. \*glunttopi, 3pl. gluntunanpi < \*gh/r:id-neh₂-ti, \*gh/r:id-r:ih₂-enti.

\*gnagan- s.v. 'to gnaw' - ON gnaga s.v. 'id.', OE gnagan s.v. 'id.', E to gnaw, OS knagan s.v. 'id.', Du. knagen w.v. 'id.', OHG (g)nagan s.v. 'id.', MHG gnagen s.v. 'to gnaw', G nagen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghn69h-e- (IE?) - YAv. aiþi-ynixta'gnawed' < \*ghnigh-to-(?); Latv. 91Jˤgiit 'to eat slowly' < \*ghneg-f\*ghneh₁gh-. An a-grade intensive verb with no certain cognates. Av. -ynixta- has been compared, but it seems impossible to reconstruct \*ghnhigh- for both the Avestan and Germanic forms (pace EWAhd: IV, 507). as this would give \*\*gii.x-ta- and \*\*gung-an- respectively. The reconstruction only works for Latv. gl}ˤgiit, but this form thus remains isolated. Note that the initial cluster \*gn- turned into \*kn- in those WGm. languages where initial g is represented by a fricative.

\*gnaista(n)- m. 'spark' - ON gneisti m. 'spark', OE gnii.st m. 'spark', OHG gneisto m. (also gneista f.) 'spark' (GM). A sound symbolic formation to the verb continued by e.g. ME gnii.sten 'to gnash the teeth' < \*gnaist6n-, cf. G knistern, gnistern 'to crackle'. Hardly to OPru. knaistis 'fire'.

\*gnidan- s.v. 'to rub' - OE gnidan s.v. 'id.', MLG gniden s.v. 'id.', OHG gnitan s.v. 'id.', MHG gniten s.v. 'id.' (EUR?). The word has been compared to Gr. Hsch. XVLEL 'grinds', OCS gniti, Ru. gnit' fgniju), SCr. gnj)'ti 'to rot' < \*ghni-ie-, but this requires the assumption of a \*dh(h₁)-extension for Germanic. Also cf. ON gnista w.v. 'to gnash the teeth; to snarl (of dogs)' < \*gnistjan-, a denominal verb from \*gnista- < \*ghneidh-sth₂-o-(?), cf. MHG gnist m. 'gravel'.

<!-- pdf-page: 224; section: dictionary -->

184 \*gnuznojan-

\*gnuznojan- w.v. 'to be sad' - OE gnornan, gnornian w.v. 'to grieve, mourn, be sad, bewail, lament', OS gornon, gnornon, grornon w.v. 'to lament, complain, be worried' (NEUR) - Ru. grust' f. 'sorrow' < \*ghrous-ti- (whence OCS S'b-grustiti Sfl 'to be troubled'); SCr. grst f. 'aversion' < \*ghrus-ti-. An apparently denominal verb probably to OE grorn, gnorn m. 'grief or perhaps to the adj. gnorn 'sad' < \*gruzna- < \*ghrus-n6-. After the rhotacism of \*z, the initial cluster became prone to dissimilation of the \*r, thus resulting in both OS gnornon and gornon. Despite the somewhat different meanings, the verb can probably be reconciled with OE greosan s.v. 'to frighten' <. \*greusan- < \*ghreus-e- and OE gryre, OS gruri m. 'fright' < \*gruzi- < \*ghrus-f-. The alternative is to connect OE gnyran w.v. 'to creak' < \*gnurjan-, thus starting from a primary meaning 'to groan'.

\*goda- adj. 'suitable, good' - Go. gops adj. 'id.', ON g6or adj. 'good; friendly', Far. goour adj. 'id.', Elfd. guoo adj. 'id.', OE god adj. 'id.', E good, OFri. god adj. 'id.', OS god adj. 'id.', Du. goed adj. 'id.', OHG guot adj. 'id.', G gut adj. 'id.' (DRV). An o-grade adjective derived from the lost strong verb \*gadan- 'to fit', for which cf. \*gadojan-.

\*gain- f. 'late winter' - ON g6i, gre f. 'id.', Ice!. g6a f. 'id.', Far. g'1 n. 'id.', Nw. gj'1, go f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghi-om- (IE) - Hitt. gimm- - gimmant- c. 'winter' < \*ghi-mn(-ent)-; Skt. hima- 'cold, frost', hfmii- f. 'winter' < \*ghi-mo/eh₂-; Skt heman Joe. 'in winter' < \*ghei-m-en; YAv. zii&, OAv. gen. zimo f. 'winter' < \*ghi-om, \*ghi-m-6s; Gr. XLWV, XL6vo<; 'snow', Arm. jiun, gen. jean 'id.' < \*ghi-om, \*ghi-om-os (both with generalization of -n from \*-m); Lat. hiems, -is f. 'storm, winter' < \*ghi-em-; Olr.gaim, OW gaem, MW gaeaf'winter' < \*gh(i)em-i-. In spite of some formal difficulties, the word must be related to the PIE word for 'winter', cf. Nw. gj'1 f. 'thin layer of snow, track in the snow'. ON g6i is inflected as an fn-stem, and can thus be reconstructed as \*gofn-. It is further likely that ON gre, Far. g'1 and Nw. gj'1 continue the same formation \*gofn-, but with absorption of the -i. Ice!. g6a < quasi-PGm. \*goon- is a late Icelandic creation. The PGm. root \*go- is most likely to have developed from \*ghi-om, a secondary nominative that generalized the radical zero grade of the PIE paradigm nom. \*ghei-om, gen. \*ghi-m-6s. The loss of\*j after a word-initial velar is paralleled by e.g. \*kewwan- 'to chew' (q.v.) < PIE \*gieuH-e-. The genitive stem \*ghi-m- can still be retrieved from OLFra. Sal. in-gimus 'one-year old animal' < \*aina-gima- and tui-gimus 'two-year old animal' (cf. Lat. bfmus 'id.') < \*Hoino-, \*dui-ghi-m-o-.

<!-- pdf-page: 225; section: dictionary -->

\*g6ja/6- 185 •goja/o- n./f. 'peritendinitis crepitans' - Far. g¢ n. 'synovitis (esp. of the wrist)', Nw. gj¢ f. 'pain in the wrist due to strain' ⇒ \*ghoH-io- (EUR) - Gr. xwM<; adj. 'lame, paralyzed (especially of a foot)' < \*ghoH-lo-. A West Norse word probably continuing ON \*gre < \*g6ja/6- (not \*gj(I in view of Far. g¢). Nw. dial. gFJyr, gj¢gr, geir m., g¢yre f. with the same meanings are difficult to account for, but may have evolved from ON \*grer < \*g6iz. The word has no etymology so far, but can probably be connected with Gr. xwA.6<;. Also cf. Shetl. (Unst) j¢ger 'painful and swollen wrist; condition of the feet of cattle'. •goljan- w.v. 'to call(?)' - Go. go/jan w.v. 'to greet', ON grela w.v. 'to comfort, soothe', OFri.gela w.v. 'to hunt'(?) (DRV). The verb is a formal causative, but the semantics could point to an original intensive formation. •goman- - •gauman- m. 'gum, palate' - ON g6mi, g6mr m. 'id.', Far. g6mi m. 'id.', Elfd. guom m. 'id.', OE g6ma m. 'palate', E gum, OHG guomo, gaumo m. 'id.', MHG guome, goume m. 'id.', G Gaumen m. 'id.' ⇒ \*gheh₂u-mon- (IE) - Close to Lith. gomurys m. 'palate', Latv. giimurs m. 'larynx, trachea' < \*gheh₂-mr- (with depalatalization from a zero-grade root \*ghh₂-mr-?). An mr/n-stem to the root stem \*gheh₂-u-, an extension of PIE \*gheh₂- 'to gape', for which see \*ganen-. The vocalic alternation of ON g6mi, OHG guomo < \*g6man- vs. OHG gaumo, G Gaumen < \*gauman- is explained by the fact that the regular Proto-Germanic change of \*-6u- to \*-6- only operated in open syllables (cf. Go. sauil 'sun' < \*soel- < \*seh₂uel-). In closed syllables, the long diphthong was simply shortened to \*-au- (cf. \*naust(r)a- 'boathouse' < \*neh₂u-sth₂-o-). As a result, the Pre-Gm. paradigm \*gheh₂u-m6n, gen. \*gheh₂u-mn-os regularly developed into PGm. \*g6m6, gen. \*gaumnaz. The variant OHG giumen is late and has umlaut from the plural (Kroonen 2011a: 304). The Pre-Gm. paradigm itself may be a remodeling of a more primary, static heteroclitic, nom. \*gh6h₂u-mr, gen. \*gheh₂u-mn-s.

\*graba- n. 'grave' - OE græf, graf n. 'grave, trench', OFri. gref n. 'grave, ditch(?)', OS graf n. 'grave', Du. graf n. 'id.', OHG grab n. 'id.', G Grab n. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghrobh-o- (DRV) - OCS grob'b 'grave, tomb', Ru. grob 'coffin, obs. grave', SCr. grab m. 'grave' < \*ghrobh-o-. An o-stem derived from \*graban- (q.v.).

\*graban- s.v. 'to dig' - Go. graban s.v. 'id.', ON grafa s.v. 'id.', Far. grava s./w.v. 'id.', Elfd. grava s.v. 'id.', OE grafan s.v. 'id.', OFri. greva s.v. 'id.', OS graban s.v. 'id.', Du. graven s.v. 'id.', OHG graban s.v. 'dig, carve', G graben s.v. 'to dig' ⇒ \*ghr6bh-e- (IE) - OCS greti 'to dig, scrape', Ru. grestf (grebU) 'to

<!-- pdf-page: 226; section: dictionary -->

186 \*graiponrow, rake', SCr. grepsti 'to scrape, scratch' < \*ghrebh-; Latv. grebt (.grebju) 'to scrape, excavate, seize' < \*ghrebh(-ie)-. On top of the aforementioned cognates, Hitt. karapi, karipanzi 'to devour' has been adduced as well (Kloekhorst 2008: 442-4), but this verb rather belongs to the iterative doublet \*garp/b6n- / \*gurpon- (q.v.). The iteratives belonging to \*graban- are \*grapp/bon- and \*grupp/b6n-. Also cf. \*graba-, Go. graba f. 'trench, ditch', ON grpf f. 'pit, ditch; grave' < \*grab6- and - with ablaut - \*grobo-.

\*graipon- w.v. 'to grope' - ON greipa w.v. 'to grasp', OE griipian w.v. 'to grope, touch with the hands', E to grope, OHG greifon w.v. 'to stroke' ⇒ \*ghroibh·neh₂- (NEUR). The o-grade variant of the iterative \*gripp/bon- (q.v.).

\*gramjan- w.v. 'to provoke, anger' - Go. gramjan w.v. 'id.', ON gremja w.v. 'id.', OE gremian w.v. 'id.', MLG gremmen w.v. 'id.', OHG grem(m}en w.v. 'id.', G griimen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghrom-eie- (IE). The causative to \*grimman- (q.v.). Also cf. ON gramr, OE, OS, OHG gram adj. 'angry' < \*grama-.

\*granna- adj. 'sharp' - ON grannr adj. 'thin, slender', Ice!. grannur adj. 'thin, slim; accurate', Far. grannur adj. 'thin, fine', Elfd. grann adj. 'thin, delicate, pretty' ⇒ \*ghroH-n-u6- (NEUR/WEUR) - Ru. gran' f. 'side, face; border, verge' < \*ghroH-n-i-; Cz. hrana f. 'corner, edge' < \*ghroH-n-eh₂-. Probably related to \*grano- 'hair of the beard'. The Nordic meaning 'thin' must have developed out of older 'sharp' in view of the Icelandic meaning 'accurate'. Also cf. the Faroese phrase ta st6o so grant 'it stood so near the edge', in which the meaning of grannur is particularly close to Cz. hrana 'edge'. Slightly less attractive is the connection to Olr. grind, grinn adj. 'sharp' < \*ghrendh-i-; PGm. \*granna- would then have to be reconstructed as \*ghrondh-no-.

\*grano- f. 'hair of the beard' - ON grpn f. 'hair on the upper lip; moustache', Far. gron f. 'nose of cattle, snout; perineum', pl. 'nostrils, nose', OE granu f. 'moustache', MDu. grane, graen f. 'hair of the beard, awn, fish-bone', OHG grana f. 'awn, beard, whiskers', G Granne f. 'awn' ⇒ \*ghroH-neh₂- (NEUR?) - Ru. grcinka f. 'tuft', SCr. gr<ina f. 'branch' < \*ghroH-neh₂-. Together with the Slavic forms, PGm. \*grano- can with Dybo's law (pretonic shortening before resonants) readily be reconstructed as PIE \*ghroH-neh₂-. There are some semantically close lookalikes in Celtic, however, cf. Olr. grend f.? 'beard, hair, bristles' < \*ghrendfh₃.eh₂- and MW grann m. 'chin, beard, cheek', MBret. grann 'eyebrow' < \*ghrandfh₃.o-, but these formations pose serious formal problems: both the intra-Celtic interchange

<!-- pdf-page: 227; section: dictionary -->

\*grapp/bon- 187 of \*a and \*e as well as the dental suffix remain unexplained. Also cf. \*granna- 'sharp'.

\*grappjbon- w.v. 'to grapple' - E to grab, MLG grabben w.v. 'to snatch', MDu. grapen, grabben w.v. 'to grab, grapple', Du. grabbelen w.v. 'to grapple' ⇒ \*ghrobh-neh₂- (IE). The o-grade iterative to \*graban- (q.v.), an ablaut variant of \*grupp/bon( q.v.). The full grade seems to have been inherited from the o-grade strong verb, which belongs to the class of intensives that originally had o/zero-ablaut (cf. \*gangan- - \*gungan-). Swi. Rhntl. gr:x:Jppa 'to grope' (etc.) < \*grebb- may continue an originally strong verb back-formed to the iterative.

\*grasa- n. 'grass' - Go. gras n. 'id.', ON gras n. 'id.', OE grers, grres n. 'id.', E grass, OFri. gers, gres n. 'id.', OS gras n. 'id.', Du. gras n. 'id.', OHG gras n. 'id.', G Gras n. 'id.' (DRV) - Lat. gram en n. 'grass' < \*ghrh₁-mn-. The most evident comparandum of this word is Lat. gramen, which can be reconstructed as both ghrh₁-mn- and ghrh₁s-mn-. PGm. \*gras-, however, cannot straightforwardly be derived from PIE \*ghrh₁s-, since this would yield \*gurs-. It must therefore rather be analyzed as a secondary s-stem created to the verb \*groan- (q.v.) < \*ghr6hi-e- (cf. \*glasa- - \*glaza- vs. \*g/Oan-). With the same ablaut, cf. MHG gruose, MLG grose, MDu. groese f. 'young vegetation' < \*groso-.

\*grauta- adj. 'coarse' - OE great adj. 'course, large, great', E great, OFri. grat adj. 'tall, big, great', OS grot adj. 'id.', Du. groat adj. 'id.', OHG groz adj. 'id.', G Groft adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghroud-o- (DRV). A regular o-grade adj. to the strong verb \*greutan- (q.v.); the original meaning was 'coarse', which is still close to the iterative Pal. grotzen 'to cut up into uneven pieces' < \*grutton- (q.v.). The meaning later shifted to 'big' in the WGm. languages, thus replacing \*mekila- (q.v.).

\*gredu- m. 'hunger, desire' - Go. gredus m. 'id.', ON graor m. 'id.', OE grti!d\* m. 'id.', E greed ⇒ \*ghrehi-tU- (IE) - Hitt. karija(tt)a(ri) 'to be gracious', Skt. hclryati 'to enjoy, like' ( < \*gherhi-), Gr. xalpw 'to rejoice', Lat. horior 'to encourage, urge' < \*ghrhi-ie-. A tu-stem to the root \*ghrehi-, whence Go. gredags adj. 'hungry', ON graougr, OE grti!dig, OS gradag, OHG gratag adj. 'greedy' < \*greduga-, \*-aga-. See also \*gerna-.

\*gretan- s.v. 'to wail' - Go. gretan s.v. 'to cry', ON grata s.v. 'id.', Elfd. grata s.v. 'id.', OE grti!tan s.v. 'id.', ME greten 'to salute', E to greet, OFri. greta w.v. 'to greet; to sue', OS gratan s.v. 'to cry', MHG grazen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghreh₁d-e-

<!-- pdf-page: 228; section: dictionary -->

188 \*greujan- (IE) - Skt. hradate 'to sound' < \*ghreh₁d-e-; W griddfan 'to groan' < \*ghreh₁d- (Fowkes 1969: 78). Also cf. ON gratr m. 'weeping', OHG griiz m. 'anger' < \*greta- ( = Skt. hriida- m. 'sound, noise, roar'). OE han-gr<£d m. 'crowing of a rooster' < \*greda- (as well as OE gr<£dan w.v. 'to cry') seems to imply a root \*ghreh₁dh-, but it is conceivable that this word was influenced by \*kreda-, cf. OHG hana-kriit 'id.'. See also \*grotjan-.

\*greujan- w.v. 'to dawn' - ON gryandi f. 'dawn', OSw. grj w.v. 'to dawn' ⇒ .

\*ghrehru (IE) - Hitt karii adv. 'early; formerly; up to now' < \*ghrh₁-eu; OCS zarja f. 'dawn, shine, ray', Ru. zarj6. (arch. zorja) f. 'dawn, sunset, reveille, retreat', SCr. zora f. 'aurora' < \*ghorh₁-ieh₂-; OCS zrak'b 'look, appearance', Ru. dial. z6rok 'look, glance, appearance', SCr. zruk m. 'sunbeam, air' < \*ghorh₁-ko-. The formation gryjandi f. is clearly derived from the verb \*gryja with the suffix -andi, cf. bindandi, -endi f. 'abstinence', bfoandi f. 'waiting, delay', kunnandi f. 'knowledge', hyggjandi, -endi f. 'wisdom', kveoandi f. 'recitation', leikandi f. 'sport, jest', pj6tandi f. 'name of a vein'. This \*gryja is not attested in ON, but is nevertheless substantiated by the Old Swedish impersonal construction OSw. daghin tok at grj 'it was getting light' (cf. Da., Sw. gry 'to dawn'), and perhaps also by Far. glfggja 'to shine, dawn' (dagur gliggjar), which might continue \*gryggja < \*gryja. The verb can technically be derived from both \*griijan- and \*greujan-, but only the latter form is reconcilable with MDu. grieken 'to dawn' (cf. griekinge f. 'dawn') < \*greukon- (with the inchoative k-suffix). The Gm. root \*greu- is reminiscent of Hitt. karii 'early', which continues either \*ghrh₁-eu or \*ghreh₁-u (for the u-suffix, cf. Skt. akt-um. 'dark, night, light' ( < 'shimmer') < \*IJkw·t-u-). The latter reconstruction is compatible with PGm. \*greu-, as \*ghrh₁-eu would probably yield \*kureu (cf. \*kuru- 'heavy' < \*gwrh₂-u-).

\*greuna- n. 'gravel' - ON grj6n n. 'groats', MLG gren n. 'sea sand', MHG grien m./n. 'gravel, sandy riverside' ⇒ \*ghreuH-no- (WEUR). Pokorny IEW: 460 reconstructs \*ghreuno- (corresponding to laryngealistic \*ghreh₂u-no-) in view of Gr. -xpauw 'to scratch' < \*ghreh₂u-ie-, but the connection with \*gruwwa- 'dregs' (q.v.) is more probable. Also cf. MDu.griend m. '(strip of) sandy ground', Du.griend 'reedy area'.

\*greupan- - \*griipan- s.v. 'to cut up, grind' - Nw. grjupa, grupa s.v. 'to grind', Sw. grjopa 'to cut out', Elfd. griuopa s.v. 'to grind coarsely' (DRV). The strong verb looks like a back-formation to the iterative \*grupp/bon'to dig, hollow out' < \*ghrubh-neh₂- (q.v.), but cf. Nw. gropa 'to grind' < \*grupon-. In view of MHG fs-griipe f. 'hailstone', G Graupe f. 'grain of pearl barley' < \*grubbon-, it is at any rate certain that the original root was

<!-- pdf-page: 229; section: dictionary -->

\*greutan- 189 \*ghreubh- rather than \*ghreub- (pace Torp 1909: 146). Further cf. Elfd. griuopa f. 'moraine gravel, ground barley' < \*greupon-, the causative ON greypa 'to groove', Nw. greypa 'to groove; to grind wheat' < \*graupjan- and ON grypja f. 'coarse meal'.

\*greutan- s.v. 'to grind, crush' - OHG ftr-grozzen ptc. 'ground', MHG ver-griezen s.v. 'to grind into very small pieces' :::) \*ghreud-e- (NEUR) - Lith. grusti (grudiiu, grildu) 'to thrust, pestle, stamp' < \*ghrud-ie-; Ru. gruda f. 'heap, pile', SCr. griida f. 'clod, lump' < \*ghroud-eh₂-. A strong verb with many different derivations, e.g. ON grj6t n. 'rubble', OS griot, OE great, OHG grioz n. 'sand, gravel, grit' < \*greuta-; ON grautr m. 'porridge' < \*grauta- and \*grutja(n)- 'groats'. The formations \*griiti-, cf. OE griit 'coarse meal, grout', MDu. grute f., Du. gruit 'herb mixture used for bittering and flavoring beer', MHG griiz, G Graus m. 'grain (of sand or wheat') < \*griiti-, could point to the existence of a secondary strong verb variant \*griitan-. See also \*grutton-.

\*grewa- adj. 'grey' - ON grar adj. 'id.', OE grrew, grǣg adj. 'id.', E grey, OS griiu adj. 'id.', Du. grauw adj. 'id.', OHG griio, grii adj. 'id.', G grau adj. 'id.' :::)

\*ghreh₁-uo- (IE) - OCS Zbreti 'to see', Ru. obs. zret' (zrju) 'to behold, gaze', Lith. zerˡti 'to glow, sparkle' < \*ghrh₁-ehi-ie-; Lith. zirti 'to scatter, sparkle' < \*ghrh₁-. An archetypal chromonym in \*-wa-. Some compare ON grey n. 'bitch, paltry fellow, coward', grey-hundr 'greyhound', ags. grieg-hund 'id.' < \*grauja- < \*ghrohi-u-io-(?), but this is uncertain on both the formal and the semantic side. For the etymology of the root \*ghreh₁-, see \*greujan-.

\*grldi- f. 'step' - Go. grips\* f. 'standing', MHG grit 'step' :::) \*ghridh-i- (NEUR) - Lith. gridyti 'to go, wander' < \*ghridh-eie-; OCS gn;sti, Ru. grjastf (grjadu), SCr. gresti 'to go' < \*ghri-n-dh-e-. An i-stem derived from the strong verb \*gridan-, cf. MHG griten s.v. 'to spread the legs wide' < \*ghreidh-e-.

\*grima/on- m./f. 'make-up' - ON grfma f. 'mask, cowl; beak', OE grima m. 'mask, visor, helmet; spectre', E grime 'soot, coal-dust', OS grimo m. 'mask'  \*ghr(e)i-mon- (EUR) - Close to Lith. grieti (grieju) 'to scoop off cream' < \*ghroi-ie- and esp. Lith. graimas n. 'cream' < \*ghroi-mo-; also cf. with an s-suffix: Gr. xpiw 'to rub, anoint; to wash with color; to sting' < \*ghris-ie(whence xpTμa f. 'ointment'). An mn-stem to the verbal root \*ghrei-. E grime 'soot' and the ablauting MLG greme f. 'dirt' < \*grimon- point to an original meaning 'to smear', but it is also entirely possible that the word was derived from \*grfnan- (q.v.), the original meaning in that case being 'to make a face, grimace'.

<!-- pdf-page: 230; section: dictionary -->

190 \*grimman-

\*grimman- s.v. 'to rage' - OE grimman s.v. 'to rage, roar; to rush', OS grimman s.v. 'to rage', MHG grimmen s.v. 'to rage, yell' ⇒ \*ghrem-n-e- or

\*ghr-en-m-e- (IE) - Av. gram- 'to become angry' < \*ghrem-. A nasal present to PIE \*ghrem-, no doubt an old sound symbolic root. See also \*gramjan-.

\*grinan- s.v. 'to flash, snarl' - ON grfna s.v. 'to grimace, grin', Far. grfna s.v. 'to grin, laugh', Nw. grine s.v. 'to grimace, whine', Da. grine w.v. 'to grimace; to laugh', E to grin, MDu. grinen s./w.v. 'to cry; to growl; to grin', OHG grfnan . s.v. 'to bark, howl, snarl', MHG grinen s.v. 'to grin, show teeth', G greinen w.v. 'to whine' ⇒ \*ghrei-n-e- (WEUR) - Olr. grfan f. 'sun' < \*ghrei-neh₂-. A strong verb whose original meaning was 'to flash', cf. Da. obs. grine 'to send a piercing light (e.g. of the sun), to light up in the dark' and esp. Far. tad grimir 'it is beginning to get light' ( < \*grimen-). It later changed into 'to snarl, bare the teeth' and 'to grin', whence both 'to cry' and 'to laugh'. The -n- of the verb must be suffixal in view of Nw. dial. grisa 'to grin' (Hellquist 1922: 201). The more primary root \*grf- has been compared to Olr. grinniguth 'le grincement des fleches' (Vendryes 1907: 140), but in view of the original meaning 'to flash', I prefer to connect it to Olr. grfan 'sun'. Also cf. OE grtinian w.v. 'to groan, lament, murmur', E to groan < \*grainolja)n-.

\*grindan- s.v. 'to grind' - OE grindan s.v. 'id.', E to grind ⇒ \*gwhrenHdh-e(EUR) - Lat. frendo, -ere 'to grind one's teeth' < \*gwhrenHdh-e-; Lith. grˢsti (grendiiu) 'to plane, scour' < \*gwhrenHdh-ie-. Also cf. OHG grint, G Grind m. 'scurf, Du. grind m. 'gravel' < \*grinda- and Ice!. grand n. 'grain, particle, little bit', LG grand 'sand' < \*granda-.

\*grindi- f. 'fence' - ON grind f. 'lattice door, gate, fence', Icel. grind f. 'trellis, grating, rack, frame', Far. grind f. 'gate, frame' ⇒ \*ghrendh-i- (NEUR) - Lith. dial. grinda f. 'flooring of a bridge', Latv. grida f. 'floor, threshing-floor', Ru. grjada f. 'ridge, bed (of flowers)', dial. 'pole, staff, SCr. greda f. 'garden bed, ridge' < \*ghnJ.dh-eh₂-. Also cf. OE grindel m. 'bar, bolt, lattice frame', OHG grintil m. 'bolt, bar, plank' < \*grindi/a-.

\*gripan- s.v. 'to grasp, seize' - Go. greipan s.v. 'id.', ON grfpa s.v. 'id.', OE grfpan s.v. 'id.', OFri. grfpa s.v. 'id.', OS gripan s.v. 'id.', Du. grijpen s.v. 'id.', OHG grifan s.v. 'id.', G greifen s.v. 'id.' (DRY). Since the root-final \*p of this verb in view of G Als. grippen < \*gribbOnmust have developed from Pre-Gm. \*-pp-, the strong verb is likely to be a back-formation to the iterative \*gripp/bon- ( q.v.). Also cf. Nw. dial. gripe, OE gripa m. 'handful' < \*gripan-; ON gripr m. 'valuable', OE gripe m. 'grip,

<!-- pdf-page: 231; section: dictionary -->

\*gripp/bOn- 191 grasp', MDu. grepe, Du. greep 'id.', MHG grif, G Griff m. 'id.' < \*gripi- and OE gripa, Far. grfpur m. 'vulture' < \*gripa(n)-.

\*gripp/bOn- w.v. 'to grasp' - ON grip/a w.v. 'to grope', MDu. grippen w.v. 'to grasp', Du. gribbelen w.v. 'to pick up', G Als. grippen w.v. 'to grab' ⇒ \*ghrfbh-neh₂- (NEUR) - Lith. griebti (griebiu) 'to seize' < \*ghreibh-ie-; Lith. graibyti (graibail) 'id.' < \*ghroibh-efe-; Latv. gribet ( gribu) 'to wish, desire' < \*ghrfbh-eh₁-. The material points to an iterative paradigm 3sg. \*grippopi, 3pl. \*gribunanpi < \*ghrfbh-neh₂-. This verb probably gave rise to the strong verb \*gripan- in view of the (shortened) geminate of the latter. Also cf. the closely related o-grade iterative \*graipon-.

\*grisa- adj. 'grey' - OE gris adj. 'id.', OS gris adj. 'id.', Du. grijs adj. 'id.', OHG gris adj. 'id.', G greis adj. 'aged' (GM). The anlaut \*gr- has spawned the idea that the adjective is related to \*grewa- with the same meaning, but this is formally difficult The only way to reconcile the two formations is to assume that \*grisa- was derived from an i-present to the root \*ghreh₁-, i.e. \*ghreh₁-i-. After the addition of an s-suffix, the resulting form \*ghreis- would then have developed into \*gris- by Osthoff's law. This scenario remains highly speculative, however. Also note that the connection with ON grfss, Far. grfsur, Elfd. grais m. 'pig' (cf. Pokorny IEW: 441-2) is erroneous: this word was derived from the strong verb \*grfsan- underlying Far. grfsa w.v. 'to be straying (of cattle)', Nw. dial. grisa s.v. 'to have holes'.

\*groan- s.v. 'to grow' - ON gr6a s.v. 'id.', OE growan s.v. 'id.', E to grow, OFri. growa, groia s.v. 'id.', MLG groien w.v. 'id.', MDu. groeyen s.v. 'id.', Du. groeien w.v. 'id.', OHG gruoan s.v. 'id.', MHG griien s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghr6h₁-e- (IE) - Hitt. karijant- 'grass' < \*ghrh₁-i-ent- (Puhvel 1984-: 4, 80); Lat grii.men n. 'id.' < \*ghrhi-mn-. An o-grade strong verb to the root \*ghreh₁-, cf. OE grti!d m. 'grass' < \*greda- < \*ghreh₁-t6-. The verb did not have a suffix \*-ie-: both the \*j of OHG -en, OFri -ia, MDu. -yen-, -ien and the \*w of OE -wan, OFri. -wa are secondary hiatus fillers. See also \*groni- 'green' and \*gropi- 'growing'. •grobo- f. 'cavity, pit' - Go. groba f. 'dugout, hole, cave', ON gr6f f. 'pit', Far. gr6gv f. 'depression, hollow', ODu. gruova f. 'pit', Du. groeve f. 'pit, ditch, hole', OHG gruoba f. 'pit, cavity', G Grube f. 'id.' (DRV). An 6-stem derived from \*graban- (q.v.).

\*groni- adj. 'green' - ON grcenn adj. 'id.', Far. gnmur adj. 'id.', Elfd. gryon adj. 'id.', OE grene adj. 'id.', OFri. grene adj. 'id.', OS groni adj. 'id.', Du. groen

<!-- pdf-page: 232; section: dictionary -->

192 \*grotjanadj. 'id.', OHG gruoni adj. 'id.', MHG griiene adj. 'id.', G griin adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghroh₁-ni- (DRV). An adjective related to \*groan- 'to grow' (q.v.).

\*grotjan- w.v. 'to make shout, greet' - ON grreta w.v. 'to make cry', OE gretan w.v. 'to greet', OS grotian w.v. 'to address, harass, enrage', Du. groeten w.v. 'to greet', OHG gruozen w.v. 'to irritate, greet, speak to', MHG gruozen, griiezen w.v. 'to cry; to greet', G griiflen w.v. 'to greet' ⇒ \*ghroh₁d-eie- (IE). The causative to \*gretan- (q.v.).

\*gropi- f. 'growing, growth, young vegetation' - ON gr6or m. 'growth, crop', Far. gr6our m. 'vegetation', OFri. grede f. 'id.', MDu. groede f. 'id.', MHG gruot f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghrohrti- (DRV). A ti-stem derived from \*groan- (q.v.). Also cf. ON gr6or n. 'growth' < \*grof>/du- < \*ghrohi-tu-.

\*gruba- adj. 'coarse' - MDu. grof adj. 'large; crude; loud', Du. grof adj. 'coarse', MHG grop adj. 'thick, strong, big' ⇒ \*ghrubh·o- (DRV) - Lith. grubiJ.s adj. 'uneven, rough' < \*ghrubh-u-; OCS grpb'b, Ru. grubyj, SCr. grilb adj. 'coarse, rough, rude' < \*ghru-m-bh-o-. An adjective derived from the root \*greub- 'to grind', which also occurs in the strong verb \*greupan- ( q.v.).

\*grumpu- - \*grundu- m. 'floor' - Go. grundu-waddjus m. 'ground-wall', ON grunnr m. 'bottom', Far. gruaur, grunnur m. 'shallow; sea bottom', Elfd. grunn n. 'id.', OE grund m. 'ground, bottom, foundation', E ground, OFri. grund m. 'id.', OS grund m. 'id.', Du. grond c. 'id.', OHG grunt m. 'id.', G Grund, Cimb. grumf m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghrm-tu- (IE?). Usually reconstructed as a tu-stem to a root \*ghren- (cf. Orel 2003: 144). Cimb. grumf, however, points to a form \*grumf/>u- with an epenthetic f between the m and the /> (for which cf. \*kwumþi- and \*sampu-). Related to Skt harmy<i- 'permanent building' < \*gher-m-io-? Also cf. ON grandi m. 'isthmus' < \*granda-.

\*grupp/bfJn- w.v. 'to dig' - Nw. gropa w.v. 'to dig up; hollow out, groove', ME grubben w.v. 'to dig in earth with a tool; to dig up; to pluck out', gropen w.v. 'to dig, to make a groove', E to grub, MLG gropen w.v. 'to hollow out', MDu. grobben w.v. 'to scrape', G Pal. groppen w.v. 'to scratch' ⇒ \*ghrbh-neh₂(IE) - Skt grbhrpfri 'to seize, take, hold' < \*ghrbh-neh₂-. An iterative to \*graban-: the evidence is suggestive of a paradigm 3sg. \*gruppof>i, 3pl. \*grubunanþi that continues PIE \*ghrbh-neh₂- ( = Skt.

<!-- pdf-page: 233; section: dictionary -->

\*grutja(n)- 193 grbhQ.cfo). The zero-grade roots \*gurb/pp- were remodeled on the basis of the full-grade strong verb. See also \*grapp/bOn-.

\*grutja(n)- m./n. 'grits' - Du. grut n. 'small wares; small stuff; young kids', OHG gruzi n. 'bran', G Swi. griitz n. 'seed mixture' (DRY). A collective formation with the same ja{n) -suffix that is found in other terms for cereals, cf. \*hersja(n)- 'millet' and \*hwaitja- 'wheat' (q.v.). Also cf. the feminine OE grytt, E grits, MLG grutte, MDu. gorte, Du. gort, G Griitze 'groat' < \*grutjo-.

\*grutton- w.v. 'to cut into pieces' - G Pal. grotzen, grutzen w.v. 'to cut into uneven pieces' ⇒ \*ghrud-neh₂- (DRY?). The iterative to \*greutan- ( q.v.).

\*gruwwa(n)- n. 'dregs' - Ice!. grugg n. 'sediment', Far. grugg n. 'sediment, dregs; enmity', Nw. grugg(e) n./m. 'dregs' ⇒ \*ghruH-6- (WEUR) - W gro 'pebbles, coarse gravel', OCorn. grow 'sand', Co. grow 'sand', Bret. groa, gro f. 'sand'. Also cf. Ice!. gregg, grogg n. 'sediment, filth' < ON \*gr'1gg < \*grawwja(Magnusson 1985), presumably a collective ja-formation to a root \*grawwa< \*ghrouH-6- (with Verschärfung). Also cf. Far. grugga, Nw. grugga w.v. 'to make muddy, turbid' < \*gruwwojan- and the more distantly related \*greuna- 'gravel'.

\*gub(b)on· w.v. 'to steam' - Ice!. gubba w.v. 'to vomit', Far. guva w.v. 'to smoke, steam', Nw. gova w.v. 'to steam; smell', gubba w.v. 'to steam, haze' ⇒ \*ghubh-neh₂- (GM). The iterative to \*geuban- - \*guban- (q.v.). The attested forms continue 3sg. \*guppoþi, 3pl. \*gubunanþi < \*ghubh-neh₂-.

\*guben- w.v. 'to stoop' - Nw. guve w.v. 'to cower', Gutn. giiua sig 'to stoop' ⇒ \*ghubh-ehi- (NEUR) - OCS S'b-gMpti 'to bend, fold' < \*ghubh-ne-; OCS po-gubiti 'to destroy' < \*ghoubh-eie-; Latv. gubt 'to bow, bend, fall' < \*ghubh-; Latv. gubinat 'to bend, crumple' < \*ghubh-nehi-. A stative verb probably related to \*beugan- - \*bii.gan- 'to bend' (with metathesis of\*ghubh- to \*bhugh- or vice versa). Also cf. \*gaupno-.

\*guda· n. 'god' - Go.guþ m. 'id.', ON guiJ m./n. 'id.', Elfd. guiJ m. 'id.', OE god n. 'id.', E god, OFri. god m. 'id.', OS god m. 'id.', Du. god c. 'id.', OHG got m. 'id.', G Gott m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghu-t6- or \*gwhu-t6-? (IE?). No accepted etymology. The traditional comparison to Skt h6tar-, Av. zaotar- 'sacrificing priest' is technically possible, but only by deriving these words from the semantically remote root \*gheu- 'to pour (libate)' rather

<!-- pdf-page: 234; section: dictionary -->

194 \*gulbathan from \*gheuH- 'to invoke'. Not convincing either is the proposed link with Gr. xuTi yata 'burial mound' (Watkins 2008: 140). Perhaps it is best to connect the word with OCS goveti 'to revere' < \*gwhou-eh₁-, thus reconstructing Pre-Gm. \*gwhu-t6-. Within Germanic, also cf. ON gooi m. 'priest' < \*gudan-, Go. gudja m. 'id.', ON gyoja f. 'id.' < \*gudja/on- and G Gotze m. 'idol' < \*gudisan-.

\*gulba- n. 'floor' - ON golf n. 'id.', Far. g6lv n. 'id.', Elfd. guov n. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghjbh-o- (EUR?) - OCS ileb'b 'groove; crib', Ru. i6lob 'chute, gutter, trunk', SCr. ileb m. 'groove, gutter, drainpipe' < \*ghe/bh-o-; Ru. gloM f. 'beam' < \*gh/obh-eh₂-. Of the aforementioned Slavic words, the link with Ru. gloM 'beam' is especially attractive. The other cognates are semantically closer to Gr. yA.acpw 'to hollow out' < \*g/bh-e-, but in spite of the divergent semantics, this verb could be related to PGm. \*gulba- as well.

\*guljon- f. 'puddle?' - Sw. dial. golja f. 'puddle, hole in the ice', MLG gale 'marshy place', MDu. gale, guelle 'rivulet', Du. geul 'rivulet', MHG gillie w.f. 'puddle' (IE?). Also cf. OSw. g¢l f. 'abyss; throat', Sw. go/ 'abyss; lake' < \*guljo- (cf. Fi. kulju 'abyss, deep water'). Both formations were derived from a PGm. root \*geul-, cf. ON -gj61 top. 'marshy area', MHG giel m. 'throat' < \*geula-. There is no need to explain the Dutch forms as loanwords from French gueule 'throat', cf. Franck 1949: 192. No certain extra-Germanic etymology: cognate with Hitt. gullija- 'hole' (cf. Melchert 1983)?

\*gullon- w.v. 'to shout, sing' - Nw. golla w.v. 'to sound; boast' ⇒ \*gh/-neh₂- (NEUR). The iterative to \*galan- and \*gel/an-. The latter verb is given its geminate likely to have been back-formed to this iterative.

\*gurpa- - \*gulda- n. 'gold' - Go. gulp n. 'id.', ON gull n. 'id.', OE gold n. 'id.', OFri. gold n. 'id.', OS gold m. 'id.', Du. goud n. 'id.', OHG gold n. 'id.', MHG gait n. 'gold', G Gold n. 'id.' ⇒ \*gh/h₃-to- (IE/EUR) - OCS zlato, Ru. z6loto, SCr. zliito n. 'id.' < \*gho/h₃-to-; Latv. z¢lts m. 'id.' < \*ghe/h₃-to-. A to-stem derived from the root \*ghe/h₃- (see \*gelwa- - \*gulu- 'yellow'). Verner alternations such as ON gull < \*gulpa- vs. OE gold < \*gulda- are more frequent in the neuter o-stems, and can usually be attributed to a singular-plural opposition of the PIE accent. This seems unlikely in the case of 'gold', however, as this word did not have a plural.

<!-- pdf-page: 235; section: dictionary -->

\*guman- 195

\*guman- m. 'man' - Go. guma m. 'id.', ON gumi m. 'id.', OE guma m. 'id.', OS gumo m. 'id.', OHG gomo m. 'id.', MHG gome m. 'id.' ⇒ \*dhghm-on- (EUR) - Lith. zm6nes m.pl. 'men', Lat. homo m. 'man' < \*dhghm-on-. An formation derived from the PIE word for 'earth, land', viz. \*dhegh-m, gen. \*dhgh-m-6s (cf. Hitt. tekan, gen. taknas c. 'land'), with the individualizing n-suffix. All of the attested forms point to \*dhgh-m-on-; contrary to the older literature, there is no compelling reason to reconstruct an ablauting paradigm \*dhegh-m-on, gen. \*dhgh-m-n-65 on the basis of Lat. homo ( < OLat. hemo by rounding before non-palatal m), as the sequence -em- may just as well have arisen from a vocalized \*m. The vocalization occurred regularly in the gen. \*dhgh-T[l-n-6s, and may have spread from there. It is also possible to assume that the vocalization was triggered by a laryngeal, which would point to the Hoffmann suffix \*-Hon-: \*dhgh·T[l·Hon-. The anlaut of Lith. zm6nes is in disagreement with this reconstruction, however. Also cf. ON bniO-gumi, Far. broiJ-gummi, OE bryd-guma, E bride-groom, OS brridi-gumo, Du. bruidegom, OHG brriti-gomo, G Briiuti-gam 'bridegroom' and OS gumiski n. 'senate' < \*gumiskja-.

\*gumban- m. 'curd(?)' - Nw. gumbe, gumme m. 'kind of sweet cheese', G Swi. giimmi 'curd' ( < \*gumbfn-) (GM). An etymologically obscure word. The Nordic form was borrowed into Sarni as gumba, gumpa.

\*gumen- w.v. 'to pay attention' - Ice!. guma w.v. 'id.', OE ofer-gumian w.v. 'to neglect', OSfar-gumon w.v. 'id.' (DRV). A secondary stative created to an unattested adjective \*gauma- 'attentive' < \*ghough-mo-, for which cf. \*gauma- 'heed, attention'; the stative required the zero-grade of the root, which apparently was introduced secondarily, since primary \*ghugh-m- would probably not result in PGm. \*gum-.

\*gump/bon- w.v. 'to hop' - ON goppa w.v. 'to jump', Sw. gumpa w.v. 'to jolt', Elfd. guppa w.v. 'to bob', MHG gumpen w.v. 'to hop, jump' ⇒ \*ghmbh-neh₂- (GM). An iterative verb \*gumpo/Ji, \*gumbunanpi < \*ghmbh-neh₂• With G gupfen 'to bump, jump', the verb has alternatively been reconstructed as \*guppon( cf. DWB 9, 1 142; De Vries 1962: 180), but the co-occurrence of Sw. gumpa and guppa clearly points to a root with a nasal. Also cf. MHG gampen w.v. 'to hop, jump, trample' < \*gambon-.

\*gunda- m. 'pus; decaying skin(?)' - Go. gund n. 'gangrene', Nw. gund m. 'skin crust, serum crust', OE gund m. 'pus', OHG gund m. 'id.' (GM).

<!-- pdf-page: 236; section: dictionary -->

196

\*gunpi- - \*gunpjo- No extra-Germanic etymology. Unrelated to Gr. Kav{luA.o<; m. 'abscess, tumor' (Holthausen 1887: 282) and Av. gur:ida- 'small round bread' (Trautmann 1906: 268).

\*gunpi- - \*gunpjo· f. 'fight' - ON gunnr, guor f. 'id.', OE gila f. 'id.', OS gildea f. 'id.' ⇒ \*gwhfi-tih₂- (IE) - Hitt. kyenzi - kunanzi 'to kill, slay, ruin', Skt hcinti, YAv. jair:iti 'to strike, slay, kill', NP zadan, zan- 'to strike, to hit' < 3sg. \*gwhen-ti, 3pl. \*gwhn-enti. An archaic feminine formation derived from the PIE root \*gwhen- 'to slay'.

\*gurdjan· w.v. 'to gird' - ON gyroa w.v. 'id.', OE gyrdan w.v. 'id.', OS gurdian w.v. 'id.', OHG gurten w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ghrdh-ie- (IE). A ie-present related to the strong verb \*gerdan- ( q.v.).

\*gut(t)on- w.v. 'to pour; to spawn' - ON gota w.v. 'to spawn', Nw. gota, dial. gatta w.v. 'id.', OHG gozzon(?) w.v. 'to percolate' ⇒ \*9hu-t-neh₂- (IE). The iterative \*gutton- is supported by Nw. gatta and possibly also by the OHG gloss cozast 'colas', which has alternatively been interpreted as gozon < \*gautojan- (cf. Mikeleitis-Winter 2001: 214-5). The geminated root \*guttis further corroborated by the deverbative MHG gutz, pl. giltze m. 'gush' (beside guz), Swi. gutz 'id.' (cf. Stickelberger 1889: 439) and probably also by OE guttas m.pl. 'guts' < \*gutta-, which in view of the Nordic meaning 'to spawn' may well belong here too. The strong verb \*geutan- is likely to have arisen as a back-formation to this iterative, which itself seems to have come about by the creation an n-present \*9hut-nehr to the 3sg. aorist form \*ghu-t (for the incorporation of the t-ending into the root, cf. \*hrepan- and \*hlapan-). Evidence for a root extension may also be present in the form of Hitt kiltt- c. 'wall' < \*9heu-t- (Kloekhorst 2008: 498).

### H

\*habanii· f. 'harbor' - ON hpfn f. 'id.', OE ha:!fen f. 'id.', E haven, MLG havene f. 'id.', MDu. haven f. 'id.', Du. haven c. 'id.', G Hafen (= LG) ⇒ \*kap-6neh₂- (WEUR) - Olr. cuan m. 'port, harbor' < \*kap·ono-. A Germanic-Celtic isogloss representing \*kap-6no/eh₂- or \*kop-6no/eh₂- (\*kVp-n6/eh₂- would have resulted in \*\*happo- under Kluge's law). The standard etymology of the word connects it to ON haf, OE h<£f, OFri. hef, MLG haf, MHG hap n. 'sea, lake' < \*haba- < \*kh₂p-6- and \*hafjan- - \*habjan'to lift' < \*kli2p-ie-, which implies an original PIE form \*kh₂p-6no/eh₂-. The limitation to Gm. and Celtic, however, makes it likely that the word spread

<!-- pdf-page: 237; section: dictionary -->

\*haben- 197 from one branch to another at a relatively late stage. Also cf. OHG havan, MHG haven, OS haban- m. 'vessel, pot' < \*hafna- ( < \*klfzp-no-?).

\*haben- w.v. 'to have' - Go. haban w.v. 'id.', ON hafa w.v. 'id.', OE habban w.v. 'id.', E to have, OFri. hebba w.v. 'id.', OS hebbian w.v. 'id.', Du. hebben w.v. 'id.', OHG haben w.v. 'id.', G haben w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*kh₂p-eh₁- (EUR). A stative verb to the European root \*kh₂p-, for which cf. \*hafjan- - \*habjan- and \*happ/bon- 1.

\*habran- m. 'oats' - ON hafri m. 'id.', Far. havri m. 'id.', OS haboro m. 'id.', Du. haver c. 'id.', OHG habaro, haparo m. 'id.', G Hafer, Haber m. 'id.' (DRV). The word is often compared to \*hagran- 'bromus' (q.v.), which, however, has a velar that in view of Olr. corca, coirce 'oats' must be old. It therefore seems more likely that \*habran- was derived from \*hafra- 'goat' (q.v.), as is substantiated by the double meaning of Far. havur 'goat; unthreshed corn'. Note that the f of G Hafer can be due to Bahder's law, and does not necessarily imply a Proto-Germanic variant \*ha/ran-. The doublet Cimb. habaro, havaro in fact suggests that the original proto-form was \*habran-.

\*habuka- m. 'hawk' - ON haukr m. 'id.', Far. heykur m. 'id.', Elfd. ok m. 'id.', OE hafoc, hafuc, heafoc m. 'id.', E hawk, OFri. hauk m. 'id.', OS habuk m. 'id.', Du. havik c. 'id.', OHG habuh m. 'id.', G Habicht 'id.' ⇒ \*kabhu-k/g- (NIE) - Ru. k6bec m. 'small hawk', SCr. kobac m. 'merlin' < \*kabhuk-io-; Lat. capus, Etr. capu 'falcon' < \*kapu-. An obscure word with no IE etymology; the different forms, viz. Pre-Gm. \*kabhug-, Pre-SI. \*kabhuk- and Pre-Italic \*kapu-, show irregular sound correspondences and cannot be reconciled with each other with the help of the known PIE phonological rules. One could perhaps argue that the Italic form \*kap- is due to folk-etymological association with the verb attested as Lat. capere 'to take' ("the snatching bird"), but this does not help much: even if one is willing to accept \*a as a PIE phoneme, the co-occurrence of a plain voiceless and a voiced aspirate stop in the same root would still be in conflict with the known PIE root restrictions. In view of all of these formal problems, it is therefore highly probable that the word for 'hawk' ended up in the European languages as a Wanderwort, spreading across Europe along with the arrival of falconry. This event, which is linked to the invasions of the Huns and Alans, is usually dated in the 4th century, however, which would be too late. For a full discussion, see Strathmann 2008: 2, 125-7. The Gm. word spread further West and North, cf. Fi. havukka and MW hebawc (whence Olr. sebocc). Note that the Welsh form is remarkable, because it has a b corresponding to OE f This may be an indication that the early OE continuant of PGm. \*b still had a plosive articulation. The remod-

<!-- pdf-page: 238; section: dictionary -->

198

\*hafjan- - \*habjaneling of the auslaut OE -oc to W -awe is due to reinterpretation: -awe < PCelt \*-iiko- was a very frequent suffix (Anders )flJrgensen, p.c.).

\*hafian- - \*habjan- s.v. 'to raise, lift' - Go. hafjan s.v. 'id.', ON hefja s.v. 'id.', OE hebban s.v. 'id.', E to heave, OFri. heva s.v. 'id.', OS hebbian, heffian s.v. 'id.', Du. heffen s.v. 'id.', OHG heffen, heven s.v. 'id.', G heben s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*kh₂p-ie- (EUR) - ?Gr. Kamw 'to gulp down', Lat capio 'to take' < \*kh₂p-ie-; Latv. kampt 'to obtain' < \*kh₂-en-p-e-. The variation of geminated and ungeminated forms points to a PGm. paradigm in which the j-suffix was and respectively was not in contact with the root-final consonant. This distribution can be interpreted as pointing to an original i-present 3sg. \*habif>i, 3pl. \*habjinf>i < \*kbzp-ei-ti, \*kbzp-i-enti, something that has already been suggested earlier for Lat. capio (Schrijver 2003b: 67). The accent must have shifted to the root in some of the forms, however, because otherwise the alternation of the roots \*hef and \*hebcannot be accounted for. See also \*haben-.

\*hafra- m. 'billy goat, buck' - ON hafr m. 'id.', Far. havur m. 'billy-goat; handful of (unthreshed) corn', OE hrefer m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kap-ro- (NIE) - Gr. KUTipoc; m. '(wild) boar', Lat. caper m. 'he-goat, buck' < \*kap-ro-; Olr. gabor m., W gafr < \*gabro-. The vacillation of \*kapro- with \*gabro- as well as the occurrence of \*a, which was no phoneme in PIE, may point to a non-IE origin (Schrijver 1991: 96). If so, the word must have been adopted by the individual IE daughter languages after their settlement among different groups of linguistically connected European agriculturalists. Note that the meaning of Far. havur 'bunch of corn' supports the idea that \*habran- 'oats' was derived from this word.

\*haga(n)- m. 'enclosure; pasture' - ON hagi m. 'pasture, field for grazing', Far. hagi m. 'id.', Elfd. agi m. 'meadow; pen', OE haga m. 'hedge, enclosure', OS hago m. 'hedge', OHG hag m. 'hedge, enclosure, dam' ⇒ \*kagh-on- (WEUR) - Lat. caulae f.pl. 'railing or lattice barrier' < \*kagh-e/-eh₂-; W cae, OBret. cai, Bret. kae 'pen, enclosure' < \*kagh-io-. The word originally denoted an enclosure created by shrubs or briars, cf. OHG hagan 'briar', MHG hagen, MDu. haghen m. 'fencing' < \*hagna-; ON heggr m. 'bird cherry (tree)' < \*hagja- and OE hecg, OHG heckia, heggia f. 'hedge' < \*hagjo-. OE hrec m./f., MDu. hec(ke) n. 'hatch', Du. hek n. 'fence' go back to a stem \*hakkja/6- with a geminated root \*hakk- that possibly arose in the genitive of the n-stem \*hagan-: nom. \*hago, gen. \*hakkaz < \*kagh-on, \*kagh·n-6s.

<!-- pdf-page: 239; section: dictionary -->

\*hagla- 199

\*hagla- m.? 'hail' - ON hag! n. 'id.', Far. hag! n. 'hail( stone); drop; shooting pain, inconvenience', OE hagol, haegel m. 'hail', E hail, OFri. heil m.? 'id.', OS hagal m. 'id.', Du. hagel c. 'id.', OHG hagal m. 'hail, thunderstorm', G Hagel m. 'id.' (GM?). A formation that is usually compared to the formally and semantically reminiscent ON hela 'hoarfrost' < \*hihe!On- (q.v.) < \*/(i-/(efhi-o-; \*haglawould then continue a similar reduplicated formation, only with the o-grade as the reduplication vowel (\*lfo-lilhi-o-). However, in view of the Faroese meaning 'pain, inconvenience', there is a chance that \*hag/a- has nothing to do with hela at all. It may alternatively be derived from a verb 'to torment', cf. Du. obs. hagen w.v. 'to torment, agonize' ( = EDu. hagghen 'to quarrel', E to hag 'to torment, to urge; to fatique, tire out', Nw. haga w.v. 'to grow tired, weak' < \*hag(g)on-). The traditional extra-Gm. connection with Gr. Kax.Ariú m. 'small stones, gravel in a riverbed' must at any rate be dropped in view of the irregular variant Kox.Aaú pointing to a non-IE source.

\*hagra- adj. 'slim' - MHG hager adj. 'id.', G hager adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ko/i-r6- (NEUR?) - Lith. kasˡti 'to become slim' < \*koli-ehi-. A ro-stem adjective to a root \*ke/(-, perhaps a non-nasalized variant of \*ken/(- as found in \*hungru- 'hunger' (q.v.). Highly reminiscent of \*magra(q.v.).

\*hagran- m. 'bro mus(?)' - Icel. hellin-hagra f. 'a kind of thyme' ( < \*hagron-), Nw. dial. hagre m. 'oats', OSw. hagri m. 'id.', Elfd. ager m. 'id.', Gutn. hagre 'id.', Da. hejre c. 'brome grass' ⇒ \*kokr6- (WEUR/NIE) - Olr. corca, coirce m. 'oats', W ceirch m. 'id.', Bret kerc'h 'id.' < \*kork-io-. In a number of Scandinavian dialects, the continuants of ON \*hagri have become synonymous with ON hafri 'oats' (< \*habran-, q.v.). Since this meaning is also attested for the cognate Olr. coirce, it can be old. In view of Da. hejre 'bromus', however, the original meaning may have been broader. Brome grass is closely related to the wheat-grass lineage, and is known to infest grain fields. Formally, the alternation of Pre-Gm. \*kokr6- vs. Pre-Celt. \*kork-io- can be explained by assuming metathesis, but the potentially related Nw. dial. hagr, harg m. 'coarse hair from a horse's manes or tail' < ON \*hargr < PGm. \*harga- and Olr. core 'hair' < \*kork-o- can alternatively be derived from an original form \*korkr6- that in Pre-Germanic was dissimilated into \*kokr6-.

\*hahsjo- f. 'ham, back of the knee' - Du. haas c. 'tenderloin', OHG hahsa f. 'back of the knee', MHG hahsa, hehse f. 'id.', G Hii.chse f. 'id.' ⇒ \*kolis-ieh₂- (IE) - Skt. kak$a- m. 'armpit', YAv. kafo- m. 'id.', NP kas 'id.' < \*kolis-o-; Lat coxa f. 'hip of a human, haunch of an animal', Olr. cos f. 'leg' < \*kolis-eh₂-.

<!-- pdf-page: 240; section: dictionary -->

200 \*haidra- An Indo-European word for 'ham' or 'back of the leg'. The forms EDu. hijse 'muscle, boneless flesh' and Du. Limb. hies, hieze '(smoked) ham, back of the knee' probably also belong here. In spite of Pokorny IEW: 543, who compares them to Skt. ki$ku- m. 'forearm' < \*kisk-u- and Lith. kiska f. 'hollow of the knee', Latv. ciska f. 'loin, shank, thigh, hip' < \*kisk-eh₂-, these attestations are more likely to represent h-less variants of G Hiichse, cf. Pal. h₃s, pl. hesa and especially Rhnl. hes, his, hias. Also cf. OHG hahsina f. 'back of the knee', MDu. haessene, heessene, heisene f. 'knee-cap; hamstring' < \*hahsino- / \*hahsnjo-.

\*haidra- adj. 'clear' - ON heior adj. 'bright, clear, cloudless', OE hiidor m./n. 'clear sky', OS hedar adj. 'serene', OHG heitar adj. 'clear, bright', G heiter adj. 'chмar, cheerful' ⇒ \*koit-r6- (IE) - Skt. citra- 'bright, variegated', Av. cifJra'clear, evident' < \*kit-r6-.

\*haifsti- f. 'conflict' - Go. haifsts f. 'contestation, rivalry, conflict', ON heift, heifst f. 'hatred, feud, war', OE hli!st f. 'ferocity', OFri. hiist f. 'anger; rush', MLG heist 'vehemence', MHG heifte f. 'tempestas' ⇒ \*/(oip-sti-? (NEUR) - Perhaps to Lith. 5aipytis 'to snarl' < \*lfoip-eie-; Lith. 5aipas m. 'derision, mockery' < \*foip-o-. The word is often compared to Skt. sfbham adv. 'fast' and sfbhya- 'id.' (cf. Pokorny IEW: 542), but the implied base \*/(iHbh- is in violation of the IE root structure, and cannot simply be transferred to Germanic. I therefore rather connect Lith. 5aipas. Within Germanic, also cf. OFri. hiist, OE hli!ste, OHG heisti, MHG heifte 'vehement' < \*haifstja-. The f was lost in Ingvaeonic, whereas the s disappeared in the remaining West Germanic dialects.

\*haiha- adj. 'one-eyed' - Go. haihs adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*keh₂i-ko- (IE?) - Identical to Lat. caecus adj. 'blind', Olr. caech adj. 'one-eyed', W coeg adj. 'empty, vain; one-eyed, blind' < \*keh₂i-ko-; perhaps further related to Skt. kekara- adj. 'squint-eyed' < \*keh₂ik-ero-.

\*haila- adj. 'whole, sound' - Go. hails adj. 'id.', ON heill adj. 'id.', OE hiil adj. 'id.', E whole, OFri. he/ adj. 'id.', OS he/ adj. 'id.', Du. heel adj. 'id.', OHG heil adj. 'id.', G heil adj. 'sound' ⇒ \*koi-lo- (EUR) - Gr. Hsch. KoV.u 'goodness, beauty'; OCS celo, Ru. celyj, SCr. cl'je/, cl'o adj. 'whole', OPru. kailiistiskan acc.sg.f. 'health' < \*koil-o-; unrelated: Lat. caelum n. 'sky, heaven', OW coil(i)ou pl. 'omens, auguries', MW coel m./f. 'belief, omen', OBret. coel 'haruspicem' < \*keh₂i-lo-. Cf. ON heilsa 'to greet', OE hiilsian 'to give oaths, exorcise', OHG heilison, MHG heilsen 'to interpret omens' < \*hail-so(ja)n- and OE hiilettan, OHG heilazzen 'to greet' < \*hailatjan-.

<!-- pdf-page: 241; section: dictionary -->

\*hailU)a(n)- 201

\*hail(j)a(n)- m. 'brain' - ON heili, heilir m. 'id.', Far. heili m. 'id.', Nw. heile m. 'id.', OFri. heila, hii.l m. 'id.' ⇒ \*koil-io- (WEUR?) - Perhaps to Olr. ccielcin m. 'intestine, gut' < \*koil-. The OFri. form does not necessarily belong here, and has also been derived from \*habulja(n}-, \*habula-, cf. OE hafola, hafela m. 'head' (see e.g. Lendinara 1990: 292), which is related to \*ha(u)beda- - \*ha[u)buda-.

\*haima- m. 'village, home' - Go. haims f. 'village', ON heimr m. 'home; world', Far. heimur m. 'world', Elfd. iem n. 'home', OE hii.m m. 'id.', E home, OFri. hem n. 'id.', OS hem n. 'id.', MDu. heem n. 'id.', OHG heim m./n. 'id.', G Heim n. 'id.' ⇒ \*/(oi-mo- (IE) - Cf. Gr. KOLμaw 'to put to sleep, lull', derived from an unattested \*KoTμo<; or \*KoLμtl 'bed' (Tucker 1990: 240); Lith. kiemas, kciimas, Latv. ciems 'farmstead, village', OPru. caymis 'id.' were borrowed from Germanic. A mo-stem to the PIE root \*liei- 'to lie', cf. Hitt. kitta[n) - kianta 'to lie, be laid, be in place, be set', Skt. sciye 'to lie', Gr. KETμm 'id.', for which cf. Olr. c6im, cciem, W cu adj. 'dear' < \*koi-mo- (with the shift 'belonging to the same home' > 'intimite', Matasovic 2009: 279). Related to \*hlwon-.

\*haino- f. 'whetstone' - ON hein f. 'id.', OE hii.n f. 'id.', E hone ǅ \*lih₃-ei-neh₂- (IE) - Skt. sisii.ti 'to sharpen' < \*lii-lieh₃-; YAv. saeni- adj. 'pointed, sharp' \*lih₃-ei-ni-; Lat. cos, -tis f. 'whetstone' < \*lieh₃-t-. PGm. \*haino- must have been derived from an i-present \*lih₃-ei-ti, \*kn3-i-enti (cf. Skt. syciti 'to sharpen' < \*lih₃-i-), and thus directly points to \*lih₃-ei-neh₂-. The original root \*lieh₃- is preserved by \*harojan-.

\*haira- adj. 'hoary, grey-haired' - ON hcirr adj. 'id.', OE hii.r adj. 'hoary, grey, old', E hoary, OS her adj. 'noble, distinguished, aged', OHG her adj. 'old, reverend, grand' (WEUR) - Olr. cfar adj. 'dark, murky, black' < \*kei-ro-. RuCS sen,, Ru. ser, Sin. sˢr adj. 'grey' < \*f<en> looks like a Germanic loanword, and does not necessarily imply a PIE form \*liHoiro- (the variant OCS sedo, Ru. sed, SCr. sl'jed is a conflation of\*f<en, with \*bl՜do 'pale'). This leaves Olr. cfar as the only possible cognate. Within Germanic, the adjective served as the basis for the comparative OHG her[i)ro, OS herro (= OE hearra, ON herra), OFri. hera, hii.ra m. 'sir', a calque of Lat. senior > Fr. sire, seigneur, sieur, and possibly also for Far. hæra f. 'grizzled or grey hair' < \*hairjon-.

\*hairsa- - \*haisra- adj. 'hoarse' - ON hciss adj. 'hoarse', Far. hcisur adj. 'id.', OSw. hes adj. 'id.', Elfd. jes adj. 'id.', OE hii.s adj. 'id.' heiser., ME hos, hors adj. 'id.', E hoarse, MDu. heesc adj. 'id.', Du. hees adj. 'id.', OHG heis[ar) adj. 'id.', MHG heis[er) adj. 'id.', G heiser adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*kois- (EUR) - Alb. kirrem 'to become hoarse' < \*kis-r-(?).

<!-- pdf-page: 242; section: dictionary -->

202 \*haita- The material contains evidence for at least two different forms: OHG heisar goes back to \*haisra-, while ON hass (E hoarse?) points to \*hairsa( with 1. \*-air- > -cir- and 2. -rs- > -ss-). As \*-ra- is a very common suffix in adjectives, it is probable that \*hairsa- arose from \*haisra- by metathesis. A third, suffixless variant \*haisa- is implied by OHG, MHG heis as well as OSw. hes, Elfd. jes (with secondary nasal vowel, cf. Steensland 2011). In addition, there is MDu. heesc, heisc, Du. hees < \*haisiska-. All of these formations were derived from a further unattested root \*hais-, which may be based on an onomatopoetic verb \*hisson-, cf. EDu. hisschen, hissen, E to hiss.

\*haita- adj. 'hot' - ON heitr adj. 'id.', Far. heitur adj. 'id.', Elfd. iet adj. 'id.', OE hat adj. 'id.', E hot, OFri. het adj. 'id.', OS het adj. 'id.', Du. heet adj. 'id.', OHG heiz adj. 'id.', G heifl adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*koid-o- (GM). An o-grade adjective created to the root \*keid-, for which cf. Go. heito f. 'heat, fever' < \*hlton-. The adj. as well as the inchoative ON hitna w.v. 'to heat up' < \*hitnan- imply a strong verb \*hitan- which, however, is not attested. The root has no certain extra-Gm. cognates, but may have been derived from PIE \*kei- 'to burn' (for which see \*haj(j)a-) with a d-suffix, e.g. \*kei-dh₃- 'to give off heat'. Cf. Go. hais\* n.? 'torch' < \*hajez- < \*koi-es-(?). See also \*hitjo-.

\*haitan- s.v. 'to call' - Go. haitan s.v. 'to call, address; to command', ON heita s.v. 'to call, be called', Far. eita s.v. 'to be called', Elfd. ietta w.v. 'id.', OE hatan w.v. 'id.', OFri. heta s.v. 'to call, to be called; to command; to promise', OS hetan s.v. 'to call, be called; to command', Du. heten s./w.v. 'to be called', OHG heizan s.v. 'id.', G heiflen s.v. 'id.' (IE?) - Sogd. sea- 'to call', Oss. sidyn / sedun 'id.' < \*l<eid-e-(?). A strong verb without extra-Gm. cognates save - perhaps - the ones found in Iranian. The original meaning of the word was 'to call', which - as in English - developed into 'to make a decision'. The meaning 'to be called' developed in medio-passive usage, cf. ON ek heiti 'my name is' < \*haitai < \*lfoid-h₂ei.

\*haipi- f. 'heath, heather' - Go. haipi f. 'open field', ON heior f. 'heath, moor', Far. heioi f. 'id.', Elfd. ieo f. 'id.', OE hci!o m., n. 'heather' ( < \*haipja-), E heath, MLG hede f. 'id.', Du. hei(de) f. 'id.', OHG heida f. 'id.', G Heide f. 'id.' ⇒ \*kaito(WEUR) - OW coit, W coed, Bret koat 'wood', OCo. cuit 'silva' < \*kaito-. An archaic ih₂-stem. E dial. hoath, on the other hand, presupposes a separate formation \*haipa-, which is identical to PCelt. \*kaito-. This is a Germanic-Celtic isogloss, but in the absence of a real Germano-Celtic subnode in the IE language family, it seems likely that the word was borrowed from one branch into the other at an early stage in the languages' histories.

<!-- pdf-page: 243; section: dictionary -->

\*hajan- 203

\*hajan- w.v. 'to pound' - MDu. heien w.v. 'id.', Du. heien w.v. 'to drive, ram' (GM). Usually connected to Arm. xayt'em 'to sting' < \*kh₂eid- and Skt. khidati 'to push' < \*kh₂id-e-, but this implies that the root \*kh₂eid- developed from \*kh₂ei- plus a dental suffix, which is uncertain. The Germanic verb could have developed from a causative \*hajjan- < \*koi(H)-eie- (cf. \*dajjan- 'to suckle' < \*dhh₁-oi-eie-) or \*hajan- < \*koHu-eie- (cf. \*ajja- 'egg' < \*h₂6u-i6-). The latter reconstruction would be the regular causative or intensive of \*hawwan- 'to hew' (q.v.).

\*haj(j)a- n. 'heat' - MDu. hei m. 'heat, drought', OHG hei, ge-hei n. 'id.', G Swi. ge-hei n. 'warm mist' ⇒ \*koi-io-(?) (GM) - Not to Lith. kaisti (kaiCiu) 'to make hot' < \*koit-ie-; Lith. kaisti (kaistu), Latv. kaist 'to become hot' < \*koit-ske-; Lith. kaitrus adj. 'hot' < \*koit-ru-. Also cf. Swi. ge-hei adj. 'warm and misty', Du. heiig adj. 'misty (of sky during hot weather)' < \*-hajja- < \*koi-io-. No accepted etymology. The established link with Lith. kaisti is erroneous: the underlying root kait- was borrowed from Germanic \*haita- (q.v.).

\*hakan- m. 'hook' - Ice!. haki m. 'pick; hook', Far. haki m. 'spade', Elfd. atji m. 'hook', OE haca m. 'hook; bolt or bar of a door', OS hako m. 'hook', Du. haak c. 'id.', OHG htiko, hii.cko m. 'id.', G Haken m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kh₂;3k-on-(?) (GM). A word with several formal problems and no certain extra-Germanic etymology. The NWGm. form \*hakan- must in view of OHG hii.(c)ko, Swi. Visp. haakko < PWGm. \*hiiggan- have secondary k from an analogically singulated geminate \*kk. This points to an n-stem inflection nom. \*hag6, gen. \*hakkaz that was remodeled into \*hak6, \*hakkaz. The long vowel of the High German attestations is probably secondary, and may represent an analogical full grade created to the root \*hak- (cf. Luhr 1988: 286) somewhere in the NWGm. period (cf. Kroonen 2011a: 227-9). The question arises whether this \*ii. replaced earlier \*6 in a paradigm \*h6h6, \*hakkaz < \*keh₂;3k-6n, \*kh₂;3k-n-6s. For an attested 6-vowel, cf. OE hoc, E hook, Du. hoek < \*hok(k)a-.

\*hakula- m. 'cloak' - Go. hakuls m. 'cloak, travelling cloak', ON hpkull m. 'covering', Far. hakil m. 'short cloak', OE ha!cla m., hace/e f. 'id.', OFri. hezil m. 'id.' ( < \*hakila-), OHG hahhu/ m. 'id.' (ORV). A derived formation perhaps related to \*h6kina- 'young goat' (q.v.). Also cf. ON hek/a f. 'cloak' < \*haki/6n-, which like OFri. hezil has a suffix \*-ii- instead of\*-u/-.

\*halan- m. 'tail, tip' - ON hali m. 'id.', Far. hali m. 'tail; top of the shaft of a scythe', Elfd. a/i m. 'tail' ⇒ \*f/(lo/H-on- (IE) - Mir. cai/ 'lance' < \*fl(l/H-i-.

<!-- pdf-page: 244; section: dictionary -->

204 \*halba- The cluster can be connected to Skt. fora- m. 'stick, prickle' < \*lfo/H-o- by assuming that the BS!. *comparanda* generalized depalatalized \*/{ from the zero-grade forms. The root may then be identical to the one of \*helman'rudder, helm' < \*(/{le/h₂-mo- and \*ha/ma- 'blade of grass', which display a similar alternation between palatal and non-palatal \*k.

\*halba- adj. 'half - Go. halbs adj. 'id.', ON ha/fr adj. 'id.', Far. halvur adj. 'id.', Elfd. av adj. 'id.', OE heal[ adj. 'id.', E half, OFri. half adj. 'id.', OS half adj. 'id.', Du. half adj. 'id.', OHG ha/b, halp adj. 'id.', G ha lb adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*kolp-6-(?) (GM). No clear etymology. The links with Skt. kalpate 'to fit, to be partaken by' and with Lith. kalpa f. 'crossbeam' are semantically uncompelling.

\*haleþ- - \*haluþ- m. 'man, hero' - ON hpldr m. 'yeoman', poet hair m. 'man', OE hreleþ, helep m. 'man, warrior, hero', OS he/ith m. 'hero, man', Du. held c. 'hero', OHG he/id m. 'man, warrior, hero', G Held m. 'hero' ⇒ \*/{H/-et(NIE?) - ToB kcilske, kcilyske 'youth, young brahmin' < \*kH/-sko-? The original inflection of the word is not entirely evident ON hali, which has gone over to the n-stems, is assumed to point to a hysterokinetic nominative \*haleþ < \*kH/-£t of an archaic t-stem (with loss of the final \*/J). In addition there is the variant \*ha/up-, which could point to an "ablauting" suffix \*-ut-, potentially a secondary zero-grade, but it might also have developed from \*-eþ-u- by Van Helten's law (e.g. in the datpl. \*-ep-umiz and the ace.pl. \*-eþ-uns). Etymologically, the connection with To. kcilske is possible, but the word further appears to be conspicuously close to Olr. caur, gen. curad m. 'warrior, hero' < \*karut-. The correspondence of \*kale/utwith \*karut- would then point to a non-IE origin.

\*haljo- f. 'hell' - Go. ha/ja f. 'id.', ON He/ f. 'goddess of death', OE he/, helle f. 'id.', OFri. he/le f. 'id.', OS hellia f. 'id.' (also hell m. 'id.' < \*halli-), Du. he/ c. 'id.', OHG hella f. 'id.', G Hiille f. 'id.' ⇒ \*lfol-ieh₂- (DRY) - Olr. eel m. 'concealment(?), dissolution, extinction, death' < \*[(e/-o-. Possibly a derivation from \*[(el- 'to cover' (see \*he/an-). Also cf. Fi. ko/jo 'giant', which, however, has been given a Uralic etymology, cf. Udm. kyl' 'evil spirit' (Collinder 1977: 106).

\*halma- m. 'blade of grass' - ON halmr m. 'id.', OE hea/m m. 'id.', E hau/m, OS halm m. 'id.', Du. ha/m c. 'id.', OHG halm m. 'id.', G Halm m. 'id.' ⇒ \*lfo/h₂-mo- (IE) - CS s/ama, Ru. so/6ma, SCr. slama f. 'straw', OPru. salme 'id.', Latv. saTms m. 'id.', Lat cu/mus m. 'reed' < \*/(o/h₂-mo/eh₂-; Gr. KcUaμo<; m. 'cane' < \*[(/h₂-(e)mo-. Probably related to \*he/man- 'helm, rudder' (through a more primitive meaning 'cane'). (E)Du., LG helm 'sedge, marram grass' (whence Da. hj;elme, Ice!. hjalmur 'bent grass, marram grass'?) represents a similar e-grade for-

<!-- pdf-page: 245; section: dictionary -->

\*halOn- - \*hulon- 205 mation \*he/ma- < \*fk'lefh₂-mo- formally identical to Lith. kelmas 'tree-stub' < \*ke/H-mo-. All these forms go back to a PIE ablauting m-stem \*l<elh₂-m, gen. \*l<lh₂-m-6s (the Baltic forms with non-palatal \*k arose in the zero-grade roots in front of the resonant).

\*halon- - \*hulon- w.v. 'to call' - OE ge-holian w.v. 'to get', OFri. halia w.v. 'to fetch', OS halon w.v. 'to fetch, bring', Du. ha/en w.v. 'to fetch, take away', OHG halon, ho/on w.v. 'to fetch, call, take', G hoh/en w.v. 'to fetch' ⇒ \*k(o)lh₁-(n}eh₂- (IE) - Hitt. kalliszi - kalis5anzi 'to call, evoke' < \*k(e)lhi-s-; Gr. KaAEW 'to call' < \*klh₁-e-; Lat calo, -are 'to announce, summon' < \*klhreh₂-ie-; Latv. kajuot 'to talk idly' < \*kolhr. The origin of the ablaut \*halon- - \*ho/on- is unknown. Since, however, there are many more iterative verbs with a/e-ablaut, we can simply reconstruct a paradigm \*hallo/Ji, \*halunanþi next to \*hullo/Ji, \*hulunanpi < \*k(o)lhi-neh₂-ti, \*k(o)lh₁-nh₂-enti, and assume that the geminated root variants were leveled out. Further cf. ON hjal n. 'talk', Nw. dial. hjal n. 'shout' < \*he/a- < \*kelhi-o-.

\*halsa- m. 'neck' - Go. hals m. 'neck', ON hals m. 'id.', Far. hcilsur m. 'id.', Elfd. as m. 'id.', OE heals, hals m. 'neck; prow of a ship', OFri. hals m. 'neck', MDu. hals m. 'id.', Du. hals m. 'id.', OHG hals m. 'id.', G Hals m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ko/s-o(EUR) - Lat. col/um n. 'neck; hill' < \*kfwlo/s-o-. An a-stem perhaps related to Nw. dial. hulsa 'to lift over one's head, hop' < \*hulson-. Outside Germanic, Lat. col/um and \*halsa- have been compared to Lith. kiiklas m. 'neck', i.e. a reduplicated formation \*kWo-kw/-o- to the root \*kwe/- 'to turn' (see \*hweula- 'wheel'). The loss of the labialization in an alleged \*kwo/so- would be irregular, however.

\*halta- adj. 'lame, limping' - Go. halts adj. 'id.', ON ha/tr adj. 'id.', OE healt adj. 'id.', OFri. halt adj. 'id.', OS halt adj. 'id.', OHG halz adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ko/d-o(WEUR) - Olr. coll n. 'destruction', MW coll m. 'id.' < \*kold-o-. A Germanic-Celtic isogloss (cf. Matasovic 2008: 212). The sometimes connected Lat. clades f. 'destruction' (cf. Pokorny IEW: 545-7) is from \*klh₂-dheh₁-, and cannot directly be compared.

\*halpa- adj. 'sloping; inclined' - Go. wilja-halpei f. 'inclination, bias', ON hallr adj. 'leaning to one side, lying over, sloping; biased, partial', Far. hallur adj. 'inclined, sloping', OE heald adj. 'inclined', OFri. hald adj. 'id.', OHG hald adj. 'sloping' ⇒ \*lfol-t6- (NEUR) - Lith. 5alis f. 'side, shore; country, nation', Latv. salis 'flitch of bacon' < \*lfol-i-. Originally the past participle of an unattested strong verb \*ha/an- 'to slope; be inclined' < \*1(6/-e-. The oldest meaning, i.e. 'to be skewed', is continued by the factitive ON he/la 'to pour', OE on-hieldan 'to lean, incline,

<!-- pdf-page: 246; section: dictionary -->

206 \*hamalabend down', OS afheldian 'to decline', OHG he/dan 'to sink, incline' <

\*halpjan- - \*haldjan- as well as ON ha/la w.v. 'to incline, turn sideways, slope', OHG ha/don w.v. 'to be inclined' < \*ha/pojan-. This points to a connection with the PIE root \*Ji/ei- 'to lean, incline', which looks like an original i-present \*Ji/-ei-f\*lil+ to the root \*lie/-.

\*hamala- adj. 'truncated(?)' - OHG hamal adj. 'mutilated' ⇒ \*Jiom-olo- (IE) - Skt. Scima- adj. 'hornless' < \*Jiem-o-; Ru. kom6/yj, Sin. dial. kom(jf adj. 'hornless' < \*kom-olo- (with depalatalization of the Ji- in the zero-grade forms). Also substantivized as OHG hamal, MHG hamel, G Hammel, (M)Du. hamel m. 'castrated ram' < \*Jiom-olo-. The root \*Jiem- in PIE seems to have meant 'to truncate (e.g. of horns)', for which see \*hindo- 'hind'. The adjective further served as the basis for ON ham/a, OE hame/ian, OFri. homelian, OHG hamalon w.v. 'to mutilate, hamble' < \*hamalojan-.

\*hama(n)- m. 'shape, physical form' - ON hamr m. 'skin, slough; shape, form', Far. hamur m. 'slough; ghost, apparition', OE hama, homa m. 'covering', OS hamo m. 'covering', OHG hamo m. 'skin, covering, net' ⇒ \*Jiom-on(IE) - Skt. scimilla-, siimulya- n. 'shirt' < \*Jiom-. Also cf. Ice!. hem n. 'layer of ice; loosened skin; bag', Elfd. iem m. 'fish skin' < \*hamja-.

\*haman- m. 'rod, shaft' - E hame 'horse collar', MDu. hame, haem 'id.', Du. haam 'id.', MHG ham(e) m. 'fishing rod' ⇒ \*Jiom-o- (IE) - Skt. samyci- f. 'yoke pin, plug, wedge' < \*Jie/om-ieh₂-; YAv. simci- f. 'yoke pin' < \*Jim-ieh₂-; Gr. Kaμa4, -aKo<; f. (m.) 'pole to support the vine, bar, shaft of a spear' < \*Jim-nli-; Arm. sami-k' pl. 'facewood of a yoke'. Also cf. MHG hamel 'stick', Sw., Da. ham me/, Nw. dial. homul, humul ( = ON \*hpmull) 'cross-beam on a wagon' < \*hamu/a- and perhaps also ON hemja 'to rein, force', OFri. hemma 'to hinder', OLFra. chamian 'to clamp', (MH)G hemmen 'to stop, hinder' < \*hamjan-.

\*hamara- m. 'hammer' - ON hamarr m. 'hammer; back of an axe; crag; precipice', Far. hamar m. 'id.', Elfd. cimar m. 'id.', OE hamor m. 'hammer', E hammer, OFri. homer, hamer m. 'id.', OS hamar, hamur m. 'id.', Du. hamer c. 'id.', OHG hamar m. 'id.', G Hammer m. 'id.' ⇒ \*Jih₂-mor-o- (IE). The Germanic word is clearly related to PIE \*h₂eli-mon- 'heavenly vault, anvil, meteorite' (see \*hemina- - \*hemna-), not least in view of the ON meaning 'precipice'. There are some formal problems, however. Especially problematic is the a-vocalism, which ostensibly points to a morphologically impossible pre-form \*h₂1io-mor-o- (thus Pokorny IEW: 18-22). I therefore assume that \*hamara- continues \*liP2-mor-o- with metathesis of the velar

<!-- pdf-page: 247; section: dictionary -->

\*hamfa- 207 and the laryngeal as in Slav. \*kamy 'stone' < \*keh₂-mon- (cf. Orel 2003: 158) and Gr. Kaμapa f. 'vault' < \*l<bz-mr-h₂-.

\*ham/a- adj. 'maimed' - Go. hamfs\* adj. 'id.', OS ha/adj. 'id.', OHG ham/adj. 'id.' Ʊ \*k6mp-o- (EUR) - Gr. Kaμmw 'to bend, bow, curve' < \*kmp-ie- (with secondary μ); Lith. kampas m. 'corner' < \*komp-o-; Latv. kumpt 'to become bent' < \*kmp- (also cf. Lith. kumpas adj. 'curved' < \*kmp-o-). An adjective to a European root \*kemp-, for which see \*hupp/bon- 1.

\*ham(m)o- f. 'shank' - ON hpm f. 'ham or haunch (of a horse)', OE ham, horn f. 'back of the knee', E ham, MDu. hamme f. 'shank, ham', Du. ham c. 'ham', OHG hama, hamma f. 'shank, back of the knee', MHG hamme f. 'id.' Ʊ \*konh₂-m-eh₂- (IE). A feminine formation varying between \*hamo(n)- and \*hammo(n)-. PGm. \*hammo(n)- is usually derived from \*konh₂-meh₂- (with -mm- < \*-nm-), but this leaves ON hpm < \*ham6- unaccounted for. Perhaps, the form with single m can be explained by analogical degemination in the nominative case of an n-stem paradigm, e.g. \*hamm6, \*hammaz < \*l<onh₂-m6n, \*l<onh₂-mn-6s. The n-stem itself seems to have arisen within Germanic itself, but can nevertheless be old in view of the masculine counterpart OHG hamo 'jlexura, suffrago'.

\*hanan- m. 'rooster, singer' - Go. hana m. 'rooster', ON hani m. 'id.', OE hana m. 'id.', OFri. hona m. 'id.', OS hano m. 'id.', Du. haan c. 'id.', OHG hano m. 'id.', G Hahne m. 'id.' Ʊ \*kh₂n-on- (EUR). In view of Gr. Hsch. ΖL-Kav6<; 'rooster', which is a compound \*h₂eus-i-kh₂(e)n-o- "dawn-singer", the Germanic word is usually compared to Lat. can6, Olr. canaid, OW canam, MW canu 'to sing' < \*kh₂(e)n-e-.
