The original meaning 'singer' may further have been preserved in MDu. sprink-hane m., Du. sprink-haan c. 'grasshopper' < \*sprinka-hanan- given the characteristic sound it produces. The masculine n-stem gave rise to the feminine derivatives ON hcena, Far. h;ma, Elfd. yona f. 'hen' < \*h6nj6n- and OE henn, OS, OHG henna f. 'hen' < \*han(n)j6- (quasi IE \*kh₂n-n-ih₂-) as well as to the neuter \*honiz- (q.v.).

\*handu- f. 'hand' - Go. handus f. 'id.', ON hpnd, pl. hendr f. 'id.', Far. hond, pl. hendur f. 'id.', OE hand, hond f. 'id.', E hand, OFri. hond f. 'id.', OS hand f. 'id.', Du. hand c. 'id.', OHG hant f. 'id.', G Hand f. 'id.' Ʊ \*!J<lont-u- (DRV). A u-stem derived from the strong verb \*hinpan- 'to reach for'. There is no compelling reason to assume that the word is somehow connected to Fi. kasi 'hand' < PFU \*kati (thus Schrijver 2003a: 223). The adjectives Go. handugs 'wise', ON hpndugr 'able', OE list-hendig 'having skillful hands', OHG

<!-- pdf-page: 248; section: dictionary -->

208

\*hangjan- - \*hankjanhantag, -fg 'sharp' and continuants only secondarily seem to have become associated with \*handu-.

\*hangjan- - \*hankjan- w.v. 'to (make) hang' - ON hengja w.v. 'id.', OE hengan w.v. 'id.', MDu. hengen w.v. 'id.', OHG henken w.v. 'id.', G hiingen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*lfonk-eie- (DRV). An apparently secondary causative to \*hanhan-: this verb was transitive in e.g. Gothic and Old High German, and may have become intransitive only due to reinterpretation of variants with a jan-suffix (cf. OSw. hamgia (tr./intr.), Elfd. aindja (intr.)) < PIE \*-ie- as (transitive) causatives. Formally, there is compelling evidence for a geminated alternant \*hankjan- in e.g. Swi. (App). heenkxa, (Visp.) heixu (also cf. nominal derivations such as MLG hank 'handle' < \*hanka- and MHG henkel. G Henkel 'id.' < \*hankila-), which points to relatively early contamination with the iterative \*hankon- or \*hunkon- < \*li(o)nk-neh₂-. Evidence for this iterative, which was derived from the strong verb, is further extant in the form of the frequentatives E to hanker 'to hang about; to crave' and Du. hunkeren 'to crave'. Note that the ablaut of these two verbs may reflect the ablaut of the original PIE paradigm that is preserved as Hitt kiinki - kankanzi < \*li6nk-ei, \*link-enti.

\*hanhan- s.v. 'to hang' - Go. hahan w.v. (tr.) 'id.', ON hanga s.v. 'id.', Far. hanga s.v. 'id.', Elfd. aindja s.v. 'id.' ( < \*hangjan-), OE hon s.v. 'id.', OFri. hua s.v. 'id.', Du. hangen s.v. 'id.', OHG hiihan w.v. 'id.', G hangen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*1<6nk-e- (IE) - Hitt kanki - kankanzi 'to hang (tr.); to weigh' < \*1<6nk-ei, \*l<nk-enti; Skt śaṅkate 'to fear', Lat. ciinctor 'to waver, hesitate' < \*l<onk-(t)o. A strong verb often conflated with the secondary stative \*hangen- < \*lionk-eh₁- (cf. Go. hahan, OE hongian, OFri. hongia, OHG hangen). The Germanic formation is directly related to Hitt. kiinki - kankanzi (Sturtevant 1931: 172), a bi-present with o/zero-ablaut: 3sg. \*l<onk-ei, 3pl. \*l<nk-enti (Kloekhorst 2008: 437-8). The Germanic a-grade is inherent to the class of intensive verbs, which is related to the Hittite bi-presents. Unlike in the case of \*9an9an- - \*9un9an- (q.v.), the zero grade seems to have been ousted at an early stage, although traces of it can be retrieved from the iteratives E to hanker 'to hang about; crave' and Du. hunkeren 'to crave' (for which see

\*hangjan- - \*hankjan-). A media-passive (un-transitive) formation is evinced by Skt. śaṅkate and Lat ciinctor, whose meaning appears to have developed from 'to be hanging'. It corresponds to Germanic formations such as ON hciski f. 'danger' < \*hanh-skan-, ON hCEtta w.v. 'to risk, venture' < \*hanhatjan- and MDu. hachte f. 'risk' < \*hanhato(n)- (whence Du. hachelijk 'risky').

<!-- pdf-page: 249; section: dictionary -->

\*hanhila(n)- 209

\*hanhila(n)- m. 'heel' - ON haJll m. 'id.', Far. hrelur m. 'id.', Elfd. iii m. 'id.', OE he/a m. 'id.', E heel, OFri. he/a, heila m. 'id.', Du. hie/ c. 'id.' ⇒ \*k6n-k-o- (IE) - Lith. kinka f. 'leg, thigh, knee-cap' < \*knh₂-k-eh₂-. A diminutive hanhila(n)- created to ON ha-, OE h6h m. 'heel' < \*hanha- < \*konh₂-ko-. The formation appeared to be limited to Nordic and Anglo-Frisian, but Swab. (Gechingen) haile 'heel' (for ai < \*a, cf. Kraibl 'muck rake' < \*kriib-lin-) indicates that the word was out there in the High German dialects as well. Du. hie/ for expected \*heel is a Frisianism. See also \*ham(m]6-.

\*hanhilo- f. 'pothook' - MDu. hael f./n. 'id.', OHG hiihala f. 'id.', G Swi. Visp. heeli f. 'id.' (ORV). An instrumental noun in \*-ila/6- (not \*hanh/6-, pace Seebold 1970: 250) derived from \*hanhan- (q.v.).

\*hanhista- - \*hangista- m. 'horse; stallion(?); gelding(?)' - ON hestr m. 'stallion; horse', Far. hestur m. 'horse; stallion', Elfd. est m. 'horse', OE hengest, hengst m. 'gelding; horse', OFri. hengst, hangst, hingst m. 'horse', ODu. hingest m. 'gelding(?)', Du. hengst 'stallion', OHG hengist m. 'gelding', Hengst m. 'stallion' ⇒ \*l<onk-is-to- (ORV). An etymologically disputed word. It is possible to connect its root to Lith. fankiis adj. 'nimble' < \*l<onk-u- and 5ankinti 'to make jump; to gallop (a horse)', but the suffix, which probably only superficially resembles the superlative element \*-ista-, as well as the origin of Verner alternation remain unclear. Also note that the often compared Lith. s6kti 'to jump, dance' < \* J(eh₂k- contains a different, unrelated root.

\*hanipa- m. 'hemp' - ON hanpr m. 'id.', Far. hampur m. 'id.', Elfd. ampa f. 'id.', OE hrenep, henep m. 'id.', E hemp, MDu. hanep, hannep m. 'id.', Du. hennep c. 'id.', OHG hanaf, hanifm. 'id.', G Hanfm. 'id.' ⇒ \*kanib- (NIE) - Gr. Kavva,Lc; (Kavva,oc;) f. 'hemp, cannabis' < \*kannabi-; Ru. konoplja f. 'id.' (» Lith. kaniipes, Latv. kal)epe, OPru. knapios) < \*kanapi-. A Wanderwort belonging to the pre-Indo-European agricultural layer in Germanic and related languages in Europe. Also cf. Turk. kenevir, Karalpak kenep and Neo-Assyr. qunnabu, qunnapu, qun(u]bu (cf. Barber 1991: 36-38). The comparison with Skt Sal)a-, Mari kene, kine and. Turk. kendir is more uncertain.

\*hanso- f. 'troop' - Go. hansa f. 'troop, cohort', OE hos f. 'company, band', MLG hanse, hense f. 'guild', OHG hansa f. 'troop', MHG hanse f. 'guild' ⇒ \*kom-sh₂-eh₂-(?) (ORV?) - Fi. kansa 'people, society' is a Germanic loanword.

<!-- pdf-page: 250; section: dictionary -->

210 \*hanpa- No certain etymology in spite of numerous proposals (see Lehmann 1986: 177). Many reconstructions assume a first member \*han- < PIE \*kom'together', which in unstressed position became the collective-perfective prefix \*ga(n)-, cf. Osthoff 1888: 425-6. Perhaps, a formation \*kom-sh₂-eh₂-, containing the root \*seh₂- 'to bind' (cf. Hitt. isbai - isbijanzi < \*sh₂-6i-ei, \*sh₂-i-entl) would be conceivable.

\*hanpa- adj. 'sharp' - ON -hannr adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*J(6nt-o- (EUR) - Gr. KE\ITEW 'to sting, goad' < \*J(ent-eie-; Latv. sits m. 'hunting spear' < \*J<nt-o-. An adjective derived from a European root \*/{ent- (LIVZ: 326-7). Also cf. OSw. hannar adj. 'ingenious' < \*hanpara- and the denominative Icel. hanna 'to devise', OSw. hanna 'to handle' < \*hanpojan-.

\*happ/bon- 1 w.v. 'to snatch, grab' - EDu. happen, habben w.v. 'to take, pick, steal', Du. hap-snap adv. 'haphazardly' ⇒ \*kh₂p-neh₂- (EUR). The iterative to \*haben- (q.v.).

\*happ/bon- 2 w.v. 'to succeed' - Sw. happa siig, dial. h<'ipa, habba siig w.v. 'to happen unexpectedly, fit, succeed' ⇒ \*kobh-neh₂- (NEUR) - OCS kobb f. 'fate', Ru. dial. kob' f. 'evil, obstinacy, scoundrel', SCr. kob f. 'encounter, omen, augury' < \*kobh-i-; Cz. po-kobiti se 'to succeed' < \*kobh-eie-; Olr. cob 'victory' < \*kobh-o-. The Swedish dial. variants h<'ipa and habba prove that the verb continues an iterative 3sg. \*happopi, 3pl. habunanpi < \*kobh-neh₂-. Sw. hampa siig w.v. 'to succeed' < \*hampon-, on the other hand, seems to continue a formation with a nasal infix. The meaning 'fate, coincidence', which is found in Germanic and Balto-Slavic, may have developed from 'to hang' in view of Lith. kabJti (kabu) 'to hang' < \*kobh-eh₁-. Also cf. ON happ n. 'good luck' < \*happaand OE ge-hrep adj. 'fit' < \*ga-happa-.

\*harbista- m. 'autumn, fall' - ON haustr m. 'id.', Icel. haust n. 'id.', Far. heyst n. 'id.', Elfd. ost m. 'id.', OE ha!rfest m. 'harvest, autumn', E harvest, MDu. herfst, heerfst, herft m. 'id.', Du. herfst c. 'autumn', OHG herbist m. 'harvest; autumn', G Herbst m. 'autumn' (DRY) - Hitt karpijezi - karpijanzi 'to take (away), take up, lift, pluck' < \*krp-ie-; Gr. Kapn6<; m. 'fruit, corn, yields' < \*krp-o-; Lat. carpo, -ere 'to pluck, gather' < \*kerp-e- (with -e- > -a- after a plain velar); Lith. kifpti (kerpu) 'to chop, cut', Latv. cirpt 'shave' < \*krp-f\*kerp-. A formation derived from \*harbjan- 'to pluck', cf. Nw. dial. herva w.v. 'to snatch'. Nordic haustr arose as a distortion of \*harostR after the syncope of the medial vowel. Also cf. Nw. harv, horv f. 'harrow' < \*harbO-, a probable source of E harrow, and the variant Icel. herfi n. 'id.' < \*harbja-.

<!-- pdf-page: 251; section: dictionary -->

\*hardi- 211

\*hardi- f. 'shoulder' - ON herar f. 'id.', Far. heraar f.pl. 'id.', Elfd. erde f. 'id.', OHG harti, herti f. 'id.', MHG herte f. 'id.' (GM). A feminine nom. \*hardfz, gen. \*hardj6z < \*kort-fh₂-s, \*kort-ieh₂-s. No further etymology.

\*hardu- adj. 'hard, severe' - Go. hardus adj. 'id.', ON harar adj. 'id.', Far. haraur adj. 'id.', Elfd. ard adj. 'id.', OE heard, hard adj. 'id.', E hard, OFri. herd adj. 'id.', OS hard adj. 'id.', Du. hard adj. 'id.', OHG hart, herti adj. 'id.', G hart adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*kort-ii.- (IE) - Gr. Kparuc; adj. 'strong, powerful' < \*krt-u-; Gr. Kpcroc;, KapTO<;, Aeol. KpETO<; n. 'strength, power, authority' < \*kret-os, gen. \*k[t-es-(o)s; Skt. kratu-, Av. xratu-, OP xratu-, xra8u- m. 'power (of the mind), will-power' < \*kret-u-s, gen. \*kr(e)t-u-6s. An adjective often compared to the aforementioned forms in the other IE languages. From this perspective, the o-grade and the Sehwebeablaut remain unexplained, however. I therefore favor the connection with Lith. kartus adj. 'bitter' and OCS krat:bk'b, Ru. kor6tkij, SCr. kr<itak adj. 'short' < \*kort-u- (Strunk 1976; Heidermanns 1993: 282), a u-stem created to the verbal cluster of Lith. kifsti (kirstu) 'to turn sour' < \*krt-ske- and kifsti (kertit) 'to chop' < \*kert-e-, for which cf. Skt krntati 'to cut' < \*kr-n-t-e-.

\*hargu- m. 'sacrificial mound?' - ON hprgr m. 'pile of rocks; sanctuary', OE hearg m. 'pagan tempel, idol', OHG harug m. 'grove, place of sacrifice' ⇒ \*kark-ii.- (NIE) - OW ereie, W eraig f. 'rock' < \*krakja-; Vann. krah 'hillock' < \*krakka-; less certain: Olr. carrae f. 'rock', OW earreee, W earreg f. 'stone, rock' < \*krs-Vk-a-(?) and Olr. earn n. 'heap of stones', W earn m./f. 'id.' < \*krn-(?). A word usually reconstructed as \*haruga-, so as to account for the u-mutation of ON hprgr. Fi. karkko, karkku 'pile, stone wall, cliff, however, proves that the original form must rather have been \*hargu-. The root structure of this form is further substantiated by Nw. (sten-)har(e) 'cliff, rocky bottom', Elfd. ar m. 'bedrock, floor', Du. dial. hare 'hillock', which developed from \*harha(n)-. Outside Germanic, there are some similar formations in Celtic, most notably W craig < \*krak-f- and Vann. krah < \*krakka-, but none of these forms can be reconciled with the Germanic forms. It is therefore possible that the word was adopted from a Pre-IE language by both Germanic and Celtic.

\*harja- m. 'host, troop, army' - Go. harjis m. 'id.', ON herr m. 'id.', Far. herur m. 'id.', OE here m. 'id.', OFri. here m./f./n. 'id.', OS heri m. 'id.', OHG hari, heri n. 'id.', G Heer n. 'id.' ⇒ \*kor-io- (IE) - OP kara- m. 'army, people', Lith. kiiras m. 'war, army' < \*kor-o-; Lith. kiirias m. 'war, army, regiment', Latv. kars m. 'war, army', OPru. kargis (att. kragis) 'army', Olr. euire m. 'troop, tribe', W eordd 'tribe, clan' < \*kor-io-.

<!-- pdf-page: 252; section: dictionary -->

212 \*harma- The neuter forms can synchronically be analyzed as an old collective formation in \*-io-, but the masculine forms seem older in view of e.g. Oir. cuire. Even older is the more primitive formation \*koro-, attested as OP kara-. The similarity of ON Herjann (a name of Odin) and Gr. KOLpavoн m. 'ruler, commander, lord' is notable.

\*harma- m. 'harm, sorrow' - ON harmr m. 'sorrow, grief, Far. harmur m. 'affliction, distress', OE hearm m. 'id.', E harm, OFri. herm, harm m. 'id.', OS harm n. 'id.', MHG harm m. 'id.', G Harm m. 'id.' ǅ \*pfor-mo- (IE) - Av._ fearama- m. 'sense of shame', OCS srama, Ru. s6rom, SCr. sriim m. 'shame' < \*pfor-mo-. A mo-stem to the root \*p/(er-. Also cf. the adjective OE hearm 'sad', NFri. F-A harem 'angry, sad', WFri. herm 'id.' (Kil.), OS harm adj. 'painful' < \*harma-.

\*harman- m. 'ermine' - OE hearma 'shrew-mouse', OS harmo m. 'ermine', OHG harmo m. 'weasel, ermine', MHG harm(e) 'id.' ǅ \*form-on- (NEUR) - Lith. 5armu6, sermu6 m. 'wildcat, stoat, weasel' < \*J(e/orm-on-. A Germanic-Baltic isogloss. Also cf. the diminutive OHG harmilf(n), G Hermelin, Du. herme/ijn. The final stress of the latter two forms is due to It ermellino (Kluge/Seebold 2002: 408), which in turn is a German loanword.

\*hariijan- w.v. 'to sharpen' - MLG haren 'to sharpen', MDu. haren 'to sharpen' ⇒ \*foh₃-r-eh₂-ie (IE/DRV) - Lat. catus adj. 'smart, shrewed', Oir. cath adj. 'wise' < \*/(03-to- (through 'sharp'). A formation derived from an unattested adjective \*hara- 'sharp', which may continue either \*1(03-ro- or \*J(oh₃-r6- (with pretonic shortening). See also \*hain6-.

\*har{r)iin- w.v. 'to wait out(?)' - ON hara w.v. 'to stare, watch motionlessly', Nw. hara w.v. 'to freeze, watch without motion', MHG harren w.v. 'to await', G harren w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*korH-neh₂-? (NEUR?). An iterative related to Ice!. hjara w.v. 'to linger on, sustain oneself, Nw. dial. jarra w.v. 'to move slowly, hang about' < \*her(r)en-. Perhaps further connected with Lith. karti 'to hang' < \*korH-.

\*harta- n. 'resin, pitch' - OS hart n. 'id.', MLG hart, hars n. 'id.', OHG harz n. 'id.', G Harz n. 'id.' ǅ \*ford-o- (IE). A word representing an o-grade formation related to \*hert6n- 'heart' (q.v.). Also cf. Nw. dial. hart m. 'tough piece of bark, gnarl' < ON \*hprtr < \*hartu-. The original meaning was 'pith, essence'.

<!-- pdf-page: 253; section: dictionary -->

\*harunda/o- 213

\*harunda/o- n./f. 'flesh' - ON hprund n./f. 'human flesh; skin, complexion', Ice!. horund n. 'skin', Nw. dial. horung m. 'skin, skin tone', OSw. harund m./f. 'flesh; skin', EDa. harend 'body' ⇒ \*f](lljr-nt-o- (WEUR?). A word possibly related to Lat. caro f. 'meat; flesh' < \*fl(JrH-on (Hellquist 1891: 21; De Vaan 2008: 94) or \*f](lljr-on-. The Germanic form \*harundcould then be an elongation of a corresponding neuter n-stem \*fl(lorH-l:J. or \*fk'lljr-on-. In synchronic Norse, however, the word clearly belonged to a wider cluster of original nt-stems denoting body parts, such as \*waisunda/6- 'wind-pipe' and \*mundo- 'hand'.

\*harwa- adj. 'bitter' - WFri. harch adj. 'dry, barren', Du. dial. haar adj. 'dry, parched', MHG har, herwe adj. 'bitter', G herb adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*kor-uo- (IE) - Gr. KELpU> 'to cut (off), shave, mow, ravage', Arm. k'erem 'to scratch; to chop; to skin' < \*ker-; Skt. carman- n. 'hide, flayed skin', YAv. caraman- n. 'hide, leather', OP carman- n. 'skin, leather', NP carm 'id.' < \*ker-men-; Lat. corium n. 'skin, animal hide' < \*kor-io-; CS kora, Ru. kora, SCr. kora f. 'bark' < \*kor-eh₂-. A wa-stem (cf. Fi. karvas 'bitter; sharp') derived from the PIE root \*ker'to cut'; its o-grade may point to derivation from a further unattested intensive verb \*k6r-e-. Also cf. ON herfiligr adj. 'wretched', OE ge-hierwan w.v. 'to despise', NFri. Hall. fer-heere w.v. 'to mock', MHG herwen w.v. 'to make bitter' < \*harwjan- and MDu. hare f. 'dry cold wind'.

\*harzwa- m. 'flax' - ON hprr m. 'id.', Far. herur m. 'id.', OFri. her m. 'flax', OHG haro, -wes m. 'id.', MHG har(e), har m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kors-uo- (EUR) - Lat. carro 'to card' < \*krs-e-; Lith. kafsti (karsiu), Latv. karst 'to card, comb' < \*kors-ie-. Cf. the diminutive MLG harl(e], harrel, herle 'stalk of flax', MDu. heerle 'id.', Du. harrel 'id.', E harl, her/ 'fiber, filament (of flax)'.

\*hasla- m. 'hazel' - ON has/ m. 'id.', Elfd. assel-buosk 'id.', OE hcesel m. 'id.', E hazel, MDu. hasel m. 'id.', Du. hazelaar c. 'id.', OHG hasal m., hasa/a f. ( <

\*-on-) 'id.', G Hase/ f. 'id.' ⇒ \*kos-lo- (WEUR) - Lat. corulus f. 'hazel-tree, hazel-wood', Olr. coll, OW coll, MW col/(en], pl. cyll 'hazel-tree' < \*kos-elo-; ?OLith. kasulas m. 'wooden spear, stick'. A European word. The Germanic forms point to \*has/a- < \*kos-/o-, but Lat. coru/us implies a full-grade suffix \*-elo- or \*-o/o-, as \*-Vs/- would have developed into \*-VI-, cf. Lat. anhelus 'gasping, panting' < \*anas/o- < \*h₂enh₁-s/o(Schrijver 1991: 44-5).

\*haswa- adj. 'grey' - ON hpss adj. 'id.', OE haso adj. 'id.', MHG heswe adj. 'pale, dull' (DRY).

<!-- pdf-page: 254; section: dictionary -->

214 \*hatiz- An adjective created to \*hesan- - \*hazan- (q.v.) with the productive \*-wasuffix for chromonyms. Also cf. OHG hasan adj. 'mat, dull' < \*hasna-, which has the same suffixation as Lat. ccinus 'grey' < \*ks-no-.

\*hatiz- n. 'hatred' - Go. hatis n. 'hostility, hate', ON hatr n. 'hatred, enmity', OE hete m. 'hatred', ME hete 'id.', OS heti m. 'hate, hostility' ⇒ \*l(!J2d-es- (ORV) - Gr. KfjcSoc; n. 'care, mourning' < \*l{eh₂d-es-. The Germanic forms point to an s-stem \*hatiz- < \*l{f,12d-es-; the loss of the final \*z in WGm. led to the rise of an i-stem \*hati- in Old English and Old Saxon. OHG haz, G Haft, Du. haat 'hatred' are probably different formations derived from the verb \*hato(ja)n-, cf. ON hata, OE hatian, OFri. hatia, OS haton 'to hate'. Also cf. OHG hazzen, G hassen 'id.' < \*haten-.

\*hatjan- w.v. 'to hate' - Go. hatjan\* w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*l{f,12d-ie- (IE) - Av. scidraadj. 'painful', n. 'woe' < \*foh₂d-ro-; Osc. cadeis gen.sg. 'enmity' < \*l{(e)h₂d-i-; Olr. cais 'love, hate', OW cas, MW cas MBret. cas, Bret. kas m. 'hatred' < \*l{h₂d-ti-. Since the Gothic verb cannot be a causative (which would be \*\*hOtjan- < \*/{oh₂d-eie-), it must continue a ie-present \*l{h₂d-ie-. Also cf. OHG hezzen, G hetzen 'to chevy, scamper' < \*hatjan-, cf. OS hettiand m. 'enemy, persecutor'.

\*hattu- m. 'hat' - ON hpttr m. 'id.', Far. hattur m. 'id.', Elfd. att m. 'id.', OE hret m. 'id.', E hat, OFri. hat m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kHt-nu- (WEUR?). The formation looks like a split-off from the ace.pl. of an n-stem \*hapi5, gen. \*hattaz, acc:pl. \*hattuns < \*kHt-on, \*kHt-n-6s, \*kHt-n-ij.s (cf. Luhr 2000: 266; Kroonen 2011a: 304-6), and served as the basis for ON hetta f. 'cowl' < \*hettjon-. The original datsg. \*hadeni < \*kHt-en-i is probably continued by ON heoinn m. 'jacket' and OE heden m. 'robe, hood, chasuble'. Possibly related to OHG hadara f. 'patch, goat skin', G Hader f. 'rag' < \*hapron-, which points to ON haona f. 'young goat' and MHG hate/e f. 'id.' < \*had-, cf. Lat. catu/us 'young animal' < \*kHt-(e/o)/o- as the ultimate source; it is not impossible that hoods and hats were made of goat skin. See also \*hoda- 'hat, hood'.

\*haparo- f. 'fight' - MHG hader f. 'id.', G Hader f. 'id.' ⇒ \*k6t-or-eh₂· (NEUR) - OCS kotora f. 'struggle', Ru. dial. kot6ra, k6tora f. 'hostility, fight' < \*kotor-eh₂·. The word is reminiscent of \*hapu- 'battle', which, however, seems to have developed from \*Cl(lh₃t-u-.

\*ha/Ju· f. 'battle' - ON hpo f. 'id.', OE heaoo- 'id.', OS hathu- 'id.', OHG hadu'id.' ⇒ \*Clþh₃-tu- (IE) - Hitt kattu- n. 'enmity, strife', Olr. cath m., OW cat, MW cad f. 'strife, battle' < \*Cl(lh₃-tu-; Gr. KOTO<; m. 'spite, anger' < \*Ck1h₃·to-.

<!-- pdf-page: 255; section: dictionary -->

\*ha(u)beda- - \*ha(u)buda- 215 A tu-stem to a root •rJ<lh₃-, perhaps the same root underlying \*harojan- 'to sharpen' (q.v.).

\*ha(u)beda- - \*ha(u)buda· n. 'head' - Go. haubip n. 'id.', ON hpfuo n. 'id.', Far. hgvd n. 'id.', Elfd. ovuo n. 'id.', OE hil!fud, hafud, heafod n. 'id.', E head, OFri. haved, hauwed, haud, had n. 'id.', OS holiid n. 'id.', ODu. hovit, houvot n. 'id.', Du. hoofd n. 'id.', OHG houbit n. 'id.', G Haupt n. 'id.' ⇒ \*kh₂p-ut- (WEUR) - Lat. caput, -itis n. 'head'; Olr. cuach, w cawg 'cup' < \*kapuko-. A word with a problematic variation of 1. the roots \*hab- vs. \*haub- and 2. the suffix \*-ed- and \*-ud-. Lat. caput as well as OE hafola, -ela m. 'head' < \*hafe/alan- (= Skt kapala- 'vessel, cup, skull' < \*kap-ola-?) indicate that the root \*haub- is more recent than \*hab-, but it is certainly of PGm. origin in view of Go. haubip. It is possible that the original paradigm was \*hafup, gen. \*habwepaz < \*kh₂p-ut, gen. \*kh₂p-uet-(o}s, and that that the root \*haubarose in the oblique cases by metathesis from \*habw- (cf. Marstrander 1925: 33ff.). Since this explanation implies an old proterodynamic paradigm, it seems unlikely that the word was borrowed from a non-IE European language (pace Beekes 1996: 218-220; Boutkan 1998: lllf.). Perhaps further related to \*hafna- 'vessel', for which see \*habano-.

\*hauha· adj. 'high' - Go. hauhs adj. 'id.', ON har, h6r, haugr adj. 'id.', Far. hdur adj. 'id.', OSw. hggher adj. 'id.', Elfd. 09 adj. 'id.', OE hea, heah adj. 'id.', E high, OS hoh adj. 'id.', Du. hoog adj. 'id.', OHG hoh adj. 'id.', G hoch adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*k6uk-o- (IE) - Identical to OCS kukb adj. 'bent' < \*kouk-o-; also cf. Olr. cuar adj. 'id.' < \*kuk-ro- (or \*kup-ro-). The oldest languages all point to \*hauha- < \*k6uk-o-, but Sw. h₁'19, Elfd. 09, Nw. hgg (Nynorsk) have a Verner variant \*hauga- < \*kouk-6-, for which cf. ON haugr m. 'hill', Lith. kaukas m. 'bump'.

\*hauja· n. 'hay' - Go. hawi n. 'id.', ON hey n. 'id.', Far. hoy99j n. 'id.', OE hfeg n. 'id.', E hay, OFri. hii n. 'id.', OS hoi\* n. 'id.', Du. hooi n. 'id.', OHG hewi, houwi n. 'id.', G Heu n. 'id.' (ORV). A ja-collective (quasi-IE \*koh₂u-io-) created to the root of \*hawwan- 'to hew, cut off (q.v.).

\*haujan· w.v. 'to see to(?)' - ON heyja w.v. 'to carry out, hold, conduct', OE hegan, hfegan w.v. 'to execute' ⇒ \*kouh₁-eie- (IE?) - Skt. a-kuvate 'to intend', SCr. cilti 'to hear' < \*kuhi-e-; Gr. KOEW 'to perceive, understand', Lat caveo, -ere 'to take care, beware' < \*kouh₁-eie-. Magnusson 1989: 324, among others, connects OFri. heia, which he glosses as 'to hold court'. AFHw, however, give a meaning 'to cherish', which makes the verb potentially identical to heia 'to elevate, highten' < \*haugjan-. The Old Norse and Old English verbs can nevertheless be derived from the

<!-- pdf-page: 256; section: dictionary -->

216 \*haukan- IE root \*kouhr 'to observe' under the assumption that the attested meaning 'to carry out' developed out of 'to see to', i.e. 'to take care of.

\*haukan- m. 'fishhook' - G Swi. (App.) h:JUxa m. 'id.' (GM). In view of Fi. haukka 'spoon hanger, fishhook', the word must despite its scant attestation (cf. Vetsch 1910: 161) be of PGm. origin. No further etymology.

\*haukjan- w.v. 'to make squat' - ON heykja-stw.v. 'to bend, cower down', Far. hoykja seg w.v. 'to sit down' (ORV). The causative to \*hrlkan- (q.v.).

\*haula(n)- m. 'hernia, lump' - ON haul/ m. 'id.', OE heala m. 'hernia, hydrocele', OHG ho/a f. 'rupture' ⇒ \*keh₂u-el- (EUR) - Gr. KfJA.ri f. 'tumor; rupture, hernia; hump' < \*keh₂u-l-eh₂-; Lith. dial. killa f. 'lump, hernia, stalk', CS kyla, kila, Ru. kil<i f. 'hernia, outgrowth on plants', SCr. kfla f. 'hernia, oedema, swelling, outgrowth (on plants)' < \*kuh₂-l-eh₂-. The different stem formations go back to an \*/-stem. Since keh₂ul would have given ON h611 by PGm. loss of the labial after \*o (cf. ON s61 < \*seh₂ul) and \*kawela- < \*kP2uel- would have resulted in ON \*\*k<ill by lenghtening of the intervocalically lost \*w, ON haull must have developed from \*haul- < \*hawul- < \*kP2uJ-. This root form can have developed from any case form with a vocalized \*/, e.g. dat pl. \*kbzu-J-mis. Assuming that Dybo's law operated in the genitive and accusative, and that the laryngeal was vocalized before consonantal \*u, the complete paradigm is expected to have developed as follows: nom. keh₂u] > \*ho/, gen. \*kh₂ul-6s > \*kulaz, Joe. \*kp2ueli > :kaweli, etc. Note that the nominative form possibly is continued by ON h611 m. 'hill, bump in a rope', Far. h6llur m. 'round hill', Nw. dial. hole 'ridge-shaped hill'. ON hv<ill m. 'hill', on the other hand, is explained as resulting from \*h6all by Magnusson 1989: 359, and thus implies a variant \*hoala- < \*keh₂u-ol-.

\*hauna· m./n. 'scorn, humilitation' - Go. hauns adj. 'base', MDu. hoon n. 'id.', Du. hoon c. 'id.', G Hohn m. 'id.' ⇒ \*koh₂u-no- (ORV) - Latv. kauns m. 'shame' < \*koh₂u-no-. Derived from a verbal root \*keh₂u-, see \*hawen- 1. Also cf. OE hean adj. 'low, mean, humble' < \*hauna-.

\*haupa- m. 'pile, heap' - OE heap m. 'id.', E heap, OFri. hap m. 'id.', OS hop m. 'id.', Du. hoop c. 'id.', OHG houf m. 'id.' ⇒ \*koHup-n6- / \*kouHp-n6- (IE) - Av. kaofa- m. 'mountain, hump' < \*koupH-o-; Olr. cuan 'pile' < \*kou(H)p-no-; Lith. kaiipas < \*koup(H)-o-, OCS kupo m. 'heap', SCr. kup m. 'heap, crowd' < \*kouHp-o-; Lith. krlpa f. 'pile, bunch' < \*kuHp-eh₂-.

<!-- pdf-page: 257; section: dictionary -->

\*haupan- 217 An a-stem probably continuing Pre-Gm. \*hauppa- < \*kouHp-n6-. Alternatively, it may continue the full-grade root \*keh₂13up- of the ablauting n-stem \*hiipan-, which implies a paradigm \*k[e)uHp-on, gen. \*kuHp-n-6s. In general, the position of the laryngeal is difficult to determine. Av. kaofa- points to \*koupH-o-, but the Slav. material is suggestive of a form \*kouHp-o-; it therefore seems likely that metathesis occurred in Iranian (cf. Skt. §aphci- m. 'hoof, YAv. safa- m. 'hoof, horse-shoe(?)' < \*J<opH-o- vs. PGm. \*hofa- 'hoof < \*koHp-o-).

\*haupan- m. 'testicle' - OFri. hatha, hotha m. 'id.', MDu. hode m. 'id.', OHG hOdo m. 'id.', MHG hode m. 'id.', G Hode m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kout-on-(?) (EUR?) - Lith. kutjis m. 'pouch' < \*kut-io-; Lat. cunnus m. 'vagina' < \*kut-no-; W cwd m. 'bag, sack' < \*kut-o-? A word usually reconstructed as \*hupan- on the basis of OFri. hotha. The OHG scribal variant haodo, on the other hand, points to \*haupan-, and has therefore been separated from \*hupan- (EWAhd: IV, 1084f.). Since, however, OFri. also has a variant hatha, it seems better to derive all of the attested forms from \*haupan-, especially since PGm. \*au gives rise to Old (West) Frisian spelling variants a and o more often, cf. brad - brad 'bread' <

\*brauda-; dad - dad 'dead' < \*dauda-; grat - grot 'great, big' < \*grauta- etc.

\*hauzjan- w.v. 'to hear' - Go. hausjan w.v. 'id.', ON heyra w.v. 'id.', Far. hoyra w.v. 'id.', Elfd. iira w.v. 'id.', OE hieran w.v. 'id.', E to hear, OFri. hera w.v. 'id.', OS horian w.v. 'id.', Du. horen w.v. 'id.', OHG horran, horen w.v. 'id.', G horen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂kous-ie- (IE?) - Gr. aKouw 'to hear, obey' < \*h₂kous-ie-. The word has often been explained as a compound from \*h₂l<- 'sharp' and \*h₂e/ous- 'ear', but the Hsch. gloss aKEUEt, apparently a Cypriotic form meaning 'he/she watches, observes', is irreconcilable with this etymology.

\*hawen- 1 w.v. 'to mock' - ON hci w.v. 'to tease', Ice!. hci w.v. 'to disdain', Nw. hG w.v. 'to mock, tease' ⇒ \*k!;z2u-eh₁- (NEUR) - Lith. kuvJtis 'to be ashamed' < \*kh₂u-eh₁-. The stative verb \*hawen- served as the basis for ON hcio n. 'mockery' <

\*hawepa- and - at an earlier stage - Go. hauns 'base', OE hean 'humble, miserable' < \*hauna-, which again gave rise to OE hienan, OFri. hena, OS gi-hOnian, OHG honen w.v. 'to scorn' < \*haunjan- and OHG hOnida, OS hon(i}tha, MDu. hoonde 'scorn', OFri. hanitha f. 'indictment' < \*haunipo-. See also \*hauna-.

\*hawen- 2 w.v. 'to observe' - Nw. hG w.v. 'to notice, sense, be aware of, OE hawian w.v. 'to view, look, observe, regard, survey, inspect' ⇒ \*kouh₁-ehi(IE) - Skt. a-kuvcite 'to intend to' < \*kuhi-e-; Gr. KOEW 'to pay attention to,

<!-- pdf-page: 258; section: dictionary -->

218 \*hawoperceive, hear', Lat. caveo 'to take care, beware' < \*kouhreie-; OCS cuti 'to sense, notice', ORu. cuti 'to feel, hear, know'; SCr. cuti 'to hear' < \*keuhr. A verb without a generally accepted reconstruction. It is attested only as OE hawian and - to my mind - Nw. hd. Neither of these formations is conclusive as to whether we must reconstruct \*hawen- or \*hewen-, however. In view of Latv. kavet 'to prevent', the former option is preferable. I therefore assume that the Germanic stative was created to an unattested adjective \*hawa- < \*k6uhi-o-, cf. Skt. ci-kava- adj. 'not stingy', Gr. 6:vaKci.ic; adv. 'attentively, heedfully'.

\*hawo- f. 'hide' - ON hci f. 'id.' ⇒ \*kouH-eh₂- (EUR). An o-stem related to \*hudi- 'hide, skin' (q.v.).

\*hawwan- s.v. 'to hew, chop' - ON hpggva s.v. 'id.', Far. hggga s.v. 'id.', Elfd. ogga s.v. 'id.', OE heawan s.v. 'id.', E to hew, OFri. hciwa, hauwa s.v. 'id.', OS hauwan s.v. 'id.', Du. houwen s.v. 'id.', OHG houwan s.v. 'id.', G hauen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*k6uh₂-e- (IE) - ToAB kaw- 'to kill' < \*keh₂u-; Lith. kciuti (kciuna) 'to murder, beat, hew', Latv. kaut 'to fight' < \*ke/oh₂u-; OCS kovati, Ru. kovcit' (kuju) 'to forge, hammer', SCr. kovati 'to forge, shoe' < \*kouh₂-eh₂- (with laryngeal metathesis). An old o-grade intensive verb. See also \*hauja- 'hey' and \*hajan- 'to pound'.

\*hazjan- w.v. 'to praise' - Go. hazjan w.v. 'id.', OE herian, hærian, hergan w.v. 'id.', ME herien w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*l<h₁s-ie- (IE) - Skt. scisti 'to teach, instruct; to punish, chasten; to command, order' < \*l<eh₁s-; ToA kci՝inn- 'to scold' < \*l<h₁s-. A ie-verb to a PIE root present 3sg. \*l<eh₁s-ti, 3pl. \*kh₁s-enti. Also cf. OHG haren w.v. 'to cry, call' < \*hazen- < \*llh₁s-ehr.

\*helan- s.v. 'to conceal, hide' - OE he/an s.v. 'to conceal, hide; cover', OFri. he/a s.v. 'to hide, conceal', OS he/an s.v. 'id.', MDu. he/en s.v. 'to hide, fence', Du. he/en w.v. 'to fence', OHG he/an s.v. 'to conceal, hide', G heh/en s.v. 'to fence' ⇒ \*l<el-e- (IE) - Lat. occulo 'to hide, conceal', Olr. ceilid, MW kelu 'id.' < \*l<el-e-. A strong verb whose original meaning probably was 'to cover', for which see \*helma-. Related to \*heli-.

\*heli- adj. 'deceitful' - MLG hcil adj. 'hidden', OHG hcili adj. 'smooth, slippery, weak', MHG hæl(e) adj. 'concealing; concealed' (DRV). A V[ddhi-gerundive to the strong verb \*he/an- 'to conceal' (q.v.). The meaning 'slippery' as found in WGm. is unexpected, but may have developed from 'trecherous'. With the same semantic shift, cf. ON hcill, Far. hcilur

<!-- pdf-page: 259; section: dictionary -->

\*helma- 219 adj. 'slippery' < \*he/a-, which is derivationally parallel to e.g. \*weta- 'wet' (q.v.).

\*helma- m. 'helmet' - Go. hi/ms m. 'id.', ON hjalmr m. 'id.', Far. hjalmur adj. 'id.', OE helm m. 'id.', OFri. helm m. 'id.', OS helm m. 'id.', MDu. helm m. 'id.', Du . . helm c. 'id.', OHG helm m. 'id.', G Helm m. 'id.' ⇒ \*lief-mo- (DRV) - Close to Skt. sarman- n. 'shelter, cover' < \*l<el-mn-. A derivation to the root of the strong verb \*he/an- (q.v.) in the meaning 'to cover', cf. OE he/an 'to hide; cover'.

\*helman- m. 'rudder' - ON hjalm-vplr m. 'id.', Far. hjalmi m. 'transverse tiller', OE he/ma m. 'id.', E helm, MDu. helm m. 'handle' ⇒ \*ke/H-mon- (IE) - Lith. kelmas m. 'tree-stub' < \*kelH-mo-; OPru. kalmus 'stick' < \*ko/H-m-. Probably related to \*ha/ma-'blade of grass' and \*ha/an- 'tail, tip'.

\*helpan- s.v. 'to help' - Go. hi/pan s.v. 'id.', ON hjalpa s.v. 'id.', Far. hjalpa s.v. 'id.', Elfd.japa s.v. 'id.', OE he/pan s.v. 'id.', E to help, OFri. he/pa s.v. 'id.', Du. he/pen s.v. 'id.', OHG he/fan s.v. 'id.', G he/fen s.v. 'id.' (NEUR?). A verb without a clear etymology. In view of the potential cognate Lith. seTpti (selpiu) 'to help', it is tempting to assume that \*he/pan- continues \*/lelp-e-, and adopted its geminate from the expected iterative \*hulp6n- < \*l<lp-neh₂-. No such iterative is attested, however, and I wonder whether seTpti cannot have been borrowed from a lost Slavic root \*selp- (cf. OCS xlap'b 'servant', Ru. xol6p 'id.', Pol. ch/op 'peasant' < PSlav. \*xolp'b « PGm. \*ha/pa-) that itself again was borrowed from PGm. \*he/pan-. With the occurrence of yet another irregular correspondence, viz. Lith. gelbeti 'to help' < \*g(h₃efb-, the etymological problems become even more complex.

\*hemero- f. 'hellebore' - OHG hemera f. 'id.', MHG hemere, hemer f. 'id.', G dial. hemern pl. 'id.' ⇒ \*kem-er-eh₂- (NIE) - Gr. Kaμapo<;, Kaμμapo<; m. 'larkspur, aconite' < \*kam(m)ar-o-; Lith. kemeras 'hemp agrimony, burr marigold', RuCS cemerb 'hellebore', Ru. dial. cemer 'crown, forelock, headache, belly-ache, horse's disease, poison', SCr. cemer 'venom, anger' < \*kemero-. The Baltic and Germanic forms can be reconciled into a reconstruction \*kemero-, \*-eh₂-. Gr. Kaμapo<;, Kaμμapo<;, on the other hand, cannot, as it displays unexpected a-vocalism and an irregular alternation of single and double μ. As a consequence, it is possible to assume that the word was not inherited from PIE, but rather adopted from an indigenous European language. Notably, a Mediterranean non-IE \*a appears to correspond to a Northern European \*e, like in the case of\*peura- 'bull' and \*ebura- 'boar'.

<!-- pdf-page: 260; section: dictionary -->

220

\*hemina- - \*hemna-

\*hemina- - \*hemna- m. 'heaven' - Go. himins m. 'id.', ON himinn m. 'id.', Far. himin m. 'id.', OE heofon m. 'id.', E heaven, OFri. himel m. 'id.', OS himil, heban m. 'id.', ODu. himil m. 'id.', Du. heme/ c. 'id.', OHG himil m. 'id.', G Himmel m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂eK-mon- (IE) - Skt. asman- m. 'stone, rock, sling-stone; thunderbolt', YAv. asman- m. 'stone, sling-stone; heaven', OCS kamy, Ru. kamen', SCr. kamen m. 'stone' ( < \*keh₂-mon- by metathesis), Lith. akmuo m. 'stone', Gr. liKμwv m. 'anvil, meteorite, sky' < \*h₂eK-mon- (depalatalization in BS!. before -m-). The origin and derivation of the word for 'heaven' are not entirely evi-. dent. Within Germanic, the dialectal difference between Go. himins, ON himinn on the one hand, and OE heofon, OS heban on the other, seems to point to two differen stems, viz. \*hemina- and \*hemna-. The alternative is to assume that \*hemina- was syncopated to \*hemna- in both Old English and Old Saxon, thus offering potential proof for a dialectal subclade. Still, it is probably more attractive to think that both stems split off from the oblique cases of a paradigm nom. \*hemo, gen. \*hemnaz, dat. \*hemeni. The continental WGm. forms with I have been explained as resulting from dissimilation of \*hemina- to \*hemila- (thus already Braune 1891: 94) and by lexical influence from \*soel- - \*sunnon- 'sun' (Wachter 1997: 18). On the PIE level, the Germanic word is usually analyzed as a distorted continuant of \*h₂eK-mi5n, gen. \*h₂K-mn-6s, loc. \*h₂K-men-i (cf. Luhr 2000: 79), which presumably meant 'heavenly vault' in view of Gr. liKμwv, which meant 'meteorite' ("chip from the vault") and 'anvil (on which the Thunder God hit with his hammer)'. Since the regular outcome of this word in Gm. would be \*ahmon, \*humnaz, \*meni (initial \*hm- does not exist in Gm.), it seems likely that the paradigm was remodeled into \*hemo, \*hemnaz, \*hemeni on the basis of the genitive form. Also cf. OHG himilizi, MHG himelz(e), MLG hemelte n. 'ceiling', Du. ge-hemelte n. 'palate' < \*(ga-)hemilatja-.

\*hera- n. 'hair' - ON har n. 'id.', Far. h6r n. 'id.', Elfd. ar n. 'id.', OE hrer n. 'id.', E hair, OFri. her n. 'id.', OS hiir n. 'id.', Du. haar n. 'id.', OHG hiir n. 'id.', G Haar n. 'id.' ⇒ \*/(es-r6- (IE) - Hitt. ki5zi - kifonzi 'to comb, card' < \*kes-ti, \*ks-enti; OCS cesati 'to scratch, pluck' < \*kes-eh₂- etc. (see \*hezdon- for more cognates). The word has been derived from \*hezra- < \*/(es-ro- by Gqsiorowski 2012, who assumes pre-rhotic loss of \*z and compensatory lengthening of the preceding vowel. It is possible, too, however, that the original form was \*f(es-r6- with a lengthened grade, cf. Olr. cir f. 'comb' < \*/(es-reh₂-. The word served as the basis for OE hǣring, hering, OFri. hering, Du. haring, OHG haring, G Hering m. 'herring', a fish named after its many thin, hair-like bones (for the suffix, cf. E whiting, Du. wijting < \*hwltinga-).

<!-- pdf-page: 261; section: dictionary -->

\*herdo- 221

\*herdo- f. 'herd' - Go. hairda f. 'id.', ON hjpriJ f. 'id.', OE heord f. 'id.', E herd, OHG herta f. 'herd; order, queue', G Herd f. 'herd' ⇒ \*kerdh-eh₂- (IE) - OCS creda f. 'order, herd', Ru. cereda f. 'sequence, file, dial. herd of cattle', SCr. creda f. 'tum' < \*kerdh-eh₂-; OPru. kerdan 'time' < \*kerdh-o-; Skt. sardha- m. 'host', YAv. saraoa- m./n. 'species, sort (of cattle)', Lith. kefdiius, skefdiius m. 'herdsman, shepherd' < \*[s)kerdh-o-. A feminine whose original meaning in view of OHG herta and OCS creda 'order, herd' must have been 'queue'. Also cf. Go. hairdeis, ON hiriJir, OHG hirti m. 'herdsman' < \*herdja- < \*CJ<Jerdh-io-.

\*hermjan- w.v. 'to rest' - MDu. hermen w.v. 'id.', OHG hirmen w.v. 'id.', MHG hirmen w.v. 'id.', G Swi. (ge-)hirmen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*l<ermH-ie-(?) (IE?) - Skt sramyati 'to be weary' < \*l<rmH-ie-.

\*her[r)an- m. 'hinge' - ON hjarri m. 'id.', Ice!. hjari m. ('geographical pole, hinge'), Far. hjflfrri f. 'hinge', OE heor[ra) m. 'id.', ME herre 'id.', MDu. harre, herre f. 'id.', Du. har 'hinge, fissure' ⇒ \*kerH-on- (EUR). Beside the masculine word, there is a feminine formation, cf. ON, Ice!. hjara, Far. hjflfrra f. 'hinge' < \*her[r)on-. The ON form hjara is usually taken to be a misspelling of \*hjarra (cf. De Vries 1962: 232), but the originality of the single r is supported by Ice!. hjara (as well as hjari). Therefore, the standard reconstruction \*herz- cannot be maintained, and must be replaced by an n-stem with consonant gradation, i.e. \*hero, \*herraz < \*ker-on, \*k[e)r-n-6s. A zero grade form \*hurran- has been suggested for OSw. hurraka f. 'hinge', but the derivation of this compound is debated. Outside Germanic, the cluster may be related to Lith. karti 'to hang' < \*korH- (cf. Holthausen 1934: 156; Franck 1949: 232). Equally plausible, however, is the connection with \*hurdi- 'door' (q.v.), Lat cratis f. 'construction of wickerwork, hurdle' < \*krh₂-ti-.

\*hersan- - \*herzan- m. 'brain' - ON hjarni m. 'id.', Nw. hjerne, dial. hjarre m. 'id.', Elfd. ienne m. 'id.', ME hernes 'id.', E obs. harn(s) 'id.', MDu. hersene m. 'id.', Du. hersenen, hersens pl. 'id.' ⇒ \*l<erh₂-s-on- (IE) - Skt siras-, gen. sfr$՞cis n. 'head, top', YAv. sarah- n. 'head', M/NP sar 'id.', Oss. stEr 'id.' < \*/ifh₂-es-; Gr. Kapa n. 'head' < \*J<r-h₂-es-n- and KEpac; n. 'horn, for blowing and drinking' < \*l<er-h₂-s-; Lat cerebrum n. 'brain' < \*J<erh₂-s-ro-. In Proto-Germanic, the word for 'brain' was inflected as nom. \*herso, gen. \*herznaz from quasi-PIE \*l<erh₂-s-on, \*l<(e)rh₂-s-n-6s. The allomorphy was given up in the daughter languages, something that is most evident in Old Norse, where the nominative give rise to hjarsi, hjassi m. 'crown of the head' < \*hersan-, the genitive to hjarni < \*herznan-. The latter stem additionally gave rise to the collective formation OHG hirni, MHG hirne, MLG herne, G Hirn, Gehirn n. 'brain' < \*[ga-)herzn-ja-. MDu. hersene < \*hersnan- and Nw.

<!-- pdf-page: 262; section: dictionary -->

222 \*hersja(n)hjarre < \*herzan-, on the other hand, arose as contamination forms of different cases. Also note Du. hoorn-do/ adj. 'mad', whose first element theoretically can be derived from a zero-grade stem \*hurzn- < \*l<rh₂-s-n-.

\*hersja(n)- m. 'millet' - ON hirsi n. 'id.', MDu. herse, heerse f. 'id.', OHG hirsi m. 'id.', G Hirse m. 'id.' ⇒ \*lierh₁-s-io{n)- (IE) - Hitt. karas- n. 'wheat, emmer-wheat' < \*lirhi-s-; Lat. Ceres f. 'goddess of grain' < \*lierhi-es-; Gr. Kop£vvuμt 'to satiate, feed' < \*liorh₁s-; Lith . . Mrti (seriu) 'to feed' < \*kerh₁-ie-. A word created with the collective suffix \*-io- to the original neuter. s-stem attested as Hitt. karas-. The latter form is usually connected to \*gersta- 'barley' < \*ghersd-, and has hence been reconstructed as \*ghrsdh(see Kloekhorst 2008: 444), but the link with \*hersja(n)- 'millet' is more straightforward.

\*herton- n. 'heart' - Go. hairto n. 'id.', ON hjarta n. 'id.', Far. hjarta n. 'id.', Elfd.jiirta n. 'id.', OE heorte n. 'id.', E heart, OFri. herte f. 'id.', OS herta n. 'id.', Du. hart n. 'id.', OHG herza n. 'id.', G Herz n. 'id.' ⇒ \*lierd-n- (IE) - Hitt ker kard(i)- n. 'heart, center, core' < \*lier, \*lird-; ToA kri m. 'will; worry', ToB kiiryafi pl. 'heart' < \*lird-ion-; Skt. hfd-, OAv. zarad- n. 'id.', Oss. zcerdce 'id.' < \*ghrd-; Arm. sirt 'id.' < \*lierd-; OPru. seyr 'id.' < \*lier-; Lith. serdis f. 'core, kernel, pith' < \*lierd-; Lith. sirdis f. 'heart; anger', Latv. sifds f. 'id.' < \*!<rd-; OCS srbdbce, Ru. serdce, SCr. si'ce n. 'heart' < \*lird-ik-io-; CS sredb, sredi, Ru. poseredf, dial. seredb, SCr. sred, dial. sredi 'middle' < Joe. \*lierd-(e)i; SCr. sfd m. 'anger' < \*lird-o-; Gr. Kfjp n. 'heart' < \*lier(d)-; Lat cor, cordis n. 'id.' < \*lird-; Olr. cride n. 'id.', W craidd 'center' < \*lird-. An old IE word, originally inflected as nom. \*lier ( < \*lierd), gen. \*lird-6s. See also \*harta-. The initial \*gh of the Indo-Iranian forms is unexplained.

\*herpa- m. 'hearth' - OE heoriJ m. 'id.', E hearth, OFri. herth m. 'id.', OS herth m. 'id.', Du. haard c. 'id.', OHG herd m. 'id.', G Herd m. 'id.' ⇒ \*kerh₃-to(NEUR). A to-stem created to the root \*kerhr 'to burn', for which see \*hurja-.

\*herpra- n. 'intestines' - Go. hairpra n.pl. 'intestines, bosom', OHG smala-herdar 'small intestine, gut' (GM). Also cf. ON hreiJjar f.pl. 'scrotum' < \*harpjo-(?).

\*heru- m. 'sword' - Go. hairus m. 'id.', ON hjprr m. 'id.', OE heoru, heoro, hioro m. 'id.', OS heru-drorag adj. "sword-bloody" ⇒ \*lier-u- (IE) - Skt sarum. 'projectile, spear, arrow'. An Indo-European term for some kind of projectile.

<!-- pdf-page: 263; section: dictionary -->

\*heruta- 223

\*heruta· m. 'stag. hart, deer' - ON hjprtr m. 'deer', Far. hjertur m. 'id.', Elfd. juort m. 'id.', OE heorot, heart m. 'hart, stag, male deer', OS hirut m. 'stag', Du. hert n. 'deer', OHG hiruz, hirz m. 'id.', G Hirsch m. 'stag, hart, deer' (IE/EUR) - Lat cervus m. 'stag, deer'; OW caru, MW carw 'deer' < \*f(er-u-s, gen. \*Kr-u-6s. A formation created to the PIE root \*/fr- 'horn' with the Germanic animal suffix \*-ut(a)- (cf. \*a/but- 'swan' and \*hurnuta/6- 'hornet'). There is no compelling reason to reconstruct a stem with a \*h₂, as e.g. in Gr. KEpa6<; adj. 'horned', although this has been done in view of the long vowel of the ON hnitr 'ram' < \*hriita-, which has been reconstructed as \*kr-uh₂-do- (Nussbaum 1986: 17). The latter word, however, is actually derived from PGm.

\*hreutan- - \*hriltan-, cf. ON hrj6ta, OE hriltan 'to snore', as is further supported by e.g. Nw. dial. rut, rjot m. 'grumbler'. For the semantics, cf. E to rout 'bellow, low, moo'.

\*herzna· n. 'hard snow' - ON hjarn n. 'id.', Nw. hjlirn f. 'id.' ⇒ \*/(ers-n6- (NEUR) - Close to Lith. sefkfoas m. 'hoarfrost', Latv. s՟rsna f. 'id.' < \*sers-neh₂- (= Nw. hjlirn) and Ru. dial. seren, seren m. 'crust of ice' < \*liers-n-o/eh₂-; with a zero grade: Lith. sifkfoas m. 'hoarfrost' < \*lirs-no-. A Germanic and Balto-Slavic isogloss. Within Germanic, possible cognates are Nw. dial. jorr m. 'layer of ice' < ON \*hjprr < \*herz(n)u- < \*liers-(n)u-. G Hornung 'February' has also been adduced, as it could continue \*hurznung6n- (Hirt 1897: 232-3).

\*hesan- - \*hazan· m. 'hare' - ON hՠri m. 'id.', Ice!. heri m. 'id.', Nw. hare, dial. jase m. 'id.', OSw. heri m. 'id.', Sw. hare m. 'id.', Elfd. eri m. 'id.', ODa. haræ 'id.', Da. hare 'id.' (» Far. hara), OE hara m. 'id.', E hare, OFri. hasa m. 'id.', OS haso m. 'id.', MDu. hase m. 'id.', Du. haas c. 'id.', OHG haso m. 'id.', G Hase m. 'id.' ⇒ \*lies-on- (IE) - Skt. 5asa- m. 'hare' (with assimilation of \*S..s to \*s ... sl, Khot saha-, MP sahog, Pash. soe 'id.' < \*lies-o-; Lat ciinus 'id.', OPru. sasins 'id.' < \*lias·no-; W ceinach f. 'hare' < \*fos-n-ih₂- ( + -ach). The alternation between WGm. \*hasan- and Anglo-Nordic \*hazan- points to an originally mobile paradigm \*hazo, gen. \*haznaz < \*kas-on, \*kas-n-6s (cf. Schaffner 2001: 528, 544-6). The Nw. dial. formjase further implies an ablauting stem \*hesan-, which probably was associated with the strong cases. If it was not introduced analogically (thus proving that the IE ablaut was still productive), the e-grade form can only be reconciled with the a-vocalism by setting up a paradigm \*lih₁es-6n, gen. \*liP1s-n-6s or rather \*lies-on, \*Ks-n-6s (under the assumption of a schwa secundum in the zero-grade forms). The ablaut of ON heri is ambiguous: it may continue \*hazan- with z-mutiation, but the Ice!. form heri points to \*hezan- > \*hjazi > hjeri (with a-breaking and R·mutation). Note that the Germanic n-stem is paralleled by other formations with n-suffixes, cf. Lat ciinus 'hare', OPru.

<!-- pdf-page: 264; section: dictionary -->

224 \*heuhmansasins 'id.' < \*/(as-no- and esp. by W ceinach 'hare' < \*/(as-n-ih₂- ( + -ach ). Related to \*haswa- 'grey'.

\*heuhman- m. 'mass, heap, multitude' - Go. hiuhma m. 'id.' ⇒ \*keuk-mon(IE). An mn-stem related to \*hauha- 'high' and \*huk(k)on- 'to bend down'. The form MLG hocke f. 'haystack' could theoretically have split off from a paradigm \*heuhmo, \*hukkaz < \*keuk-m6'!, \*kuk-mn-6s (with dissimilation of the m in the genitive). The alternative is to reconstruct an independent for-· mation, quasi-PIE \*kuk-neh₂-.

\*heuja- n. 'visible layer, appearance' - Go. hiwi n. 'surface appearance, complexion', ON hy n. 'the down of plants, hair, feathers', Far. hyggj n. 'film of fungus', Nw. hy n. 'fungus, downy beard, young grass', OE hfw, heow n. 'shape, form, hue, color, fashion', ME hue ⇒ \*liieh₁-u-io- (DRV). Probably an early derivation from the base \*liieh₁-u- as found in \*hewja'bluish' (q.v.). The form \*heuja- probably developed from \*keu-i6- < \*kieh₁u-i6- by Dybo's law. Also cf. Ice!. he n. 'dew', Da. dial. hi(e) 'skin on milk' < \*hfwa-.

\*heupa/on- m./f. 'rose hip' - Ice!. hjupa n., hjupur m. 'id.', Nw. dial. hjupa f. 'id.', OSw. hjupon f. 'id.', OE heope f. 'id.', E rose hip, OS hiopo m. 'id.', MDu. hiepe, iepe, niepe f. 'cornell', OHG hiufo m. 'rose hip', MHG hiefe f. 'id.' ⇒ \*keub-on-(?) (NEUR?) - OPru. kaaubri 'thorn'. Also note MHG hie/alter, -ofter 'rose hip', which has the productive tree-suffix -alter as in apfalter 'apple-tree' ( < \*apa/dra/6-), mazalter 'field maple', reckholter 'juniper', wechalter, -ofter 'id.', etc. No certain etymology: the only potential extra-Germanic cognate is OPru. kaaubri, but the appurtenance of this word has been doubted (cf. De Vries 1971: 257). Note the neuter gender in Icelandic, which can be old.

\*hewja- adj. 'dark blue(?)' - OE hawi, hrewi, heawi adj. 'blue, purple, grey, discolored', E obs. haw adj. 'blue, azure, bluish, dull leaden blue; livid' ⇒ \*liieh₁-uo- (IE) - Skt. syava- adj. 'dark-brown, black-brown, dark', YAv. siiauuarfon- prop. 'having dark steeds', MP siya adj. 'black', NP siyah adj. 'id.', Oss. sau adj. 'id.' < \*liiehi-uo-; Lith. syvas, OCS siv'b 'gray' < \*liih₁-uo-. Within Germanic, the word is formally identical to OE -hawe 'visible', the gerundive of OE hawian 'to observe' < \*hawen- 2. The adjective OE hrewen 'blue', on the other hand, with its \*-Ina- suffix seems to have been derived from a noun, possibly \*hæwe as implied by E obs. haw 'nictitating membrane, third eyelid (of a horse, dog, etc.)' < \*hewja-. The whole cluster can thus be related to \*heuja- (q.v.), which occurs with a meaning 'shape' as well as 'membrane'.

<!-- pdf-page: 265; section: dictionary -->

\*hezdon- 225

\*hezdon- f. 'flax fibers' - OE heord f. 'hair', heordan pl. 'rope', E hards, hurds 'the coarser parts of flax or hemp separated in hackling', MDu. hede f. 'flax fiber' 'id.', EDu. herde, heerde 'id.' ⇒ \*kes-dhh₁-eh₂-? (IE) - Hitt kiszi kifanzi 'to comb, card' < \*kes-ti, \*ks-enti; OCS cesati 'to scratch, pluck', Ru. cesat' (cesu) 'id.', SCr. cesati 'to scratch' < \*kes-eh₂-; Lith. kasa f. 'braid', Ru. kosa 'braid, plait', SCr. kosa 'hair, wool' < \*kos-eh₂-; OPru. kexti 'braided hair'; Alb. korr m. 'cut with a sickle, harvest, reap' < \*kos-ro-. The word is usually connected to the PIE root \*kes- 'to comb, card', which is defendable. The PGm. formation \*hezdo- must then have acquired a \*dhh₁-extension, like e.g. \*mizdo{n)- 'reward' < \*mis-dhh₁-eh₂-. For the loss of \*z before \*d in dialectal Germanic, see the same word. ON haddr m. 'hair of the head' < \*hazda- is formally close, but continues an o-grade formation \*kos-dhh₁-o-. Also cf. OFr. hart 'rope'.

\*hi- pron. 'this, these' - Go. his\* pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*l<i- (IE) - Hitt kin. 'this' < \*l<i, Lith. sis pron. 'this' (cf. siafi-dien 'today', sf-met 'this year', se 'here'), ocs Sb pron. 'id.' (cf. dbnb-Sb 'today'), Arm. sa pron. 'id.' < \*l<i-; also in e.g. Gr. crij·w;, Alb. si-vjet 'this year' < \*l<i-uet-es; Olr. ce 'here', Lat. -c(e) in ecce and hi-c < \*J<e. A marginally attested pronoun. Gothic preserved the dat. himma, the acc. hina and the neut. hita. In West Germanic, the original instrumental form \*hio < \*l<i-oh₁ was preserved in some fixed phrases, e.g. OE heodceg, OS hiudiga, OHG hiu tagu, hiutu (G heute). See also \*hiar 'here'.

\*hiar adv. 'here' - Go. her adv. 'id.', ON her adv. 'id.', Elfd. jiir adv. 'id.', OE her adv. 'id.', E here, OFri. hfr adv. 'id.', OS her, hier adv. 'id.', Du. hier adv. 'id.', OHG hiar, hia adv. 'id.', G hier adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*J<i-or (DRV). The North-West Germanic languages all point to \*hiar from PIE \*l<i-or (Kortlandt 2006), which contains the root \*hi- < \*Ii- 'this', cf. Lith. sis 'this' and the suffix -ar as in \*par 'there' < \*to-r. The surfacing of the diphthong \*ia as Go. e, ON e, OHG ia indicates that it merged with \*ez as early as the PGm. period (cf. Kortlandt 2006). Also note Go. hiri, which presupposes a more primary form \*J<i-r plus an obscure suffix -i ( < \*-ih₁ ?).

\*higen- - \*hiken- w.v. 'to pant(?)' - Nw. hika w.v. 'to long for', Sw. dial. hikja 'to pant', Da. hige w.v. 'to long for, wait for', OE hfgian w.v. 'to hie, haste, strive', MDu. higen w.v. 'to pant', Du. hijgen w.v. 'to pant', obs. 'to yearn' (GM). A verb with a semantic range between 'to pant' and 'to long for' (cf. Du. snakken 'to gasp for breath; to yearn'). The alternation of \*hfg- and \*hfk- can be due to infection with the geminate of the iterative \*hikkon-, cf. Du. aan-hikken tegen 'to postpone, procrastinate'). No further etymology: the connection with Skt. sfghr<i- 'fast' and Ru. sigcit' 'to leap' must be abandoned

<!-- pdf-page: 266; section: dictionary -->

226 \*hihel6nbecause the original meaning was 'to pant' in Germanic. Also cf. the highly onomatopoetic Du. hikken, Fri. hikje 'to hiccup'.

\*hihelon- f. 'hoarfrost' - ON Mia f. 'id.', Elfd. ila f. 'id.' ⇒ \*/(i-Kelh₁-eh₂-(?) (IE) - Lith. 5alna, Latv. safna f. 'light frost', OCS slana, SCr. sJQna f. 'hoarfrost' < \*l(olhi-neh₂-; Lith. salti (sqlu), Latv. saft 'to freeze' < \*/(o(-n-)lhi-. The Germanic word looks like a reduplicated formation to the root \*/(e/hi'to be cold', cf. Skt. sisira- 'cold' < \*/(i-K}h₁-o-. The underlying proto-form, however, would through PGm. \*hihul-on- result in ON \*\*hj61-a. For this rea- · son, I reconstruct the word as \*hihel6n- < \*l<i-Kelhi-. Also cf. PGm. \*hagla'hail', which usually is assumed to be another, closely related reduplicated formation. Certainly unreduplicated is Du. ha/ n. 'frozen soil' < \*ha/a- < \*l(olhi-o- (cf. Lith. pii-falas m. 'id.').

\*hindo- f. 'hind' - ON hind f. 'id.', Far. hind f. 'id.', OE hind f. 'id.', E hind, OFri. hinde f. 'id.', OS hind-beri 'raspberry', Du. hinde c. 'hind', OHG hinta f. 'id.', G Hinde f. 'id.' ⇒ \*/(em-teh₂- (IE) - Gr. K£μac;, -Moc; f. 'young deer, pricket' < \*/(em-nd-o-. A word probably derived from the PIE root \*/(em- 'hornless', cf. Skt. samaadj. 'id.' < \*/(em-o-. Related to \*hamala- 'mutilated' (q.v.).

\*hinkan- s.v. 'to limp, hobble' - OE hincian w.v. 'id.', MDu. hinken w.v. 'id.', Du. hinken w.v. 'id.', OHG hinkan s.v. 'id.', G hinken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*keng-e(WEUR) - Olr. cingid 'to tread, step, walk', MW ry-gyng 'to amble' < \*keng-e-. Also cf. OE helle-hinca m. 'devil' and the zero-grade intensive OE huncettan w.v. 'to limp' < \*hunk-atjan-, which is based on a further unattested iterative \*hunk6n- < \*kv9-neh₂-. Apart from Olr. cingid, \*hinkan- is often connected to Gr. uKa0w 'to limp' < \*skng-ie- and Skt. khafijati 'id.' < \*skeng-e- (cf. Seebold 1970: 255), but these forms are rather cognate with

\*skinkan- - \*skankan- - \*skunkan- 'ham, shank' (q.v.).

\*hinno- f. 'thin skin, membrane' - ON hinna f. 'id.', Far. hinna f. 'id.', Elfd. inna f. 'id.', OE hion f. 'meninx(?)', NFri. ha.nn 'lobe of the brain' ⇒ \*kent-n-eh₂-(?) (WEUR?) - Close to Olr. ceinn 'head', cenni pl. 'scales', W cen m. 'skin' < \*ken(t)no-. The connection with PCelt. \*ken(t)no- could imply a form \*kent-neh₂-, and indeed PGm. \*hindn6- would have regularly given \*hinnii-. The existence of a dental is further supported by Far. hind f. 'membrane, film' < \*hindii- (as well as Lat. cento n. 'blanket, patched cloth' if related). In view of Ice!. himna f. 'membrane, film, layer' < \*himnii-, however, it may be preferable to reconstruct an mn -stem \*ken(t)-miin, gen. \*ken(t)-mn-6s, which would probably give PGm. \*himmii, \*hinnaz. Yet another possibility is to connect Nw. hime f. 'thin layer' < \*himiin-, which could require the reconstruction of the para-

<!-- pdf-page: 267; section: dictionary -->

\*hinpan- 227 digm as \*kein-mon, \*kin-mn-6s. The root of the latter formation is irreconcilable with the Celtic forms, however.

\*hinþan- s.v. 'to reach for' - Go. hinpan s.v. 'to catch', OSw. hinna s.v. 'to obtain' ⇒ \*ff(Jent-e- (EUR). The strong verb gave rise to some nominal derivations, viz. Go. hunps f. 'captivity' < \*hunpi-, OE hiio, OHG hunda 'booty' < \*hundii- and \*handu'hand' (q.v.). For a verbal cognate, cf. the iterative \*huntiin- 'to chase, hunt'.

\*hitjo- f. 'heat' - OFri. hete f. 'id.', OS hittia f. 'id.', Du. hitte c. 'id.', OHG hizzea, hizza f. 'id.', G Hitze f. 'id.' ⇒ \*kid-ieh₂- (GM). Related to \*haita- (q.v.). Also cf. ON hiti m. 'heat' < \*hitan-.

\*hittjan- w.v. 'to hit (upon)' - ON hitta w.v. 'to hit upon; to hit, strike; to meet', Far. hitta w.v. 'to meet; to come across', Elfd. itta w.v. 'id.', OE hittan w.v. 'to hit upon, meet with', MDu. hitten w.v. 'to obtain; to succeed' ⇒ \*kh₂idhh₁-neh₂-? (IE?) - Lat. caedo 'to cut, hew, fell' < ?\*kh₂eidfhh₁)-e-. The derivation of the verb is not entirely clear, but it might have been created to an iterative formation \*hittiin-. The formation is often assumed to be related to Lat. caedii, which would imply a Pre-Gm. form \*kh₂eid-neh₂-, but it is not impossible that the Latin verb continues \*f(h₂-ei-dhhi- 'to make fall', i.e. a compound of \*Kh₂-ei- (cf. Skt. slyate 'to fall' < metathesized \*Kih₂-ie-) and \*dheh₁- 'to put'. Similarly, W cwyddaw 'to fall', Bret. coezaff'to fall, happen' may continue \*Kh₂-oi-dhh₁-eh₂-ie-, an extension ofW -chiawr'to fall' < \*Kh₂i-o-. The original Germanic meaning seems to have been 'to strike', cf. the full grade form Far. hfta w.v. 'to spank, beat' < \*hit-.

\*hiwiskja- n. 'household' - ON hyski n. 'id.', Far. huski, hyski n. 'id.', OE hiwisc n. 'id.', OS hiwiski n. 'id.', OHG hiwisci n. 'id.', MHG hiwisch(e) n. 'id.' (DRV). Derived from \*hiwa-, for which see \*hiwon-.

\*hiwon- n. 'married couple, household' - ON hju(n}, hj6(n) n.pl., Far. hjun n.pl. 'id.', OE hfwan n.pl. 'married couple, household members', OFri. hione, hiiine, hine n.pl. 'id.', OS hiwun, hiun n.pl. 'married couple', MDu. huwe f. 'id.', OHG hiiin, hiwen, hfen n.pl. 'id.' ⇒ \*/(ei-uo- (IE) - Skt. seva- adj. 'dear, precious, friendly' < \*/(ei-uo-; Latv. sieva f. 'wife' < \*/(ei-ueh₂- (with unclear accentuation); Lat. civis m./f. 'citizen' < \*/(ei-ui-. A neuter plural n-stem \*hiwiino created to \*hiwa- 'marriage', cf. Go. heiwa-frauja m. 'master of the house', ON hy-byli n.pl. 'homestead', OE hiw-cund adj. 'domestic', G Hei-rat f. 'marriage'. The singular is continued as MHG hie n. 'household member', Cimb. haje, pl. hajar, hair n. 'child'. Also cf. \*hiwiskjaand - on a deeper level - \*haima-.

<!-- pdf-page: 268; section: dictionary -->

228 \*hlahU)an-

\*hlahO)an- s.v. 'to laugh' - ON hlceja s.v. 'id.', Far. lrea w.v. 'id.', Elfd. liia s.v. 'id.', OS hlahan s.v. 'id.', Du. lachen s./w.v., MHG lachen w.v. 'id.', G lachen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*klok-ie- (NEUR) - Ru. klekotcit' 'to scream (of bird of prey)'. The meaning of Ru. klekotat' 'to scream (of birds of prey)' is especially close to the one of the iterative ON hlakka 'to scream (of birds); to rejoice' < \*hlak(k)on- (q.v.), which may indicate that the root originally meant 'to cackle'. Regarding form of the strong verb, the suffix alternation of ON hlreja < \*hlahjan- vs. OS hlahan < \*hlahan- is reminiscent of the one displayed by \*hanhan- 'to hang'. Perhaps, this alternation echoes the paradigm of an an old i-present 3sg. \*klok-ei-ti, 3pl. \*klok-i-enti. See also \*hlahtra- and \*hlogjan-.

\*hlahtra- m. (n.?) 'laughter' - ON hlcitr m. 'id.', Far. lcitur m. 'id.', OE hleahtor, hlehter m. 'id.', E laughter, OHG lahtar, hlahtar n. 'id.', MHG lahter n. 'id.' (DRV). A tro-formation derived from \*hlahjan- (q.v.).

\*hlaiba- n. 'bread' - Go. hlaifs m. 'id.', ON hleifr m. 'id.', OE hliif m. 'id.', E loaf. OHG leip m. 'id.', G Laib m. 'id.' (GM). Possibly related to MLG leve-koke, MHG lebe-kuoche, G Leh-kuchen m. 'gingerbread' < \*hliba-kokan-? No further etymology. The comparison with Gr. KA.lоavo<; m. 'oven, furnace' is spurious.

\*hlainjan- w.v. 'to cause to lean' - OE hlrenan w.v. 'id.', E to lean, OHG leinen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). A late causative to \*hlinen- ( q.v.). Also cf. Go. hlain 'hill', ON hlein f. 'projecting rock, slope' < \*hlaina/6-.

\*hlaiwa- n. 'barrow, funeral mound' - Go. hlaiw m. 'tomb, grave', OE hlciw m. 'id.', OS hleo m. 'grave', OHG leo, hleo m. 'id.', MHG le m. 'id.' ⇒ \*l<loi-uo(IE). A wa-stem to the root \*l<lei- 'to be inclined', for which see \*hlinen-.

\*hlak(k)on- w.v. 'to laugh' - ON hlakka w.v. 'to cry, scream (of eagles); to rejoice, exult', OFri. hlakkia, hlakia w.v. 'id.', WFri. laitsje w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*klok-neh₂- (DRV). The iterative 3sg. \*hlakkofJi, 3pl. \*hlagunanpi to \*hlahUJan- (q.v.). OFri. hlakia and WFri. laitsje continue a variant \*hlakon- with a secondary singulate.

\*hlankjan- w.v. 'to twist, turn' - OE ge-hlencan w.v. 'to braid', MHG lenken w.v. 'to bend' ⇒ \*klong-eie- (GM).

<!-- pdf-page: 269; section: dictionary -->

•hlanpojan- 229 Also cf. MHG, G link adj. 'left' < \*hlinka-, OHG lanka, hlanka, MHG lanke f. 'thigh' < \*hlank6- (» OFr. flanc) and ON hlekkr m. 'chain, fetter' < •h/anki-, OE hlence f. 'link' < \*hlankj6-. The extra-Germanic comparison with the marginally attested Lat cling6, -ere 'to gird' (Paul. ex Fest) or 'to close' (Isidore) < •kfeng-e- and ToA klank 'riding animal', ToB klenke m. 'vehicle' (Adams 1999: 227) is doubtful. •h₃anpojan- w.v. 'to rob' - ON hlanna w.v. 'to rob' (GM). A verb only attested in ON, but further supported by OHG landeri, OE hl6oere m. 'robber' < \*hlanfJ-arja- (cf. with an older suffix: ON hlenni m. 'id.' < \*hlanpjan-) and OE h/60, ODu. hlotha 'booty' < •h/an/J6- (Quak 1979). No clear etymology: hardly an n-infixed cognate of\*hlafJan- (q.v.).

\*hlapan- s.v. 'to load' - Go. af-hlapan s.v. 'to burden, load down', ON hlaoa s.v. 'to pile up; build; load', Far. laoa s./w.v. 'id.', OE hladan s.v. 'to heap, pile up, build, load', E to load, OFri. hleda s.v. 'Id.', OS hladan s.v. 'id.', Du. laden w./s.v. 'id.', OHG ladan, hladan s.v. 'id.', G laden s.v. 'id.' (NEUR) - Lith. kl6ti (kloju), Latv. kliit 'to cover' < \*kleh₂-ie-; OCS klasti 'to put', Ru. klast' (kladu), SCr. arch. klllsti 'to put' < \*kleh₂-dhh₁-. The Balta-Slavic forms imply a root \*kleh₂-, which means that the -d- in Germanic must be suffixal. Presumably, a t was added to the root when the original aorist \*klot < \*kleh₂-t was reanalyzed as a perfect (cf. \*st6d- 'stood' < \*steh₂-t). This reanalysis must have taken place after the merger of \*a and \*6, but before the great sound shifts, as is implied by e.g. the nominal derivative ON hlass n. 'cart-load' < \*klat-to- or \*klat-so-.

\*hlauni- f. 'loin, buttock' - ON hlaun n. 'id.', Nw. dial. faun f. 'id.' ⇒ \*l(/ou-ni(IE) - Skt. sr61J.i-, YAv. sraoni- f. 'buttock, hip'; OPru. slaunis 'thigh', Lith. slaunis f. 'thigh, hip', Latv. slaiina, slaune f. 'hip' < \*l(/ouni- (\*-(i)eh₂-); Lat. cliinis m./f. 'buttocks, haunch', W clun f. 'thigh'; with irregular loss of \*u: Gr. tl6vL<; f. 'haunch' < \*/(/on+. An old IE term for 'thigh'. •h/aupan- s.v. 'to jump, leap' - Go. us-hlaupan 'to leap forth, jump up', ON hlaupa s.v. 'to leap, jump; to run', Far. leypa s.v. 'to jump; to fall, tumble; to curdle', Elfd. Iopa s.v. 'to leap, run; to creep', OE h/eapan s.v. 'to leap, jump, dance, run', E to leap, OFri. hlapa s.v. 'to walk, run; to flow', OS hl6pan s.v. 'to run', Du. /open s.v. 'to walk, run', OHG loufan, hloufan s.v. 'id.', G laufen s.v. 'id.' (DRV) - Lith. klupti (klumpu), Latv. klupt 'to stumble' < \*klu-m-p-. A strong verb back-formed to the iterative \*hlupp6n- (q.v.) < \*klup-neh₂-. The root-final p continues a Pre-Gm. geminate, for which cf. \*hluppjan-. OFri. hlepa w.v. 'to call(?), summon(?)' appears to continue the causative \*hlaupjan-.

<!-- pdf-page: 270; section: dictionary -->

230 \*hlauta-

\*hlauta- m. 'lot' - Go. h/auts m. 'id.', ON hlautr m. 'id.', OS h/ot m. 'id.', OHG /oz, hloz m. 'id.' (DRV). An o-grade formation to \*hleutan- (q.v.).

\*hie/an- s.v. 'to steal' - Go. h/ifan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*k/ep-e- (EUR) - Identical to Lat c/epo, -ere 'id.'; further cf. Gr. KJ.brw 'to steal' < \*klep-ie-; Olr. cluain f. 'deceit' < \*k/op-ni-; Cz. klopa f. 'lapel, flap' < \*klop-eh₂-; OPru. au-klipts 'concealed' < \*-kip-to- (with secondary zero-grade -i-?). A strong verb derived from a European root \*klep-. Also cf. Go. hliftus m. 'thief < \*klep-tu- with an agentive tu-suffix (for which cf. \*werdu- 'host').

\*hleuman- m. 'hearing' - Go. hliuma m. 'id.', ON hlj6mr m. 'sound, tone', Far. lj6mur m. 'id.' ⇒ \*J{/eu-mon- (IE) - Identical Av. sraoman- n. 'hearing'. A mn-stem created to the PIE root \*J{/eu-, for which see \*h/eupa- and \*h/ilda-. Also cf. OHG liumunt, hliumunt m. 'reputation', Skt. sr6mata n. 'id.' < \*J{/eu-mn-to-.

\*hleutan- s.v. 'to obtain by lot' - ON hlj6ta s.v. 'to get, obtain; must', Far. lj6ta s.v. 'to get as one's share; to be obliged, compelled to', OE h/eotan s.v. 'to cast lots; to obtain by lot', OS hliotan s.v. 'to acquire, receive', OHG /iozan, hliozan s.v. 'to draw lots', MHG liezen s.v. 'to draw lots; to soothsay, perform magic' ⇒ \*k/euHd-e- (NEUR) - Lith. kliiiti (kliilvu) 'to stick', Latv. k]iit 'to become, succeed' < \*kleuH-; Latv. k]iitas m. 'fate' < \*kleuH-to-. A strong verb apparently created to a Germanic and Baltic root \*kleuH-. The root-final dental of the Germanic verb is probably etymologically dis• similar from the dental of Lith. kliudyti (kliudail) 'to stick', -dyti < \*-dhh₁-eiebeing a productive verbal suffix. It is possible to speculate that the Germanic t continues the root \*dh₃- 'to give'. The underlying stem \*k/euH-dh₃would then originally have meant 'to give by lot'.

\*hleupa- n. 'listening' - Go. hliup\* n. 'hearing', ON hlj6o n. 'listening; silence; sound', Far. ljoo n. 'id.', Elfd. liuoo f. 'sound' ⇒ \*J{/eu-to- (IE) - Av. sraota- n. 'listening'. A to-stem derived from the PIE root \*J{/eu- 'to listen'. See also \*hlilda-.

\*hleuza- n. 'cheek' - ON hljr n. 'cheek; bow of a vessel; flat of an axe', Ice!. hljr n. 'cheek; bow of a vessel; flat of an axe', OE h/eor n. 'cheek, face', OS h/ior n. 'cheek', MDu. lier n. 'cheek, cheekbone' ⇒ \*k/eus-6- (IE?). In view of the potential etymological link with \*hlusen- 'to listen', the word is usually taken to have originally denoted the area around the ears. A typological parallel is provided by Olr. cluas f. 'hearing; ear' < \*li/ou-sth₂-eh₂-.

<!-- pdf-page: 271; section: dictionary -->

\*hlewa- 231

\*hlewa- n. 'shelter (from wind)' - ON hie n. 'shelter, lee', OE hleo(w) n. 'id.', ME le 'id.', OFri. hli m./n.? 'id.', OS hleo n. 'concealment', EDu. lije 'lee side', Du. lij-zijde 'id.' ⇒ \*J(lehru6- (IE). Also cf. the derived adjective ON hlyr 'mild, warm', OE hlfwe, hleowe 'warm, sheltered', E lew 'lukewarm', MDu. lieu, Du. luw 'sheltered' < \*hleuja-. The word is not ablauting with \*hlewa- (q.v.) < \*lilehruo-, as the regular zero grade \*lilhruo- would never have resulted in PGm. \*hlewa-. It is likely, therefore, that the short \*e of \*hlewa- arose from \*e due to Dybo's law of pretonic shortening.

\*hlewa- adj. 'lukewarm' - Icel. hlcir adj. 'melted; warm', MDu. laeu adj. 'lukewarm', Du. lauw adj. 'id.', OHG liio adj. 'id.', MHG Iii adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*k1ehi-uo(IE) - Lat caleo, -ere 'to be warm' < \*J(lh₁-ehr; without laryngeal: W clyd adj. 'warm', Lith. siftas, Latv. silts adj. 'id.' < \*lil-t6-. Closely related to \*hlewa- 'shelter'. Also cf. ON hl;;er adj. 'warm, mild' < \*hleja- < \*J(lehi-io-.

\*hlinen- w.v. 'to lean' - OE hlinian, hleonian w.v. 'id.', OS hlinon w.v. 'id.', Du. leunen w.v. 'id.', OHG linen, hlinen w.v. 'id.', G lehnen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*kli-n-ehr (IE) - YAv. ni-srinaomi lsg.pres. 'to lean' < \*lili-neu-; Gr. tlfvw 'to bend, incline, lean on, sink' < \*J(li-n-ie-; Lat. -clino, cliniire 'to bend' < \*lilei-neh₂-; Lith. slifiti (slinu) 'id.' < \*lili-n-e-. A stative verb created to an old nasal present \*J(li-neh₂- or \*lili-neu-. The root \*lilei- is again likely to continue an i-present to the root \*lie/-, for which see \*ha/pa-. See further \*hlainjan- and \*hlaiwa-.

\*hloan- s.v. 'to low, bellow' - OE hlowan s.v. 'id.', E to low, OS hloinga f. 'bellowing', ODu. luon, luogin 'id.', Du. loeien w.v. 'id.', OHG luoen s.v. 'id.', MHG liiejen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*lil6hi-e- (IE). An originally strong verb that in view of its o-grade and iterative semantics must continue an intensive liloh₁-e- to the root \*lielh₁- (for which see \*hal6n- / \*hulon-). In spite of OLFr. luogin and OHG luoen, there is no evidence for a j-suffix: this glide just like the w of OE hlowan arose as a hiatus filler in the separate Germanic daughter languages.

\*hlogjan- w.v. 'to make laugh' - Go. uf-hlohjan\* w.v. 'id.', ON hlregja wv. 'id.', Icel. hl;;egja w.v. 'id.', Nw. dial. lngja w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The causative to \*hlahOJan- (q.v.). Also cf. Far. /!iJgin, Nw. lgyen (lngjen) adj. 'funny'.

\*hliida- adj. 'loud' - OE hlud adj. 'id.', E loud, OFri. hllid adj. 'id.', WFri. IUd adj. 'id.', OS hllid adj. 'id.', Du. luid adj. 'id.', OHG Wt, hllit adj. 'id.', G laut adj. 'id.' (DRV).

<!-- pdf-page: 272; section: dictionary -->

232 \*hluni- An adjective derived from the root \*lileu- (for which cf. \*hleupa-). Since this root did not end in a laryngeal, the long \*u must have arisen secondarily within Germanic. Poпsibly, the adjective is to be analyzed as a vṛddhi-gerundive originally meaning 'bearable'.

\*hluni- m. 'maple tree' - ON hlynr m. 'id.', 0Sw. /f1n m.(?) 'id.', Sw. /On c. 'id.', OE hlyn 'id.(?)', MLG /onen-ho/t n. 'maple wood' ⇒ \*k/un-i- (EUR) - Lith. klevas, Latv. k]avs m. 'maple tree' < \*kleu-o-; OCS klen'b, Ru. k/en, SCr. klen m. 'id.' < \*klen-o-. A European word. The alternation of Gm. \*k/un-, Bait. \*kleu- and Slav. \*k/en- is difficult to account for, but can perhaps be resolved by reconstructing a proterodynamic uen-stem, nom. \*k/eu-un, gen. \*klu-en-s that was remodeled into \*k/e-un, \*k/-en-s in Slavic. The Germanic form \*h/uni- would, then again, point to a remodeling \*k/eu-un, \*k/u-n-es. For another potential un-stem, see \*be/unon-.

\*hluppjan- w.v. 'to lift' - MHG lupfen, liipfen w.v. 'to lift; to rush' (DRV). The meaning 'to lift' allows for two potential etymologies, i.e. 1) to \*luftu'sky' (whence ON lyfta, MHG liiften w.v. 'to lift' < \*/uftjan-); and 2) to \*h/aupan- 'to jump, leap'. Since the MHG verb also has an attestation with the meaning 'to rush' (see MHW: 1, 1988), the latter possibility is probably more plausible. The jan-verb may then have directly been derived from the iterative \*hluppon-.

\*hluppon- w.v. 'to run' - MDu. loppen w.v. 'to run' ⇒ \*klup-neh₂- (NEUR) - Latv. klupinat 'to keep stumbling' < \*klup-neh₂-. The iterative belonging to the strong verb \*h/aupan-, apparently continuing a paradigm 3sg. \*h/uppopi, 3pl. \*hlubunanpi < \*klup-neh₂-ti, \*klup-nhi-enti (also cf. ME /open w.v. 'to jump, leap', which does not necessarily have to be a loanword from ON hlaupa, but can also continue \*hlupon-). The formation is exactly parallel to Latv. klupinat, the iterative to klupt. Note that the suffix -ina- in this language arose as a conflation of the zero grade -in- < \*-l}h₂- and the full grade -na- < \*-neh₂-.

\*hlusen- w.v. 'to listen' - OHG losen, h/osen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*lilus-eh₁-ie (IE) - Skt. 5r6$ati 'to listen; to obey' < \*li/eus-e-; Arm. lsem 'to hear, listen; to obey' < \*liluli- by assimilation from \*Ii/us- (cf. luf adj. 'silent' < \*lilus-ro-); OCS slu5ati, SCr. s/Ufoti 'to hear' < \*lilous-ie- (with secondary \*y also OCS sly5atl); ToA klyos-, ToB klyaus- 'id.' < \*lileus-; Lith. klausyti (klausau), Latv. k/ausft 'id.' < \*J(/ous-eie- (with depalatalization of \*libefore -/-). The root \*/(/eus- is an extension of \*J(/eu-, for which see e.g. \*h/euman- and \*h/Uda-, and may have arisen from the s-aorist \*lileus-t 'notices, picks up'. It

<!-- pdf-page: 273; section: dictionary -->

\*hlusti- 233 is further attested in the formations \*hlusti-, \*hluza- and perhaps also in \*hleuza-.

\*hlusti- f. 'hearing, listening' - ON hlust n. 'ear', Far. lust f. 'opening of the auditory meatus', OE hlyst m./f. 'hearing', OS hlust f. 'ear, hearing, listening' : \*l<lus-ti- (DRV /IE) - Skt. SrU$ti- 'complaisance'. A ti-stem derivation to the root \*l<lus-, see \*hlusen-. Cf. the secondary derivations ON hlusta w.v. 'to listen' < \*hlustojan- and OE hlystan w.v. 'id.' < \*hlustjan-. E listen (= OE North. lysna) probably corresponds to MHG liisenen 'to listen' < \*hlusinon-.

\*hluti- m. 'share' - ON hlutr m. 'lot; share, part', Far. lutur m. 'id.', E lot, OHG luz, hluz m. 'id.', MHG luz m. 'share ofland' (DRV). An i-stem derived from \*hleutan- (q.v.). Also cf. OE, OFri. hlot, OHG /oz, hloz, G Los n. 'lot; share' < \*hluta-.

\*hluza- n. 'listening' - ON h/f!Jr, hler n. 'listening, eavesdropping', Ice!. hler n. 'id.', Far. fer n. 'waiting', Nw. dial. standa a /ere 'to stand and listen; to peak' (cf. Icel. standa a hleri 'to stand eavesdroping') ⇒ \*J<lus-6- (IE) - Close to OCS slux"O 'hearing' < \*l<lous-o-. From Proto-Norse \*hloRa-, which gave ON hltar by R-fronting. Also cf. ON hlera w.v. 'to eavesdrop', Far. /era w.v. 'to stand and wait' and ON hleoi, Ice!. hleoi, obs. hleri m. 'shutter, trapdoor' ( < 'to stand ajar'). The correspondence of ON -o- with Ice!. -r- in the latter word is irregular, but has a parallel in the dissimilation of ON yor obi.du. 'you' < \*iowiR < \*iRwiR, cf. Go. izwis < \*izwiz. Note that the forms with -r- must be primary, as the vocalism of ON hleoi, lee!. hleri points to R-fronting of PN \*hloR-. The dissimilation of z to o is likely to have taken place after the devoicing of hi- to J-, so \*hloRen > \*hff!JRi > \*]f!JRi > \*Jf!Joi > Jeoi.

\*hnaigjan- - \*hnaiwjan- w.v. 'to make bow (down)' - Go. hnaiwjan w.v. 'to make go down; to humble', ON hneigja w.v. 'to make bow, incline; to lean', OE hnregan w.v. 'to humble', OS gi-hnegian w.v. 'to bend down', Du. neigen w.v. 'to incline', OHG neigan, hneiken w.v. 'to bow', G neigen w.v. 'to tend, to incline, to bend' (WEUR). The causative of \*hniwan- - \*hnigan- (q.v.). The Pre-Gm. form \*knoigwh-eie- regularly lost the labialization after \*o and before \*i/j (cf. \*sagja- 1 'man, hero' < \*sokwH-i6-). Go. hnaiwjan has its w from hneiwan.

\*hnaskwu- adj. 'soft' - Go. hnasqus adj. 'soft, delicate', OE hnesce adj. 'nesh, soft, delicate, tender, effeminate', E nesh adj. 'soft, tender', WFri. nesk adj. 'delicate', MDu. nesch, nisch adj. 'soft' (DRV).

<!-- pdf-page: 274; section: dictionary -->

234 \*hnawwu- A wa-stem possibly derived from the stem of OHG hnaskon w.v. 'to nibble, have a sweet tooth'. This may in turn be an sk-present to a root \*knes- as retrieved from Lith. knisti_(knisu) 'to break up' (= \*kns- with secondary zero grade -i-).

\*hnawwu- adj. 'stingy; tight' - ON hn119gr adj. 'stingy', OE hneaw adj. 'stingy; near', MDu. nauwe adj. 'tight; precise', Du. nauw adj. 'id.', MHG nou adj. 'id.', G ge-nau adv. 'precisely' (DRV). • A u-stem to the strong verb \*hnewwan- ( q.v.). Also cf. Sw. njugg adj. 'stingy' < \*hnewwja- with East Norse breaking of -i- to -ju-.

\*hne/an- m. 'fist' - ON hnefi m. 'id.' (» ME neve, E nieve), Far. nevi m. 'id.', Elfd. nevi m. 'hand', MHG neve-mez 'a fistful' ⇒ \*knep-on- (GM). The -v- of MHG neve-mez proves that the word must be reconstruced as \*hnefan- rather than \*hneban-. This precludes the received connection with the predominantly Baltic root \*knebh- e.g. Lith. knibti (knimbU) 'to kink, snap' < \*kn-rp-bh-e- and Latv. knibinat 'to pick, collect, gather' < \*knbh-neh₂- (with secondary zero grade in -i-) as well as the semantically more attractive link with Lith. tnybti, gnybti 'to pinch' (see \*knfpan-). Also cf. Elfd. nava w.v. 'to dig; to fumble; to grab' < \*hnafojan- and perhaps also MHG nef m. 'begging', G Swab. nafen 'to beg'.

\*hnekkan- - \*hnakka(n)- m. 'neck' - ON hnakki m. 'id.', Far. nakki m. 'neck; face of a hammer; top of a rock drill', Elfd. nakke m. 'neck', OE hnecca m. 'id.', E neck, OFri. hnekka m. 'id.', ODu. nee, nae m. 'id.', Du. nek c. 'id.', OHG nack, hnack m. 'id.', G Nacken m. 'id.' ⇒ \*knek-on- (IE) - ToA kiiuk 'neck' < \*knek-on-. The word for 'neck' emerges with at least two different ablauting n-stems, viz. \*hnekkan- in Ingvaeonic (but also cf. MHG ge-nicke, G Ge-nick n. 'neck' < \*ga-hnekkja-), \*hnakkan- in Nordic and High German. In addition, a zero-grade formation \*hnukkan-, apparently the source of It., Sp. nuca, Fr. nuque, is evidenced by EDu. nocke 'nucha, spina dorsi, medulla spinalis'. In view of the close formal and semantic similarities with To. kiiuk, it seems possible to reconstruct a PIE n-stem \*knek-on, gen. \*kr;,zk-n-6s, ace.pl. \*knok-n-ၶs (Kroonen 2011a: 167ff.). The regular outcome of this paradigm, viz. \*hneho, \*hunkkaz, \*hnakkuns was apparently remodeled into \*hnekko, \*hnukkaz, \*hnakkuns. Since both the a- and zero-grade forms also occur with the meaning 'top, summit', cf. Far. nakkur m. 'steep rock', OHG hnack m. 'summit' < \*hnakka- and Far. nokki m. 'top of the yard', MDu. nocke 'tip' < \*hnukkan-, it is possible that G Hunke 'hillock' is a vestige of the original genitive form. It is therefore likely that Olr. cnoc, W cnwch m. 'hill' was borrowed from Germanic rather than the other way around.

<!-- pdf-page: 275; section: dictionary -->

\*hneudan- 235

\*hneudan- s.v. 'to rivet' - ON hnj6oa s.v. 'to beat; to rivet, clinch', OHG bi-hneotan s.v. 'to fasten, rivet' (GM). A strong verb usually linked to Gr. Hsch. KV\)flo<; 'small thorn'. This connection is semantically uncompelling, however. Within Germanic, there is a clear link with the iterative \*hnutt/don- 1 (q.v.). The weak verbs MLG neden and nieden, nieten 'to rivet' were derived from the deverbal noun MHG niet, MLG ned 'rivet' < \*neuda-. Further cf. Swi. niitte/ 'penis' < \*hnudi/a-.

\*hneupan- s.v. 'to pluck' - Go. dis-hniupan s.v. s.v. 'to break apart, tear', OSw. niupa s.v. 'to pinch', Elfd. ni1Jopa s.v. 'id.', OE ii.-hneopan s.v. 'to pluck off, E to nip (DRV). A secondary strong verb derived from the iterative \*hnupp/bOn- (cf. Luhr 1988: 369-70; Kroonen 2011a: 106). The root-final \*p continues a shortened geminate, and does not presuppose a PIE \*b (pace Lehmann 1986: 91).

\*hnewwan- s.v. 'to hit' - ON hnfi/ggva, hnyggja s.v. 'to strike, cut off, Elfd. negga s.v. 'to butt', OHG niuwan s.v. 'to thrust; to grind' ⇒ \*kneuH-e- (EUR) - Gr. KVUW 'to scratch' < \*knuH-ie-; Latv. kniit 'to itch'. A strong verb \*hnewwan-. The p of ON hnpggva (for expected \*hnyggva) is analogical after hpggva < \*hawwan- (Rasmussen 1989): the original e-vocalism is confirmed by Elfd. negga. The verb was created to a European root \*kneuH-. The secondary link with Skt. k$m:16ti 'to hurt' < \*tfk'ln-neu(Gunnarson 1971; LIV2: 645-6) is formally difficult, as the European forms seem to point to a root with a laryngeal. See also \*hnawwu-.

\*hnipan- s.v. 'to pinch, squeeze' - MLG nipen s.v. 'id.', MDu. nipen s.v. 'the squeeze; to tear off (with nails or teeth); to snap', Du. nijpen w.v. 'to press' (DRV). The verb continues Pre-Gm. \*hnippan- and must in view of the root-final geminate have been derived from the iterative \*hnippon- (q.v.).

\*hnipon- w.v. 'to be downcast' - ON hnipa w.v. 'to be downcast, droop' ⇒ \*knibh-neh₂-(?) (NEUR) - Lith. knibti (knimbU, knibaii) 'to collapse, sink down' < \*kni-m-bh-e-(?). A secondary stem variant of an iterative 3sg. \*hnippopi, 3pl. \*hnibunanpi possibly continuing \*knibh-neh₂-ti, \*knibh-nh₂-enti. Also cf. ON hnfpa, Far. nfpa w.v. 'to be downcast, droop' < \*hnipen-. The appurtenance of Go. ga-nipnan w.v. 'to be sorrowful' hinges on the emendation to \*ga-hnipnan-. The verb has also been connected to OE (ge-)nipan s.v. 'to grow dark' and ge-nip n. 'cloud', however.

<!-- pdf-page: 276; section: dictionary -->

236 \*hnippon-

\*hnippon- w.v. 'to pinch' - ON hnippa w.v. 'to poke', ME nippen w.v. 'to nip; to pinch; to grab', E to nip ⇒ \*knibh-neh₂- (NEUR?) - Lith. kniebti (kniebiu), Latv. kniebt 'to pinch' \*kneibh-ie-. A marginally attested iterative. The link with Lith. kniebti is attractive, but both in Germanic and Baltic there is an irregular and unresolved variation between \*kneibh- and \*gneibh- (see \*knipan-). Within Germanic, the iterative served as the basis for the strong verb \*hnipan- (cr.v.).

\*hnissa- m. 'smell' - !eel. hniss n. 'smell (from cooking)', Far. nissur m. '(funny) taste, smell', Nw. dial. niss m. 'smell' ⇒ \*knid-so- (EUR) - Gr. Hom. KVLO"Ol] f. 'steam and odor of fat, smell and savor of burnt sacrifice, fat caul' < \*knid-s-eh₂-; Lat. nidor m. 'strong smell, fumes' < \*kneid-os-. The forms in the different IE languages all go back to an s-stem, nom. \*kneid-os, gen. \*knid-s-6s. Also cf. Ice!. hnissa f. 'strong smell' < \*hnisson-.

\*hnit- f. 'nit' - ON gnit f. 'id.', Ice!. gnit, nit f. 'id.', Far. nitrur pl. 'id.', Elfd. gnit f. 'id.', OE hnitu f. 'id.', E nit, MDu. nete f. 'id.', Du. neet c. 'id.', OHG niz, hniz f. 'id.', G Nifi f. 'id.' ⇒ \*IEh₃nid-(?) (EUR) - Gr. Kov[<; f. 'nit; flea; bug', Alb. therf / theni f. 'nit', Arm. anic 'id.' < \*J(h₃nid- (with loss of IE in Arm.?); Olr. sned f., W nedd, nedden sgl. 'id.' < \*snid-eh₂-. A European word with many different irregular cognates. Gr. Kovl<;, Alb. then[ and Arm. anic can perhaps be reconciled into a proto-form \*J(h₃nid-, assuming that the Albanian form went through a stage \*fan id- and that Arm. anic developed from a simplified form \*onid-s. PGm. \*hnit- < \*IEnid-, on the other hand, shows no trace of a laryngeal, and neither does Olr. sned < \*snid-eh₂-. Originally, the word may have been a compound, but due to all kinds of - apparently tabooistic distortions, its reconstruction remains problematic. Also cf. the formally more dissimilar Lat. lens, -endis and Ru. gnfda, SCr. gnjl'da, Lith. glinda, Latv. gnida, gl)ida f. 'nit'.
