The word was probably borrowed from Celtic, cf. Olr /fa, acc. /iaic m. 'stone' and Olr. /ecc f. 'stone, flat slab of rock', MW llech f. 'slate, slab of stone' < PCelt. \*qJlikkii.-, which has been compared to PGm. \*flaho- (Matasovic 2009: 134). The origin of the geminate \*-kk- is uncertain, however.

\*lakjan- 1 w.v. 'to grab' - OE lreccan, /reccean w.v. 'to take, grasp, seize, catch', E to latch ϊ \*lh₂9-ie- (EUR} - Gr. Mϣoμm 'seize, grasp, take, get hold of < \*lh₂g-ie-. Possibly a Germanic-Greek isogloss. Note, however, that A.c:Xϣoμm has also been connected to Gr. A.aμĢc:Xvw 'to take, grasp', which implies an unrelated formation \*/gw-ie-.

\*lakjan- 2 w.v. 'to cause to leak, moisten' - Elfd. lekka w.v. 'id.', OE /eccan w.v. 'id.', MDu. /ecken w.v. 'id.', Du. lekken w.v. 'id.', OHG lecken w.v. 'id.', G /ecken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*log-eie- (EUR}. The causative to \*/ekan- (q.v.).

\*lak(k)on- w.v. 'to reproach, blame' - MDu. /acken, /aken w.v. 'id.', Du. /aken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*lok-neh₂- (DRV). The iterative to \*lahan- (q.v.). Also cf. ON lakr adj. 'deficient', MDu. lac adj. 'lascivious' < \*/aka- and OFri. lek m./n. 'shame, blemish', MDu. lak m./n. 'lack, deficiency', E lack < \*/aka-.

\*lambiz- n. 'lamb' - Go. lamb n. 'lamb, sheep', ON lamb n. 'lamb', Far. lamb n. 'id.', OE lamb n. 'id.', OFri. lam n. 'id.', OS lamb n. 'id.', Du. lam n. 'id.', OHG lamb n. 'id.', G Lamm n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁/-on-bh-es- (IE) - Gr. E:A.acpo<; m. 'deer' < \*h₁/-n-bho-. An s-stem to the base \*h₁l-n-bho-, which is supported by Gr. EA.acpoc;. In both Gothic and Gutnish, the word means 'sheep' (cf. Fi. /ammas 'id.'), and

<!-- pdf-page: 366; section: dictionary -->

326 \*lamjanthis has been taken as an argument in favor of the hypothesis that the Goths emigrated from Gotland. See further \*algi- and \*elha(n)-.

\*lamjan- w.v. 'to beat (up), cripple' - ON lemja w.v. 'to flog, beat (down), suppress', Far. lemja w.v. 'to paralyze; to beat, hit', OE lemian w.v. 'to lame, cripple; to strike', OFri. lemma, lamma w.v. 'to cripple', OS lemmian w.v. 'id.', MDu. lemmen w.v. 'to damage, mutilate', OHG lemjan w.v. 'to cripple, lame', G liihmen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₃/omh₁-eie- (EUR) - Identical to OCS lomiti, Ru. lomft' (lomlju), SCr. /Omiti 'to break'; further cf. Gr. vwA.EμE<;, -tw<; adv. 'without pause, unceasingly' < \*n-h₃/emh₁-es; Lat. lanius m. 'butcher', Mir. laime 'axe' < \*h₃/om-io- (with unrounding of o to a as in lacus 'lake', for which see \*lagu-); Lith. lemti (lemiu), Latv. lemt 'to decide, determine, 'predestine' < \*h₃/em-ie-. A merger of 1) an old causative with direct cognates in Slavic, and 2) a factitive to ON lami, OE loma, OFri. /om, OS, Du. lam, OHG, G lam adj. 'lame' < \*lama(n)- < \*lomH-o-. See also \*lumen-.

\*lamsa- n. 'lock' - ON 16.ss m. 'id.', Far. las n. 'id.', Elfd. lqs n. 'id.', MLG los n. 'padlock' (GM). The nasal in the proto-form \*lamsa-, which is already implied by the etymological link with ON /pm (pl. lamar) f. 'hinges (of a chest)', Nw. lam, /om f. '(metal strap of a) hinge', Elfd. Ian n. 'hinge', is confirmed by Elfd. lqs. The noun, apparently a thematicized s-stem, in turn served as the basis for ON /resa, Elfd. 1£;sa w.v. 'to lock', OHG /emsen\* w.v. 'to bar, shut' < \*lamsjan-. No clear etymology: the often proposed link with Lat. lamina, lammina f. 'thin sheet of metal' is to be rejected.

\*landa- n. 'land' - Go. land n. 'id.', ON land n. 'id.', Far. land n. 'id.', Elfd. land n. 'id.', OE land, lond n. 'id.', E land, OFri. fond n. 'id.', OS land n. 'id.', Du. land n. 'id.', OHG /ant n. 'id.', G Land n. 'id.' ⇒ \*londh-o- (EUR). An o-grade thematic noun to a European root \*lendh- 'unused land'. It is accompanied by the full-grade formation Elfd. linda f. 'overgrown field; meadow' and by the zero-grade \*/unda/6- 'grove'. Possibly, the different ablaut variants split off from a static paradigm, nom. \*/6ndh, gen. \*Jendh-s, Joe. \*lndh-en-i (cf. Matasovic 2009: 232).

\*landjan- w.v. 'to go slowly(?)' - MHG lendern w.v. 'to stroll, amble' ⇒ \*londh-eie- (NEUR) - Lith. lfsti (lefida) 'to crawl, creep, get (in, into), taste good', Latv. list 'crawl, creep, get (in, into), taste good, cut' < \*/ndh-. MHG lendern can be a frequentative in r created to an iterative \*landjan-, which would imply a root \*lendh- that is also found in Baltic. Note, however, the clear association with the semantically identical G schlendern.

<!-- pdf-page: 367; section: dictionary -->

\*landjo- 327

\*landjo- f. 'lower part of the back, kidney, loin' - ON lend f. 'id.', Far. lend f. 'id.', OE Iendenu f.pl. 'id.', ODu. lendin f.pl. 'id.', Du. Ienden pl. 'id.', OHG Ientfn f.pl. 'id.', G Lende f. 'id.' ⇒ \*londh-ih₂- (IE) - Skt. rtindhra- n. 'part of the body of animals, loin' < \*londh-ro-; Lat. lumbus m. 'hips, loins' < \*londh-uo-; OCS l<;dvijǻ f.pl. 'loins', CS lǻdvije n. 'loin', lǻdvija f. 'id.', Ru. obs. Ijtidveja f. 'thigh', SCr. ledvija n.pl. 'loins' < \*lndh-u-io/ehr. The WGm. forms point to an in-stem, which probably represents a remodeling of the jo-stem continued by ON lend (cf. Fi. Iantio ). An ablaut variant of \*lundOJo- ( q.v.).

\*langa- adj. 'long' - Go. laggs adj. 'id.', ON langr adj. 'id.', Far. Iangur adj. 'id.', Elfd. Jaungg adj. 'id.', OE fang adj. 'id.', E long, OFri. long adj. 'id.', OS fang adj. 'id.', Du. fang adj. 'id.', OHG fang adj. 'id.', G fang adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*dlonhigh-o(IE) - Identical to Lat. longus adj. 'long'. A nasalized variant of the PIE root \*dlh₁gh- 'long', for which see \*tulgu-. The adjective thus implies a nasal present \*dlenhigh-e-, which, however, is not attested.

\*lapan- s.v. 'to lick up, lap' - OHG laffan s.v. 'id.', MHG laffen s.v. 'id.', G Swi. lajfen s.fw.v. 'id.' (DRV). A strong verb back-formed to the iterative \*lapp/bOn- ( q.v.). Also cf. Ice!. lepja w.v. 'to lap' < \*lapjan- and OHG leffil, G Loffel, MDu., Du. lepel 'spoon' < \*lapila-.

\*lapp/bon- w.v. 'to lick up, lap' - OSw. lapa w.v. 'id.', Elfd. ldpd w.v. 'id.', OE lapian w.v. 'id.', E to lap, EDu. labben, lappen w.v. 'id.', G Swi. labben w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*Iap-neh₂- (EUR) - Identical to Lat. lambo, -ere 'to lick', Lith. lapenti 'to drink greedily' (of pigs); also cf. Gr. A.amw 'id.' < \*Iap-ie-; Alb. lap 'to lick up water' < \*lap-; Ru. 16pat"to gobble up' < \*lap-ehr. An iterative verb 3sg. \*lappopi, 3pl. \*labunanpi ( < \*lap-neh₂-ti, \*lap-nh₂-entl) directly corresponding to Lat. lambO, which itself developed from \*lap-nehr by Thurneysen's law: \*lapno > \*labo- > Iambo. Lith. Iapenti appears to continue \*/ap-nehr as well, the suffix -en- being a remodeling of the zero-grade -in- < \*-nh₂-. See further \*Japan-.

\*lasiwa- adj. 'weak, feeble' - Go. lasiws adj. 'id.', OE Iysu, lesu adj. 'depraved, corrupt, evil' ⇒ \*losi-uo- (IE) - ToB leswi pl. 'attacks of weakness' < \*losi-u-; Ru. dial. /6Sij, SCr. los adj. 'poor, bad, evil' < \*los-io-. OE Iysu, Iesu has also been taken to continue a zero-grade variant \*lusiwa-, but it is unclear how this zero grade could have arisen. E lazy (continuing OE \*leasu?), despite its unclear derivation, also seems to point to \*Jas(wa)-. A potential zero-grade root \*/us- can possibly be retrieved, however, from MLG losich, lasich, EDu. leusigh adj. 'lax, slow' < \*lusfga-. Further

<!-- pdf-page: 368; section: dictionary -->

328 \*latacf. MHG er-leswen w.v. 'to become weak' < \*lasiwjan-, ON lasinn adj. 'ill' < \*lasina- and MLG Iasich adj. 'tired' < \*las/ga-.

\*lata- adj. 'lax, sluggish' - Go. lats adj. 'id.', ON latr adj. 'slow, lazy', Far. latur adj. 'lazy, idle', Elfd. lat adj. 'id.', OE fret adj. 'slow, late', E late, OFri. let adj. 'late', OS lat adj. 'lazy', Du. laat adj. 'late', OHG laz adj. 'lazy' ⇒ \*lh₁d-o(IE) - Oir. ladan adj. 'dumb' < \*lh₁d-ono-. Also cf. the factitive Go. latjan 'to make slow', ON letja 'to impede', OE lettan 'to delay', OFri. Jetta 'id.', OS lettian 'to impede', OHG lezzen 'id.' < \*latjan-. See \*letan-.

\*laf>ojan- w.v. 'to invite' - Go. laþon w.v. 'id.', OE laoian w.v. 'id.', OFri. lathia w.v. 'id.', OS lathian w.v. 'id.', OHG ladon w.v. 'id.', G laden w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂lot-eh₂-ie- (IE) - Hitt. balzai - balzijanzi 'to cry out, to shout, to call; to invoke, to recite', CLuv. balta/i- 'to call, appeal(?)' < \*h₂lt-(o)i-. A Germanic-Hittite isogloss. The Germanic verb is based on Go. laþo, ON /90 f. 'invitation' < \*lapo- < \*hi/ot-eh₂-.

\*lauba- m./n. 'leaf, foliage' - Go. laufn. 'id.', ON lauf n. 'id.', Far. leyv n. 'id.', Elfd. lov n. 'id.', OE leaf n. 'id.', E leaf, OFri. Iii/ n. 'id.', OS lofn. 'id.', Du. loofn. 'id.', OHG loub n. 'id.', G Laub n. 'id.' ⇒ \*loubh-o- (EUR) - Ru. lub, SCr. lib m. 'bast', Alb. labe 'rind, cork' < \*loubh-o-; Lith. Juba f. 'plank', OPru. lubbo 'bast, plank, shelf < \*lubh-eh₂-; Lat. liber m. 'bark, rind' < \*lubh-ro-. A European word with no clear PIE etymology. Also cf. \*lubja- and \*luftu-.

\*lauda- n. 'lead' - OE lead n. 'id.', E lead, OFri. liid n. 'id.', Du. lood n. 'id.' (LW). A loanword from Celt. \*cploud-io-, cf. Mir. luaide m. 'lead' < \*ploud-, which is connected to Lat. plumbum ( < \*plumdh-) and Gr. μ6A.uĢcSo<; (μ6A.LĢO<;, var. μ6A.uĢo<;, μ6A.LĢOo<;, Ģ6A.uĢooc;, Ģ6A.Lμo<;, Ģ6ALĢo<;). The same word through a different (Pre-Indo-European) route also served as the basis for \*blfwa- 2 'lead'. The idea that the Greek word was borrowed from Lydian mariwda-, allegedly 'the dark metal', cf. CLuv. maryai- adj. 'black, dark-colored(?)' (Melchert 2008) is ingenious, but fails to explain how Lydian r could have given Greek A.

\*lauda- m. 'man' - Go.jugga-laups m. 'young man' ⇒ \*h₁loudh-o- (IE). An o-grade formation to \*leudi- 'people' ( q.v.).

\*laugnjan- w.v. 'to keep hidden, deny' - Go. laugnjan w.v. 'to reject, deny, cover up', ON leyna w.v. 'to hide, conceal', Far. loyna w.v. 'id.', OE lfegnian w.v. 'to deny', OFri. leina, laina w.v. 'id.', OS IOgnian w.v. 'id.', Du. loochenen w.v. 'id.', OHG loug(a]nen w.v. 'id.', G liiugnen w.v. 'id.' (DRY).

<!-- pdf-page: 369; section: dictionary -->

\*Jauka- 329 Probably a factitive to the adjective \*laugna-, cf. Go. ana-laugns adj. 'hidden', and thus related to \*leugan- (q.v.). With the same derivational base, cf. ON faun f. 'secrecy', OHG lougna f. 'denial' < \*laugno-.

\*lauka- m./n. 'onion, leek' - ON laukr m. 'leek, onion, garlic', Far. leykur m. 'onion', Elfd. lok m. 'id.', OE leac m. 'leek, onion, garlic', E leek, MDu. looc n. 'garlic', Du. Flem. look 'id.', OHG louh n. 'leek', G Lauch m. 'id.' (DRY). A word without a certain etymology. It is widely assumed that it was derived from the strong verb \*leukan- \*liikan- 'to close' (q.v.) in view of the leaves or scales enclosing a stem or growing-point in both leeks and onions.

\*fauna- n. 'reward, recompense' - Go. faun n. 'id.', ON faun n.pl. 'id.', OSw. fen n.pl./f. 'id.', Da. /!Jn c. 'id.', OE lean n. 'id.', OFri. Ian n. 'id.', OS Ion n. 'id.', Du. loon n. 'id.', OHG Ion m./n. 'id.', G Lohn m. 'id.' ⇒ \*louh₂-no- (IE) - Gr. cino-A.auw 'to enjoy' < \*leh₂u-ie-; Gr. Af:i.a, Dor. A.al.a f. 'booty' < \*leh₂u-ieh₂- ( < \*A.af-i.O:); Lat. lucrum m. 'profit' < \*lh₂u-tlo- (with dissimilation of the second I). A no-stem to the PIE root \*leh₂u-. Since \*leh₂u-no- and \*loh₂u-no- would have given PGm. \*\*Iona- by the regular loss of u after o in open syllables, PGm. \*fauna- can only be reconstructed as \*lh₂eu-no- or \*louh₂-no- (with laryngeal metathesis). The word is unrelated to \*leusan- 'to lose' and \*lewan- 'scythe'.

\*lausa- adj. 'empty, idle' - Go. laus adj. 'empty, devoid', ON lauss adj. 'loose, free', Far. leysur adj. 'loose, free; idle; uncertain, vague', Elfd. los adj. 'free, loose', OE leas adj. 'loose, free; void of; vain, false, deceitful', OFri. las adj. 'free; lost; deceitful', OS los adj. 'loose, free', Du. loos adj. 'idle, stray', OHG los adj. 'lax, light-hearted, loose', G los adj. 'loose' (DRY). An o-grade adjective derived from \*leusan- 'to lose' ( q.v.). Also cf. the factitive Go. lausjan 'to free, recue; to collect (money)', ON leysa 'to free; to solve', OE liesan 'to loosen, free, deliver', OFri. lesa 'id.', OS losian 'id.', Du. lozen 'to ditch', OHG /osen 'to free', G !Osen 'id.' < \*/ausjan-.

\*laupra- n. 'lather' - ON lauor n. 'lather; froth, foam of the sea', OE leaoor n. 'lather', E lather, OS lothar n. 'detergent' ⇒ \*/h₃-eu-tro- (IE) - Identical to Gr. A.oucp6v n. 'bath'; also cf. Gr. A.ouw, -oμm 'to bathe, wash', Lat. lavo, -ii.re 'id.', Alb. /aj 'id.', Arm. loganam 'id.' < \*/h₃-eu-e-; also cf. Hitt. labui - labuanzi 'to pour, cast' < \*l6h₃-u-ei, \*/h₃-u-enti. An instrumental noun created to a thematicized u-stem verb \*/h₃-eu-e- 'to wash'. See also \*law[w)a-.

\*lawiz- n. 'ham, thigh' - ON la!r n. 'id.', Far. la!r n. 'id.', OSw. liir n. 'id.', Elfd. /cir n. 'id.', OE leow, pl. leower(a) n. 'id.' ⇒ \*/ogwh.es- (GM).

<!-- pdf-page: 370; section: dictionary -->

330 \*law(w)a- While OSw. liir and ON leer (with R-fronting) go back to \*lawaz-, OE leow presupposes a proto-form \*lawiz-. Both of these variants split off from a neuter s-stem \*lawaz, pl. \*lawizo. This formation may be related to \*lagja'leg' (q.v.).

\*law(w)a- n. 'oak bark, tan' - MDu. loo n. 'id.', Du. looi n. 'id.', OHG li5 n. 'id.', G Lohe f. 'id.' ⇒ \*/h₃-0U-O- (IE). Also cf. MDu. looien, louwen, Du. looien w.v. 'to tan'. Related to \*lauþra(q.v.).

\*lawwo- f. 'groove' - ON lpgg f. 'groove in the staves of a cask:, Far. !egg f. 'id.', OE lim-lci!w f. 'injury to the limb, mutilation', OHG lih-loa f. 'scar' ⇒ \*louH-eh₂- (IE). An o-stem to the root \*leuH- 'to cut off, for which cf. \*lewan- 'scythe'. The geminate \*-ww- points to original oxytony.

\*leg(j)an- s.v. 'to lie' - Go. ligan s.v. 'id.', ON liggja s.v. 'id.', Far. liggja s.v. 'id.', Elfd. ligga s.v. 'id.', OE licgan s.v. 'id.', E to lie, OFri. lidza s.v. 'id.', OS liggian s.v. 'id.', Du. liggen sv. 'id.', OHG lickan, ligan s.v. 'id.', G liegen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*fegh-ie- (IE) - Gr. Hsch. A.£xoμm 'to go to sleep', Olr. laigid 'to lay, lie down', OCS lesti (/figp), Ru. lee' (ljagu), SCr. !eCi 'to lie (down)' (the nasal present after e.g. OCS sesti (sfidp) 'to sit') < \*fegh-e-; Lat. lectus m. 'bed, couch' < \*legh-to-. See also \*legu- and \*lagjan-.

\*legu- adj. 'low' - ON l<igr adj. 'id.', Far. l<igur adj. 'id.', Elfd. l<i.g adj. 'id.', OFri. lech, leich adj. 'id.', WFri. leech adj. 'id.', MDu. /age adj. 'id.', Du. laag adj. 'id.', MHG lrege adj. 'id.' (DRV). A lengthened grade adjective derived from \*leg(j)an- (q.v.). In view of the ja-stem forms in West Germanic, the word must probably be reconstructed as a u-stem. ME liih, E /ow is assumed to be a Nordic loanword.

\*lehizan- m. 'fleshy part of the body, calf - OE lira m. 'muscle, soft parts of the body', E lire 'flesh, muscle, brawn', MDu. liere f. 'fleshy part of the leg, calf ⇒ \*fekfwJ.es-(?) (GM). An etymologically problematic word. The reconstruction \*ligizan- (thus Pokorny IEW: 673) must be abandoned in view of MDu. liere. The material, in fact, straightforwardly points to a proto-form \*liran-. The alternative, \*lehizan-, can theoretically be connected to PGm. \*lawiz- 'ham, thigh', but only if this formation developed from \*lokw-es- by Verner's law, which is only one of many possibilities.

<!-- pdf-page: 371; section: dictionary -->

\*lekan- 331

\*lekan- s.v. 'to leak, drip' - ON leka s.v. 'id.', Far. leka s.v. 'id.', E to leak, MDu. leken s./w.v. 'id.', OHG lehhan s.v. 'id.', MHG lechen s./w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*/eg-e(EUR) - Arm. lie 'lake' < \*leg-ieh₂-; MW dilein 'to destroy' < \*-leg-e-; Olr. legaid 'to melt, perish' < \*leg-eh₂-ie-; W llaith adj. 'wet' < \*leg-to-. A strong verb to a marginally attested root \*leg-. See also \*lakjan- 2 and \*loki-. Arm. lie is formally and semantically close to Far. lrekja f. 'well, puddle' < \*lekjon- (q.v.).

\*lekinon- w.v. 'to heal' - Go. lekinon w.v. 'id.', ON lrekna w.v. 'id.', OE lcecnian w.v. 'id.', ODu. liiknon w.v. 'id.', OHG liichinon w.v. 'id.', MHG liichenen w.v. 'id.' (DRY). A verb derived from \*/ekja- 'doctor; leech' (q.v.). It served as the derivational base for ON, Icel. lreknir, Far. lrekni m. 'doctor' < \*lekinja-.

\*lekja- m. 'doctor; leech' - Go. lekeis m. 'doctor', ON lrekir m. 'id.', Da. lrege c. 'id.', OE lci!ce m. 'doctor; leech', E leech, OFri. letza m. 'doctor', ODu. lake m. 'leech', MDu. laecke 'id.', OHG liihhi, liichi m. 'id.' (Lw?). A word that is usually assumed to have been borrowed from Celtic \*legio-, cf. Olr. liaig 'doctor', before the great sound shifts. If the original meaning was 'blood-letter' ('leech'), however, the word can definitely be linked to the cluster of \*lekan- 'to leak' (q.v.), in particular to the formally close \*lekjon- 'rivulet' (q.v.). This would rather imply a Germanic origin. Also cf. Nw. lrekjar, Sw. liikare m. 'doctor' < \*lekjarjan- (= OCS /ekaro m. 'id.'), which was derived from a factitive \*lekjan-, cf. Nw. lrekja, Elfd. liitja w.v. 'to heal' ( = OCS leCiti 'id.'). See further \*lekinon-.

\*Iekjon- f. 'rivulet(?)' - Far. lrekja f. 'well, waterhole, waterspout', Nw. lrekje f. 'rivulet, wooden water-pipe' ⇒ \*leg-ieh₂- (DRY) - Close to Arm. lie 'lake' < \*leg-ieh₂-. Within Germanic, this formation seems to have been derived from \*lekan( q.v.). It nevertheless displays a remarkable similarity with Armenian lie. See also \*lekja- 'healer'.

\*lepjan- m. 'lip' - OE lippa m. 'id.', E lip, OFri. lippa m. 'id.', MDu. lippe f. 'id.', Du. lip c. 'id.' ⇒ \*leb-ion- (EUR) - Close to Gr. A.oĢ6<; [m.] 'lobe, lap, slip' < \*lob-o- and Lat. labium n. 'lip' < \*lob-io- (with a < \*o as in lacus 'lake' < \*lok-u-). Within Germanic, also cf. ODu. lepora pl., OHG leffur m. 'lip' < \*lepaza-. See also \*werila/o-.

\*lesan- s.v. 'to pick, select' - Go. lisan s.v. 'to collect', ON lesa s.v. 'to grasp, pick up, to gather; to knit', OE lesan s.v. 'to gather, collect', E to lease ('to glean'), OS lesan s.Y. 'to gather', Du. lezen s.Y. 'to read; to select', OHG lesan

<!-- pdf-page: 372; section: dictionary -->

332 \*letans.v. 'to read, examine; to collect', G lesen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*Jes- (IE) - Identical to Hitt. leszi - lisfonzi 'to pick, to gather'; Lith. /esti (lesu) 'to pick (of birds)', Latv. /est 'to pick; to count'. An old athematic verb 3sg. \*Jes-ti, 3pl. \*ls-enti. The original meaning 'to pick, select' developed into 'to read' through the intermediate sense 'to examine, analyze', not through 'to pick letters', as is often assumed.

\*letan- s.v. 'to let, allow' - Go. letan s.v. 'id.', ON lcita s.v. 'id.', Far. lcita s.v. 'id.', Elfd. Mta w.v. 'id.', OE lii!tan s.v. 'id.', E to let, OFri. feta s.v. 'id.', OS Iatan s.v. 'id.', Du. laten s.v. 'id.', OHG lazan s.v. 'id.', G /assen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*Jeh₁d-e(EUR) - Gr. ATJOE°lv 'to be tired', Alb. /odh 'to tire, fatigue, harass' < \*leh₁d-e-; Lat. lassus adj. 'tired, weary' < \*lh₁d-to-. A reduplicated strong verb, cf. Go. /ailot, ON /6t, Jet etc. See also \*lata-.

\*le/Jra- n. 'leather' - ON lerir n. 'id.', Far. leaur n. 'id.', Elfd. leoer n. 'id.', OE leoer n. 'id.', E leather, OFri. !ether n. 'id.', MDu. /eder n. 'id.', Du. leer n. 'id.', OHG ledar n. 'id.', G Leder n. 'id.' (LW). A Celtic loanword, cf. Mir. lethar, MW l/edr m. 'leather' < PCelt. \*rplitro- < PIE \*pl-tro-. See also \*fella-.

\*leuba- adj. 'dear, beloved' - Go. liufs adj. 'id.', ON ljufr adj. 'id.', OE /eof adj. 'id.', OS liof adj. 'id.', Du. lief adj. 'id.', OHG liub, liob adj. 'id.', G lieb adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*leubh·o- (IE) - Formally identical to OCS ljub'b adj. 'sweet, pleasant', Ru. ljub6j 'any, either', SCr. ljub adj. 'dear, beloved'; also cf. Alb. lume adj. 'blessed' (if from \*lubh-no-). A European adjective. Related to \*Juba- (q.v.).

\*leudan- s.v. 'to grow' - Go. liudan s.v. 'id.', OE leodan s.v. 'id.', OS liodan s.v. 'id.', OHG liotan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁Ieudh·e- (IE) - ToA lii.t-, lii.nt-, ToB lii.t-, lii.nn- 'to go out' < \*h₁lu-n-dh-; Skt. r6dhati 'to grow', YAv. raoSaryti 3pl.pres. 'id.' < \*h₁Ieudh-e-; Gr. ùA.ui9ov aor. 'went, came', Olr. luid pret. 'went' < \*h₁/udh-. A present created to an originally aoristic root \*h₁ludh- 'to come out'. Also cf. the iterative MDu. loden w.v. 'to sprout' < \*Judon-. See also \*leudi-, \*laudaand \*/udja/o-.

\*leudi- m. 'man; people' - ON ljor m. 'people, men', Far. undir-ljour m. 'weak-willed person', OFri. liud n. 'people', OS liud m. 'people', OHG liut m./n. 'people' ⇒ \*h₁/eudh·i- (IE) - Lith. liaudis, Latv. jaudis pl. 'people' < \*h₁leudh-i-; Gr. £A£ui9£po<; adj. 'free', Lat. fiber adj. 'id.' < \*h₁leudh-ero-. A Baltic-Germanic i-stem, likely to have been derived from the verb \*leudan- 'to grow'. From a social perspective, the \*leudi- probably referred to the unbound 'common people' in view of the link with Gr. tA.£Ui9£po<; and

<!-- pdf-page: 373; section: dictionary -->

\*leugan- 333 Lat lfber 'free'. A plain a-stem \*leuda- is attested as ON lj6iJr m. 'people', OE /eod m. 'man'.

\*Ieugan- s.v. 'to lie' - Go. liugan s.v. 'id.', ON ljuga s.v. 'id.', Far. Iugva s.v. 'id.', Elfd. liuoga s.v. 'id.', OE Ieogan s.v. 'id.', E to lie, OFri. liii.ga s.v. 'id.', OS liogan s.v. 'id.', Du. liegen s.v. 'id.', OHG liugan, liogan s.v. 'id.', G Iiigen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*Ieugh-e- (EUR) - OCS l'bgati (/'bip) 'to lie', Olr. fol-lugaid 'to hide' < \*lugh-eh₂-. A strong verb to a European root \*leugh-. See also \*laugnjan-, \*lugino-, \*lugi- and \*luk[k)on-.

\*leugo- f. 'marriage' - Go. liuga f. 'id.' (WEUR) - Olr. luige n. 'oath' < \*lugh-io-. Also cf. Go. Iiugan w.v. 'to marry' < \*leugen- and OFri. logia, Ioia w.v. 'to arrange, allot; to join; to vouch; to marry' < \*lugen-. In view of Olr. luige, the original meaning of the root \*/ugh- should probably be reconstructed as 'to bind by oath'. It belongs to a wider range of Germanic-Celtic legal isoglosses (cf. \*aipa- 'oath'), which together are a reflection of a shared cultural sphere.

\*leuhada- n. 'light' - Go. liuhap f. 'id.', OE leoht n. 'id.', E light, OFri. liii.cht n. 'id.', WFri. ljocht n. 'id.', OS lioht n. 'id.', Du. licht n. 'id.', OHG lioht n. 'id.', G Licht n. 'id.' ⇒ \*leuk-ot- (IE) - Hitt lukkatt- c. 'dawn, next morning' < \*luk-ot-. An old t-stem to the root \*leuk- 'to shine, be bright', for which cf. \*leuhna-, \*leuhsa-, \*leuskan- and \*leuskan-. The formati_on is semantically and derivationally parallel to \*naht- 'night' (q.v.), which also continues a PIE t-stem.

\*leuhman- m. 'beam of light' - ON lj6mi m. 'flash of light, radiance', Far. lj6mi m. 'light, glare, brilliance', OE leoma m. '(beam of) light, radiance', OS liomo m. 'shine' ⇒ \*Ieuk-mon- (IE) - Lat lilmen n. 'light' < \*leuk[s)-men-. An mn-stem related to \*leuhada- 'light' (q.v.).

\*leuhna- n. 'lightning' - Nw. ljon, lyn n. 'id.', Sw. ljung-eld 'id.', Gutn. Iiaugn n. 'id.', Da. lyn n. 'id.' ⇒ \*Ieuk-no- (IE). A formation closely related to Go. lauhmuni f. 'flash' < \*I[a)uhmunjo- and ME /even 'lightning, flash, flame' < \*l(a)uhumna-. The West Norse forms directly point to a proto-form \*leuhna-, but Sw. ljung-eld and Gutn. liaugn developed from OEN \*ljiifn by regular dissimilation of -ufn- to -ugn-, cf. Sw. ugn, Elfd. ungen m. 'oven' < PGm. \*ufna-. The cluster -ufn- must itself have arisen from PGm. \*-uhn-. Also cf. the derived jon-stem OSw. Iyghna f. 'lightning'.

<!-- pdf-page: 374; section: dictionary -->

334 \*leuhsa-

\*leuhsa- adj. 'light, bright' - ON /joss adj. 'id.', Far. lj6sur adj. 'id.', Elfd. liuos adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*leuk-so- (IE) - Skt. r6cas-, Av. raocah- n. 'light' < \*leuk-es-. An adjective close to ON lj6s n. 'light' < \*/euhsa-, which is no doubt a thematicization of the PIE s-stem \*leuk-es-. It gave rise to the factitive ON Iysa, OE /lexan w.v. 'to shine' < \*leuhsjan-. See also \*leuhman-, \*leuhna- and \*leuskan-.

\*leukan- - \*IUkan- s.v. 'to close; to pull' - Go. ga-/ukan s.v. 'to close', us-lukan s.v. 'to pull (a sword)', ON Iuka, ljuka s.v. 'to close, finish', Far. Iuka s.v. 'to close; to weed, clean out', OE liican s.v. 'to plait', OFri. liika s.v. 'to close; to join; to pull', MDu. luken s.v. 'to weed; to pull; to draw a sword or knife; to stab(?)', Du. luiken s.v. 'to close', OHG liohhan s.v. 'to pull, pluck', liihhan s.v. 'to close', MHG liechen, liichen s.v. 'to close; to pull; to shirk, crouch; to slip' ⇒ \*Jeurgl-e- (IE). A verb with a complex semantic field. It is likely that two entirely different verbs merged in view of the formal distinction between OHG /iohhan 'to pull' and OHG /iihhan 'to close'. The former can be compared to Gr. Auyoٷ 'flexible branch, twig suited for twisting' < \*lug-a- and Lith. lugnas adj. 'flexible' < \*lug-no-, by assuming that the original meaning 'to bend', cf. ON lykna w.v. 'to bend the knees', developed into 'to jerk' and 'to pull out' (cf. \*lukka'tuft'). The verb 'to close', on the other hand, is semantically closer to Skt. ruj<iti 'to break', Lith. 16zti 'id.' < \*Jurgl-.

\*leusan- s.v. 'to lose' - Go. fra-liusan s.v. 'id.', OE for-/eosan s.v. 'id.', OFri. for-liasa, for-liesa s.v. 'id.', OS far-/iosan s.v. 'id.', Du. ver-liezen s.v. 'id.', G ver-lieren s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*Ieus-e- (IE) - Hitt. /iiri- c./n. 'loss of honor, disgrace, humiliation; financial loss' < \*luh₁-ri-; ToAB lawa- 'to send' < \*leuh₁-; Skt. /undti 'to cut (oft)' < \*/u-n-eh₁-; Gr. Mw 'to loosen, liberate, make loose, destroy, pay', Lat. /uo, -ere 'to suffer, make amends for', Olr. as-lof 'to escape' < \*eks-luw-e- < \*/uhre-. A strong verb based on an extension of the PIE root \*leuhr 'to loosen'. See also \*lausa-.

\*leuskan- m. 'groin' - Ice!. lj6ski m. 'id.', OSw. liuske m. 'id.', Sw. ljumske c. 'id.', Elfd. liuoska f. 'id.', E Jeske 'flank', NFri. Hall. jaoska pl. 'id.', MLG lesche, /eische 'genitalia', MDu. liesche f. 'groin; membrane', Du. lies c. 'groin' (DRV). A word of disputed origin. Some have compared \*lawiz- (cf. De Vries 1971: 400), but in view of the formally identical Far. lj6ski m. 'light, glimmer, gleam', it is more likely that the word was derived from \*leuhsa- adj. 'light' (with the kan-suffix for body parts). The original meaning may then have been 'transparent or light skin'. MLG liese 'thin skin' is probably the same word (Teuchert 1972: 330), cf. Brandenburg /iese f. 'layer of fat around the kidneys'.

<!-- pdf-page: 375; section: dictionary -->

\*leuta- 335

\*leuta- adj. 'ugly, bad' - Go. liuts adj. 'deceitful, fraudulent', ON lj6tr adj. 'ugly', Far. lj6tur adj. 'ugly; bad', Elfd. liuot adj. 'bad, mean' (NEUR) - Cf. CS /ud'b m. 'foolish', Ru. lud adj. 'stupid, mad, crazy', SCr. lild 'stupid, crazy, immature' < \*loud-o- and Lith. liudnas m. 'sad' (with a secondary zero grade). A Germanic/Balto-Slavic isogloss. Also cf. ON fYti n. 'fault, blemish; disgrace' < \*leutja- and OE lot n. 'deceit, fraud' < \*luta-.

\*lewan- m. 'scythe' - ON le, obi. lja m. 'id.', Far. lfggi m. 'id.', Nw. lja m. 'id.', sw. lie c. 'id.', Elfd. lja m. 'id.' ⇒ \*Ieuh₁-on- (IE) - Skt lun&ti 'to cut (off)' < \*lu-n-eH- (whence e.g. lavftra- n. 'sickle' < \*/euH-tro-); unrelated to Gr. A.aTov acc. 'ploughshare(?)'. An n-stem created to a root \*leuh₁- 'to cut off, which is probably related to \*leusan- 'to lose' ( q.v.). The form is of importance to Germanic historical phonology, since it proves that laryngeals did not necessarily geminate preceding glides (Holtzmann's law). See also \*lawwo-.

\*lewjan- w.v. 'to deliver, betray' - Go. lewjan w.v. 'id.', OE /æwan w.v. 'id.', ME be-lewen w.v. 'id.', OHG gi-liien, fir-Iii.en w.v. 'id.' (IE) - Close to Lith. liauti (lic1una, liauja) 'to stop', Latv. ,laiit 'to allow', refl. 'to stop, rely on', OPru. au-/aiit 'to die', Cz. leviti 'to reduce, give up, release', SCr. Jeviti 'to waste time, loaf. A factitive verb created to Go. lew n. 'occasion' < \*lewa- < \*Ieh₁u-o-. The original meaning was 'to let (go)', whence Germanic 'to give away, deliver, betray'. See also \*loma- 'betrayal'.

\*liba- n. 'life; body' - ON lif n. 'id.', Far. lfv n. 'id.', Elfd. laiv n. 'life', OE lifn. 'id.', E life, OFri. lifn. 'body; womb', OS lif n. 'life', Du. /ijfn. 'body', OHG lib m./n. 'body; life', G Leib m. 'body' (DRY). A neuter derived from the strong verb \*liban- ( q.v.).

\*liban- w.v. 'to stay' - Go. bi-leiban s.v. 'to stay', OE be-lifan s.v. 'to stay, remain', OFri. bi-liva s.v. 'id.', OS bi-I/ban s.v. 'id.', Du. blijven s.v. 'id.', OHG bi-liban s.v. 'to stay, stop', G bleiben s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*Ieip-e- (IE) - ToAB lipii- 'to be left (over)' < \*Ieip-e-; Gr. A.map6<; adj. 'fat, gleaming (of oil or unguent), fruitful' < \*lip-r-; Skt limpati 'to smear, adhere to, deceive', Lith. lipti (limpu, lempu, limpstU), Latv. lipt 'to stick' < \*li-n-p-e-; OCS pri-lbnpti, Ru. (pri-)l'nut', SCr. pri-onuti 'to stick' < \*lip-ne-. A strong verb to the PIE root \*leip-. The origin of the Germanic root-final \*b (for expected \*f) is obscure, but it is possible that the strong verb was derived from a more primary stative \*liben- 'to live' (q.v.). See also \*laibjanand \*laibo- as well as \*aina/ifa- and \*twa/ifa-.

<!-- pdf-page: 376; section: dictionary -->

336 \*liben-

\*liben- w.v. 'to be left; to live' - Go. liban w.v. 'to live', ON lifa w.v. 'to live; to be left', Far. liva w.v. 'to live', Elfd. livci w.v. 'id.', OE libban w.v. 'id.', E to live, OFri. libba w.v. 'id.', OS libbian w.v. 'id.', Du. /even w.v. 'id.', OHG leben w.v. 'id.', G leben w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*lip-eh₁-ie- (IE). The stative to \*liban- 'to stick' (q.v.). The meaning 'to live' developed from 'to remain'.

\*libro- f. 'liver' - ON lifr f. 'id.', Far. livur f. 'id.', OSw. liver, /yr f. 'id.', Elfd. /yr f. 'id.', OE lifer f. 'id.', E liver, OFri. livere f. 'id.', Du. lever c. 'id.', OHG libara, lebara, lebera f. 'id.', G Leber f. 'id.' ⇒ \*iekw-rjn- (IE) - Skt.yak[t, gen.yakncis n. 'liver', YAv. yakara n. 'id.', NP jigar, Oss. igcer 'id.', Gr. žnap, -aw<; n. 'id.', Lat. iecur, gen. iocinoris, iecoris n. 'id.', Lith. dial. jeknos f.pl. 'id.', Latv. aknas pl.f. 'id.', OPru. lagno (for \*iagno?) < \*iekw-r/n-; also cf. Mir. i(u)chair f. 'fishroe', Lith. ikras m. 'id.', Latv. ikri m.pl. 'id.', OPru.yccroy 'calf (of the leg)' < \*ikW-r-. Possibly an irregular continuation of PIE \*iekw-r/n- 'liver'. The Germanic word seems to continue an intermediate form \*lipr-eh₂with unetymological initial /- and the sporadic change of \*kw to \*p in the vicinity of resonants (cf. \*fimfe 'five' < \*penkwe). The variant OSw. /yr, Elfd. /yr has umlaut, which is difficult to account for but fully parallel to that of e.g. ON nyra 'kidney' (vs. OSw. niura < \*neura/on-). Perhaps, the umlaut was leveled from an old locative form PN \*leurini < \*libur-eni.

\*lihwan- s.v. 'to loan' - Go. leih.Jan s.v. 'id.', ON lja w.v. 'to lend; to give, grant', Far. liggja s.v. 'to borrow, take out', OE /eon s.v. 'to lend', OS far-lihan s.v. 'id.', MDu. lien s.v. 'id.', OHG lihan s.v. 'id.', G leihen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*/eikw-e- (IE) - Formally identical to Gr. A.drrw 'to leave (behind)', med. 'to depart', Oss. liJ.Yn / le3un 'to run away; abandon' < \*/eikw-e-; also cf. Skt. rii:icikti 'to leave, release, set free', YAv. irinaxti 'id.' < \*li-n-ekw-; Arm. lk'anem 'to leave, let go, abandon'; Lat. linquo, -ere 'to leave', Olr. /eicid 'to leave, let, allow' < \*li-n-kw-; Lith. likti (lieku) 'to hold, keep', Latv. likt 'to leave, lay, put', OPru. po-/aikt 'to stay' < \*loik-; OCS ot:o-lek'b m. 'remainder, rest', Lith. c'it-laikas m. 'remainder, remnant' < \*-loikw-o-. A thematic present created to a probably originally aoristic root \*fikw-. See also \*laihna- 'loan'.

\*lika- 1 n. '(dead) body' - Go. leik n. 'id.', ON lik n. 'id.', Far. lik n. 'id.', Elfd. laik n. 'id.', OE lie n. 'id.', OFri. lfk n. 'id.', OS lfk n. 'id.', Du. lijk n. 'id.', OHG lih n./f. 'id.', G Leiche f. 'id.' ⇒ \*leig-o- (DRY). A word originally meaning 'likeness, shape'. Derived from \*lfka- 2.

\*lika- 2 adj. 'similar, equal' - Go. ga-leiks adj. 'id.', ON glikr, likr adj. 'id.', Far. likur adj. 'id.', Elfd. laik adj. 'id.', OE ge-lfc adj. 'id.', E like, OFri. ge-lfk adj.

<!-- pdf-page: 377; section: dictionary -->

\*Ilka- 3 337 'id.', OS gi-lik adj. 'id.', Du. ge-lijk adj. 'id.', OHG ga-lfh adj. 'id.', G gleich adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*leig-o- (NEUR) - Lith. ljgus, lygus, l.atv. lidzs adj. 'flat, even, equal', OPru. po-ligu adv. 'similarly' < \*lig-o-; Lith. ljginti, Latv. lfdzinc'it 'to compare, even, equalize', OPru. liginton 'to judge' < \*lig-neh₂-. A Baltic-Germanic isogloss. The original meaning was 'equal, even', cf. OHG lfchon, EDu. lijcken w.v. 'to smoothen' < \*lfkojan- and EDu. lick adj. 'plane, even' < \*likka- < \*lig-n6-, whence EDu. licken w.v. 'to smoothen', Du. ge-likt adj. 'slick' < \*likkojan-. The latter formations, unlike the other Baltic and Germanic attestations, prove that the underlying root was \*Jig- rather than \*IiHg-. Also cf. the stative verb Go. (ga-)leikan, ON lfka, OE lfcian, OS /ikon, OHG lfchen w.v. 'to like' < \*liken-, whose meaning developed in an impersonal construction, cf. ON mer lfkar 'I like' < "it suits me". Related to \*lfka- 1.

\*lika- 3 n. 'leech-line, bolt-rope' - ON lfk n. 'id.', E leech, MLG lfk n. 'id.', Du. Iijk n. 'id.', MHG ge-leich n. 'joint, link' ⇒ \*leifgl-o- (EUR) - lat. ligo, -are 'fasten, bind' < \*lig-eh₂-ie-; Alb. Iidh 'to bind, tie' < \*Jig-; BRu. po-lyhaty-s'a 'to connect' < \*-lig-eh₂-. A formation derived from a European base \*leig- or \*leig-. Albanian points to a palatovelar, but BRu. po-lyhaty-s'a has a plain velar. I wonder whether the root can be related to \*lika- 1 and \*lika- 2 (q.v.), e.g. through a semantic shift 'to connect' > 'to compare' > 'to be (a)like'. Alb. lice f. 'flax tow', if a Slavic loanword, implies that the Germanic word was borrowed into Proto-Slavic as \*lice.

\*likkon- 1 w.v. 'to lick' - OE liccian w.v. 'id.', E to lick, OS likkon w.v. 'id.', Du. likken w.v. 'id.', OHG leckon w.v. 'id.', G lecken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*Jigh-neh₂- (IE) - Identical to Lat. lingo, -ere 'id.' < \*Jigh-neh₂- (with Thurneysen's law); further cf. Skt. re<;lhi 'to lick' < \*leigh-; Olr. ligid 'id.', W llyu 'id.' < \*Jigh-e; Av. raerizaite 3sg.int.med. 'id.' < \*lei-/igh-e-; Gr. AELXW 'id.', Arm. lizem, lizanem 'id.', Ru. Iizcit' (Iiiu) 'id.', SCr. lfzati 'id.', Lith. Iieiti (lieziu) 'id.' < \*Ieigh-e-. An iterative verb closely related to the o-grade variant Go. bi-laigon with the same meaning. The formation continues an old neh₂-present (cf. Lat lingua) derived from a primary aorist. See also \*likkra-.

\*likkon- 2 w.v. 'to jump' - MHG lecken w.v. 'to kick, jump' ⇒ \*lig-neh₂- (IE). An old iterative verb. MHG lecken can be from either \*lekkon- or \*likkon-, but the latter option is preferable in view of the evident link with leichen s.v. 'to jump, play' < \*laikan- ( q.v.). It has been argued that MHG vro-locken, G froh-locken w.v. 'to rejoice, exult' belongs here, too, representing a distortion of \*froh-Iecken (e.g. Drosdowski 1989: 207). It may alternatively be compared to Du. obs. vrolijken 'to become happy' and E to frolic.

<!-- pdf-page: 378; section: dictionary -->

338 \*likkra-

\*likkra- w.v. 'delicious' - MDu. /ecker adj. 'id.', Du. /ekker adj. 'id.', MHG lecker adj. 'id.', G /ecker adj. 'id.' (DRY). A ra-stem derived from the iterative \*likkon- 1 (q.v.): the original meaning seems to have been "lickable".

\*lima- m.jn. 'clay, mud, lime' - ON lfm n. 'lime', Far. lfm n. 'glue', OE lim m. 'lime', E lime, OS lim m. 'glue', Du. lijm c. 'id.', OHG /im m. 'clay; lime', G Leim m. 'glue; paste' Ǽ \*h₂leihi-mo- (IE) - Lat. limus m. 'mud, slime' < \*h₂leih₁-mo-. _ An mn-stem to the root \*h₂leihr 'to smear', for which cf. \*laiman-.

\*limu- m. 'limb' - ON limr m. 'limb; joint', Far. limur m. 'id.', E limb Ǽ

\*lei-mon-(?) (NEUR) - Lith. fiemuo m. 'log, trunk, body' < \*/ei-mon-. A Nordic u-stem. In view of the closely related ON lim n. 'twig', OE lim n. 'limb; twig' and the ablauting ON /imi, Elfd. laime m. 'broom' < \*liman-, it is possible that the u-stem split off from the dat.pl. \*!imummiz < \*li-mr.;i-mis of a primary mn-stem \*liman- (cf. Kroonen 2011a: 62). See also \*lipu-.

\*lindo- f. 'lime tree' - ON find f. 'id.', Far. find f. 'id.', Elfd. find f. 'id.', OE find f. 'id.', E lime, OS /inda f. 'id.', Du. /inde c. 'id.', OHG linta f. 'id.', G Linde f. 'id.' Ǽ

\*lent-eh₂- (NEUR) - Ru. dial. fut m. 'bark (of a limetree)' < \*lont-o-; Ru. lut'e n. 'young lime trees to be stripped of their bark', Ukr. fut'e n. 'bark of a lime tree, twigs of a willow' < \*lont-io-; Lith. lentil, Latv. /՚fita f. 'board, plank' < \*lent-eh₂-. A European tree name. Unrelated to \*finpa- 'mild, soft'.

\*lingwa- n. 'heather' - ON lyng n. 'id.', Far. lyngur m. 'id.', OSw. liung n. 'id.', Sw. ljung n. 'id.', Elfd. /ingg n. 'id.' Ǽ \*lengwh.o- (NEUR) - OCS lpg'b m. 'meadow, underbrush', Ru. lug m. 'meadow' < \*longwh.o-. A Nordic word related to the aforementioned forms in Slavic (Charpentier 1906: 222). The meaning 'heather' probably developed from 'unused land, moor' (cf. \*haipi-). The alternative connection with Lith. /efikti 'to bend' and OCS lpk'b adj. 'bent' (Torp 1909: 360) is semantically unattractive. For a potential zero-grade, cf. Sw. dial. /ung-b/Ot, Nw. dial. lunge-blaut adj. 'soaked' < quasi-PGm. \*lungwa-blauta-.

\*lingwan- s.v. 'to succeed, make progress' - OHG gi-lingan s.v. 'id.', MHG lingen, ge-lingen s.v. 'id.', G ge-lingen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁lengwh-e- (IE) - Skt. laghu-. raghu- adj. 'quick, light, small', YAv. rauui- adj.(f.) 'agile, fast, quick', Gr. E:A.axuc; adj. 'small' < \*h₁lngwh.u-; Lith. lengvas adj. 'easy, light' < \*h₁lengwh.o-; Lat. /evis adj. 'light, small, quick', OCS lbg'b-k'b adj. 'light' < \*h₁legwh.u-; Olr. laigiu comp. 'smaller, worse' < \*h₁legwh.ios- (with -aig- < \*-eig-).

<!-- pdf-page: 379; section: dictionary -->

\*linhta- 339 A strong verb continuing an old nasal present to the root \*h₁/egwh_. See also \*linhta- and \*lungra-.

\*linhta- adj. 'light' - Go. /eihts adj. 'id.', ON Iettr adj. 'light, easy; nimble, active', Far. /CEttur adj. 'light, easy', Elfd. litt adj. 'id.', OE liht, leoht adj. 'id.', E light, OFri. licht adj. 'id.', Du. licht adj. 'id.', OHG liht adj. 'easy', G /eicht adj. 'id.' (DRV). A to-stem adjective derived from \*lingwan- (q.v.).

\*linPa- adj. 'flexible, soft' - ON linnr adj. 'mild, soft', Far. linur adj. 'soft, slack, limp', Elfd. fin adj. 'soft', OE lio, /ioe adj. 'id.', E lithe, OS /ithi adj. 'id.', OHG find, lindi adj. 'mild, tender', G ge-linde adj. 'delicate, soft' ⇒ \*Ien-to(EUR) - Identical to Lat. /entus adj. 'pliant, flexible; tough; sticky; slow' and Lith. dial. lefitas adj. 'quiet, calm'. Also cf. the inchoative Go. af-linnan 'to abate, go away', ON linna 'to desist from', OE linnan 'to cease', OHG bi-linnan 'to give away' < \*linnon- < \*lin/mon-.

\*lisan- pret-pres. 'to know' - Go. lisan\*, 3sg. /ais 'id.' (EUR). A marginally attested preterite-present, 3sg. \*/aise, 3pl. \*lisunþ, to a root \*leis-, cf. Go. lubja-leis adj. 'having knowledge about drugs' < \*-lisa-. The meaning of the verb must in view of the obviously related \*laisti- 'track; model' (q.v.) have shifted from an aspectually perfective 'to have traced'. See also \*/aizjan-, \*liznon- and \*listi-.

\*listi- f. 'skill' - Go. list f. 'cunning', ON list f. 'art, craft', Far. list f. 'art; cunning', OE list f. 'art, skill; trick', OFri. list f. 'knowledge; deceit', OS list f. 'craftiness, cleverness', Du. list c. 'trick', OHG list m./f. 'knowledge', G List f. 'cunning' ⇒ \*/is-ti- (DRV). A ti-stem to the preterite-present \*lisan- (q.v.).

\*litila- adj. 'small' - Go. leitils adj. 'id.', ON liti/l adj. 'id.', Far. lftil adj. 'id.', Elfd. litn, /isl- adj. 'id.', OE ljtel, lite/, Nrth. lyttil adj. 'id.', E little, OS luttil adj. 'id.', Du. lutte/ adj. 'few', OHG liizil, luzzil adj. 'id.', MHG liitzel adj. 'id.' (GM). The default Germanic word for 'small'. The derivation of the word is still problematic. The suffix \*-ila- is paralleled by the antonym \*mekila- 'big, much' (q.v.). Equally parallel is the lack of this suffix in the closely related OE lyt, OS fut, MHG liiz adj. 'few' < \*liitja- - \*luttja- and the antonym ME mich(e), much(e}, E much < \*mekja-. The radical alternation of \*/ft-, \*liit- and \*lutt- remains unexplained, however. Until this alternation has been resolved, it will be difficult to find a good etymology. The comparisons with \*/Utan- 'to stoop' and Olr. lutu 'little finger' (cf. Elfd. sar-ludd 'finger stall'?) are futile, for instance, because they fail to account for the root variant \*lit-.

<!-- pdf-page: 380; section: dictionary -->

340 \*lipan-

\*liþan- s.v. 'to go (by), pass' - Go. ga-/eipan s.v. 'to go, travel', ON lfoa s.v. 'to go, pass by; to pass away, to elapse', Far. /foa s.v. 'to move smoothly, stride; to pass (away), elapse, proceed', Elfd. /aioa s.v. 'to go by, pass', OE liOan s.v. 'to go, sail', OFri. litha s.v. 'to go', OS /fthan s.v. 'to go, leave', Du. lijden s.v. 'to suffer', OHG lidan s.v. 'to go, pass; to tolerate, suffer', G leiden s.v. 'to suffer' Ǽ \*/eit-e- (IE) - ToAB litii- 'to pass on' < \*/eit-e-; YAv. irii9iieiti 'to die, pass away' < \*lit-ie-; Gr. AOLTI'J f. 'tomb' < \*loit-eh₂-; Lat. /ftus, -oris n. 'sea-shore, coast' < \*leit-es-. A strong verb to the PIE root \*leit- 'to go (down)'. See also \*laidjan-.

\*liþu- m. 'member, body part' - Go. lipus m. 'id.', ON lirJr m. 'joint', Far. liour m. 'joint; knot', Elfd. lio m. 'id.', OE lio m./n. 'joint', OFri. lith n. 'joint, articulation', OS /ith m. 'id.', Du. lid n. 'id.', OHG lid m.fn. 'id.', G Glied n. 'id.' (NEUR). A derivation created to the same Baltic-Germanic root \*lei- as found in \*/imu- (q.v.). Also cf. ON liougr adj. 'smooth, lithe', OFri. /ethoch adj. 'free', OE lioig adj. 'flexible', MHG /edic adj. 'free', G ledig, Du. /eeg adj. 'empty'.

\*liþu- n. 'strong drink, cider' - Go. /eipu n. 'id.', ON lio n. 'id.', OE /io n. 'id.', OFri. lfth n. 'id.', OS lith n. 'id.', OHG lid m./n. 'id.' ⇒ \*l[e)h₃i-tu- (IE) - Lith. ljti (lyju) 'to rain', Latv. /ft 'to flow, rain' (also cf. Lith. dial. /yti.is, Latv. /fts m. 'rain' < \*/h₃i-tu-), OCS -liti, Ru. lit' (þū), SCr. /l'ti 'to pour' < \*/eh₃i-. A tu-stem created to the root \*/eh₃i-, which itself appears to have split off from a PIE i-present 3sg. \*/h₃-ei-ti, 3pl. \*/h₃-i-enti, cf. Hitt. labui - labuanzi 'to pour, cast' < \*/6h₃-u-ei, \*/h₃-u-enti.

\*liznon- w.v. 'to learn' - OE leornian w.v. 'id.', E to learn, OFri. lirna, lerna w.v. 'id.', OS lfnon w.v. 'id.', OHG lemon, lernen, lirnen w.v. 'id.', G lernen w.v. 'id.' (EUR). An inchoative verb continuing a medio-passive factitive neh₂-present \*lis-nh₂- 'to make oneself know'. See also \*lisan-.

\*16/an- - \*lappan- m. 'palm of the hand' - Go. lofa m. 'id.', ON /6fi m. 'id.', Ice!. 16fi m. 'id.', Far. /6gvi m. 'palm of the hand; small glove', /abbi m. 'paw', Nw. /abb m. 'paw, big hand', ME /Ove 'palm of the hand', OHG lappo m., /affa f. 'palm of the hand; blade of an oar', MHG /affe f. 'id.' ⇒ \*!eh₂p-on- (NEUR) - Ru. lclpa, SCr. lupa f. 'paw', Lith. Iopa f. 'id.' < \*leh₂p-eh₂- (the initial dental of Cz. tlapa, dial. dlapa, Pol. dlapa 'id.' was adopted from Cz. d/an, Pol. dlorl 'palm of the hand' < \*doln&). An ablauting n-stem nom. \*Jojo, gen. \*/appaz, dat. \*/abeni < \*!eh₂p-on, \*Jh₂p-n-6s, \*Jh₂p-en-i (cf. Kauffmann 1887: 544; Liihr 1987: 68; Kroonen 201 1a: 309-10). The allomorphy was resolved by the different daughter languages in several different ways, giving rise to a number of secondary root variants, e.g. Far. /abbi, OHG Jappo < \*/abban- and OHG laffa < \*Japan-.

<!-- pdf-page: 381; section: dictionary -->

\*Joki- 341 The original genitival root is preserved by Ice!. 16pp f. 'paw' < \*lappo-. Also cf. ON gl6fi, Far. gl6gvi, OE glofm. 'glove' < \*ga-lofa(n)-.

\*IOki- m. 'creek' - ON lcekr m. 'brook, rivulet', Far. lږkur m. 'brooklet' (DRV). An i-stem with a secondary lengthened grade. Cf. without umlaut: Elfd. luok m. 'puddle'. Derived from the strong verb \*lekan- ( q.v.).

\*Joma- m. 'betrayal' - Ice!. 16mur m. 'id.' (DRV). A mo-stem created to the root of \*lewjan- (q.v.), to be reconstructed as \*loh₁u-mo- (with the regular development \*ou > \*o). Further cf. Ice!. luma w.v. 'to conceal' < \*lh₁um-ehr (through \*liime- < \*luh₁-m-eh₁- with pretonic shortening of the \*ii).

\*Jami- adj. 'crippled(?)' - MDu. loem adj. 'dull', EDu. loom adj. 'sluggish', MHG liieme adj. 'dull, pliant, mild' (DRV). An adjective derived from the verbal root of \*lamjan- (q.v.) with a lengthened grade: originally a gerundive?

\*Juba- n. 'praise' - ON /of n. 'praise; permission', Far. lov n. 'id.', Elfd. luv n. 'id.', OE lofn. 'praise', OFri. lofn. 'id.', Du. /ofn. 'id.', OHG lob n. 'id.', G Lob n. 'id.' ⇒ \*fubh-o- (IE) - Skt. lobhaya- 'to make crazy' < \*leubh-eie-; NP ii-luftan 'to be tangled, be hit down, be in love' < \*lubh-; CS ljuby f. 'love' < \*leubh-uh₂-; OCS ljubiti, Ru. ljubft' 'to love' < \*leubh-eie-; Lat. lubet, -ere 'to please' < \*lubh-eh₁-. A zero-grade formation, possibly derived from a lost strong verb \*leuban-. The same ablaut is also found in OE lufu f. 'love'. Also cf. the denominal ON lofa 'to praise; to allow', OE lofian 'to praise', OFri. lovia 'id.', OS looon 'id.', OHS /obon 'id.' < \*lubojan-. Go. lubains f. 'hope', on the other hand, presupposes a stative verb \*luben-, which is formally parallel to Lat. lubere 'to love' < \*lubh-ehr.

\*Jubja- n. 'herb, potion' - Go. lubja-leisei f. 'witchcraft', ON lyf n. 'medicine, healing herb', Elfd. lyv n. 'remedy', OE lyb n. 'medicine, drug, potion', MDu. lubbe n. 'rennet', OHG luppi n. 'poison, witchcraft', G Alem. lupp n. 'rennet' ⇒ \*lubh-io- (EUR) - Olr. luib f. 'herb, plant' < \*/ubh-ih₂/eh₂-. A concept associated with magic, which apparently also was thought to be an aspect of cheese-making in view of OHG kiisi-luppa, OE cfes-lyb 'rennet'. The term represents a Celtic-Germanic isogloss, but given the obvious etymological link with PGm. \*lauba- 'foliage', it seems likely that the word spread from Germanic to Celtic (not vice versa). It has alternatively been suggested by Hyllested 2010: 123, however, that both the Germanic and the Celtic word were adopted from Uralic \*/uppo, cf. Fi. luppo 'lichen'.

<!-- pdf-page: 382; section: dictionary -->

342 \*luden-

\*luden- w.v. 'to dangle' - Icel. looa w.v. 'to hang down, stick to', lodda w.v. 'to dangle', Far. looa w.v. 'to hang down', Nw. loda w.v. 'to hang down, stick to' (GM). Also cf. OHG lotar adj. 'loose, light-hearted, lazy' < \*ludra- and MDu. Joi, Du. Jui adj. 'lazy' < \*luþja-.

\*ludja/6- n./f. 'face' - Go. ludja f. 'id.', OHG ant-lutti n. 'id.', G Antlitz n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁ludh-io- (IE) - Av. rao8a- m. 'growth, shape' < \*Hloudh-o-. The formally attractive connection with OS Jud 'generative power' < \*ludja-(?) and Go. laudi f. 'image' < \*laudja make it likely that the word was derived from \*leudan- 'to grow' (q.v.).

\*luftu- m. 'roof, air' - Go. luftus m. 'air', ON loft n. 'air, sky; loft', Far. loft n. 'air; ceiling, loft', Elfd. /opt n. 'id.' ( < \*lufta-), OE lyft m./f./n. 'air, sky', OFri. lufte, luft f. 'id.', OS luft f. 'air', Du. lucht c. 'air, sky', OHG luft m./f. 'id.', G Luft f. 'id.' ⇒ \*lubh-tu- (EUR). A tu-stem derived from the same root as found in \*lauba- 'foliage'. The original meaning was 'roof, which in view of the etymological link with Ru. lub 'bast', OPru. lubbo 'bast, plank' may have been fashioned from patches of bark in Proto-Germanic times. Unrelated to Hitt. a/pa- c. 'sky' (pace Puhvel 1984-: 1-2, 38).

\*lugi- adj. 'deceitful, false' - OE lygge adj. 'id.', OS luggi adj. 'deceitful', OHG lucki adj. 'id.', MHG liige, liicke adj. 'id.' (DRY). A WGm. gerundive \*Jugja- (continuing older \*lugi-) to the strong verb \*leugan- 'to lie' (q.v.). Derivationally parallel to \*flugi- 'fledged' to \*fleugan'to fly' ( q .v.).

\*lugino- f. 'lie, falsehood' - Far. lygn f. 'id.', Elfd. lyngen f. 'id.', OE lygen f. 'id.', OFri. leine f. 'id.', OS lugina f. 'id.', Du. leugen c. 'id.', OHG lugina f. 'id.', G Liige f. 'id.' (DRY). Derived from \*leugan- with the same suffix as in \*pakin6- 'cover', for which see \*paka-.

\*luho- f. 'lynx' - OSw. 16 f. 'id.', Elfd. luo f. 'id.' ⇒ \*luk-eh₂- (EUR). The feminine form of \*luhsa- (q.v.). Technically, ON /6 can be reconstructed as both \*lunh6- (cf. Gr. A.uyٸ and \*luh6-, but the first form is precluded by the non-nasal vowel of Elfd. luo.

\*luhsa- m. 'lynx' - OE lox m. 'id.', Du. los c. 'id.', OHG luhs m. 'id.', G Luchs m. 'id.' ⇒ \*luk-so- (EUR) - Gr. A.uyٹ m./f. 'lynx' < \*lunli-; Arm. lusanun 'lynx; hyena; marten', Lith. lusis, dial. lqnsis m./f. 'lynx', OPru. luysis 'id.' < \*luHlt-; Ru. rjs' m. 'id.' < \*ruHlt-i-; Mir. lug m. 'lynx; warrior' < \*lugu-.

<!-- pdf-page: 383; section: dictionary -->

\*Iuka- 343 A European word for 'lynx'. The form \*luhsa- corresponds to the feminine \*luho- (q.v.) as \*fuhsa- 'fox' to \*fuhon- 'vixen', but in spite of this regular proportion, it is difficult to reconstruct a proto-form beyond Germanic. All potential cognates show some kind of irregular sound correspondence: the laryngeal of Baltic, the r of Slavic, the n of Greek, the g of Irish. Perhaps, these irregularities result from multiple tabooistic distortions. The alternative is to assume repeated borrowing from a non-Indo-European language (or languages).

\*Iuka- n. 'weed(s)' - ON lok n. 'id.', Far. lok n. 'id.', Sw. dial. /uk 'id.', EDa. lug 'id.' (ORV). The etymological link with Gr. t.uyo<; 'twig' (e.g. Falk/Torp 1960: 653) is formally apt, but since the Germanic word was derived directly from to strong verb \*leukan- - \*liikan- 'to pull' (q.v.), the formal and semantic match with the Greek word is likely to be coincidental.

\*lukka- m. 'tuft' - ON /okkr m. 'lock of hair', Far. /okkur m. 'id.', OE foe m. 'id.', E lock, Du. lok c. 'id.', OHG foe m. 'id.', G Locke f. 'id.' (ORV). An a-stem that in view of Cimb. lock m. 'tuft of wool' must have been derived from the strong verb \*leukan- - \*liikan- 'to pull' (cf. also \*luka'weeds'). There is no direct link with Lith. lugnas adj. 'flexible' (pace Pokorny IEW: 685-6).

\*luk(k)on- w.v. 'to caress, lure, entice' - ON lokka w.v. 'to allure, entice', Elfd. lukka w.v. 'id.', OE a-loccian w.v. 'to entice', ge-loccian w.v. 'to caress', MDu. locken w.v. 'to entice', Du. lokken w.v. 'id.', OHG lockon, /ohhon w.v. 'id.', G locken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*lugrhl-neh₂- (NEUR) - Lith. lu(n)ginti 'to allure, fondle' < \*lugrhl-T).h₂-. An iterative verb of ambiguous origin. It is usually derived from \*leugan'to lie' or connected to Lith. lug6ti 'to ask' (cf. Falk/Torp 1960: 653). Alternatively, it has been interpreted as belonging to \*leukan- - \*/iikan- 'to pull' with a semantic shift of 'to attract' to 'to entice'. The Germanic meaning 'to entice', however, clearly developed from a more primitive 'to caress', which occurs both in Old Norse and Old High German. A more suitable cognate therefore is Lith. lu(n)ginti, which similarly combines both meanings. In addition, it is formally parallel to the Pre-Germanic neh₂-present, and thus allows us to explain the variation of OHG lockon < \*lukkon- and /ohhon < \*lukonas resulting from an iterative paradigm 3sg. \*lukkopi, 3pl. \*lukunanpi < \*lug-neh₂-ti, \*lug-nh₂-enti.

\*lumen- w.v. 'to beat' - Ice!. luma w.v. 'to beat, thrash' ⇒ \*h₃/mhi-ehi- (EUR) - Lith. /imti (/imstu, limaii) 'to bend, stoop', dial. 'to break', Latv. limt2 'to slip, collapse', OPru. limtwei 'to break' < \*h₃/mhi-.

<!-- pdf-page: 384; section: dictionary -->

344 \*lunda/o- A stative formation closely related to \*lamjan- ( q.v.).

\*lunda/o- m.jf. 'grove' - ON lundr m. 'id.', Ice!. lundur m. 'id.', Far. fund f. 'id.', Sw. fund c. 'id.' ⇒ \*lndh-o/ehr (EUR) - Ir. land m./f. 'land, ground, plot; house, building, church', MW llann f. 'church-yard', OPru. lindan acc. 'valley', Ru. ljada f. 'recently overgrown land, fallow land' < \*lndh-o/eh₂- (also cf. CS lǻdina, SCr. /Mina, ledina f. 'id.'). A zero-grade formation closely related to \*landa- (q.v.).

\*lundlj)o- f. 'fat' - ON fund f. 'mind, temper', Far. fund f. 'disposition', pl. 'internal back muscles, tenderloin', OE lynd f. 'fat', OHG lunda f. 'tallow' ⇒ \*lndh-(i)eh₂- (IE). A zero-grade formation related to \*landjo- (q.v.). With OE lynd < \*fund/-, it is possible to postulate an ablauting ih₂-stem nom. \*landi, gen. \*lundjoz.

\*lungon- f. 'lung' - ON lungu n.pl. 'id.', Far. lunga n. 'id.', Elfd. lungga f. 'id.', E lung, OS lunga f. 'id.', Du. long c. 'id.', OHG lungun f.pl. 'id.', G Lunge f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁lngwh.on- (IE) - Arm. lanj-k' pl. 'breast' < \*h₁lngwh.ihi- (Beekes 2003: 178, 190). A neuter n-stem derived from the stem of \*lungra- 'light' (q.v.). For the semantic development, cf. Ru. /egkoe, which belongs to Ru. /egkij adj. 'light' < \*h₁lngwh_u-, Lat. /eve iecur, EDu. lichte 'lung', E lights pl. 'lungs (esp. of cattle)'. Also cf. OE lungen, OFri. lungen(e), OS lungannia, OHG lungunna f. 'lung' < \*lungunjo-. There probably is no link with ON fungi m. 'core, essence'.

\*lungra- adj. 'quick, light' - OE lungre adv. 'id.', OHG lungar adj. 'easy, quick', MHG lunger adj. 'quick, happy' ⇒ \*h₁lngwh.ro- (IE). A PIE ro-stem identical to Gr. D .. mpp6<; adj. 'light, nimble, quick, small'. Related to \*linhta- ( q.v.).

\*luniz- n. 'linchpin' - OE lynis m. 'id.', E finch, OS lunis m. 'id.', MDu. lunse, lense f. 'id.', Du. funs c. 'id.', OHG fun f. 'id.', G Liinse f. 'id.' (GM). All dialectal variants split off from a primary s-stem \*lunaz, gen. \*lunizaz: 1. OHG fun < \*luni(z)-; 2. OE lynis, OS lunis < \*lunisa-; 3. MDu. lunse, G Liinse < \*luniso-. MDu. lense could theoretically be based on a full-grade root (Franck 1949: 404), thus implying a paradigm nom. \*lenaz, gen. \*lunizaz < \*!enH-os, \*lnH-es-os. Extra-Gm. parallels are lacking, however, and lense may actually be a Frisianism, the expected outcome of PGm. \*luniso- being \*lense in Old Frisian. The traditionally compared Skt. iii:zf- 'axle-pin, linchpin; part of the leg above the knee' as well as ToB yofiiye 'hip' were borrowed from a lost language in Central Asia (Pinault 2003).

<!-- pdf-page: 385; section: dictionary -->

\*luppon- 345

\*luppon- f. 'flea' - Far. loppa f. 'id.', OSw. loppa f. 'id.', Elfd. luppa f. 'id.', OE loppe f. 'id.' (GM). An Anglo-Norse words perhaps created to MHG lupfen, liipfen w.v. 'to lift (oneself) up, hop' < \*luppjan-.

\*lusti- f. 'loss' - Go. fra-lusts f. 'id.', OS far-lust f. 'id.', OHG for-lust f. 'id.', G Ver-lust m. 'id.' (ORV). A ti-stem derived from \*leusan- 'to lose' (q.v.).

\*lustu- m. 'desire, lust' - Go. lustus m. 'id.', OE lust m. 'id.', E lust, OFri. lust m.jf. 'id.', OS lust f. 'id.', Du. lust c. 'id.', OHG lust m./f. 'id.', G Lust f. 'id.' (ORV) - Gr. A.LA.a(oμm 'to desire, long for' < \*li-lh₂s-ie-; Lat. lascfvus adj. 'playful, unrestrained' < \*lh₂s-sko- (plus fvus-suffix); Olr. lainn adj. 'avaricious' < \*lh₂s-ni-; Ru. l<isyj, Pol. obs. lasy adj. 'greedy, eager' < \*leh₂s-o-; Lith. loksnus adj. 'sensitive' < \*leh₂s-nu- (with secondary k emerging before sibilants in acute syllables). A tu-stem to a verbal root \*/us- (it was replaced by an n-stem in ON losti m. 'id.' < \*lustan-, just as in the case of \*purstu- 'thirst', q.v.). The Germanic root \*/us- has often been compared to the PIE root \*lh₂s- 'to desire' as supported by the aforementioned non-Germanic forms, but this connection can only be maintained by assuming an awkward secondary zero grade. Perhaps then, the word was simply derived from the strong verb \*leusan- 'to lose, be empty' (q.v.) through a semantic shift 'idle' > 'frivolous' > 'lustful'. Further cf. the denominal OE lystan, E to list, OS fustian, Du. lusten, OHG lusten, G liisten < \*lustjan-.

\*liitan- s.v. 'to stoop, bend forward' - ON luta s.v. 'id.', Far. /Uta w.v. 'id.', Elfd. lauta s.v. 'id.', OE liitan s.v. 'id.', E to lout (ORV). In view of the long \*ii of this strong verb, it is likely to be a back-formation to the iterative \*lutton- (q.v.). The root-final \*t may then continue a Pre-Germanic geminate \*tt. Also cf. Far. lotur adj. 'curved (of a ship's deck); embarrassed' < \*luta- and Elfd. lutti, obi. lutu f. 'slope' < \*luto-.

\*lutta- n. 'puddle(?)' - G Rhnl. Lotz n. 'deep spot in a creek (to scoop water)' ⇒ \*fut-no- (WEUR?) - Olr. lath f. 'mud, mire', Lat. lutum 'mud' < \*lut-o/eh₂-. An obscure word with only a few uncertain cognates in the ltalo-Celtic languages.

\*lutton- w.v. 'to stoop, droop(?)' - Nw. Iota w.v. 'to be askew, slanted', G Rhnl. lotzen w.v. 'to feel drowsy, sleep' ⇒ \*ludCh₃-neh₂- (GM). The iterative to the strong verb \*liltan- 'to stoop' (q.v.). It is identical to \*lud(d)on- 'to dangle', cf. lcel. loaa, lodda, Far. loaa, Nw. loda w.v. 'to hang

<!-- pdf-page: 386; section: dictionary -->

346 \*madrondown; to stick (to)', which would imply that the PGm. root was \*!up- or \*ludrather than \*fut-. Outside Germanic, the word has been connected to Olr. Iott m. 'damage, destruction', but it is unclear whether this form can be derived from \*ludfhl-no-, as n-gemination (Stokes' law) remains controversial in Celtic.

### M

\*madron- f. 'madder' - ON maora f. 'id.', Nw. modre f. 'id.', OE mcedere, mceddre f. 'id.', E madder, MHG matare, metere f. 'id.' (IE?). A herbaceous scrambling plant cultivated as early as the 14th century BCE in the East Mediterranean (Zohary/Hopf 2000: 210) for the reddish-purple dye obtained from the root. ON maora, OE mcedre and OHG matara go back to a proto-form \*mad(a)ron- (cf. Fi. matara, Latv. madaras). It has furthermore been argued that MLG, MDu. mede f. 'id.', Du. mee-krap 'id.' and WFri. miede 'id.' represent an ablauting formation \*medjon- (Franck 1949: 418) or even \*maidon- (Torp 1909: 306), but I assume that this is the Frisian outcome of a variant \*madon- that spread to the surrounding Saxon and Franconian dialects. No certain etymology. Pokorny IEW: 747 connects CS modrb, Cz. modry, SCr. modar adj. 'blue' < PS!av. \*modrb, which can be related to Hitt. antara- adj. 'id.' < \*mdh-ro- (Machek 1949: 131-2). Perhaps, Germanic had a related adjective \*madra- that served as the derivational base for \*madr6n-. Or was the Slavic adjective borrowed from Germanic?

\*magan· m. 'stomach' - ON magi m. 'id.', Far. magi m. 'id.', Elfd. magi m. 'id.', OE maga m. 'id.', E maw, OFri. maga m. 'id.', Du. maag c. 'id.', OHG mago m. 'id.', G Mage m. 'id.' ⇒ \*mok-on- (EUR/NIE) Ś Lith. miikas, Latv. maks m. 'id.'; OPru. danti-max 'gums' < \*mak-o-; OCS mosbna f. 'small bag, scrip', Ru. mosn<i f. 'pouch, purse', SCr. mosnja 'purse, scrotum' < \*mok-in-eh₂-; MW megin f. 'bellows', MBret. meguin, Bret. megin f. 'id.' < PCelt \*mokinii. A European word with no IE etymology. The occurrence of a plain \*k could point to a non-IE origin. Also cf. Swi. (App.) mega 'rennet', EDu. meghe 'whey; curd' < \*magin-(?).

\*magapi- f. 'girl, maiden' - Go. magaps\* f. 'id.', OE mceg(e)o f. 'id.', E maid, OFri. megith f. 'id.', OS magath f. 'id.', Du. maagd f. 'id.', OHG magad f. 'id.', G Magd f. 'id.' ⇒ \*magh·ot-i- (DRY). A feminine derived from \*magu- ( q.v.). The feminizing suffix \*-api- has been analyzed as an indication that the word was borrowed from a language related to Semitic, since this language family has a productive femi-

<!-- pdf-page: 387; section: dictionary -->

\*magra- 347 nizing t-suffix (Vennemann 2002; Boutkan 2003: 11-17). PIE did have semantically comparable t-stems as well, however, cf. PGm. \*nefan- 'cousin' < \*nep-ot-. It is not implausible, therefore, that PGm. \*magapi- inherited a PIE suffix \*-ot-i-. Further evidence for this derivational element comes from the Middle Flemish suffix -ede < \*-apf-, which was often added to masculine n-stems, cf. MDu. graefnede 'countess' to grafe 'count' < \*ga-refan- and swasenede 'sister-in-law' to an unattested \*swase 'brother-in-law' < \*swesan-. Also cf. \*briidi- 'bride'.

\*magra- adj. 'lean' - ON magr adj. 'id.', Elfd. mager adj. 'id.', OE mceger adj. 'id.', E meager (= Anglo-Norman megre, meigre), Du. mager adj. 'id.', OHG magar adj. 'id.', G mager adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*mP21<-r6- (IE) - Identical to Gr. μακρός adj. 'long', Lat. macer adj. 'thin, lean'; also Hitt. maklant- adj. 'thin, meagre' < \*meh₂l<-1-ent-. An old ro-stem, with regular vocalization of the laryngeal between initial m and a consonantal element. Elfd. maungen w.v. 'to lose weight' < \*magnan- is based on the suffixless form. For another word with approximately the same meaning, see \*hurha-.

\*magu- m. 'boy, relative' - Go. magus m. 'boy', ON mpgr m. 'id.', OE magu m. 'child, young man, son', OFri. mach n. 'child', OS magu m. 'son', OHG maga-zoho 'nurturer' ⇒ \*magh-u- (WEUR) - Olr. mug 'servant', Corn. maw 'id.' < \*magh-u-. A West European word of obscure origin. See also \*magafJi-, \*mawf- and \*mega-.

\*mahti- f. 'strength' - Go. mahts f. 'id.', OE meaht, m.iht f. 'id.', E might, OFri. macht f. 'id.', OS maht f. 'id.', Du. macht c. 'id.', OHG maht f. 'id.', G Macht f. 'id.' (ORV). A ti-stem derived from the preterite-present \*mugan- (q.v.). ON mattr m. 'id.' continues a tu-stem \*mahtu-.

\*maida- m. 'post' - ON meior m. 'beam; log, tree', lcel. meiour m. 'id.', Nw. mei m. 'skid', Sw. med c. 'beam; skid' ⇒ \*moi-to- (IE) - Latv. miets m. 'post, stick' < \*moi-to-. A to-stem derived from a PIE root \*mei-, cf. Skt min6ti 'to fix, build' < \*mi-neu- and Latv. miet (meju, mienu) 'to set a post' < \*moi-ie-. Related to \*mairja- 'mooring-post' (q.v.).

\*maidjan- w.v. 'to damage, hurt' - ON meioa w.v. 'to hurt; to damage, destroy', Far. meioa w.v. 'to injure a limb' ⇒ \*moith₂-eie- (IE) - Skt methati 'to hurt' < \*m6itH-e-; Gr. μ[ruJ.oc; adj. 'mutilated' < \*mitH-ulo-; OLith.

<!-- pdf-page: 388; section: dictionary -->

348 \*mainaap-maitinti 'to wound' < \*-moitH-neh₂-; Latv. maitat 'to spoil, destroy' < \*moitH-eh₂-ie-. A causative probably related to \*maitan- and \*mitton- (q.v.).

\*maina- adj. 'common' - Go. ga-mains adj. 'shared, communal, common, vulgar', OE ge-mii!ne adj. 'common', E mean, OS gi-meni adj. 'joined, common, ordinary', Du. ge-meen adj. 'mean; common', OHG gi-mein, gi-meini adj. 'common, public', G ge-mein adj. 'mean; common' ⇒ \*kom-moi-no- (IE) - Lat. commiinis adj. 'common, general' < \*kom-moi-no-; Lith. mainas m. 'exchange' < \*moi-no-. An formation that in view of the parallelism with Lat. commiinis must have arisen prior to the rise of Germanic as a separate language. The adjective is also attested without a prefix, in the PGm. word for 'perjury', cf. ON mein-eiar, OE man-lip, OFri. mena eth, OS meneth, Du. mein-eed, OHG mein-eid, G Mein-eid < \*maina-aipa- 'false oath'.

\*mainjan- w.v. 'to think' - OE mii!nian w.v. 'to mean, intend', E to mean, OFri. mena w.v. 'to mean, believe; to suspect; to intend', OS menian w.v. 'to mean, mention', Du. menen w.v. 'to think', OHG meinen w.v. 'to mean; to believe; to say', G meinen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*moin-eie- (WEUR) - ocs meniti 'to suppose, think, reckon', SCr. mijeniti 'to think, say, remember' < \*moin-eie-; Olr. mfan f. 'desire; inclination' < \*mein-eh₂-. A Germanic-Baltic causative formation. Also cf. OFri. mene f. 'opinion', OHG meina f. 'meaning, intention, opinion' < \*main(j)o-. \*mairjan. 'mooring-post' - ON /anda-m;;eri n. 'border post', OE /and-gemii!re n. 'id.', MDu. mere, meer f. 'post, boundary, mooring-post; pillory', Du. meer-paal c. 'mooring-post' ⇒ \*moi-r-io- (IE) - Lat. miirus m. 'wall' < \*moi-ro-; Skt. min6ti 'to fix, build', Olr. do-dimen\* 'to fix, tie' < mi-neu-; YAv. barazi-mita- adj. 'highly built' < \*mi-to-. A formation related to \*maida- 'post' (q.v.). Also cf. OE mii!rels-rap 'mooring-rope' < \*mairisla- and MDu. meren w.v. 'to moor; to mark with posts; to dwell', Du. aan-meren 'to moor', ME moren 'to take root, fasten, fix', E to moore < \*mairojan-, whence British NLat. moragium 'moorage'. MDu. maren 'to moor' is an Ingvaeonism and LG ver-moren was probably borrowed from English.

\*maisa- m. 'backpack' - ON meiss m. 'wooden box, basket', Far. meis f. 'net basket; net stretched on frame on which corn is dried; certain amount of corn; tousled hair', Nw. meis m./f. 'wicker basket, wicker backpack, haynet' ( < \*maisa/o-), MDu. mese f. 'basket, hamper', OHG meis(s)a f. 'carriage' ( < \*mais-jo-), MHG meise f. 'a frame to carry on the back' ( < \*maisjo-) ⇒ \*mois-o- (IE) - Lith. maifas m. 'bag, sack', Latv. maiss m. 'bag', OPru. moasis

<!-- pdf-page: 389; section: dictionary -->

\*maison- 349 'bellows', OCS mexa m. 'wine-skin', Ru. mex m. 'fur', dial. 'bag', SCr. ml'jeh m. 'bellows, wine-skin' < \*mois-o-. If its original meaning was 'skin-bag', the Germanic word can be equated with Skt. meڗa- m. 'ram' and Gr. μ£lov n. 'sacrificial sheep or lambs'. De Vries 1962: 382 rejected this linkage in view of the supposed primary meaning 'net', but this sense can no doubt be secondary.

\*malson- f. 'titmouse' - lcel. -meisa f. 'id.', Nw. meis f. 'id.' ( < \*maiso-), OE miise f. 'id.', E tit-mouse (with -mouse supplanting regular \*-mose), MDu. mese f. 'id.', Du. mees c. 'id.', OHG meisa f. 'id.', G Meise f. 'id.' (GM). An exclusively Germanic bird name. It is often compared to W myalch 'thrush' < \*meis-alko-, but this word is more likely to be connected with PGm. \*ams/on- 'blackbird' (q.v.). The internally Germanic derivation from Nw. meis adj. 'weak' (De Vries 1962: 382; Strathmann 2008: 2, 414) is formally possible, but the adjective is absent from the dictionaries and may be a ghost word. Presumably, Nw. meis m. 'titmouse; weak person' (cf. Elfd. mjes m. 'titmouse') was mistaken for an adjective. With secondary suffixation, cf. ON meisingr m. 'id.' and ODu. \*mesinga as continued by MLat. misinga, OFr. mesinga, Fr. mesange < \*maisinga/o-.

\*maitan- s.v. 'to hew, cut' - Go. maitan s.v. 'id.', ON meita w.v. 'id.', OHG meizan s.v. 'id.', MHG meizen s.v. 'id.' (DRY). An o-grade intensive verb. The root-final \*t points to contamination with or derivation from the related iterative \*mitton- 'to cut' (q.v.). In view of the presumably related causative \*maidjan- 'to hurt' it seems reasonable, at any rate, to assume that the original form of the verb was \*maiþan-. As a result, the reconstruction of a PIE root \*meid- (Pokorny IEW: 697) must be abandoned. See further \*amaitjo- and \*mfton- 'mite'.

\*maiwa- 1 adj. 'slim, narrow' - ON mj6rr adv. 'id.', Far. mjaur adj. 'id.', Elfd. mjq adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*moiH-uo- (IE). A wa-stem identical to ToB maiwe adj. 'young' < \*moiH-uo-. Derived from the PIE root \*meiH-, for which cf. \*minniz comp. 'smaller, less'.

\*maiwa- 2 m. 'sea-gull' - ON mar, m6r m. 'id.', Icel. mafur, mavur m. 'id.', Far. ma(v)ur m. 'id.', OE miiw m. 'id.', Du. meeuw c. 'id.', OHG meh m. 'id.', G Mowe m. 'id.' (= LG) (GM?). An exclusively Germanic word. The traditional comparison with Skt. mecaka- 'blue' is a mere shot in the dark. Schrijver 1997: 305 more appropriately compared the word to OFr. moue, Picardian mauve 'gull', which he assumed to continue a loanword from Celtic \*maw-. The alternation with PGm. \*maiw- would then point to a non-Indo-European word. It cannot be excluded, however, that the Old French word was borrowed from Germanic

<!-- pdf-page: 390; section: dictionary -->

350 \*maizan- \*maiwa-. Further cf. OE mci!w, meaw m. 'id.', E mew < \*maiwi- as well as the Nordic diminutives Far. masi, Nw. mase, Sw. mas < \*-san- and Sw. make, Da. mage < \*-kan-. The latter formation was presumably adopted by Frisian and Dutch as mok 'sea-gull'.

\*maizan- comp. 'more' - Go. maiza comp. 'id.', ON meiti comp. 'id.', OE mara comp. 'id.', E more, OFri. mara, mera comp. 'id.', OS mero comp. 'id.', Du. meer comp. 'id.', OHG mero comp. 'id.', G mehr comp. 'id.' ⇒ \*meh₂-is-on(IE) - Olr. m6r adj. 'great' < \*meh₂-ro-; W mwy comp. 'more' < \*meh₂-i6s (Osc. mais comp. 'id.' does not belong here, but is to be equated with Lat mag is with schwa secundum as in maior < \*mg-ios- (cf. \*mekila-). Since PIE comparatives in \*-is- had root stress, the fact that Verner's law operated in \*maizan- proves that the word originally must have been trisyllabic, i.e. \*m&isan- < PIE \*meh₂is-on-. Just like the development of PGm. \*winda- 'wind' < PIE \*h₂uehrento-, this comparative thus demonstrates that Verner's law preceded Osthoff's law. This again implies that Osthoff's law in Germanic must be separated from Osthoff's law in Greek and Latin. As for the etymology of the root \*meh₂-, it is likely, in my opinion, that it served as the basis for the comparative and superlative suffix PIE \*-1'[1h₂(-on)- > PGm. \*-uman-, cf. Go. auhuma 'higher' < \*ufuman- < \*up-1'[1h₂-on-, etc.

\*maka- adj. 'fit, comfortable' - ON makr adj. 'easy to deal with; suitable', Far. makur adj. 'quite, still, calm', MDu. ge-mak adj. 'settled, calm, pleasant', Du. mak adj. 'tame', OHG gi-mah adj. 'modest, suitable', MHG ge-mach adj. 'appropriate, convenient, pleasant' (IE?) - Olr. mam 'yoke' < \*mag-mo-(?); Lith. mJgti (mJgiu) 'to like', Latv. megt 'to be able' < \*meg-ie-. An adjective with an original meaning 'fit, suitable' (cf. \*makan- 'match'). It served as the derivational base for the weak verb OE macian 'to prepare, make', E to make, OFri. makia 'id.', OS (gi-)makon 'to make, form, fix', Du. maken 'to make', OHG mahh6n 'id.', G machen 'id.' < \*makojan-. Under the assumption that the original meaning was 'to knead', the verb is usually compared to Gr. μacrcrw 'to knead, mold; to wipe' < \*mh₂g-ie and OCS mazati 'to anoint', SCr. mdzati 'to smear' < \*meh₂9-eh₂-. The original meaning is more likely to have been 'to match, put together', however, which renders these connections obsolete. The etymological link with the aforementioned non-Germanic forms is more attractive, but the reconstruction of a PIE root \*meg- nevertheless remains uncertain.

\*makan- m. 'match' - ON maki m. 'id.', Far. maki m. 'equal, peer; spouse', Elfd. matji m. 'spouse; match', OE ge-maca m. 'mate, equal, companion', ME make 'spouse', OS gi-mako m. 'comrade', OHG ga-mahho m. 'id.' (DRY). Derived from \*mak6jan-, for which see \*maka- adj. 'fit'. Also cf. OE ge-mæcca m. 'match' < \*ga-makjan- and - for the original meaning 'to fix up'

<!-- pdf-page: 391; section: dictionary -->

\*malan- 351 - OFri. mekere m. 'matchmaker', MDu. maeke/aer m. 'matchmaker, intermediary, middle-man', Du. make/aar c. 'broker'.

\*malan- s.v. 'to grind' - Go. ma/an s.v. 'id.', ON ma/a s.v. 'id.', Far. ma/a s.v. 'id.', Elfd. mci/Q s.v. 'id.', OS ma/an s.v. 'id.', Du. ma/en s.jw.v. 'id.', OHG ma/an s.v. 'id.', G mah/en s./w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*m6lh₁-e- (IE) - Hitt ma//ai - mallanzi 'to grind' < \*m6lh₁-ei, \*mlhi-enti; Lat mo/i5, -ere 'id.', Arm. ma/em 'to crush', Lith. ma/ti (malu), Latv. maTt 'to grind' < \*m6/hi-e-; OCS m/eti 'id.', Ru. mo/6t' (melju), SCr. mljeti 'id.', Olr. mei/id 'id.' < \*melh₁-ie-; Skt. mṛṇāti 'to crush, grind' < \*ml-neh₁-. An o-grade intensive verb. Related to \*malwjan- and \*melwa- (q.v.).

\*maldjo- f. 'saltbush, orache' - Nw. svine-me/de f. 'id.', OSw. mre/d f. 'id.', OE me/de f. 'id.', OS maldia f. 'id.', Du. me/de c. 'id.', OHG me/ta, me/da f. 'id.', G Me/de f. 'id.' ⇒ \*molt-ieh₂-(?) (NIE?). An etymologically obscure plant name. If Gr. Ģt.hov, Ģ1.iiwv n. 'purple amaranth' is connected (thus Pokorny IEW: 716-9), it would point to a non-IE word varying between \*melt-, \*mlit- and \*m/et-. The Germanic word, however, is not to be reconstructed as \*me/do- (pace Torp 1909: 317) in view of OS ma/dia and the more primary formation Sw. molla and G Cimb. ma/da < \*ma/do-. The proto-form \*me/do- is also contradicted by Fi. maltsa 'chickweed' < PFi. \*malcca (cf. LAGLOS, II, 248-9), although this word could also have been borrowed from the potentially unrelated \*maldja-, cf. Nw. me/de n. 'seeds from weeds mixed with grain', dial. melde-stokk m. 'goosefoot, Chenopodium album'.

\*malha/o- m.jf. 'bag' - ON malr m. 'knapsack', MDu. male f. 'pouch, purse, bag', OHG ma/aha f. 'bag, knapsack', MHG ma/he f. 'leather bag' ⇒ \*mo/k(LW) - To Gr. μot.y6c; m. 'bag made of cowhide'? No IE etymology. Even if the supposed link with Gr. μot.y6c; is correct, the word still exhibits a formal mismatch with PGm. \*ma/ha-. It has therefore been suggested that we are dealing with a Thracian word (Pokorny IEW: 747) or a prehistoric Wanderwort (Furnee 1972: 126).

\*malta- adj. 'soft; gone bad(?)' - ON ma/tr adj. 'bitter', OHG malz adj. 'mild' =? \*mold-o- (IE) - OCS mlado adj. 'young', Ru. molod6j adj. 'id.', SCr. mliid adj. 'id.' < \*mold-o-. An adjective derived from the strong verb \*me/tan- ( q.v.). Also cf. ON malt, Far. ma/ti, Elfd. molt, OE mealt, Du. mout, OHG ma/z, G Malz 'malt' < \*ma/ta-.

\*maltjan- w.v. 'to make dissolve' - Go. ga-maltjan\* w.v. 'to dissolve', ON me/ta w.v. 'to malt for brewing; to digest', Far. me/ta w.v. 'to break, smash,

<!-- pdf-page: 392; section: dictionary -->

352 \*malwjancrush; to digest, dissolve in the stomach', OE mieltan w.v. 'to (make) melt', ODu. gi-me/tian w.v. 'to malt' ⇒ \*mo/d-eie- (IE) - Skt. mradayati 'to make soft' < \*m/od-eie-; Skt. mrdnati 'to crush' < \*mld-neh₂-; OAv. m6ral)dat 3sg.pres.inj. 'to ruin, crush' < \*ml-n-d-e-. The causative to \*me/tan- (q.v.). Of all Germanic languages, only Faroese seems to have preserved the original meaning 'to crush'. It thus directly corresponds to Skt. mrdniiti. The meaning 'to be weak' probably developed from 'to be crushed' in medio-passive use of the verb.

\*malwjan- w.v. 'to crush, pound' - Go. ga-ma/wjan w.v. 'id.', ON mfiJ/va w.v. 'id.' (IE). A causative formation containing the suffix \*-wjan-, for which cf. \*kaizwjan- 'to turn'. Related to \*ma/an- 'to grind' (q.v.).

\*mampjan- w.v. 'to mock, scorn' - Go. bi-mampjan w.v. 'id.' (DRV). A causative or intensive formation created to the iterative \*mump/b6n( q.v.).

\*managa- adj. 'many' - Go. manags adj. 'id.', ON mangr, margr (with assimilation) adj. 'id.', Far. mangur adj. 'id.', Elfd. maung adj. 'id.', OE manig, monig, menig adj. 'id.', E many, OFri. manich, monich, menich adj. 'id.', WFri. mennich adj. 'id.', OS manag adj. 'id.', Du. menig adj. 'id.', OHG manag, manig adj. 'id.', G manch adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*monogh-o- (NIE) - OCS mMog'b adj. 'much, many', Ru. mn6gij adj. 'much, many, numerous', SCr. mnogi adj. 'much, many, numerous' < \*munogho-; Olr. menic, Ir. minic 'often', MW mynych 'id.' < PCelt. \*menekki-. A Northwest Indo-European word that due to the irregular formal correspondences cannot be reconciled into a single proto-form. It has therefore been suggested that the word was borrowed from a non-IE source (Boutkan 1998: 124-5; Schrijver 2001: 422). This view is strengthened by the no doubt connected Fi. moni, which cannot have been borrowed from Germanic. The connection with Lith. minia f. 'crowd' (Schulze 1913: 333) must at any rate be abandoned, as this word was derived from the unrelated Lith. minti 'to tread'.

\*mandula- m. 'handle on a grinding mill' - ON mpndull m. 'id.', Far. mondul m. 'id.' (IE) - Skt. manthati, mathn&ti 'to whirl round' < \*mentH-e·, \*mnt-neH-; Skt. manthii- m. 'churning stick, whirl' < \*montH-ehi-; Lith. meii.te f. 'shoulder blade, paddle, trowel', Latv. meii.te f. 'ladle, stirring spoon' < \*mentH-ieh₂-; Lith. mࢭsti (menCiit) 'to mix', OCS mf(sti 'to stir, trouble', Ru. mjastf'id.', SCr. mesti 'to disturb, mix, stir' < \*mentH-. An instrumental noun derived from the PIE root \*me-n-tH- 'to stir'. G Mangel, dial. Mandel f. 'mangle' does not belong here, but was borrowed

<!-- pdf-page: 393; section: dictionary -->

\*mangjan- 353 from Romance, cf. It. mangano m. 'mangle; pressing machine; calendar' < Lat. manganum n. 'military catapult, mangonel', which in turn was adopted from Greek μayyavov n. 'philtre; catapult; block of a pulley; hunting-net'.

\*mangjan- w.v. 'to mix' - OE mengan w.v. 'id.', OFri. mengia w.v. 'id.', OS mengian w.v. 'id.', Du. mengen w.v. 'id.', OHG mengen w.v. 'id.', G mengen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*monHk-eie- (NEUR) - Lith. mankyti 'to knead' < \*monHk-eie-; unrelated to OCS mpka, Ru. muka, SCr. muka f. 'flour' < \*monHk-eh₂-. Also cf. OE ge-mong 'crowd', OS gi-mang 'id.', MDu. ge-manc m./n. 'mingling, company' (whence E among), MDu. ge-manc adj. 'mixed' < \*ga-mangaand ON manga w.v. 'to barter', OE ge-mangian w.v. 'to mix; to trade', OS mangon w.v. 'to trade', MDu. mange/en w.v. 'to mingle; to exchange; to argue' < \*(ga-)mangojan-. ON mengdr, mengjaar, menginn 'mixed, blended' is considered to be a Low German loanword.

\*mango- f. 'lack' - MHG mane f. 'id.' ⇒ \*monk-eh₂- (IE) - ToA miinkii- 'to be guilty'. ToB miirikii- 'to be inferior, lack' < \*m(e)nk-; Lat. mancus adj. 'maimed' < \*monk-a- (with unclear a); Lith. meiikas adj. 'small, weak' < \*menk-o-. Also cf. the denominal formations OHG mengen w.v. 'to lack' < \*mangjanand OHG mango/on, G mangeln w.v. 'id.' < \*manglojan-.

\*manja- n. 'necklace' - ON men n. 'id.', OE mene n. 'id.', OHG menni n. 'id.' ⇒ \*mon-io- (IE) - Skt. ma1Jf- m. 'amulet' (with unexplained retroflex), Olr. muin f. 'neck' < \*mon-i-; Skt. manyii- f.du. 'neck' < \*mon-ieh₂-; Av. manaot9rif. 'neck' (of unclear derivation); Olr. muinel, W mwnwg(l) 'collar' < \*moniklo(adopted by Latin as monfle 'necklace, collar'?). An inherited word for 'necklace'. It was probably derived from the PIE word for 'neck', for which see \*mano- 'mane, neck' and \*mankan- 'mane'.

\*mankan- m. 'mane; upper part of a horse's neck' - ON makki m. 'id.', Nw. manke m. 'id.', Elfd. maunke m. 'id.', Da. manke c. 'id.' ⇒ \*mon-gon(WEUR? /LW?) - Olr. mong f. 'tuft of hair', W mwng m. 'mane' < \*mon-go/eh₂-. A derivation from \*mano- 'mane, neck' with the more common kan-suffix for body parts, cf. ON kjalki 'jaw-bone' < \*kelkan- and Icel. lj6ski 'groin' < \*Ieuskan-). The parallelism with Olr. mong nevertheless indicates that addition of the velar happened at an early stage. Or was the Germanic word borrowed from Celtic?

\*mannan- m. 'man' - Go. manna, pl. manns m. 'id.', ON maar, pl. meor, menn m. 'id.', Far. maour, pl. menn m. 'id.', OE mann(a), pl. menn m. 'id.', E man, OFri. mon m. 'id.', Du. man c. 'id.', OHG man c. 'id.', G Mann c. 'id.' ⇒

<!-- pdf-page: 394; section: dictionary -->

354 \*manniska-

\*dhghm-on- (IE) - Lat. homo, -inis m. 'human being, man', Lith. obs. imuo m. 'man', OPru. smoy 'id.' < \*dhghm-on-; Lith. zmogits m. 'man' (for the suffix, cf. OCS mþ-Zb 'man' < \*mon-gh-io-). A word that was inflected as an n-stem in the singular and as a root noun in the plural. Presumably, the original paradigm \*manna, pl. \*mannaniz developed into \*manna, \*manniz by regular Proto-Germanic syncope of the unstressed vowel in the sequence \*-nnan-. The n-stem endings were then removed from the singular in most languages except for Gothic and OE manna. The etymology of the word is disputed. Some connect Skt manu'man' (cf. Ringe 2006: 139), but the underlying formation \*men-u- (not \*mon-u-) would regularly give \*minn- in Germanic. It is therefore more likely that the word split off from the PIE word for man, viz. \*dhghm-on-, which is the form underlying the parallel formation \*guman- 'man' (q.v.). The accusative stem \*dhghm-on- regularly Jost the initial stops, the initial cluster Cm- being impossible in Germanic, and thus gave rise to a PGm. root \*man- (Berneker 1898: 360-1). The geminate probably arose secondarily in an analogical paradigm nom. \*mano, gen. \*mannaz < quasi-PIE \*m6n-an, \*mon-n-6s. See further also \*manniska-.

\*manniska- adj. 'human' - Go. mannisks adj. 'id.', ON men{n)skr adj. 'id.', OE mennisc adj. 'id.', OHG mennisk adj. 'id.' (DRY). An adjective derived from \*mannan- 'man' with the adjectival suffix \*-iska-. Also cf. OFri. menneska, OS menisco, Du. mens, OHG mannisco, G Mensch m. 'man, mankind' < \*manniskan-.

\*mano- f. 'mane, neck' - ON m{m f. 'id.', Far. mon f. 'mane hair', OE manu f. 'mane', E mane, OFri. mona m. 'id.', WFri. moannen pl. 'id.', MDu. mane f. 'id.', Du. manen pl. 'id.', OHG mana f.
