\*þaggon- w.v. 'to silence' - ON þagga w.v. 'id.', Nw. tagga w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*tHk-neh₂- (WEUR). An interesting formation. Formally, the verb looks like the iterative (3sg. \*þakkopi, 3pl. \*þagunanpi < \*tHk-neh₂-ti, \*tHk-nhi-entl) to \*þagen-, but semantically it functions as a causative-factitive ('to put to silence'). As such, it is comparable to nasal presents such as Skt. ri1;ufri 'to make flow', the causative to rfyate 'to flow'. Also compare the inchoative ON þagna, Nw. tagna, Elfd. taungen w.v. 'to become silent' < \*þagnan-, which started out as the media-passive of the same neh₂-present.

\*þahsu- m. 'badger' - MDu. das m. 'id.', Du. das c. 'id.', MHG dahs m. 'id.', G Dachs m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ta ks- (WEUR/NIE). Most WGm. forms can straightforwardly be derived from \*dahsa-, but dial. Nw. toks implies ON \*þǭx < \*þahsu-. This u-stem may have split off from a dat.pl. of an n-stem \*þahsan- (cf. \*uhsan- 'ox') that can be postulated on the basis of the late Latin loanword taxo (beside taxus ), the source of Fr. dial. taisson, It. tasso, Sp. tej6n 'badger' (EWAhd: II, 498-501) and possibly also Basque dial. azkoin 'id.' (with loss of initial t, cf. Trask 2008: 122). Outside Germanic, one compares Mir. Tadg, the name of a king whose totem was a badger. The underlying proto-form \*tazgo- is further retrieved from the Gaulish proper names Tascos and Tasco-uanus. In view of the irregular alternation of Pre-Gm. \*taks- and PCelt. \*tazg-, the word may be of non-IE provenance (Watkins 1985: 69). Unrelated to Hitt. tasku-, an unspecified subcaudal bodypart (pace Katz 1998).

\*þaismjan- m. 'sourdough' - OE þæsma m. 'id.', MLG desem m. 'id.', MDu. desem m. 'id.', Du. zuur-desem c. 'id.', OHG deismo m. 'id.', MHG deisme m. 'id.' ⇒ \*teh₂is-mon- (EUR) - Ru. testo, SCr. ti'jesto n. 'dough'; Olr. mis, W toes m. 'id.' < \*teh₂is-to-; CS tiskati, Ru. tiskat', SCr. ti'skati 'to press, squeeze' < \*tih₂s-sk-eh₂-.

\*þaka- n. 'roof - ON þak n. 'id.', Far. tak n. 'id.', Elfd. tak n. 'id.', OE þæc n. 'id.', Du. dak n. 'id.', OHG dach n. 'id.', G Dach n. 'id.' ⇒ \*tog-o- (IE) - Gr.

<!-- pdf-page: 572; section: dictionary -->

532 \*þakjan- (o)•fyoЎ n. 'roof, house', Olr. tech n., OW tig, W ty m. 'id.' < \*(s)teg-os-; W to 'roof < \*togo-; Olr. tuige 'deck' < \*tog-io-; OPru. stogis, Lith. st6gas m. 'roof < \*stog-o-. A noun derived from a thematic verb \*(s)teg-e-, cf. Lat. tego 'to cover' and - with s-mobile - Gr. o•E:yw 'id.'. Also cf. ON þekja, OE þeccan, OFri. thekka, OS bi-thekkia, ODu. thecoda pret., Du. dekken, (OH)G decken w.v. 'to cover, thatch' < \*þakjan- and OS thekina f. 'roof, OE pecen f. 'id.', (M)Du. deken c. 'blanket' < \*þakino-.

\*þakjan- w.v. 'to burn' - OE þeccan w.v. 'to burn' ⇒ \*thl1jl-ie- (EUR?). Also cf. OHG dahhazen w.v. 'to blaze, flare' < \*þakatjan-. No further etymology, but possibly related to Gr. TI'Jyavov n. 'frying pan'; since Greek had a suffix -avov for tools, the variant niyr1vov is likely to have come about by metathesis.

\*þakkja- / \*þekka- adj. 'mild(?), sweet(?)' - ON þekkr adj. 'pleasant', OHG decki adj. 'dear' ⇒ \*tok-nf- / \*tek-nō- (WEUR) - Olr. etig adj. 'unnatural, un.seemly', W annheg adj. 'unfair, unelegant' < \*IJ·tek-i-. The etymon has been reconstructed as \*þakkja- on the basis of the reconstruction of the OHG adj. as decki\* (EWAhd: II, 555). The adjective is attested only once as the superlative dechisto, and can alternatively also be traced back to \*dee. With ON þekkr, this form could then be derived from PGm. \*þekka-. Both \*þakkja- and \*þekka- can be reconciled with PCel. \*-tek-i- by assuming n-gemination (cf. Luhr 1988: 232).

\*þak(k)on- w.v. 'to touch' - OE þaccian w.v. 'to þat, clap, strike gently', MDu. tacken w.v. 'to touch' ⇒ \*th₂g-neh₂- (IE) - Lat. tango, -ere 'to touch' < \*th₂g-neh₂-. An old PIE iterative formation to the strong verb \*takan- - \*tekan-. The dental fricative of OE þaccian is regular: MDu. tacken has its t from the strong verb, where it arose as the result of restored reduplication.

\*þambo- f. 'string' - ON ppmb f. 'guts, womb; bow-string' ⇒ \*tomp-eh₂- (NEVR) - Lith. tempti ( tempiu) 'to stretch' < \*temp-ie-; Lith. timpa f. 'sinew, string' < \*tmp-eh₂-. Cf. Nw. dial. temba f. 'bloatedness' < \*þambjon- and OFri. thempene, thampene f. 'doggedness' < \*þampfn- (with \*þamp- < \*tomp-n-l For the shift of meaning from 'stretch' to 'swell', see \*þinsan-.

\*þanga- n. 'seaweed' - ON pang n. 'id.', MLG dank 'id.' (GM). ON pang is formally reconcilable with OE flung, a word used for several poisonous plants, e.g. aconite, hellebore and LG woden-dung 'hemlock' < \*þunga- (cf. Pokorny JEW: 1068; Magnusson 1989: 1171), but the semantic

<!-- pdf-page: 573; section: dictionary -->

\*þanhon- 533 difference is problematic. Weijnen 1996: 207 further connects Du. dial. tongel 'cleavers' (cf. ON ppngull 'seaweed' < \*þangula-), but this is formally difficult in view of the initial t. Can there be a correlation with \*þēka- 'seaweed'?

\*þanhōn- f. 'clay' - Go. þahō f. 'clay', OE þōhe, þāhe, þō f. 'id.', OHG diiha f. 'id.', MHG diihe, tiihe f. 'id.', G Ton f. 'id.' ⇒ \*tonk-eh₂- (IE). A formation derived from the PIE root \*tenk- 'to thicken'. Cf. \*þinhla- and \*þinhtu-.

\*þanhtu- m. 'thread' - ON þáttr m. 'strand, thread; section, part; story', Far. táttur m. 'strand of a rope; part; song', Elfd. tqt m. 'id.', OHG tiiht m./n. 'wick', G Docht m. 'id.' ⇒ \*tonk-tu- (IE) - Wkh. tan3- / tan3d-, tanz- / tanzd'to fortify the warp (of textiles); to draw a web (of spiders)'. A tu-stem usually reconstructed as \*þehta-. Elfd. tqt m. 'id.', however, proves that the original form must have been \*þanhtu-.

\*þanjan- w.v. 'to stretch, extend' - Go. uf-þanjan w.v. 'to stretch, extend; to strive for', ON penja w.v. 'to stretch, extend', Far. tenja w.v. 'id.', OE pennan, penian w.v. 'to stretch, spread out, extend, bend (a bow)', OS thenian w.v. 'to stretch', MDu. denen w.v. 'to stretch; to torture', Du. deinen w.v. 'to wave', OHG dennan w.v. 'to stretch, extend', MHG den(n)en s./w.v. 'id.', G dehnen w.v. 'to extend' ⇒ \*ton(h₂)-eie- (IE) - Skt. -tiinayati 'to stretch' < \*ton-eie-; Gr. n-ra(vw 'id.' < \*ti-tn-ie-; Skt. tan6ti 'to stretch, extend', Gr. -ravuμm, ·ravuw 'to stretch, tense, extend, expand' < \*tn-neu-; OAv. us-tiina- adj. 'stretched out' < \*-tnh₂-nō-; Lat. tene6 'to hold, grasp' < \*tnh₂-eie-; Lith. tinti (tinsta) 'to swell, bloat', Latv. tit 'to wrap, wind' < \*tnh₂-ske-. A causative to the root \*ten(h₂)- 'to extend'. See also \*þuna-, \*þunnu- and \*þinna- - \*þunnō-.

\*þanka- m. 'thanks' - Go. pagks m. 'id.', ON f:Jpkk f. 'id.', Far. tekk f. 'id.', Elfd. tokk 'id.' ( < \*þanko-), OE pane m. 'thanks, satisfaction', E thanks, OFri. thank m. 'thanks; satisfaction; intention', OS thank m. 'thanks; grace', Du. dank c. 'thanks', OHG dank m. 'thanks; mercy, gratification', G Dank m. 'thanks' ⇒ \*tong-a- (IE). An a-stem related to \*þankjan- (q.v.). Also cf. ON pakka, Far. takka, Elfd. tokka, OE pancian, OFri. thonkia, OS thankon, Du. danken, OHG dankon, G danken 'to thank' < \*þankojan-.

\*þankjan- w.v. 'to think' - Go. pagkjan w.v. 'to think, plan', ON pekkja w.v. 'to perceive, notice; to comprehend; to know, recognize', OE pencan w.v. 'to think', E to think, OS thenkian w.v. 'to think, consider, watch', Du. denken w.v. 'id.', OHG denkan w.v. 'id.', G denken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*tong-eie- (EUR) - Iden-

<!-- pdf-page: 574; section: dictionary -->

534 \*þansjantical to Lat. tango, -ere 'to know'; also cf. ToA turik, ToB tarikw m. 'love' < PTo. \*tan kw < \*tng-u-. The verb formally resembles a causative formation, but in view of the meaning, which is stative rather than iterative, it seems likely that PGm. \*þankjan- as well as Lat. tongere continue a PIE i-present with a-grade of the root (De Vaan 2008: 623). See also \*þanka- and \*þunkjan-.

\*þansjan- w.v. 'to move back' - MLG deisen w.v. 'to sneak away', MDu. dei(n]sen w.v. 'to draw back, stand back', Du. deinzen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*tons-eie(IE) - Close to Skt. taTJ1sayati 'to shake', Lith. tqsyti 'to jerk' < \*tons-eie-. MDu. dei{n)sen can have developed out of \*þansjan-, a regular causative (intensive?) to \*þinsan- (q.v.), by diphthongization of e before n plus dental (cf. MDu. ende, einde 'end' < \*andja-), but the corresponding MLG form should then have been \*\*densen (Franck 1949: 110), which is not the case. Perhaps, the original causative \*þansjan- was remodeled into quasi-PGm. \*þaisjan- in the Ingvaeonic area after the regular change of PGm. \*þinsaninto \*þisan-. The nasal that can be found in (Middle) Dutch must then have. been restored under the influence of the non-Ingvaeonic dialects. In fact, the Ingvaeonic form \*þisan- is not attested in any of the old North Sea Germanic languages, and may have had a marginal distribution right from the beginning.

\*þanson- w.v. 'to stretch' - OHG danson w.v. 'to pull, stretch', MHG dansen w.v. 'id.' (DRY). The verb looks like an a-grade iterative to the strong verb \*þinsan- (q.v.), quasi-PIE \*tons-neh₂-. It was borrowed into Romance, cf. It. danzare, Fr. dancer, from where it again spread to the Gm. languages, e.g. E to dance, G tanzen, Du. dansen.

\*þar adv. 'there' - Go. þar adv. 'id.', ON þar adv. 'id.', Far. har, poet. tar adv. 'id.', Elfd. dar adv. 'id.', OE þar, pikr adv. 'id.', E there, OFri. ther adv. 'id.', OS thar adv. 'id.', Du. daar adv. 'id.', OHG dar(a) adv. 'id.', MHG dar, da adv. 'id.', G da adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*to-r (DRY). An adverb created to the root \*to- 'that, those' with the locative suffix \*-r. Cf. \*hwar.

\*þarbo- f. 'need, want, lack' - Go. parba f. 'want, need, necessity', ON pprf f. 'id.', OFri. therve, there f. 'need, want', OS tharf f. 'need, lack' ⇒ \*torp-eh₂- (IE). Also cf. Go. ala-parba adj. 'thoroughly destitute' < \*ala-parban- and the stative Go. ga-parban w.v. 'to abstain', ON parfa 'to be needed', Far. poet. tarva 'to need, want', OE pearjian 'to be in need', OS tharbon 'to Jack', OHG darben 'id.' < \*þarben-.

<!-- pdf-page: 575; section: dictionary -->

\*þarha(n)- 535

\*þarha(n)- m. 'seaweed, driftwood' - ON pari m. 'id.', Far. tari m. 'id.', Nw. tar(r}e m. 'seaweed; washed-up wood', MDu. dary, darich m. 'peat sod; muck', Du. derrie c. 'muck' ⇒ \*torl<-on-(?) (NEUR?). Du. derrie has been compared to Go. parihs(?) adj. 'unfulled, unprocessed' by Franck 1949: 1 12, but the connection with ON pari seems more likely in view of Du. (Zaan) derg 'floating island of reed or peat'. No received extra-Germanic etymology. I suspect the word to be related to Lith. tefSti (tersiu, tersiaii) 'to make dirty; to become muddy (of pastures); to spread out hay or grass' < \*terl<-ie-.

\*þarma- m. 'bowel, intestine' - ON þarmr m. 'id.', OE þearm m. 'id.', OFri. therm m. 'id.', OS tharm m. 'id.', MLG darm m. 'intestine', MDu. dar(e)m m. 'id.', Du. darm c. 'id.', OHG daram, darm m. 'id.', G Darm m. 'id.' ⇒ \*torm-o(EUR) - Gr. i:þāμu; f. 'space between the anus and the genitals, perineum' < \*trm-i-. An o-stem potentially related to the aforementioned Greek word. No further etymology.

\*þarzjan- w.v. 'to make dry' - ON þerra w.v. 'to dry, make dry; to dry, wipe', MLG derren w.v. 'to dry', MHG derren w.v. 'to wither, dry up' ⇒ \*tors-eie- (IE) - Cf. Skt. tar?ciyati 'to make thirsty' < \*tors-eie-. The causative to \*þersan-. Cf. with the same vowel grade Nw. tarre m .. OHG darra, G Darre f. 'kiln, oast' < \*þarza/ōn-.

\*þaula(n)- m. 'reciter, mumbler(?)' - Ice!. þauli m. 'speaker, orator', Nw. dial. tau! m. 'fool, half-wit' (GM). An ablauting form to \*þuli- 'reciter'. Also cf. Nw. dial. tu! and tyl m. 'fool' < \*þula/i-.

\*þawa- m. 'custom' - OE peaw m. 'custom, usage', ME thew 'habit, practice, virtue', OFri. thaw m. 'custom', OS thau m. 'id.', MLG duw 'conduct', OHG dou m. 'instruction' (GM). The primary meaning of PGm. \*þawa- ( < \*tou(H)o-, \*tof<w-6-?) could have been 'to be observant', which is semantically close to e.g. Lat. tu(e)or 'to look at, watch over, safeguard' < \*tuh₂-ehi-ie- (see \*þeudja-). Within Germanic, it could further be related to OE ge-þeon w.v. 'to do, commit, perform' < \*þewōjan-. Also cf. OE peawlic 'usual', OHG daulih 'decent' < \*þawa-lfka-.

\*þedura- m. 'wood grouse, capercaillie' - ON piourr m. 'id.', OSw. picedur m. 'id.', Elfd. tyooer m. 'id.', Da. tjur c. 'id.' ⇒ \*tetr(-uo)- (IE) - Skt tittira- m. 'partridge'; Gr. i:i-rpa4 m. 'black grouse(?)'; Lith. teterva f. 'black grouse'; Ru. teterev m. 'id.'.

<!-- pdf-page: 576; section: dictionary -->

536 \*þegjan- The Indo-European word for a type of grouse. The word for 'female capercaille', Nw. dial. rfiJy, Elfd. rii f. < \*raujō-, has no etymology.

\*þegjan- s.v. 'to request' - ON þiggja s.v. 'to obtain, accept', OE þicgan s.v. 'id.', OS thiggian w.v. 'to ask, request; to endure', OHG dicken, digen w.v. 'to beg for, request', MHG digen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*tek-ie- (EUR) - Olr. ad-teich 'to ask', Bret. tizaff 'to receive' < -tek-e-; Lith. tekti (tenku] 'to reach (for); to suffice; to be granted' < \*te-n-k-e-. Both in Germanic and the other IE branches, the original meaning seems to have been 'to reach out the hand' (cf. LIV2: 618-9), whence both 'to request' and 'to accept'. Also cf. \*þegna- 'retainer'.

\*þegna- m. 'retainer' - ON þegn m. 'thane, franklin, freeman, man; Hegeman, subject', OE þegn m. 'thane, servant, retainer', OS thegan m. 'youth, man, warrior, disciple', MDu. degen m. 'boy, prince, hero', OHG degan m. 'warrior, valet, companion, hero, soldier', MHG degen m. 'lad, servant, warrior' (DRY). A derivation from the verb \*þegjan- 'to request, beg'. The original meaning was 'retainer', i.e. an aristocratic dependant of a king or nobleman who gives service in return for patronage. The word is traditionally compared to Gr. TEKvov n. 'child', a formation belonging to TLKTW 'to give birth' < \*ti-tk-e(cf. Torp 1909: 177; Pokorny IEW: 1057; Shields 2006). The implied proto-form \*tek-no-, however, would depending on the place of the accent have yielded either \*\*þehna- or \*\*þekka- (the latter with Kluge's law). This not being the case, the etymology must be rejected.

\*þēka- m. 'drifting seaweed' - WFri. obs. teek 'id.', NFri. (F) tiak, (Hlg., Mo., Wi., Ka.) teek, (Go., Ha.) tiiik n. 'id.', LG deek, teek, Brem. [eek 'id.', Du. dial., arch. daak, deek, vaak, veek c. 'id.' (GM). An etymologically obscure word (cf. Sjolin 2006: 231) found only dialectally along the North Sea coast (cf. Opprel 1937). The distribution of the coastal deek vs. the more inland daak in the Dutch dialects points to a vowel \*e (rather than \*ai or \*a). In combination with WFri., NFri. teek, which implies an initial fricative, the word can confidently be reconstructed as PGm. \*þēka-. It seems probable, given the geographic distribution, that the word was spread by the Frisians. This is corroborated by the unexpected attestations with initial \*/ on both sides of the Frisian dialectal continuum, cf. LG [eek, Du. vaak, veek. Frisian is known to display a similar vacillation of/ and

þ in fiāl, tiāl n. 'wheel' < \*hwehla- - \*hweula- (q.v.), and is therefore a likely source of the forms with \*f Alternatively, it is possible that the adoption of the word by the non-Frisian population took place before the change of \*þ to d in the Frankish and Saxon dialects, thus resulting in daak or deek. The

<!-- pdf-page: 577; section: dictionary -->

\*þeku- 537 forms with initial \*f, on the other hand, may point to mere substitution of Fri. \*þ by non-Fri. \*f

\*þeku- adj. 'fat' - ON þykkr, þjokkr, þjukkr adj. 'id.', Far. tjukkur, tjukkur adj. 'id.', Elfd. tjokk adj. 'id.', OE þicce adj. 'id.', E thick, OFri. thikke adj. 'manifold, multiple', OS thikki adj. 'id.', Du. dik adj. 'fat; thick', OHG dicki adj. 'dense, thick; frequent', G dick adj. 'fat, thick' ⇒ \*tegu- (WEUR) - Olr. tiug adj. 'fat' < \*tegu- (with u-raising and u-infection). An old u-stem that was remodeled into the PNWGm. ja-stem \*þekwja-. The labial element caused breaking of -y- ( < i with w-mutation) to -ju- or -join ON þjukkr, Nw. tjukk, Sw. tjock (for which cf. Nw. tjurr 'dry' < \*þurzu-), and of -i- to -iu- in Frisian, cf. OFri. thiukke f. 'breadth' < \*þekwfn-. Etymologically, we are probably dealing with a Celtic-Germanic isogloss. The adjective has additionally been connected to Hitt. ljitenas tagaljaS 'flood' < "thick water" (Neu 1995), but this is more uncertain. The connection with the root of.\*þaka- 'cover, roof (q.v.) is highly doubtful in spite of the semantic parallel offered by Gr. crfyvoc; adj. 'dense' < \*steg-no-.

\*þemestra- adj. 'dark, dusky' - OE þīestre adj. 'id.', OFri. thiustere adj. 'id.', OS thiustri adj. 'id.' ( < \*þiustrja-), MLG demster adj. 'id.', MDu. dee(m)ster, duuster, vinster adj. 'id.', Du. duister, obs. deemster adj. 'id.', OHG dinstar, finstar adj. 'id.', MHG dinster, dimster, finster adj. 'id.', G finster adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*temhres-rō- (IE) - Skt. tamas- n. 'darkness' < \*temhres- and the derived Skt. tamisra- < \*temh₁-s-ro-; Lith. tamsa f. 'id.' < \*temhrs-eh₂- (whence tamsits adj. 'dark' < \*tomh₁-s-u-); Lat. tenebrae f.pl. 'darkness' < \*temhres-reh₂-; Olr. temel m. 'shadow, darkness', OW timuil, W tywyll 'dark, darkness' < \*temh₁-es-lo-. An old IE word that despite the dialectal irregularities can straightforwardly be derived from \*temhres-ro- (with t-epenthesis between s and r), a ro-stem created to the neuter s-stem \*temhres-. In Germanic, the most primary form \*þemestra- is represented by OHG dinstar, MLG demster, MDu. dee(m)ster. The variant \*þiustrOJa-, which is often considered to be unrelated (cf. Pokorny IEW: 1080-5) developed from (apparently syncopated) \*þemstra- by "a perfectly natural development of \*-ems- > \*-ims- > \*-iws- > \*-iljs-" (Gqsiorowski 2012). The third variant is represented by OHG fin(i)star, G finster, which arose due to assimilation of \*þ to the labial articulation of the \*m (Polome 1967: 810). Further cf. OS/OLFra. thiustarnussi f. 'darkness', Du. duisternis c. 'id.' and the more primary OS thimm adj. 'dark' < \*þimza- < \*temh₁s-ō and \*þemra- 'darkness'.

\*þemra- m. 'darkness' - OHG demar n. 'dusk' ⇒ \*temh₁-ro- (IE) - Skt. tamra- adj. 'darkening' < \*te/om(h₁}-r6-; Olr. tern adj. 'dark' < \*temh₁-ō-; OCS tbma f. 'darkness' < \*tmh₁-eh₂-.

<!-- pdf-page: 578; section: dictionary -->

538 \*þenura- Also cf. OHG demerunga, G Diimmerung f. 'dusk' and \*þemestra-.

\*þenura- m. 'reaction wood' - ON þinurr m. 'resinous fir-tree (of which bows and hoops were made); bow', Nw. tinur, tinar m. 'hard (piece of) wood', Elfd. tiqor m. 'reaction wood' (GM). The Nordic word referred to parts of trees consisting of wood that was particularly strong (socalled 'reaction wood'), e.g. the downward side of an overhanging tree. In view of the additional Old Norse meaning 'bow', this wood was apparently used to make this particular weapon. Formally, there is a complex discrepancy between ON flinurr and Elfd. tiqor, Sw. tjur-, the former continuing \*þinura-, the latter ON \*þjōrr < \*þenura- (with u-breaking, syncope and loss of -nr- as in \*Þōrr 'Thor' (cf. Elfd. tups-dag 'Thursday') < \*þunra- < \*þunara-). Both forms split off from a PN paradigm \*þenuran, dat. \*þenure that evolved from PGm. \*þenuraz, dat. \*þenurai. No clear etymology. ON flinurr is formally close to ON flinull, Ice!. þinul/, obs. flinill m. 'edge-rope of a net', Nw. tenel m. 'id.', which is usually connected to flenja w.v. 'to stretch' < \*þanjan- (with replacement of þinill < \*þenila- by flinull?), but the semantics are not entirely clear (something stretched?). There cannot be a connection with PGm. \*terwa/on- 'tar' either, as Elfd. tiqor points to a nasal root.

\*þerba- adj. 'tough(?)' - ON þjarfr adj. 'unleavened (of bread); fresh (of water); insipid, flat', OE þeorf adj. 'unleavened', OFri. therve adj. 'rough, fierce', OHG derb adj. 'unleavened', MHG derp adj. 'id.', G dial. derb adj. 'unleavened', Bav. 'dry' ⇒ \*terp-ō- (EUR) - Lith. tii'pti (tirpstu) 'to stiffen' < \*trp-ske-; Lat. torpere 'to be stiff, OCS u-tropeti 'id.' < \*trp-ehi-. An adjective to the root \*terp-. See also \*þurfan- 'to need'.

\*þerhwe - \*þurhwe prep., adv. 'through' - Go. flairh prep., adv. 'id.', OE per(i)h, pur(u)h, florh prep., adv. 'id.', E through, OFri. thriuch, thruch prep., adv. 'id.', NFri. troch prep., adv. 'id.', OS thurh, thoro, thuru prep., adv. 'id.', ODu. thuro prep., adv. 'id.', MDu. dare, dure, do(o)rch prep., adv. 'id.', Du. door prep., adv. 'id.', OHG durh, durah, duruh prep., adv. 'id.', G durch prep., adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*terh₂-h₃kw-e (IE) - Hitt. taragzi 'to overcome', Skt. tárati, -tirati 'to pass, cross over' < \*terh₂-e-, \*trh₂-e-; YAv. titarat 3sg.inj. 'to overcome' < \*ti-trh₂-e-; Hitt. tarna-i 'to let in' < IE \*tr-ne-h₂-; Skt. tiras prep. 'through, across, beyond', Lat. triins prep., adv. 'across, through', Olr. tar prep. 'across' < \*trh₂-ns; MW tarddu 'to emerge, issue, sprout, appear' < \*trh₂-ie-; Hitt. tarbuzi 'to prevail, be able', Skt. tilrvati 'to excell, overpower' < \*trh₂-u-e-. The Germanic preposition and adverb may have been formed with an element \*-kwe, cf. Skt. -ca in e.g. uccd 'from above, up' < \*ud-kweh₁, nicd 'downwards' < \*ni-h₃kweh₁, which ultimately goes back to the PIE word \*h₃ekw- 'eye'. The ablaut of e.g. OE per(i)h and flur(u)h results from an ar-

<!-- pdf-page: 579; section: dictionary -->

\*þerkōn- 539 chaic ablauting directional paradigm e.g. acc. \*terh₂-m, gen. \*trh₂-es (cf. Skt. tirasc& 'through' < \*trh₂-es-kweh₁), for which cf. \*aba 'from, off < \*h₂ep-ō vs. \*Jane 'from' < \*h₂p-o-neh₁. Further cf. OE þyrel, OHG durihhil, durchil, MHG diirchel 'perforated' < \*þUrhila- and \*þerkōn- 'hole' (q.v.). OHG derh adj. 'perforated' is a back-formation from the adverb (EWAhd: II, 881).

\*þerkon- f. 'hole' - Go. þairko f. 'hole' ⇒ \*terh₂-ueh₂- (DRY). An n-stem derived from \*þerhwe - \*þurhwe. The formation is likely to continue Pre-Gm. \*þerkkōn-, which possibly received its geminate in a Pre-Gm. paradigm \*terk-ōn, gen. \*t( e)rk-n-6s (Luhr 1988: 269). Alternatively, the \*k may have arisen from a laryngeal occurring after a liquid and before a \*u, e.g. in a ueh₂-stem \*terh₂-ueh₂- (cf. \*taikwer-).

\*þersan- s.v. 'to be dry' - Go. ga-þairsan s.v. 'to dry up, wither' ⇒ \*ters-e(IE) - Hitt. tarfri\* - tarfanzi 'to become dry', Gr. n:pooμm 'id.' < \*ters-e-. An old strong verb. See also \*þarzjan-, \*þurzu- and \*þurzjan-.

\*þeuba- m. 'thief - Go. þiubs m. 'id.', ON pj6fr m. 'id.', OE þeof m. 'id.', E thief, OFri. thiiifm. 'id.', OS thiofm. 'id.', Du. dief c. 'id.', OHG diob, diub m. 'id.', G Dieb m. 'id.' ⇒ \*teup-ō- (GM?) - Lith. tupJti (tupiu) 'to cower' < \*tup-eh₁-ie-. Etymology unclear. The formation OHG diuva, diuba f. 'id.', MHG diuve, diube f. 'id.', G obs. Deufe, Deube f. 'id.' points to a Verner alternation \*þeujjo- \*þeubjō- < \*teup-ieh₂-, and the underlying root \*teup- has by some been compared to Lith. tupJti 'to cower' (cf. \*stelan- 'to sneak; to steal'). Within Germanic, there may be a connection with Ice!. þufla w.v. 'to roam, wander; to stagger; to grope, fumble', Nw. tuvla w.v. 'to loiter' and G dial. dupp(el)n w.v. 'id.' (cf. Magnusson 1989: 1200), which together point to an iterative \*þupp/bOn- < \*tup-neh₂-.

\*þeudja- adj. 'favorable' - ON pyor adj. 'kind, affectionate, affable', OE ge-þiede adj. 'good, decent' ⇒ \*teuh₂-tiō- (IE?) - Lat. tutus adj. 'safe' < \*tuh₂-tō- (derived from Lat. tueor, tuor 'to look at, watch over, safeguard' < \*tuh₂-ehi-ie-); Gr. ow<;, oao<; adj. 'safe, healthy, intact' (a thematicization of \*oau<; < \*tueh₂-u- Beekes 2010: 1440-1). The word is usually connected to the PIE root \*teuh₂- 'strong', cf. Skt. ut taviti 'to be strong'. The appurtenance of OBret. tut 'good, favorable'(?) is more uncertain in view of the meanings of Olr. tUath adj. 'northern, left; perverse, wicked, evil' and OBret. tuthe 'demon'. Within Germanic, cf. the factitive Go. þiupjan w.v. 'to bless', ON pyoa w.v. 'to win over, make friends with', OE ge-þiedan 'to join, connect, associate' < \*þeudjan- and Go. þiuþ n. 'good'.

<!-- pdf-page: 580; section: dictionary -->

540 \*þeudō-

\*þeudō- f. 'nation, people' - Go. þiuda f. 'id.', ON pj6o f. 'id.', Far. tj6o f. 'id.', OE þeod f. 'id.', OFri. thiiide, thiede f. 'id.', OS thiod, thioda f. 'id.', OHG diota f. 'id.', MHG diet f. 'id.' ⇒ \*teut-eh₂- (EUR) - Osc. touto 'community', Olr. tuath f. 'people, tribe', W tud m. 'people, nation; region, district' < \*te/out-eh₂-; OPru. tauto, Lith. tauta f. 'land', Latv. tauta f. 'people' < \*tout-eh₂-. A European word: the alleged cognates Hitt tuzzi- 'army' (Feist 1924: 130) and Sogd. two'k 'crowd', NP toda 'heap, pile' (Schmid 1968: 10) are uncertain. PGm. \*þeudō- possibly continues an old neuter collective to OHG diot, MHG diet, MDu. diet m. '(free) man' < \*þeuda-. The word was associated with cultural and especially linguistic identity, and gave rise to derivations such as OS thiudisk, MDu. duutsc, dietsc, Du. duits, OHG diutisk adj. 'the people's language' < \*þeudiska- and ON pyoa 'to interpret, mean', OE ge-pleden 'to translate', OFri. bi-thiiida 'to explain', MDu. dieden, duden, Du. duiden 'to interpret, indicate'; OHG diuten, G deuten 'to interpret, explain' < \*þeudjan-. The latter word originally meant 'to vernacularize', whence the derived meaning 'to interpret, mean'. OCS (s)tuldb, Ru. cuz6j, SCr. tild adj. 'alien' < \*tjudjb was adopted from Germanic.

\*þeuha- n. 'thigh' - ON pj6 n. 'id.', Far. tj6gv n. 'id.', OE þeoh n. 'id.', E thigh, OFri. thiiich n. 'id.', MDu. die n./f. 'id.', Du. dij c. 'id.', OHG dioh n. 'id.' ⇒ \*teuHk-o- (EUR) - Lith. taukai, Latv. tauki m.pl. 'lard' OPru. taukis 'id.', OCS tuko, Ru. tuk m. 'fat' < \*touHk-o-; Olr. t6n f. 'behind, bottom', MW tin f./m. 'bottom, arse, rear' < \*tuHk-neh₂-. A European word for 'thigh, hip'.

\*þeura- m. 'bull' - ON pj6rr m. 'id.', Far. tj6rur m. 'castrated bull', OSw. piur m. 'bull', Du. dial. duur m. 'id.' (NIE) - Gr. -raupo<; m. 'bull', Lat. taurus m. 'id.', Lith. tailras m. 'id.', OCS tu/'"'b m. 'id.', Alb. ter 'id.'; Akk. silru, Arab. tawr, Hebr. s6r 'steer' < PSem. \*tawr; Etr. thevru-mines 'Minotaur'. A word with an etymological trajectory leading back to the Middle East rather than the IE homeland on the Pontic-Caspian steppes. I assume that a pre-Indo-European form \*þaur just as in Etruscan thevr- was raised to \*þeur before it was adopted by the speakers of Proto-Germanic, and gave rise to both \*þeura- and \*steura- (q.v.). Note that Limburgian duur, which technically looks like the regular correspondence of ON þj6rr, has been explained as a Norse loanword, as its geographic distribution coincides with the historical Viking settlement at Beek-Elsoo (Van Ginneken 1941: 313).

\*þeutan- - \*þūtan- s.v. 'to howl, roar' - Go. þut-haurn n. 'trumpet', ON pj6ta s.v. 'to howl, whistle, roar', Nw. tjota, tyta s.v. 'id.', OE þeotan, þiitan s.v. 'to howl', OHG diozan s.v. 'to roar', MHG diezen s.v. 'to roar, woosh' (DRY). A strong verb back-formed to the iterative \*tutt/don- (q.v.). No further etymology. The link with Lith. tiitUoti 'to blow' (cf. Fraenkel 1965: 1070) is

<!-- pdf-page: 581; section: dictionary -->

\*þewa- 541 formally and semantically possible, but the verb is likely to have been borrowed from Low German, cf. G tuten, Du. toet(er)en 'to toot'. The alleged link with Skt. tudciti 'to hit' < \*tud-e- (see \*stautan-) is erroneous, because the aforementioned iterative points to a Pre-Gm. root \*tudh- rather than \*tud-. This root may be onomatopoetic in origin (EWAhd: II, 690), cf. Lat. tuba f. 'trumpet' ( < \*tudh-ehr?).

\*þewa- m. 'servant' - Go. þius m. 'thrall, bondsman, servant', ON -Per þrop. 'id.', OE þeow, þeowa m. 'servant', ME theow 'id.', OS theo-lico adv. 'humble', Du. dee-moedig adj. 'id.', MHG deo m. 'servant' ⇒ \*teu-o- (GM). Also cf. Go. þiwi, ON py, pir, OS thiwi, OE þeowu, -e, OHG diu f. 'female servant' < \*þewf-, gen. -joz < \*teu-i(e)h₂- as well as \*þewen- 'to subdue'.

\*þewen- w.v. 'to subdue' - Go. ga-þiwan w.v. 'to subdue, subjugate', ON þjci w.v. 'to constrain; to enslave' (GM). The common opinion on the derivation of the verb is that it was derived from \*þewa- 'servant' (q.v.), and the verb indeed means 'to enslave' in both Gothic and Old English. However, the Old Norse verb pjci has an additional, more primary meaning 'to force' (e.g. hann var Pf cir ti/ vinnu 'he was forced to work'), which implies that the meaning 'to enslave' is secondary. This is further supported by OE ge-þeon 'to tame, oppress' < \*þewojan- and the marginally attested adjective OE þeow adj. 'subservient', OS thio-/iko adv. 'humble', OHG diu adj. 'unfree' < \*þewa- (Heidermanns 1993: 615-16), to which \*þewa- 'slave' may be a nominalization. It thus seems conceivable that at least ON pjci and OE ge-þeon were derived from the adjective in the primary sense 'bound, unfree, tamed'. This is a serious complication regarding the standard etymology of the word, which consists of a connection with the PIE root \*tekw- 'to run', cf. Skt. t<ikti 'to rush, fly', Av. tacaiti 'id.', Lith. tekJti (teku) 'to run, flow', OCS testi (tekp) 'id.', Olr. techid 'to flee' < \*tekw(-e)- (Pokorny IEW: 1059-60).

\*þīna- pron. 'your' - Go. þeins pron. 'id.', ON pinn pron. 'id.', Far. tin pron. 'id.', Elfd. denn pron. 'id.', OE þīn pron. 'id.', OFri. thfn pron. 'id.', OS thfn pron. 'id.', MDu. dijn pron. 'id.', OHG dfn pron. 'id.', G dein pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*t-iHno- (IE). The possessive pronoun pertaining to \*þū 'you' (q.v.). The suffix is likely to be identical to \*-fna- used in e.g. \*aizfna- 'iron' to \*aiza- ( q.v.).

\*þinan- s.v. 'to become moist' - OE þīnan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*tei-nh₁-e- (IE) - Hitt zeja(ri} - zejanta(ri) 'to cook, be cooked' < \*tieh₁-o, \*tihi-ōnto; Hitt zanu-zi 'to (make) cook' < \*tihrneu-; Olr. tinaid 'to disappear; to melt' < \*tihi-neh₂-. A nasal present to the root \*teihi-. The original, nasal-less past participle is preserved as ON pilJr adj. 'thawed' < \*þih₁-tō-, whence ON þilJna w.v. 'to

<!-- pdf-page: 582; section: dictionary -->

542 \*þingathaw' < \*þidnan-. Also cf. the secondary causative OE þā!nian w.v. 'to make wet' < \*þainjan-.

\*þinga- n. 'meeting, case, thing' - ON þing n. 'id.', OE þing n. 'id.', E thing, OFri. thing n. 'id.', OS thing n. 'thing, court, meeting', Du. ding n. 'thing', OHG ding n. 'meeting, court, thing, affair', G Ding n. 'thing' ⇒ \*tenk-ō- (DRV). Related to Got. peihs n. 'time', Lomb. thinx 'legal procedure, assembly' < \*þinhsa-. The original meaning may have been 'scheduled time', whence 'assembly', '(legal) case' and 'object'. As such, the word can be derived from the strong verb \*þinhan- 'to be suitable' (q.v.).

\*þingjan- w.v. 'to believe' - OHG dingan w.v. 'id.', MHG dingen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ti-n-k-ie- (NEUR) - Lith. tik&i (tikiu) 'to believe'. A Germanic-Baltic isogloss.

\*þinhan- s.v. 'to thrive, prosper' - Go. þeihan s.v. 'id.', OE þeon, (ge-)þingan s.v. 'id.', OS thihan s.v. 'id.', Du. gedijen w.v. 'id.', OHG dihan s.v. 'id.', G gedeihen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*tenk-e- (EUR) - Lith. tikti (tinku) 'to be good (for), to be suitable' < \*tnk-e- (also cf. tikras adj. 'right' and - with Ablautentgleisung - Lith. taikyti 'to arrange, fit'); Ukr. t'aknuty 'to be helpful' < \*tnk-neu-; Olr. tocad m. 'fortune, chance, good luck', MW tynghet f./m. 'destiny, fate' < \*tonk-eta-; OW tagc, MW tang, tanc m. 'peace' < \*tnk-o-. The original verb \*þinhan-, pret. 3sg. \*þanhe, 3pl. pungun gave rise to secondary \*þihan-, \*þaihe, pigun after the loss of the nasal before h. In Old English, the verbal paradigm was split into \*þihan- s.v.l and \*þingan- s.v.3, the latter being accompanied by the lexicalized past participle OE ge-pungen 'mature' (cf. OS gi-thungan 'respected', er-thungan 'dignified'). Also cf. OHG gi-dig n. 'progress' < \*dig-a-; MDu. dege m. 'growth, thrift' < \*digan-.

\*þinhla- n. 'curds(?)' - ON þel n. 'fresh-curded milk' ⇒ \*tenk-lo- (IE) - Skt. takra- n. 'buttermilk mixed with water' < \*tnk-1ō-. A Nordic word directly corresponding to Skt. takra-. The formation is usually compared to the etymological cluster of \*þinhtu- 'thick' (q.v.), and accordingly reconstructed as \*tenk-lo-. It can alternatively be linked to \*hwaja- Ƕ \*huja- 'whey' < \*tkw·oi-o-, \*tkw·i-o-, so as to arrive at a proto-form PIE \*tekw-/o-, PGm. \*þehla-.

\*þinhslo- f. 'drawbar, cart-pole' - ON þīsl f. 'cart-pole', OE þix/, þis/ f. 'beam or pole of a wagon', MLG disel, dissel f. 'shaft, drawbar', Du. disse/ c. 'id.', OHG dihsila f. 'id.', MHG dihsel f. 'id.', G Deichsel f. 'id.' ⇒ \*tenk-s-l-eh₂- (EUR) - Lat temo m. 'pole, yoke-beam' < \*tenk-s-mon-; OPru. teansis 'drawbar' < \*tenk-s-i-.

<!-- pdf-page: 583; section: dictionary -->

\*þinhtu- 543 A European word of unclear derivation. The connection with CS ras-tՙsti (ras-tՙgQ) 'to tear apart', SCr. -tegnuti 'to pull' < \*tngh- is improbable, as this root's original meaning was 'to be burdened', cf. \*þungu- 'heavy'.

\*þinhtu- adj. 'dense, tight, close' - ON þettr adj. 'id.', Far. tettur adj. 'id.', Elfd. ti'tt adj. 'id.', OE -piht adj. 'strong', ME thiht, tiht (influenced by MScand. tettr?) adj. 'dense, tight', E tight, WFri. ticht adj. 'id.', MDu. dichte adj. 'id.', Du. dicht adj. 'id.', G dicht, dial. deicht adj. 'tight, close, dense' ⇒ \*tenk-tu- (IE) - Close to Olr. techt adj. 'solidified' < \*tnk-to- (whence techtaid 'to freeze, congeal, coagulate' < \*tnk-t-eh₂-ie-); also cf. Skt. tanakti 'to draw together' < \*tn-n-ek-; Olr. techt adj. 'frozen' < \*tnk-to-; Lith. tankus adj. 'dense' < \*tonk-u-. Also cf. the adverb MHG gedichte, MDu. dicht(e), gedichte, bedichte 'close, continuous, often'. The co-occurence of a Nordic a-stem with a WGm. ja-stem probably points to an original u-stem. Related to \*þinhla- and \*þanhon-.

\*þinhwon- f. 'thunder' - Go. þeifvo f. 'id.' ⇒ \*tenk-ueh₂- (NEUR) - OCS tpca f. 'snowstorm' < \*tonk-ieh₂-.

\*þinkila- m. 'spelt' - OS thinkil m. 'id.', OHG dinkil m. 'spelt, wheat', G Dinkel m. 'spelt' (GM). No clear etymology. It has been suggested that the original meaning was 'prickle', thus allowing for a connection with Arm. t'akn 'club' (EWAhd: II, 657-8): uncertain.

\*þinna- - \*þunnō- f. 'forehead, temple' - MHG tinne, tunne f. 'forehead, temple' ⇒ \*tenh₂-uh₂- (IE). A word also occuring as the first member of some compounds with the meaning 'temple', cf. OHG tinna-bacho m., dun-wangi n., MHG tune-wenge n., OE pun-wang(e) f., ON pun-vangi m., Sw. tinning. The alternation of MHG tinna and tunna is remarkable; since both forms have the exact same meaning, it is unlikely that they represent two different formations. I therefore assume that they continue two ablauting stem variants of one single paradigm, e.g. nom. \*tinna, gen. \*tunnoz < PIE \*ten{h₂)-uh₂, \*tr;i(h₂)-ueh₂-s.

\*þinsan- s.v. 'to stretch' - Go. at-þinsan s.v. 'to draw towards, pull up to', OS thinsan s.v. 'to pull, tear', MDu. (E) dinsen w.v. 'to drag', OHG dinsan s.v. 'to pull, trail', MHG dinsen s.v. 'to pull, yank, trail', G gedunsen adj. 'bloated' ⇒ \*tens-e- (IE) - Skt. tatasre perf.3pl. 'they moved' < \*te-tns-; Lith. tၮsti (tၯsiu) 'to stretch' < \*tens-ie-; Lith. tqsus adj. 'tensile' < \*tons-u-.

<!-- pdf-page: 584; section: dictionary -->

544 \*þleuhan- A formation containing the verbal base \*tens-, which can be an extension of a more primary PIE base \*ten- (cf. \*þanjan- 'to stretch'). The strong verb probably gave rise to the iterative \*þanson- and the possibly ancient intensive formation \*þansjan- ( q.v.).

\*þleuhan- s.v. 'to flee' - Go. þliuhan s.v. 'id.', OE fleon s.v. 'id.', E to flee, OFri. flia s.v. 'to reconcile', OS fliohan s.v. 'to flee', MDu. vlien s./w.v. 'id.', OHG fliohan s.v. 'id.', Gfliehen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*tleuk-e- (GM). A Germanic word. Go. þliuhan is often assumed to have developed from \*fliuhan, but since not all initial clusters fl- are affected (cf. Go. flodus < \*flōdu-), the reality of this change is doubtful. Probably unrelated to \*fleugan- 'to fly'.

\*þragjan- w.v. 'to run' - Go. þragjan w.v. 'id.', OE prcegan w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*trogh-eie- (EUR) - To Gr. TpE:xw 'to run' < \*tregh-e-; Gr. Dor. Tpaxw 'id.', Olr. tethraig\* pret. 'ran away, receded' < PC!. \*te-trgh-; less attractive is the link with OCS tnk'b n. 'course, flight' < \*trkfwl.o-; Slav. trcati 'to run' < \*trkfwl.j-eh₂-. An intensive formation to a European root \*tregh- that with its combination of a plain and an aspirated stop violates the PIE root structure. Also cf. OHG drigil m. 'servant' < \*dregila-.

\*þraibjan- w.v. 'to seize' - ON þreifa w.v. 'to feel with the hand, touch', Icel. þreifa w.v. 'to touch, feel, finger' ⇒ \*troip-eie- (GM). An intensive-iterative formation to \*þrifan- (q.v.).

\*þrangwjan- w.v. 'to press' - ON þrengva w.v. 'to press, force', Far. treingja w.v. 'to push, press, urge; to need; to make narrow(er)', Elfd. traindja w.v. 'to push', MDu. drengen w.v. 'to make penetrate', MHG drengen w.v. 'to urge' ⇒ \*tronkw-eie- (NEUR). The causative to \*þrinhan- (q.v.). Far. treingja in the sense of 'to make narrow( er)' is a factitive to ON þrǫngr adj. 'narrow', MDu. drang adj. 'pressing' < \*þrangwa-.

\*þranha- adj. 'dirty' - !eel. þrár adj. 'rancid', Far. traur adj. 'id.', Nw. trri adj. 'id.', OE þroh adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*trōnli-o- (IE) - Lith. tresti (trࢭstu) 'to wither' < \*tre(n)li-; Lith. trqsa f. 'dung' < \*tronli-eh₂-; W trwnc m. 'urine', Bret. stroffilk m. 'excrement' < \*(s)tronli-o-. A European word, no doubt of IE origin in view of the root structure and the occurrence of a palatovelar. Also cf. OE þroh 'rancor'.

\*þrapp/bōn- w.v. 'to trot' - Elfd. trrivri w.v. 'id.', OE þrafian w.v. 'to urge, press', OS thra'bon w.v. 'to trot', EDu. draven, drabben 'id.', Du. draven, dial.

<!-- pdf-page: 585; section: dictionary -->

\*þrastu- 545 drabben 'id.', MHG draben w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*trop-neh₂- (EUR) - Formally identical to Lith. trapinhi 'to kick' < \*trop-n(e)h₂-; also cf. OPru. trapt 'to trample'; Ru. dial. tropat' 'to trample, stamp' < \*trop-ehrie-; Ru. tropa f. 'path' < \*trop-eh₂and Gr. TpmtE:w 'to press (grapes)' < \*trp-eie-. An old iterative formation \*trop-neh₂-. Also cf. E thrave 'herd' < \*þrabo-, which is formally identical to Ru. tropa < \*trop-eh₂-. The original meaning of the root was 'to trample'.

\*þrastu- m. 'thrush' - ON prpstr m. 'id.', Far. trustur m. 'id.', Elfd. trast m. 'id.', OE þrysce, prcesce f. 'id.', E thrush, OHG drosca f. 'id.', G Droschel f. 'id.' ⇒ \*trozd-u- (EUR) - Arm. tordik 'thrush' < \*dorzdh-; Lat. turdus m. 'id.' < \*torzd-o-, Mir. truit, truid f. 'id.' < \*trozd-i-; Lith. striizdas m. 'id.' < \*strozd-o-; Ru. drozd, SCr. drozd m. 'id.' < \*drozd-o-. The Nordic form \*þrastu- straightforwardly goes back to the widespread o-grade root \*trozd-, but the derivation of the corresponding forms in West Germanic is highly problematic. OHG drosca together with OE priksce is taken to represent a form \*þrauskon-, whereas OE þrysce, E thrush is derived from \*þriiskjon-. However, since the vocalisms of these forms cannot possibly be explained from the European base \*trozd-, it is tempting to alternatively reconstruct \*þraskonon the basis of OE prcesce, and \*þrusk(j)on- on the basis of OHG drosca and OE þrysce. The form \*þraskōnis corroborated by Far. treska f. 'thrush (candidiasis)', and straightforwardly continues Pre-Gm. \*trozd-k-eh₂- (with \*-zdk- > \*-zg- > \*-sk-), apparently a diminutive to the original word. The form \*þruskon-, since it is restricted to Germanic, is likely to represent some kind of secondary zero-grade formation, quasi-PIE \*trzd-k-eh₂-.

\*þrautjan- w.v. 'to exhaust' - ON preyta w.v. 'to strive, struggle', Far. troyta w.v. 'to complete; to drag on, exert one's strength; to exhaust' ⇒ \*troud-eie(NEUR). The causative to \*þreutan- (q.v.).

\*þrawjan- w.v. 'to grow' - OHG trowwen s./w.v. 'to grow (up)', MHG ge-drouwen past ptc. 'grown' ⇒ \*trōuH-ie- (IE). An o-grade ie-verb closely related to \*þrewan- 'to grow, thrive' (q.v.). OHG trowwen cannot possibly continue a causative \*þrawjan- from PIE \*troHu-eie- (thus Muller 2007: 311-2), as the development \*-ōu- > \*-ō- preceded pretonic shortening, cf. \*ajja- 'egg' < \*h₂ōu-iō-. MHG druo f. 'fruit' goes back to \*droo-, and may continue a root noun \*trouH-s, \*truH-es.

\*þrawō- f. 'longing, suffering' - ON þrá f. 'longing, yearning', OE prea f. 'rebuke, threat; punishment; infliction', OS thrii-werk n. 'pain', OHG drawa, drowa, dr6a f. 'threat, blame, burden', MHG dro(u)we, dr6 f. 'threat' 

<!-- pdf-page: 586; section: dictionary -->

546 \*þre an-

\*trouH-eh₂- (NEUR) - Lith. triv6ti (triv6ju) 'to endure' < \*truH-ehi-ie- (with a secondary zero-grade +?). In ablaut relation to \*þrōen- (q.v.). Also cf. the denominal ON þrá w.v. 'to long', OS gi-throon w.v. 'to threaten' < \*þrawojan- and ON preyja w.v. 'to long', OHG drewen, drouwen, G drohen w.v. 'to threaten' < \*þraujan-. ON þrár adj. 'stubborn, obstinate' < \*þrawa- < \*trouH-o- seems to be more primary.

\*þrēan- s.v. 'to turn' - OE þrāwan s.v. 'to twist, turn; to throw', E to throw, OFri. drii.ia w.v. 'to turn', Du. draaien w.v. 'id.', OHG drii.en s.v. 'id.', G drehen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*treh₁-e (EUR) - Gr. TETpa[vw 'to pierce, perforate' (also cf. TEpETpov n. 'auger, borer' < \*terhi-tro-); Lat. tero 'to rub' < \*terh₁-e-; Lith. trinti (trinit) 'to rub, file' < \*tr-n-h₁-e-. A strong verb derived from a root \*treh₁-, apparently a variant of the more wide-spread \*terh₁-. See also \*þrēdu-.

\*þrēdu- m. 'thread' - ON þrāðr m. 'id.', OE þrǣd m. 'id.', E thread, OFri. thred m. 'id.', MDu. draet m. 'id.', Du. draad c. 'id.', OHG drii.t m. 'id.', G Draht m. 'id.' (ORV). A tu-stem derived from the strong verb \*þrēan- ( q.v.).

\*þreutan- s.v. 'to bother' - Go. us-þriutan s.v. 'to harass, bother, persecute', ON þrjóta s.v. (imp.) 'to fail, come to an end, become exhausted', Far. tr6ta s.v. 'id.', OE þreotan s.v. 'to weary', MLG dreten, vor-dreten s.v. 'to annoy', Du. ver-drieten 'to make sad', OHG bi-driozan s.v. (imp.) 'to weary, trouble', G ver-drieflen s.v. 'to annoy' ⇒ \*treud-e- (NEUR) - OCS truzditi (truzdp) 'to toil, harass' < \*troud-eie-; OCS trud'b 'travail' < \*troud-o-. A strong verb to a Germanic-Slavic root \*treud-. See also \*þrautjan- and \*þrutton-.

\*þrewan- s.v. 'to grow, thrive' - OHG driuwan s.v. 'to thrive' ⇒ \*treuH-e(IE) - OAv. Braostii. 2pl.aor. and YAv. tutJruiie 3sg.perf.med. 'to nourish' < \*treu(H)-. A strong verb postulated on the basis of the OHG gl. triuuit 'excellet, þō/let, floret. Related to \*þrawjan-, \*þrūwen- and \*þruþi- (q.v.). Also cf. OHG driuwi adj. 'energetic, strong' < \*þrewja-.

\*þrī- num. 'three' - Go. þreis\*, acc. þrins, n. þrija, gen. þrije, dat. þrim num. 'id.', ON þrīr, acc. þrjá, f. þrjár, n. þrjú, gen. þriggja, dat. Prim(r}, prem(r) num. 'id.', Far. triggir, acc. trfggjar, f. trfggjar, n. try, gen. trfggja, dat. trimum n. 'id.', Elfd. trair, acc. tril/O, f. trjdr, n. tray, dat. trim num. 'id.', OE pr!, f./n. prfo, Preo num. 'id.', E three, OFri. thre num. 'id.', OS thria, threa, n. thriu num. 'id.', Du. drie num. 'id.', OHG drf, drfe f. drfo, n. driu num. 'id.', G drei num. 'id.' ⇒ \*trei- (IE) - ToA tre, f. tri, ToB trai, trey, f. tarya num. 'id.', Skt. trayas, f.

<!-- pdf-page: 587; section: dictionary -->

\*þridjan- 547 tisras, n. trf num. 'id.', YAv. t9riiii6, f. tiSro, n. t9ri num. 'id.', Gr. -rpET<;, n. -rpta num. 'id.', Lat. tres, n. tria num. 'id.', Olr. trf, tri, f. teoir, n. tre num. 'id.', MW tri, f. teir num. 'id.', Lith. trys, OCS trbje, f. tri num. 'id.'. The IE numeral 'three'. The inflected forms are to be reconstructed as nom. \*þriz, acc. prins, f. \*þrioz, n. \*þrio, gen. \*þrijjan, dat. primiz < Pre-Gm. nom. \*trei-es, \*trei-eh₂-es, \*trei-eh₂, gen. \*trei-om, dat. \*tri-mis, acc. \*tri-ns.

\*þridjan- num. 'third' - Go. þridja num. 'id.', ON þriði num. 'id.', Far. trioi num. 'id.', Elfd. trio num. 'id.', OE þridda num. 'id.', E third, OFri. thredda num. 'id.', OS thriddio num. 'id.', Du. derde num. 'id.', OHG dritto num. 'id.', G dritte num. 'id.' ⇒ \*tri-tiHo- (IE) - Skt. qtfya-, Av. t9ritiia-, Lat. tertius, W trydydd num. 'id.' < \*tr(i)-tiHo-; Lith. treCias, Latv. tress, OCS tretii, Ru. tretij, SCr. treCinum. 'id.' < \*tre-ti(H)o-; OPru. tirts num. 'id.' < \*tr-to-(?). The ordinal number pertaining to \*þrī- 'three' (q.v.). The Germanic attestations point to \*tri-tiHo-, but the oldest PIE form probably did not have an i in the root, cf. Skt. trtfya- < \*tr-tiH-o-.

\*þrifan- s.v. 'to seize' - ON þrifa s.v. 'to catch, grasp', refl. 'to thrive, grow', Far. trfva s.v. 'to seize, grip, grasp', Nw. triva s.v. 'to snatch' ⇒ \*treip-e- (GM). An exclusively Nordic word; ME thrifen, E to thrive was probably borrowed from ON þrifa-st 'to thrive, grow' (for the meaning, cf. ON taka-st refl. 'to succeed'). See also \*þraibjan- and \*þripp/bōn-.

\*þrinhan- s.v. 'to press' - Go. þreihan s.v. 'to press, crowd', ON þryngva s.v. 'to press, thrust; to rush', Nw. trenga, tr¢nga s.v. 'to need', OE þringan s.v. 'to press, crowd throng', OS thringan s.v. 'to press, urge', Du. dringen s.v. 'id.', OHG dringan s.v. 'id.', G dringen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*trenkw·e- (NEUR) - Lith. treiikti (trenkiu) 'to bump, hit' < \*trenk-ie-. The original verb \*þrinhan- was replaced by \*þringan- in West Germanic. ON þryngva < \*þringwan- points to a root \*trenkw- with a labiovelar. See also \*þrangwjan-.

\*þripp/bōn- w.v. 'to seize' - ON þrifa w.v. 'to seize', Icel. þrifla w.v. 'to touch, feel, finger', Nw. trip/a w.v. 'to finger, touch upon', trivia w.v. 'to grope, feel tentatively', ODa. threve 'to seize', Da. treve, trreve, trave 'to fumble, grope' ⇒ \*trip-nehr (GM). The iterative to \*þrifan- (q.v.). The appurtenance of G Pal. trippen w.v. 'to gulp' and Rhnl. ver-drippen w.v. 'to devour' is uncertain.

\*þrōdjan- w.v. 'to train(?)' - Go. þropjan w.v. 'to exercise, train, drill' (EUR?) - MW trawd adj. 'weak' < \*troH-to-; Lith. tr6tinti 'to irritate' < \*troHt-nehr. Formation unclear. If MW trawd belongs here, the verb could be a factitive to a lost adjective \*fm5da- 'exhausted'.

<!-- pdf-page: 588; section: dictionary -->

548 \*þrōen-

\*þrōen- w.v. 'to endure' - OE f:m5wian w.v. 'to endure', OHG druoen w.v. 'to suffer' (NEUR) - Lith. triv6ti (triv6ju) 'to endure' < \*truH-eh₂-ie- (with a secondary zero-grade -i-). A stative probably derived from a lost adjective \*/m5a-, cf. OHG druoa f. 'burden' < \*þroo-. Related to \*þrawō- (q.v.).

\*þrūban- m. 'cluster' - MDu. drubbel m. 'cluster (of plants)' ⇒ \*trup-on(IE) - Hitt. tarupzi - taruppanzi 'to collect; to unite'. MDu. drubbel continues a form \*þrubla-, a diminutive created to an n-stem \*þrubo, gen. \*þruppaz < \*trup-ōn-, \*-n-ōs. The attestation is limited, but VLat. troppus 'flock' (cf. Fr. trop 'too much') is likely to have been borrowed from Franconian, and thus confirms the reality of the Germanic n-stem. In addition, there is OHG trubo, G Traube, Du. druif 'grape' < \*þrūban-, which no doubt belongs here, too, especially in view of the second meaning of MDu. druve m. 'grape; grape cluster'. The two formations have not yet received a convincing etymology, but I would like to connect Hitt. tarupzi - taruppanzi 'to collect', which by Kloekhorst 2008: 850-2 is derived from a root present \*treup-ti, \*trup-enti. The long \*u of \*þrūban- must then be secondary, probably an analogical full-grade. OHG drappo, MHG drappe, trappe m. 'cluster, bunch' was remodeled after OHG krappo 'racemus', a Romance loan, cf. It.grappo 'grape' (EWAhd: II, 756).

\*þrunhta- m. 'hardship(?)' - ON þróttr m. 'stamina, strength', OE þroht m. 'oppression, hardship' (GM). A tu-stem probably related to OE ge-þrīn w.v. 'to contrain, restrain, repress' < \*þruhjan-. Also cf. OE þroht adj. 'oppressive, grievous' and ON i-pr6tt f. 'skill, accomplishment'.

\*þrutton- w.v. 'to exhaust' - G dial. trotzen w.v. 'to over-exploit' (DWB: 22, 1115) ⇒ \*trud-neh:r (NEUR). The iterative to \*þreutan- (q.v.).

\*þrūþi- f. 'strength' - ON þrūð- 'id.', OE þrȳð f. 'id.' ⇒ \*truH-ti- (DRY). Usually assumed to be a ti-stem to the verbs \*þrewan- and \*þrawjan(q.v.). Also cf. ON pruoigr, -ugr adj. 'strong, powerful'.

\*þrūwen- w.v. 'to thrive, mature' - ON þrōa-st w.v. 'to increase, grow' ⇒ \*truH-eh₂-ie- (DRY). A denominal formation to an unattested zero-grade adjective \*þru(w)a- < \*truH-o-, cf. ON proskr 'vigorous, mature, full-grown' < \*þru(w}iska- and proski m. 'maturity, manhood; promotion, advancement' < \*þru(w}iskan-. The reconstructions \*þroiska- (Torp 1909: 193; Muller 2007: 312) and \*þruska- (Pokorny IEW: 1095) would point to proto-forms \*troHu-isk-o-

<!-- pdf-page: 589; section: dictionary -->

\*þū 549 and \*tru-sk-o-, which cannot be reconciled with the PIE root \*truH-, as supported by the related \*þrawjan-, \*þrewan- and \*þrūþi- (q.v.). Also cf. early G druhen, Swi. trii(e)hen, triijen 'to thrive' < \*dn5jan-.

\*þū pron. 'you' - Go. þu, dat þus, acc. þuk 'id.', ON þu, dat per, acc. pik pron. 'id.', Far. tu, obi. teg pron. 'id.', Elfd. du, obi. dig pron. 'id.', OE pit, obi. pe, acc. pee, pe pron. 'id.', OFri. thu, obi. thi pron. 'id.', OS thu, obi. thf, acc. thik pron. 'id.', MDu. du, obi. dij pron. 'id.', OHG du, dat. dir, acc. dih pron. 'id.', G du, dat dir, acc. dich pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*tuH (IE) - Hitt zik, acc. tuk < \*ti-ge, \*tu-ge; ToA tu, ToB tuwe, Skt tvam, acc. tv&m, OAv. tuuam, acc. -8{3qm < \*tuH-(H)om, \*tue-(H)om; Gr. m'.l, acc. crf. < \*tuH, \*tue; Arm. du, Alb. ti, Lat. tit, Olr. ru, OW ti, MW ti, OCS ty, Ru. ty, SCr. ti, OPru. tu, tou, toil, Lith. tU pron. 'id.' < \*tuH. The IE pronoun of the second person. In the nominative, the form was \*tuH, which in view of the similarity with the plural \*iuH (see \*jitz) may have arisen due to analogy: Hitt. zik taken at face value at any rate rather points to \*tiH (Kloekhorst 2008). The accusative is usually reconstructed as \*þike < \*te-ge (with the emphatic suffix \*ge), but Go. þuk together with Hitt tuk may represent more a primitive form \*tu-ge. The dative can be reconstructed as \*þiz < \*tes(i).

\*þuftōn- f. 'thwart, rowing bench' - ON þofta f. 'id.', OE þoft, þofte f. 'id.', MLG ducht f. 'thwart', MDu. dochte f. 'id.', Du. doft c. 'id.', OHG dofta f. 'id.' ⇒ \*tup-t-eh₂- (GM?) - Lith. tupti (tupiu), Latv. tupt 'to squat down' < \*tup-ie-; Lith. tupJti 'to cower' < \*tup-ehr. No certain etymology; the connection with the Baltic forms is possible, but semantically uncompelling. Also cf. ON þofti, OE ge-þofta, OHG gi-dufto m. '(rowing) mate' < \*ga-puftan-.

\*þuka/ōn- m./f. 'fog, mist' - ON þoka f. 'id.', Far. toka f. 'id.', MLG dake m. 'id.', Du. dial. dook m. 'id.' (GM). An exclusively Germanic word: the only serious extra-Germanic comparandum, Latv. tuce 'cloud', looks like a loanword from Slav. \*t9ca 'cloud, rain' (for which see \*þinhwon- 'thunder'). Within Germanic, further cf. OE puxian w.v. 'to make misty' < \*þuskōjan-, apparently derived from an unattested noun \*þuk-ska-, and OSw. thukna, thekn, Sw. tockna f., tocken n./f. 'mist' < \*þukina/ō- or \*þaukina/ō- (Hellquist 1922: 1051). The unrelated Far. tjukn f. 'darkening of the sky; thicket', brodda-tjukn f. 'heavy shower', Nw. dial. tjukkn f. 'fog' < \*þekwino- was derived from ON þykkr, þjukkr adj. 'thick' < \*þeku- (q.v.).

\*þuk(k)ōn- w.v. 'to shove' - ON þokka w.v. 'to move', Ice!. pok(k)a w.v. 'to move slowly/slightly; to fall back, make room for', Far. toka w.v. 'to force, press, push, advance', Nw. tok(k)a w.v. 'to move, shove', OE pocerian w.v. 'to

<!-- pdf-page: 590; section: dictionary -->

550 \*þulirun to and fro, run about' ⇒ \*tuk-neh₂- (IE) - Gr. wKoc; m. 'hammer' < \*tuk-o-; RuCS is-tbknpti 'to dig out', SCr. taknuti 'to push' < \*tuk-ne-. An iterative 3sg. \*þukkopi, 3pl. \*þugunanpi that I connect to the PIE root \*teuk- 'to shove'. It gave rise to the secondary strong verb \*teukan- as continued by Nw. tjuka w.v. 'to grope (in the dark); to push forth'.

\*þuli- m. 'reciter' - ON þulr m. 'sage', OE þyle m. 'orator, spokesman' (IE?) - To Hitt. tulija- (c.) 'gathering, assembly' < \*tul-io-? Related to ON pylja w.v. 'to speak, recite, murmur' < \*þuljan- and OE ge-pyll 'breeze' < \*þulja-. The etymological cluster exhibits meanings such as 'to talk', 'to rattle off but also 'to sing'. It has therefore been connected with Hitt tallija-zi 'to pray to, to evoke (a deity)' (Polome 1975: 661-2), thus pointing to a root \*t/H- possibly connected with IE oral tradition (Brink 2005: 88). The Hittite verb is usually compared to PGm. \*taljan- (for which see \*ta/a-), however, and is incompatible with the ablauting \*þau/a{n)- < \*toul-o{n)-. I therefore tentatively connect Hitt tu/ija- ( c.) 'gathering, assembly'. PGm. \*þuljan- would then have developed from PIE \*tuHl-ie- by pretonic shortening.

\*þulla- m. 'thole, peg in the side of a boat to keep the oar in place' - Ice!. þollur m. 'peg; thole', Far. tollur m. 'tholepin', Nw. toll(e) m. 'thole, tholepin', Da. to/ c. 'id.', OE þoll m. 'id.', MLG do/le, du/le f. 'id.', MDu. do/le f. 'id.', Du. do/ c. 'id.' ⇒ \*tul-nō- (EUR) - Gr. wt.oc; m. 'bulge, callosity, hunch, nail, peg'; Lith. tu/is f. 'nail on a car, plug, nail of an axle'. Also cf. ON pollr m. 'fir, tree' < \*þulia- and the related ON pp/I, Elfd. toll f. 'fir-tree' < \*þa/lo-.

\*þuman- m. 'thumb' - Ice!. þumi m. 'id.', Far. tummi m. 'inch', Nw. tomme m. 'id.', OSw. þumi m. 'thumb; inch', Sw. tumme c. 'id.', ODa. thumCE m. 'id.', Da. tomme c. 'id.', OE þuma m. 'id.', E thumb, OFri. thuma m. 'id.', MLG dum(e) m. 'id.', MDu. dume m. 'id.', Du. duim c. 'id.', OHG dumo m. 'id.', G Daumen m. 'id.' ⇒ \*tum-on- (IE?) - Skt. tumra- adj. 'strong, big' < \*tum-rō-; Lat. tumeo, -ere 'to be swollen', Lith. tumhi 'to coagulate' < \*tum-ehi-ie-; W twf 'strength' < \*tumo-. The standard etymology of this word links it with the PIE root \*teum- 'to swell', which is inspired by the alleged Benennungsmotiv of the thumb as "the big finger". More straightforward is the connection with the obviously related MHG doum m. 'peg, chock' < \*þauma-. Note that the variation of NGm. \*þuman- vs. WGm. \*þuman- must be a Germanic innovation; the \*u probably arose secondarily as an analogical full grade. Also cf. OSw. th um n. 'inch', EDu. dom 'thumb' < \*þuma- and ON, Ice!. pumall, Far. tummil, Nw., Da. tom me/ 'thumb (of a glove') < \*þuma/a-.

<!-- pdf-page: 591; section: dictionary -->

\*þuna- 551

\*þuna- adj. 'stretched' - MDu. done, duene adj. 'tight, close', MHG don adj. 'stretched out' ⇒ \*tnh₂-(n)o- (IE). Closely related to \*þunnu- (q.v). The adjective gave rise to the stative verb OE þunian w.v. 'to stretch, swell up', OHG donen, MHG donen w.v. 'to stretch oneself < \*þunen-.

\*þungu- adj. 'heavy' - ON þungr adj. 'id.', Far. tungur adj. 'id.', Elfd. tungg adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*tngh-u- (NEUR) - Identical to Lith. tingus adj. 'lazy' < \*tngh-u-; also cf. Lith. tingti (tingstu) 'to become slow' < \*tngh-ske-; CS ras-tՙsti 'to tear apart' < \*tngh-ie- (cf. OCS tՙgota f. 'burden' < \*tngh-ot-eh₂-); Ru. tjaga f. 'traction', SCr. teg m. 'weight, traction' < \*tngh-o/eh₂-. A Germanic-Baltic isogloss. Also cf. ON pyngja w.v. 'to weigh down, make heavy; to grow worse' < \*þungjan-.

\*þunhjan- w.v. 'to press' - Far. tjggja w.v. 'to be hard on', OE ge-pywan w.v. 'to press, impel, urge', MDu. duwen, douwen w.v. 'to push, press', Du. duwen, reg. douwen w.v. 'to push', MHG diuhen, diihen s.v. 'to push, press down' ⇒ \*tunk-ie- (IE) - Gr. oanw, Ion. oaoow 'to stuff, compress, pack, load, equip' < \*tui;,ik-ie-. A very old ie-present related to \*þwinhan- (q.v.). Also cf. Ice!. pua w.v. 'to press (down) on' < \*þunhon-.

\*þunkjan- w.v. 'to think' - Go. þugkjan w.v. 'to have the impression, suppose, appear', ON þykkja w.v. 'to deem, think', Far. tykja w.v. 'to seem, appear; to think, believe', Elfd. tyttja w.v. 'to think; to like', OE þyncan w:v. 'to seem, appear', E to think, OFri. thinka, thinza w.v. 'to seem, think', OS thunkian w.v. 'consider', Du. dunken w.v. 'to think, deem', OHG dunken w.v. 'to deem, think, mean', G diinken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*tng-ie- (IE). A zero-grade formation related to \*þankjan-. The verb could occur with a dative subject, cf. Go. 1-lJa izwis pugkeip? 'What think ye?' (Mk 14:64). This construction indicated the lack of control or agentivity as compared to \*þankjan- 'to think' (q.v.), which was more active.

\*þunkōn- w.v. 'to make wet' - OHG dunkon w.v. 'to dip', MHG dunken, tunken w.v. 'id.', G tunken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*tng-neh₂- (EUR) - Gr. -rfyw 'to make wet', Lat. tingo, -ere 'to wet, imbue, dye' < \*teng-e-. An iterative to a lost strong verb \*þinkan- 'to make wet' (cf. Mikeleitis-Winter 2001: 196). Also cf. Swi. tink 'moist' < \*þinka- < \*teng-o-.

\*þunnu- adj. 'thin' - ON þunnr adj. 'id.', Far. tunnur adj. 'id.', Elfd. tunn adj. 'id.', OE þynne adj. 'id.', E thin, MDu. dunne adj. 'id.', Du. dun adj. 'id.', OHG dunni adj. 'id.', G diinn adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*tnh₂-u- (IE) - Skt. tanu(ka)- adj. 'thin, slender, small, fine', NP tanuk adj. 'thin, flat', OCS thnok'b, Ru. t6nkij, SCr.

<!-- pdf-page: 592; section: dictionary -->

552 \*þurfantiinak adj. 'thin, slender, fine'; Lith. (Zem.) tࢮvas, Latv. tievs adj. 'thin, slender', Gr. Tava6<;, Tavu- adj. 'thin, slender, elongated, expanding far', Lat. tenuis adj. 'thin, fine, delicate', Oir. tanae adj. 'delicate, thin' < \*tnh₂-u(\*tnh₂-u-ko-). An IE u-stem replaced by a ja-stem in WGm. The stem \*þunnu- was generalized from a paradigm \*þ(e)nuz, gen. \*þunnaz < \*t(e)nh₂-u-s, \*tnh₂-u-ōs. Also cf. \*þuna-.

\*þurfan- pret.-pres. 'to need' - Go. þaurban pret.-pres. 'id.', ON þurfa pret.-pres. 'id.', Far. poet. turva pret.-pres. 'id.', OE purfan pret.-pres. 'to be wanton', OFri. thurva pret.-pres. 'id.', OS thurban pret.-pres. 'id.', MDu. dorven pret.-pres. 'to need; to dare', Du. durven w.v. 'to dare', OHG durfan pret.-pres. 'id.', G diirfen pret.-pres. 'to be allowed to' ⇒ \*trp- (IE) - Skt. tfpyati 'to delight, satisfy' < \*trp-ie-; Gr. TEprrw 'to satisfy, to refresh' < \*terp-e-; OCS tnpeti 'to suffer, endure' < \*trp-ehr; Lith. taf'pti (tarpstu) 'to thrive, to increase' < \*torp-ske-; OPru. en-terpo 'is useful'. Most IE languages point to a meaning 'to be satisfied', but PGm. \*þurfanmay have preserved a more primitive meaning 'to use'. OCS treb'b adj. 'necessary' must in view of the root-final b have been adopted from PGm. \*þerba-. This adjective primarily meant 'unleavened' in the NWGm. languages, but ON þjarfr is also attested with the meaning 'destitute' (cf. Grondal 1864: 145).

\*þurfti- f. 'need' - Go. þaurfts f. 'id.', ON þurft f. 'id.', OS thurft, thuruft f. 'id.', OHG durft f. 'id.', MHG durft f. 'need, want, necessity' ⇒ \*trp-ti- (IE/DRV) - Skt. tfpti- f. 'satisfaction'. A ti-stem derivation to \*þurfan- (q.v.).

\*þurisa- m. 'giant' - ON þurs m. 'id.', Far. poet. tussur m. 'id.', Nw. tuss m. 'id.', OE þyrs m. 'id.', OHG durs, duris m. 'id.', MHG diirse, turse, tiirse, tiirsch m. 'id.' (GM). One of the Proto-Germanic words for 'giant', cf. Fi. turisas 'god of war; sea monster'. It gave rise to Ice!. pursi, Far. tussi, Nw. tusse, EDa. tusse, tosse 'giant' < \*þurisan-. No clear etymology. The standard connection with ON þyrja 'to rush' < \*þurjan- (q.v.) is formally possible, but semantically uncompelling. Perhaps rather related to Icel. pursi m. 'quarrel, anger, rage'.

\*þurjan- w.v. 'to sweep' - ON þyrja w.v. 'to sweep, rush' ⇒ \*turH-ie- (IE) - Gr. aaipw 'to sweep' < \*turH-ie-. An apparently PIE ie-present closely related to \*þweran- ( q.v.).

\*þurna- m. 'thorn, briar' - Go. þaurnus m. 'id.' ( < \*þurnu-), ON þorn m. 'id.', OE þorn m. 'id.', E thorn, OFri. thorn m. 'id.', ODu. thorn m. 'id.', Du. doorn c.

<!-- pdf-page: 593; section: dictionary -->

\*þurpa- 553 'id.', OHG dorn m. 'id.', G Dorn m. 'id.' ⇒ \*tr-no- (IE) - Skt. tfTJa- n. 'grass, blade of grass, herb', OCS tron'b m. 'thorn', Ru. tern m. 'blackthorn, sloe', SCr. tfn m. 'id.'. An old no-stem to a PIE root \*ter-. In view of the extra-Germanic forms, it is likely that the Go. u-stem is secondary. Also cf. OE pyrne f. 'briar' < \*þurnjo- and pyrnet n. 'thorn-bush thicket' < \*þurnatja-.

\*þurpa- n. 'crowd(?)' - Go. þaurp n. 'farmland', ON þorp n. 'isolated settlement', Nw. tarp n. 'group; small settlement', OE þorp, þrop m. 'crowd', OFri. therp, thorp n. 'village; (fallow) land', OS thorp n. 'id.', Du. dorp n. 'id.', OHG dorfn. 'id.', G Dorf n. 'id.' ⇒ \*trb-o- (EUR) - Lith. trobd f. 'cottage, farmhouse', Latv. traba f. 'hut, hovel' < \*trob-eh₂-; Oir. treb f. 'hamlet, village', MW tref f. 'town' < \*treb-eh₂-. The received connection of \*þurpa- with the aforementioned forms meaning 'hamlet' is straightforward. Within Germanic, the word cannot be dissociated from ON pyrpast 'to crowd, throng', Far. tyrpa seg 'to crowd, flock' < \*þurpjan-, however, which implies that Germanic preserved the original meaning 'to gather'. The link with Hitt. tarupzi ` taruppanzi 'to collect, to unite' (Holma 1916: 36) cannot be maintained, however, as this verb goes back to a root \*trup- rather than \*turb- (Kloekhorst 2008: 850-2). Gr. wp,TJ f. 'confusion, noise, tumult' and Lat. turba f. 'commotion' < \*turb-eh₂are not related either.

\*þurska- m. 'cod' - ON þorskr m. 'id.', Far. toskur m. 'id.', Elfd. tuosk m. 'id.', MDu. dorsch m. 'id.', G Dorsch m. 'id.' ⇒ \*trsk-o- (NEUR) - Ru. treskci, Slov. tfska f. 'cod' < \*trsk-eh₂-. If Ru. treskci is not a Scandanavian loanword (thus Magnusson 1989: 1188), we are probably dealing with a Germanic-Slavic isogloss.

\*þurstu- m. 'thirst' - Elfd. tuost f. 'id.', OE þurst, þyrst m. 'id.', E thirst, OS thurst m. 'id.', Du. dorst c. 'id.', OHG durst m. 'id.', G Durst m. 'id.' ⇒ \*trs-tu(IE) - Olr. tart m. 'dryness, drought' < \*trs-tu-. A tu-stem derived from the verb \*þersan- (q.v.). It was replaced by a ti-stem in Old English, and by an n-stem in ON porsti, Far. tosti m. 'thirst' < \*þurstan- (for a similar transfer, see \*lustu-). Further cf. the denominal impersonal verb ON pyrsta, OE pyrstan, OS thurstian, OHG dursten, G diirsten w.v. 'to be thirsty' < \*þurstjan-.

\*þurzēn- w.v. 'to be dry' - Sw. dial. torra w.v. 'id.', OS thorron w.v. 'to waste away', Du. ver-dorren w.v. 'to wither', OHG dorren, dorren w.v. 'to dry, wither, stiffen', G ver-dorren w.v. 'to wither' ⇒ \*trs-eh₁- (IE) - Formally identical to Lat. torreo, -ere 'to scorch, roast'. A stative formation to \*þersan- or \*þurzu- ( q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 594; section: dictionary -->

554 \*þurzjan-

\*þurzjan- w.v. 'to be thirsty' - Go. þaursjan w.v. 'id.', OE pyrreo p.ptc. 'dried out' ⇒ \*trs-ie- (IE) - Identical to Skt tf$yati 'to be thirsty'. An old ie-present ON pyrstr adj. 'thirsty' has been interpreted as the original past participle \*þursipa- of this verb, but it is more likely in view of its \*s that it represents the past participle to \*þurstjan- (for which cf. \*þurstu-). See also \*þurzu- and \*þurzēn-.

\*þurzu- adj. 'dry' - Go. þaursus adj. 'id.', ON þurr, þyrr adj. 'id.', Far. turrur adj. 'id.', Nw. ti1rr, turr, dial. tjurr adj. 'id.', Elfd. tuorr adj. 'id.', OE f>yrre adj. 'id.', OFri. thurre adj. 'id.(?)', OS thurri adj. 'id.', Du. dor adj. 'id.', OHG durri adj. 'id.', G diirr adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*trs-u- (IE) - Identical to Skt. t[$U- adj. 'avaricious', YAv. tar5u- 'dry, not fluid' < \*trs-u-; also cf. Olr. tfr adj. 'dry' < \*ters-o-. An old u-stem continuing PIE \*trs-u-. Nw. dial. tjurr developed from an Old Norse stem \*þyrrv- by labial breaking ofy (cf. Nw. tjukk 'fat' < \*þekwja< \*þeku-). The variant þyrr itself continues a secondary ja-stem \*þurzwja-. Also note \*þurzjan- and the inchoative Go. ga-paursnan, ON porna w.v. 'to dry up' < \*þurznan- < \*trs-neh₂-.

\*þūshundī- f. 'thousand' - Go. þusundi f. 'id.', ON pusund f. 'id.', Far. tusundi num. 'id.', Elfd. tusn num. 'id.', OE þūsend num. 'id.', E thousand, OFri. thusend num. 'id.', OS thusundig num. 'id.', Du. duizend num. 'id.', OHG dusunt num. 'id.', G Tausend f. 'id.' ⇒ \*tuHs-d/{mt-o- (NEUR) - OCS tyspsti, Ru. tjsjaca, SCr. dial. tl'suca f. 'thousand'; OPru. tii.simtons ace.pl., Lith, tilkstantis, tiikstuotis m. 'id.' < \*tuHs-e/ont-io/ih₂-. A numeral usually reconstructed as \*þus-hund-f-, a collective meaning "Grofi-gehunderte", which was derived from \*hunda- 1 'hundred' (q.v.) < \*(d)Kmt-t6- with a prefix \*þus- (cf. Far. tusna w.v. 'to swell up') < \*tuH-s. This etymology is not undisputed due to some formal problems (cf. Pijnenburg 1989). The Slavic evidence, for instance, straightforwardly points to \*tuHs-e/ont-ih₂-, a formation that looks like a participle to a root \*tuHs-. In addition, there are hardly any traces of the initial h- of \*hunda- in Germanic. Still, the presence of the element \*dJ<m-t- seems to be confirmed by the m in OPru. tii.simtons, the Salian Fra. attestation thuschunde and not least by Far. poet. tus-hundrao 'thousand'.

\*þusiþa- adj. 'silent' - Nw. tyst adj. 'id.', OSw. thyster, ti1ster adj. 'id.', Elfd. tyst adj. 'id.', ODa. t(h)yst(ær) adj. 'id.', Da. tyst adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*tus-i-to- (IE) - Skt tu$yati 'to be calmed, be satisfied' < \*tus-ie-; OPru. tusnan 'silent' < \*tus-no-; Lith. tausytis (tailsos) 'to subside', Ru. tusft' 'to extinguish' < \*tous-eie-; Ru. tuxnut' 'to go out' < \*tous-ne-; Oir. t6e f. 'silence', MW taw m. 'id.' < \*tous-o/eh₂-; Olr. toe adj. 'deaf < \*tous-io-. The past participle of a verb \*þusjan-, which directly corresponds to Skt. tu$yati.

<!-- pdf-page: 595; section: dictionary -->

\*þuti- SSS

\*þuti- m. 'roaring' - ON pytrm. 'howling', MHG duz m. 'sound, noise' (ORV). An i-stem to the strong verb \*þeutan- - \*þūtan- (q.v.).

\*þutt/dōn- w.v. 'to bump' - Far. tota w.v. 'to dash', OE þodettan w.v. 'to hit, bump', Du. dial dodderen 'to bump', G dotzen, dutzen w.v. 'to bounce, bump' : \*tudh-neh₂- (GM). Presumably the iterative to \*þeutan- - \*þūtan- (q.v.). Also cf. OE pyddan w.v. 'to beat, bump, throw', ME thudden w.v. 'to push; thrust; throw; force', E to thud < \*þudjan- and OE podor m. 'ball' < \*þudra- ( < \*tudh-dhrō-?).

\*þwahan- s.v. 'to wash' - Go. þwahan s.v. 'id.', ON þvá s.v. 'id.', Far. tvaa s.fw.v. 'id.', Elfd. twa irr.v. 'id.', OE þwēan s.v. 'id.', OS thwahan s.v. 'id.', MDu. dwaen s.v. 'id.', OHG dwahan s.v. 'id.', MHG dwahen, twahen, zwahen s.v. 'id.' : \*tuōk-e (GM) - OPru. twaxtan 'brushwood for bathing' < \*tuok-sto-(?). An a-grade intensive formation. Cf. \*þwahla- as well as ON pvattr, OSw. thv<l!tter m. 'washing' < \*þwahtu- (whence e.g. Ice!. pv<l!tta, Sw. tviitta w.v. 'to wash' < \*þwahtjan-).

\*þwahla- n. 'washing, bathing' - Go. þwahl n. 'id.', ON þvall m. 'soap', OE þweal, þwahl m./n. 'washing', OHG dwahal n. 'bath, baptism' (ORV). Derived from \*þwahan- with the suffix \*-la-, possibly the outcome of secondarily added \*-pla- ( < \*-tlo-) after \*h. Also cf. the instrumental noun OE pwii!le f. 'band, fillet', Du. dweil c. 'mob', OHG dwahila, dwehila, G Zwehle f. 'towel' < \*þwahilon-. Far. dveylur m. 'swab' was borrowed from Low Ger-. man.

\*þwangi- m. 'latchet' - ON þvengr m. 'thong, latchet', Far. tvongur m. 'shoelace', OE þwang m.f. 'thong, strip ofleather', E thong ϊ \*tuon/{-f- (ORV). An i-stem to the strong verb \*þwinhan- ( q.v.).

\*þwarōn- f. 'stirrer' - ON þvara f. 'pot-stick, stirrer', Far. tvl!lra f. 'stirrer with four scoops' (ORV). An a-grade formation to the strong verb \*þweran- 'to swirl, stir' ( q.v.).

\*þweran- s.v. 'to swirl' - OE þweran s.v. 'to twirl, stir', OHG dweran s.v. 'to stir up', MHG twern s.v. 'to turn quickly, stir up' ⇒ \*tuerH-e- (IE) - Skt. tvarate, turati 'to rush' < \*tuerH-e-f\*turH-e-. Also cf. the instrumental noun ON pyrill, OE pwirel, OHG thwiril, G Quirl m. 'beater, whisk' < \*þwerila- and OE ge-þweor n. 'curds' < \*þwera-, which is semanticaly close to OCS tvarog'b 'curds' < \*tuor-. The oldest meaning of the root, i.e. 'to sweep', is preserved by the zero-grade ie-present \*þurjan- ( q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 596; section: dictionary -->

556 \*þwerha-

\*þwerha- adj. 'cross' - Go. þwairhs adj. 'angry, irate, quicktempered', ON þverr adj. 'transverse; adverse, contrary; cross, obstinate', Icel. pver adj. 'transverse, lying-across; headstrong, unyielding', Far. tverur adj. 'transverse; difficult; obstinate; strange', OE pweorh, þwerh adj. 'crooked, cross; adverse, opposed', OFri. thweres adv. 'cross', OS thwerh adj. 'stupid', MLG dwer adj. 'cross, lateral', OHG dwerah adj. 'cross, lateral', MHG dwerch, twerch adj. 'id.', G quer, obs. zwerch adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*terkw·o-(?) (IE) - Hitt. tarkuzi, taru(k)zi ~ tarkuyanzi 'to dance' < \*terkw·ti, \*trkw-enti; ToB tiirk- 'to turn'; Skt. tarku- 'spindle' < \*terkw·u-; Lat. torqueo 'to twist, wind' < \*torkw·eie-. In view of the non-Germanic evidence pointing to a root \*terkw-, I assume that PGm. \*þwerh- came about by metathesis of \*terkw- to "\*twerk-". Also cf. MHG twirhen w.v. 'to put across' and ON þvers, OE pweores, MHG twerhes, MLG dwers, MDu. dwe(e)rs, dwars, Du. dwars adv. 'crosswise, athwart'. The etymologically disputed ON pverra, Far. tverra, Nw. tverra w.v. 'to diminish; come to an end' is likely to belong here, too, cf. Far. mangt fer fyri tvert 'much goes to waste'.

\*þwinan- s.v. 'to abate, disappear' - OSw. þvina w.v. 'to dwindle, pine', Sw. tvina w.v. 'id.', Da. tvine s./w.v. 'to disappear', OE þwīnan s.v. 'to go down, lessen, abate' (IE). An n-present to the root \*dhgwhei-, probably back-formed to the inchoative OSw. pvcena w.v. 'to dwindle' < \*þwinon- < \*dhgwhi-nehr. Note that the Germanic reflexes of this verbal cluster show a remarkable alternation between \*dwinan- and \*swinan-; the outcome of the initial cluster \*dhgwh. was apparently unstable in Germanic.

\*þwinhan- s.v. 'to force' - OFri. thwinga s.v. 'to force', OS thwingan s.v. 'to force, urge', Du. dwingen s.v. 'id.', OHG dwingan s.v. 'to force, subdue, oppress', G zwingen s.v. 'to force' ⇒ \*tuenk-e- (IE) - ToAB twiinkii.- 'to wear' < \*tuonk-eh₂-; Lith. tvefikti (tvenkiu) 'to dam up' < \*tuenk-ie-. A strong verb to the PIE base \*tuenk- 'to push, stuff, pack, equip, wear', for which see \*þunhjan-. Note that the expected PGm. form \*þwinhan- was replaced by the Verner variant \*þwingan-. Also cf. \*þwangi-. u

\*uba prep. 'under; above' - Go. u/prep. 'under', ON o/prep. 'over; about', OHG ob( a) prep. 'above, on, over', G obs. ob prep. 'above; due to' ⇒ \*h₁up-ō (IE) - Skt. upa adv., prev., prep. 'towards, together with, under', OAv. upci,

<!-- pdf-page: 597; section: dictionary -->

\*uber- 557 YAv. upa prev., prep. 'towards, with, on, in', Gr. urro, im6 adv., prep. 'under; by', Olr./o prev., prep. 'under'. A petrified allative in \*-o. See also \*eup - \*upp and \*ufuman-.

\*uber- adv., prep. 'above, over' - Go. ufar prep. 'over', ON yfir prep. 'over, above, beyond, across', Far.yvir prep. 'id.', Elfd.yiyr prep. 'id.', OE ofer, ofor prep. 'above, over', E over, OS obar prep. 'over, across', Du. over prep. 'id.', OHG ubar prep. 'id.', G iiber prep. 'id.' ϊ \*h₁uperi (IE) - Identical to Skt. upciri adv. 'above, over, upwards', YAv. upairi adv. 'above, over', OP upariy adv. 'above', prep 'over, upon', NP bar prep. 'on', Gr. um:p, imE:p adv. 'over, above; in excess', prep. 'over', Lat. super adv., prep. 'over, above, beyond', Olr./or, MW gor prep. 'over, on'.

\*ubila- adj. 'evil, bad' - Go. ubils adj. 'evil, bad', OEyfel adj. 'evil, ill', OS ubil adj. 'evil', OHG ubil adj. 'evil, bad, wrong' (NEUR?) - Close to Olr.fel adj. 'bad' < \*up-elo-. An etymologically debated word, possibly derived from \*uba (cf. ON of adv. 'excessively; too') with the suffix \*-ila- (not \*-ela-, pace Orel 2003: 433). The original meaning may then have been 'overstepping a boundary'. For a similar derivation, cf. OHG uppi adj. 'vain, evil' < \*ubja-. Outside Germanic, there can be a link with Olr. fel. The connection with Hitt. buyappi buppanzi 'to be hostile towards, to do evil against; to throw (down), to hurl' < \*h₂u6ph₁-ei, \*h₂uph₁-i-enti must be rejected, on the other hand, because the meaning 'to do evil' developed from 'to overthrow' in this language (Kloekhorst 2008: 369-372).

\*ufna- m. 'oven' - Go. auhns m. 'id.', ON ofn m. 'oven, furnace', lee!.. ofn m. 'oven, stove, furnace, kiln', 6nn m. 'furnace, ash-hole under a kiln', Far. ovnur m. 'oven, stove', OSw. ughn, oghn, ofn, omn m. 'id.', Elfd. ungen m. 'id.', OE ofen, ofn m. 'oven, furnace', E oven, OFri. oven m. 'id.', MDu. oven m. 'id.', Du. oven c. 'id.', OHG ofan, ovan m. 'id.', G Ofen m. 'id.' ϊ \*upno- (NIE) - Hitt. bappen-, bapn- 'baking kiln, fire-pit, broiler (oven)' < \*h₂ep-n-(?); Gr. hrv6<; m. 'furnace; kitchen; lantern', Myc. i-po-no < \*ip-no-(?); OPru. wumpnis 'oven' < \*u{m)p-no-(?). The Germanic word for 'oven' in view of OE ofnet 'vessel' probably designated a small ceramic oven. The word displays some formal problems. Go. auhns and lee!. 6nn straightforwardly continue \*uhna-, which by some is taken to be the oldest form. Others have argued that it developed out of \*ufna-, and this is more probable in view PGm. \*ufuman- yielding Go. auhuma\*. OSw. ughn does not represent a third form \*\*ugna-, but developed out of \*ufn (by dissimilation after -u-), cf. ON gaupn, Sw. dial. gocken 'palm of the hand' < \*gaupn6-.

<!-- pdf-page: 598; section: dictionary -->

558 \*ufto Considering the etymology, the irregularities between the IE languages suggest that the word entered Germanic as a prehistoric Wanderwort adopted from an indigenous European language that was spoken before the arrival of the Indo-Europeans.

\*ufto adv. 'often' - Go. ufta adv. 'id.', ON oft adv. 'id.', Far. ofta adv. 'id.', OE oft adv. 'id.', E often, OS ofto adv. 'id.', OHG ofto adv. 'id.', G oft adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁up-to- (DRV). An adverb derived from \*uba (q.v.).

\*ujuman- comp. 'upmost, highest' - Go. auhuma\* comp. 'higher', auhumists superl. 'highest', OE ufemest superl. 'highest' ⇒ \*h₁up-rpHo- (IE) - Skt upama- 'upmost, highest, next from below', YAv. upama-, upama- 'upmost, highest, closest'. An old adverbial comparative to \*uba 'above'. In Gothic, the f was dissimilated to h, for which cf. Go. auhns 'oven' < \*ufna-. The dissimilation was triggered by the labial environment.

\*uhjon- w.v. 'to sound' - Icel. yja w.v. 'to remind, drop a hint' ⇒ \*uk-ie(NEUR). A ie-present possibly related to \*wahan- and \*wogjan- (Magnusson 1989: 1164). Go. auhjon w.v. 'to sound' rather belongs to Latv. ailka f. 'gale', SCr. uka f. 'shouting' < \*Houk-eh₂-.

\*uhsan- m. 'ox' - Go. auhsa, dat./acc. auhsau, gen.pl. auhsne, ace.pl. auhsnuns m. 'id.', ON uxi m. 'id.', Far. oksi m. 'ox; castrated bull', Elfd. ukse m. 'id.', OE oxa m. 'id.', E ox, OFri. oxa m. 'id.', OS ohso m. 'id.', Du. os c. 'id.', OHG ohso m. 'id.', G Ochse m. 'id.' ⇒ \*Huks-en- (IE) - ToB okso m. 'ox', Skt. uk$<in-, Av. uxsan- m. 'young bull', Mir. oss 'deer', MW ych 'ox'. The Indo-European word for 'ox'. It was inflected as an n-stem in Proto-Germanic, but in Gothic, it partly surfaces as a u-stem, probably due to leveling from the <lat.pl. \*uhsummiz < \*uks-n-mis. Within PIE, the derivation of the word is unclear. It is usually compared to Skt. uk$6ti 'to moisten, sprinkle', Av. uxsiieiti 'to sprinkle, spray' through an original meaning "inseminator" (cf. Pokorny IEW: 1118), but this is in contradiction with the fact that the word refers to a calf that has not yet procreated in Sanskrit, and to a castrated bull (i.e. 'ox') in Tocharian, Germanic and Middle Welsh. Kiehnle 1979 alternatively derived the word from the IE base \*h₂ueks- 'to grow', for which see \*wahs(j)an-.

\*umbi- prep. 'around, about' - ON umb, um prep. 'id.', Far. um prep. 'id.', Elfd. um prep. 'id.', OEymbe,ymb prep. 'id.', OS umbi prep. 'id.', Du. om prep. 'id.', OHG umbi prep. 'id.', G um prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂nt-bhi (IE) - Skt. abhf adv.,

<!-- pdf-page: 599; section: dictionary -->

\*unda 559 prev. 'to, towards, against, upon', OAv. aibi adv., prev. 'towards, against, upon', YAv. auui adv., prev., aiþi- pref. 'towards', OP abiy adv., prev. 'towards, against, upon', Gr. aμcpt adv., prep. 'around, on both sides', Lat. amb{i)-, am(b}-, an- pref. 'around', Olr. imb, imm, MW am, Bret. am, em prep. 'around'. A petrified instrumental to the root noun \*h₂ent-s 'face, front', for which see \*anpja- 'forehead'.

\*unda prep. 'until' - Go. und prep. 'unto, up to, until', ON unz prep. 'until' ( < \*und es conj.), lcel. uns prep. 'until', OFri. und prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂i;,it-6 (IE). The original allative in -o of the root noun \*h₂ent-s 'front, face', see \*andi.

\*under- adv., prep. 'under; among' - Go. undar prep. 'under, underneath', ON undir adv., prep. 'under', OFri. under adv., prep. 'under, underneath', OS undar adv., prep. 'under, between', Du. onder prep. 'id.', OHG untar adv., prep. 'below, between', G unter prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*(H)ndher- / \*h₁n-ter- (IE) - Skt. adhara- comp. 'lower', YAv. aoara- comp. 'lower, more to the West' < \*(H)ndh-ero-; Skt. antar, OAv. ai:itara, YAv. ai:itara prep. 'between, within', Lat. inter prep. 'among, between', Olr. eter, OW ithr prep. 'between' < \*h₁n-ter. PIE \*(H)ndh-er- and \*h₁n-ter- merged into \*under- in Germanic. This resulted in a preposition that means both 'under' and 'among'.

\*undi- f. 'affection' - ON Qfund f. 'grudge, envy, ill-will', OHG abunt, abunst m. 'envy' ⇒ \*h₃nh₂-ti- (DRV). The oldest form is \*undi-, a PIE ti-stem created to the root of \*unnan( q.v.). It was replaced by a sti-stem in West Germanic: the sti-suffix became productive after roots ending in a nasal.

\*unhta- adj. 'accustomed' - Go. bi-uhts adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁u-n-k-to- (IE) - Close to Lith.junkti Uunkstu), Latv.jukt 'to get used to' (withj- from the full grade, cf. the factitive Lith. jaukinti 'to tame, domesticate', Latv. jaucet 'to accustom', OPru. iaukint 'to exercize' < \*h₁euk-i;,ih₂-), Olr. do-ucai 'to understand' < \*tu-u-n-k- < \*h₁u-n-k-; also cf. Skt. -ucyati 'to be used to; to take pleasure in' < \*h₁uk-ie-; OCS uCiti, Ru. ucft' (ucu), SCr. uciti 'to teach' < \*h₁ouk-eie-; OCS vykn9ti, Ru. privyknut', SCr. v/'knuti 'to get accustomed' < \*h₁uk-ne-. An old participle to a nasal present \*h₁unk-e-, cf. Lith. junkti. Also cf. Go. bi-uhti n. 'custom' < \*unhtja-.

\*unhtwon- f. 'last part of the night' - Go. uhtwo f. 'foredawn, dawn, daybreak', ON 6tta f. 'last part of the night', Far. 6tta f. 'id.', OE iiht(a) m. 'the time just before daybreak' ( < \*unht(w}a(n}-), Du. ochtend c. 'morning' (with -d from avond 'evening' < \*ebanp- - \*ebund-), OHG iihta f. 'morning, dawn'

<!-- pdf-page: 600; section: dictionary -->

560 \*unka(n)- ⇒ \*nkw-tu- (IE) - Skt. aktu- m. 'dark, night' < \*nkw-t-u- (cf. aktd 'night' < \*nkw-t-ehi-); perhaps also Lith. ankstus adj. 'early' (whence OPru. angstainai, angsteina adv. 'early in the morning') with secondary a-grade(?). A PIE derivation from the word \*nokw-t- 'night' (see \*naht-). In Germanic, the word referred to the last part of the night. PGm. \*murge/ana- 'morning' (q.v.) was associated with daybreak, cf. the secondary meaning 'tomorrow'.

\*unka(n)- m. 'snake' - OHG unk m. 'snake, lizard', MHG unc, unke m. 'snake', G Rhnl. unke m. 'snake; newt' ⇒ \*h₂Tjgwh-on- (IE) - Arm. awj 'snake' ( < \*anwghi- with \*gh > j after \*u), Lith. angis f. 'snake', Latv. uodzs f. 'adder', Ru. uz, gen. uza m. 'grass-snake' ( < \*h₂ngwh-io-), Lat. anguis m. 'snake', Olr. escong 'eel' (lit. "fish-snake"), W llys-yw-en 'id.' (with -yw- < \*-angwi-) < \*h₂(e)ngwh+, The Balto-Slavic accent as well as the Baltic and Armenian forms point to a root \*h₂engwh_ with a voiced aspirate \*gwh rather than \*gw. The expected Germanic outcome of this reconstruction would be \*ungwi-, but High German only provides evidence for \*unka- (= MHG unk?) and an n-stem \*unkan-. Presumably, these formations split off from an n-stem \*ungwo, \*unkaz < \*h₂ngwh-on, \*h₂ngwh_n-os. See also \*angra- 2.
