\*unnan- pret.-pres. 'to grant' - ON unna pret.-pres. 'to grant; to love', Far. poet. unna w.v. 'to love, like', Elfd. unna w.v. 'to grant', OE unnan pret.-pres. 'id.', OS gi-unnan pret.-pres. 'id.', Du. gunnen w.v. 'id.', OHG unnan pret.-pres. 'id.', G gonnen w.v. 'id.' (IE) - Gr. 6viv1iμL 'to be of use, help'. The preterite-present conjugation of the verb, i.e. 3sg. \*anne, 3pl. \*unnunp, quasi-PIE \*h₃on-n-h₂-e, \*h₃Tj-n-h₂-Tjt, is secondary and fully parallel to \*kunnan- 'can' (q.v.), cf. \*kanne, \*kunnunp < quasi-PIE \*gon-n-h₁-e, \*9TJ-n-h₁-Tjt. In view of the primary 3sg. med. aorist Gr. an-6vl]TO 'to have joy of something', the verb originally must have been aoristic. Possibly, the creation of the verb went through an intermediate n-present, \*unnopi < \*h₃n-neh₂-ti. See also \*ansti- and \*undi-.

\*unsti- m./f. 'storm' - OEyst m. 'id.', OHG unst f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂nh₁-sti- (IE). Possibly a sti-stem created to the zero-grade root of \*anan- 'to breathe' (q.v.).

\*unþi- - \*unþjo- f. 'wave' - ON unnr, uor f. 'id.', OE yo f. 'id.', OS uthia f. 'id.', OHG undea f. 'flood, wave', MHG iinde f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂n-tih₂- (IE) - Cf. Hitt. biini - bananzi 'to draw (liquids)' < \*h₂6n-ei, \*h₂n-enti; Gr. aVTAO<; m. 'hold of a ship; bilge-water, flood' < \*h₂n-tlo- (also cf. Gr. untp-Cl'.VTAO<; adj. 'full of water, water-logged; overflowing'). The word is often linked to the PIE root \*ued- 'water' (Pokorny JEW: 78-81), but the PIE \*d is irreconcilable with the PGm. \*p. I therefore propose

<!-- pdf-page: 601; section: dictionary -->

\*iira- 561 to reconstruct a tihrstem to the PIE root \*h₂en- 'to scoop(?)' as evinced by the aforementioned forms in Hittite and Greek (cf. LIV2: 266). The appurtenance of Arm. hanem 'to take out, draw out' (see Martirosyan 2008: 389) is less certain.

\*iira- n. '(ferriferous) sand' - Du. oer n. 'ferriferous sand, bog iron' (WEUR?). Usually compared to Olr. u(i)r 'earth; clay', but the variant far seems to point to \*(s)wur-. See also \*aura-.

\*urba- n. 'inheritance' - OSw. orf, urf n. 'piece of cattle, property, inheritance', OE orfn. 'cattle' ⇒ \*h₃rbh·o- (IE). A sparsely attested, uniquely Germanic zero-grade formation to the PIE root \*h₃erbh-. See also \*arba-.

\*iiru- m. 'aurochs' - ON urr m. 'id.', OE ur m. 'kind of OX, bison', OHG uro m. 'aurochs', MHG ure, ur m. 'id.', G Auer-ochs m. 'id.' (GM). A u-stem of disputed origin. It is formally and semantically close to the IE word for 'male animal', \*(u)rs-en- (for which see \*kursan- and \*urzan-), but it is impossible to explain the Germanic long u from this form. It has therefore been suggested that the word was adopted from a lost European language (Schrijver 2003a: 223). It seems likely, however, that the u-stem split off from a regular n-stem, just as ON Qrn 'eagle' < \*arnu- split off from PGm. \*aran- 1. If so, it must be assumed that the n-stems OHG uro and MHG ure < \*uran- are primary, which would bring the word closer to the allegedly Indo-European \*(u)rs-en-. Gcisiorowski 2012: 120 rather derived the word from \*uzran-, comparing it to Skt. usra- m. '(red) bull' under the assumption of z-loss and compensatory lengthening of the preceding vowel. This usrais probably a late derivation from Skt. U?<ir- 'dawn', however, for which see \*austron-.

\*urzan- m. 'black grouse, capercaillie' - ON orri m. 'black grouse', Far. orri m. 'id.', Elfd. uorre m. 'id.', OE wor-hana m. 'pheasant', Du. woer-haan 'male pheasant', OHG orre-huon m. 'capercaillie', MHG or-han, ur-han m. 'id.', G Auer-huhn n. 'id.' ϊ \*rs-en- (IE). The word originally denoted only the male bird, the feminine form being represented by Elfd. ynn < \*urznf-, Nw. dial. yrkne < \*urznjo- < \*rs-n-ih₂-. It continues a formal variant of PIE \*urs-en- 'male animal' (for which see \*kursan- 'calf) plus another unknown element. In view of the seemingly ablauting variants OE wor-hana, Du. dial. woer-haan < \*worzan- < \*ueHursen- and MHG ur-hane, G Auer-huhn < \*u(w)urzan- < \*uHrsen-(?), it is tempting the reconstruct this element as \*ueH-, but 1 cannot find any possible cognates in the non-Germanic languages. Note that the occasionally

<!-- pdf-page: 602; section: dictionary -->

562 \*ustrO)acompared ON runi, Nw. rone, OSw., Sw., Da. orne 'boar' does not belong here, but was derived from ON \*runa, cf. Ice!., Nw. runa w.v. 'to burn, to be in heat'.

\*ustr(j)a- adj. 'energetic' - Sw. yster adj. 'lively', OHG ustar adj. 'stingy' (GM). No clear etymology. The comparison with Skt. 0$6m adv. 'with flames, burning; quickly' (e.g. Torp 1909: 33) is weak from the semantic perspective. Lat. industrius 'active' does not belong here either, since it must be derived from \*h₁endo-stru-io- "input".

\*iit adv. 'out' - Go. ut adv. 'id.', ON ut adv. 'id.', Far. ut adv. 'id.', Elfd. aut adv. 'id.', OE ut adv. 'id.', E out, OFri. ut adv. 'id.', OS ut adv. 'id.', Du. uit prep./ adv. 'id.', OHG uz prep./ adv. 'out, outside, but', G aus prep./ adv. 'id.' ϊ \*ud (IE) - Skt. ud prev. 'up, upwards, away, out of, Av. us-, uz- prev. 'up, out', Oss. (ce)sprev. 'id.', OCS, Ru. vy- pref. 'out'. An adverb directly continuing PIE \*ud. The vowel was lengthened as a result of the monosyllabicity of the word. See also \*uz.

\*utra- m. 'otter' - ON otr m. 'id.', Far. otur m. 'id.', Elfd. uotter m. 'id.', OE oter, otter m. 'id.', E otter, Du. otter m. 'id.', OHG otter m. 'id.', G Otter m. 'id.' ϊ \*ud-r-o- (IE) - Identical to Skt. udra-, YAv. udra- m. '(fish) otter'. The feminine formation, cf. G Otter < \*udro- is more wide-spread, cf. Gr. uopa f. 'water-serpent', Lat. lutra (with secondary /), Lith. ildra, OPru. wudro, Ru. vjdra, SCr. vl'dra f. 'otter' < \*ud-r-eh₂-. The word is a direct derivation from the IE word for 'water', for which see \*watar- - \*watan-.

\*u(w)ur- n. 'drizzle' - ON ur n. 'drizzling rain', Elfd. aur-weoer n. 'snowstorm' ⇒ \*uh₁-r (IE) - Lith. jilra, Latv. jura f. 'sea', OPru. wurs 'pond' < \*uh₁-r-o-, -eh₂-. The word can directly be compared to Lith. jilra < \*uH-reh₂- and possibly also to CLuv. yiir 'water', Skt. vJr- n. 'id.', YAv. viira- m. 'rain' < \*ueH-r(o)-. The root \*ueH- developed from \*ued- 'water' as PIE \*-dr regularly became \*-h₁r in early Proto-Indo-European (Lubotsky 2012). Note that the Germanic form must be reconstructed as continuing a heteroclitic nominative \*u{w)ur < \*uhr[, as thematic \*uh₁ro- would have developed into \*\*wara( cf. \*wana- 1 'lacking' < PIE \*u!Jrno-).

\*uwwilon- f. 'owl' - ON ugla f. 'id.', Far. ugli m., ugla f. 'id.', Elfd. ugel f. 'id.', OE ule f. 'id.', E owl, MDu. ule m.jf. 'id.', Du. uil c. 'id.', OHG uwila, ula f. 'id.', G Eule f. 'id.' (GM). Etymology obscure, probably an onomatopoeia, cf. ON ufr 'horned owl', OHG uvo, MHG ufe m. 'eagle owl', Cimb. auvo m. 'owl' < \*ufa(n)-.

<!-- pdf-page: 603; section: dictionary -->

\*uz 563

\*uz prep. 'out (of)' - Go. us prep. 'id.', ON 6r, ur prep. 'id.', Far. ur prep. 'id.', Nw. or prep. 'id.', Elfd. yr prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*ud-s (IE). Apparently from \*ud-s, i.e. the adverb \*ud (see \*ut) plus a genitival ending.

\*uzda- m. 'tip, prickle' - ON oddr m. 'point of a weapon; spear; spur', Far. oddur m. 'point, tip, end', Elfd. udd m. 'point, edge, cusp', OE ord m. 'point (of a weapon); line of battle; beginning', OS ord m. 'point', Du. oord n. 'location', OHG art m./n. 'point, end, prickle', G Ort m. 'place, spot' ⇒ \*uz-dhh₁-o-(?) (DRV?). Possibly an old compound of the preposition \*uz 'out' < PIE \*ud-s and the verb \*dhehi- 'to put', cf. Lith. ifidas 'vessel' < \*h₁en-dhhi-o-, lit. "in-put".

\*uz-etan- m. 'manger; feed' - Go. uz-eta m. 'crib, manger', ME orte '(refuse of) animal feed', E orts, MLG ort(e) 'remainder of animal feed; undigested food', MDu. or(a)ete 'leftovers from animal feed', Du. obs. oort n. 'leftovers' (DRY). A formation consisting of the adv. \*uz 'out' and the strong verb \*etan- 'to eat'. Also cf OFri. erta w.v. 'to browse', WFri. eertje w.v. 'to browse, eat away': according to Brandsma 1936: 69, the Frisian forms belong to OE earo, iero f. 'plowing', but the development of \*p to \*t only takes place word-initially.

\*uznan- w.v. 'to become warm' - Far. orna w.v. 'id.', OSw. orna w.v. 'id.', Elfd. uonna w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*Hus-neh₂- (IE) - Either to Skt. 6$ati 'to burn, scorch', Gr. ruw 'to singe', Lat. uro, -ere 'to burn, scorch' < PIE \*h₁eus-e- or to Gr. auw 'to light a fire' < \*h₂(e)us-ie- (cf. \*austron- 'dawn'). A medial factitive neh₂-verb to a PIE root \*h₁eus- or \*h₂eus- 'to burn'. Related to \*usjon-, for which see \*aima-uzjon-. w

\*wabja- n. 'web' - ON vefr m. 'web; woven cloth', Far. vevur m. 'web; texture, tissue', Elfd. wev m. 'web', OE webb n. 'id.', E web, OFri. web, wob, wub n. 'id.', OS webbi n. 'id.', Du. web n. 'id.', OHG weppi n. 'id.', MHG weppe n. 'id.' (DRV). A (collective) ja-stem related to the strong verb \*weban- (q.v.). A zero-grade variant is attested as OHG wuppi, MHG wiippe n. 'web' < \*wubja-.

<!-- pdf-page: 604; section: dictionary -->

564 \*wabjan-

\*wabjan- w.v. 'to wrap' - ON vejja w.v. 'to wrap, fold; to entangle', Far. vevja w.v. 'to twist, twin; to wrap, swaddle', Elfd. weva w.v. 'to wrap' w \*h₁uobh-eie- (IE). The causative to \*weban- ( q.v.).

\*wada- m. '(fishing-)line' - ON vaor m. 'fishing-line; line for measuring', Far. vao n. 'fishing-line' ⇒ \*uodh-o- (NEUR) - Lith. viidas, Latv. vads m. 'seine, sweep-net'; OCS nevod'b, Ru. nevod m. 'id.' < \*(ne-)uodh-o-; with (analogical?) zero grade: Lith. udas m. 'eel line' < \*udh-o-. A Germanic/Balto-Slavic root also found in MHG wate f. 'seine' < \*wado-. For the formation ON vaztir f.pl. 'fishing-bank' < \*wadisto-, compare \*awista- 'sheep pen' < \*h₂oui-sth₂-o-.

\*wadan- s.v. 'to wade' - ON vaoa s.v. 'id.', Far. vaoa s.v. 'to wade; to wash up to', OE wadan s.v. 'to go, wade', E to wade, OFri. wada s.v. 'to wade', MDu. waden s.v. 'to go, proceed, wade; to flow, melt; to sink into', Du. waden w.v. 'to wade', OHG watan s.v. 'id.', G waten s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uh₂dh-e- (WEUR) - Lat viido, -ere 'to advance, proceed' < \*ueh₂d-e-. The verb is usually connected to Lat viido (esp. cf. Lat vadum n. 'ford' to ON vao n. 'id.', OHG wat n. 'id.', OE woed n. 'sea' < \*uh₂dh-o-), but PGm. \*wadan- has a zero grade of the root, and would then classify as a tudati-verb or aorist present. Or should we reconstruct a root present 3sg. \*ueh₂dh-ti, 3pl. \*uh₂dh-enti (De Vaan 2008: 650)? It is further attractive to assume that the meaning 'to flow, to wash' as attested in Faroese is old, and that 'to wade' arose secondarily, e.g. in an oblique subject construction.

\*wadja- n. 'pledge, surety' - Go. wadi n. 'id.', ON veo n. 'id.', Far. veo n. 'id.', OE wed n. 'id.', OFri. wed n. 'id.', MLG wedde n. 'pledge, pledge agreement, fine, agreement, law', MDu. wedde m./n. 'pledge; surety; dowry; stake(s)', Du. wedde c. 'salary', OHG weti, wetti n. 'pledge; agreement; penance', G Wette f. 'bet' ⇒ \*Huodh-io- (IE) - OCS voditi, Ru. vodit' (vozu), SCr. vOditi 'to lead' < \*Huodh-eie-. The root of this word is associated with the institution of marriage (cf. ORu. voditi 'to get married', MDu. wedde 'dowry' and the denominal Go. ga-wadjon w.v. 'to engage by a pledge', ON veoja w.v. 'to stake in a wager', OE weddian w.v. 'to engage; to marry', OFri. weddia w.v. 'to pledge, promise' < \*wadjojan-). This is a secondary association that evolved from the original meaning 'to lead'. The Indo-Europeans were culturally exogamous (on the female side), which implied that the bride was led away from her father's to her new husband's family. For the same semantic field, cf. the related \*wed an-.

<!-- pdf-page: 605; section: dictionary -->

\*wagja- 565

\*wagja- m. 'wedge' - ON veggr m. 'id.', Elfd. wegg m. 'id.', OE wecg m. 'id.', E wedge, OS weggi m. 'id.', EDu. wig c. 'id.', OHG wecki m. 'id.', G Weck m. 'bread roll' ⇒ \*uogrwJh-io- (NEUR) - Lith. vii.gis, Latv. vadzis m. 'wedge'. The word is formally identical to the Baltic forms, but the close formal and semantic match may point to borrowing from Germanic. The IE form can be reconstructed as \*uok(wl-i6- or \*uogrwJh-io- (for the loss of the labialization, cf. \*sagja- 2). The latter option is etymologically reconcilable with OHG waggo 'silex', MHG wacke m. 'rock' < \*waggan- and \*wagnisan- 'plowshare' (q.v.). The comparison with Dir.fee f. 'spade; tusk' < PCelt. \*we/ikkii.is more doubtful.

\*wagjan- w.v. 'to shake, move' - Go. wagjan w.v. 'id.', OE wecgan w.v. 'id.', OHG weggen, wegen w.v. 'to shake, provoke' ⇒ \*uogh-eie- (IE) - Gr. oxf.w 'to carry, bear; to let mount or ride; to anchor' < \*uogh-eie-. An iterative-intensive formation derived from \*wegan- (q.v.).

\*wagna- m. 'wagon' - ON vagn m. 'id.', Far. vognur m. 'id.', Elfd. waungen m. 'id.', OE wægn, wægen m. 'id.', E wain, OFri. wein m. 'id.', Du. wagen c. 'id.', OHG wagan m. 'id.', G Wagen m. 'id.' ⇒ \*uogh-no- (IE) - Olr. fen m. 'wagon', W gwain 'id.' < \*uegh-no-. A no-stem derived from the root \*uegh- as found in e.g. \*wegan- (q.v.).

\*wagnisan- m. 'plowshare' - ON vangsni m. 'id.', Nw. vagnse, vangsne m. 'id.', OHG waganso m. 'id.', G Swi. wiige(n)se m. 'id.' ⇒ \*uogwh-ni- (EUR) - Gr. 6<pvic; 'plowshare', OPru. wagnis 'coulter' < \*uogwh_ni-; Lat. vomer m. 'id.' < \*uogwh-mi- (with assimilation of n to m?). A European word. Gr. 6qivic;, OPru. wagnis and probably also Lat. vomer point to \*uogwh_ni-, and it is possible that the Germanic form is an extension of it. The exact suffixation is difficult to recover. OHG waganso is usually assumed to point to a proto-form \*wagansan-, but the umlaut' of Swi. wiige(n)se rather seems to imply \*wagnisan-, apparently an extension of \*uogwh-ni-. This \*wagnisan- is also reconcilable with ON vangsni, which continues \*vagnsi, cf. Nw. vagnse. See also the possibly related \*wagja- 'wedge' (q.v.).

\*wahan- s.v. 'to remark' - OS gi-wegi 'suggerat:, MLG ge-wagen 'to notice', MDu. ge-waghen s.v. 'to make notice of, tell', Du. ge-wagen 'to make mention of, OHG gi-wahan s.v. (pret. gi-wuog) 'to memorate, mention' ⇒ \*u6kw-e(IE) - Skt. vfvakti 'to speak, tell, say, call' < \*ui-ue/okw-; Gr. dnov 'said' < \*ue-u(e)kW-; Oir.focal,focul n. 'word' < \*uokw-t/o-. An a-grade present to the root \*uekW- 'to say'. Cf. ON vcittr m. 'witness' < \*wahta-, MHG gi-waht f. 'mention, fame' < \*ga-wahti- and Du. ge-wag

<!-- pdf-page: 606; section: dictionary -->

566 \*wahsa- (maken) n. '(to make) mention' < \*ga-waga-. See further \*wogjan- and \*uhjon-.

\*wahsa- n. 'wax' - ON vax n. 'id.', Far. vaks n. 'id.', Elfd. waks n. 'id.', OE weax n. 'id.', E wax, OFri. wax n. 'id.', Du. was c. 'id.', OHG wahs n. 'id.', G Wachs n. 'id.' ⇒ \*uol<s-o- (IE) - Lith. vaskas, Latv. vasks m. 'id.'; OCS voskb, Ru. vosk, gen. v6ska, SCr. vosak, gen. voska m. 'id.'. The Balto-Slavic forms are suggestive of a formation \*uosk-o-. However, the neuter gender of the Germanic word could point to an s-stem nom. \*uoli-os, gen. \*uoli-s-os, in which metathesis could have affected the genitive case. The alternative is to reconstruct \*uoli-sko- and assume dissimilar dissimilation in Germanic and Balto-Slavic (Kortlandt 1979: 59).

\*wahsO)an- s.v. 'to grow' - Go. wahsjan s.v. 'id.', ON vaxa, vexa s.v. 'id.', Far. vaksa s.v. 'id.', Elfd. weksa w.v. 'id.', OE weahsan s.v. 'id.', E to wax, OFri. waxa, wexa s.v. 'id.', OS wahsan s.v. 'id.', OHG wahsan s.v. 'id.', G wachsen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂ueg-s- (IE) - Gr. aE:4w 'to increase' < \*h₂ueg-s-e-; Skt. uk$ati 'to grow', Gr. au4w 'id.' < \*h₂ug-s-e-. An s-extension to the more common IE base \*h₂eug- 'to grow', for which see \*aukan-. The Germanic dialects vary between the pure a-grade present \*wahsan- and the apparently secondary ie-present \*wahsjan-. See also \*wahstu-.

\*wahstu- m. 'growth; stature' - Go. wahstus m. 'id.', ON vpxtr m. 'growth; stature; yield', Far. vekstur m. 'id.' (DRV). A tu-stem derived from \*wahs(j)an- (q.v.). With a similar meaning, also cf. ODu. wa(h)smo, OHG wahsamo m. 'growth, fruit' < \*wahsman-.

\*wai interj. 'woe' - Go. wai interj. 'id.', ON vei interj. 'id.', OE wa interj. 'id.', E woe, OS we interj. 'id.', Du. wee interj. 'id.', OHG we interj. 'id.', G Weh interj. 'id.' ⇒ \*uai (IE) - Av. vaii-oi, Arm. vay, Lat. vae, Olr. Jae, W gwae, Latv. vai interj. 'id.'. An IE interjection. See also \*wainaga-.

\*waigo- f. 'strength' - ON veig f. 'pith, strength' ⇒ \*uoik-eh₂- (EUR) - Lith. dial. viekas m. (also vieka f.) 'physical strength, century, age, lifetime', OCS vek'b, Ru. vek m. < \*uoik-o-. Also cf. Ice!. vo f. 'id.' < \*waiho-. Related to \*wlhan- (q.v.).

\*waigra- adj. 'resistant' - MDu. we(i)ger adj. 'id.', OHG weigar adj. 'id.' Թ \*uoili-r6- (DRV). A ra-stem derived from \*wlhan- (q.v). Also cf. OFri. weigeria, MDu. we{i}geren, OHG weigeron, G weigern w.v. 'to refuse, reject' < \*waigrojan-.

<!-- pdf-page: 607; section: dictionary -->

\*waika- 567

\*waika- adj. 'weak' - ON veikr adj. 'weak, sick', Far. veikur adj. 'weak', OE wac adj. 'weak, miserable', OS wek adj. 'id.', Du. week adj. 'soft, weak', OHG weich adj. 'id.', G weich adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₃uoig-o- (DRY) - Formally identical to Av.fra-uuaeya- adj. 'swinging forward' and Slov. vၬg adj. 'uneven, bent'. An o-stem adj. created to the strong verb \*wikwan- (q.v.): the original meaning was 'giving way'.

\*wainaga- adj. 'miserable, poor' - Go. wainags adj. 'id.', Du. weinig adj. 'few, little', OHG wenag adj. 'id.', G wenig adj. 'few, little' (DRY). Derived from \*wain6n- w.v. 'to whine', cf. ON veina, OE wanian, OFri. wenia, OHG wein6n, G weinen, which agains appears to be an n-present \*uai-neh₂- to the interjection \*wai ( q.v.).

\*waisunda/6- m./f. 'windpipe; gullet' - OE wasend m. 'windpipe; gullet', OFri. wasanda, wasenda m. 'wind-pipe', OHG weisunt, weisant f. 'wind-pipe; vein' (DRY). Usually assumed to be related to Skt. ve$ati 'to flow' < PIE \*ueis- (Pokorny JEW 1134) through an intermediate meaning 'the flowing one' (cf. the OHG meaning 'vein'). This etymology seems to be strengthened by Nw. veise f. 'rivulet; horsetail' < \*wais6n-, especially since the noded stalk of the horsetail is reminiscent of the windpipe. PGm. \*waisunda- contains the suffix \*-und- < PIE \*-nt-, for which cf. ON hprund n./f. 'flesh', vitund f. 'consciousness, skill' (cf. also OGutn. hugund f. 'need, nature's call'). Note that, in English, dial. wozzen ( = OE wasend) is accompanied by the umlauted form weasand < OE \*wii!send.

\*waiza- n. 'seaweed(?)' - OE war n. 'seaweed; sand', ME w6r 'seashore, beach; seaweed', MDu. wier n. 'id.', woer f. 'seaweed', EDu. Zeal. woer 'id.', Du. wier n. 'id.' (IE). The correspondence of OE war with Du. wier (with e < \*ai becoming ie in dialectal Dutch) points to \*waira- or \*waiza-. The coastal variant woer is an lngvaeonic form. The usually assumed link with ON vfrr, OE wir m. 'wire' < \*wira- is possible, but semantically improbable in view of the OE and ME meanings 'beach' and 'sand'. I therefore assume a connection with Icel. veisa f. 'moist, swamp, mud; filth; gutter' < \*wais6n-, derived from the PIE root \*ueis- 'to flow' (for which cf. \*waisunda/6-).

\*waizda- n. 'woad' - OE wad n. 'id.', E woad, OFri. wede f.(?) 'id.', OS wed m.(?) 'id.', ODu. wed{e) m.(?) 'id.', Du. wede c. 'id.', OHG weit m. 'id.', G Weit m. 'id.' ⇒ \*uaisd- (NIE) - Gr. rcrcxn<; f. 'id.' < \*uisat-(?); Lat. vitrum n. 'glass; woad' < \*uit-ro-(?). Also cf. OE wii!den 'purple, blue', OFri. weden 'blue' < \*waizdina-. A word with several formal problems. The \*z is reconstructed on the basis of MLat.

<!-- pdf-page: 608; section: dictionary -->

568 \*wajjuuuaisdus (> Fr. guede) and the ablauting formations OE weard m. 'vermillion' and Go. \*wisdila f. 'id.', although the latter is only attested indirectly through Lat. uuisdil{e), gisdil (cf. Schwenter 1957-8: 37-38). The origin of the etymon is unclear. Given the fact that dying with woad is a technique that spread from Southwest Asia and the Mediterranian basin (Zohary/Hopf 2000: 208-9), it is extremely unlikely that the word be of Indo-European stock. I therefore assume that Gr. '(cmn<; and PGm. \*waizdwere adopted from a pre-Indo-European Wanderwort, i.e. from a source form varying between \*uisat- and \*uaisd-.

\*wajju- m. 'wall' - Go. waddjus m. 'id.', ON veggr m. 'id.', Far. veggur m. 'id.', Elfd. wegg m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ul;i1i-u- (IE). A u-stem derived from the verbal root \*ueh₁i- 'to twist'. The exact Pre-Gm. form was \*ul;i1i-u-: it resulted in PGm. \*wajju- by 1) vocalization of the laryngeal between consonantic resonants; and 2) Holtzmann's law. A closely related formation is OE wag, OFri. wii.ch, MDu. weeg m. 'wall', which is usually derived from \*uoh₁i-k6-.

\*wakan- s.v. 'to awaken' - Go. wakan s.v. 'id.', ON vakinn ptc. 'awake', OE wacan s.v. 'to be born' ⇒ \*uh₂g-e- (IE) - Lat. vigil adj. 'alert' < \*ueg-el-o-. A verb sometimes compared to Lat vii.gire 'to cry (of new-born children)' and Lith. v6grauti 'to babble' < \*ueh₂9-. This link is semantically uncompelling, however: the original meaning of the Germanic root was 'to be incited', as is demonstrated by the striking similarity of the causative \*wakjan- 'to wake up' (q.v.) with Skt. vii.jayati 'to incite'. See also \*wakra-.

\*wakjan- w.v. 'to awaken' - Go. us-wakjan w.v. 'id.', ON vekja w.v. 'id.', Far. vekja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. wettja w.v. 'id.', OE weccan w.v. 'id.', OS wekkian w.v. 'id.', Du. wekken w.v. 'id.', OHG wecken w.v. 'id.', G wecken w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uog-eie(IE) - Skt. vii.jayati 'to incite, spur', Lat. vegeo 'to enliven' < \*uog-eie-. The causative to \*wakan- (q.v.).

\*wakk/g6n- w.v. 'to move, shake' - ON vaga w.v. 'to move', Far. vagga w.v. 'to rock; to stagger', Nw. vakka s.v. 'to travel', OE wagian w.v. 'to oscillate, shake', ME wag(g)en 'id.', E to wag, MDu. waggen s.v. 'to shake, waver', Du. waggelen w.v. 'to stagger', OHG wagon w.v. 'to move, shake; to dare', G wagen w.v. 'to dare' ⇒ \*uogh-nehr (IE). An a-grade iterative to the strong verb \*wegan- (q.v.).

\*wakra- adj. 'alert, awake' - ON vakr adj. 'id.', OE wacor adj. 'id.', Du. wakker adj. 'id.', OHG wackar adj. 'id.', G wacker adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*uog-ro- (ORV). A ro-stem derived from \*wakan- (q.v.). With a different suffix, cf. OHG wachal adj. 'awake' < \*waka/a-.

<!-- pdf-page: 609; section: dictionary -->

\*wakwa- 569

\*wakwa· adj. 'moist' - ON vpkr adj. 'id.', MDu. wac adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*uogw.0• (EUR) - Gr. uyp6<; adj. 'moist, fluid' < \*ugrwLro-; Lat. iividus, iidus adj. 'wet, soaked' < Pit. \*uogw-edho-; Mir. JU.al 'urine' < \*uogw-/o-. Also cf. ON vpkva f. 'moist', Far. eyr-veka, -vika, -vrJka f. 'moist soil' < \*wakwon- and ON vrJkva, vekkja w.v. 'to pour' < \*wakwjan-. ON vpk f. 'hole in the ice' corresponds to MLG wake f. 'id.' < \*wakwo-. The latter formations have alternatively been derived from PIE \*uehi,9- 'to break', cf. Hitt. !!iiki - !!akkanzi 'to bite' < \*ueh/gl-ei, \*uh/gl-enti, ToA wiikii- 'to burst', ToB wiikii'to split; to flourish' < \*ueh/gl- and Gr. CiyvuμL 'to break' < \*uh/gl-neu-, but the connection with \*wakwa- is preferable.

\*wala- n. 'the slain' - ON valr m. 'id.', OE wee/ n. 'id.; battle field', OHG wa/ n. 'id.' ⇒ \*uo/h₃-o- (IE) - To Hitt. J!albzi - J!albanzi 'to hit, to strike', ToA wiil'to die', Gr. E:aA.wv 'was captured' < \*ue/h₃-ti, \*u/h₃-enti; Gr. out.Ժ f. 'scarred wound, scar' < \*uo/h₃-n-eh₂-; Lat. vulnus/volnus n. 'wound' < \*ue/h₃-n-es-; Olr. Jui! f. 'blood', W gweli 'blood, wound' < \*ue/h₃-i-; Lith. veles f.pl. 'ghost' < \*ue/h₃-ieh₂- (with metatony). A formation based on the ancient IE root \*ue/h₃- meaning 'to wound' or 'to kill'. The element is also present in the mythical figure known as ON valkyrja f. 'valkurie', OE walcyrie f. 'witch' < \*wala-kuzjon-. See also \*wola-.

\*waldan- s.v. 'to rule, have authority over' - Go. waldan s.v. 'id.', ON valda s.v. 'to rule over; to cause', OE wea/dan s.v. 'to have power over', E to wield, OFri. wa/da s.v. 'id.', OHG waltan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂ulh₁-t- (IE) - Hitt. bullezi bullanzi 'to smash, quash, defeat' < \*h₁ul-n-ehi-ti, \*h₁ul-nh₁-enti; ToA wiil, ToB walo m. 'king' < \*h₂ulhi-ont-; Lat. valeii, -ere 'to be strong' < \*h₂ulh₁-eh₁-; Olr. flaith f. (m.) 'sovereignty, ruler', OW gulat, MW gwlad 'country' < \*h₂ulh₁-ti-; Olr. fa/ 'rule' < \*h₂ulh₁-o-; Lith. valdyti, Latv. valdft 'to rule, govern, wield' < \*h₂uolhi-dhh₁-eie-; OCS vlasti (vladp) 'to rule'; CS vladeti, Ru. vladet' 'to own, control, wield', SCr. vlcidati 'to reign' < \*h₂uolh₁-dhh,i-eh₁-. An a-grade present probably back-formed to the preterite \*wulþin-, cf. the Old Norse preterite ol/a, which continues a PIE primary aorist form \*ulhrt. Instead of linking the Germanic verb directly to e.g. Lith. valdyti, which has a secondary \*dhhi-suffix itself, it is better to assume that the dental suffix \*p ~ \*d became attached to the root within Germanic itself. Note that the Balto-Slavic forms can actually also be regarded as Germanic loanwords.

\*waldii· f. 'dyer's rocket' - ME we/de, wolde 'id.', E weld, MDu. wolde, woude f. 'id.', Du. wouw c. 'id.', G Wau m. 'id.' ⇒ \*uolt-eh₂-(?) (WEUR/NIE) - Lat. liitum n. 'dyer's rocket; yellow dye' < \*ulout-o-(?). A plant native to Europe, known to have been cultivated for its yellow pigment at least in the Mediterranean from the first millennium BCE

<!-- pdf-page: 610; section: dictionary -->

570 \*waljan- (Zohary and Hopf 2000: 209). Geographically, the word is limited to the Dutch and English language area, cf. E weld, Du. wouw (G Wau was borrowed from Dutch, as the dye used to be imported from the Low Countries). An Old Dutch form \*walda has been reconstructed on the basis of Fr. gaude, Sp. gualda (cf. Sp. gualdo 'yellow'). No certain extra-Gm. etymology: the OED mentions the connection with \*walpu- 'wood, forest', but there is no compelling semantic motivation for this suggestion. More attractive is the linkage with Lat. liitum (Wood 1898: 220-221). It was dismissed by Polome 1948 in view of the formal incongruence (Gm. \*yolt- vs. Lat. \*(y)lout-), but this may be exactly the point, of course, the formal irreconcilability potentially being the result of post-PIE borrowing into both Latin and Germanic. For other old dye plants, cf. \*madron-, \*waizda- and \*wratjon-.

\*waljan- w.v. 'to choose' - Go. waljan w.v. 'id.', ON velja w.v. 'id.', Far. velja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. we/a w.v. 'id.', OS wellian w.v. 'id.', OHG wellan w.v. 'id.', G wiihlen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uolhi-eie- (IE) - Skt. varayati 'to choose', OCS voliti (voljp) 'to want, wish' (also cf. OCS volja, Ru. v6/ja, SCr. volja f. 'will' < \*uolhi-ieh₂-) < \*uolh₁-eie-. A causative or factitive to the root \*uelh₁-, cf. \*weljan- 1 and \*weljan- 2.

\*walkan- s.v. 'to roll' - OE wealcan s.v. 'to roll, toss; to wind', ME walken s.fw.v. 'to roll, toss; to wander, proceed', E to walk, OHG walkan s.v. 'to full, felt' (IE?). A root with an original Gm. meaning 'to roll (over), to toss'. The word was often used in the context of rolling waves cf. ON va/k, Far. valk n. 'swell (at sea)', OE wea/ca m. 'wave' < \*walka(n)-. On the IE level, the usual etymological connection with Skt. valgati 'to move up and down, boil up, spring' < \*uelg-e-(?) and Latv. vaigs m. 'rope' < \*uolg-o- (cf. LIV2), is improbable in view of the obvious intra-Gm. link with \*walk/gon- 'to roll', impers. 'to be nauseated'. It follows from this link that the k of \*walkan- must have arisen due to influence from the pertaining iterative verb \*wulk/gon- (q.v.). The underlying root \*uelk- or \*ue/gh- can be an extension of \*uelhi- 'to roll', for which see \*wal(l}on-.

\*walk/gem- w.v. 'to roll' - ON valka w.v. 'toss to and fro', Far. valka w.v. 'to make a mess', MLG walgen w.v. 'to struggle, wrestle; imp. to be disgusted', MDu. walgen w.v.(imp.) 'to be nauseated', Du. walgen w.v. 'to be disgusted', OHG walgon w.v. 'to roll' ⇒ \*uo/gh-neh₂- (IE?). An o-grade iterative parallel to \*wulk/g6n- (q.v.). The original meaning was 'to roll', but in impersonal use, the verb came to mean 'to be disgusted', cf. MDu. mi walget 'I am nauseated'.

<!-- pdf-page: 611; section: dictionary -->

\*wallan- 571

\*wallan- s.v. 'to well up, boil, seethe' - OE weal/an s.v. 'to well, bubble forth, spring out, flow', OFri. walla s.v. 'id.', OS wallan s.v. 'id.', OHG wallan s.v. 'id.', G wallen w.v. 'id.' (IE) - Skt iirmf- m. 'wave' < \*u/H-mi-; Lith. vilnis f. 'id.' < \*u/H-ni-; OCS vlbna, Ru. volna f. 'id.' < \*u/H-nehi-. An a-grade intensive verb probably continuing an n-present \*uo/H-ne-. It is accompanied by the e-grade verb \*wellan-, attested as ON vella s.v. 'to seethe' ( < \*ue/H-n-). Both of these strong verbs may have been created to the archaic zero-grade present Go. wulan < \*wulan- (q.v.).

\*wal(l)on- w.v. 'to roll' - OE weallian w.v. 'to wander, roam', MDu. wal(l)en 'to turn, roll, wander', EDu. wallen 'to turn', OHG wal(l)on w.v. 'to wander, roam' (IE) - ToA wa/-, ToB wiilii- 'to cover' < \*ulh₁-; Gr. dAtw 'to roll, turn, wind, revolve' < \*uel-n-h₁-e-; Olr. fillid 'to bend' < \*uel-ne-; Lith. velti 'to felt, full, tousle', Latv. veft 'to roll, felt' < \*uelh₁-ie-. The different dialectal forms appear to continue an iterative 3sg. \*wallopi, 3pl. \*walunanpi < \*uolh₁-n(e)h₂-. See also \*walwjan-.

\*walO- f. 'golden oriole' - ME wade-wale 'golden oriole, green woodpecker', E wood-wall, hick-wall, WFri.gie/e-gou c. 'golden oriole' (= "yellow-wall", i.e. with restored reduplication), MDu. wede-wale f. 'id.', Du. wiele-waal c. 'id.', MHG wite-wal f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ui-uh₂l-(?) (NEUR) - Lith. volunge, dial. v6Iunge f. 'golden oriole', Latv. viiluodze '(golden) oriole' < \*ueh₂l-; CS vhga f. 'oriole', Ru. fvolga f. 'id.', Pol. wilga, wywielga f. 'id.', SCr. vuga f. 'titmouse' < \*(ui-)u/H-. A Germanic-Balto-Slavic isogloss. In view of the possible reduplicated form in e.g. Pol. wywielga (for the loss of PSlav. v- in Ru. fvolga, cf. Ru. dial. iz{j)ubr 'Manchurian red deer' vs. OPru. wissambs"wisent' under \*wisund-), it is conceivable that the PGm. form \*widu-wa/6- 'wood-wall' arose due to folk etymology, replacing older \*wiwa/6-. Or does the Ru. form continue \*jbVblga < \*jbVO-Vblga "willow-wall" (Bulaxovskij 1968: 104)? Anyhow, the root of this formation can only be reconstructed with great difficulty: PGm. \*wal- can go back to \*uol-, \*uljl- or \*ueHI- (with pretonfc shortening); the Baltic forms, which point to \*ueh₂l- with \*h₂, can be reconciled with both Pre-Gm. \*u՗2l-eh₂- and \*ueh₂l-eh₂-, but CS vl'bga seems to go back to \*u/{H)-gh-ehr (with laryngeal metathesis?). Alternatively, the formal difficulties have been interpreted as an indication that the word was absorbed from a lost European language (Derksen 2008: 216-7). We may then reconstruct something like \*wal- or \*wal-.

\*walf>u- m. 'field, uncultivated area, wood' - ON vpllr m. 'field', Far. vږllur m. 'greensward', Elfd. wall m. 'field', OE weald m. 'wood', OFri. wald m./n. 'id.', OS wald m. 'id.', Du. woud n. 'id.', OHG wald m. 'id.', G Wald m. 'id.' Ի

<!-- pdf-page: 612; section: dictionary -->

572 \*walu-

\*u61H-tu- (EUR) - To Olr. folt m. 'hair', W gwallt 'id.' < \*uo/H-to-; Lith. valtis 'panicle', OPru. wolti 'id.', Ukr. vol6tb 'id.', SCr. vltit m. 'id.' < \*uolH-tio-. A tu-stem created to a European root \*ue!H-.

\*walu- m. 'lump; staff - Go. walus m. 'id.', ON vplr m. 'stick', Far. velur m. 'lump, tumor, growth; steering pole', OFri. walu-bera m. 'pilgrim' ⇒ \*uolhi-u(IE) - Lith. ap-valus adj. 'round' < \*uolh₁-u-; OPru. walis 'whippletree, drawbar' < \*uol-io-. The word has sometimes been connected to Lat. val/um n. 'palisade' < \*ueh₂l-so- (with Osthoff's law), Gr. ŽA.o<; m. 'nail' < \*uh₂/-so-. The Farnese form, however, proves that the original meaning was 'something round', cf. ON valr adj. 'round' < \*uolhi-o-. It thus seems to belong to the verb \*wal(l)on- (q.v.) and cognates.

\*walwjan- w.v. 'to roll' - Go. -walwjan w.v. 'id.', OE wielwan w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uolhi-u-eie- (IE) - Arm. gelum 'to turn, wind', Lat. volvo, -ere 'to (cause to) go round, roll' < \*uelh₁-u-e-. A causative closely related to \*wal(l)on-, but the origin of the w-suffix (which has an echo in Lat volvere) is not entirely clear. One possibility is that the verbal root was derived from a u-stem adjective \*walu-, cf. Lith. ap-valus adj. 'round', like OE wielwan 'to roll, wallow' < \*walwojan-. Or was it back-formed from the nu-present 3sg. \*ulh₁-neu-ti, 3pl. \*ulh₁-nu-enti? The non-Gm. languages (see under \*wal(l)on-) rather point to an n-present 3sg. \*u/-neh₁-ti, 3pl. \*ul-nh₁-enti, however.

\*wambo- f. 'womb, belly' - Go. wamba f. 'id.', ON vpmb f. 'id.', Far. vomb f. 'id.', Elfd. uomb f. 'id.', OE wamb f. 'id.', E womb, OFri. wamme f. 'id.', ODu. wamba f. 'id.', MDu. wamme f. 'id.', OHG wamba f. 'id.', G Wamme, coll. Wampe f. 'id.' (IE?). A Germanic word. It has been compared to OE umbor n. 'child' (Holthausen 1934: 376), but the implied PIE reconstruction \*umbh-ro(\*umbh-os-o-?) would have given \*wumbra-. More attractive is the connection with Skt. gabh<i- m. 'vagina' < \*gwh'lbh-o-. Note, however, that the latter word may belong to Skt. gabhira- adj. 'deep', which in turn must have been dissimilated from \*grabhira- in view of Gr. yA.mpup6<; adj. 'hollow' (Burrow 1954: 6). Also cf. OHG wambo m. 'paunch, tripe' < \*wamban-.

\*wana- 1 adj. 'Jacking, missing, void' - Go. wans adj. 'id.', ON vanr adj. 'id.', OE wan adj. 'id.', OHG wan adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*u!J2-no-j\*ueh₂-n6- (IE) - Skt. iina- adj. 'wanting, deficient, not sufficient, Jess, too small', YAv. iina- adj. 'deficient' < \*uh₂-n6- (perhaps also here: Arm. unayn 'empty' < \*uh₂n- plus the adj. suffix -ayn?); Lat. viinus m. 'idle' < \*ueh₂-no-.

<!-- pdf-page: 613; section: dictionary -->

\*wana- 2 573 Also cf. the noun, Go. wan, MDu. wan n. 'lack' < \*wana- and the stative verb ON vana, OE wanian, OHG wanon w.v. 'to wane' < \*wanen- (whence Go. wanains f. 'lack'). The primary adjective can continue both \*uh₂-no- (with vocalization of the laryngeal) and \*ueh₂-n6- (with Dybo's law of pretonic shortening): both reconstructions have counterparts in the non-Germanic languages (see above).

\*wana- 2 adj. 'accustomed' - ON vanr adj. 'id.', Far. vanur adj. 'id.', Elfd. wan adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*Huonh₁-o- (IE) - Skt. vcinate 'to love, desire' < \*Huenhi-e-. An o-stem created to the PIE root \*Huenhi-. ON venja, OE wenian, wennan, OS gi-wennian, Du. ge-wennen, OHG wenjan, wennan, G ge-w6hnen w.v. 'to get accustomed' < \*wanjan- is a factitive derived from the adjective. See also \*weni- and \*wuna(n}-.

\*wanda- adj. 'turned, twisted' - ON vandr adj. 'difficult, bad', Elfd. uond adj. 'bad', OS wand adj. 'changeable' (DRV). An o-grade adjective derived from \*windan- (q.v.).

\*wandjan- w.v. 'to cause to turn' - Go. ga-wandjan w.v. 'to turn, convert', ON venda w.v. 'to turn; to change, convert', Far. venda w.v. 'id.', Elfd. wenda w.v. 'to turn (around)', OE wendan w.v. 'to turn, cause to move, change direction', OFri. wenda w.v. 'id.', OS wendian w.v. 'to turn', Du. wenden w.v. 'id.', OHG wenten w.v. 'id.', G wenden w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uondh-eie- (EUR). The causative of\*windan- (q.v.).

\*wandu- m. 'rod, cane' - Go. wandus m. 'id.', ON vpndr m. 'switch, twig; stripe (in cloth)', Far. vond f. 'mast; thin strip of wood nailed over a split in a boat; structure; weave of cloth', Elfd. uond m. 'stick put on top of a hayrack' (DRV?). A u-stem perhaps derived from \*windan- (q.v.). Unrelated to Lith. vcinta f. 'besom'.

\*wanga- m. 'meadow' - Go. waggs m. 'meadow, park, paradise', ON vangr m. 'field', OE wang m. 'field; land', OS wang m. 'grove', OHG holz-wanga f. 'wooded area' ⇒ \*uongh-o- (NEUR) - OPru. wangus 'oak forest' < \*uongh-u-. Also cf. vangi m., OE wange n., OS wanga f., Du. wang c., OHG wanga f., G Wange f. 'cheek' < \*wanga/on-.

\*wantu- m. 'mitten' - ON vpttr m. 'id.', Far. vettur m. 'id.', Sw. vante c. 'id.', Elfd. uott m. 'id.', MDu. want, wante m. 'id.', Du. want c. 'id.' (DRV). A formation probably derived from \*windan- 'to wind' (q.v.). The root-final \*t continues a geminate: \*wantu- < \*wanttu- < \*uondh-nu-.

<!-- pdf-page: 614; section: dictionary -->

574 \*wara-

\*wara- adj. 'aware' - Go. war\* adj. 'cautious', ON varr adj. 'aware', Far. varur adj. 'id.', Elfd. war adj. 'id.', OE wa?r adj. 'id.', E aware, OS war adj. 'aware, careful', Du. ge-waar adj. 'id.', OHG gi-war adj. 'id.', G ge-wahr adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*uor-o- (IE) - ToB war- 'to smell', Lat. vereor 'to show respect, fear', Latv. vert (veru) 'to watch, notice' < \*uer-e-; Olr. c6i"r, coair adj. 'proper, fitting', OW couer 'complete', W cyweir adj. 'ready, equipped' < \*kom-ur-io-. An o-stem to the root \*uer-, for which cf. \*werdu- 'host'.

\*wardjan· w.v. 'to cause to degenerate' - Go. fra-wardjan w.v. 'to corrupt, destroy', OE a-wierdan w.v. 'to damage', OHG far-werten w.v. 'to damage, corrupt' ⇒ \*uort-eie- (IE) - Lith. vartjti, Latv. v&rtft 'to turn, turn over', OPru. wartint 'to turn', OCS vratiti sǻ 'to return, turn', Ru. vorotit' (vorocu) 'to bring back, turn aside', SCr. vratiti 'to return, bring back' < \*uort-eie-. The causative to \*werpan- ( q.v.). Cf. with the same prefix OS far-werthan s.v. 'to perish, ruin'.

\*warha- n. 'rheum' - ON var n. 'rheum', Nw. var n. 'id.', OSw. var n. 'mucus, rheum, pus', Elfd. war n. 'id.', OHG warah n. 'pus, liquid manure' ⇒ \*u6rk-o(NEUR) - Lith. vefkti (verkiit) 'to cry' < \*uerk-ie-. A word with an original meaning 'rheum' or 'serum'. I assume that it was derived from a root \*uerk- 'to cry' as found in Baltic.

\*warjan- w.v. 'to hinder, prevent' - Go. warjan w.v. 'id.', ON verja w.v. 'to defend, guard', Far. verja w.v. 'to defent, protect, cover', Elfd. wera-s w.v. 'to watch, guard', OE werian w.v. 'to hinder, check, restrain', OFri. wera w.v. 'to defend, resist', OS werian w.v. 'to prevent, hinder, protect', Du. weren w.v. 'to keep out', OHG werren, weren w.v. 'to defend, protect, hinder', G wehren w.v. 'to defend, fight' ⇒ \*h₂uor-eie- (IE) - Skt. V[TJ6ti 'to cover, stop', YAv. varanaoti 'id.' < \*h₂ur-neu-; Gr. aEtpw 'to raise (up)', Lat. aperio, -ire 'to open', Lith. verti (veriit) 'to pierce, string', Latv. vert 'to open, close' < \*h₂uer-ie-. A causative to the PIE root \*h₂uer- (on which see Lubotsky 2000), cf. \*wera- and \*wurpi-. Also cf. MHG were m. 'guarantor' < \*weran- and Far. veri m. 'possession', which is semantically close to MHG were f. 'investiture, right of ownership' < \*warin-. ON, Far. ver n. 'cover, case' may also belong to \*wazjan- 'to cover, dress' (q.v.) < PIE \*uos-eie-.

\*warki- m. 'pain, ache' - ON verkr, gen. -jar m. 'id.', Far. verkur m. 'pain, hurt; swelling, blister', Elfd. werk m. 'id.' ⇒ \*uorg-i- (NEUR) - Lith. vafgas m. 'hardship, misery', Latv. dial. v&rgs m. 'misery', OPru. wargan acc.sg. 'misery, suffering, danger' < \*uorg-o-; Lith. vafgti (vargstit) 'to live in poverty, to take pains', Latv. vargt 'to be ailing, pine away' < \*uorg-ske-; Lith. vargits adj.

<!-- pdf-page: 615; section: dictionary -->

\*warma- 575 'hard, difficult, tiresome', Latv. v&rgs adj. 'pining, miserable', OPru. wargs adj. 'evil' < \*uorg-u-/-o-. Within Germanic, it is possible to explain \*warki- as a cognate of \*werka'work', as the meaning of this word may have developed into 'pain' through 'labor', cf. OE weorc m. 'work, pain'. However, the Germanic homonymy must be secondary, as PIE had two different roots, viz. \*uerg- 'to work' and \*uerg- 'to suffer'.

\*warma- adj. 'warm' - ON varmr adj. 'id.', OE wearm adj. 'id.', E warm, OFri. warm adj. 'id.', OS warm adj. 'id.', Du. warm adj. 'id.', OHG warm adj. 'id.', G warm adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*gwhor-mo- (EUR) - Identical to Lat. formus adj. 'warm' < \*gwhor-mo-; also cf. Gr. 8Epμ6<; adj. 'warm' < \*gwher-mo-.

\*warza- m. 'callus' - OE wearr m. 'id.', MDu. war, weer m. 'gnarl; callus', EDu. weer 'id.' ⇒ \*uors-6- (EUR) - Lat. verruca f. 'wart; hillock' < \*u(e/o)rs(plus a suffix -ilea). Usually connected to Skt. vcir$man- n. 'height, top' < \*uers-mn-; Lith. virsus, Latv. virsus, OCS vrbX'b, Ru. verx m. 'top, upper part' < \*urs-u-, but the semantics are not compelling. See also \*werila/6- 'lip'.

\*wasra- - \*wazra- n. 'spring' - ON var n. 'id.', Far. var n. 'id.', Elfd. war n. 'id.', OFri. wars, wersm. 'id.' ⇒ \*uos-r/n- (IE) - Skt. vasanta- m. 'spring', YAv. vaT)ri loc.sg. 'in the spring', OP Bara-vii.hara- m. 'name of a spring month', NP bahii.r 'spring', Gr. fop, fopo<; n. 'id.', Lat. ver, veris n. 'id.', OW guiannuin, W gwanwyn 'id.' ( < PCelt. \*wesant-), Lith. viisara, Latv. vasara f. 'summer', OCS vesna, Ru. vesna f. 'spring', Arm. garun ( < \*gehar-), gen.garnan 'id.'. An old heteroclitic noun, probably to be reconstructed as PIE \*u6s-r, \*ues-n-s. The Germanic material contains at least two different forms. OFri. wars, wers appears to be metathesized from \*wasra- or \*wesra- (cf. Hofmann 1969), a form in Verner opposition to ON var, which usually is assumed to have developed from \*wezra-. G<!siorowski 2012, however, argued for a PGm. development \*ၭVzr- > \*-Vr-, according to which we can reconstruct both \*wezra- and \*wazra- for the Nordic form.

\*waskan- s.v. 'to wash' - OE wa?scan s.v. 'id.', E to wash, OFri. waska s.v. 'id.', OS waskan s.v. 'id.', Du. wassen s./w.v. 'id.', OHG wascan s.v. 'id.', G waschen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uod-ske- (DRV). A ske-present derived from \*watar- - \*watan- 'water' (q.v.).

\*watar- - \*watan- n. 'water' - Go. wato, gen. watins n. 'id.', ON vatn n. 'id.', Far. vatn n. 'id.', Elfd. watten n. 'id.', OE Wa?ter n. 'id.', E water, OFri. weter n. 'id.', OS watar n. 'id.', Du. water n. 'id.', OHG wazzar n. 'id.', G Wasser n. 'id.' ⇒ \*uod-r/n- (IE).

<!-- pdf-page: 616; section: dictionary -->

576 \*wapwan- The IE word for 'water'. The original paradigm may have been static \*u6d-r, gen. \*ued-n-s, but the Proto-Germanic material straightforwardly points to \*wator, \*watenaz < \*u6d-or, \*uod-en-(o)s. A trace of an e-grade is nevertheless to be found in OSw. Ve:etur, Sw. Viittern (= OSw. \*we:etr-in n.pl.def.), the name of several lakes in Sweden (cf. Hellquist 1903-06: 1, 748ff.; Schalin 2010) as well as in Nw. dial. vete f. 'wetland' < \*weton-. Also note that the default Nordic forms continue thematicized \*watna-, but Far. poet. ve:etur preserved the r-ending that is also found in WGm. \*watra-. See also \*weta- 'wet' and \*nata- 'id.'.

\*wapwan- m. 'calf of the leg(?)' - ON v9avi m. 'muscle', kvikva-v9avi m. 'calf, Far. veddi m. 'muscle; the thick of the thigh, buttock', OS watho m. 'calf, MDu. waede, waey m. 'id.', OHG wado m., wada f. 'id.', G Wade f. 'id.' ⇒ \*uH-tuo- (IE?) - To Skt. iirzi- m. 'thigh, shank' < \*uH-ru-? Not related to Lat. vatcix, -cicis adj. 'having a deformity of the feet' (cf. Pokorny JEW: 1113), which belongs to vatius adj. 'bent outwards'. I rather assume that the word contains a tu(o)-suffix (cf. \*skadu- 'shadow' < \*skh₃-tu-), which reduces the root to \*ueH-. This root may be identical to the derivational base of Skt. iiru-.

\*wazjan- w.v. 'to dress' - Go. wasjan w.v. 'id.', ON verja w.v. 'id.', OE werian w.v. 'id.', E to wear, OHG werian w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uos-eie- (IE) - Identical to Skt. vasayati 'to clothe'; further cf. Hitt. yesta ~ yesfanda 'to be dressed, wear', ToAB wiis- 'id.', Skt. vaste, OAv. vaste 'id.' Gr. Elμm 'id.' < \*ues-to, \*us-onto. A causative of Indo-European origin, derived from the root \*ues- 'to be clothed, dress'.

\*wean- s.v. 'to blow' - Go. waian s.v. 'id.', OE wciwan s.v. 'id.', OFri. wciia s.v. 'id.', Du. waaien s./w.v. 'id.', OHG wcien s.v. 'id.', G wehen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂ueh₁-e(1E) - Skt. vdti, YAv. vciiti 'to blow (of wind)', Gr. Ci1iμL 'id.', OCS vejati 'id.', Ru. vejat' (veju) 'to blow; to winnow', SCr. vl'jati 'to winnow; to snow' < \*h₂uehr; Hitt. buycii ~ bujanzi 'to run, hurry' < \*h₂uh₁-6i-ei, \*h₂uhri-enti. A strong verb to the IE root \*h₂uehr. See also \*wepra- - \*wedra- and \*winda- 'wind'.

\*weban- s.v. 'to weave' - ON vefa s.v. 'id.', Far. veva s.v. 'id.', Elfd. weva s.v. 'id.', OE we/an s.v. 'id.', E to weave, Du. weven s./w.v. 'id.', OHG weban s.v. 'id.', G weben s./w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁uebh-e- (IE) - ToAB wcipa- 'to weave' < \*h₁uobh-eh₂-; Skt. ubhndti, umbhclti, unabdhi 'to bind, fetter' < \*h₁ubh-neh₂-, \*h₁u-m-bh-e-, \*h₁u-n-ebh-; NP bciftan, Oss. wafyn / wafun 'id.' < \*h₁uobh-; Gr. ucpaivw 'id.' < \*h₁ubh-n-ie-; Alb. venj 'id.' < \*h₁uebh-n-ie-. A strong verb with clear IE roots. See also \*wabja- and \*wabjan-.

<!-- pdf-page: 617; section: dictionary -->

\*webna- \*wepna- 577

\*webna- - \*wepna- n. 'weapon' - Go. wepn\* n. 'id.', ON vapn, vakn, vamn n. 'id.', Icel. vopn, obs. vokn n. 'id.', Far. vapn, vakn n. 'weapon; whaling lance', OSw. viipn, viikn n. 'id.', Elfd. wapen n. 'id.', OE wrepn, wremn n. 'id.', E weapon, OFri. wepen n. 'id.', OS wiipan n. 'id.', Du. wapen n. 'id.', OHG wiifan n. 'id.', G Waffe f. 'id.' (DRV). A Germanic word displaying two different stems, viz. \*webna- (cf. ON vamn, OE wremn) and \*wepna- (cf. ON vapn, OE wrepn, OFri. wepen, OS wiipan, OHG wiifan): the Nordic forms with k, cf. Ice!. vokn, Far. vakn (with semantic specialization), OSw. viikn (= Fi. vaakuna) developed from vapn (with -pn- > -kn- as in ON gaupn vs. Gutn. gaukn 'handful'). The alternation between \*webna- and \*wepna- points to the pre-existence of an n-stem nom. \*webO, gen. \*wep(p)az that was thematicized (Luhr 1980: 253-54). The thematicization happened at a surprisingly early stage, however, as other n-stem thematicizations usually appear only in the North and West Germanic dialects, whereas in this case Go. wepn proves that the form \*wepna- existed already at the PGm. level. Anyhow, the word was no doubt derived from the iterative \*wab/ppon-, cf. OE wafian w.v. 'to wave', MHG waben, wappen w.v. 'to waver, shake', Du. wapperen w.v. 'to wave' (Luhr I.e.), probably through a meaning 'to brandish'. This is especially attractive in view of Du. obs. wappen w.v. 'to strike, knock, cuff, G Rhnl. wappen w.v. 'to cuff. For other explanations, see Blazek 2008.

\*wedan- s.v. 'to conjoin' - Go. ga-widan s.v. 'id.', OHG wetan s.v. 'to join, yoke', MHG weten s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*(H)uedh-e- (IE) - Skt. vf-vadh<i- m. 'yoke' < \*h₁ui-(H)uedh-o-(?); Av. vad- 'to lead', Olr. feidid 'to lead, bring together', W gweddu 'to subject oneself to'; Lith. vesti (vedu) 'to lead', OPru. westwei 'id.', OCS vesti, Ru. vest{ ( vedu) 'to lead, conduct'. An old strong verb. Also cf. Go. ga-wiss f. 'joint' < \*wessi- < \*(H)uedh-ti-.

\*wega- m. 'way, road' - Go. wigs m. 'id.', ON vegr m. 'id.', OE weg m. 'id.', E way, OFri. wei m. 'id.', OS weg m. 'id.', Du. weg c. 'id.', OHG weg m. 'id.', G Weg m. 'id.' (DRV). Derived from the strong verb \*wegan- ( q.v.).

\*wegan- s.v. 'to move, carry' - Go. ga-wigan s.v. 'to shake, move', ON vega s.v. 'to move, carry, lift, weigh', Far. vega s.v. 'to lift, raise; to weigh', OE wegan s.v. 'to move, carry, bring', E to weigh, OFri. wega s.v. 'to bring', OS gi-wegan s.v. 'to bring up', wegan s.v. 'to weigh', Du. wegen s.v. 'to weigh', OHG wegan s.v. 'to move; to weigh; to consider', G wegen s.v. 'to weigh' ⇒ \*uegh-e- (IE) - Skt. vahati 'to carry, convey, drive', YAv. vazaiti 'to pull, drive (a chariot)'. Lat veho, -ere 'to convey, carry', Lith. veiti (veiu) 'to lead, convey', OCS vesti 'to cart, lead, convey', Ru. veztf (vezu) 'cart, convey', SCr. vesti

<!-- pdf-page: 618; section: dictionary -->

578 \*wehti- 1 'to cart, convey', Alb. vjedh 'to rob, steal, purloin' < \*uegh-e-; ToA wiiskii-, ToB wask- 'to move' < \*uegh-ske-. A PIE verb associated with transport by carts. The meaning 'to weigh' presumably arose from 'to lift'. See also \*wega-, \*wehti- 2, \*wogi-, \*wagjan-, \*wagna- and \*wakk/gon-.

\*wehti- 1 f. 'thing' - Go. waihts f. 'thing, entity, matter', ON vettr f. 'being, thing', Far. vættur f. 'spirit', OE wiht f. 'being, demon, thing', OS wiht n. 'something', m.pl. 'being, demon', Du. wicht n. 'girl', OHG wiht n. 'being, demon, thing', G Wicht m. 'midget' ⇒ \*uegh-ti-(?) (NEUR) - OCS vestb f. 'thing' < \*uell-ti-(?). A Germanic-Slavic isogloss. Or can we compare Olr. fecht, MW gweith f. 'time, course; work, act' < \*uegh-ti-? If so, see \*wegan-. Also cf. Far. -vætti n. 'miserable creature' < \*wehtja-.

\*wehti- 2 f. 'weight' - ON vett f. 'id.', OE wiht f. 'id.', E weight, OFri. wicht f. 'id.' ⇒ \*uegh-ti- (DRY). A ti-stem derived from \*wegan- in the sense of 'to weigh'. Also cf. OE ge-wihte, OE, MHG ge-wihte, G Ge-wicht, Du. ge-wicht n. 'id.' < \*ga-weht-ja-.

\*weljan- 1 s.v. 'to want' - Go. wiljan w.v. 'id.', ON vilja w.v. 'id.', Far. vilja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. wila w.v. 'id.', OE willan w.v. 'id.', OFri. willa w.v. 'id.', Du. willen w.v. 'id.', OHG wellan w.v. 'id.', G wollen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uelh₁- (IE) - Skt V[IJfte 3sg.pres.med. 'to choose, opt for', OAv. vara1Jte 3sg.pres.med. 'to choose', Lat. volo, velle 'to want' < \*ue/H-e-; OLith. velti (velml) 'to allow', Lith. pa-velti 'to want', OCS veleti, Ru. velet' (velju) 'to wish' < \*uelhi-; Gr. Dor. Atw, 'Aw 'to want, wish' < \*ulhr (cf. 'Afjμa n. 'will, spirit, courage, insolence'). In Germanic, the infinitive in \*-jan- is based on the subjunctive conjugation of the verb, cf. Go. wiljau, wileis, wili, ON vilja, viii, vii < \*weljeu, \*-fs, \*-fp, which continues a PIE optative paradigm \*uelhi-iehi-m, \*-ih₁-s, \*-ihi-t. In IE, the present was probably athematic: 3sg. \*uelh₁-ti, 3pl. \*ulhi-enti (medio-passive \*ulhi-toi, \*ulh₁-nto1). The Germanic preterite, i.e. Go. wilda vs. OE wolde, E would, OS wolda, Du. wou, OHG wolta, G wollte appears to continue a primary aorist 3sg. \*uelhrt, 3pl. \*ulh₁-nt that also formed the basis for Gr. Dor. 'Atw < \*ulh₁-e-. See also \*weljan- 2 and \*waljan-.

\*weljan- 2 m. 'will, wish' - Go. wilja m. 'id.', ON viii m. 'id.', Far. vilji m. 'id.', Elfd. wile m. 'id.', OE willa m. 'id.', E will, OFri. willa m. 'id.', OS willio m. 'id.', Du. wil c. 'id.', OHG willo, willio m. 'id.', G Wille m. 'id.' ⇒ \*uelh₁-ion- (DRY /IE). Ajan-stem to \*weljan- 1 (q.v.). Sw. vilja f. 'id.' is close to the equally feminine OCS volja f. 'will'. According to Kortlandt 1997b: 162, this volja may

<!-- pdf-page: 619; section: dictionary -->

\*welpja- 579 continue a proterodynamic ihi-stem. The Germanic word is best reconstructed as ajan-stem, however.

\*welfJja- adj. 'wild, uncultivated, untamed' - Go. wilpeis adj. 'id.', ON villr adj. 'id.', OE wild adj. 'id.', E wild, OFri. wilde adj. 'id.', OS wild adj. 'id.', Du. wild adj. 'id.', OHG wildi adj. 'id.', G wild adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ue/t-io- (WEUR) - MW gwyllt adj. 'wild' < \*uelt-io-; Mir. geilt adj. 'lunatic, panic-stricken fugitive from battle' < \*uelt-i-. A Germanic-Celtic isogloss, possibly related to \*wa!pu- (q.v.). Also cf. OE wildorn. 'wild animal'.

\*weman- s.v. 'to drill(?)' - MDu. wemen s.v.(?) 'to drill(?)' (GM). The meaning of the Middle Dutch verb is not entirely certain, but in view of the instrumental noun OS wimil m. 'drill' < \*wemila-, it is plausible to assume that it meanԼ 'to perforate, bore'.

\*weni- m. 'friend' - ON vinr adj. 'id.', Far. vinur adj. 'id.', Elfd. wenn m. 'id.', OE wine m. 'id.', OFri. wine m. 'id.', OS wini m. 'id.', OHG wini m. 'friend, beloved', MHG wine m. 'id.' ⇒ \*Huenh₁-i- (IE) - Close to Olr. fine f. 'family' < \*Huen-ieh₂-; also ToA wani, ToB wina 'pleasure' and Lat. venus, -eris n. 'love, charm' < \*Huenh₁-es-. An i-stem to the root also found in \*wan a- 2 ( q.v.).

\*weni- f. 'expectation' - Go. wens f. 'hope, expectation', ON van f. 'hop·e, expectation, prospect', Far. v6n f. 'id.', Elfd. uon indecl. 'id.', OE wen f. 'supposition, opinion, thought', OFri. wen m. f. 'suspition', OS wan m. 'expectation', Du. waan c. 'delusion', OHG wan m. 'opinion, hope, fancy', MHG wan m. 'assumption, illusion, expectation', G Wahn m. 'illusion, delusion' ⇒ \*Huenh₁-i(1E). An i-stem to the root also found in \*wunskjan- ( q.v.).

\*wera- n. 'fishing weir' - Ice!. ver n. 'fishing station', OE wer m. 'weir', E weir, Du. weer n. 'fishing weir; strip ofland between two canals', MHG wer n. 'dam' ⇒ \*h₂uer-o- (IE). A noun derived from the verbal root \*h₂uer- 'to defend', for which see \*warjan-.

\*werdu- m. 'host' - Go. wairdus m. 'id.', OFri. hils-werda m. 'landlord', OS werd, wird m. 'host, landlord', Du. waard c. 'host', OHG wirt m. 'landlord, husband, host', G Wirt m. 'host' (DRY). A formation that can be analyzed as an agentive tu-stem (cf. Go. hliftus 'thief under \*hie/an- 'to steal') derived from an unknown root \*{H)uer-, which may or may not be connected with \*wara- 'aware'. If correct, this

<!-- pdf-page: 620; section: dictionary -->

580 \*werganetymology implies a proto-meaning 'attender', while the related ON veror m. 'meal' < \*werdu- could have started off meaning 'attendance'. An alternative is to derive \*werdu- from \*werpan- 'to turn, become' ( q.v.) under the assumption of an intermediate meaning 'turn' (Seebold 1980) or 'participation' (Liihr 2000: 76). Related to OFri. warst 'banquet' < \*wersta-.

\*wergan- s.v. 'to strangle' - MHG er-wergen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uergh-e- (NEUR) - Lith. veFzti (verziu) 'to tighten, restrict, tie, press' < \*uergh-ie-; OCS -vresti 'to tie' < \*uergh-e-; Pol. po-wr6z m. 'noose' < \*uorgh-o-. A strong verb related to \*wurgjan- and possibly also to \*werka- 2 (q.v.). Also cf. ON virgill m. 'strap' < \*wergila-.

\*werila/6- m./f. 'lip' - Go. wairila\* f. 'id.', OE we/eras m.pl. 'id.' (EUR/IE) - OPru. warsus 'lip' < \*uors-u-. A diminutive of \*wero-, cf. OFri. were f. 'lip'. OE we/eras was metathesized from \*were/as, possibly under the influence of PGm. \*lepaza- 'lip', cf. OHG le/fur, ODu. lepora (pl.). The word is further related to ON v9rr f. 'id.' < \*warzo-, and has sometimes been connected to \*warza- 'callus' (q.v.).

\*werka- 1 n. 'labor, work, act' - ON verk n. 'id.', Far. verk n. 'id.', Elfd. werk n. 'id.', OE weore n. 'id.', E work, OFri. werk n. 'id.', WFri. wurk n. 'id.', OS werk n. 'id.', Du. werk n. 'id.', OHG werah, werh, were n. 'id.', G Werk n. 'id.' ⇒ \*uerg-o- (IE) - Identical to Gr. £pyov n. 'work, labour, work of art' < \*uerg-o-; further cf. W gwery adj. 'active' < \*uerg-o-; Arm. gore 'work' < \* , uorg-o-. A neuter a-stem to \*wurkjan- (q.v.).

\*werka- 2 n. 'rope' - WFri. wurk n. 'string of hemp, rope', MDu. were n. 'id.', OHG werh, werih n. 'id.', G Werg n. 'id.' ⇒ \*uergh-n6- (IE? /WEUR?). Probably related to \*wergan- and \*wurgjan-, the final \*k being the result of Kluge's law (\*werka- < Pre-Gm. \*werkka- < \*uergh-n6-). Alternatively, the word can be linked to W eywareh 'hemp, rope' < \*kom-uork'lo-. The Germanic form would then have to go back to \*uer{/(l.n6-.

\*werkan- s.v. 'to bend, warp' - Sw. vurken adj. 'pulled crooked', Du. werken w.v. 'to warp (of e.g. wood)' ⇒ \*h₂uerg-e- (IE) - Hitt. !Jurki- c. 'wheel' < \*h₂urg-i-; Skt. vri:iakti 'to turn (around), lay around, avert' < \*h₂ur-n-eg-; Lat vergo, -ere 'to incline' < \*h₂uerg-e-. A marginally attested strong verb to the PIE root \*h₂uerg-.

\*weron- f. 'stye, pustule' - Nw. vere f. 'larva of the gad-fly, hole in the skin resulting from gad-fly infestation', Sw. dial. verna f. 'id.', Da. verne c. 'warble; warble fly larva', Du. weern c. 'stye', G Werre f. 'mole cricket' ⇒ \*uerH-eh₂-

<!-- pdf-page: 621; section: dictionary -->

\*werpan- 581 (IE) - Lat. varus m. 'pimple, inflamed spot on the skin', Lith. viras m. 'tapeworm, larva, pimple' < \*urH-o-; ToB yoro\* 'pustule, boil(?)', ToB weru 'blister(?)'(?). An ancient IE word for 'pustule' or 'warble (a swelling under the skin of cattle caused by the warble fly larva)' in view of the Lithuanian meanings 'tapeworm' and 'larva', which correspond quite well to G Werre f. 'mole cricket' and Sw. dial. verna, Nw. vere f. 'larva of the gad-fly' < \*wer(r)on-, \*wernon-. The latter forms, n-stems, are all derived from Pre-Gm. \*uerH-on, gen. \*uerH-n-6s (for the formal variation, cf. \*ster(r)a/on- 'star'), as are OHG werra and werna f. 'varicose'. Swi. (App.) wera f. 'stye', on the other hand, can only continue \*warj6-, the e continuing \*a with primary umlaut.

\*werpan- s.v. 'to warp, sling, throw' - Go. wairpan s.v. 'to throw', ON verpa s.v. 'to throw; to pile up; to lay eggs', Far. verpa s.v. 'to lay eggs', OE weorpan s.v. 'to cast, throw, fling', E to warp, OFri. werpa s.v. 'id.', OS werpan s.v. 'id.', Du. werpen s.v. 'id.', OHG werfan s.v. 'id.', G werfen s.v. 'id.' ̈ \*uergw·e- (IE) - Gr. p£μǢoμm 'to roam, go around' < \*ure-m-gw·e-; Gr. p6μǢoc; m. 'top, wheel, spinning motion' < \*uro-m-gw·o-. LIV2: 689 connects OCS vresti (vrbgp) 'to throw' (for which see \*wrekan-), but the original meaning of the Germanic verb was 'to bend', cf. E warped wood and ON aldri orpinn 'bent by age'. This allows for a link with Gr. p£μǢoμm 'to roam, go around' and p6μǢoc; 'circular movement', assuming that this is an n-present of a root \*uergw-. The Gm. p may very well be a reflex of PIE \*gw, this development being expected after resonants in words starting with a labial (cf. \*fimfe, \*wulfa- and perhaps also \*snerkan- - \*snerpan-). Note that OHG worpfozen 'iactare' < \*wurpatjan- implies an iterative \*wurpon-.

\*werpa- adj. 'worth' - Go. wairps adj. 'id.', ON verðr adj. 'id.', Far. verður adj. 'id.', Elfd. werd adj. 'id.', OE weorp, warp adj. 'id.', E worth, OFri. werth, werd, wird adj. 'id.' (GM). An adjective of unknown origin: W gwerth m. 'price' is a loanword from OE weorp n. 'id.' < \*werpa-, cf. Go. wairp, ON verfJ, OFri., OS werth, OHG werd n. 'id.'. Also cf. Go. wairpon, OE weorpian, OS gi-werthon w.v. 'to estimate' < \*werpojan- and ON virða, OE ge-wierpan, MHG wirden w.v. 'to estimate' < \*werþjan-.

\*werpan- s.v. 'to come about, happen, become' - Go. wairpan s.v. 'id.', ON verða s.v. 'id.', Far. verða s.v. 'id.', Nw. verta s.v. 'id.', Elfd. werda s.v. 'id.', OE weorfJan s.v. 'id.', OFri. wertha s.v. 'id.', OS werthan s.v. 'id.', Du. warden irr.v. 'id.', OHG werdan s.v. 'id.', G werden s.v. 'id.' ̈ \*uert-e- (IE) - ToA wart- 'to throw', Skt. vartate 'to turn (around), roll', Lat. verto, -ere 'to turn' < \*uert-e-;

<!-- pdf-page: 622; section: dictionary -->

582 \*wesan- 1 Lith. virsti (virstu) 'to fall, collapse, turn into', OPru. wlrst 'to become' < \*urt-ske-. A strong verb of IE origin. The original meaning 'to turn' developed into 'to become' in Germanic, as e.g. in English, but OHG wurst, G Wurst f. 'sausage' < \*wursti- may have preserved the older 'to twist'. Note that the West Norse forms with t, e.g. Far. veroa, pret. var{), vart, ptc. voroin, Nw. verta, pret. vart, ptc. vorte, Elfd. werda, pret. wart, ptc. worteo, developed from ON varp ( < PGm. \*warpe) with t from word-final p. See also \*wardjan- and \*wurdi-.

\*wesan- 1 s.v. 'to be' - Go. wisan s.v. 'id.', ON vesa, vera s.v. 'id.', Far. vera s.v. 'id.', Elfd. wcirci s.v. 'id.', OE wesan s.v. 'id.', OFri. wesa s.v. 'id.', OS wesan s.v. 'id.', Du. wezen s.v. 'id.', OHG wesan s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂ues-e- (IE) - Hitt. buiszi - bu5anzi\* 'to live; to survive' < \*h₂ues-ti, \*h₂us-enti; Skt. vasati, YAv. vavhaiti 'to dwell' < \*h₂ues-e-; Gr. tauw 'to sleep, rest; pass the night' < \*h₂i-h₂ues-e-; ToB was- 'to dwell, stay'; Olr. foaid 'to spend the night, sleep with someone', MW kyuarhos < \*kom-ari-wos-o- 'to wait, expect' < \*h₂uos-. The verb, which in IE meant 'to stay', became part of a suppletive paradigm with the presentic forms of the IE root \*hies- 'to be', cf. Go. im, is, ist, ON em, est, es, OE eom, eart, is < PGm. \*immi, \*esi, \*esti corresponding to e.g. Skt. asmi, asi, asti and Gr. dμ[, £l<;, d < PIE \*hies-mi, \*hiesi, \*hies-ti etc. As a result, only the preterite forms are attested, cf. Go. 3sg. was, 3pl. wesun, ON vas, vpru, OE wres, wæron, OS, OHG was, warun < \*wase, \*wezunp. See also \*westi-.

\*wesan- 2 s.v. 'to consume, feast' - Go. waila wisan s.v. 'to feast, enjoy', fra-wisan s.v. 'to consume', OE wesan s.v. 'to feast', ME wes 'tended livestock, pastured', OHG jir-wesan w.v. 'id.', ⇒ \*ues-e- (IE) - To Hitt. 1Jesi-, 1Je5ai- c. 'pasture' (whence 1Jesije/a- 'to pasture' < \*ues-oi-; ToA wasri 'pasture' < \*wes-ri-; Av. vastra- n. 'pasture' < \*ues-tro-; OI fess f. 'food' < \*ues-teh₂- (LIV2: 693-4). The link with PIE \*ues- 'to pasture' is corroborated by ME wes pret. 'tended livestock, grazed' (Stiles 1985). See also \*westi-.

\*westera- adv. 'west, westwards' - ON vestr adv. 'id.', Far. vestur adv. 'id.', Elfd. wester adv. 'id.', OFri. wester adv. 'id.', OS westar adv. 'id.', OHG westar adv. 'id.', MHG wester adv. 'id.' ⇒ \*uekwsp-tero-(?) (IE?) - Hitt. ispant- c. 'night' < \*(kw)sp-ent-; Skt. k!jap-, YAv. x5ap- f. 'id.', NP fob 'id.', Oss. rexsrev / cexsrevre 'id.' < \*kwsep-; Arm. giser 'id.' < \*ueskw-ero-(?); Gr. E01t£po<; m. 'evening', Lat. vesper m. 'id.' < \*ue(kw)sp-ero-; MW ucher m./f. 'id.' < \*euskw-ero-; Lith. viikaras, Latv. vakars m. 'id.' < \*uokw-oro-; OCS vecero, Ru. veter, SCr. vece m. 'id.' < \*uekw-ero-.

<!-- pdf-page: 623; section: dictionary -->

\*westi- 583 An adverb without a certain IE etymology. It was created with the contrastive \*tero-suffix, which leaves a base \*wes-. This element has been compared to Skt. avas adv. 'down', but the -as of this form is a productive adverbial suffix in Sanskrit, and is therefore not likely to be old. The alternative connection with the PIE word for 'evening' (see above) is semantically more attractive in view of \*sunpera- 'south' (= 'towards the (rising) sun'), but it is not entirely certain whether the implied reconstruction \*uekwsp-terowould give PGm. \*westera-. For a recent discussion of the Indo-European word, see Beekes 2004.

\*westi- f. 'being; staying' - Go. wists f. 'essence, nature', ON vist f. 'stay, abode; subsistence, food', OE wist f. 'being, subsistence, food', OS wist m. 'food' (DRV). A merger of two different ti-stems: one created to \*wesan- 1 'to stay; to be', another to \*wesan- 2 'to consume, feast'.

\*weta- adj. 'wet' - ON vatr adj. 'id.', Far. vatur adj. 'id.', Elfd. wdt adj. 'id.', OE w.i?t adj. 'id.', E wet, OFri. wet adj. 'id.', WFri. wiet adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ued-o- (DRV). A V[ddhi-adjective related to \*watar- - \*watan- 'water' (q.v.). Also cf. Ice!., Far. va?ta, Far. va?ti f. 'moisture, liquid' < \*wetf-, \*wetjo[n)- and Ice!. va?ska f. 'moist' < \*wetiskon-.

\*wetman- m. 'dowry' - OE weotuma, wetma-, wituma m. 'id.', OFri. wetma m. 'id.', ODu. withemo m. 'id.', OHG widomo, widemo m. 'id.', G Wittum m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁ued-mon- (IE) - Gr. £c5vov n. 'dowry', Hom. EEc5va pl. 'id.', ORu. veno, Ru. veno, SCr. dial. vijeno n. 'id.' < \*h₁ued-no-. Germanic preserves the original mn-stem nom. \*h₁ued-mn, gen. \*h₁ud-mn-6s that developed into the no-stems found in Slavic and Greek due to dissimilation of the m in the weak cases. The d in OHG widomo may have arisen due to secondary voicing of PGm. \*t before m, cf. OHG gi-smagmo m. 'taste' < \*smakman-. In view of the parallelism with ODu. withemo, it is more likely, however, that WGm. had a variant \*wepman-. This form can technically have developed out of \*wedman- by Bahder's law (-dm- > \*-Pm-), for which cf. \*budman- - \*buttman-. The origin of a possible form \*wedman(cf. G Wittum), is unclear as well, as the PIE root was \*h₁ued- rather than \*h₁uedh-. Perhaps, the formation was influenced by \*wedan- 'to conjoin'.

\*wepra- - \*wedra· n. 'weather' - ON veor n. 'id.', Far. veour n. 'id.', Elfd. weoer n. 'id.', OE weder n. 'id.', E weather, OFri. weder n. 'id.', OS wedar n. 'id.', Du. weer n. 'id.', OHG wetar n. 'id.', G Wetter n. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂uh₁-etr-o-(?) (IE?). The Walser German form wedar as in 's wedar dr fenstro 'windowpane' (Schott 1842: 340) points to a variant \*wepra- beside \*wedra-. This means that the word may have been a mobile neuter in Pre-Gm., viz. \*uetr-om, pl.

<!-- pdf-page: 624; section: dictionary -->

584 \*wepru- \*uetr-eh₂. This proto-form further implies that the received etymological link with OCS vedro n. 'clear sky' < \*uedhro- cannot be maintained. The connection with OCS vetro 'air, wind' < \*uehi-tro-, Lith. vJtra < \*h₂uehi-treh₂(see \*wean-) is formally problematic as well, because the underlying form would give PGm. \*wepra- (laryngeals are not lost before \*CR-, pace Schindler apud Peters 1999). Olr. feth f. 'wind(?)' can theoretically be derived from \*ueto- without a laryngeal, but is more likely to have developed out of \*h₂uehi-t6- by pretonic shortening (Dybo's law). The only way to retain the connection of the Germanic word with the PIE root \*h₂ueh₁- 'to blow' is to reconstruct it as \*h₂uh₁-etr-o- (or \*h₂uhi-edhr-o- in view of OCS vedro) with the same suffix as found in e.g. Gr. Hom. AO£Tp6v 'bath, bathing site' (derived from A.ouw 'to bathe').

\*wepru- adv. '(male) lamb; yearling' - Go. wiprus m. 'lamb, young ram', ON veiJr m. 'ram', OE weiJer m. 'id.', E wether, OS withar m. 'id.', EDu. weer c. 'id.', OHG widar m. 'id.', G Widder m. 'id.' ⇒ \*uet-ru- (IE) - Skt. vatsa- m. 'calf, Oss. w.1'1s 'id.' < \*uet-so-; Gr. fral.ov n. 'young animal, yearling', Lat. vitu/us m. 'calf < \*uet-elo-; Olr.feis f. '(young) sow', W gwys 'id.' < \*uet-s-ih₂-(?); Alb. viң m. 'one-year-old calf. If the etymological connection with PIE \*uet- 'year' (see \*ferudl) is correct, the original meaning of the word must have been 'yearling, one-year-old animal' (see further Rau 2007).

\*wia- n. 'egg(?), brood(?)' - Ice!. v[ n. 'fly egg', Far. vfggj n. 'id.', Sw. dial. vi n. 'mating of birds; brood of birds' ⇒ \*h₂uei-(i)o- (IE). An exclusively Norse word. In view of the semantic scope of Finland Sw. vi, it is not inconceivable that PGm. \*w/a- (\*wija-) represents a closely related, ablauting formation to PGm. \*ajja- 'egg' (q.v.) < \*h₂ou-io-. The latter is generally derived from the IE word for 'bird', \*h₂eu-i-s, gen. \*h₂u-ei-s (cf. Skt. vfs, gen. ves), and the oblique stem of this paradigm would be an attractive derivational base for \*wla-. Also cf. Ice!. vfna-st, Nw. vina w.v. 'to mate (of birds and insects)'.

\*wiba- n. 'woman, wife' - ON vif n. 'id.', Far. poet. viv n. 'id.', OE wif n. 'id.', E wife, OS wifn. 'id.', Du. wijfn. 'id.', OHG wlb n. 'id.', G Weib n. 'id.' (GM?). No clear etymology. Some have connected the word to Go. bi-waibjan, OE wcefan w.v. 'to cover, wrap', assuming that the primary meaning was a 'veiled woman'. The connection with To A kip, ToB kwipe 'shame; modesty' (Schmidt/Strunk 1989) is possible, too, but requires a considerable semantic shift.

\*wlda- adj. 'wide, distant' - ON vfiJr adj. 'wide, large, extensive', OE wld adj. 'wide', E wide, OFri. wld adj. 'wide, broad', Du. wijd adj. 'wide', OHG wft adj.

<!-- pdf-page: 625; section: dictionary -->

\*widu- 585 'wide, spacious', G weit adj. 'wide, distant' ⇒ \*h₁uei-dhh₁-o- (IE) - ToAB wiitk- 'to separate, distinguish' < \*h₁ui-dhh₁-sk-e-; Lat divido, -ere 'to separate, divide' \*-h₁ui-dhhi-. A full-grade adj. created to a verbal formation \*h₁ui-dheh₁- 'to set apart', with \*h₁ui- being dissimilated from \*dui- 'in two' due to the following dental (Lubotsky 1994). The same univerbation is found in \*widu(w)on- 'widow' (q.v.) and perhaps also in \*widu- 'wood'.

\*widu- m. (n.?) 'tree; wood' - ON vior, gen. vioar m. 'id.', Far. viour m. 'id.', Elfd. wio m. 'id.', OE widu, wiodu, wudu m. 'id.', E wood, OHG witu m./n. 'id.', MHG wite, wit m. 'id.' ⇒ \*(h₁)uidh-u- (EUR/IE?) - Olr. fid m. 'tree; wood; forest', OW guid, MW gwyd coll. 'trees' < \*uidh·u-. The formation \*uidh-u- could constitute a Celto-Germanic isogloss, but it is likely that also Lith. vidus m. 'middle' ( < "forested area between two centers of habitation") belongs here. If so, the connection further allows for a PIE reconstruction \*h₁ui-dhh₁-o- with an original meaning 'set apart, abandoned' (cf. \*wida- 'wide' and \*widu(w)on- 'widow'). Note that the word is embedded in a wider etymological cluster in Celtic, cf. W gwyd adj. 'wild, untilled, overgrown' < \*ueidh-o- and Olr. ftad m. '1) wild animal; 2) uncultivated land, a waste spot' < \*ueidh-u- (for the full grade, see Malzahn 2011).

\*widu(w)6n- f. 'widow' - Go. widuwo f. 'id.', OE widewe, wudewe f. 'id.', E widow, OFri. widwe f. 'id.', OS widowa f. 'id.', Du. weduwe c. 'id.', OHG wituwa f. 'id.', G Witwe f. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁ui-dhhruh₂- (IE) - Gr. i]WEo<; m. 'unmarried youth; unmarried young woman', Lat vidua f. 'widow', Olr. fedb f. 'id.', OPru. widdewil 'widow', OCS vodova, Ru. vdova, SCr. udov-ica f. 'widow' < \*h₁ui-dhh₁-(e)u-o/eh₂-. An on-stem created on the basis of PIE \*h₁ui-dhhruh₂-. Also cf. \*widu(w)ernan-: Go. widuwairna m. 'orphan' (with a suffix \*-ern- as in ON perna, OHG diorna f. 'maid; girl' < \*pew-ern-on-) and OE widuwa, OHG wituwo m. 'widower' < PGm. \*widu(w)an-.

\*wiggan- f. 'weevil(?)' - OE wicga, ear-wicga m. 'beetle, earwig', E earwig ⇒ \*uegh-on- (IE) - Hitt yagai- 'grain weevil' < \*uo(gJh.oi-? The OE form points to a Pre-Gm. root \*uigh- rather thԽn \*uegh-, but nevertheless seems conceivable that the word is connected to Hitt yagai- < \*uogh-oi-; perhaps the OE word is some kind of expressive distortion.

\*wiha- 1 adj. 'holy' - Go. weihs adj. 'id.', OHG wih adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ueik-o- (WEUR) - Lat. victima f. 'sacrificial animal' < \*uik-tm-eh₂-. Also cf. Go. weihan w.v. 'to bless, consecrate' < \*wfhen- and ON vigja, OFri. wi(g)a, OS wihian, Du. wijden, OHG wihen, G weihen w.v. 'id.' < \*wih/gjan-. Related to \*wiha- 2 and \*wikki5n- (q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 626; section: dictionary -->

586 \*wiha- 2

\*wiha- 2 m./n. 'sanctuary' - ON ve n. 'mansion; sanctuary', OE weoh, wig m. 'idol', OS wfh m. 'temple' (WEUR). Closely related to \*wiha- 1 'holy' (q.v.).

\*wfhan- s.v. 'to fight' - Go. weihan s.v. 'id.', ON vega s.v. 'id.', Far. vega s.v. 'to fight, struggle; to fence; to chop', OE wigan s.v. 'to fight, do battle', MDu. wijgen w.v. 'id.', OHG wfgan s.v. 'id.', MHG wigen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uik-e- (EUR) - Lat. vinco, -ere 'to conquer, overcome' < \*ui-n-k-e-; Olr. fichid 'to fight', W amwyn 'id.' ( < \*ambi-wik-) < \*uik-e-. A strong verb. The original zero-grade present \*wihan- - \*wigan- is continued as Go. and-waihando ptc. 'warring', ON vega s.v. 'to fight' and MHG wehen s.v. 'to brandish', the latter two forms displaying Ab/autentgleisung due to a-mutation. The zero-grade formations were usually replaced by full-grade forms in the different Germanic dialects.

\*wikkon- w.v. 'to practice sorcery' - OE wiccian w.v. 'id.', WFri. wikje w.v. 'to tell the future, warn', MDu. wicken w.v. 'to practice sorcery', MHG wicken · w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uik-neh₂- (WEUR). Derived from the same root as found in \*wfha- 1 and \*wfha- 2 (q.v.). The verb served as the derivational base for OE wicca m. 'witch', wicce f. 'id.' < \*wikka/on- and MHG wicker m. 'soothsayer'. Also cf. OE wigo/ adj. 'prophetic' < \*wigala- and OE wlglian, (M)Du. wichelen 'to practice divination'.

\*wikon- f. 'sequence, week' - Go. wiko f. 'order, sequence', ON vika f. 'week; sea-mile', Far. vika f. 'week', Elfd. wikd f. 'id.', OE wice, wucu f. 'id.', E week, OFri. wike f. 'id.', Du. week c. 'id.', OHG wecha f. 'id.', G Woche f. 'id.' (IE). The NWGm. meaning 'week' is generally assumed to have developed from 'to give way' (see \*wfkwan-). The clearly connected Far. vika w.v. 'to shift, move' < \*wiko(ja)n- is semantically closer, however, to the Gothic meaning 'sequence' as well as to ON vfxl, OS, OHG wehsa/, Du. wisse/, G Wechsel m. 'shift' < \*wfh-sla-, and thus presupposes an original meaning 'shift'.

\*wikwan- s.v. 'to give way; to turn (away)' - ON vfkva, ykva, vfkja s.v. 'id.', Far. vfkja s.v. 'id.', Elfd. waika s.v. 'id.', OE wfcan s.v. 'id.', OFri. wfka s.v. 'id.', OS wfkan s.v. 'id.', Du. wijken s.v. 'id.', OHG wfhhan s.v. 'id.', G weichen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₃ueig-u-e- (IE) - Skt. vijate 'to tremble, wince, move up (of a wave); shrink, start back', Av. vaejar:it- ptc. 'swinging', NP bextan 'to shake', Oss. wijjyn / wejjun 'to shake, wave' < \*h₃u(e)ig-e-; ToAB wikii.- 'to dwindle'; Gr. oi'.yvuμt, Aeol. o'(yw 'to open'; Lith. vigrus adj. 'fast' < \*h₃uig-ru-. A w-present to the PIE root \*h₃ueig-. The origin of the w-suffix is unclear: perhaps it arose in a back-formation to a nu-present \*h₃uig-neu-. See also \*wikon- and \*waika-.

<!-- pdf-page: 627; section: dictionary -->

\*wlman- 587

\*wiman- m. 'osier, wattle' - MLG wfm{e) m. 'id.', MDu. wfme f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ueh₁i-mon-(?) (IE) - Lat. vfmen, -inis n. 'flexible branch, used for wickerwork', Mir.flam f. 'chain' < \*ueh₁i-mon-. Derived from the verbal root \*ueh₁i- 'to twist', for which see \*wajju-.

\*winda- m. 'wind' - Go. winds m. 'id.', ON vindr m. 'id.', OE wind m. 'id.', E wind, OFri. wind m. 'id.', WFri. wyn c. 'id.', OS wind m. 'id.', Du. wind c. 'id.', OHG wint m. 'id.', G Wind m. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₂uehi-ent-o- (IE) - Hitt. hu!!ant- c. 'wind' < \*h₂uhi-ent-; ToA want, want, ToB yente f. 'wind', Lat. ventus m. 'id.', W gwynt 'id.' < \*h₂ueh₁-(e)nt-o-; Skt. vāta-, OAv. viita-, YAv. viita- m. 'id.', NP biid 'id.', Oss. wad / wad[ CE) 'storm' < \*h₂uehi-nt-o-. The word is usually reconstructed as \*h₂uehi-nt-6-: the PGm. \*i would then be due to Osthoff's law and the PGm. \*d to Verner's law. It is unlikely, however, that this proto-form can be maintained, as it in all probability would yield PGm. \*\*we(w)unda- with vocalization of the n after the laryngeal (cf. \*ju[w)unpi- 'youth') < \*h₂iu-Hn-ti-. I therefore reconstruct a trisyllabic proto-form \*h₂uehi-ent-o-, which through \*wenda- would regularly yield PGm. \*winda- assuming that the \*d arose from \*t in post-post-tonic position (note that Verner's law apparently preceded Osthoff's law). The presence of two full grades is not problematic, because the original paradigm must have had two stem variants anyway in view of Hitt. bu!fant- < \*h₂uh₁-ent- and Skt. vāta- < \*h₂uehi-nt-. It is therefore not unlikely that Germanic generalized the full grade of both the root and the suffix.

\*windan- s.v. 'to wind' - Go. bi-windan s.v. 'to wind, wrap', ON vinda s.v. 'to wring, twist', Far. vinda s.v. 'to turn; to twist, wring', Elfd. winda w.v. 'to hurl, sling', OS windan s.v. 'turn, move', Du. winden w.v. 'to wind', OHG wintan s.v. 'id.', G winden s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uendh-e- (EUR) - Umb. vetu, aha-uendu, pre-uendu 3sg.ipv. 'must turn' < \*uend-etod; Arm. gind 'ring' < \*uendh-eh₂-; perhaps also Skt. vandhur- m. 'chariot-seat, frame-work of a chariot' (RV); vandhuran. 'chariot-seat'. A strong verb to a marginally attested root \*uendh-. See also \*wandjan\*wandu-, and \*wan tu-.

\*winnan- s.v. 'to suffer' - Go. winnan s.v. 'to suffer', ON vinna s.v. 'to work, labor', Far. vinna s.v. 'to carry out, perform; to gain, win', Elfd. winna s.v. 'to reach; to win', OE winnan s.v. 'id.', E to win, OFri. winna s.v. 'to achieve, win', OS winnan s.v. 'to suffer; to gain', Du. winnen s.v. 'to gain; to win', OHG winnan s.v. 'to labor; to win', G winnen s.v. 'to win' ⇒ \*uenu-e- (IE) - Skt. van6ti 'to win, defeat, procure' < \*un-neu-; Skt. vanate 3sg.med. 'to win', YAv. vanaiti 'to defeat' < \*uen-. A strong verb derived from the PIE root \*uen-, probably back-formed to a nu-present like Skt. van6ti: PIE \*uen-nye- would regularly give \*winnan-

<!-- pdf-page: 628; section: dictionary -->

588 \*wintruthrough assimilation of the labial glide. Cf. Go. winna, winno f. 'suffering', OHG winna f. 'battle' < \*winno(n)- as well as \*wunni-.

\*wintru- m. 'winter; year' - Go. wintrus adj. 'id.', ON vetr m. 'id.', Far. vetur m. 'id.', Elfd. witter m. 'id.', OE winter m. 'id.', E winter, OFri. winter m. 'id.', OS wintar m. 'id.', Du. winter c. 'id.', OHG wintar m. 'id.', G Winter m. 'id.' (GM). A word with no certain etymology. The traditional derivation from a nasalized variant of the root \*wed- 'to be wet' (see \*weta-) is unconvincing: it is based on the wrong analysis of Lat unda f. 'wave' as continuing \*u-n-d-eh₂- rather than correct \*ud-neh₂- (with Thurneysen's law). The connection with Olr. find adj. 'white' < \*windo- is semantically weak.

\*wira- m. 'man' - Go. wair m. 'id.', ON verr m. 'id.', OE wer m. 'id.', OS wer m. 'id.', OHG wer m. 'id.' ::) \*uiH-r6- (IE) - Identical to Skt. virci- m. 'man, hero, son', Av. vira- m. 'man, human', MP wir 'man, hero', Lith. vjras, Latv. virs, OPru. wijrs m. 'man', Lat. vir m. 'id.', Olr. fer m. 'id.', OW gur, MW gwr 'id.'; ToA wir adj. 'young; fresh'(?). One of the IE words for 'man'. The Gm. form developed from \*ylr6- by Dybo's law (pretonic shortening).

\*wissa- adj. 'certain' - Go. un-wiss adj. 'uncertain', ON viss adj. 'id.', OE wiss adj. 'id.', OFri. wis adj. 'id.', Du. wis adj. 'id.', on-gewis adj. 'uncertain', OHG gi-wis adj. 'certain', G ge-wifl adj. 'id.' ::) \*uid-to- (IE) - Cf. Skt. vittci- adj. 'id.', Gr. CiLOTO<; adj. 'unseen'. An adjective continuing the original past ptc. to \*witan- 'to know'.

\*wisund- m. 'wisent' - ON vfsundr, gen. visundar m. 'id.' ( « WGm.?), OE wesend, weosend m. 'id.', MLG wisent, wesent m. 'id.', MDu. wesent m. 'id.', OHG wisunt, wisant, pl. wisunti m. 'id.', G Wisund m. 'id.' :::) \*uisondfh₃. (NEUR/NIE) - Lith. stumbras m. 'wisent, aurochs', Latv. sumbrs, stumbr(i)s, siibrs m. 'aurochs', OPru. wissambs' 'id.'; Ru. zubr m. 'wisent', dial. iz(j)ubr 'red deer', Oss. dombaj 'aurochs', Abkh. a-dawp-ey 'id.'. The word has been derived from several IE bases, e.g. \*uis- 'to smell' (VLat. vissio 'smell, fart'), \*uis- 'horned' (Skt. vi$&na- f. 'horn') and \*ues- 'to consume' (Stiles 2004), but none of these IE-oriented explanations seem convincing. The various Balta-Slavic forms combined with the Germanic word result in a remarkable cluster of formally irreconcilable proto-forms. This, as well as the occurrence of some correspondences in the Caucasian languages, is highly suggestive of a non-IE word that passed into the different daughter languages independently: 1) Lith. stumbras and Ru. zubr point to \*tsombfhL and \*dzombfhL correspondingly; 2) Ru. izOJubr and OPru. wissambs' appear to continue \*uidzombfh₃_; and 3) Gm. \*wisund- seems to have developed from \*uisondfh₃ .. I therefore start from a non-IE element

<!-- pdf-page: 629; section: dictionary -->

\*wit- 589 \*dzombfh₃ or \*dzondfh₃that could be prefixed by an element \*wi-. Note that the Germanic word in view of the OHG i-stem inflection is likely to have been a root noun, a morphological category that demonstrably incorporated several other ancient loanwords, cf. e.g. \*arwit- 'pea' and \*gait- 'goat' (q.v.).

\*wit- pron. 'we (du.)' - Go. wit, obi. ugkis pron. 'id.', ON vit, obi. okkr pron. 'id.', Far. vit, obi. okkum pron. 'id.', Elfd. wjo, obi. uoss pron. 'id.', OE wit, obi. unc pron. 'id.', OS wit, obi. unk pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*uid (IE) - Lith. vedu 'we (du.)', ocs ve 'id.' < \*ued-. The first person dual pronoun. The nominative \*wit was accompanied by an oblique form \*unke. This form may be composed of the PIE first person dual base \*nhr (Skt. nau, YAv. na, Gr. vw 'us (du.)', OCS na 'we (pl.)' < \*noh₁-) plus the emphatic particle \*ge. It is not unlikely either, however, that it regularly continues PIE \*nh₁ue, cf. Skt. avcim pron. 'we (du.)' ( < \*nh₁u+ \*-(H)om), with oralization of the laryngeal between a resonant and a labial glide (cf. \*taikwer-). Note that the salient dative form \*unkiz as represented by Go. ugkis and ON okkr (with o after oss 'us (pl.)') was created by the addition of the dative element \*-iz. See also \*wiz and \*jut.

\*witan- pret.-pres. 'to know' - Go. witan pret.-pres. 'id.', ON vita pret.-pres., Elfd. wita pret.-pres. 'id.', OE witan pret-pres. 'id.', OFri. wita pret-pres. 'id.', OS witan pret.-pres. 'id.', EDu. weten pret.-pres. 'id.', OHG wizzan pret-pres. 'id.', G wissen pret-pres. 'id.' ⇒ \*uoid- (IE). A verb continuing a PIE primary perfect: PGm. 3sg. \*waite, 3pl. \*witun from \*uoid-e, \*uid-nt. The verbal root \*ueid- had the primary meaning 'to see' (cf. \*wftan-), but in perfective use came to mean 'to know' through 'to have seen'.
