\*witan- s.v. 'to see; to blame' - Go.fra-weitan s.v. 'to avenge', OE ge-wftan s.v. 'to see, look; to go', Du. wijten s.v. 'to blame', OHG (far-)wfzan s.v. 'to rebuke', G ver-weisen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*ueid-e- (IE) - Gr. Et6oμm 'to seem, appear, give the appearance, resemble' < \*ueid-e-; Skt. vindciti 'to find, acquire, get', Olr. ro-finnadar 'to find, experience' < \*ui-n-d-e-. A strong verb related to the preterite-present \*witan- (q.v.). The meaning 'to blame' appears to have developed from 'to witness', a meaning that was already in the parent language in a legal or ritual contexts, cf. Go. weitwops m. 'witness', OPru. waidewut 'priest', ToB uwe adj. 'learned' < \* uid-uon-.

\*witen- w.v. 'to watch, heed' - Go. witan w.v. 'id.', ON vitaor ptc. 'known', OE witian w.v. 'id.', OHG wizzen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*uid-ehr (IE) - Identical to OCS videti, Ru. vfdet' (vftu), SCr. vl'djeti 'to see', Lat video, -ere''id.'. A stative formation to \*witan- ( q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 630; section: dictionary -->

590 \*witopa-

\*witopa- n. 'law' - Go. witop n. 'id.', Du. wet c. 'law', OHG wizzod m./n. 'law, order, divine will' ⇒ \*uid-ot- (DRV). A PIE t-stem created to the root of\*witan- (q.v.). The formation is derivationally close to ON vitaor adj. 'known, proved; marked out', OS witod adj. 'destined'.

\*witt/don- w.v. 'to tremble' - Go. wipon w.v. 'id.', Ice!. vioa w.v. 'to waver, swing', Nw. vitta w.v. 'to wave, beckon' ⇒ \*uit(h₂)-neh₂- (IE) - Skt. vyathate 'to tremble' < \*uieth₂-e-; Skt. 6-vithura- adj. 'not weakened, not trembling', YAv. aif3i8iira- (for \*auui{)ura-) adj. 'strong' < \*o-uith₂-ur-o-. If Go. wipon is secondary for \*widon, it can together with ON vioa and Nw. vitta probably be identified as an iterative 3sg. \*wittopi, 3pl. \*widunanþi < \*uit(h₂)-neh₂-ti, \*uit(h₂)-nh₂-enti. The appurtenance of G wedeln w.v. 'to wag; to wave; whirl' is more uncertain, on the other hand, because of the potential link with OHG wadalon 'to waver' < \*wap-.

\*wiþra prep. 'toward, against' - Go. wiþra prep. 'id.', ON vio, vior prep. 'against; along with; with, by', Far. vio prep. 'with, at, by', OE wioer prep. 'id.', OFri. wither prep. 'against', OS withar prep. 'against, in return', MDu. weder prep. 'against, toward', OHG widar prep. 'id.' ⇒ \*h₁ui-tro- (IE) - Skt. vitaram adv. 'further' < \*h₁ui-tero-. A formation with the PIE root \*h₁ui- 'apart' and the contrastive suffix \*-tero-. See also \*wfda- 'wide'.

\*wiwan- m. 'kite' - MDu. wouwe m. 'id.', Du. wouw c. 'id.', OHG wf(w)o m. 'id.', MHG wf(w)e, wewe m. 'id.', G Weihe, Cimb. bibo m. 'id.' ⇒ \*uei-ou- (IE). The cross-dialectal variation of OHG wfwo, MDu. wouw < \*wfwan- vs. MHG wewe, Cimb. bibo < \*wiwan- points to an originally ablauting paradigm, possibly an amphidynamic u-stem nom. \*uei-ou, gen. \*ui-u-6s comparable to the inflection of PIE \*gerh₂-ou, gen. \*grh₂-u-6s 'crane' (cf. Kroonen 2011a: 258-60), cf. SCr. zeriiv < \*gerh₂-ou, Lith. gerve < \*gerh₂-u- and Lat griis < \*grh₂-u- (see under \*krana/on-). Within Germanic, a likely cognate of this formation is Ice!. via w.v. 'to guard, spy' (cf. orninn viar yfir hrceinu 'the eagle is preying on the carcass'), which is related to Skt. veti 'to strive', Lat venor, -arf 'to hunt, pursue', Lith. vyti 'to chase, hunt' < \*ueihi-. Outside Germanic, it is further possible to compare Gr. alETOI) m. 'eagle', which is usually derived from PIE \*h₂eu-i- 'bird'. The semantic shift to 'eagle' (through "big bird", cf. Vine 1998: 11-12) is remarkable, however. I therefore assume that alETOI) continues \*h₂uihi-eto-, i.e. some kind of haplology for \*h₂ui-uihi-eto"bird-hunter''.

\*wfz pron. 'we' - Go. weis, obi. uns, unsis pron. 'id.', ON ver, obi. 6s, oss, f'}ss pron. 'id.', OE we, obi. iis, acc. iisic pron. 'id.', E we, obi. us pron. 'id.', OS wl,

<!-- pdf-page: 631; section: dictionary -->

\*wlaiton- 591 we, obl. iis pron. 'id.', Du. wij, we, obl. ons pron. 'id.', OHG wir, obl. uns, acc. unsih pron. 'id.', G wir, obl. uns pron. 'id.' ⇒ \*ueies (IE). The plural pronoun of the first person. The nominative \*wfz was accompanied by the oblique form \*uns (or \*unse) < PIE \*ns (cf. Hitt. anz- 'we, us' < \*ns, Lat. nos 'we', OCS naso, Ru. nas, SCr. nas 'us' < \*nos). In West Germanic, the accusative could be marked by adding the suffix \*-ike, cf. OE iisic, OHG unsih, while in North- and East Germanic, a salient dative appears to have been created by the addition of the element \*-iz, cf. Go. unsiz, ON ess < \*unsiz. This element was abstracted from the singular dative forms \*miz and \*piz, probably also under the influence of the nominal dative plural ending \*-miz. See also \*wit and \*jiiz.

\*wlaiton· w.v. 'to look' - Go. wlaiton w.v. 'to look around', ON leita w.v. 'to search, look for', Far. leita w.v. 'id.', Elfd. Iieta w.v. 'to look for, search; to inspect', OE wliitian w.v. 'to gaze, look' (GM). An o-grade iterative to the strong verb \*wlftan- (q.v.). Note that the meaning 'to search' in ON is typically frequentative ('to look repeatedly').

\*wlakwa- adj. 'lukewarm, tepid' - OE wlacu, wlcec adj. 'id.', ME wlak adj. 'id.', MLG wlak adj. 'id.', MHG vlac adj. 'id.' (GM). An etymologically obscure adjective. The original paradigm, \*w/akwaz, gen. \*wlakwesa, developed into \*wlaku, gen. \*w/akkwes in West Germanic, with gemination in the genitive. Dialectal leveling can account for the difference between OE wlacu < \*w/ak- and MHG v/ac(k) < \*wlakk-.

\*wlanka· adj. 'bold' - OE wlanc, wlonc adj. 'proud, bold', ME wlonk adj. 'excellent, fine; haughty, arrogant', OS wlank adj. 'proud, arrogant' (GM). An adjective derived from an unattested strong verb \*wlinkan-. Also cf. the factitive OE wlencan, OS wlenkian w.v. 'to make arrogant' < \*wlankjan-.

\*wlitan- s.v. 'to see' - ON /[ta s.v. 'id.', Far. lfta s.v. 'to cast a glance (in a certain direction)', OE wlftan s.v. 'to look, gaze' ⇒ \*uleid-e-(?) (GM). No clear etymology. Pokorny IEW: 1136-7 analyzes the Gm. root \*wlft- as a root extension of PIE \*uel- 'to see' (cf. \*wulpu-), but the origins of the alleged elements -i- and \*-d- remain obscure. It is furthermore far from certain that the Gm. root-final \*t continues PIE \*d, as it may also have developed from a geminate \*tt. This would imply influence from the iterative \*wlitton- (q.v.). See also \*w/aiton-.

\*wlitton· w.v. 'to strive' - MHG litzen w.v. 'to strive for, desire' (GM). The iterative of \*wlftan- 'to see' (q.v.). The meaning 'to strive for' developed from 'to look repeatedly', which is in agreement with the iterative formation.

<!-- pdf-page: 632; section: dictionary -->

592 \*wl6ma-

\*wl6ma- adj. 'turbulent(?)' - MLG w/6m, wloum adj. 'turbulent', MDu. wloemich adj. 'confused, anxious' (GM). Perhaps from \*wl6tma-, cf. OE wlatian w.v. 'to be nauseated', MLG wlaten w.v. 'to be disgusted'. The primary meaning would then be 'to turn'.

\*woda- adj. 'delirious' - Go. wads adj. 'possessed', ON 6or adj. 'frantic, furious', OE wad adj. 'insane', Du. obs. woed adj. 'frantic, wild, crazy' w

\*uoh₂-t6- (WEUR) - Olr. faith m. 'sooth-sayer, prophet'; Oir.fath m. 'prophesy', W gwawd 'poem, satire' < \*ueh₂-tu- (Lat. viites, -is m. 'prophet, seer' is probably a Celtic loanword, cf. Gaul. Ouateis pl. 'prophet'). A Germanic-Celtic isogloss. Also cf. OHG wuot, G Wut m. 'rage, frenzy', MDu. woet m./f. 'id.', Du. woede c. 'id.' < \*w6di- < \*ueh₂-tf- and ON 6or m. 'mind, feeling; song, poetry', OE woo f. 'sound, noise; voice, song' < \*wopa/6-.

\*w6gi- adj. 'moving a lot' - MDu. woeg adj. 'fidgety, restive' (DRV). A V[ddhi-gerundive to \*wegan- (q.v.), or perhaps rather to a related, but unattested intensive verb \*wagan- < \*u6gh-e-, for which see \*wakk/gon-.

\*w6gjan- w.v. 'to (make) remember' - MHG wiiegen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). A secondary causative to \*wahan- (q.v.).

\*wokra- m. 'growth' - Go. wokrs m. 'interest, additional increment', ON okr n. 'usury', OE wocor f. 'offspring, interest', OFri. wok er m./n. 'interest; usury', Du. woeker c. 'rampant growth; usury', OHG wuohhar m.,n. 'fruit, harvest, gain', G Wucher m. 'usury' ⇒ \*h₂u6g-ro- (IE). A formation to the PIE root \*h₂eug- 'to grow', for which see \*aukan-. Since the ablaut slot is in front of the \*u in the other PIE languages, the root \*wokmust have arisen under the influence of the suffixed variant PIE \*h₂ueg-s-, for which see \*wahs(j)an-. It reappears in G Cimb. biige adj. 'fat' < \*wokja(Matzel 1989: 81) and WPhal. wauken w.v. 'to yield; to practice usury'. The latter can be a back-formation to \*wokra-, however.

\*wola- m. 'doom(?)' - OE wol m./f. 'pest, plague', OS wol m. 'pest, ruin', OHG wuol m. 'ruin, disaster' (DRV). A lengthened grade form, quasi PIE \*u6/h₃-o-, closely related to \*wa/a'the slain' (q.v.).

\*w6n6- f. 'hole in the ice' - MDu. woene f. 'id.', MHG wuone f. 'id.', G Wuhne f. 'id.' (DRV). A V[ddhi-formation derived from \*wana- 1 (q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 633; section: dictionary -->

\*worja- 593

\*worja- adj. 'intoxicated' - ON rerr adj. 'raging, furious', OE werig adj. 'tired', E weary, OFri. wirch adj. 'id.', WFri. wurch adj. 'id.', OS worig adj. 'worn out' ⇒ \*h₂uoH-r-io- (IE) - Gr. C:,poc;, Aeol. awpoc; m. 'sleep' < \*h₂uoH-ro- (cf. wpaKL<lW 'to faint' from an unattested \*wpa(}. Also cf. ON 6rar f.pl. 'fits of madness' < \*woro- and ON 6ra w.v. 'to rave, wrangle', OE worian w.v. 'to wander about, ramble' < \*worojan-.

\*wostu- adj. 'desert, waste' - OE weste adj. 'id.', OS wosti adj. 'id.', Du. woest adj. 'id.', OHG wuosti adj. 'id.', G wiist adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*ueh₂s-tu- (EUR) - Lat. vastus adj. 'empty, desolate' < \*ueh₂s-tu-; Olr. fas adj. 'empty, uninhabited, waste' < \*ueh₂s-to-. A West Germanic adjective that in all likelihood continues the same u-stem as found in Latin. In spite of the cited attestations, it therefore seems best to reconstruct the PGm. form as \*wostu- rather than \*wostja-. Further cf. OE westen, OFri. westene, OS w6stin(nia), OHG wuostinna f. 'desert' < \*w6stenj6-.

\*wopja- adj. 'sweet, pleasant' - Go. woþeis adj. 'id.', OE weoe adj. 'id.', OS wothi adj. 'id.', OHG wuodi adj. 'id.' (GM). Also cf. ON reori comp. 'preferably, better' < \*woþizan-. No further etymology.

\*wraiha- adj. 'crooked' - Sw. dial. vrci adj. 'obstinate', ME wrau adj. 'contrary, angered, cross' ⇒ \*ur6il<-o- (IE) - Identical to Gr. poLKO<; adj. 'bent'; also cf. Av. uruuisia- 'to turn' < \*uril<-ie- and OPru. per-reist 'to link' < \*-ureil<-. An old o-stem adjective to the PIE root \*ureil<- 'to twist'. Related to \*wrfhan- (q.v.).

\*wraikwa- adj. 'crooked' - Go. wraiqus\* adj. 'id.', OFri. wrcik adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*uroiH-uo-(?) (IE). An adjective usually compared to Gr. pm,6<; adj. 'crooked, bent' (cf. Beekes 2010: 1272). This etymology can theoretically be upheld by reconstructing an adjective \*ureh₂igw-o-, but the root structure of this form is rather intricate. It is more attractive to assume that \*wraikwa- is related to \*wraiha- 'crooked' (q.v.), assuming that it continues a nu-stem adjective \*uroil<-nu- that under Kluge's Jaw became Pre-Gm. \*wraikku-, thematicized \*wraikkwa-. A third possibility is to assume that the velar of \*wraikwaarose by velarization of a laryngeal between -i- and -u-: \*uroiH-uo- > \*wraikwa-, a development paralleled by e.g. \*taikwer- and \*kwi(k)wa-. The word may then be compared to Skt. vlinciti 'to crush, fell', YAv. uruuinaitis apl.f. 'pressing together') < \*uriH-nehi-.

<!-- pdf-page: 634; section: dictionary -->

594 \*wraitjan-

\*wraitjan- w.v. 'to vex, excite' - ON reita w.v. 'id.', OHG reizen w.v. 'to vex, tease, tempt', G reizen w.v. 'id.' (GM). A causative-iterative formation to \*wrltan- (q.v.).

\*wraipa- adj. 'twisted; angry' - ON reiðr adj. 'angry', Far. reiður adj. 'id.', OE wrii!/J adj. 'id.', OS wreth adj. 'wretched, hostile', OHG reid, reidi adj. 'curly, wavy' (ORV). An adjective derived from the strong verb \*wrlpan- - \*wrltan- (q.v.). Also cf. ON, Icel., Far. reioi, OSw. vrepe, Elfd. rwieoe m. 'anger' < \*wraipan-.

\*wrakjan- m. 'persecutor' - OE wrecca m. 'exile, stranger', OS wrekkio m. 'foreign warrior, stranger, exile', OHG reck(e)o m. 'fugitive, hero', G Recke m. 'champion' (ORV). A jan-stem directly derived from \*wrekan- (q.v.). The word probably originally meant 'avenger', i.e. one who ventures into unknown territory, whence both 'champion' and 'exile'. Also note OFr. garңun, Fr. garңon 'boy, servant', which is assumed to continue OLFra. \*wrakkio.

\*wrandan-(?) m. 'wren' - ON rindil-pvari m. 'id.', Ice!. rindill m. 'id.', Nw. dial. rennil m. 'wagtail', OE wrenna, werna m. 'wren', E wren, OS wrendo, wrendilo m. 'id.', OHG wrentilo m. 'id.' (GM). A word that is difficult to reconstruct because of the derivational irreconcilability of the attested forms: 1) ON rindill < \*wrendila-; 2) OS wrendilo, OHG wrentilo < \*wrandilan-; 3) OE wrenna < \*wrandjan-. No certain etymology. Unrelated to MDu. wranten w.v. 'to mope', but possibly connected to Ice!. rindill m. 'tail'. Elfd. rwainjugel m. 'brambling' does not belong here, but was derived from rwaina s.v. 'to wail' < \*wrlnan- (q.v.).

\*wranga- adj. 'twisted, wrong' - ON rangr adj. 'wry, crooked; wrong', Far. rangur adj. 'slanted, inside out, wrong', Elfd. rwaungg adj. 'contrary, obstinate', OE wrang n. 'wrong', E wrong, MDu. wranc adj. 'bitter, sharp, unfriendly, brave', Du. wrang adj. 'bitter' (ORV). An o-grade adjective derived from \*wringan- (q.v.).

\*wrankjan- w.v. 'to twist, wring' - OE wrencan w.v. 'id.', MHG renken w.v. 'id.', G renken w.v. 'id.' (ORV). One way of arriving at an etymology is to assume a causative formation related to an unattested verb \*wrinkan-, which in turn could be a nasal present to \*werkan- (q.v.). The verb is more likely to have been derived from the iterative \*wrunkon- < \*urr.;igh-neh₂-, however, for which cf. MDu. wronckelen 'to twist, squirm, wrap'. This iterative clearly belongs to the strong verb \*wringan- (q.v.).

<!-- pdf-page: 635; section: dictionary -->

\*wratjon- 595

\*wratjon- f. 'madder' - OE wrcette f. 'id.', OHG rezza f. 'id.' ⇒ \*urod-ieh₂(IE). A formation closely related to \*wr6t- 'root' ( q.v.); the madder's roots were used to obtain a red textile dye. See also \*madron-.

\*wraton- w.v. 'to roam' - Go. wraton w.v. 'to travel', ON rata w.v. 'to travel; to meet with, find (the way)', MHG razzeln w.v. 'to turn' ⇒ \*urod-eh₂- (NEUR) - Li th. r6dyti (r6dau ), Latv. radft 'to show' < \*urod-eie-. A Balta-Germanic isogloss. The link with Lith. rasti (randail) 'to find' (Trautmann 1905: 308ff.) cannot be maintained, as the accentuation of this verb points to PIE \*urodh- rather than \*urod-. Also cf. Go. wratodus m. 'trip' < \*wratodu- and Ice!. rati m. 'companion' < \*ga-wratan-.

\*wrekan- s.v. 'to pursue' - Go. wrikan s.v. 'to persecute', ON reka s.v. 'to hunt, pursue', OE wrecan s.v. 'to drive; to punish', E to wreak, OFri. wreka s.v. 'to revenge', OS wrekan s.v. 'id.', Du. wreken s.v. 'id.', OHG rehhan s.v. 'id.', G riichen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*(H)ureg-e- (IE) - Hitt. lirki- c. 'trace' < \*(H)urg-i-; Skt vrajati 'to walk, stride', OCS vresti (vrbBQ) 'to throw', SCr. vrCi 'to put, throw' < \*(H)ureg-e-. A verb that probably had some kind of legal connotation in PIE already, cf. Skt. para·vrj- 'outlaw'. Also cf. \*wrakjan- and \*wreko- (q.v.).

\*wreko- f. 'persecution, revenge' - ON rak f. 'id.', OFri. wreke f. 'revenge', OS wraka f. 'id.', Du. wraak c. 'id.', OHG rah ha f. 'id.', G Rache f. 'id.' (DRY). A lengthened-grade formation derived from \*wrekan- (q.v.). Also cf. the vṛddhi-gerundive ON rcekr adj. 'abominable' < \*wreki- and Go. wrekei f. 'persecution'.

\*wreskwan- s.v. 'to mature' - Go. ga-wrisqan s.v. 'id.', ON roskinn adj. 'mature', Ice!. roskinn adj. 'middle-aged' ⇒ \*uresgw·e- (GM). Also cf. ON r9skr adj. 'vigorous' < \*wraskwa- (whence ON r9skvast w.v. 'to grow, mature' < \*wraskwojan-) and Nw. rusk m. 'tall man' < \*wruskwa-. All formations were derived from the strong verb which, however, has no certain etymology. The connection with W gwrysg f. 'branch' < PCelt. \*wre/iskis flawed, as this word is more likely to be related to gwreid 'roots' (see under \*wurti-). The derivation from \*uredh-ske- (cf. Magnusson 1989: 790), where \*uredh- is taken to be a Schwebeablaut form of \*uerdh- 'to grow', is even more unlikely.

\*wrepu- m. 'herd (of swine)' - Go. wriþus (for expected \*wreþus) m. 'id.', ODa. vrath 'swineherd', Da. vrad c. 'id.', OE wrϋo m. 'herd; host' ⇒ \*ureh₁-tu(IE) - Skt vr&ta- m. 'troop, herd' < \*urehi-to-.

<!-- pdf-page: 636; section: dictionary -->

596 \*wrihan- Also cf. ON rdoi m. 'boar' < \*wrepan-, ON rdoa 'to have sex with' < \*wrepojan-(?) and Nw. ræda, vræda w.v. 'to be in heat' < \*wrepjan-.

\*wrlhan- m. 'instep' - MDu. wrijghe, wrijch, wrijf m. 'id.', Du. wreef c. 'id.', OHG riho m. 'id.', G Reihen m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ureili-on- (IE) - Lith. rfefos m., rfefa f. 'instep. wrist, knuckle' < \*ureili-o- (with metatonie rude). An ablauting n-stem nom. \*wriho, gen. \*wrikkaz, dat \*wriheni < \*ureil<-on, \*urili-n-6s, \*urili-en-i (Kroonen 2011a: 261-2). The zero grade forms are attested in late and dialectal Germanic only, cf. Du. dial. wree, Swi. (Rhtl.) reaha < \*wrihan- and Du. obs. wreeg < \*wrigan-. Related to \*wraiha- ( q.v.).

\*wrinan- s.v. 'to howl' - Nw. vrina s.v. 'to howl; to bare one's teeth', Elfd. rwaina s.v. 'to howl, wail' ⇒ \*ureiH-ne- (IE?). A strong verb, in association with \*hrinan-, cf. ON hrfna, Far. rfna s.v. 'to cry, wail'. Related to OE wrci!ne adj. 'lascivious' < \*wrainja- and ODu. wrenisk adj. 'id.' < \*wrainiska-. Also cf. OHG reinnio, OS wrenio, MDu. wrene m. 'stallion' < \*wrainjan-.

\*wringan- s.v. 'to wring, squeeze' - OE wringan s.v. 'to wring, twist', E to wring, OS iit-wringan s.v. 'to squeeze out', Du. wringen s.v. 'to wring', OHG ringan s.v. 'to wring; to wrestle', G ringen s.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*urengh-e- (NEUR) - Lith. rangyti 'to wind, bend' < \*urongh-eie-; Lith. rafigas m. 'wrap, clew, ring' < \*urongh-o-. On the basis of the WGm. forms, the verb is traditionally reconstructed as \*wringan-, which could be related to Lith. rangyti. There is a possibility, however, that \*wringan- replaced older \*wrinhan-, a formation that perhaps can be retrieved from !eel. rja w.v. 'to wrestle, fight; to move, tear' (related to Far. rfggin adj. 'fickle; resentful'?). This would imply a PIE root \*urenkrather than \*urengh-. However, Ice!. rjd can alternatively be reconstructed as \*rewan- (q.v.). See also \*wranga-, \*wrankjan- and \*wrungon-.

\*wrinpla-(?) m. 'a certain plant' - Gutn. rind 'clubmoss', ME writhe/ 'wild lettuce(?)' (GM). Etymology uncertain. It is possible that both the formations Gutn. rind ( < \*wrinda-) and ME writhe/ ( < \*wrinpla-) were derived from a root \*wrinp- 'to wind'. This could technically be a nasal infIXed root belonging to the strong verb \*wripan- - \*writan- 'to twist' (q.v.).

\*writan- s.v. 'to carve' - OE writan s.v. 'to write; to carve', OFri. writa s.v. 'to write', WFri. write s.v. 'to tear; to ache, hurt', OS writan s.v. 'to tear; to write' ⇒ \*ureid-e- (GM). A strong verb derived from a further unknown root \*ureid-. It merged with \*ritan- 'to tear' (cf. Du. rijten s.v. 'id.') in Old Norse and Old High Ger-

<!-- pdf-page: 637; section: dictionary -->

\*writi- 597 man, where initial \*w was lost in front of r. Also cf. \*writi-, \*wraitjan- and \*writt6n-.

\*writi- m. 'stroke' - Go. writs m. 'id.', MDu. ret m. 'crack, fissure, lacuna', oHG riz m. 'scratching, line, letter', G Rifl m. 'crack, scratch' (ORV). An i-stem to the strong verb \*wrftan- ( q.v.).

\*writton- w.v. 'to carve, write' - OHG rizz6n, rezz6n w.v. 'id.', G ritzen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*urid-neh₂- (GM). The iterative to \*writan- (q.v.). It is possible that the \*t of the strong verb continues \*-tt-, thus pointing to influence from this iterative.

\*wripan- - \*writan- s.v. 'to twist' - ON rfoa s.v. 'to turn, ring, tie', OSw. vripa s.v. 'to ring', Elfd. rwaioa s.v. 'to turn, twist, ring', OE wrioen s.v. 'to twist, ring', E writhe s.v. 'id.', MDu. writen s.v. 'to twist, ring, wrap', OHG ridan s.v. 'to wind, turn', MHG riden s.v. 'to wind, wrap' ⇒ \*ureit-e- (NEUR) - Lith. riesti (rieCiu) 'to bend, curve, warp', Latv. riest 'to fall off, shed, shoot, sprout' < \*ureit-ie-; Lith. rietJti 'to hatch' < \*ureit-ehr; Lith. risti 'to roll', Latv. rist 'id.' < \*urit-. The original meaning was 'to twist', which remarkably enough in both Baltic and Germanic developed into 'to squirm' (cf. WPhal. vrissen 'worm' < \*wrip-man- and Lith. rietJti 'to hatch') as well as 'to sprout' (cf. OE wridian 'to bud', Latv. riest 'to sprout'). MDu. writen continues \*wrftan- from Pre-Gm. \*writtan-, a variant whose geminate implies that it was influenced by an iterative \*writt6n-, cf. Nw. ritla w.v. 'to swerve'. See also \*wraipa-.

\*wrot- f. 'root' - ON rot, p. rretr f. 'id.', Far. rot, pl. rږtur f. 'id.', OSw. rot f. 'id.', Elfd. ruot, pl. ryoter f. 'id.' (EUR?). A root noun usually reconstructed as PGm. \*wrot-. The Joss of the initial \*w is unexpected in East Norse, however. This means that the standard etymological connection with \*wurti- 'root' can only be maintained by assuming ad hoc deletion of\*w.

\*wrungon- f. 'snare' - Go. wruggo f. 'snare, noose' (ORV). Also cf. MDu. wronc m. 'noose, wreath; twisting; anger', Du. wrong c. 'braid' < \*wrunga-. Derived from \*wringan- ( q.v.).

\*wulan- s.v. 'to well up, boil, seethe' - Go. wulan s.v. 'to boil, be hot' ⇒ \*u/H-e- (IE). A zero-grade present closely related to \*wallan- (q.v.). With the same vocalism, cf. ON olmr adj. 'raging' < \*wulma- and Nw. olle f. 'wave' < \*wullon-, which is fully parallel to OCS vlbna 'id.' < \*u/H-neh₂-.

<!-- pdf-page: 638; section: dictionary -->

598 \*wulfa-

\*wulfa- m. 'wolf - Go. wulfs m. 'id.', ON u/fr m. 'id.', Far. ulvur m. 'id.', Elfd. ulv m. 'id.', OE wu/fm. 'id.', E wolf, OS wulf, wolfm. 'id.', Du. wolf c. 'id.', OHG wo/f m. 'id.', G Wo/fm. 'id.' ⇒ \*ufkw·o- (IE) - Skt. vfka-, YAv. vahrka- m. 'wolf, NP gurg 'id.'; Gr. AUKO<; m. 'id.', Lat. lupus m. 'id.', Lith. viTkas, Latv. vi/ks m. 'id.', OPru. wilkis 'id.' OCS vlbko, Ru. volk, SCr. vilk m. 'id.', Alb. ujk m. 'id.'. The IE word for 'wolf. In Germanic, the expected form would be \*\*wulhwaz, but the \*kw was changed into \*p as in \*fimfe '5' < \*penkwe and \*werpan- 'to throw' < \*uergw-e-. This change seems to have taken place after resonants in words with initial labials. See also \*wulgf- - \*wulbjo-.

\*wulgi- - \*wulbjo- f. 'she-wolf - ONylgr,ylgja f. 'id.', Elfd.ylva f. 'id.', OHG wulpa f. 'id.', MHG wiilpe f. 'id.' ⇒ \*ulkw-fhr (IE) - Skt. vrkf- f. 'she-wolf, Lith. vilke f. 'id.', Ru. volcfca; SCr. vuCica f. 'id.' < \*ulkW-ihr. The feminine counterpart of \*wu/fa- 'wolf (q.v.). ON ylgr continues the most archaic form, viz. \*wulgfz. (According to Kortlandt 1997a, it proves that the unrounding of \*kw was prior to Verner's law, but see the introduction §2.2.4.) The form \*wulbjo{n}-, as supported by OHG wulpa and Elfd. ylva, is more secondary, but still must have adopted the p ( < \*kW) from the masculine form before the operation ofVerner's law.

\*wulhja- m. 'haze' - ON ylr m. 'warmth', Nw. el m. '(mild) warmth; steam' ⇒ \*ufk-i- (IE) - To Arm. gaff adj. 'lukewarm' < \*ul(H]-i-? To be derived either from \*wulja- (Magnusson 1989: 1165; Olsen 1999: 204) or from \*wulhja-. The latter option is supported by the potential link with Ice!. volgur adj. 'lukewarm' < \*walga-. Also cf. Icel.ylja w.v. 'to heat up', Nw. elja w.v. 'to radiate heat' < \*wul(h}jojan-.

\*wulkjgon- w.v. 'to be nauseated' - Ice!. 6lga 'to foam, swell, seethe' (also cf. 6lgra w.v. 'to ferment, boil'), Far. ulka w.v. 'to vomit, be sick', Nw. ulka w.v. 'to be nauseated, feel sick', G wulgern w.v. 'to be nauseated' ⇒ \*ulgh-neh₂- (IE?). The iterative to \*walkan- (q.v.), originally conjugated as 3sg. \*wulkopi, 3pl. \*wulgunanpi < \*ulgh-neh₂-ti, \*ulgh-nh₂-enti. The geminate of the singular was apparently adopted by the strong verb.

\*wullo- f. 'wool' - Go. wulla f. 'id.', ON ull f. 'id.', Far. ul/ f., OE wull f. 'id.', E wool, OFri. wol/e f. 'id.', Du. wol c. 'id.', OHG wolla f. 'id.', G Wolle f. 'id.' ⇒ \*Hulh₁-neh₂- (IE) - Hitt. bulana- c. 'wool' < \*Hulhr(e)no-(?); Skt. iirХii-, YAv. varanii- f. 'id.'; Lith. vilna, Latv. vilna f. 'id.', OPru. wilna 'skirt', OCS vlbna, Ru. dial. v6lna, volna, SCr. vilna f. 'wool', Lat. liina f. 'id.', Olr. olann f. 'id.', MW gwlan 'id.' < \*Hulhrneh₂-; Gr. A.fjvo<; n. 'id.' < \*Hulhrno- (with secondary transfer to the s-stems); Lat. vel/us 'fleece' < \*Huelhrno-; Gr. 01'.iA.o<; adj. 'fleecy' < \*Houlhro-.

<!-- pdf-page: 639; section: dictionary -->

\*wulpu- 599 Unlike e.g. Skt. urХa- and Lat lana, the Germanic word with its double I points to a suffix-stressed form \*Hulhrneh₂-. This accentual mobility is an additional argument for reconstructing a consonant stem, which is already implied by the potential suffix ablaut of Hitt. bulana- < \*Hu/hi-en-. The alternative is to reconstruct a mobile neuter o-stem \*Hufhi-n-om, pl. \*Hulhi-n-eh₂.

\*wulfJU· m. 'glory' - Go. wuljms m. 'id.' ⇒ \*ul-tu- (EUR) - Lat vultus m. 'facial expression, face' < \*u(e)l-tu-; MW gwelet 'to see' < \*ue/-e-; Olr. fili,fi/ed m. 'seer' < \*ue/-et-. A tu-stem to the PIE root \*uel- 'to see'. Also cf. OE wuldor n. 'glory' < \*wuldra- < \*ul-tr6- / \*u/-dhro-.

\*wuna(n)- adj. 'accustomed' - OEge-wun adj. 'id.', OSgi-wono,gi-wuno adj. 'id.', Du. ge-woon adj. 'id.', OHG gi-won adj. 'id.', MHG ge-won, ge-wone adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*Hunh₁-on- (IE) - OCS unii comp. 'better' < \*HunhriHo-. A zero-grade formation closely related to \*wana- 2 ( q.v.).

\*wunda- adj. 'wounded' - Go. wunds adj. 'id.', OE wund adj. 'id.', OS wund adj. 'id.', MDu. wont adj. 'id.', OHG wunt adj. 'id.', MHG wunde adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*un-t6- (DRV). An adjective continuing the past participle of \*winnan- 'to suffer' (q.v.). Also cf. ON und, OE wund, OFri. wunde, OS wunda, Du. wand, OHG wunta, G Wunde f. 'wound' < \*wundo-.

\*wunen- w.v. 'to be used to' - Go. un-wunands adj. 'discontent, unhappy', ON una w.v. 'to be content, to enjoy; to dwell, abide', Elfd. uona w.v. 'to rest, stay', OE wunian w.v. 'to dwell, remain', OFri. wona, wuna w.v. 'id.', OS wonon, wunon w.v. 'id.', Du. wonen w.v. 'id.', OHG wonen w.v. 'to dwell, be used to', G wohnen w.v. 'to dwell, live' ⇒ \*Hunhi-ehi- (DRY). A stative to the adjective \*wuna(n)- (q.v.).

\*wunni-f. 'suffering' - Go. wunns f. 'id.' (DRY). An i-stem derived from the strong verb \*winnan- ( q.v.).

\*wunskjan- w.v. 'to wish' - ON reskja w.v. 'id.', OE wjiscan w.v. 'id.', E to wish, MDu. wunscen, wenscen w.v. 'id.', Du. wensen w.v. 'id.', OHG wunscan w.v. 'id.', G wiinschen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*Hunh₁-sk- (DRY) - Skt vaiichantu 3pl.imp. 'to love, desire' < \*Hunh₁-sk- (with a restored nasal). A jan-verb derived from \*wunska- 'wish', cf. OHG wunsc, G Wunsch m. 'wish, desire', which in turn was created to a PIE sk-present \*Hnhi-ske- 'to desire'. Similarly, ON 6ska w.v. 'to wish' < \*wunskojan- was derived from ON 6ska f. 'id.' < \*wunsko-. See also \*wana- 2, \*weni- and \*wunen-.

<!-- pdf-page: 640; section: dictionary -->

600 \*wurda-

\*wurda- n. 'word' - Go. waurd n. 'id.', ON ora n. 'id.', OE word n. 'id.', E word, OFri. word n. 'id.', OS word n. 'id.', Du. woord n. 'id.', OHG wort n. 'id.', G Wort n. 'id.' ⇒ \*(h₂)urdh·o- (IE?) - (?)Hitt. buyartai - burtanzi\* 'to curse' < \*h₂u6rdh-ei, \*h₂urdh-enti; Lat. verbum n. 'word' < \*h₂u(e/o)rdh-o-; Lith. vafdas, Latv. v&rds m. 'id.' < \*h₂uordh-o-; OPru. wirds 'id.' < \*h₂urdh-. The forms in the European languages straightforwardly point to \*urdh-1 but if the Hittite verb belongs here, it proves that the root had an initial laryngeal.

\*wurdi- f. 'fate, chance' - OE wyrd f. 'id.', OS wurd f. 'id.', OHG wurt f. 'id.' ⇒ \*urt-f- (DRV). An i-stem to the root of\*werpan- (q.v.).

\*wurgjan- w.v. 'to strangle' - OE wyrigan, wirgan w.v. 'id.', OFri. wergia w.v. 'id.', WFri. wjirgje w.v. 'id.', Du. wurgen w.v. 'id.', OHG wurgen w.v. 'id.', G wiirgen w.v. 'id.' ⇒ \*urgh-ie- (NEUR). A zero-grade jan-present related to \*wergan-. Also cf. ON urga f. 'strap' < \*wurgon-.

\*wurkjan- w.v. 'to work, produce, effect' - Go. waurkjan w.v. 'id.', ON yrkja w.v. 'to work; to write verses', Far.yrkja w.v. 'to compose, write', OE wyrcan w.v. 'to work', OHG wurchen w.v. 'id.', MHG wurken, wiirken w.v. 'id.' ⇒

\*urg-ie- (IE) - Identical to YAv. varaziieiti 'to do, act' < \*urg-ie-; also cf. Gr. £pow, p£0w 'to act, perform; to sacrifice' (with secondary full grades?). A zero-grade ie-present related to \*werka- 1. It occurs beside the mixed strong and weak verb \*werkjan-, pret. \*warht-, cf. OFri. werkia, wertzia, wirtza, pret. wrocht, OS wirka, pret. warhta, OHG wirchen, pret. warahta 'to work, produce; to perform' < \*uerg-ie-. Also cf. Go. waurstw n. 'deed' < \*wurhstwa-.

\*wurmi- m. 'worm' - Go. waurms m. 'snake', ON ormr m. 'id.', Far. ormur m. 'id.', Elfd. uorm m. 'id.', OE wyrm m. 'snake, worm', OFri. wirm m. 'worm', WFri. wjirm c. 'id.', OS wurm m. 'id.', Du. worm, wurm c. 'id.', OHG wurm m. 'worm, snake', G Wurm m. 'id.' ⇒ \*urm-i- (IE) - Identical to Lat. vermis m. 'worm, maggot' < \*urmi-; further cf. Gr. p6μo<; m. 'wood-worm' < \*urm-o( with dialectal vocalization of \*r?); Lith. vafmas m. 'insect, mosquito' < \*uorm-o-; OCS Vbrmbje n. 'insects' < \*urm-io-; OPru. wormyan adj. 'red' < \*urm-io-. Also note Nw. dial. yrme f. 'female snake' < \*wurmjo- (quasi PIE \*urm-ieh₂-) and ON yrmi n. 'vermin' < \*ga-wurmja-, which is formally identical to OCS Vbrmbje. OHG wormo m. 'purple', OFri. worma m. 'id.', OE wurma m. 'purple snail, woad' is a derivation referring to the color of the earthworm.

<!-- pdf-page: 641; section: dictionary -->

\*wurti- 601

\*wurti- f. '(medicinal) root; herb' - Go. waurts f. 'root', ON urt f. 'id.', OE wyrt f. 'plant, herb', OS wurt f. 'id.', MDu. wort f. 'id.', OHG wurz f. 'root, herb, spice' ⇒ \*urd-i- (EUR) - Gr. pt0a 'root' \*urd-ieh₂-(?); Lat. radix f. 'id.' < \*urH-d-ik-; Olr.fren f. 'id.' < \*urd-neh₂-; MW gwreid m. 'id.' < \*ur(e)h₂d-io-. A word whose PIE root can hardly be reconstructed. The presumably related PGm. \*wrot- together with Lat. radix presupposes \*ureh₂d-, but Olr. fren < PCelt. \*wrid-nii- < \*urd-neh₂- as well as \*wratjon- 'madder' < \*urod-ioimply variants without a laryngeal. Equally problematic are Gr. pt0a ( < \*urd-ieh₂- with schwa secundum as per Vine 1999: 5-30?) and W gwreidd which points to PCelt. \*wradi- ( < \*ureh₂d-f- with pretonic shortening or with \*CRHC- > \*CRaC-). The appurtenance of Gr. pfr6Lø -i:Koc; m. 'branch, twig; palm leaf is uncertain. Within Germanic, also cf. the compound OE wyrt-wala m. 'id.', MDu. wortel(e) m./f. 'id.', Du. wortel c. 'id.', OHG wurzala f. 'id.', G Wurzel f. 'id.' < \*wurti-wala/on- "root-stick" (cf. \*walu-).

\*wurpi- f. 'mound, elevation' - ON ura f. 'heap of stones fallen from a hill', Far. ura f. 'scree; collection of stones or boulders', Elfd. urd f. 'stony slope', OFri. wurth f. 'raised homestead', OS wurth f. 'mound, elevated homestead' ⇒ \*h₂ur-ti- (DRV). A ti-stem to the root of \*warjan- (q.v.). Related to Elfd. ward m. 'long or high mountain' < \*warpa-?

## Index
The order of the languages is as follows: Germanic (Gothic; Old Norse; Icelandic; Faroese; Norwegian; Elfdalian; Old & Modern Swedish (including Gutnish); Old, Early & Modern Danish; Old, Middle & Modern English; Old Frisian as well as Modern West, East & North Frisian; Old Saxon; Middle & New Low German; Old Low Franconian & Old Dutch followed by Middle, Early & Modern Dutch; Old, Middle & New High German plus Swiss German and Lombardic); Other Indo-European languages: Anatolian (Hittite; Luvian; Lycian); Tocharian (A & B); Italic (Oscan & Umbrian; Old, Classical, Vulgar, Middle and Neo-Latin; Italian; Lombard; Spanish; Portuguese; Old, Middle & Modern French); Venetic; Celtic (Celtiberian; Gaulish; Old, Middle & Modern Irish (including Scottish Gaelic); Old, Middle & Modern Welsh; Old, Middle & Modern Breton; Old, Middle & Modern Cornish); Bal½n-Indo-European (Mycenaean; Ancient & Modern Greek; Phrygian Albanian; Armenian); Thracian; Iranian (Old & Young Avestan, Old, Middle & New Persian; Sogdian; Khotanese; Pashto; Wakhi; Kurdish; Ossetic); lndic (Sanskrit); Slavic (all variants of Church Slavonic plus Old Church Slavonic; Serbian & Croatian; Slovene; Old and Modern Russian; Ukrainian; Byelorussian; Old & Modern Czech; Slovak; Old & Modern Polish); Baltic (Old Prussian; Old & Modem Lithuanian; Latvian); Non-Indo-European languages (in alphabetical order). With minor variations, the alphabetical order of the cited forms is everywhere (except for Greek) the one of the Roman alphabet, and whenever possible, l have brought it in agreement with the official (Roman) orthographies of the modern standard languages.

second-third-pass-notes:
  - Second pass: reflowed safe hard-wrapped text, normalized page-anchor spacing, escaped literal linguistic asterisks for Markdown rendering, corrected high-confidence front-matter and English OCR errors visible from the source pages.
  - Second pass: retained page anchors as HTML comments because they remain useful for citation and later verification.
  - Third pass: generated a character audit and replaced high-confidence OCR artifacts that functioned as the etymological arrow before reconstructed forms.
  - Third pass: did not attempt full character-authoritative verification of every Greek form, IPA character, laryngeal, diacritic, or reconstructed form against page images. A future character-authority pass would still add value.
  - Style decision: clean Markdown should not preserve line-breaks matching page width; line breaks should represent semantic structure, not scan/OCR geometry.
-->


<!--
fifth-pass-notes:
  - Fifth pass date: 2026-04-30T11:30:22Z
  - Scope: targeted dictionary-entry character audit plus bibliography cleanup.
  - Main-file changes: converted high-confidence OCR/text-layer character artifacts, including ASCII and OCR-damaged laryngeal notation (h1/h2/h3/hz/hJ), OCR arrow substitutes (⇒, =⇒, and selected artifact arrows), micro-sign Greek mu, explicit unclear markers for remaining unprintable control characters, and labiovelar kʷ fragments of the form /<W.
  - Bibliography: applied high-confidence corrections to the bibliographical abbreviations and selected cited-publication entries. The original reference text remains structurally intact; a companion normalized references file is included for review.
  - Audit: companion CSV files list remaining suspicious character contexts and headwords/forms likely to need future page-image checking, especially thorn/eth, Greek, £/¢/Ȭ/Ģ OCR remnants, and index-only Greek forms.
  - Limitation: this pass is not a full page-by-page character-authoritative edition of all 833 PDF pages.
-->


<!--
seventh-pass-notes:
  - Seventh pass date: 2026-04-30T18:35:00Z
  - Scope: combined targeted pass: residual audit cleanup, selected page-image verification, reference extraction/verification support, dictionary headword/opening-line audit, and structured lexical dataset extraction.
  - Main-file changes: corrected remaining high-confidence arrow remnants, repaired the OCR-corrupted term Verschärfung, corrected selected bibliography diacritics, and page-image-verified selected Greek and thorn/eth problem passages on pages 91, 172, 310, and 531.
  - Companion files: correction ledger, residual audit, headword-line verification CSV, lexical dataset CSV/Markdown, rebuilt references draft from the PDF text layer, and references verification report.
  - Limitation: this remains a targeted pass, not full line-by-line page-image verification of all 833 pages.
-->

thirteenth-pass-greek-index:
